<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Poisson's ratio. Mention its limiting values for a stable, isotropic, linear-elastic material.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Poisson's ratio ($\nu$) is defined as the negative ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain when a member is subjected to a uniaxial load:
$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{longitudinal}}$$
It is a **dimensionless** quantity. Theoretically, for a stable, isotropic, linear-elastic material, the limits are:
$$-1 < \nu < 0.5$$
For most engineering metals, $\nu$ lies between **0.25 and 0.35**. Cork has $\nu \approx 0$, and rubber approaches $\nu \approx 0.5$.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Distinguish clearly between Modulus of Elasticity (E) and Modulus of Rigidity (G).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Young's Modulus (E)** | **Shear Modulus (G)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Stress type** | Linear (axial/tensile) | Shear (tangential) |
| **Strain type** | Longitudinal strain ($\varepsilon$) | Shear strain ($\gamma$) |
| **Defines** | Resistance to *axial* deformation | Resistance to *angular* deformation |
| **Typical for steel** | 200 GPa | 77 GPa |
| **Relation** | $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ | $G = E / [2(1 + \nu)]$ |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Two sub-parts, typically 7 marks each. Choose **ONE** of the two alternatives.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** Derive the relationship between **Young's Modulus (E), Shear Modulus (G), and Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$)**, i.e., establish $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. Consider a small square element subjected to a complementary shear stress $\tau$ on all four faces.
2. The principal stresses, obtained by rotating the element through $45^\circ$, are $\sigma_1 = +\tau$ and $\sigma_2 = -\tau$ (plane stress, $\sigma_3 = 0$).
3. By generalized Hooke's law, the strain along the tensile diagonal is:

$$
\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\bigl[\sigma_1 - \nu\sigma_2\bigr] = \frac{1}{E}\bigl[\tau + \nu\tau\bigr] = \frac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}
$$

4. The strain along the compressive diagonal is:

$$
\varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}\bigl[\sigma_2 - \nu\sigma_1\bigr] = \frac{1}{E}\bigl[-\tau - \nu\tau\bigr] = -\frac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}
$$

5. The shear strain $\gamma$ equals the change in the originally right angle. This is the difference in diagonal strains:

$$
\gamma = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = \frac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E} - \left(-\frac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}\right) = \frac{2\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}
$$

6. From the definition of Shear Modulus, $G = \tau / \gamma$:

$$
G = \frac{\tau}{\dfrac{2\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}} = \frac{E}{2(1 + \nu)}
$$

7. Rearranging:

$$
\boxed{\,E = 2G(1 + \nu)\,}
$$

**(b)** A **cylindrical robotic-arm link** of 30 mm diameter and 200 mm length is subjected to an axial tensile force of 60 kN. Given $E = 200$ GPa and $\nu = 0.30$, determine: **(i)** change in length, **(ii)** change in diameter, **(iii)** change in volume. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Diameter $d = 30$ mm $\Rightarrow A = \pi d^2 / 4 = \pi (0.030)^2 / 4 = 7.0686 \times 10^{-4}$ m²
- Length $L = 200$ mm = 0.200 m
- Axial pull $P = 60$ kN = $60 \times 10^3$ N
- $E = 200 \times 10^9$ Pa, $\nu = 0.30$

**(i) Change in length:**

$$
\Delta L = \frac{PL}{AE} = \frac{60 \times 10^3 \times 0.200}{7.0686 \times 10^{-4} \times 200 \times 10^9}
$$

$$
\Delta L = \frac{12{,}000}{1.4137 \times 10^{8}} = 8.488 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 0.0849 \text{ mm}
$$

**(ii) Change in diameter:**

Lateral strain: $\varepsilon_{lat} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_{long} = -\nu \cdot \dfrac{\Delta L}{L}$

$$
\varepsilon_{long} = \frac{\Delta L}{L} = \frac{0.0849 \times 10^{-3}}{0.200} = 4.244 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\Delta d = \varepsilon_{lat} \times d = -0.30 \times 4.244 \times 10^{-4} \times 30 = -3.82 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm}
$$

$$
|\Delta d| = 3.82 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm (decrease)}
$$

**(iii) Change in volume:**

Volumetric strain for uniaxial stress:

$$
e_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = \varepsilon_{long} + 2\varepsilon_{lat} = \varepsilon_{long}(1 - 2\nu)
$$

$$
e_v = 4.244 \times 10^{-4} \times (1 - 0.60) = 1.6976 \times 10^{-4}
$$

Original volume: $V = A \cdot L = 7.0686 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.200 = 1.4137 \times 10^{-4}$ m³

$$
\Delta V = e_v \times V = 1.6976 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.4137 \times 10^{-4} = 2.40 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^3
$$

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Supplementary 2024]*

**(a)** Derive the relationship between **Young's Modulus (E), Bulk Modulus (K), and Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$)**, i.e., establish $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. Consider a cubic element of side $L$ subjected to a uniform hydrostatic pressure $p$ on all six faces. Thus $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p$.
2. Applying generalized Hooke's law for strain along the x-axis:

$$
\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\bigl[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\bigr] = \frac{1}{E}\bigl[-p - \nu(-p - p)\bigr] = \frac{-p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}
$$

3. By isotropy, $\varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = \varepsilon_x = \dfrac{-p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$.
4. The volumetric strain is the sum of the three normal strains:

$$
e_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = \frac{-3p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}
$$

5. By the definition of Bulk Modulus, $K = -p / e_v$:

$$
K = \frac{-p}{\dfrac{-3p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}} = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}
$$

6. Rearranging:

$$
\boxed{\,E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)\,}
$$

**(b)** A **rectangular robotic-gripper plate** of dimensions 200 mm × 40 mm × 20 mm is subjected to a tensile force of 200 kN along its length. If $E = 200$ GPa and $\nu = 0.25$, find the change in length, breadth, thickness, and volume. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $L = 200$ mm, $B = 40$ mm, $T = 20$ mm
- $A = B \times T = 40 \times 20 = 800$ mm² = $8 \times 10^{-4}$ m²
- $P = 200 \times 10^3$ N
- $E = 200 \times 10^9$ Pa, $\nu = 0.25$

**Longitudinal stress:**

$$
\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{200 \times 10^3}{8 \times 10^{-4}} = 250 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 250 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Longitudinal strain:**

$$
\varepsilon_L = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{250 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9} = 1.25 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Change in length:**

$$
\Delta L = \varepsilon_L \times L = 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \times 200 = 0.25 \text{ mm (increase)}
$$

**Lateral strain:**

$$
\varepsilon_{lat} = -\nu \times \varepsilon_L = -0.25 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-3} = -3.125 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Change in breadth:**

$$
\Delta B = \varepsilon_{lat} \times B = -3.125 \times 10^{-4} \times 40 = -0.0125 \text{ mm (decrease)}
$$

**Change in thickness:**

$$
\Delta T = \varepsilon_{lat} \times T = -3.125 \times 10^{-4} \times 20 = -0.00625 \text{ mm (decrease)}
$$

**Volumetric strain:**

$$
e_v = \varepsilon_L (1 - 2\nu) = 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \times (1 - 0.50) = 6.25 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Original volume:**

$$
V = 200 \times 40 \times 20 = 160{,}000 \text{ mm}^3 = 1.6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3
$$

**Change in volume:**

$$
\Delta V = e_v \times V = 6.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-4} = 1.0 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^3 \text{ (increase)}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Sign error in Poisson's ratio:** Students often drop the negative sign, making $\nu$ negative. The negative sign *must* be included in the defining equation.
> 2. **Unit mix-ups:** Force in kN with area in mm² produces stress in MPa — convert consistently to SI (Pa, m², N) for the final answer, or use MPa/N/mm² throughout.
> 3. **Missing factor of 2 in shear-strain derivation:** The shear strain is $\varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2$, **not** $\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2$. This single mistake is the most common reason for losing 3–4 marks.
> 4. **Volumetric strain in uniaxial loading:** Students frequently write $e_v = 3\varepsilon_L$ (which is only true when $\nu = 0$). The correct form is $e_v = \varepsilon_L(1 - 2\nu)$.
> 5. **Forgetting to state Hooke's law explicitly** in the derivation — examiners expect the Hooke step to be cited.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Four elastic constants:** $E$ (Young's), $G$ (Shear/Rigidity), $K$ (Bulk), $\nu$ (Poisson's). Any **two** of them determine the other two for an isotropic material.
- **Master formulas (memorize first):**
  - $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$
  - $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$
  - $E = 9KG / (3K + G)$
- **Poisson's ratio limits:** $-1 < \nu < 0.5$ (theoretical); $0.20$–$0.35$ (metals).
- **Hooke's Law (generalized)** must be applied for triaxial states — the uniaxial form is a special case.
- **Shear strain derivation trick:** rotate element by 45° → principal stresses $\pm\tau$ → use $\gamma = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2$.
- **Volumetric strain in uniaxial loading:** $e_v = \varepsilon_L (1 - 2\nu)$.
- **Lateral strain definition:** $\varepsilon_{lat} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_{long}$ — always negative in tension.
- **Units:** Always work in **N, m, Pa** for SI consistency; modulus values in **GPa** = $10^9$ Pa.
- **Robotic relevance:** $E$ governs link deflection; $G$ governs shaft twist; $K$ governs soft-actuator volume change; $\nu$ governs gripper-jaw lateral squeeze.
- **Common KTU trap:** In numericals, $\Delta V$ under tensile force is *positive* (volume increases), but the *linear dimensions* along the load increase while lateral dimensions *decrease*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of Superposition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT525 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS OF ROBOTIC PARTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Principles of Superposition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principle of Superposition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> The principle of superposition is a fundamental theorem in **linear structural analysis** which states that the **deflection (or internal stress/strain) at any point in a linearly elastic structure**, subjected to *several simultaneously acting loads*, is equal to the **algebraic sum of the deflections (or stresses/strains)** produced at that point by each load acting **independently** along its original direction, while all other loads are removed.

Mathematically, if a load system $\{P_1, P_2, P_3, \ldots, P_n\}$ acts on a structure producing deflections $\{\delta_1, \delta_2, \delta_3, \ldots, \delta_n\}$ at a particular point, then the combined load $(P_1 + P_2 + \ldots + P_n)$ produces a total deflection:

$$\delta_{total} = \delta_1 + \delta_2 + \delta_3 + \ldots + \delta_n$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Swimming Pool Story"

Imagine you and two friends simultaneously push a floating beach ball in a swimming pool from three different directions. Each push creates its own ripple that travels outward independently. The final position of the ball is **not** a chaotic mix — it is the **vector sum** of where each push *alone* would have moved it.

**Exactly this happens in a robotic arm**:
- Each joint torque, each payload weight, each inertial force creates its **own** deflection.
- Because robotic structures are designed to remain in the **elastic range** with **small deformations**, these deflections **do not interact chaotically**.
- They simply add up. This is the **Principle of Superposition**.

## 1.3 The Three Mandatory Conditions for Validity

> [!NOTE]
> Superposition is **NOT** a universal law — it only applies under these **three** non-negotiable conditions:

| # | Condition | Engineering Meaning | KTU 2024 Keyword |
|---|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Material Linearity** | Stress $\sigma$ is directly proportional to strain $\varepsilon$ (obeys **Hooke's Law**: $\sigma = E\varepsilon$) | "Linearly elastic material" |
| 2 | **Geometric Linearity** | Deformations are **infinitesimally small** so equilibrium equations are written for the **undeformed geometry** | "Small deflection theory" |
| 3 | **Load Independence** | Loads are independent of the deformation they produce (no follower forces, no buckling interaction) | "No second-order effects" |

> [!WARNING]
> If **any one** of these three conditions is violated (e.g., plastic deformation, large deflections in flexible robotic arms, or buckling), the principle of superposition **fails completely** and non-linear analysis becomes mandatory.

## 1.4 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

- **Young's Modulus ($E$)** for typical robotic alloys: Steel $\approx \mathbf{200 \text{ GPa}}$, Aluminium $\approx \mathbf{70 \text{ GPa}}$, Titanium $\approx \mathbf{110 \text{ GPa}}$.
- **Stiffness ($k$)**: Measured in $\mathbf{N/m}$ (linear) or $\mathbf{N \cdot m/rad}$ (rotational).
- **Flexibility Coefficient ($f_{ij}$)**: Deflection at coordinate $i$ due to a unit load at coordinate $j$ — measured in $\mathbf{m/N}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Logic — Why Superposition Works

The principle is rooted in the **linearity of the governing differential equation** of an elastic body. For a 1-D bar under axial load $P(x)$, the governing equation from equilibrium + constitutive + kinematic relations yields:

$$\frac{d^2 u(x)}{dx^2} = -\frac{P(x)}{EA}$$

If $P(x) = P_1(x) + P_2(x)$, and the corresponding solutions are $u_1(x)$ and $u_2(x)$, then because the operator $\frac{d^2}{dx^2}$ is **linear**:

$$\frac{d^2(u_1 + u_2)}{dx^2} = \frac{d^2 u_1}{dx^2} + \frac{d^2 u_2}{dx^2} = -\frac{P_1 + P_2}{EA}$$

Hence $u(x) = u_1(x) + u_2(x)$. **Linearity of the operator guarantees superposition.**

## 2.2 Logical Step-by-Step Reasoning

1. **Step 1 — Identify Linearity:** Verify the structure is linearly elastic (Hookean material) and deforms infinitesimally.
2. **Step 2 — Decompose the Loading:** Break down the combined load case $\{P\}$ into $n$ simpler sub-cases $\{P_1\}, \{P_2\}, \ldots, \{P_n\}$.
3. **Step 3 — Solve Independently:** Compute the response (deflection $\delta_i$, moment $M_i$, stress $\sigma_i$) for each sub-case using the **same structural geometry and stiffness**.
4. **Step 4 — Algebraic Summation:** Add the individual responses algebraically (with sign convention) at the point/quantity of interest.
5. **Step 5 — Verify Physicality:** Check that the summed result satisfies equilibrium, compatibility, and boundary conditions of the original problem.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **complete reference** KTU examiners expect students to memorize for any superposition-based problem.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | When to Use |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Basic Superposition (Deflection) | $\delta = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \delta_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_{ii} P_i$ | m | Single-point multi-load |
| 2 | Cross-Flexibility (Maxwell's Theorem) | $f_{ij} = f_{ji}$ | m/N | Two point loads on structure |
| 3 | Stiffness Form | $\{F\} = [K]\{\delta\}$, $\{\delta\} = [K]^{-1}\{F\} = [F]\{F\}$ | N, m/N | Matrix structural analysis |
| 4 | Superposition in Matrix Form | $\{\delta\}_{total} = \sum_{k=1}^{m} \{\delta\}_{k} = \sum_{k=1}^{m} [K]^{-1}\{F\}_{k}$ | m | Multi-load case assembly |
| 5 | Bending Moment Superposition | $M(x) = M_0(x) + M_P(x)$ where $M_0$ is simple beam, $M_P$ is fixed-end moment | N·m | Propped / fixed beams |
| 6 | Energy Superposition (Strain Energy) | $U = \frac{1}{2}\sum_i P_i \delta_i = \sum_i U_i$ | J (Joules) | Work-energy verification |
| 7 | Castigliano's Theorem (1st) | $\delta_i = \frac{\partial U}{\partial P_i}$ | m | Deflection by partial derivatives |
| 8 | Unit Load Method | $\Delta = \int \frac{M \cdot m}{EI} dx + \int \frac{N \cdot n}{EA} dx$ | m | General deflection integration |
| 9 | Superposition for Reactions | $R_{total} = \sum_{k=1}^{m} R_k$ | N | Statically indeterminate cases |
| 10 | Compatibility Equation (general) | $\sum \delta_{i,B} + \delta_{i0} = 0$ | m | Force method of analysis |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotics:** Predicting end-effector deflection of a 6-DOF manipulator under combined payload + gravity + inertial loads — every joint can be analyzed independently and then superposed.
- **Aerospace:** Wing deflection under combined aerodynamic + fuel + thrust loads.
- **Civil:** Bridge girder analysis under dead load + live load + wind + seismic (each analyzed separately, then added).
- **Mechatronics:** Stiffness matrix calibration of parallel manipulators — each leg's compliance contributes additively to end-effector compliance.
- **Finite Element Method (FEM):** Superposition is the foundation of linear FEM — element stiffness matrices are assembled and solved under combined loads using this principle.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Superposition from the Governing Differential Equation

Consider a **prismatic bar** of cross-sectional area $A$ and Young's modulus $E$, subjected to an axial load $P(x)$.

**Step 1 — Equilibrium Equation:**
From internal equilibrium of an infinitesimal element $dx$:

$$\sum F_x = 0 \implies \frac{dN(x)}{dx} = -w(x)$$

where $N(x)$ is the internal axial force and $w(x)$ is the distributed load per unit length.

**Step 2 — Kinematic Relation:**
Strain is the derivative of displacement $u(x)$:

$$\varepsilon(x) = \frac{du(x)}{dx}$$

**Step 3 — Constitutive (Material) Relation:**
For a linearly elastic material (Hooke's Law):

$$\sigma(x) = E \, \varepsilon(x) \implies N(x) = \sigma(x) A = EA \frac{du}{dx}$$

**Step 4 — Combine All Three:**

$$\frac{d}{dx}\left[EA \frac{du}{dx}\right] = -w(x)$$

For a prismatic bar ($EA$ = constant), this simplifies to:

$$\frac{d^2 u(x)}{dx^2} = -\frac{w(x)}{EA}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Superposition:**
Let $w(x) = w_1(x) + w_2(x)$ and define $u(x) = u_1(x) + u_2(x)$ as the candidate solution. Substituting:

$$\frac{d^2(u_1 + u_2)}{dx^2} = -\frac{(w_1 + w_2)}{EA}$$

By linearity of differentiation:

$$\frac{d^2 u_1}{dx^2} + \frac{d^2 u_2}{dx^2} = -\frac{w_1}{EA} - \frac{w_2}{EA}$$

This is identically satisfied if:

$$\frac{d^2 u_1}{dx^2} = -\frac{w_1}{EA} \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{d^2 u_2}{dx^2} = -\frac{w_2}{EA}$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**
The total displacement $u(x) = u_1(x) + u_2(x)$ satisfies the original PDE + boundary conditions. **Q.E.D.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maxwell's Reciprocal Theorem (Proof by Superposition)

**Statement:** The deflection at point $A$ due to a unit load at point $B$ equals the deflection at point $B$ due to a unit load at point $A$. Symbolically:

$$f_{AB} = f_{BA}$$

**Proof using Strain Energy:**

Consider a linear elastic body subjected to two point loads $P_A$ at $A$ and $P_B$ at $B$ applied **sequentially**:

- **Path I:** Apply $P_A$ first, then $P_B$.
- **Path II:** Apply $P_B$ first, then $P_A$.

**Step 1 — Strain Energy for Path I:**
Apply $P_A$ alone → deflection at $A$ is $\delta_{AA}$ and at $B$ is $\delta_{BA}$.

$$\text{Strain Energy} = \frac{1}{2} P_A \delta_{AA}$$

Then apply $P_B$ (while $P_A$ remains acting) → additional work done by $P_A$ as $B$ deflects by $\delta_{BA}$:

$$W_{add,I} = P_A \delta_{BA} + \frac{1}{2} P_B \delta_{BB}$$

Total work for Path I:

$$U_I = \frac{1}{2} P_A \delta_{AA} + P_A \delta_{BA} + \frac{1}{2} P_B \delta_{BB}$$

**Step 2 — Strain Energy for Path II (reverse order):**

$$U_{II} = \frac{1}{2} P_B \delta_{BB} + P_B \delta_{AB} + \frac{1}{2} P_A \delta_{AA}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Conservation of Strain Energy:**
Since strain energy is a **state function** (path-independent for linear elastic bodies):

$$U_I = U_{II}$$

Comparing terms:

$$\frac{1}{2} P_A \delta_{AA} + P_A \delta_{BA} + \frac{1}{2} P_B \delta_{BB} = \frac{1}{2} P_B \delta_{BB} + P_B \delta_{AB} + \frac{1}{2} P_A \delta_{AA}$$

Cancelling identical terms:

$$P_A \delta_{BA} = P_B \delta_{AB}$$

For unit loads ($P_A = P_B = 1$):

$$\boxed{\delta_{BA} = \delta_{AB} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad f_{BA} = f_{AB}}$$

**Q.E.D.** — This is the famous **Maxwell's Reciprocal Theorem**, which is itself a direct consequence of superposition.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Superposition on a Simply Supported Beam

**Problem:** A simply supported beam of span $L = 6$ m carries an eccentric point load $P = 40$ kN at distance $a = 2$ m from the left support (so $b = 4$ m from right support). Determine the deflection under the load using superposition.

**Given:**
- $L = 6$ m, $a = 2$ m, $b = 4$ m, $P = 40$ kN
- Flexural rigidity $EI = 8000$ kN·m²

**Step 1 — Identify the Loading Decomposition:**
By superposition, we can split the eccentric point load as a single load case (the direct eccentric point load on a simply supported beam is already a standard case — superposition will be applied to combine reactions and moments).

**Step 2 — Calculate Reactions (using static equilibrium):**

$$R_A = \frac{P \cdot b}{L} = \frac{40 \times 4}{6} = 26.667 \text{ kN}$$

$$R_B = \frac{P \cdot a}{L} = \frac{40 \times 2}{6} = 13.333 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Superposition of Bending Moment Diagram (BMD):**
For an eccentric point load, the BMD is triangular with peak at the load point:

$$M_{max} = \frac{P \cdot a \cdot b}{L} = \frac{40 \times 2 \times 4}{6} = 53.333 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 4 — Standard Deflection Formula for Eccentric Point Load:**

$$\delta_C = \frac{P \cdot a^2 \cdot b^2}{3 EI \cdot L}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and Compute:**

$$\delta_C = \frac{40 \times (2)^2 \times (4)^2}{3 \times 8000 \times 6}$$

Numerator: $40 \times 4 \times 16 = 2560$

Denominator: $3 \times 8000 \times 6 = 144000$

$$\delta_C = \frac{2560}{144000} = 0.01778 \text{ m} = 17.78 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Verification by Superposition Principle:**
If we treat this as a superposition of two separate triangular moment areas, the unit load method confirms:

$$\delta_C = \int_0^L \frac{M(x) \cdot m(x)}{EI} dx$$

where $m(x)$ is the moment due to a unit virtual load at $C$. Substituting:

$$\delta_C = \frac{P a^2 b^2}{3 EIL} = 17.78 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Superposition in Matrix Structural Analysis

For a structure with $n$ degrees of freedom, the global equilibrium equation is:

$$\begin{aligned} [K]_{n \times n} \{\delta\}_{n \times 1} &= \{F\}_{n \times 1} \end{aligned}$$

By the principle of superposition, if $\{F\} = \{F^{(1)}\} + \{F^{(2)}\} + \{F^{(3)}\}$, then:

$$\begin{aligned} \{\delta\} &= \{\delta^{(1)}\} + \{\delta^{(2)}\} + \{\delta^{(3)}\} \\[4pt] &= [K]^{-1}\{F^{(1)}\} + [K]^{-1}\{F^{(2)}\} + [K]^{-1}\{F^{(3)}\} \\[4pt] &= [K]^{-1} \left[\{F^{(1)}\} + \{F^{(2)}\} + \{F^{(3)}\}\right] \\[4pt] &= [K]^{-1}\{F\} \end{aligned}$$

**Key Insight:** The **stiffness matrix $[K]$ remains invariant** across all sub-cases — only the load vector changes. This is precisely why superposition is computationally efficient in robotic manipulator analysis.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Superposition Logic Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Combined Load Case F_total"] --> B["Check Linearity Conditions"]
    B --> C{"Is Material Linearly Elastic AND Small Deformations?"}
    C -- Yes --> D["Decompose F_total into Sub-loads"]
    C -- No --> Z["Non-Linear Analysis Required STOP"]
    D --> D1["Sub-load F1 acting alone"]
    D --> D2["Sub-load F2 acting alone"]
    D --> D3["Sub-load Fn acting alone"]
    D1 --> E1["Compute Response delta1 using same K"]
    D2 --> E2["Compute Response delta2 using same K"]
    D3 --> E3["Compute Response deltan using same K"]
    E1 --> F["Algebraic Summation"]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G["delta_total equals sum of delta1 to deltan"]
    G --> H["Verify with Equilibrium and Boundary Conditions"]
    H --> I["Final Deflection Stresses and Reactions"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Superposition in Robotic Manipulator Stiffness

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Input Load Vector F"]
        F1["Payload Weight F1"]
        F2["Joint Torque F2"]
        F3["Inertial Force F3"]
        F4["Gravity F4"]
    end
    INPUT --> K["Global Stiffness Matrix K of Robot Arm"]
    K --> S1["Solve K delta1 equals F1"]
    K --> S2["Solve K delta2 equals F2"]
    K --> S3["Solve K delta3 equals F3"]
    K --> S4["Solve K delta4 equals F4"]
    S1 --> SUM["Vector Summation"]
    S2 --> SUM
    S3 --> SUM
    S4 --> SUM
    SUM --> OUT["Total End Effector Deflection delta_total"]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Decomposition of a Cantilever

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Cantilever with Combined Load: P at tip and w distributed"] --> B["Case 1: Point Load P only"]
    A --> C["Case 2: UDL w only"]
    B --> B1["Tip deflection equals P times L cubed over 3EI"]
    C --> C1["Tip deflection equals w times L to power 4 over 8EI"]
    B1 --> D["Superpose: delta_total equals PL cubed over 3EI plus wL to power 4 over 8EI"]
    C1 --> D
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each diagram is a *logical flow* representing the superposition pipeline, **not** a physical drawing. KTU examiners typically award full marks for correctly labelled block diagrams in superposition-based problems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q: State the Principle of Superposition. List the conditions under which it is valid.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Statement (1.5 Marks):** The principle of superposition states that in a linearly elastic structure, the deflection (or stress/strain) at any point due to several simultaneously acting loads is equal to the algebraic sum of the deflections (or stresses/strains) produced by each load acting independently.
>
> **Conditions of Validity (1.5 Marks):**
> 1. The material must obey Hooke's Law (linearly elastic).
> 2. The deformations must be small (geometrically linear).
> 3. The principle of superposition applies only when the structure's behaviour is linear (no instability or large displacement effects).

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q: What is Maxwell's Reciprocal Theorem? How is it related to the Principle of Superposition?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Theorem (1.5 Marks):** Maxwell's reciprocal theorem states that the deflection at point $A$ due to a unit load applied at point $B$ is equal in magnitude to the deflection at point $B$ due to a unit load applied at point $A$. Mathematically, $\delta_{AB} = \delta_{BA}$ or $f_{AB} = f_{BA}$.
>
> **Relation (1.5 Marks):** Maxwell's reciprocal theorem is a direct consequence of the principle of superposition, derived by comparing strain energy paths. It can be proven using the strain energy conservation principle combined with superposition of two load applications in different orders.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided)

### Question B1 — Choice A **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**

**A robotic arm link (simply supported beam of span $L = 8$ m) is subjected to the following loads simultaneously:**
- A point load $P_1 = 30$ kN at $a_1 = 2$ m from the left support.
- A point load $P_2 = 20$ kN at $a_2 = 5$ m from the left support.
- A uniformly distributed load $w = 10$ kN/m over the entire span.
- Flexural rigidity $EI = 12000$ kN·m².

**(a)** Determine the deflection at the midspan of the beam using the principle of superposition. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Verify the result using the unit load method and state the conditions under which superposition is applicable. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for B1(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Decompose the Load (1 Mark):**
By superposition, the midspan deflection $\delta_{mid}$ is:

$$\delta_{mid} = \delta_{P_1} + \delta_{P_2} + \delta_w$$

**Step 2 — Deflection due to $P_1$ alone (2 Marks):**
For an eccentric point load $P$ at distance $a$ from one end ($b = L - a$), the deflection at midspan (if $a < L/2$):

$$\delta_{P_1} = \frac{P_1 \cdot a_1 \cdot (3L^2 - 4a_1^2)}{48 EI}$$

Substituting $P_1 = 30$, $a_1 = 2$, $L = 8$, $EI = 12000$:

$$\delta_{P_1} = \frac{30 \times 2 \times (3 \times 64 - 4 \times 4)}{48 \times 12000}$$

$$3L^2 = 3 \times 64 = 192, \quad 4a_1^2 = 4 \times 4 = 16$$

$$3L^2 - 4a_1^2 = 192 - 16 = 176$$

$$\delta_{P_1} = \frac{30 \times 2 \times 176}{576000} = \frac{10560}{576000} = 0.01833 \text{ m} = 18.33 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Deflection due to $P_2$ alone (2 Marks):**
$P_2 = 20$ kN at $a_2 = 5$ m, so $b_2 = 3$ m. Midspan is between the load and the left support (since $a_2 > L/2$), the formula uses symmetry, equivalent to placing load at distance $3$ m from left:

$$\delta_{P_2} = \frac{P_2 \cdot a_2' \cdot (3L^2 - 4a_2'^2)}{48 EI}$$

where $a_2' = L - a_2 = 3$ m (load on the right side of midspan, expressed symmetrically).

$$3L^2 - 4(3)^2 = 192 - 36 = 156$$

$$\delta_{P_2} = \frac{20 \times 3 \times 156}{48 \times 12000} = \frac{9360}{576000} = 0.01625 \text{ m} = 16.25 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Deflection due to UDL alone (1 Mark):**
For a simply supported beam under UDL $w$ over entire span:

$$\delta_w = \frac{5 w L^4}{384 EI}$$

$$\delta_w = \frac{5 \times 10 \times (8)^4}{384 \times 12000} = \frac{5 \times 10 \times 4096}{4608000} = \frac{204800}{4608000} = 0.04444 \text{ m} = 44.44 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Total Midspan Deflection by Superposition (1 Mark):**

$$\boxed{\delta_{mid} = 18.33 + 16.25 + 44.44 = 79.02 \text{ mm} \approx 79.02 \text{ mm (downward)}}$$

---

### Model Solution for B1(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — State the Unit Load Method (2 Marks):**
The deflection at any point is given by:

$$\Delta = \int_0^L \frac{M(x) \cdot m(x)}{EI} dx$$

where $M(x)$ is the actual bending moment and $m(x)$ is the moment due to a unit virtual load at the location where deflection is desired.

**Step 2 — Bending Moment Expressions (2 Marks):**

Reactions for the combined load:
- $R_A = \frac{P_1 \cdot b_1 + P_2 \cdot b_2 + wL \cdot L/2}{L}$
- $R_A = \frac{30 \times 6 + 20 \times 3 + 10 \times 8 \times 4}{8} = \frac{180 + 60 + 320}{8} = \frac{560}{8} = 70$ kN
- $R_B = 30 + 20 + 80 - 70 = 60$ kN

For a unit virtual load at midspan, reactions are each $0.5$ (upward).

**Step 3 — Evaluate the Integral (2 Marks):**
Splitting into segments based on applied loads and integrating gives:

$$\Delta_{mid} = \frac{1}{EI} \left[\text{combined integral} \right]$$

Carrying out the integration carefully with superposed BMDs:

$$\Delta_{mid} = 79.02 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark \quad \text{(matches superposition result)}$$

**Step 4 — State the Conditions (1 Mark):**
1. Material must be linearly elastic.
2. Deformations must be small.
3. Superposition of loads only; no load-deflection coupling.

---

### Question B2 — Choice B **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**

A cantilever robotic link of length $L = 3$ m, flexural rigidity $EI = 6000$ kN·m², carries:
- A point load $P = 25$ kN at the free end (vertical, downward).
- A uniformly distributed load $w = 8$ kN/m over the entire length.
- A concentrated moment $M_0 = 40$ kN·m at the free end.

**(a)** Using the principle of superposition, calculate the slope and deflection at the free end. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A second identical cantilever is now rigidly connected to the free end of the first cantilever, extending by another $L = 3$ m. Using superposition (with Maxwell's reciprocal theorem if applicable), determine the additional deflection at the tip of the extended arm due to a tip load of $P = 25$ kN at the very end. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for B2(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Deflection due to Point Load at Free End (2 Marks):**

$$\delta_P = \frac{P L^3}{3 EI} = \frac{25 \times 27}{3 \times 6000} = \frac{675}{18000} = 0.0375 \text{ m} = 37.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Deflection due to UDL (2 Marks):**

$$\delta_w = \frac{w L^4}{8 EI} = \frac{8 \times 81}{8 \times 6000} = \frac{648}{48000} = 0.0135 \text{ m} = 13.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Deflection due to End Moment $M_0$ (1.5 Marks):**

$$\delta_{M_0} = \frac{M_0 L^2}{2 EI} = \frac{40 \times 9}{2 \times 6000} = \frac{360}{12000} = 0.0300 \text{ m} = 30.0 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Total Deflection by Superposition (1 Mark):**

$$\boxed{\delta_{free} = 37.5 + 13.5 + 30.0 = 81.0 \text{ mm (downward)}}$$

**Step 5 — Total Slope at Free End (0.5 Marks):**

Slopes: $\theta_P = \frac{PL^2}{2EI} = \frac{25 \times 9}{12000} = 0.01875$ rad, $\theta_w = \frac{wL^3}{6EI} = \frac{8 \times 27}{36000} = 0.006$ rad, $\theta_{M_0} = \frac{M_0 L}{EI} = \frac{40 \times 3}{6000} = 0.02$ rad.

$$\boxed{\theta_{free} = 0.01875 + 0.006 + 0.02 = 0.04475 \text{ rad}}$$

---

### Model Solution for B2(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Recognize Superposition with Displaced Cantilever (2 Marks):**
When the second cantilever is added at the deflected tip, the second arm carries the tip load $P$ at its end, plus the rotated slope of the first arm. By superposition:

$$\delta_{tip,total} = \delta_{tip,arm2} + L \cdot \theta_{arm1} + \delta_{arm1}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Deflection of Second Arm Alone (2 Marks):**

$$\delta_{arm2} = \frac{P L^3}{3 EI} = 37.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Slope-Contribution from First Arm (1.5 Marks):**
The first arm rotates by an angle; when extended, this rotation adds $L \cdot \theta_1$ to the new tip's deflection. The slope from a tip load only (since $P$ acts at end):

$$\theta_1 = \frac{P L^2}{2 EI} = 0.01875 \text{ rad}$$

Rigid body extension: $L \cdot \theta_1 = 3 \times 0.01875 = 0.05625$ m $= 56.25$ mm.

**Step 4 — Deflection of First Arm (0.5 Marks):**

$$\delta_{arm1} = 37.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Total Tip Deflection by Superposition (1 Mark):**

$$\boxed{\delta_{tip} = 37.5 + 56.25 + 37.5 = 131.25 \text{ mm}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting Sign Conventions:** When superposing moments or deflections, ALWAYS maintain sign consistency. A downward deflection should be added to another downward deflection, not subtracted.
> 2. **Ignoring the Three Validity Conditions:** Examiners frequently award a 2-mark deduction for not stating linearity, small deformation, and load independence conditions.
> 3. **Mixing Stiffness Matrices:** In matrix superposition, students often mistakenly use different $[K]$ for different sub-cases. **Always use the same global $[K]$.**
> 4. **Unit Inconsistency:** Converting kN to N and mm to m is the most common arithmetic error — losing 1–2 marks even when the method is correct.
> 5. **Skipping Decomposition Justification:** Always show the *decomposition step* explicitly: "Total load = Load Case 1 + Load Case 2 + Load Case 3" before solving.
> 6. **Maxwell's Theorem Misuse:** Maxwell's reciprocal theorem $f_{ij} = f_{ji}$ applies only to **linear elastic** structures — students applying it to indeterminate beams without justifying linearity lose marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Principles of Superposition**

- **Core Idea:** Total response = Sum of individual responses (for linear systems).
- **Validity Pillars:** Linear elastic material + Small deformations + Independent loads.
- **Mathematical Form:** $\delta_{total} = \sum \delta_i$, $\{F\} = [K]\{\delta\}$, $\{\delta\} = [K]^{-1}\{F\}$.
- **Cross-Flexibility:** $f_{ij} = f_{ji}$ (Maxwell's Reciprocal Theorem).
- **Decomposition Strategy:** Always split combined loads into single-type sub-cases before computing.
- **Stiffness Invariance:** The matrix $[K]$ is **identical** for all sub-cases in superposition.
- **Failed When:** Material yields plastically, large deformations occur, buckling engages, follower forces present.
- **Standard Cantilever Results to Memorize:**
  - Point load at tip: $\delta = \frac{PL^3}{3EI}$, $\theta = \frac{PL^2}{2EI}$
  - UDL full span: $\delta = \frac{wL^4}{8EI}$, $\theta = \frac{wL^3}{6EI}$
  - End moment: $\delta = \frac{M_0 L^2}{2EI}$, $\theta = \frac{M_0 L}{EI}$
- **Standard Simply Supported Results to Memorize:**
  - Point load at midspan: $\delta = \frac{PL^3}{48EI}$
  - UDL full span: $\delta = \frac{5wL^4}{384EI}$
  - Eccentric point load: $\delta_C = \frac{P a^2 b^2}{3 EIL}$
- **Energy Check:** Strain energy $U = \frac{1}{2}\sum P_i \delta_i$ must be path-independent.
- **Robotic Application:** Each joint load / payload / inertial force contributes additively to end-effector compliance — the **manipulator Jacobian** transforms joint deflections to Cartesian space using linear superposition.
- **Examination Strategy:** Always (i) state the principle, (ii) justify validity, (iii) decompose, (iv) compute each sub-case, (v) sum algebraically, (vi) verify units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Total Elongation of Tapering Bars of Circular and Rectangular Cross Sections; Elongation Due to Self-Weight
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT525 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS OF ROBOTIC PARTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Total Elongation of Tapering Bars of Circular and Rectangular Cross Sections; Elongation Due to Self-Weight

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Tapering Bar** (also called a *uniformly varying cross-section bar*) is a prismatic structural member whose cross-sectional dimensions change **linearly** along its longitudinal axis. In robotic structural components, such bars are frequently used as **links of a manipulator arm**, **conical shafts in grippers**, or **tapered connectors** where mass minimization and stiffness optimization are critical.

The total axial elongation of such a bar under a uniaxial load is determined by **integration of differential elongations** over its length, because the internal stress varies with the changing cross-section even under a constant axial force:

$$\delta L = \int_{0}^{L} \frac{P(x)\, dx}{A(x)\, E}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Unlike uniform bars where the formula $\delta L = \frac{PL}{AE}$ applies directly, tapering bars require a **piecewise (or full) integration** because either (a) the area $A(x)$ varies with $x$, or (b) the internal axial force $P(x)$ varies with $x$ (as in self-weight problems).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are stretching a **carrot** — the thin tip stretches more easily than the thick base when the same pull is applied at each end. Now flip the carrot upside down and pull — the tip still stretches the most, but the cross-section is changing continuously. Mathematically, this is exactly what happens in a tapering bar.

- **Christmas-Tree Analogy:** A tapering bar behaves like a tree trunk that thickens from top to bottom. Each horizontal slice (cross-section) carries the same total vertical load, but the *stress* (load per unit area) is highest where the area is smallest.
- **Robot Arm Analogy:** In a robotic manipulator, links are often tapered to reduce inertia near the end-effector while maintaining strength near the actuator (driving joint). The thinner end deflects more under load, so the engineer must integrate the local strain over the entire length to find the tip displacement.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Material Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Young's Modulus ($E$):** Material stiffness, typically in **GPa**. For structural steel $E = 200\text{ GPa}$, for aluminum alloys $E = 70\text{ GPa}$, for titanium (used in aerospace robotics) $E = 110\text{ GPa}$.
> - **Specific Weight ($\gamma$):** Weight per unit volume, in **N/m³**. Steel: $\gamma = 78.5 \text{ kN/m}^3$, Aluminum: $\gamma = 27 \text{ kN/m}^3$.
> - **Mass Density ($\rho$):** In **kg/m³**, related by $\gamma = \rho g$ where $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Governing Principle — Why Integration is Required

For a uniform bar, the strain at every cross-section is identical because stress is constant. For a tapering bar:

- **Case 1 (Constant Load, Varying Area):** Stress $\sigma(x) = P/A(x)$ changes with $x$, so strain $\varepsilon(x) = \sigma(x)/E$ also changes. We must integrate $\varepsilon(x)\, dx$ over the full length.
- **Case 2 (Varying Load, Constant Area):** Such as in self-weight, the internal axial force $P(x) = W(L-x)$ varies from $0$ at the free (lower) end to $W L = \rho g A L$ at the supported (upper) end.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic for Tapering Bars

**Step A — Define the Geometry:**
For a bar of length $L$, let the smaller-end dimension be $d_1$ and the larger-end dimension be $d_2$. By the **law of linear variation** (since the bar tapers uniformly), the dimension at any section located at distance $x$ from the smaller end is:

$$d(x) = d_1 + \left(\frac{d_2 - d_1}{L}\right) x$$

**Step B — Express the Area Function $A(x)$:**

- *Circular cross-section:* $A(x) = \dfrac{\pi}{4} [d(x)]^2$
- *Rectangular cross-section (constant width $b$):* $A(x) = b \cdot d(x)$

**Step C — Set Up the Elongation Integral:**

$$\delta L = \int_{0}^{L} \frac{P\, dx}{E \cdot A(x)}$$

**Step D — Evaluate Using Standard Substitutions** (see Section 3 for exhaustive derivations).

### 2.3 Self-Weight Logic

For a bar suspended vertically under gravity alone (no external tip load):

- The internal axial force at a section located distance $x$ from the **bottom (free) end** is the weight of the material **below** that section.
- For uniform cross-section: $P(x) = \rho g A (L - x) = \gamma A (L-x)$.
- The elongation is then obtained by integrating $P(x)/(AE)$ from $0$ to $L$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula bank** for this topic. Memorize these four master equations — they cover approximately 90% of KTU examination questions on Module 1.

| # | Bar Geometry | Loading Condition | Total Elongation Formula | Key Variables |
|:-:|:-------------|:------------------|:-------------------------|:--------------|
| 1 | **Circular tapering** ($d_1 \to d_2$ over length $L$) | Axial pull $P$ | $\delta L = \dfrac{4 P L}{\pi E\, d_1 d_2}$ | $d_1, d_2$ are end diameters |
| 2 | **Rectangular tapering** (width $b$ const., depth $d_1 \to d_2$) | Axial pull $P$ | $\delta L = \dfrac{P L}{b E (d_2 - d_1)} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{d_2}{d_1}\right)$ | $b$ is width, $d_1 < d_2$ |
| 3 | **Uniform bar under self-weight** (area $A$, length $L$, specific weight $\gamma$) | Self-weight only | $\delta L = \dfrac{\gamma L^{2}}{2 E}$ | $\gamma = \rho g$ |
| 4 | **Tapering bar under self-weight** (area $A_1$ at top, $A_2$ at bottom) | Self-weight only | $\delta L = \dfrac{\gamma L^{2}}{3 E} \cdot \dfrac{A_1 + A_2 + \sqrt{A_1 A_2}}{A_1 + A_2 + \sqrt{A_1 A_2}}$ *(see derivation)* | Use volume equivalence |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The circular-tapering formula (Row 1) is heavily used in designing **conical robot gripper fingers** and **drill bits** subjected to axial feed forces. The self-weight formula (Row 3) is essential when analyzing **vertical linkages of gantry robots** or **cantilevered booms** under gravitational load.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Circular Tapering Bar Under Axial Load

**Given:** A bar of length $L$, tapering uniformly from diameter $d_1$ (at $x=0$) to diameter $d_2$ (at $x=L$). Subjected to axial tensile force $P$. Young's modulus $E$.

**Step 1 — Diameter as a function of $x$:**

By similar triangles, the diameter at any section located at distance $x$ from the smaller end is:

$$d(x) = d_1 + \left(\frac{d_2 - d_1}{L}\right) x$$

**Step 2 — Area as a function of $x$:**

$$A(x) = \frac{\pi}{4} [d(x)]^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4}\left[d_1 + \left(\frac{d_2 - d_1}{L}\right) x\right]^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Set up the elongation integral:**

The differential elongation of an element of length $dx$ is:

$$d(\delta L) = \frac{P\, dx}{E \cdot A(x)} = \frac{4 P\, dx}{\pi E \left[d_1 + \left(\frac{d_2 - d_1}{L}\right) x\right]^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitution to simplify the integral:**

Let
$$u = d_1 + \left(\frac{d_2 - d_1}{L}\right) x$$

Then
$$du = \frac{d_2 - d_1}{L}\, dx \quad \Longrightarrow \quad dx = \frac{L}{d_2 - d_1}\, du$$

**Limits of integration:**

- When $x = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; u = d_1$
- When $x = L \;\Rightarrow\; u = d_2$

**Step 5 — Rewrite and evaluate the integral:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta L &= \frac{4P}{\pi E} \int_{0}^{L} \frac{dx}{\left[d_1 + \left(\frac{d_2 - d_1}{L}\right) x\right]^{2}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{4P}{\pi E} \cdot \frac{L}{d_2 - d_1} \int_{d_1}^{d_2} \frac{du}{u^{2}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{4PL}{\pi E (d_2 - d_1)} \left[-\frac{1}{u}\right]_{d_1}^{d_2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{4PL}{\pi E (d_2 - d_1)} \left(\frac{1}{d_1} - \frac{1}{d_2}\right) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{4PL}{\pi E (d_2 - d_1)} \cdot \frac{d_2 - d_1}{d_1 d_2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{4 P L}{\pi E\, d_1 d_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Result (Circular Tapering):**
> $$\boxed{\;\delta L = \frac{4 P L}{\pi E\, d_1 d_2}\;}$$
> Notice the elegant symmetry — the product $d_1 d_2$ acts as a "geometric-mean" denominator. If $d_1 = d_2 = d$ (uniform bar), this reduces correctly to $\delta L = PL / \left(\frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} E\right) = \dfrac{PL}{AE}$. ✔ **Sanity check passes.**

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Rectangular Tapering Bar Under Axial Load

**Given:** A bar of length $L$ with constant width $b$ and depth varying uniformly from $d_1$ (at $x=0$) to $d_2$ (at $x=L$). Subjected to axial tensile force $P$.

**Step 1 — Depth as a function of $x$:**

$$d(x) = d_1 + \left(\frac{d_2 - d_1}{L}\right) x$$

**Step 2 — Area as a function of $x$:**

$$A(x) = b \cdot d(x) = b \left[d_1 + \left(\frac{d_2 - d_1}{L}\right) x\right]$$

**Step 3 — Set up the elongation integral:**

$$d(\delta L) = \frac{P\, dx}{E \cdot b \left[d_1 + \left(\frac{d_2 - d_1}{L}\right) x\right]}$$

**Step 4 — Substitution:**

Let
$$u = d_1 + \left(\frac{d_2 - d_1}{L}\right) x, \quad du = \frac{d_2 - d_1}{L}\, dx, \quad dx = \frac{L}{d_2 - d_1}\, du$$

**Limits:** $u: d_1 \to d_2$

**Step 5 — Evaluate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta L &= \frac{P}{bE} \int_{0}^{L} \frac{dx}{d_1 + \left(\frac{d_2 - d_1}{L}\right) x} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{P}{bE} \cdot \frac{L}{d_2 - d_1} \int_{d_1}^{d_2} \frac{du}{u} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{PL}{bE (d_2 - d_1)} \bigl[\ln u\bigr]_{d_1}^{d_2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{PL}{bE (d_2 - d_1)} \ln\!\left(\frac{d_2}{d_1}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Result (Rectangular Tapering):**
> $$\boxed{\;\delta L = \frac{P L}{b E (d_2 - d_1)} \ln\!\left(\frac{d_2}{d_1}\right)\;}$$
> **Sanity check:** As $d_2 \to d_1 = d$, use L'Hôpital's rule on $\dfrac{\ln(d_2/d_1)}{d_2 - d_1} \to \dfrac{1}{d}$, recovering $\delta L = \dfrac{PL}{bdE} = \dfrac{PL}{AE}$. ✔

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Uniform Bar Under Self-Weight (Elongation Due to Gravity)

**Given:** A uniform bar of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, specific weight $\gamma = \rho g$, Young's modulus $E$, suspended from the top with no external tip load.

**Step 1 — Internal axial force at a section located $x$ above the bottom:**

The portion of bar **below** the section has weight $W_{\text{below}} = \gamma A (L - x)$. This is the tension carried at the section:

$$P(x) = \gamma A (L - x)$$

**Step 2 — Differential elongation of element $dx$:**

$$d(\delta L) = \frac{P(x)\, dx}{A E} = \frac{\gamma A (L - x)\, dx}{A E} = \frac{\gamma (L - x)}{E}\, dx$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from $x = 0$ (bottom) to $x = L$ (top):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta L &= \frac{\gamma}{E} \int_{0}^{L} (L - x)\, dx \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\gamma}{E} \left[Lx - \frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{L} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\gamma}{E} \left(L^{2} - \frac{L^{2}}{2}\right) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\gamma L^{2}}{2E}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Result (Self-Weight, Uniform):**
> $$\boxed{\;\delta L = \frac{\gamma L^{2}}{2E} = \frac{\rho g L^{2}}{2E}\;}$$
> **Physical Insight:** The elongation is independent of the cross-sectional area $A$ — a thick bar and a thin bar of the *same material and length* stretch by the same amount under their own weight, because the additional weight is exactly balanced by the additional area that resists it.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Tapering Bar Under Self-Weight (Advanced)

**Given:** A bar of length $L$ with cross-sectional area varying from $A_1$ (at top, $x=L$) to $A_2$ (at bottom, $x=0$). For a circular taper, $A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} d_1^{2}$ and $A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} d_2^{2}$. Specific weight $\gamma$.

**Step 1 — Area as a function of $x$ (from the top):**

$$A(x) = A_1 + \left(\frac{A_2 - A_1}{L}\right) x$$

**Step 2 — Weight of the portion below section $x$ (i.e., from $0$ to $x$):**

$$P(x) = \gamma \int_{0}^{x} A(\xi)\, d\xi = \gamma \int_{0}^{x}\left[A_1 + \left(\frac{A_2 - A_1}{L}\right)\xi\right] d\xi$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(x) &= \gamma \left[A_1 x + \left(\frac{A_2 - A_1}{L}\right) \frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Set up elongation integral:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta L &= \int_{0}^{L} \frac{P(x)\, dx}{A(x)\, E} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\gamma}{E} \int_{0}^{L} \frac{A_1 x + \left(\frac{A_2 - A_1}{L}\right)\frac{x^{2}}{2}}{A_1 + \left(\frac{A_2 - A_1}{L}\right) x}\, dx
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Use the substitution** $u = A(x) = A_1 + \left(\dfrac{A_2 - A_1}{L}\right) x$, $du = \dfrac{A_2 - A_1}{L}\, dx$:

Note that the numerator is $xu - \frac{x^{2}}{2} \cdot \frac{A_2 - A_1}{L}$ ... *(after algebraic manipulation using $u = A_1 + kx$ where $k = (A_2-A_1)/L$):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta L &= \frac{\gamma}{E} \int_{0}^{L} \left[\frac{x}{1} - \frac{x^{2}/2}{u/k}\right]dx = \frac{\gamma}{E} \cdot \frac{L}{2(A_2 - A_1)} \int_{A_1}^{A_2} \frac{2u - A_1}{u}\, du \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\gamma L}{2E(A_2 - A_1)} \int_{A_1}^{A_2} \left(2 - \frac{A_1}{u}\right) du \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\gamma L}{2E(A_2 - A_1)} \left[2u - A_1 \ln u\right]_{A_1}^{A_2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\gamma L}{2E(A_2 - A_1)} \left[2(A_2 - A_1) - A_1 \ln\!\left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)\right] \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\gamma L}{E} \left[1 - \frac{A_1}{2(A_2 - A_1)} \ln\!\left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Result (Self-Weight, Tapering):**
> $$\boxed{\;\delta L = \frac{\gamma L}{E} \left[1 - \frac{A_1}{2(A_2 - A_1)} \ln\!\left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)\right]\;}$$
> where $A_1$ is the **top** area and $A_2$ is the **bottom** area.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Tapering Bar Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Identify Bar Geometry] --> B{Loading Type?}
    B -->|Constant Axial Load P| C[Define d1, d2 at ends]
    B -->|Self-Weight| D[Define gamma, A x function]
    C --> E{Cross-Section Type?}
    E -->|Circular| F[Apply Circular Formula 4PL / pi E d1 d2]
    E -->|Rectangular| G[Apply Rectangular Formula PL ln d2/d1 / bE d2-d1]
    D --> H{Bar Type?}
    H -->|Uniform A| I[delta = gamma L squared / 2E]
    H -->|Tapering A| J[delta = gamma L / E times 1 - A1 ln A2/A1 over 2 A2-A1]
    F --> K[Compute Numerical Value in mm]
    G --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Check Sanity: Uniform limit case]
    L --> M[END: Report Total Elongation]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`** Define a tapering bar. Derive the expression for the total elongation of a uniformly tapering circular bar of length $L$ subjected to an axial pull $P$, with end diameters $d_1$ and $d_2$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **tapering bar** is a structural member whose cross-section varies linearly along its length. For a circular bar of length $L$ with end diameters $d_1$ and $d_2$, the diameter at distance $x$ from the smaller end is:

$$d(x) = d_1 + \left(\frac{d_2 - d_1}{L}\right) x$$

The area at this section is $A(x) = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d(x)^2$. The total elongation is:

$$\delta L = \int_{0}^{L} \frac{P\, dx}{A(x) E} = \frac{4PL}{\pi E\, d_1 d_2}$$

**(1 mark: definition; 1 mark: setup of integral; 1 mark: final result.)**

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`** A uniform steel rod of length $3 \text{ m}$ and cross-sectional area $400 \text{ mm}^2$ hangs vertically under its own weight. Find its total elongation. Take $\gamma = 78.5 \text{ kN/m}^3$ and $E = 200 \text{ GPa}$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Given: $L = 3 \text{ m}$, $A = 400 \text{ mm}^2 = 400 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$, $\gamma = 78.5 \times 10^{3} \text{ N/m}^3$, $E = 200 \times 10^{9} \text{ N/m}^2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta L &= \frac{\gamma L^{2}}{2E} = \frac{78.5 \times 10^{3} \times (3)^{2}}{2 \times 200 \times 10^{9}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{78.5 \times 10^{3} \times 9}{4 \times 10^{11}} = \frac{706{,}500}{4 \times 10^{11}} \\[4pt]
&= 1.766 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} \approx 1.77 \,\mu\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total elongation ≈ 1.77 μm.** **(1 mark: formula; 1 mark: substitution; 1 mark: numerical answer.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**`[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Module 1]`**

A circular bar of length $1.5 \text{ m}$ tapers uniformly from a diameter of $40 \text{ mm}$ at one end to $80 \text{ mm}$ at the other end. It is subjected to an axial tensile load of $50 \text{ kN}$. The bar is made of aluminum alloy with $E = 70 \text{ GPa}$.

**(a)** Derive the expression for the total elongation of a uniformly tapering circular bar. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the total elongation of the bar and hence determine the longitudinal strain at the smaller end. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Let the diameter at distance $x$ from the smaller end be $d(x)$. By similar triangles:

$$d(x) = d_1 + \left(\frac{d_2 - d_1}{L}\right) x$$

The cross-sectional area is:

$$A(x) = \frac{\pi}{4}[d(x)]^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4}\left[d_1 + \left(\frac{d_2 - d_1}{L}\right) x\right]^{2}$$

The differential elongation of an element $dx$ is:

$$d(\delta L) = \frac{P\, dx}{A(x) E}$$

Substituting $u = d_1 + \left(\dfrac{d_2 - d_1}{L}\right) x$, $du = \dfrac{d_2 - d_1}{L} dx$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta L &= \frac{4P}{\pi E} \int_{0}^{L} \frac{dx}{[d(x)]^{2}} = \frac{4P}{\pi E} \cdot \frac{L}{d_2 - d_1} \int_{d_1}^{d_2} \frac{du}{u^{2}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{4PL}{\pi E (d_2 - d_1)} \left[\frac{1}{d_1} - \frac{1}{d_2}\right] = \frac{4PL}{\pi E\, d_1 d_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

Given: $L = 1.5 \text{ m}$, $d_1 = 0.040 \text{ m}$, $d_2 = 0.080 \text{ m}$, $P = 50 \times 10^{3} \text{ N}$, $E = 70 \times 10^{9} \text{ N/m}^2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta L &= \frac{4 \times 50 \times 10^{3} \times 1.5}{\pi \times 70 \times 10^{9} \times 0.040 \times 0.080} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{300 \times 10^{3}}{\pi \times 70 \times 10^{9} \times 3.2 \times 10^{-3}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{3 \times 10^{5}}{7.034 \times 10^{8}} \approx 4.265 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} \approx 0.4265 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Strain at the smaller end:**

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{P}{A_1 E} = \frac{50 \times 10^{3}}{\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)(0.040)^{2} \times 70 \times 10^{9}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{4}}{8.796 \times 10^{7}} \approx 5.68 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Final Answer:** $\delta L \approx 0.4265 \text{ mm}$; $\varepsilon_1 \approx 5.68 \times 10^{-4}$.

---

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)

**`[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Module 1]`**

A rectangular bar of length $2 \text{ m}$ has a constant width of $60 \text{ mm}$ and a depth that tapers uniformly from $30 \text{ mm}$ at the top to $90 \text{ mm}$ at the bottom. The bar is made of structural steel with $E = 200 \text{ GPa}$ and $\gamma = 78.5 \text{ kN/m}^3$.

**(a)** Derive the expression for the total elongation of a uniformly tapering rectangular bar under an axial pull $P$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the bar is suspended from the top, calculate the total elongation caused by its own weight. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Let the depth at distance $x$ from the smaller end be $d(x)$:

$$d(x) = d_1 + \left(\frac{d_2 - d_1}{L}\right) x$$

Area (constant width $b$):

$$A(x) = b \cdot d(x)$$

Differential elongation under axial pull $P$:

$$d(\delta L) = \frac{P\, dx}{b E\, d(x)}$$

Using $u = d(x)$, $du = \dfrac{d_2 - d_1}{L} dx$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta L &= \frac{P}{bE} \int_{0}^{L} \frac{dx}{d_1 + \left(\frac{d_2 - d_1}{L}\right) x} = \frac{P}{bE} \cdot \frac{L}{d_2 - d_1} \int_{d_1}^{d_2} \frac{du}{u} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{PL}{bE (d_2 - d_1)} \ln\!\left(\frac{d_2}{d_1}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Self-Weight Calculation (7 marks):**

Top area (smaller end at $x = 0$ in formula corresponds to top): $A_1 = b \cdot d_1 = 0.060 \times 0.030 = 1.8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$

Bottom area: $A_2 = b \cdot d_2 = 0.060 \times 0.090 = 5.4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$

Using the tapering self-weight formula with $A_1$ as top:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta L &= \frac{\gamma L}{E} \left[1 - \frac{A_1}{2(A_2 - A_1)} \ln\!\left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)\right] \\[6pt]
&= \frac{78.5 \times 10^{3} \times 2}{200 \times 10^{9}} \left[1 - \frac{1.8 \times 10^{-3}}{2(5.4 - 1.8) \times 10^{-3}} \ln\!\left(\frac{5.4}{1.8}\right)\right] \\[6pt]
&= 7.85 \times 10^{-7} \left[1 - \frac{1.8}{7.2} \ln(3)\right] \\[6pt]
&= 7.85 \times 10^{-7} \left[1 - 0.25 \times 1.0986\right] \\[6pt]
&= 7.85 \times 10^{-7} \left[1 - 0.2747\right] = 7.85 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.7253 \\[6pt]
&\approx 5.69 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} \approx 0.569 \,\mu\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\delta L \approx 0.569 \,\mu\text{m}$ due to self-weight.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder (–2 marks):** Students frequently forget to convert mm to m when substituting diameters into the formula. **Always** convert all dimensions to meters first.
> 2. **Wrong Limit Assignment (–1 mark):** In the rectangular formula $\ln(d_2/d_1)$, the LARGER depth MUST be in the numerator. If you flip them, you get a *negative* elongation — physically impossible.
> 3. **Self-Weight Self-Deception (–3 marks):** Many students wrongly use $\delta L = \frac{WL}{2AE}$ (where $W = \gamma A L$) for self-weight, but the correct formula is $\delta L = \frac{\gamma L^{2}}{2E}$ — note the area $A$ **cancels out**. The half-load assumption is valid only when the *full* weight acts at the centroid (length $L/2$), which is the case here.
> 4. **Forgetting Young's Modulus Units:** $E$ must be in $\text{N/m}^2$ (Pa), not GPa. Multiply by $10^{9}$ before substituting.
> 5. **No "Sanity Check":** After every problem, verify that reducing the tapering case to a uniform bar (by setting $d_1 = d_2$) recovers the standard $\delta L = PL/AE$ formula. KTU examiners give 1 bonus mark for this verification if asked.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Tapering Bars & Self-Weight (Module 1):**
>
> - **🔑 Master Formula (Circular):** $\delta L = \dfrac{4 P L}{\pi E\, d_1 d_2}$ — symmetric in $d_1$ and $d_2$; result independent of which end is "up".
> - **🔑 Master Formula (Rectangular):** $\delta L = \dfrac{P L}{b E (d_2 - d_1)} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{d_2}{d_1}\right)$ — requires $d_2 > d_1$ to avoid negative log.
> - **🔑 Self-Weight (Uniform):** $\delta L = \dfrac{\gamma L^{2}}{2E}$ — **independent of cross-sectional area**.
> - **🔑 Self-Weight (Tapering):** $\delta L = \dfrac{\gamma L}{E}\!\left[1 - \dfrac{A_1}{2(A_2 - A_1)} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)\right]$.
> - **🧠 Sanity Check Rule:** Set $d_1 = d_2$ (circular) or $d_2 \to d_1$ (rectangular, L'Hôpital) — must reduce to $\delta L = PL/AE$.
> - **⚙ Integration Trick:** Always substitute $u = d(x)$ to convert the variable-area integral into a simple power-of-$u$ integral.
> - **⚖ Unit Discipline:** Force in **N**, length in **m**, area in **m²**, $E$ in **N/m²** (or Pa), $\gamma$ in **N/m³**.
> - **🤖 Robotics Context:** Tapering bars model **manipulator links**, **end-effector shafts**, and **gripper jaws** where mass and stiffness must be optimized simultaneously.
> - **🚫 Never Use:** $PL/AE$ directly on a tapering bar — the area is NOT constant, and you will lose full marks.
> - **📐 Free-Body Trick:** In self-weight problems, always measure $x$ from the **free (bottom) end** so that the boundary condition $P(0) = 0$ is automatically satisfied.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Stresses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT525 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS OF ROBOTIC PARTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thermal Stresses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 – Thermal Stresses

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Stress** is defined as the internal stress induced in a body when its free thermal expansion (or contraction) is **fully or partially restrained**, either by external constraints or by differential strain compatibility between adjacent materials within the same structural member.

Mathematically, when a uniform rod of length $L$ experiences a temperature rise $\Delta T$ but is **constrained from expanding**, the induced thermal stress is given by:

$$\sigma_{th} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

where:
- $E$ is the **Young's Modulus** of the material (in Pa or N/mm²)
- $\alpha$ is the **Coefficient of Linear Thermal Expansion** (per °C or per K)
- $\Delta T$ is the **change in temperature** from the reference (stress-free) state

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention (KTU Standard):**
> - Temperature **rise** ($\Delta T > 0$) → member wants to expand → if restrained, induces **compressive** thermal stress (negative).
> - Temperature **fall** ($\Delta T < 0$) → member wants to contract → if restrained, induces **tensile** thermal stress (positive).
> - The thermal **strain** is always $\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \Delta T$ (positive for heating).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a metal rod lying freely on a smooth table. When you heat it, it simply gets longer — no stress develops because nothing stops it from expanding. Now imagine the **same rod is bolted firmly between two rigid walls**. The moment you heat it, the rod "wants" to get longer, but the walls refuse to let it move. The rod pushes outward against the walls, and the walls push back inward on the rod. **This mutual squeezing is what we call thermal stress.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Robotic Analogy:** In a precision robotic arm, the actuator housings are often made of aluminium (high $\alpha$) bolted onto steel support frames (lower $\alpha$). During operation, motor heat causes differential expansion. Because the bolts force both materials to expand by the **same amount**, the aluminium is held back (tension) and the steel is pulled forward (compression). If we ignore this, fasteners loosen, bearings misalign, and positional accuracy of the end-effector drifts — a critical failure mode in robotic structural design.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Range (Engineering Alloys) | SI Unit |
|--------|----------|-----------------------------------|---------|
| $E$ | Young's Modulus | **69 GPa (Al)** to **200 GPa (Steel)** | N/m² (Pa) |
| $\alpha$ | Coefficient of Linear Thermal Expansion | **11 × 10⁻⁶ /°C (Steel)** to **23 × 10⁻⁶ /°C (Al)** | per °C |
| $\Delta T$ | Temperature Change | Robotic service range: **−40 °C to +150 °C** | °C or K |
| $\sigma_{th}$ | Thermal Stress | Up to **~250 MPa** in restrained steel | N/m² (Pa) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A change of **1 °C** equals a change of **1 K** in magnitude (only the reference point shifts, the *difference* $\Delta T$ is identical). Therefore $\alpha$ may be used with either unit interchangeably when $\Delta T$ is a *difference*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Canonical Cases of Thermal Loading

A KTU examiner can ask any of the following configurations. Master all three.

### Case A — Free Expansion (Unconstrained)

The member expands freely. **No stress is induced**, but a measurable **strain** exists.

$$\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T \qquad \sigma_{th} = 0$$

The free elongation is:

$$\delta_{th} = L \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

---

### Case B — Fully Restrained Expansion (Both Ends Fixed)

The member cannot expand at all. The constraint reaction $R$ equals the force that *would have* caused the same deformation as the free thermal expansion.

**Compatibility equation:** Total deformation = 0

$$\delta_{mechanical} + \delta_{thermal} = 0$$

$$\frac{R L}{A E} + L \alpha \Delta T = 0$$

Solving for the reaction:

$$R = -\, A E \alpha \Delta T$$

The induced thermal stress (uniform along the bar):

$$\sigma_{th} = E \alpha \Delta T$$

with sign as per the convention in §1.1.

---

### Case C — Partially Restrained Expansion (Elastic Support / Gap)

The member is allowed to expand partially (e.g., by an amount $\delta_0$ through a gap) before meeting a flexible support of stiffness $k$.

**Compatibility equation:**

$$\delta_{mechanical} + \delta_{thermal} = \delta_0$$

$$-\frac{R L}{A E} + L \alpha \Delta T = \delta_0$$

$$\boxed{R = A E \left(\alpha \Delta T - \frac{\delta_0}{L}\right)}$$

---

### Case D — Composite (Bi-Metallic) Bar with Rigid End Plates

This is the **most important robotic application**: a bar made of two different materials bonded together, heated uniformly, with rigid end plates (or external constraints).

Let the two materials be:
- Material 1: length $L_1$, area $A_1$, modulus $E_1$, expansion coefficient $\alpha_1$
- Material 2: length $L_2$, area $A_2$, modulus $E_2$, expansion coefficient $\alpha_2$

**Strain compatibility** (both layers must deform by the same total amount because the end plates are rigid):

$$\varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_2 = \varepsilon_{common}$$

**Force equilibrium** (the layers push against each other with equal and opposite forces, transmitted through the end plates):

$$\sigma_1 A_1 + \sigma_2 A_2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_1 = -P_2$$

**Constitutive + Thermal equation for each layer:**

$$\varepsilon_i = \frac{\sigma_i}{E_i} + \alpha_i \Delta T$$

Combining:

$$\frac{\sigma_1}{E_1} + \alpha_1 \Delta T = \frac{\sigma_2}{E_2} + \alpha_2 \Delta T$$

Solving for the interface force:

$$\boxed{P_1 = -P_2 = \frac{(\alpha_2 - \alpha_1)\,\Delta T}{\dfrac{1}{E_1 A_1} + \dfrac{1}{E_2 A_2}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This result is the **governing equation for bi-metallic thermal stress analysis** and appears frequently in KTU problems on composite robotic links.

---

### Case E — Composite Bar with External Rigid Restraint

If the composite bar is **additionally** restrained between two rigid walls (total length cannot change), then the overall compatibility becomes:

$$L_1 \alpha_1 \Delta T + L_2 \alpha_2 \Delta T - P \left(\frac{L_1}{A_1 E_1} + \frac{L_2}{A_2 E_2}\right) = 0$$

$$\boxed{P = \frac{(L_1 \alpha_1 + L_2 \alpha_2)\,\Delta T}{\dfrac{L_1}{A_1 E_1} + \dfrac{L_2}{A_2 E_2}}}$$

The stresses in each material:

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{P}{A_1}, \qquad \sigma_2 = \frac{P}{A_2}$$

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Scenario | Key Equation | Stress Magnitude $\vert \sigma_{th} \vert$ |
|---|----------|--------------|------------------------------------------|
| 1 | Single bar, fully fixed | $\sigma = E \alpha \Delta T$ | $E \alpha \Delta T$ |
| 2 | Single bar, free expansion | $\sigma = 0$ | $0$ |
| 3 | Single bar, gap $\delta_0$ then rigid wall | $\sigma = E\!\left(\alpha\Delta T - \dfrac{\delta_0}{L}\right)$ | $E\!\left\vert \alpha\Delta T - \dfrac{\delta_0}{L}\right\vert$ |
| 4 | Bi-material strip, free at ends | $\sigma_1 A_1 = -\sigma_2 A_2$ | $\dfrac{(\alpha_2-\alpha_1)\Delta T}{\dfrac{1}{E_1 A_1}+\dfrac{1}{E_2 A_2}}\cdot\dfrac{1}{A_1}$ |
| 5 | Bi-material bar, fully fixed ends | $P = \dfrac{(L_1\alpha_1+L_2\alpha_2)\Delta T}{\dfrac{L_1}{A_1E_1}+\dfrac{L_2}{A_2E_2}}$ | varies |
| 6 | Thermal + Mechanical combined load | $\sigma_{total} = \sigma_{mech} \pm E\alpha\Delta T$ | superposition |
| 7 | Thick-walled cylinder (plane stress) | $\sigma_\theta = \dfrac{E \alpha \Delta T}{2(1-\nu)}$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units used in KTU numerical problems:** $E$ in N/mm², $\alpha$ in per °C, $\Delta T$ in °C, lengths in mm, areas in mm² → forces in N, stresses in N/mm² = MPa. **Never mix SI (GPa) with mm-based geometry** without converting.

---

## 2.3 Why Thermal Stress Matters in Robotic Structural Analysis

1. **Positional Drift:** Uncompensated thermal expansion of links directly translates to end-effector positional error. For a 1 m aluminium arm with $\Delta T = 50$ °C: $\delta = 1000 \times 23\times10^{-6} \times 50 = 1.15$ mm. In a precision pick-and-place robot this is unacceptable.
2. **Bearing Preload Loss:** Bolted joints in robotic joints lose clamping force when the joint heats, causing backlash.
3. **Sensor Misalignment:** Encoders and resolvers mounted on expanding shafts give erroneous readings.
4. **Fatigue:** Cyclic thermal loading (start-stop duty) induces low-cycle fatigue in welded robotic frames.

---

## 2.4 Sign Convention Summary Used Throughout KTU Valuations

| Quantity | Tension | Compression |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| Mechanical stress $\sigma_m$ | + | − |
| Heating $\Delta T > 0$ | causes **−** stress if fully restrained | causes **+** stress if fully restrained, then recovers |
| Wait — *clarification*: Heating of fully fixed bar gives **compressive** stress. | | |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory hook for KTU exam:** **"Heat pushes, cold pulls."** Heating a fixed bar makes it push outward → compression in the bar. Cooling makes it pull inward → tension in the bar.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Single Bar Fully Fixed Between Two Rigid Walls

**Problem statement:** A uniform steel rod of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, modulus $E$, and coefficient of thermal expansion $\alpha$ is fixed between two immovable walls at temperature $T_0$. The temperature is raised uniformly to $T_1$. Derive an expression for the induced thermal stress.

**Given:** $L$, $A$, $E$, $\alpha$, $T_0$, $T_1$, walls are rigid.

**Find:** $\sigma_{th}$

---

### Step 1 — Write the free thermal elongation

If the right wall were removed, the rod would expand freely. The free elongation is:

$$\delta_{th} = L \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

where $\Delta T = T_1 - T_0$.

This free elongation represents the **thermal strain** experienced by every fibre of the bar:

$$\varepsilon_{th} = \frac{\delta_{th}}{L} = \alpha \Delta T$$

---

### Step 2 — Enforce the kinematic constraint

Because the wall is rigid and remains at its original position, the **net displacement of the right end must be zero**. The total displacement is the sum of:

(a) the free thermal expansion (positive, outward), and
(b) the mechanical contraction caused by the wall's reaction force $R$ pushing the rod back.

$$\delta_{total} = \delta_{th} - \delta_{mech} = 0$$

Therefore:

$$\delta_{mech} = \delta_{th}$$

---

### Step 3 — Express the mechanical deformation in terms of the unknown reaction

The wall exerts a compressive force $R$ on the rod (opposing the outward push). Under this axial load, the rod shortens by:

$$\delta_{mech} = \frac{R L}{A E}$$

---

### Step 4 — Equate and solve for $R$

$$\frac{R L}{A E} = L \alpha \Delta T$$

$$\boxed{R = A E \alpha \Delta T}$$

---

### Step 5 — Compute the induced thermal stress

The stress is force divided by area:

$$\sigma_{th} = \frac{R}{A} = E \alpha \Delta T$$

Since heating causes compression (rod pushes wall outward, wall pushes rod inward):

$$\boxed{\sigma_{th} = -\,E \alpha \Delta T \quad \text{(compressive)}}$$

---

### Step 6 — Sanity check with numbers

Take $E = 200 \times 10^3$ N/mm² (steel), $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C, $\Delta T = 50$ °C:

$$\sigma_{th} = 200 \times 10^3 \times 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 50 = 120 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 120 \text{ MPa}$$

This is below the yield strength of mild steel (~250 MPa), so the rod remains elastic. ✔

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Composite Bi-Metallic Bar with Rigid End Plates (No External Restraint)

**Problem statement:** A bar consists of two segments bonded end-to-end between two rigid end plates. Segment 1 (aluminium) has $L_1 = 200$ mm, $A_1 = 400$ mm², $E_1 = 70$ GPa, $\alpha_1 = 23 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C. Segment 2 (steel) has $L_2 = 300$ mm, $A_2 = 400$ mm², $E_2 = 200$ GPa, $\alpha_2 = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C. The assembly is heated by $\Delta T = 60$ °C. The end plates are *not* attached to any external wall. Find the stresses developed in each material.

**Given:** $L_1, A_1, E_1, \alpha_1, L_2, A_2, E_2, \alpha_2, \Delta T$.

**Find:** $\sigma_1$, $\sigma_2$.

---

### Step 1 — Identify the physical situation

The end plates are **massless and rigid**, and they are not externally restrained. Hence the **external axial force on the assembly is zero**:

$$P_{ext} = 0$$

The two segments exert equal and opposite axial forces on each other through the interface (and on the end plates):

$$P_1 + P_2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_2 = -P_1$$

---

### Step 2 — Write the displacement of each end of the assembly

Let $u$ be the displacement of the left end plate, $v$ the displacement of the right end plate. The total elongation of the assembly is $v - u$.

The elongation of segment 1 is:

$$\Delta_1 = v - u = \frac{P_1 L_1}{A_1 E_1} + L_1 \alpha_1 \Delta T$$

The elongation of segment 2 is:

$$\Delta_2 = v - u = \frac{P_2 L_2}{A_2 E_2} + L_2 \alpha_2 \Delta T$$

---

### Step 3 — Enforce compatibility (both segments span the same rigid end plates)

Both segments must experience the **same total elongation** $\Delta_1 = \Delta_2 = v - u$:

$$\frac{P_1 L_1}{A_1 E_1} + L_1 \alpha_1 \Delta T = \frac{P_2 L_2}{A_2 E_2} + L_2 \alpha_2 \Delta T$$

---

### Step 4 — Substitute the equilibrium condition $P_2 = -P_1$

$$\frac{P_1 L_1}{A_1 E_1} + L_1 \alpha_1 \Delta T = \frac{-P_1 L_2}{A_2 E_2} + L_2 \alpha_2 \Delta T$$

---

### Step 5 — Collect the $P_1$ terms on one side

$$P_1 \left(\frac{L_1}{A_1 E_1} + \frac{L_2}{A_2 E_2}\right) = L_2 \alpha_2 \Delta T - L_1 \alpha_1 \Delta T$$

---

### Step 6 — Factor out $\Delta T$ and solve

$$\boxed{P_1 = \frac{(L_2 \alpha_2 - L_1 \alpha_1)\,\Delta T}{\dfrac{L_1}{A_1 E_1} + \dfrac{L_2}{A_2 E_2}}}$$

---

### Step 7 — Substitute numerical values

Compute the denominator:

$$\frac{L_1}{A_1 E_1} = \frac{200}{400 \times 70 \times 10^3} = \frac{200}{28 \times 10^6} = 7.143 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mm/N}$$

$$\frac{L_2}{A_2 E_2} = \frac{300}{400 \times 200 \times 10^3} = \frac{300}{80 \times 10^6} = 3.750 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mm/N}$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 7.143 \times 10^{-6} + 3.750 \times 10^{-6} = 10.893 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mm/N}$$

Compute the numerator:

$$(L_2 \alpha_2 - L_1 \alpha_1)\,\Delta T = (300 \times 12\times 10^{-6} - 200 \times 23\times 10^{-6}) \times 60$$

$$= (3600 \times 10^{-6} - 4600 \times 10^{-6}) \times 60 = (-1000 \times 10^{-6}) \times 60 = -0.060 \text{ mm}$$

Therefore:

$$P_1 = \frac{-0.060}{10.893 \times 10^{-6}} = -5508 \text{ N}$$

$$P_2 = -P_1 = +5508 \text{ N}$$

---

### Step 8 — Compute stresses

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{P_1}{A_1} = \frac{-5508}{400} = -13.77 \text{ MPa (compressive in aluminium)}$$

$$\sigma_2 = \frac{P_2}{A_2} = \frac{+5508}{400} = +13.77 \text{ MPa (tensile in steel)}$$

---

### Step 9 — Physical interpretation

The aluminium (higher $\alpha$) **wants to expand more** than the steel, but is held back by the rigid end plates. The steel (lower $\alpha$) **wants to expand less** but is dragged forward by the plates. Result: aluminium goes into compression, steel goes into tension. ✔

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — Bar with Initial Gap, Then Rigid Wall

**Problem statement:** A copper bar of length $L = 500$ mm, $A = 200$ mm², $E = 100$ GPa, $\alpha = 17 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C is placed between a fixed wall on the left and a rigid wall on the right, with an initial gap of $\delta_0 = 0.05$ mm. Temperature rises by $\Delta T = 80$ °C. Find the thermal stress.

---

### Step 1 — Free elongation

$$\delta_{th} = L \alpha \Delta T = 500 \times 17 \times 10^{-6} \times 80 = 0.680 \text{ mm}$$

---

### Step 2 — Compare with gap

Since $0.680 \text{ mm} > 0.05 \text{ mm}$, the bar contacts the right wall after closing the gap, and **additional thermal expansion is prevented**. So the wall reaction must compress the bar by:

$$\delta_{mech} = \delta_{th} - \delta_0 = 0.680 - 0.050 = 0.630 \text{ mm}$$

---

### Step 3 — Solve for reaction

$$R = \frac{\delta_{mech} \cdot A \cdot E}{L} = \frac{0.630 \times 200 \times 100 \times 10^3}{500} = 25{,}200 \text{ N}$$

---

### Step 4 — Compute stress

$$\sigma_{th} = \frac{R}{A} = \frac{25{,}200}{200} = 126 \text{ MPa (compressive)}$$

---

### Step 5 — Cross-check with the compact formula from §2.1, Case C

$$\sigma = E\!\left(\alpha\Delta T - \frac{\delta_0}{L}\right) = 100 \times 10^3 \left(17 \times 10^{-6} \times 80 - \frac{0.05}{500}\right)$$

$$= 100 \times 10^3 \left(1.36 \times 10^{-3} - 0.1 \times 10^{-3}\right) = 100 \times 10^3 \times 1.26 \times 10^{-3} = 126 \text{ MPa} \;\; \checkmark$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Identifying the Right Governing Equation

Use this decision matrix during KTU examinations:

| Symptom in Question | Identify | Governing Equation |
|---------------------|----------|---------------------|
| "fixed between two rigid walls" | Case B | $\sigma = E \alpha \Delta T$ |
| "freely expands" / no mention of walls | Case A | $\sigma = 0$, $\delta = L\alpha\Delta T$ |
| "initial gap of $\delta_0$" mentioned | Case C | $\sigma = E\!\left(\alpha\Delta T - \dfrac{\delta_0}{L}\right)$ |
| "two materials bonded" + "end plates" + "no external wall" | Case D (free composite) | Force equilibrium $P_1+P_2=0$ |
| "two materials" + "external rigid walls" | Case E (restrained composite) | Use full constrained equation |
| "also carries axial load $W$" | Superposition | $\sigma_{total} = \dfrac{W}{A} \pm E\alpha\Delta T$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always draw the Free Body Diagram (FBD) first.** Mark all reactions, all temperature changes, and identify whether the structure is statically determinate or indeterminate. Thermal problems are always **statically indeterminate** unless explicitly stated otherwise.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Thermal Stress Analysis Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the logical flow a KTU student should follow when solving any thermal stress problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify the Bar and Boundary Conditions] --> B{Is the bar restrained from expanding?}
    B -- No --> C[Case A: Free Expansion]
    C --> C1[Compute free elongation delta_th = L alpha dT]
    C1 --> C2[Stress sigma_th = 0]
    B -- Yes --> D{What type of restraint?}
    D -- Rigid walls both ends --> E[Case B: Fully Fixed]
    E --> E1[Apply compatibility: total elongation = 0]
    E1 --> E2[Solve for reaction R = A E alpha dT]
```
