```mermaid
    E2 --> E3[Stress sigma = E alpha dT]
    D -- Gap then wall --> F[Case C: Partial Restraint]
    F --> F1[Check if delta_th exceeds gap delta_0]
    F1 -- No --> F2[No contact, sigma = 0]
    F1 -- Yes --> F3[Mechanism closes, partial stress]
    F3 --> F4[sigma = E times alpha dT minus delta_0 over L]
    D -- Bi-material with end plates --> G[Case D or E]
    G --> G1[Write strain compatibility]
    G1 --> G2[Apply force equilibrium]
    G2 --> G3[Solve simultaneous equations]
    G3 --> G4[Compute stresses in each material]
    B -- Yes with axial load W --> H[Superposition Case]
    H --> H1[Compute thermal stress]
    H1 --> H2[Compute mechanical stress W over A]
    H2 --> H3[Total stress sigma_total = sigma_mech plus sigma_thermal]
    E3 --> I[Final: Report stress, deformation, safety factor]
    F4 --> I
    G4 --> I
    H3 --> I
    C2 --> I
    F2 --> I
```

---

## 4.2 Schematic — Bi-Metallic Composite Bar Under Temperature Rise

The following Mermaid block renders the structural arrangement and force flows of the canonical Case D problem.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph leftplate[Left Rigid End Plate]
        LP[LP]
    end
    subgraph seg1[Segment 1: Aluminium]
        AL[Material 1]
    end
    subgraph seg2[Segment 2: Steel]
        ST[Material 2]
    end
    subgraph rightplate[Right Rigid End Plate]
        RP[RP]
    end
    LP --> AL
    AL --> ST
    ST --> RP
    AL -. P1 inward force .-> LP
    LP -. reaction R1 .-> AL
    ST -. P2 outward force .-> RP
    RP -. reaction R2 .-> ST
    AL -- wants to expand by alpha1 dT --> ST
    ST -- wants to expand by alpha2 dT --> AL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the schematic:** The arrows on the dashed lines represent the internal axial forces transmitted at the interfaces. The labels `alpha1 dT` and `alpha2 dT` denote the *desired* free thermal expansions. Because $\alpha_1 > \alpha_2$, the aluminium wants to expand more. The end plates force equal total elongation → aluminium is compressed (P1 inward) and steel is stretched (P2 outward).

---

## 4.3 Schematic — Decision Tree for Choosing the Right Formula

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Given: Material, geometry, temperature change] --> Q2{Are there TWO different materials in series?}
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Single bar. Is it fixed at both ends?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4[Use sigma = E alpha dT]
    Q3 -- No, free --> Q5[sigma equals zero, compute delta only]
    Q3 -- One end fixed, other with gap --> Q6[Compare delta_th with gap delta_0]
    Q6 --> Q7a[delta_th less than delta_0: no contact, sigma equals zero]
    Q6 --> Q7b[delta_th greater than delta_0: use sigma equals E times bracket]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q8{Are external ends restrained to a wall?}
    Q8 -- No --> Q9[Force equilibrium P1 + P2 = 0]
    Q8 -- Yes --> Q10[Force equilibrium P = external reaction, solve compatibility over full bar]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Conceptual) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Define thermal stress. Under what conditions is thermal stress induced in a structural member?

**Model Answer:**

Thermal stress is the internal stress developed in a body when its free thermal deformation (expansion or contraction) is prevented. Mathematically it is given by $\sigma_{th} = E \alpha \Delta T$ for a fully restrained bar.

Thermal stress is induced when **any one** of the following conditions holds:

1. The member is **fully restrained** at its ends by external rigid supports.
2. The member is **partially restrained** by an initial gap or an elastic support.
3. The member is part of a **composite structure** (bi-metallic or layered) where different materials have different thermal expansion coefficients and must strain together due to geometric compatibility.
4. The member is **internally constrained**, e.g., in thick-walled cylinders or shafts, where outer fibres restrain inner fibres.

When a member is completely free to expand or contract (no restraint of any kind), thermal strain is present but **thermal stress is zero**.

---

### Question 2 (Conceptual) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** A brass rod and a steel rod, both of the same length and cross-section, are joined at their ends to form a composite bar. The free ends of the composite bar are kept at a fixed distance apart. Explain, without calculation, which material will be in compression and which in tension when the assembly is heated.

**Model Answer:**

The coefficient of thermal expansion of brass ($\alpha_{brass} \approx 19 \times 10^{-6} /°C$) is **greater** than that of steel ($\alpha_{steel} \approx 12 \times 10^{-6} /°C$).

On heating, the brass *wants* to expand more than the steel. However, because the outer ends are kept at a fixed distance apart (geometric constraint), both materials are forced to undergo the **same total elongation**.

This compatibility demands that the brass is **restrained from expanding fully** → it goes into **compression**. Conversely, the steel is **forced to expand more** than it naturally would → it goes into **tension**.

The two internal axial forces are equal in magnitude (because external force is zero) and opposite in sign, transmitting through the rigid end plates.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]`

**(a)** A steel rod of length 1.2 m, cross-sectional area 600 mm², Young's modulus 200 GPa, and coefficient of thermal expansion $12 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C is fixed between two rigid walls at 30 °C. The temperature is raised to 130 °C. Determine:
   (i) the thermal stress developed in the rod,
   (ii) the magnitude of the reaction at the walls,
   (iii) the free thermal elongation that *would have* occurred if the rod were not restrained. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A composite bar consists of a copper section (length 400 mm, area 500 mm², $E = 100$ GPa, $\alpha = 17 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C) rigidly connected to a steel section (length 600 mm, area 500 mm², $E = 200$ GPa, $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C). The composite bar is placed between two rigid walls with **no initial gap** and heated through 75 °C. Determine the stresses in the copper and the steel sections. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Given:**
- $L = 1.2$ m $= 1200$ mm
- $A = 600$ mm²
- $E = 200 \text{ GPa} = 200 \times 10^3$ N/mm²
- $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C
- $T_0 = 30$ °C, $T_1 = 130$ °C → $\Delta T = 100$ °C
- Both ends fixed → fully restrained

**(i) Thermal stress:**

From the derivation in §3.1:

$$\sigma_{th} = E \alpha \Delta T = (200 \times 10^3)(12 \times 10^{-6})(100)$$

$$\sigma_{th} = 240 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 240 \text{ MPa (compressive)}$$

**(ii) Reaction at the walls:**

$$R = \sigma_{th} \cdot A = 240 \times 600 = 144{,}000 \text{ N} = 144 \text{ kN (compressive)}$$

**(iii) Free thermal elongation:**

$$\delta_{th} = L \alpha \Delta T = 1200 \times 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 = 1.44 \text{ mm}$$

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Given (units converted to mm and N/mm²):**
- Copper: $L_1 = 400$ mm, $A_1 = 500$ mm², $E_1 = 100 \times 10^3$ N/mm², $\alpha_1 = 17 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C
- Steel: $L_2 = 600$ mm, $A_2 = 500$ mm², $E_2 = 200 \times 10^3$ N/mm², $\alpha_2 = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C
- $\Delta T = 75$ °C, no gap, both ends rigidly fixed.

**Step 1 — Apply overall compatibility.** The total elongation of the composite bar must be zero (walls are rigid):

$$\delta_1 + \delta_2 = 0$$

$$\left(\frac{P L_1}{A_1 E_1} + L_1 \alpha_1 \Delta T\right) + \left(\frac{P L_2}{A_2 E_2} + L_2 \alpha_2 \Delta T\right) = 0$$

Note: same $P$ because the sections are in series between the two walls.

**Step 2 — Collect $P$ terms:**

$$P\left(\frac{L_1}{A_1 E_1} + \frac{L_2}{A_2 E_2}\right) = -\,(L_1 \alpha_1 + L_2 \alpha_2)\,\Delta T$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator:**

$$\frac{L_1}{A_1 E_1} = \frac{400}{500 \times 100 \times 10^3} = \frac{400}{50 \times 10^6} = 8.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mm/N}$$

$$\frac{L_2}{A_2 E_2} = \frac{600}{500 \times 200 \times 10^3} = \frac{600}{100 \times 10^6} = 6.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mm/N}$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 14.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mm/N}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator:**

$$(L_1 \alpha_1 + L_2 \alpha_2)\,\Delta T = (400 \times 17 \times 10^{-6} + 600 \times 12 \times 10^{-6}) \times 75$$

$$= (6800 \times 10^{-6} + 7200 \times 10^{-6}) \times 75 = (14{,}000 \times 10^{-6}) \times 75 = 1.05 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $P$:**

$$P = -\frac{1.05}{14.0 \times 10^{-6}} = -75{,}000 \text{ N} = -75 \text{ kN (compressive)}$$

**Step 6 — Compute stresses:**

$$\sigma_1 \text{ (copper)} = \frac{P}{A_1} = \frac{-75{,}000}{500} = -150 \text{ MPa (compressive)}$$

$$\sigma_2 \text{ (steel)} = \frac{P}{A_2} = \frac{-75{,}000}{500} = -150 \text{ MPa (compressive)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Both stresses are equal because $A_1 = A_2$. In the *composite-with-end-plates but no external wall* case, stresses are opposite in sign. Here, with **external rigid walls**, the same compressive force $P$ passes through both sections, so both stresses are equal in sign. This is a critical distinction in KTU valuation.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]`  *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** A brass tube of length 500 mm, outer diameter 40 mm, and inner diameter 30 mm is placed between two rigid supports with an initial gap of 0.1 mm. For brass: $E = 100$ GPa, $\alpha = 18 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C. Find the temperature rise at which the bar will just touch the right support, and the thermal stress when the temperature rises an additional 30 °C above that point. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A circular steel rod of 30 mm diameter is placed inside a brass sleeve of inner diameter 30.05 mm, outer diameter 50 mm, and length 200 mm. Both materials are bonded at the ends by rigid plates. For steel: $E = 200$ GPa, $\alpha_s = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C. For brass: $E = 100$ GPa, $\alpha_b = 18 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C. The assembly is heated through 100 °C. Find the stresses in steel and brass. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Given:**
- $L = 500$ mm
- $A = \frac{\pi}{4}(40^2 - 30^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(1600 - 900) = \frac{\pi}{4}(700) \approx 549.78$ mm²
- $E = 100 \times 10^3$ N/mm²
- $\alpha = 18 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C
- $\delta_0 = 0.1$ mm

**Step 1 — Find the temperature rise $\Delta T_1$ at which the bar just touches the wall.**

At the moment of contact, the free thermal elongation equals the gap:

$$L \alpha \Delta T_1 = \delta_0$$

$$\Delta T_1 = \frac{\delta_0}{L \alpha} = \frac{0.1}{500 \times 18 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.1}{9 \times 10^{-3}} = 11.11 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 2 — Now the temperature is raised an *additional* 30 °C above this point. The bar is fully in contact. Effective $\Delta T$ above the contact point = 30 °C. From this moment onward the wall prevents further expansion.**

The mechanical compression required:

$$\delta_{mech} = L \alpha (\Delta T_{extra}) = 500 \times 18 \times 10^{-6} \times 30 = 0.270 \text{ mm}$$

The reaction:

$$R = \frac{\delta_{mech} \cdot A \cdot E}{L} = \frac{0.270 \times 549.78 \times 100 \times 10^3}{500}$$

$$R = \frac{0.270 \times 549.78 \times 10^5}{500} = \frac{1.4844 \times 10^7}{500} = 29{,}688 \text{ N} \approx 29.69 \text{ kN}$$

The thermal stress:

$$\sigma_{th} = \frac{R}{A} = \frac{29{,}688}{549.78} = 54.0 \text{ MPa (compressive)}$$

**Cross-check with compact formula from §2.1 Case C, using the *total* effective temperature drop in expansion:**

$$\sigma = E\!\left(\alpha \Delta T_{extra} - 0\right) = 100 \times 10^3 \times 18 \times 10^{-6} \times 30 = 54 \text{ MPa} \;\; \checkmark$$

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Given:**
- Steel rod: $D_s = 30$ mm → $A_s = \frac{\pi}{4}(30)^2 = 706.86$ mm², $E_s = 200 \times 10^3$ N/mm², $\alpha_s = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C
- Brass sleeve: inner dia 30.05 mm, outer dia 50 mm, length 200 mm
   - $A_b = \frac{\pi}{4}(50^2 - 30.05^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(2500 - 903.0) = \frac{\pi}{4}(1597) = 1254.4$ mm²
   - $E_b = 100 \times 10^3$ N/mm², $\alpha_b = 18 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C
- Length $L = 200$ mm (same for both)
- $\Delta T = 100$ °C, end plates rigid, **no external wall**.

**Step 1 — Force equilibrium.** No external axial force:

$$P_s + P_b = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_b = -P_s$$

**Step 2 — Compatibility.** Same elongation $\Delta$ for both:

$$\Delta = \frac{P_s L}{A_s E_s} + L \alpha_s \Delta T = \frac{P_b L}{A_b E_b} + L \alpha_b \Delta T$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $P_b = -P_s$:**

$$\frac{P_s L}{A_s E_s} + L \alpha_s \Delta T = \frac{-P_s L}{A_b E_b} + L \alpha_b \Delta T$$

$$P_s \left(\frac{1}{A_s E_s} + \frac{1}{A_b E_b}\right) = (\alpha_b - \alpha_s)\,\Delta T$$

**Step 4 — Compute denominator:**

$$\frac{1}{A_s E_s} = \frac{1}{706.86 \times 200 \times 10^3} = \frac{1}{1.4137 \times 10^8} = 7.074 \times 10^{-9} \text{ per N}$$

$$\frac{1}{A_b E_b} = \frac{1}{1254.4 \times 100 \times 10^3} = \frac{1}{1.2544 \times 10^8} = 7.972 \times 10^{-9} \text{ per N}$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 15.046 \times 10^{-9} \text{ per N}$$

**Step 5 — Compute numerator:**

$$(\alpha_b - \alpha_s)\,\Delta T = (18 - 12)\times 10^{-6} \times 100 = 6 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 6 — Solve:**

$$P_s = \frac{6 \times 10^{-4}}{15.046 \times 10^{-9}} = 39{,}876 \text{ N} \approx 39.88 \text{ kN (tensile)}$$

$$P_b = -P_s = -39{,}876 \text{ N (compressive)}$$

**Step 7 — Stresses:**

$$\sigma_s = \frac{P_s}{A_s} = \frac{39{,}876}{706.86} = 56.4 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$$

$$\sigma_b = \frac{P_b}{A_b} = \frac{-39{,}876}{1254.4} = -31.8 \text{ MPa (compressive)}$$

**Physical interpretation:** Brass has higher $\alpha$ → wants to expand more → is restrained by the steel rod → goes into compression. Steel is dragged along beyond its natural expansion → goes into tension. ✔

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Thermal Stress Problems:**
>
> 1. **Sign error on $\Delta T$:** If the temperature *falls* (e.g., from 30 °C to −10 °C, so $\Delta T = -40$ °C), students often forget the negative sign and report a compressive stress instead of tensile (or vice versa). *Always* substitute $\Delta T$ with its **signed** value.
>
> 2. **Wrong governing equation:** The single-bar formula $\sigma = E \alpha \Delta T$ applies **only** when both ends are rigidly fixed with **no gap**. If the problem says "free ends," the answer is $\sigma = 0$, not zero deformation. If it says "with a gap," you must check whether the bar reaches the wall before applying the formula.
>
> 3. **Unit mix-up:** $E$ in GPa and area in mm² cannot be multiplied directly. Convert $E$ to N/mm² first ($1 \text{ GPa} = 10^3 \text{ N/mm}^2$).
>
> 4. **Forgetting superposition:** If the bar simultaneously carries a mechanical axial load $W$ and a temperature change, the total stress is $\sigma_{total} = W/A \pm E \alpha \Delta T$, **not** just one of the two.
>
> 5. **Composite bar case confusion:** With end plates but *no* external wall → use $P_1 + P_2 = 0$. With end plates *and* external rigid walls → use the total length constraint equation. These give opposite signs on the stresses.
>
> 6. **Area confusion for hollow sections:** Always use $\frac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2)$, **not** $\pi D t$. KTU examiners test this.
>
> 7. **Neglecting the compatibility step:** Every thermal stress problem is **statically indeterminate** unless free expansion is allowed. You *must* write the compatibility equation and solve simultaneously with equilibrium.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Thermal Stresses"**
>
> ✔ **Thermal strain** exists even when stress is zero — always report $\delta_{th} = L\alpha\Delta T$ in free-expansion cases.
>
> ✔ **Thermal stress** exists **only** when deformation is restrained: $\sigma_{th} = E\alpha\Delta T$ for a fully fixed single bar.
>
> ✔ The **three equations** of thermal problems are:
>   - Equilibrium: $\sum F = 0$
>   - Compatibility: total deformation = imposed deformation
>   - Constitutive: $\sigma = E(\varepsilon - \alpha\Delta T)$ or $\varepsilon = \sigma/E + \alpha\Delta T$
>
> ✔ For **composite bars** with rigid end plates but **no external wall**: use $P_1 + P_2 = 0$. The two stresses are **opposite in sign**.
>
> ✔ For **composite bars** with rigid end plates **and** external rigid walls: total elongation of the entire bar must equal zero. Both stresses are typically **same in sign** (both compressive if heating, both tensile if cooling).
>
> ✔ **Memory hooks:**
>   - **"Heat pushes, cold pulls"** — sign of restrained stress
>   - **"Higher alpha wants to grow more, gets compressed"** — composite bar behaviour
>   - **"Gap first, then stress"** — check $\delta_{th}$ vs $\delta_0$ before applying formula
>
> ✔ **Units to memorize:** $1 \text{ GPa} = 10^3 \text{ N/mm}^2$; $1 \text{ MPa} = 1 \text{ N/mm}^2$.
>
> ✔ **Robotic relevance:** Always justify *why* the answer matters for robotic structural design (positional drift, bearing preload, sensor misalignment, fatigue).
>
> ✔ **Sanity-check rule:** A thermal stress higher than the material's yield strength means the problem is unrealistic (or you made a sign error). Cross-check with $\sigma_{th} < \sigma_y$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definition of Beam
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT525 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS OF ROBOTIC PARTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Definition of Beam

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Standard Definition

A **beam** is a slender, prismatic (or non-prismatic) structural member whose **primary function is to resist transverse (lateral) loads applied perpendicular to its longitudinal axis**, thereby developing **internal bending moments, shear forces, and in some cases axial forces** as the dominant internal reactions. Beams are one of the most fundamental load-carrying elements in any mechanical structure, civil framework, or robotic assembly.

From a structural mechanics perspective, the KTU 2024 Scheme defines a beam as:

> **Definition (KTU 2024):** *A one-dimensional structural element characterized by a length dimension ($L$) that is significantly greater than its cross-sectional dimensions ($b$ and $h$), where $L \gg b, h$, and whose deformation under transverse loading is governed principally by flexure (bending).*

The standard slenderness criterion used in robotic arm analysis is:

$$\frac{L}{h} \geq 5 \quad \text{(for flexure to dominate over shear deformation)}$$

Where:
- $L$ = span length of the beam
- $h$ = depth of the cross-section in the plane of bending
- $b$ = width of the cross-section

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In robotic structures, manipulator links, gripper jaws, and tool holders are *all* modelled as beams. The "Definition of Beam" is therefore the foundational concept upon which the entire Module 2 (Bending of Beams) is built.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are holding a **wooden ruler** horizontally with both hands and placing a small weight in the middle. What do you observe?

1. The ruler **bends downward** in the middle.
2. The **top surface stretches** (tension).
3. The **bottom surface compresses** (compression).
4. Somewhere **in between**, there is a layer that **does not change length** — this is the **neutral axis**.

That wooden ruler, when subjected to a transverse force, behaves exactly like a **beam in flexure**. In a robotic arm, every link that connects two joints and carries a payload behaves in precisely this manner. The "skeleton" of any robot — from a 6-DOF industrial manipulator to a 3-DOF pick-and-place arm — is essentially a **chain of interconnected beams**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Robot Example:** The links of a **KUKA KR 16 R2010** industrial robot are essentially cantilever beams fixed at the joint bases, carrying motor housings and end-effectors as transverse loads.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities in Beam Analysis

The following quantities are the **fundamental response parameters** of any beam under loading:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bending Moment | $M$ | N·m | Internal moment resisting curvature |
| Shear Force | $V$ | N | Internal force resisting transverse sliding |
| Deflection | $\delta$ or $y$ | m | Vertical displacement of the neutral axis |
| Slope | $\theta$ or $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ | rad | Rotation of the cross-section |
| Section Modulus | $Z$ | m³ | Geometric bending strength property |

> [!NOTE]
> A **robot link** designed with a **higher Section Modulus ($Z$)** will deflect less under the same payload, which directly improves the **positional accuracy** of the end-effector. This is why robotic link design starts with beam theory.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometric & Kinematic Classification of Beams

A beam in robotic structural analysis is classified along **three independent axes**:

### A. Classification by Support Conditions
- **Simply Supported Beam** — Pinned at one end, roller at the other. Statically determinate. **Reaction count = 3**.
- **Cantilever Beam** — Fixed at one end, free at the other. **Reaction count = 3**.
- **Fixed Beam (Propped / Built-in)** — Fixed at both ends. Statically indeterminate to **degree 1**.
- **Overhanging Beam** — Simply supported with one or both ends projecting beyond supports.
- **Continuous Beam** — Spans over more than two supports. Indeterminate to **degree $\geq 1$**.

### B. Classification by Geometry
- **Straight Beam** — Constant cross-section, axis is a straight line.
- **Curved Beam** — Initial axis is curved (e.g., hooks, robot gripper fingers with arcs).
- **Tapered Beam** — Cross-section varies along length (e.g., optimized robotic links).

### C. Classification by Loading Direction
- **Transversely Loaded Beam** — Loads perpendicular to axis (most common in robotics).
- **Axially Loaded Beam** — Loads along the axis (e.g., telescoping robot arms under compression).
- **Eccentrically Loaded Beam** — Combined axial + bending (e.g., a column supporting a bracket).

## 2.2 Underlying Assumptions of Beam Theory

The **Euler-Bernoulli Beam Theory**, the workhorse of KTU Module 2, rests on four key assumptions:

1. **Plane sections remain plane** after bending (no warping of cross-section).
2. **Shear deformation is neglected** — the cross-section rotates as a rigid disk.
3. **The material is homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** (Hooke's Law applies: $\sigma = E\epsilon$).
4. **Deflections are small** compared to the span ($y_{\max} \ll L$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **short, deep robotic links** (e.g., in micro-assembly robots) where $L/h < 5$, **Timoshenko Beam Theory** is required because shear deformation can no longer be neglected. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to recognize when Euler-Bernoulli breaks down.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates **all high-yield equations** for Module 2 — Definition of Beam and its governing relations.

| # | Equation | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ | Flexure formula (bending equation) | $M$: N·m, $\sigma$: Pa |
| 2 | $EI \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$ | Governing differential equation of beam | $EI$: N·m² |
| 3 | $EI \dfrac{d^3y}{dx^3} = V(x)$ | Shear force from deflection | $V$: N |
| 4 | $EI \dfrac{d^4y}{dx^4} = w(x)$ | Load distribution from deflection | $w$: N/m |
| 5 | $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$ | Equilibrium: shear-load relation | — |
| 6 | $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V(x)$ | Equilibrium: moment-shear relation | — |
| 7 | $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M_{\max}}{Z}$ | Maximum bending stress | Pa |
| 8 | $Z = \dfrac{I}{c}$ | Section modulus (for symmetric sections) | m³ |
| 9 | $R = \dfrac{EI}{M}$ | Radius of curvature | m |
| 10 | $\theta = \dfrac{dy}{dx}$ | Slope of the elastic curve | rad |

> **Where:**
> - $E$ = Young's Modulus (Pa) — **For structural steel: $E = 200$ GPa**; **For aluminium 6061-T6: $E = 69$ GPa**; **For titanium Ti-6Al-4V: $E = 114$ GPa**.
> - $I$ = Second Moment of Area (m⁴) — For a rectangle: $I = bh^3 / 12$.
> - $c$ = distance from neutral axis to the extreme fibre (m).
> - $w(x)$ = distributed load intensity (N/m).

> [!NOTE]
> **Caution on Markdown Syntax:** In exam scripts, students sometimes write $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value. In the cheat sheet above, mathematical bars have been encoded as $c$ (extreme fibre distance) and $Z$ (section modulus) to avoid table-rendering conflicts.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Robotic Structural Analysis

The definition of a beam is not merely an academic construct — it has **direct engineering utility** in:

- **Link Design of Manipulators:** Each link of a robot arm is modelled as a beam to compute its deflection under payload, which directly affects end-effector positioning accuracy.
- **End-Effector Design:** Gripper jaws, tool holders, and suction-cup mounts are cantilever beams.
- **Mobile Robot Chassis:** The frame of an AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) is analysed as a beam-grid.
- **Compliance and Stiffness Analysis:** Robotic tasks like deburring and polishing require controlled beam-like flexibility.
- **Vibration Analysis:** Beams in robotic structures have natural frequencies; understanding beam dynamics prevents resonance during high-speed motion.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Euler-Bernoulli Bending Equation

This is the **single most important derivation** in Module 2. It links bending moment $M$, curvature $R$, and bending stress $\sigma$ in a beam.

### Step 1 — Geometric Strain Relation

Consider a small element of a beam of length $dx$ on the neutral axis. When bent, it subtends an angle $d\theta$ at the centre of curvature $R$. From pure geometry:

$$dx = R \, d\theta$$

For a fibre at distance $y$ from the neutral axis, the original length is also $dx$, but its new length on the bent arc is $(R - y) \, d\theta$ (above the neutral axis, $y$ is conventionally taken positive upward; below, negative). The **longitudinal strain** is:

$$\epsilon = \frac{(R - y)d\theta - R \, d\theta}{R \, d\theta} = \frac{-y \, d\theta}{R \, d\theta}$$

$$\boxed{\epsilon = -\frac{y}{R}}$$

**Explanation:** A fibre above the neutral axis ($y > 0$) contracts (negative strain → compression in classical convention), while a fibre below ($y < 0$) extends. The neutral axis itself ($y = 0$) experiences zero strain — confirming the existence of an unstretched neutral layer.

### Step 2 — Apply Hooke's Law

For a linearly elastic material, $\sigma = E\epsilon$. Therefore:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \left(-\frac{y}{R}\right) = -\frac{E y}{R}$$

### Step 3 — Moment Equilibrium of the Cross-Section

The internal bending moment $M$ is the integral of the bending stress $\sigma$ times its lever arm $y$, taken over the entire cross-sectional area $A$:

$$M = \int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA$$

Substituting $\sigma = -\dfrac{E y}{R}$:

$$M = \int_A \left(-\frac{E y}{R}\right) y \, dA = -\frac{E}{R} \int_A y^2 \, dA$$

By definition, the **Second Moment of Area** (Area Moment of Inertia) is:

$$I = \int_A y^2 \, dA$$

Hence:

$$M = -\frac{E I}{R}$$

Taking magnitudes (sign convention handled separately):

$$\boxed{\frac{M}{I} = \frac{E}{R}}$$

### Step 4 — Combine the Three Relations

From Step 1: $\epsilon = -y / R$, which gives $1/R = -\epsilon / y$.
From Step 2: $\sigma = E \epsilon$, which gives $\epsilon = \sigma / E$.
From Step 3: $M/I = E/R$.

Combining, we get the **Master Flexure Formula**:

$$\boxed{\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}}$$

This is the **defining relation of a beam** under pure bending.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Governing Differential Equation of the Beam

Starting from the geometric relation between curvature and the deflected shape $y(x)$:

$$\frac{1}{R} \approx \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} \quad \text{(for small deflections)}$$

From the flexure formula: $M/I = E/R$. Equating:

$$\frac{M}{EI} = \frac{1}{R} = \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2}$$

$$\boxed{EI \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x)}$$

**Explanation:** This second-order ODE is the **governing equation of beam bending**. Integrate it twice, and you get the elastic curve $y(x)$. The two constants of integration are found from **boundary conditions** that depend on the beam's supports.

## 3.3 Derivation of Shear-Load and Moment-Shear Equilibrium

For a free-body element of length $dx$ with distributed load $w(x)$ acting downward, force equilibrium in the vertical direction gives:

$$V - (V + dV) - w(x) \, dx = 0$$

$$\boxed{\frac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)}$$

Moment equilibrium about the right face of the element (taking $M$ positive when sagging):

$$M + V \, dx - (M + dM) - w(x) \, dx \cdot \frac{dx}{2} = 0$$

Neglecting the second-order term $w \, dx^2 / 2$:

$$\boxed{\frac{dM}{dx} = V(x)}$$

Combining both relations:

$$\boxed{\frac{d^2 M}{dx^2} = -w(x)}$$

Differentiating the beam ODE $EI \, y'' = M$ twice with respect to $x$:

$$\boxed{EI \frac{d^4 y}{dx^4} = w(x)}$$

**Explanation:** This fourth-order ODE is the **complete governing equation** of an Euler-Bernoulli beam. Four boundary conditions are required — exactly two at each end of the beam.

## 3.4 Numerical Evaluation: Maximum Bending Stress in a Robotic Link

Consider a **robotic arm link** of rectangular cross-section, with the following design parameters:

- Span (cantilever from motor housing): $L = 0.5$ m
- Cross-section: $b = 0.04$ m, $h = 0.02$ m
- Material: **Aluminium 6061-T6**, $E = 69$ GPa
- End payload: $P = 100$ N acting vertically at the tip

**Step 1 — Maximum Bending Moment (at fixed end of cantilever):**

$$M_{\max} = P \cdot L = 100 \times 0.5 = 50 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 2 — Second Moment of Area for a Rectangle:**

$$I = \frac{b h^3}{12} = \frac{0.04 \times (0.02)^3}{12} = \frac{0.04 \times 8 \times 10^{-6}}{12}$$

$$I = \frac{3.2 \times 10^{-7}}{12} = 2.667 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 3 — Section Modulus:**

$$Z = \frac{I}{c} = \frac{2.667 \times 10^{-8}}{0.01} = 2.667 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Bending Stress:**

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M_{\max}}{Z} = \frac{50}{2.667 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\sigma_{\max} = 18.75 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Deflection at the Tip (Standard cantilever result):**

$$\delta_{\text{tip}} = \frac{P L^3}{3 E I} = \frac{100 \times (0.5)^3}{3 \times 69 \times 10^9 \times 2.667 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$\delta_{\text{tip}} = \frac{12.5}{5.52} = 2.26 \text{ mm}$$

**Interpretation:** The tip deflects by **2.26 mm** under a 100 N load. For high-precision robotic tasks, this deflection must be compensated either by **stiffer cross-section design** (larger $h$) or by **higher-modulus materials** (steel or carbon fibre).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of Beam Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robotic Link Input Geometry] --> B[Define Beam Type]
    B --> B1[Simply Supported]
    B --> B2[Cantilever]
    B --> B3[Fixed-Fixed]
    B --> B4[Overhanging]

    B1 --> C[Apply Loading w of x]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C

    C --> D[Compute Reactions]
    D --> E[Shear Force V of x]
    E --> F[Bending Moment M of x]
    F --> G[Bending Stress Sigma]
    G --> H[Deflection y of x]

    G --> I[Check Sigma less than Sigma yield]
    H --> J[Check delta less than delta allowable]

    I --> K[Design Adequate]
    J --> K
    I --> L[Redesign Section]
    J --> L
    L --> B

    style A fill:E6F2FF,stroke:003366,color:000000
    style B fill:FFF2CC,stroke:996600,color:000000
    style C fill:FFE6E6,stroke:990000,color:000000
    style D fill:E6FFE6,stroke:006600,color:000000
    style E fill:F2E6FF,stroke:330099,color:000000
    style F fill:F2E6FF,stroke:330099,color:000000
    style G fill:FFF2CC,stroke:996600,color:000000
    style H fill:FFF2CC,stroke:996600,color:000000
    style I fill:E6F2FF,stroke:003366,color:000000
    style J fill:E6F2FF,stroke:003366,color:000000
    style K fill:99FF99,stroke:006600,color:000000
    style L fill:FFB3B3,stroke:990000,color:000000
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The left branch classifies the beam by support condition.
- The middle branch applies equilibrium and kinematic relations to extract internal forces.
- The right branch performs design checks against **yield stress** and **allowable deflection** — these are the **two governing KTU design criteria** for Module 2.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Stress Distribution Across a Beam Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Top Fibre y equals c] --> S1[Compression Sigma negative]
    L2[Upper Half y positive] --> S1
    L3[Neutral Axis y equals 0] --> S2[Zero Stress]
    L4[Lower Half y negative] --> S3[Tension Sigma positive]
    L5[Bottom Fibre y equals minus c] --> S3

    S1 --> F[Linear Distribution]
    S2 --> F
    S3 --> F
    F --> M[Resultant M equals integral of sigma y dA]

    style L1 fill:FFE6E6,stroke:990000,color:000000
    style L2 fill:FFE6E6,stroke:990000,color:000000
    style L3 fill:FFFFFF,stroke:000000,color:000000
    style L4 fill:E6FFE6,stroke:006600,color:000000
    style L5 fill:E6FFE6,stroke:006600,color:000000
    style F fill:FFF2CC,stroke:996600,color:000000
    style M fill:E6F2FF,stroke:003366,color:000000
```

**Reading the Diagram:** This block diagram illustrates the **linear variation of bending stress** across the depth of a beam cross-section, transitioning from maximum compression at the top fibre, through zero at the neutral axis, to maximum tension at the bottom fibre. The resultant moment $M$ is the integral of this stress distribution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for robots:** In a robotic arm under a downward payload, the **upper fibres of the link are compressed** and the **lower fibres are tensed**. The neutral axis — the boundary between these two zones — has zero stress. This is why **hollow tubes** (which remove material near the neutral axis) are commonly used in robot links: they retain bending stiffness while drastically reducing mass.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
Define a beam. State and briefly explain the four essential assumptions of the Euler-Bernoulli beam theory.

**Model Answer:**

A **beam** is a structural member whose length is significantly greater than its lateral dimensions ($L \gg b, h$) and which primarily resists loads applied transversely to its longitudinal axis through **bending action**, developing internal **bending moments ($M$) and shear forces ($V$)** as the dominant internal reactions.

The four assumptions of Euler-Bernoulli beam theory are:

1. **Plane sections remain plane** after bending — there is no warping of cross-sections perpendicular to the neutral axis.
2. **Shear deformation is neglected** — the cross-section rotates as a rigid body, so cross-sections remain normal to the deformed neutral axis.
3. **The material is homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic**, obeying Hooke's law $\sigma = E\epsilon$ throughout.
4. **Deflections are small** compared to the span, so the slope $\theta = dy/dx \ll 1$, permitting the approximation $1/R \approx d^2y/dx^2$.

> **Marking Note:** Award 1 mark for the definition, and 0.5 marks × 4 = 2 marks for the four assumptions (1.5 if any are imprecise).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Sessional QP]**
A robotic manipulator link is subjected to a pure bending moment of 250 N·m. The cross-section is rectangular with width $b = 50$ mm and depth $h = 80$ mm. Compute the maximum bending stress in the link.

**Model Answer:**

Given: $M = 250$ N·m, $b = 0.050$ m, $h = 0.080$ m.

**Step 1 — Section Modulus for a Rectangle:**

$$Z = \frac{b h^2}{6} = \frac{0.050 \times (0.080)^2}{6} = \frac{0.050 \times 0.0064}{6} = \frac{3.2 \times 10^{-4}}{6}$$

$$Z = 5.333 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Bending Stress:**

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{250}{5.333 \times 10^{-5}} = 4.6875 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{\max} \approx 4.69 \text{ MPa}}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice:** Students answer **ONE** of the two alternatives.

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the **Euler-Bernoulli flexure formula** $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ starting from the geometric strain relation of a bent beam element. State clearly the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A simply supported steel beam of span $L = 4$ m carries a uniformly distributed load $w = 20$ kN/m over its entire length. The cross-section is rectangular with $b = 100$ mm and $h = 200$ mm. Determine:
- (i) The maximum bending moment in the beam.
- (ii) The maximum bending stress.
- (iii) The maximum deflection of the beam.

Take $E = 200$ GPa for steel. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

Refer to the **Step-by-Step Derivation in Section 3.1** of these notes. The derivation proceeds in four stages:

1. **Geometric strain:** $\epsilon = -y/R$
2. **Hooke's Law:** $\sigma = E\epsilon = -Ey/R$
3. **Moment equilibrium:** $M = -\dfrac{E}{R}\displaystyle\int_A y^2 \, dA = -\dfrac{EI}{R}$
4. **Combined result:** $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$

The four assumptions are: (1) plane sections remain plane, (2) shear deformation is neglected, (3) linear elastic homogeneous isotropic material, (4) small deflections.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Part (i) — Maximum Bending Moment:**

For a simply supported beam with UDL, the maximum bending moment occurs at mid-span:

$$M_{\max} = \frac{w L^2}{8} = \frac{20 \times 10^3 \times (4)^2}{8} = \frac{320 \times 10^3}{8} = 40 \times 10^3 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{M_{\max} = 40 \text{ kN·m}}$$

**Part (ii) — Maximum Bending Stress:**

Second moment of area: $I = \dfrac{b h^3}{12} = \dfrac{0.100 \times (0.200)^3}{12} = \dfrac{0.100 \times 0.008}{12} = 6.667 \times 10^{-5}$ m⁴

Section modulus: $Z = \dfrac{I}{c} = \dfrac{6.667 \times 10^{-5}}{0.100} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4}$ m³

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M_{\max}}{Z} = \frac{40 \times 10^3}{6.667 \times 10^{-4}} = 60 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{\max} = 60 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Part (iii) — Maximum Deflection:**

For a simply supported beam with UDL, the standard result is:

$$\delta_{\max} = \frac{5 w L^4}{384 EI} = \frac{5 \times 20 \times 10^3 \times (4)^4}{384 \times 200 \times 10^9 \times 6.667 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$\delta_{\max} = \frac{5 \times 20 \times 10^3 \times 256}{384 \times 200 \times 10^9 \times 6.667 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$\delta_{\max} = \frac{25.6 \times 10^6}{5.12 \times 10^9} = 5.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\delta_{\max} = 5.0 \text{ mm}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Alternative]**

**(a)** Classify beams based on **support conditions** and **geometry**. Draw neat sketches of each type and state the degree of static indeterminacy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cantilever robotic link of length $L = 1.2$ m has a circular hollow cross-section with outer diameter $D = 60$ mm and inner diameter $d = 50$ mm. A vertical point load $P = 250$ N acts at the free end. The material is aluminium with $E = 70$ GPa. Compute:
- (i) The second moment of area of the cross-section.
- (ii) The maximum bending stress.
- (iii) The tip deflection. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Classification by Support Conditions:**

1. **Simply Supported Beam** — Hinged at one end, roller at the other. Statically determinate. **Indeterminacy = 0**.
2. **Cantilever Beam** — Fixed at one end, free at the other. Statically determinate. **Indeterminacy = 0**.
3. **Fixed Beam** — Fixed at both ends. Statically indeterminate. **Indeterminacy = 1**.
4. **Overhanging Beam** — Simply supported with ends projecting beyond supports. Statically determinate. **Indeterminacy = 0**.
5. **Continuous Beam** — Spans over more than two supports. **Indeterminacy = (number of interior supports)**.

**Classification by Geometry:**

1. **Straight Beam** — Constant cross-section, straight axis.
2. **Curved Beam** — Initial axis is curved (e.g., crane hooks, robot grippers).
3. **Tapered Beam** — Cross-section varies along length (e.g., optimized robot arms).

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Part (i) — Second Moment of Area (Hollow Circular Section):**

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64} \left( D^4 - d^4 \right) = \frac{\pi}{64} \left( (0.060)^4 - (0.050)^4 \right)$$

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64} \left( 1.296 \times 10^{-5} - 6.25 \times 10^{-6} \right) = \frac{\pi}{64} \times 6.71 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$I = \frac{\pi \times 6.71 \times 10^{-6}}{64} = 3.293 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4$$

**Part (ii) — Maximum Bending Stress:**

Maximum moment (at fixed end of cantilever): $M_{\max} = P \cdot L = 250 \times 1.2 = 300$ N·m

Section modulus: $Z = \dfrac{I}{c} = \dfrac{3.293 \times 10^{-7}}{0.030} = 1.098 \times 10^{-5}$ m³

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M_{\max}}{Z} = \frac{300}{1.098 \times 10^{-5}} = 27.32 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{\max} \approx 27.3 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Part (iii) — Tip Deflection:**

For a cantilever with end point load:

$$\delta_{\text{tip}} = \frac{P L^3}{3 E I} = \frac{250 \times (1.2)^3}{3 \times 70 \times 10^9 \times 3.293 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$\delta_{\text{tip}} = \frac{250 \times 1.728}{69.15} = \frac{432}{69.15} = 6.247 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\delta_{\text{tip}} \approx 6.25 \text{ mm}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for This Topic:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** Students frequently drop the negative sign in the moment-curvature relation $M = -EI/R$ vs $M = EI/R$. Always check which sign convention you are following (sagging-positive or hogging-positive).
> 2. **Confusing $I$ and $Z$:** The **Second Moment of Area** ($I$) has units of m⁴, while the **Section Modulus** ($Z$) has units of m³. Mixing them up leads to numerical errors in stress calculations.
> 3. **Forgetting $c$:** For a rectangular section, $c = h/2$, NOT $h$. The extreme fibre distance is half the depth.
> 4. **Using Wrong Deflection Formula:** The standard formulas $\delta = PL^3 / 3EI$ (cantilever) and $\delta = 5wL^4 / 384EI$ (simply supported with UDL) are *not* interchangeable.
> 5. **Mixing Units:** Always convert mm to m before substituting into any formula. Using $I$ in mm⁴ with $M$ in N·m gives an answer that is off by a factor of $10^{12}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for "Definition of Beam":**

- **Definition:** A beam is a slender structural element ($L \gg b, h$) that resists transverse loads primarily through bending.
- **Slenderness Criterion:** $L/h \geq 5$ for flexure to dominate over shear.
- **Euler-Bernoulli Master Formula:** $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$
- **Governing Differential Equation:** $EI \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$ — requires **2** boundary conditions.
- **Equilibrium Relations:** $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w$ and $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$
- **Full Fourth-Order ODE:** $EI \dfrac{d^4y}{dx^4} = w(x)$ — requires **4** boundary conditions.
- **Bending Stress:** $\sigma_{\max} = M_{\max} / Z$, where $Z = I/c$.
- **Key Material Constants:** $E_{\text{steel}} = 200$ GPa, $E_{\text{aluminium}} = 70$ GPa, $E_{\text{titanium}} = 114$ GPa.
- **Standard $I$ Values:** Rectangle: $bh^3/12$; Hollow Rectangle: $(BH^3 - bh^3)/12$; Solid Circle: $\pi d^4/64$; Hollow Circle: $\pi(D^4 - d^4)/64$.
- **Cantilever with End Load:** $\delta = PL^3 / 3EI$, $\theta = PL^2 / 2EI$, $M_{\max} = PL$ (at fixed end).
- **Simply Supported with UDL:** $\delta_{\max} = 5wL^4 / 384EI$, $M_{\max} = wL^2 / 8$ (at mid-span).
- **Classification by Support:** Simply supported, cantilever, fixed, overhanging, continuous.
- **Classification by Geometry:** Straight, curved, tapered.
- **Assumptions of Euler-Bernoulli:** Plane sections remain plane; shear deformation neglected; linear elastic material; small deflections.
- **Neutral Axis:** The fibre of zero strain and zero stress, located at the centroid of the cross-section for homogeneous beams.
- **Timoshenko Beam Theory:** Required when $L/h < 5$ (shear deformation becomes significant).
- **Robotic Link Parallel:** Every robot link, gripper jaw, and end-effector is fundamentally a beam — bending analysis gives its stiffness.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Beams, Loadings and Supports
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT525 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS OF ROBOTIC PARTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Beams, Loadings and Supports

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Beam (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Beam (KTU Definition):** A *beam* is a slender, straight structural member of uniform cross-section, subjected to transverse loads acting perpendicular (or inclined) to its longitudinal axis, and whose primary mode of internal resistance is **bending**. In the context of **PERAT525 — Structural Analysis of Robotic Parts**, every link, arm segment, gripper jaw, and connecting rod of a robot is idealized as a beam for force, moment, and deflection analysis.

In structural mechanics, beams are idealized as **one-dimensional (1-D) elements** lying along an axis (typically the X-axis) and loaded in the transverse (Y or Z) direction. The cross-section may be rectangular, circular, I-section, T-section, or any arbitrary profile — what matters is that the **length is much greater than the depth** (L >> h).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Axiom for Robotic Links:** Every robotic manipulator link — from a **SCARA** arm to a 6-DOF industrial robot — is modeled as a beam for static and dynamic structural analysis. The accuracy of stress, deflection, and vibration prediction depends entirely on the correct classification of beam type, loading, and support conditions.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — A Beam is Like a "Robotic Arm on a Bench"

Imagine a wooden plank resting on two bricks on a workshop bench, with a heavy toolbox placed at its center.

- The **plank** is the beam.
- The **two bricks** are the supports.
- The **toolbox** is the load.
- The **sagging of the plank** is the bending.

Now translate this to a **6-axis industrial robot arm**:

- The **shoulder joint (bolted to the floor)** acts as a **Fixed Support**.
- The **elbow joint (motor housing)** acts as a **Pinned Support** (allows rotation, resists translation).
- The **wrist** at the end of the forearm is the **Free End** — making the forearm a perfect **Cantilever Beam**.
- The **payload held by the gripper** is a **Point Load** at the free end.
- The **weight of the link itself** is a **Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL)** acting along its length.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Robotic Application:** In a **KUKA KR 16 R1610** robot, the forearm link (length ≈ 1610 mm) carrying a 16 kg payload behaves as a cantilever beam fixed at the elbow joint. The bending moment at the elbow is **M = P × L** (where P is the payload weight plus self-weight), which dictates the **motor torque selection** at the elbow joint. Misclassifying this as a simply-supported beam would lead to dangerously underestimated moment and motor sizing errors.

---

### 1.3 Why Classification Matters in PERAT525

For structural analysis of robotic parts, you must answer **three fundamental questions** before drawing a single free-body diagram:

| # | Question | Why it Matters |
|---|----------|----------------|
| 1 | **What TYPE of beam is the robotic link?** | Determines **degree of static indeterminacy (DSI)** and choice of analysis method (statics vs. methods of superposition / Castigliano / stiffness matrix). |
| 2 | **What TYPE of load is acting?** | Determines the **shape of the Shear Force Diagram (SFD)** and **Bending Moment Diagram (BMD)**. |
| 3 | **What TYPE of support does the link have?** | Determines the **number and nature of reaction components** (vertical, horizontal, moment). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Exam Tip:** Examiners often give a single robotic-link diagram and ask students to: *"Identify the type of beam, classify the supports, and list the loadings."* Marks are awarded for **correct naming + correct reasoning**. Memorize the classification tree before attempting any numerical problem.

---

### 1.4 Preliminary Visual Overview of the Classification Tree

The entire Module-2 syllabus is essentially a classification problem with three independent sub-trees:

1. **Beam Classification** → based on support geometry.
2. **Loading Classification** → based on spatial distribution of force.
3. **Support Classification** → based on the number and nature of reaction components.

We will dissect each of these three sub-trees rigorously in **SECTION 2** below.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Beams (Based on Support Conditions)

A beam is classified by the **arrangement of its supports along its length** and the **degrees of freedom (DOF) constrained** at those supports.

> [!NOTE]
> **Degree of Static Indeterminacy (DSI):** $DSI = R - (3 + C)$ where R is the number of unknown reactions, C is the number of internal hinges (condition equations). Statically determinate beams have DSI = 0.

#### (A) Simply Supported Beam (SSB)

- **Configuration:** Pinned support at one end + Roller support at the other.
- **Reactions:** Vertical reaction at pin ($R_A$), vertical reaction at roller ($R_B$); **no fixed-end moment**.
- **DSI = 0** (statically determinate).
- **Engineering Use:** Truss chords, bridge girders with pin-roller bearings, floor joists in light robotic platforms.

#### (B) Cantilever Beam

- **Configuration:** One end is **fully fixed (built-in)**, the other end is **completely free**.
- **Reactions:** Vertical reaction ($R_A$), horizontal reaction ($H_A$), and a **fixed-end moment ($M_A$)** at the fixed end.
- **DSI = 0** (statically determinate).
- **Engineering Use:** Robotic forearm, gripper fingers, diving boards, balcony slabs, aircraft wing cantilevers.

#### (C) Overhanging Beam

- **Configuration:** Pinned + Roller supports placed **not at the ends** — one portion of the beam extends (overhangs) beyond a support.
- **Reactions:** Two vertical reactions only.
- **DSI = 0** (statically determinate).
- **Engineering Use:** Crane jibs, ship hulls, vehicle chassis frames.

#### (D) Fixed Beam (Built-in / Encastered Beam)

- **Configuration:** **Both ends fully fixed** — neither rotation nor translation allowed at either end.
- **Reactions:** 4 vertical reactions + 2 fixed-end moments = **6 unknowns**.
- **DSI = 3** (statically indeterminate to the 3rd degree).
- **Engineering Use:** Robot base pedestal beams, high-precision machine tool beds, pressure vessel end-caps.

#### (E) Continuous Beam

- **Configuration:** **More than two supports** along a single continuous span (e.g., a beam over 3, 4, or more roller/pin supports).
- **Reactions:** 3 reactions per support (pin) or 1 (roller); total reactions = n.
- **DSI = (R − 3)** — typically highly indeterminate.
- **Engineering Use:** Multi-span bridge decks, long conveyor support structures in assembly lines.

#### (F) Propped Cantilever Beam

- **Configuration:** A cantilever beam with **an additional simple support (prop)** added somewhere along its span.
- **Reactions:** 3 vertical reactions + 1 fixed-end moment = **4 unknowns**.
- **DSI = 1** (statically indeterminate to the 1st degree).
- **Engineering Use:** Temporary construction shores, robotic manipulator arm with a mid-span cable support.

---

### 2.2 Classification of Loadings

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** The "shape" of the **Shear Force Diagram (SFD)** and **Bending Moment Diagram (BMD)** is a direct fingerprint of the load type. Examiners often give a load diagram and ask *"Identify the loading type and sketch the SFD/BMD."*

| Loading Type | Symbol / Description | SFD Signature | BMD Signature |
|--------------|----------------------|---------------|---------------|
| **Point / Concentrated Load (P)** | Single force at a discrete point | Vertical jump (discontinuity) of magnitude P at the load point | Slope changes abruptly at the load point (kink) |
| **Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL)** | $w$ (kN/m) constant over a length | Linear (straight-line) variation | Parabolic (2nd-degree) curve |
| **Uniformly Varying Load (UVL)** | Linearly varying from 0 to $w$ over a length | Parabolic variation | Cubic (3rd-degree) curve |
| **Concentrated Couple / Moment (M₀)** | Pure moment at a point | **No effect on SFD** | Vertical jump (discontinuity) of magnitude $M_0$ |
| **Self-weight** | Effectively a UDL = $\rho g A$ (N/m) | Linear | Parabolic |

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalence Rule:** Any distributed load (UDL, UVL, triangular) can be replaced by a **statically equivalent point load** equal to the **area under the load-intensity diagram** acting at the **centroid of that area**. This single trick simplifies 80% of KTU exam problems.

---

### 2.3 Classification of Supports

A support is classified by **how many and what kind of reaction components** it provides. A support that prevents translation in a direction provides a reaction force in that direction. A support that prevents rotation provides a reaction moment.

| Support Type | Diagram Symbol | Reactions Provided | DOF Restricted | DOF Allowed |
|--------------|----------------|---------------------|----------------|-------------|
| **Roller Support** | Circle on a horizontal line / inclined plane | 1 reaction (perpendicular to rolling surface) | Translation in 1 direction | Translation along surface, all rotations |
| **Pinned / Hinged Support** | Triangle (with or without pin) | 2 reactions ($R_x$, $R_y$) — both perpendicular and parallel to beam axis | Translation in 2 directions (X and Y) | Rotation about the pin |
| **Fixed Support (Encastered / Built-in)** | Hatched wall with the beam embedded | 3 reactions ($R_x$, $R_y$, $M$) | Translation in 2 directions + rotation | None |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "RRP = 1, 2, 3":** **R**oller → 1 reaction, **P**in → 2 reactions, **F**ixed → 3 reactions. This is the single most-tested line in Module 2. Memorize it.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All reactions below assume a horizontal beam of length **L** loaded with the indicated load. Use these as **standard results** to verify your derivation in the exam.

**Table 2.4.1 — Reaction Formulas for Standard Beam-Loading Combinations**

| Beam Type | Loading | Reaction at Left ($R_A$) | Reaction at Right ($R_B$) | Fixed-End Moment ($M_A$) |
|-----------|---------|--------------------------|---------------------------|--------------------------|
| Simply Supported (SSB) | Point load **P** at midspan | $P/2$ | $P/2$ | Not applicable (no fixed end) |
| Simply Supported (SSB) | Point load **P** at distance **a** from left, **b** from right ($L = a + b$) | $P \cdot b / L$ | $P \cdot a / L$ | Not applicable |
| Simply Supported (SSB) | UDL **w** over full span | $wL/2$ | $wL/2$ | Not applicable |
| Cantilever | Point load **P** at free end | $P$ (at fixed end) | — | $P \cdot L$ |
| Cantilever | UDL **w** over full span | $wL$ (at fixed end) | — | $wL^2 / 2$ |
| Propped Cantilever | UDL **w** over full span (prop at free end) | $3wL/8$ (fixed end) | $5wL/8$ (roller at free end) | $wL^2 / 8$ |
| Fixed Beam | UDL **w** over full span | $wL/2$ | $wL/2$ | $wL^2 / 12$ at each end |

**Table 2.4.2 — Standard Equilibrium Equations (Applicable to ALL Statically Determinate Beams)**

| Equation | Meaning | Use |
|----------|---------|-----|
| $\sum F_x = 0$ | Sum of horizontal forces = 0 | Solve horizontal reactions (relevant when beam is inclined or has axial load) |
| $\sum F_y = 0$ | Sum of vertical forces = 0 | Solve vertical reactions (most common) |
| $\sum M_O = 0$ | Sum of moments about ANY point O = 0 | Solve moments and one of the forces simultaneously |

**Table 2.4.3 — Standard Sign Conventions for SFD and BMD**

| Quantity | Positive Convention | Visual Cue |
|----------|---------------------|------------|
| **Shear Force (V)** | Left portion pushed **UP**, right portion pushed **DOWN** (clockwise couple of the two halves) | Draw the SFD on the **positive Y-axis** above the beam axis |
| **Bending Moment (M)** | **Sagging** (concave up) — bottom fibers in tension, top fibers in compression | Draw the BMD on the **tension side** of the beam (i.e., below the beam axis for a simply supported beam) |
| **Concentrated Moment** | **Counter-clockwise = positive** | Standard right-hand rule |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** These three tables cover approximately **70% of the reactions, SFD, and BMD problems** asked in PERAT525 Module 2. Master the formulas in Table 2.4.1 cold, and you can solve any determinate beam problem in under 5 minutes.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of Beam-Loading-Support Classification

- **Robotics:** Selecting motor torque at each joint (joint torque = bending moment at the corresponding cross-section).
- **Mechanical Design:** Sizing cross-sections to keep **maximum bending stress** $\sigma = Mc/I$ below the **allowable stress** $[\sigma]$.
- **Deflection Control:** Ensuring **maximum deflection** $\delta_{max} \leq \delta_{allowable}$ (typically $L/300$ for robotic arms, $L/360$ for floor beams).
- **Vibration Analysis:** Natural frequency $\omega_n \propto \sqrt{EI/(\rho A L^4)}$ depends on **beam end conditions** — a cantilever has a different $\omega_n$ than a fixed-fixed beam of the same length and material.
- **Failure Diagnosis:** Knowing the beam type tells the designer **where cracks initiate** (e.g., at the fixed end of a cantilever, at the midspan bottom of an SSB).

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In this section, we derive the **reaction components** for the **three most-frequently tested beam-loading combinations** in PERAT525. The derivations are deliberately exhaustive — every algebraic step is shown — so that the student can replicate the same logic on any variant in the exam.

---

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Simply Supported Beam with an Eccentric Point Load

**Problem Setup:** A simply supported beam of length $L$ carries a point load $P$ at a distance $a$ from the **left** support A and a distance $b$ from the **right** support B, where $L = a + b$.

```
        P (downward)
         |
         v
    A •———————|———————————• B
    ^         a          ^   ^
    |Ra                  |Rb |
    |<——————— L ————————>|   |
    |<——— a ——>|<— b —->|
```

**Step 1 — Identify Unknowns:**
- Vertical reaction at A: $R_A$ (upward, positive)
- Vertical reaction at B: $R_B$ (upward, positive)
- **Total unknowns = 2** (no horizontal forces, no fixed-end moment)

**Step 2 — Identify Available Equations:**
- $\sum F_y = 0$ → 1 equation
- $\sum M_A = 0$ → 1 equation
- **Total independent equations = 2**

**Step 3 — Since unknowns = equations = 2, the beam is STATICALLY DETERMINATE (DSI = 0).**

**Step 4 — Apply $\sum M_A = 0$** (take clockwise moments as positive, anticlockwise as negative; or vice versa — be consistent):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Taking moments about A, with clockwise positive:} \quad & R_B \cdot L \;-\; P \cdot a \;=\; 0 \\
\text{Move } P \cdot a \text{ to the other side:} \quad & R_B \cdot L \;=\; P \cdot a \\
\text{Divide both sides by } L: \quad & R_B \;=\; \frac{P \cdot a}{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply $\sum F_y = 0$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_A + R_B - P &= 0 \\
R_A &= P - R_B \\
R_A &= P - \frac{P \cdot a}{L} \\
R_A &= P \left(1 - \frac{a}{L}\right) \\
R_A &= P \left(\frac{L - a}{L}\right) \\
\text{Since } L - a = b: \quad R_A &= \frac{P \cdot b}{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Result (boxed for exam use):**

$$
\boxed{R_A = \frac{P \cdot b}{L} \quad ; \quad R_B = \frac{P \cdot a}{L}}
$$

**Step 7 — Sanity Check (Verification):**
- Total vertical force: $R_A + R_B = \frac{Pb}{L} + \frac{Pa}{L} = \frac{P(a+b)}{L} = \frac{P \cdot L}{L} = P$ ✓ (equals applied load)
- Special case: if $a = b = L/2$ (load at midspan), then $R_A = R_B = P/2$ ✓ (matches the formula in Table 2.4.1).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Cantilever Beam with a Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL)

**Problem Setup:** A cantilever beam of length $L$ is fixed at the left end A and free at the right end B. A UDL of intensity $w$ (kN/m) acts over the **entire** length, directed downward.

```
     w w w w w w w w
     | | | | | | | |
     v v v v v v v v
   ||——————————————————• B (free end)
   ||A
   || (fixed support)
```

**Step 1 — Replace the UDL by a Statically Equivalent Point Load:**
The total equivalent point load = area under the load diagram:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{eq} &= \text{area of rectangle} = w \times L \\
\text{Acts at the centroid of the rectangle} &= \frac{L}{2} \text{ from A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify Unknowns at the Fixed End A:**
- Vertical reaction: $R_A$ (upward)
- Fixed-end moment: $M_A$ (will determine its sense shortly)
- **Total unknowns = 2** (no horizontal load in this problem)

**Step 3 — Apply $\sum F_y = 0$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_A - W_{eq} &= 0 \\
R_A - wL &= 0 \\
R_A &= wL \quad (\text{upward, makes physical sense — supports the entire weight})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply $\sum M_A = 0$** (taking counter-clockwise as positive):

The equivalent point load $W_{eq} = wL$ acts downward at $L/2$ from A, producing a **clockwise** moment about A (because it pulls the right side down). To balance this, the fixed-end moment $M_A$ must act **counter-clockwise**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_A - W_{eq} \cdot \frac{L}{2} &= 0 \\
M_A - (wL) \cdot \frac{L}{2} &= 0 \\
M_A - \frac{wL^2}{2} &= 0 \\
M_A &= \frac{wL^2}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Result (boxed for exam use):**

$$
\boxed{R_A = wL \quad ; \quad M_A = \frac{wL^2}{2}}
$$

**Step 6 — Sanity Check:**
- Units: $R_A$ has units of $(kN/m) \times (m) = kN$ ✓
- Units: $M_A$ has units of $(kN/m) \times (m^2) = kN \cdot m$ ✓
- Cross-check with Table 2.4.1: matches the cantilever-UDL row exactly ✓

**Step 7 — Physical Interpretation for a Robotic Link:**
- The **fixed-end moment $M_A = wL^2/2$** is precisely the **bending moment that the shoulder/elbow motor must resist** to keep the forearm from rotating downward under self-weight.
- If the forearm is made of aluminum ($E = 70$ GPa) with rectangular cross-section $b \times h$, the **maximum bending stress** is:

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_A \cdot (h/2)}{I} = \frac{(wL^2/2) \cdot (h/2)}{bh^3/12} = \frac{3wL^2}{bh^2}
$$

This stress must be $\leq [\sigma_{allowable}]$ for safe design.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Simply Supported Beam with a UDL over Part of the Span

**Problem Setup:** A simply supported beam of length $L$ carries a UDL of intensity $w$ (kN/m) over a length $c$ starting at distance $d$ from the left support A.

```
         w w w w w w w
         | | | | | | |
         v v v v v v v
   A •———————|————————|————• B
   ^Ra       d         d+c  ^Rb
   |<——————————— L ———————————>|
```

**Step 1 — Equivalent Point Load:**

$$
W_{eq} = w \cdot c
$$

The centroid of the UDL block is at a distance from A:

$$
\bar{x} = d + \frac{c}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply $\sum M_A = 0$** to find $R_B$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_B \cdot L - W_{eq} \cdot \bar{x} &= 0 \\
R_B \cdot L &= (wc) \cdot \left(d + \frac{c}{2}\right) \\
R_B &= \frac{wc \left(d + \frac{c}{2}\right)}{L} = \frac{wc(2d + c)}{2L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply $\sum F_y = 0$** to find $R_A$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_A + R_B - W_{eq} &= 0 \\
R_A &= wc - R_B \\
R_A &= wc - \frac{wc(2d+c)}{2L} \\
R_A &= wc \left[1 - \frac{2d+c}{2L}\right] \\
R_A &= wc \cdot \frac{2L - 2d - c}{2L} \\
R_A &= \frac{wc(2L - 2d - c)}{2L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Result (boxed for exam use):**

$$
\boxed{R_A = \frac{wc(2L - 2d - c)}{2L} \quad ; \quad R_B = \frac{wc(2d + c)}{2L}}
$$

**Step 5 — Sanity Check (Special Case):**
- If $d = 0$ and $c = L$ (UDL over **entire** span), then:
  - $R_A = \frac{wL(2L - 0 - L)}{2L} = \frac{wL \cdot L}{2L} = \frac{wL}{2}$ ✓
  - $R_B = \frac{wL(0 + L)}{2L} = \frac{wL}{2}$ ✓
- Matches the SSB-UDL row in Table 2.4.1 exactly.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Identification Guide for the Exam

> [!TIP]
> Print this matrix on a single sheet and use it as your **last-15-minute revision aid** before the KTU exam.

| Clue in the Problem | Beam Type | Loading | DSI | First Equation to Write |
|---------------------|-----------|---------|-----|--------------------------|
| "One end built into a wall" | Cantilever | Point load at free end | 0 | $\sum M_{fixed} = 0$ |
| "Both ends fixed, uniform load" | Fixed beam | UDL | 3 | Use standard table (Table 2.4.1) + compatibility |
| "Pinned at A, roller at B, point load at midspan" | SSB | Concentrated P | 0 | $\sum M_A = 0$ |
| "Cantilever with a prop at the free end" | Propped cantilever | UDL or P | 1 | Use standard table; do NOT use statics alone |
| "Two roller supports + overhang on one side" | Overhanging | UDL or P | 0 | $\sum M_{pin} = 0$ |
| "Beam over 3 or more supports" | Continuous | UDL or P | $\geq 1$ | Use Three-Moment Theorem or stiffness method |
| "Couple M₀ applied at midspan" | Any | Pure moment | Depends on beam | The SFD is **zero**; BMD has a jump of $M_0$ |

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Support Reaction Mapping

This diagram shows the **functional mapping** from a physical support to its reaction components, which is essential for the robotic-link modeling workflow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startNode2([PHYSICAL SUPPORT IN ROBOT]) --> sup1{SUPPORT TYPE}
    sup1 -- ROLLER --> react1[REACTION R<br>One force only<br>Perpendicular to<br>rolling surface]
    sup1 -- PIN --> react2[REACTION SET R<br>Two forces Rx and Ry<br>Resists translation<br>Allows rotation]
    sup1 -- FIXED --> react3[REACTION SET R<br>Two forces Rx and Ry<br>Plus moment M<br>Resists translation<br>Resists rotation]
    react1 --> eqCheck1[APPLY EQUILIBRIUM<br>Sum Fx 0<br>Sum Fy 0<br>Sum M 0]
    react2 --> eqCheck2[APPLY EQUILIBRIUM<br>Sum Fx 0<br>Sum Fy 0<br>Sum M 0]
    react3 --> eqCheck3[APPLY EQUILIBRIUM<br>Sum Fx 0<br>Sum Fy 0<br>Sum M 0]
    eqCheck1 --> dsi1[DSI 0<br>STATICALLY<br>DETERMINATE]
    eqCheck2 --> dsi2[DSI 0<br>STATICALLY<br>DETERMINATE]
    eqCheck3 --> dsi3[DSI MAY BE<br>0 OR HIGHER<br>CHECK UNKNOWNS]
    dsi1 --> finishNode2([SOLVE FOR<br>REACTIONS])
    dsi2 --> finishNode2
    dsi3 --> finishNode2
```

### 4.3 Load-Type to SFD/BMD Signature Map

This block diagram illustrates the **cause-effect chain** from a load type to its visible signature on the SFD and BMD.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    loadStart([LOAD TYPE]) --> loadQ{WHAT IS THE<br>SPATIAL DISTRIBUTION?}
    loadQ -- DISCRETE POINT --> loadA[CONCENTRATED LOAD P]
    loadQ -- CONSTANT PER UNIT LENGTH --> loadB[UNIFORMLY DISTRIBUTED LOAD w]
    loadQ -- LINEARLY VARYING --> loadC[UNIFORMLY VARYING LOAD]
    loadQ -- PURE ROTATION --> loadD[CONCENTRATED MOMENT M0]
    loadA --> sfdA[SFD SHAPE<br>VERTICAL JUMP<br>OF MAGNITUDE P]
    loadA --> bmdA[BMD SHAPE<br>SLOPE KINK<br>LINEAR SEGMENT]
    loadB --> sfdB[SFD SHAPE<br>LINEAR SLOPE OF w]
    loadB --> bmdB[BMD SHAPE<br>PARABOLIC CURVE]
    loadC --> sfdC[SFD SHAPE<br>PARABOLIC CURVE]
    loadC --> bmdC[BMD SHAPE<br>CUBIC CURVE]
    loadD --> sfdD[SFD SHAPE<br>NO EFFECT<br>REMAINS UNCHANGED]
    loadD --> bmdD[BMD SHAPE<br>VERTICAL JUMP<br>OF MAGNITUDE M0]
    sfdA --> finNode([SKETCH SFD AND BMD])

    sfdB --> finNode
    bmdB --> finNode
    sfdC --> finNode
    bmdC --> finNode
    sfdD --> finNode
    bmdD --> finNode
```

### 4.4 Robotic Link — Canonical Mapping Table (Text Block Fallback)

| Robotic Component | Idealized As | Support(s) | Typical Loading |
|-------------------|--------------|------------|-----------------|
| Industrial robot base (bolted to floor) | Fixed end of a cantilever column | Fixed support (anchored) | Self-weight, reaction from arm |
| Shoulder-driven link (upper arm) | Cantilever beam | Fixed at shoulder joint | Payload (point) at elbow, self-weight (UDL) |
| Elbow-driven link (forearm) | Cantilever beam | Fixed at elbow joint | Gripper payload (point) at wrist, self-weight (UDL) |
| Gripper finger | Cantilever beam | Fixed at palm mounting | Gripping force (point) at the contact patch |
| Mobile robot chassis member | SSB or overhanging | Pin-roller depending on suspension | Distributed cargo load |
| Conveyor support beam in assembly line | Continuous beam over 3+ pedestals | Multiple rollers/pins | UDL from conveyed parts |
| Spacecraft robotic arm (e.g., Canadarm) | Propped cantilever during docking | Fixed at one end, cable prop elsewhere | Launch load + payload |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Assessment Structure (as per PERAT525 syllabus):**
> - **Part A:** 2-mark and 3-mark short-answer questions (answer in 1–2 lines / one diagram).
> - **Part B:** 14-mark descriptive/numerical questions with **internal choice** between two full questions. Each 14-mark question typically splits into (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.
