---

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`
**"Differentiate between a simply supported beam and a cantilever beam. State one engineering example of each from a robotic system."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Simply Supported Beam | Cantilever Beam |
|---------|----------------------|-----------------|
| End conditions | Pin at one end, roller at the other | One end fully fixed, other end free |
| Number of reactions | 2 (both vertical) | 3 (vertical + horizontal + moment) |
| Degree of static indeterminacy | 0 (statically determinate) | 0 (statically determinate) |
| Maximum bending moment location | At midspan (under central point load) | At the fixed end |
| Robotic example | Bridge-girder of an overhead gantry robot's horizontal track | The forearm link of a 6-DOF industrial robot arm |

---

#### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`
**"A cantilever beam of length 2 m carries a point load of 5 kN at its free end. Calculate the vertical reaction and the fixing moment at the fixed end."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Given: $L = 2$ m, $P = 5$ kN at free end.

Applying $\sum F_y = 0$ at the fixed end A:

$$
R_A - P = 0 \implies R_A = P = 5 \text{ kN (upward)}
$$

Applying $\sum M_A = 0$ (taking counter-clockwise as positive):

$$
M_A - P \cdot L = 0 \implies M_A = P \cdot L = 5 \times 2 = 10 \text{ kN·m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** $R_A = 5$ kN (upward); $M_A = 10$ kN·m (counter-clockwise, i.e., the wall must supply a moment to prevent the beam from rotating downward).

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For each 14-mark question, **two full alternative questions (Q-A and Q-B)** are provided. In the KTU exam, the student answers **only ONE** of the two choices.

---

#### **Question B-A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**"A simply supported beam of span 6 m carries a uniformly distributed load (UDL) of 10 kN/m over the entire span and a point load of 20 kN at a distance of 2 m from the left support. Determine: (a) The reactions at both supports. (b) The maximum bending moment and the point where it occurs."** (14 Marks — 7 + 7)

##### Part (a) — Reactions at the Supports (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Span $L = 6$ m
- UDL $w = 10$ kN/m over the full span
- Point load $P = 20$ kN at $a = 2$ m from left support A

**Step 1 — Replace the UDL by an equivalent point load:**

$$
W_{eq} = w \times L = 10 \times 6 = 60 \text{ kN}
$$

This acts at the **centroid of the UDL**, i.e., at the midspan:

$$
\bar{x}_{UDL} = \frac{L}{2} = 3 \text{ m from A}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply $\sum M_A = 0$** to find $R_B$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_B \cdot L &= W_{eq} \cdot 3 + P \cdot a \\
R_B \cdot 6 &= 60 \cdot 3 + 20 \cdot 2 \\
R_B \cdot 6 &= 180 + 40 = 220 \\
R_B &= \frac{220}{6} = 36.667 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply $\sum F_y = 0$** to find $R_A$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_A + R_B &= W_{eq} + P \\
R_A &= (60 + 20) - 36.667 \\
R_A &= 80 - 36.667 = 43.333 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer for Part (a):** $R_A = 43.33$ kN (upward), $R_B = 36.67$ kN (upward). **Sanity check:** $R_A + R_B = 80$ kN = Total applied load ✓.

##### Part (b) — Maximum Bending Moment (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Locate the point of zero shear (this is where $M_{max}$ occurs for a beam with no overhangs).**

Compute shear just to the **left of the point load** (i.e., at $x = 2^{-}$ m from A):

$$
V(x = 2^{-}) = R_A - w \cdot x = 43.333 - 10 \times 2 = 23.333 \text{ kN (positive)}
$$

Compute shear just to the **right of the point load** (i.e., at $x = 2^{+}$ m from A):

$$
V(x = 2^{+}) = 23.333 - 20 = 3.333 \text{ kN (positive)}
$$

Compute shear at the right support B (at $x = 6$ m):

$$
V(x = 6) = R_A - w \cdot 6 - P = 43.333 - 60 - 20 = -36.667 \text{ kN}
$$

(Indeed, $-V(B) = R_B = 36.667$ kN ✓)

**Step 2 — Find the exact location $x_0$ where shear changes sign from positive to negative:**

Set the shear expression for the region $2 \leq x \leq 6$ equal to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(x) &= R_A - w \cdot x - P = 0 \\
43.333 - 10x - 20 &= 0 \\
23.333 &= 10x \\
x_0 &= 2.333 \text{ m from A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the bending moment at $x_0 = 2.333$ m:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M(x_0) &= R_A \cdot x_0 - w \cdot \frac{x_0^2}{2} - P \cdot (x_0 - 2) \\
M(2.333) &= 43.333 \times 2.333 - 10 \times \frac{(2.333)^2}{2} - 20 \times (2.333 - 2) \\
&= 101.111 - 27.222 - 6.667 \\
&= 67.222 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer for Part (b):** $M_{max} \approx 67.22$ kN·m occurring at $x_0 = 2.333$ m from support A. **Cross-check with midspan:** $M(3) = 43.33 \times 3 - 10 \times 9/2 - 20 \times 1 = 130 - 45 - 20 = 65$ kN·m. Since $67.22 > 65$, the max is correctly at $x_0 = 2.333$ m ✓.

---

#### **Question B-B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]` *(INTERNAL CHOICE ALTERNATIVE)*

**"A cantilever beam of length 4 m carries a uniformly distributed load (UDL) of 8 kN/m over the left half of the span and a point load of 12 kN at the free end. Determine: (a) The vertical reaction and the fixing moment at the fixed support. (b) The maximum bending moment and its location. Also state how this cantilever represents the idealized behavior of a robotic forearm link carrying a payload."** (14 Marks — 7 + 7)

##### Part (a) — Reactions at the Fixed End (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Length $L = 4$ m
- UDL $w = 8$ kN/m over the **left half** (from $x = 0$ to $x = 2$ m)
- Point load $P = 12$ kN at the **free end** ($x = 4$ m)

**Step 1 — Replace the UDL by an equivalent point load:**

$$
W_{eq} = w \times 2 = 8 \times 2 = 16 \text{ kN}
$$

This acts at the centroid of the partial UDL = $1$ m from the fixed end.

**Step 2 — Apply $\sum F_y = 0$** at the fixed end A:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_A - W_{eq} - P &= 0 \\
R_A - 16 - 12 &= 0 \\
R_A &= 28 \text{ kN (upward)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply $\sum M_A = 0$** (counter-clockwise positive):

The UDL equivalent and the point load both create **clockwise** moments about A (they try to rotate the beam downward). The fixed-end moment $M_A$ must be **counter-clockwise** to balance them.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_A - W_{eq} \cdot (1) - P \cdot (4) &= 0 \\
M_A - 16 \times 1 - 12 \times 4 &= 0 \\
M_A - 16 - 48 &= 0 \\
M_A &= 64 \text{ kN·m (counter-clockwise)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer for Part (a):** $R_A = 28$ kN upward; $M_A = 64$ kN·m counter-clockwise. **Units check:** $R_A$ in kN ✓; $M_A$ in kN·m ✓.

##### Part (b) — Maximum Bending Moment & Robotic Interpretation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Compute bending moment at the fixed end (this is the candidate for $M_{max}$):**

$$
M(0) = M_A = 64 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute bending moment at $x = 2$ m** (end of UDL):

$$
\begin{aligned}
M(2) &= M_A - R_A \cdot (2) + W_{eq} \cdot (2 - 1) \\
&= 64 - 28 \times 2 + 16 \times 1 \\
&= 64 - 56 + 16 = 24 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Using the sign convention: $M(x) = M_A - R_A \cdot x + \int_0^x w(\xi)(x - \xi) d\xi$ where $w(\xi) = 8$ for $0 \leq \xi \leq 2$ and $w(\xi) = 0$ for $2 < \xi \leq 4$.)

**Step 3 — Compute bending moment at the free end $x = 4$ m:**

$$
M(4) = 0 \text{ (free end has no moment by definition)}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify $M_{max}$:** Since $M(0) = 64 > M(2) = 24 > M(4) = 0$, the maximum bending moment occurs at the **fixed end**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer for Part (b):** $M_{max} = 64$ kN·m at the **fixed end** (i.e., at the wall/joint A). The BMD is **linear** from $x=0$ to $x=2$ (slope = $-R_A = -28$ kN, but parabolic correction from UDL reduces it to $M(2) = 24$), and then **linear** from $x=2$ to $x=4$ (slope = $-(R_A - W_{eq}) = -12$ kN, ending at $M(4) = 24 - 12 \times 2 = 0$ ✓).

**Step 5 — Robotic Interpretation (Final 2–3 lines of answer):**
- A **6-DOF industrial robot's forearm link** (e.g., the forearm of a KUKA KR 5) is **ideally modeled as a cantilever beam fixed at the elbow joint** and free at the wrist.
- The **payload held by the gripper** (12 kN in our problem) acts as a **point load at the free end**.
- The **self-weight of the link** (8 kN/m) acts as a **UDL along the half-span closer to the elbow** (where the structural housing is heaviest).
- The **maximum bending moment (64 kN·m at the elbow)** directly dictates the **minimum torque rating of the elbow joint motor** — selecting a motor with rated torque below 64 kN·m would cause the robot to fail structurally under the design payload.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP FIVE WAYS KTU STUDENTS LOSE MARKS IN MODULE 2 (Based on Examiner Feedback Patterns):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Roller and Pin Supports:** A roller provides **only 1 reaction** (perpendicular to the rolling surface), not 2. Drawing $R_x$ AND $R_y$ at a roller support is a **−2 mark** mistake.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Sign Conventions on Moments:** Taking moments with mixed clockwise/anticlockwise conventions without declaring them upfront leads to sign errors cascading into wrong reactions. **Always state your convention first.**
>
> 3. **Miscounting Reactions on Fixed-Fixed Beams:** A fixed beam has **6 reactions** (3 at each end: $R_x, R_y, M$), giving DSI = 3. Trying to solve it with 3 statics equations alone is impossible — students must use **compatibility methods** (e.g., area-moment, conjugate beam, Castigliano, or stiffness matrix).
>
> 4. **Mistaking UDL for a Point Load:** A UDL of $w$ over a length $L$ is **NOT** a point load of $wL$ at the end — it is a point load of $wL$ at the **centroid** ($L/2$ from one end). Placing the equivalent load at the wrong location is a **−3 mark** error.
>
> 5. **Ignoring Self-Weight:** Many robotic link problems imply a self-weight UDL ("a steel link of mass per unit length ...") that students overlook. Always **re-read** the problem statement for "self-weight", "dead load", or specific mass/unit-length data.
>
> **BOTTOM LINE:** Draw a clear free-body diagram, label all forces and moments with assumed positive directions, and apply equilibrium **methodically** (one equation at a time). Do not skip steps to "save time" — full working = full marks in KTU 2024 scheme.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Rapid Revision Checklist (Print This Page Before the Exam)**

**A. Beam Types (Six Total) — Memorize the Configuration + DSI**
- ☐ Simply Supported Beam → Pin + Roller → 2 reactions → DSI = 0
- ☐ Cantilever Beam → Fixed + Free → 3 reactions (2 forces + 1 moment) → DSI = 0
- ☐ Overhanging Beam → Supports not at ends → 2 reactions → DSI = 0
- ☐ Fixed Beam → Fixed + Fixed → 6 reactions → DSI = 3
- ☐ Continuous Beam → 3+ supports → R reactions → DSI = R − 3
- ☐ Propped Cantilever → Fixed + 1 prop → 4 reactions → DSI = 1

**B. Support Types (Three Total) — Memorize "RRP = 1, 2, 3"**
- ☐ Roller → 1 reaction (perpendicular to surface)
- ☐ Pinned/Hinged → 2 reactions ($R_x$, $R_y$)
- ☐ Fixed → 3 reactions ($R_x$, $R_y$, $M$)

**C. Loading Types (Four Total) — Memorize the SFD/BMD Fingerprint**
- ☐ Point Load $P$ → SFD has a **vertical jump of P**; BMD has a **slope kink**
- ☐ UDL $w$ → SFD is **linear** (slope = $w$); BMD is **parabolic**
- ☐ UVL → SFD is **parabolic**; BMD is **cubic**
- ☐ Concentrated Moment $M_0$ → SFD is **unchanged**; BMD has a **vertical jump of $M_0$**

**D. Equivalence Rule (THE Most-Used Trick)**
- ☐ Any distributed load = (area under load diagram) as a point load at the **centroid of that area**.
- ☐ UDL of $w$ over length $L$ → $W_{eq} = wL$ at $L/2$ from one end.
- ☐ Triangular UVL from 0 to $w$ over length $L$ → $W_{eq} = wL/2$ at $2L/3$ from the zero-intensity end.

**E. The Three Equilibrium Equations (Always Available for Determinate Beams)**
- ☐ $\sum F_x = 0$
- ☐ $\sum F_y = 0$
- ☐ $\sum M_O = 0$ (choose $O$ wisely to eliminate as many unknowns as possible)

**F. Standard Reaction Formulas (Memorize the Two Most Common)**
- ☐ SSB with point load P at distance $a$ from A, $b$ from B:
  $R_A = Pb/L$ and $R_B = Pa/L$
- ☐ Cantilever with UDL $w$ over full span:
  $R_A = wL$ and $M_A = wL^2/2$

**G. Robotic Mapping (One-Liner for Each Link Type)**
- ☐ Forearm = **Cantilever** (fixed at elbow, free at wrist, payload at free end)
- ☐ Upper arm = **Cantilever** (fixed at shoulder, elbow at free end, payload transferred)
- ☐ Gripper finger = **Cantilever** (fixed at palm, gripping force at tip)
- ☐ Conveyor support = **Continuous beam** (3+ pedestals)
- ☐ Gantry track = **Simply Supported Beam** (pin-roller on rails)
- ☐ Space arm with cable = **Propped Cantilever**

**H. Final Exam-Day Mantra**
- ☐ **"Draw FBD → Count unknowns → Apply ΣFₓ = 0, ΣF_y = 0, ΣM = 0 → Solve → Verify by substituting back."**
- ☐ Never trust an answer that has not been **sanity-checked** against the total applied load.
- ☐ Units are your friend — a wrong unit is an instant **−1 mark** deduction in KTU grading.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Shear Force and Bending Moment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT525 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS OF ROBOTIC PARTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Concept of Shear Force and Bending Moment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Shear Force (SF or V):**
The algebraic sum of the transverse forces (vertical forces in most cases) acting on either side of a section of a beam is known as the **Shear Force** at that section. It represents the internal resisting force parallel to the cross-section of the beam.

**Bending Moment (BM or M):**
The algebraic sum of the moments of all the forces acting on either side of a section of a beam about that section is known as the **Bending Moment** at that section. It represents the internal resisting moment that tends to bend the beam.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The concept of Shear Force and Bending Moment is the foundation for analyzing any **robotic link, manipulator arm, end-effector shaft, or structural chassis member** that experiences bending during operation. Robotic arms are essentially cantilever or simply-supported beams in disguise!

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture)

Imagine you are holding a **wooden ruler** horizontally with one hand at each end (this is a **simply supported beam**), and you place a heavy book at the center.

- **What do you feel in your hands?** A vertical push trying to "slice" through the ruler at the point you are holding. This slicing tendency is the **Shear Force** — it is what would shear off a small piece of the ruler if it were made of two glued parts.
- **What shape does the ruler take?** It bends downward in the middle, forming a curve. The amount it bends is governed by the **Bending Moment** — the internal "couple" trying to fold the ruler.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robotic Parts Analogy:** A robot arm picking up a payload is exactly this ruler! The motor at the "shoulder" applies shear forces and bending moments on the link. If the link is too thin, the moment will exceed its capacity and the arm will snap — this is precisely the failure mode we design against.

### 1.3 Standard Sign Convention (KTU Board Standard)

The **right-hand side of the section** is most commonly used for sign convention:

| Quantity | Positive Sign | Negative Sign |
|---|---|---|
| **Shear Force (V)** | Left part pushed **UP**, or right part pushed **DOWN** (clockwise rotation tendency) | Left part pushed **DOWN**, or right part pushed **UP** (anticlockwise rotation tendency) |
| **Bending Moment (M)** | Causes **SAGGING** (concave upwards, "smile") — top fiber in compression, bottom in tension | Causes **HOGGING** (concave downwards, "frown") — top fiber in tension, bottom in compression |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention to Memorize:** 
> - **Sagging = Positive Moment** (think of a *smile*, which is *positive* and *happy*)
> - **Hogging = Negative Moment** (think of a *frown*, which is *negative* and *sad*)

### 1.4 SFD and BMD — The Visual Story

- **Shear Force Diagram (SFD):** A graphical plot showing how shear force $V$ varies along the length of the beam.
- **Bending Moment Diagram (BMD):** A graphical plot showing how bending moment $M$ varies along the length of the beam.

These two diagrams together are the **"fingerprint"** of how a beam (or robotic link) is loaded, and they are the most frequently asked KTU 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — How SF and BM Arise

Consider an infinitesimal element of a beam of length $dx$ loaded with:
- Shear force $V$ on the left face, $V + dV$ on the right face
- Bending moment $M$ on the left face, $M + dM$ on the right face
- Distributed load intensity $w(x)$ acting downward

**Step 1:** Apply vertical equilibrium ($\sum F_y = 0$):
$$V - (V + dV) - w \cdot dx = 0$$

**Step 2:** Simplify the equilibrium equation:
$$-dV - w \cdot dx = 0$$

**Step 3:** Obtain the **first fundamental relation**:
$$\boxed{\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)}$$

**Step 4:** Apply moment equilibrium about the right face ($\sum M = 0$):
$$M + V \cdot dx - (M + dM) - w \cdot dx \cdot \dfrac{dx}{2} = 0$$

**Step 5:** Neglect the second-order term ($w \cdot dx^2 / 2 \to 0$):
$$V \cdot dx - dM = 0$$

**Step 6:** Obtain the **second fundamental relation**:
$$\boxed{\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V}$$

**Step 7:** Differentiate the second relation and substitute the first to get the **third fundamental relation**:
$$\boxed{\dfrac{d^{2}M}{dx^{2}} = -w(x)}$$

### 2.2 Why These Relations Matter (The 'Why' Behind the Math)

- The **slope of the SFD** at any point equals the **negative of the load intensity** at that point. So, where load is zero, SFD is horizontal; where load is constant (UDL), SFD is linear.
- The **slope of the BMD** at any point equals the **shear force** at that point. So, where SFD is zero, BMD has an extremum (maxima or minima).
- Where the SFD crosses zero, the BMD has a **local extremum** — this is usually where the **maximum bending moment** occurs.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table: Complete Formula Sheet for SF and BM (Memorize This!)**

| # | Relation / Quantity | Mathematical Form | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rate of change of SF | $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w$ | Slope of SFD = negative of load intensity |
| 2 | Rate of change of BM | $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$ | Slope of BMD = Shear Force |
| 3 | Rate of change of slope of BM | $\dfrac{d^{2}M}{dx^{2}} = -w$ | Curvature of BMD relates to load |
| 4 | SF due to point load $P$ | Step jump of magnitude $P$ | Concentrated load causes vertical jump in SFD |
| 5 | BM due to point load $P$ | Linear with slope $P$ | Concentrated load causes a kink in BMD |
| 6 | SF due to UDL $w$ | Linear with slope $-w$ | UDL gives a sloped SFD |
| 7 | BM due to UDL $w$ | Parabolic ($\propto x^{2}$) | UDL gives a parabolic BMD |
| 8 | SF due to UVL (triangular) | Parabolic ($\propto x^{2}$) | UVL gives parabolic SFD |
| 9 | BM due to UVL (triangular) | Cubic ($\propto x^{3}$) | UVL gives cubic BMD |
| 10 | Area under SFD | $\int V \cdot dx = \Delta M$ | Area of SFD between two points = change in BM |
| 11 | Point of contraflexure | $M = 0$ | Where BMD crosses zero axis |
| 12 | Max BM location | $V = 0$ | Where SFD crosses zero axis |

> [!NOTE]
> **No vertical pipe symbol issue:** All relations use standard operators. In case of a magnitude such as $\vert V \vert$, use $\vert V \vert$ written in LaTeX math mode outside the table, or write `mod V` inside the table.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Robotic Manipulator Link Design:** A robot arm holding a payload at its end is essentially a cantilever beam. The SF and BM diagrams tell the engineer the **critical cross-section** where the arm is most likely to fail.
2. **End-Effector Shafts:** Drills, grippers, and screwdrivers mounted on robot wrists experience torsion + bending. The bending moment diagram gives the bending stress component.
3. **Mobile Robot Chassis:** The chassis of an autonomous vehicle (e.g., AGV in a warehouse) experiences complex loading during turning. SFD/BMD are used to design the chassis frame ribs.
4. **Aerospace Robot Arms:** Used on the ISS (Canadarm), the BM at any point must be calculated to ensure the structure does not yield under micro-gravity payload swings.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Three Fundamental Relationships

**Starting Setup:** Consider a small beam element of length $dx$ cut from a loaded beam. The forces and moments acting on it are shown below in the standard convention.

**Step 1 — Vertical Force Equilibrium:**
Summing forces in the vertical direction, with downward distributed load $w$ taken as positive:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum F_y &= 0 \\
V - (V + dV) - w \cdot dx &= 0 \\
-dV - w \cdot dx &= 0 \\
dV &= -w \cdot dx \\
\dfrac{dV}{dx} &= -w(x) \quad \text{...(Relation 1)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Moment Equilibrium:**
Taking moments about the right-hand face of the element (positive anticlockwise) and neglecting the second-order small quantity $w \cdot dx \cdot (dx/2)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum M_{\text{right face}} &= 0 \\
M + V \cdot dx - (M + dM) - w \cdot dx \cdot \dfrac{dx}{2} &= 0 \\
V \cdot dx - dM - \dfrac{w \cdot (dx)^2}{2} &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Neglect Higher-Order Term:**
Since $(dx)^2$ is infinitesimally small compared to $dx$, the last term is neglected:

$$\begin{aligned}
V \cdot dx - dM &= 0 \\
dM &= V \cdot dx \\
\dfrac{dM}{dx} &= V \quad \text{...(Relation 2)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate Again:**
Differentiating Relation 2 with respect to $x$ and substituting Relation 1:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{d^2 M}{dx^2} &= \dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w(x) \quad \text{...(Relation 3)}
\end{aligned}$$

These three relations are the **backbone of every SFD/BMD problem** in KTU exams.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Solving a Beam Step-by-Step

The standard KTU-methodology for solving any SFD/BMD problem is summarized below:

| Step | Action | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | Sketch the beam with all supports and loads clearly shown | Visual clarity, no missing force |
| **2** | Calculate all **support reactions** using $\sum F_y = 0$ and $\sum M = 0$ | Statics must be satisfied |
| **3** | Identify distinct **segments** of the beam (separated by point loads, UDL start/end, supports) | SF/BM equations are different in each segment |
| **4** | Take a section at distance $x$ from a convenient origin (usually the left end) | Universal method |
| **5** | Consider either left portion or right portion — whichever has **fewer forces** | Reduces calculation effort |
| **6** | Apply sign convention and write **V(x)** by summing vertical forces | Get SF equation |
| **7** | Write **M(x)** by summing moments about the section | Get BM equation |
| **8** | Plot SFD and BMD using these equations | Visual diagrams |
| **9** | Identify **points of zero shear** (max BM) and **points of zero moment** (contraflexure) | Critical design points |
| **10** | Calculate **maximum bending moment** and its location | Key design quantity |

### 3.3 Worked Derivation — Simply Supported Beam with UDL

**Problem Setup:** A simply supported beam of span $L$ carries a uniformly distributed load (UDL) of intensity $w$ per unit length over the entire span.

**Step 1 — Reactions:**
By symmetry, both reactions are equal:
$$R_A = R_B = \dfrac{wL}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Section at Distance x from Left End:**
Consider the left portion of length $x$. The forces acting are:
- Reaction $R_A$ (upward) = $wL/2$
- UDL over length $x$ (downward) = $wx$

**Step 3 — Shear Force Equation:**
Applying the sign convention (upward force on left = positive shear):
$$V(x) = \dfrac{wL}{2} - wx$$

**Step 4 — Bending Moment Equation:**
Taking moments about the section (sagging = positive):
$$M(x) = \dfrac{wL}{2} \cdot x - wx \cdot \dfrac{x}{2}$$
$$M(x) = \dfrac{wL \cdot x}{2} - \dfrac{wx^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Critical Values:**

To find where $V = 0$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{wL}{2} - wx &= 0 \\
x &= \dfrac{L}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore, the **maximum bending moment** occurs at the center ($x = L/2$):
$$\begin{aligned}
M_{\max} &= \dfrac{wL}{2} \cdot \dfrac{L}{2} - \dfrac{w}{2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)^{2} \\
M_{\max} &= \dfrac{wL^{2}}{4} - \dfrac{wL^{2}}{8} \\
M_{\max} &= \dfrac{wL^{2}}{8}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Result (Must Memorize):** For a simply supported beam with UDL, $M_{\max} = \dfrac{wL^{2}}{8}$ and occurs at mid-span. This appears in nearly every KTU exam paper.

### 3.4 Worked Derivation — Cantilever Beam with Point Load at Free End

**Problem Setup:** A cantilever beam of length $L$ is fixed at the left end (A) and free at the right end (B). A point load $W$ acts downward at the free end B.

**Step 1 — Reactions at Fixed End:**
- Vertical reaction: $R_A = W$ (upward)
- Moment reaction: $M_A = W \cdot L$ (to balance the load)

**Step 2 — Section at Distance x from Free End (B):**
Considering the right portion (simpler — only has the point load $W$):
- Forces on right portion: $W$ downward at distance $(L - x)$ from the section.

**Step 3 — Shear Force Equation:**
Upward forces on right side give positive shear. Since $W$ is downward on the right of section:
$$V(x) = -W \quad (\text{constant, negative})$$

**Step 4 — Bending Moment Equation:**
Taking moments about the section (sagging positive). The load $W$ creates a hogging moment:
$$M(x) = -W \cdot (L - x)$$

At the free end ($x = L$): $M = 0$ ✓
At the fixed end ($x = 0$): $M = -WL$ (maximum hogging)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Result (Must Memorize):** For a cantilever with point load at free end, $M_{\max} = W \cdot L$ (hogging) at the fixed end. A robot arm holding a payload at its tip experiences exactly this condition!

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Sign Convention for Shear Force and Bending Moment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["BEAM ELEMENT OF LENGTH dx"] --> B1["Left Face: V and M"]
    A0 --> B2["Right Face: V plus dV, M plus dM"]
    A0 --> B3["Top: Distributed Load w per unit length acting DOWN"]
    B1 --> C1["Apply sum of Fy equals zero"]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> D1["RESULT: dV by dx equals negative w"]
    B1 --> C2["Apply sum of Moments equals zero"]
    B2 --> C2
    C2 --> D2["RESULT: dM by dx equals V"]
    D1 --> E1["Combine both relations"]
    D2 --> E1
    E1 --> F1["FINAL: d2M by dx2 equals negative w"]
    style A0 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D1 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D2 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Steps to Construct SFD and BMD

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["START: Given Beam Problem"] --> S1["Step 1: Draw Free Body Diagram of beam with all loads and supports"]
    S1 --> S2["Step 2: Calculate Support Reactions using Static Equilibrium"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Divide beam into distinct Segments at load discontinuities"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: For each segment, take a section at distance x"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5: Consider the side with fewer forces to write V of x"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6: Take moments to write M of x"]
    S6 --> S7["Step 7: Tabulate V and M values at critical x locations"]
    S7 --> S8["Step 8: Plot SFD using V values versus x"]
    S8 --> S9["Step 9: Plot BMD using M values versus x"]
    S9 --> S10["Step 10: Identify Max BM location, Max SF, Points of Contraflexure"]
    S10 --> S11["END: SFD and BMD Complete"]
    style S0 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S11 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S10 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Architecture — Functional Mapping of SF/BM in Robotic Links

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LOAD["EXTERNAL LOAD ON ROBOTIC LINK\nPayload Force, Gravity, Acceleration"] --> SF1["SHEAR FORCE V at section x\nInternal Vertical Resistance"]
    LOAD --> BM1["BENDING MOMENT M at section x\nInternal Couple Resistance"]
    SF1 --> SF2["SLOPE OF SFD equals negative of w\nTells where load intensity acts"]
    BM1 --> BM2["SLOPE OF BMD equals V\nTells where shear crosses zero"]
    SF2 --> CRIT1["CRITICAL SECTION IDENTIFICATION\nMaximum Stress Location"]
    BM2 --> CRIT1
    CRIT1 --> DESIGN["STRUCTURAL DESIGN OF LINK\nChoose Cross Section, Material"]
    SF1 --> SFD_PLOT["PLOT SFD"]
    BM1 --> BMD_PLOT["PLOT BMD"]
    SFD_PLOT --> CRIT1
    BMD_PLOT --> CRIT1
    style LOAD fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style DESIGN fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style CRIT1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style SFD_PLOT fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style BMD_PLOT fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Comparison Chart — Effect of Different Load Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["LOAD TYPE"] --> B1["Concentrated Point Load P"]
    A1 --> B2["Uniformly Distributed Load UDL w"]

    B1 --> C1["SFD: Rectangular Jump\nBMD: Linear Slope"]
    B2 --> C2["SFD: Linear Slope\nBMD: Parabolic Curve"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**
**Define Shear Force and Bending Moment. State the sign convention used in their calculation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Shear Force (V):** The algebraic sum of all vertical (transverse) forces acting on either side of a beam section is called the shear force at that section. It represents the internal resistance against transverse shearing.

**Bending Moment (M):** The algebraic sum of moments of all forces acting on either side of a beam section, taken about that section, is called the bending moment at that section. It represents the internal resistance against bending.

**Sign Convention (Right Side of Section):**
- **Shear Force (V):** Positive if the resultant of forces on the right side is **downward** (or on left side is **upward**).
- **Bending Moment (M):** Positive if it causes **sagging** (concave upward, compression on top fiber); Negative if it causes **hogging** (concave downward, tension on top fiber).

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip (3-mark question):** Examiner expects: 1 mark for each definition + 1 mark for the sign convention with a neat sketch. Skipping the sketch = lose 0.5 mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**
**State and explain the relationship between the rate of loading, shear force, and bending moment.**

**Model Answer:**

The three fundamental differential relationships are:

$$\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$$

$$\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V(x)$$

$$\dfrac{d^{2}M}{dx^{2}} = -w(x)$$

**Explanation:**
- The **slope of the Shear Force Diagram** at any point equals the **negative of the load intensity** $w$ at that point.
- The **slope of the Bending Moment Diagram** at any point equals the **shear force** $V$ at that point.
- The **curvature of the BMD** at any point equals the **negative of the load intensity** at that point.

**Engineering Consequence:** Where the SFD crosses the zero axis, the slope of the BMD is zero — meaning the BM attains a **local maximum or minimum**, which is the **critical design point** for the beam (or robotic link).

---

### PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Simply Supported Beam with UDL

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

A simply supported beam of span **6 m** carries a uniformly distributed load of **10 kN/m** over the entire span. Draw the Shear Force Diagram (SFD) and Bending Moment Diagram (BMD). Also determine the **maximum bending moment** and its location.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) [7 marks] — Calculation of Reactions and Shear Force Diagram:**

*Step 1: Calculate Support Reactions.*

By symmetry (UDL over entire span):
$$R_A = R_B = \dfrac{wL}{2} = \dfrac{10 \times 6}{2} = 30 \text{ kN}$$

*Step 2: Write Shear Force Equation at a section x from A.*

Consider the left portion:
$$V(x) = R_A - wx = 30 - 10x$$

*Step 3: Key Shear Force Values.*

| Position $x$ (m) | Shear Force $V(x)$ (kN) |
|---|---|
| 0 (at A) | $+30$ |
| 3 (mid-span) | $0$ |
| 6 (at B) | $-30$ |

The SFD is a **straight line** decreasing from $+30$ kN at A to $-30$ kN at B, crossing zero at mid-span.

*Part (b) [7 marks] — Bending Moment Equation, BMD, and Maximum BM*

*Step 4: Write Bending Moment Equation at section x from A.*

Consider the left portion, taking moments about the section:
$$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - wx \cdot \dfrac{x}{2} = 30x - 5x^{2}$$

*Step 5: Key Bending Moment Values.*

| Position $x$ (m) | Bending Moment $M(x)$ (kN·m) |
|---|---|
| 0 (at A) | $0$ |
| 3 (mid-span) | $30(3) - 5(9) = 90 - 45 = 45$ |
| 6 (at B) | $0$ |

*Step 6: Maximum Bending Moment.*

Maximum BM occurs where $V = 0$, i.e., at $x = 3$ m (mid-span):
$$M_{\max} = \dfrac{wL^{2}}{8} = \dfrac{10 \times 6^{2}}{8} = \dfrac{360}{8} = 45 \text{ kN·m}$$

The BMD is a **parabolic curve** with peak value $+45$ kN·m at mid-span.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plotting the Diagrams:** SFD is a triangle (linear, positive to negative), and BMD is a parabola (positive, peak at center).

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Cantilever Beam with Point Load at Free End (ALTERNATIVE)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

A cantilever beam of length **4 m** is fixed at end A and free at end B. A point load of **20 kN** acts at the free end B. Draw the SFD and BMD. Determine the **maximum bending moment** and its location.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) [7 marks] — Reactions and Shear Force Diagram:**

*Step 1: Calculate Support Reactions at Fixed End A.*

- Vertical reaction: $R_A = 20$ kN (upward)
- Moment reaction: $M_A = 20 \times 4 = 80$ kN·m (anticlockwise, to balance the load)

*Step 2: Section at distance x from the Free End B.*

Consider the right portion (simpler — only the point load):
$$V(x) = -20 \text{ kN (constant)}$$

*Step 3: SFD Description.*

The SFD is a **rectangle** of magnitude $-20$ kN throughout the entire span (from $x = 0$ at B to $x = 4$ m at A).

*Part (b) [7 marks] — Bending Moment and BMD:*

*Step 4: Bending Moment Equation at section x from B.*

The point load $W = 20$ kN is at distance $(4 - x)$ from the section. Taking moments:
$$M(x) = -20 \cdot (4 - x)$$

*Step 5: Key Bending Moment Values.*

| Position $x$ (m from B) | Bending Moment $M(x)$ (kN·m) |
|---|---|
| 0 (at B, free end) | $-20 \times 4 = -80$ |

Wait — correct: at $x = 0$ (free end B), $M = -20 \times 4 = -80$ kN·m? Let me recompute correctly.

Re-doing: $M(x) = -20 \cdot (4 - x)$:
- At $x = 0$ (free end B): $M = -20 \times 4 = -80$ kN·m
- At $x = 4$ m (fixed end A): $M = -20 \times 0 = 0$ kN·m

> [!WARNING]
> **Correction Note:** This is a classic case of a sign-convention slip-up. Since we are considering the right side, the load $W$ acting downward at distance $(4 - x)$ produces a **hogging** (negative) moment when computed about the section. So at the free end ($x = 0$), the moment is the **maximum negative** value of $-80$ kN·m, and at the fixed end ($x = 4$ m), the moment is zero. The BMD is a **triangle** (linear) varying from $0$ at A (fixed end) to $-80$ kN·m at B (free end).

*Step 6: Maximum Bending Moment.*

$$\vert M_{\max} \vert = W \cdot L = 20 \times 4 = 80 \text{ kN·m (hogging)}$$

This occurs at the **fixed end A**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Mistakes (Most Common, -2 marks):** Many students forget the sign and write $M_{\max} = +80$ kN·m. Always re-verify the shape of the bending: cantilever with downward tip load **HOGS** at the fixed end, hence $M$ is **negative**.
> 2. **Reaction Direction Error (Common, -1.5 marks):** The fixed-end moment reaction $M_A$ is anticlockwise. Drawing it clockwise will lose full reaction marks.
> 3. **Skipping Units (Small but Cumulative, -0.5 mark per occurrence):** Always write kN, kN·m, or N·m in the final answers. Numerical answers without units are penalized.
> 4. **Not drawing the SFD/BMD shape correctly (Major, -3 marks):** For Question A (UDL case), the BMD must be a **smooth parabola** (not a triangle!). For Question B (cantilever), the BMD must be a **straight line** (linear, not curved!). Examiner checks the shape.
> 5. **Forgetting the $V=0$ condition for $M_{\max}$ (Frequent, -1 mark):** Always write "Since $V = 0$ at $x = L/2$, $M$ is maximum at $x = L/2$" to get full credit.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read this just before entering the exam hall!)**

- **Shear Force (V):** Algebraic sum of transverse forces on either side of a beam section.
- **Bending Moment (M):** Algebraic sum of moments of all forces on either side of a beam section, taken about that section.
- **Three Golden Relations (Most Tested in KTU):**
  - $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$
  - $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$
  - $\dfrac{d^{2}M}{dx^{2}} = -w(x)$
- **Sign Convention:** Sagging = Positive, Hogging = Negative. (Smile = +ve, Frown = -ve.)
- **Standard High-Yield Results (Memorize!):**
  - Simply supported beam with UDL: $V_{\max} = wL/2$ at supports, $M_{\max} = wL^{2}/8$ at center.
  - Simply supported beam with central point load: $V_{\max} = P/2$, $M_{\max} = PL/4$ at center.
  - Cantilever with point load at free end: $V = -W$ (constant), $M_{\max} = -WL$ at fixed end.
  - Cantilever with UDL: $M_{\max} = -wL^{2}/2$ at fixed end.
- **Slope-Curvature Rules:**
  - Slope of SFD = $-w$ → constant load = linear SFD.
  - Slope of BMD = $V$ → where $V = 0$, BMD has extremum.
  - BMD is parabolic under UDL, cubic under UVL.
- **Key Locations to Identify:**
  - **Point of Contraflexure:** Where $M = 0$ (BMD crosses zero).
  - **Point of Maximum BM:** Where $V = 0$ (SFD crosses zero).
- **Robotic Linkage Application:** A horizontal robot arm = cantilever beam → max BM at base (motor housing) → choose cross-section accordingly.
- **Methodology Order:** (1) Reactions → (2) Sections → (3) $V(x)$ from fewer-force side → (4) $M(x)$ by moments → (5) Plot → (6) Identify critical points.
- **Area Method:** Area of SFD between two points = change in BM between those points. (Useful for quick verification.)
- **Always annotate units** (kN, kN·m) and **draw the shape of the SFD/BMD** to get full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sign Convention
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT525 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS OF ROBOTIC PARTS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Sign Convention ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sign Convention** is a universally accepted, mathematically consistent set of rules that assigns **positive (+)** or **negative (−)** values to internal forces, moments, stresses, strains, displacements, and rotations in a structural member so that equilibrium, compatibility, and constitutive equations can be written in a single, unambiguous algebraic form. In the context of **PERAT525 – Structural Analysis of Robotic Parts**, sign convention governs how we interpret: - Internal forces in **linkages, arms, grippers, and joint shafts** of a robot - Bending and torsion in **cantilever-type robot appendages** - Deformations in **compliant mechanisms and flexure bearings** ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Ledger" Viewpoint Imagine a structural member as a **double-entry accounting ledger**: | Action | Ledger (Analogy) | Structural Meaning | |---|---|---| | Money **coming in** | Credit (+) | Tension, sagging moment | | Money **going out** | Debit (−) | Compression, hogging moment | Just as a bookkeeper must decide *which side is positive* before recording any transaction, an analyst must declare the convention *before* applying equilibrium. Once declared, **every** force, moment, and deformation in the entire robot structure must obey that single rule. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A consistent sign convention is the *prerequisite* for deriving the load–shear–moment differential relations, drawing SFDs/BMDs, and writing the moment–curvature equation $EI\,\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = M(x)$. ### 1.3 The Two Dominant Conventions in Engineering Practice While several conventions exist, the syllabus focuses on the two most commonly used: 1. **Tension-Positive / Sagging-Positive Convention (TPSP)** — Used for axial members, frames, and beams in structural mechanics. 2. **Right-Hand Rule / Vector Convention** — Used for 3-D trusses, space frames, and robotic manipulators in dynamics. Both are mathematically equivalent; TPSP is preferred for 1-D bar/beam problems, while the vector convention is essential for multi-axial robotic link analysis. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — Layer by Layer The sign convention in structural analysis is built on **three orthogonal layers**: **Layer 1 — External Actions (Loads & Reactions)** - Downward forces on a beam → **negative** (consistent with gravity axis) - Upward reactions → **positive** - Clockwise moments applied externally → **negative**, counter-clockwise → **positive** **Layer 2 — Internal Forces at a Section** Once a member is "cut," the exposed face shows internal resultants. The convention is: - **Normal/Axial Force $N$**: Aimed *outward* from the cut face = **Tension (+)**; aimed *inward* = **Compression (−)**. - **Shear Force $V$**: On the **left face** of the cut element, an *upward* internal shear is **positive**; on the **right face**, a *downward* shear is **positive**. The net effect on a free-body element is a **clockwise rotation = positive $V$**. - **Bending Moment $M$**: A moment that **sags** the beam (tension on the bottom fiber, compression on the top) = **positive**. A **hogging** moment = **negative**. **Layer 3 — Deformations & Stresses** - Elongation $\Delta L > 0$ → **Tensile strain $\varepsilon > 0$** → **Tensile stress $\sigma > 0$** - Shortening $\Delta L < 0$ → **Compressive state** → **Negative sign throughout** ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Convention The TPSP convention is chosen because it makes the differential equilibrium relations **free of sign-corrections**: $$\frac{dV}{dx} = -w(x), \qquad \frac{dM}{dx} = V(x)$$ If a different convention were chosen, every equation would carry an extra negative sign, making it error-prone. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master the table below** — these are the equations you will use in 80% of the 14-mark problems on this module. | # | Quantity | Symbol | Positive Rule | Negative Rule | Governing Equation | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Axial Force | $N$ or $P$ | Tension (member elongates) | Compression (member shortens) | $\sigma = N/A$ | | 2 | Shear Force | $V$ | Left-face upward, right-face downward (clockwise spin) | Counter-clockwise spin on element | $dV/dx = -w$ | | 3 | Bending Moment | $M$ | Sagging (concave up, bottom in tension) | Hogging (concave down, top in tension) | $dM/dx = V$ | | 4 | Torsion | $T$ | Right-hand thumb out of cross-section (vector along axis) | Opposite to right-hand rule | $\tau = T r / J$ | | 5 | Normal Stress | $\sigma$ | Tensile | Compressive | $\sigma = E\,\varepsilon$ | | 6 | Shear Stress | $\tau$ | On +face in +direction | On +face in −direction | $\tau = G\,\gamma$ | | 7 | Normal Strain | $\varepsilon$ | Elongation per unit length | Contraction per unit length | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ | | 8 | Deflection | $y$ or $\delta$ | In assumed positive $y$-axis direction | Opposite to assumed axis | $EI\,y'' = M$ | | 9 | Slope | $\theta = y'$ | CCW rotation of tangent | CW rotation of tangent | $\theta = dy/dx$ | | 10 | Curvature | $\kappa$ | Concave up (sagging) | Concave down (hogging) | $\kappa = y'' / (1+y'^{2})^{3/2}$ | **Units Convention:** Force in **N** (or kN), moment in **N·m** (or kN·m), stress in **Pa** (or N/mm² = MPa), strain is **dimensionless**, lengths in **m** (or mm). > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility in Robotics:** In a 6-DOF robotic arm, the wrist joint carries combined **bending + torsion + axial load**. The sign convention lets the controller's Jacobian matrix correctly map joint torques to Cartesian forces at the end-effector — without this convention, the forward dynamics would be inconsistent. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Load–Shear Differential Relation Consider a small beam element of length $dx$ subjected to a downward distributed load $w(x)$ (N/m, taken positive downward in this derivation context). Internal shear $V$ acts on the left face; on the right face, it is $V + dV$. **Step 1:** Sum vertical forces on the element (upward taken as positive, consistent with TPSP): $$V - (V + dV) - w(x)\,dx = 0$$ **Step 2:** Algebraic simplification (subtract $V$ from both sides): $$-dV - w(x)\,dx = 0$$ **Step 3:** Solve for the derivative: $$\frac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$$ **Interpretation:** The shear force **decreases** along the beam wherever a downward load is present. This is the canonical TPSP form. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Shear–Moment Differential Relation The same element carries internal moments $M$ on the left face and $M + dM$ on the right face, plus a couple due to the changing shear. **Step 1:** Take moments about the **right-hand face** of the element (CCW positive): $$-M + (M + dM) - V\,dx + w(x)\,dx \cdot \frac{dx}{2} = 0$$ **Step 2:** The $w(x)\,dx \cdot (dx/2)$ term is a second-order differential; in the limit $dx \to 0$, it vanishes: $$-M + M + dM - V\,dx + \mathcal{O}(dx^{2}) = 0$$ **Step 3:** Discard the higher-order term and isolate: $$dM = V\,dx \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{dM}{dx} = V(x)$$ **Interpretation:** The slope of the BMD at any section equals the shear force at that section. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Moment–Curvature Equation Using the **Euler–Bernoulli beam theory** assumption that plane sections remain plane and normal to the deformed neutral axis: **Step 1:** A fiber at distance $y$ from the neutral axis undergoes a change in length equal to the arc-length difference: $$\Delta L = -y \cdot d\theta$$ where $d\theta$ is the rotation of the cross-section. The negative sign arises because a fiber *below* the neutral axis ($y < 0$ in standard beam coordinates) must **elongate** during sagging — this is the geometric embodiment of the **sagging-positive** sign convention. **Step 2:** The normal strain at that fiber: $$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{dx} = -y\,\frac{d\theta}{dx}$$ **Step 3:** For small slopes, the curvature is $\kappa = d\theta/dx$. The bending strain is therefore: $$\varepsilon = -y\,\kappa$$ **Step 4:** Apply Hooke's Law ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$): $$\sigma = -E\,y\,\kappa$$ **Step 5:** The internal bending moment is the moment of the stress distribution about the neutral axis: $$M = -\int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA = -\int_A (-E\,y\,\kappa)\,y\,dA = E\,\kappa \int_A y^{2}\,dA$$ **Step 6:** Recognize the second moment of area $I = \int_A y^{2}\,dA$: $$M = E\,I\,\kappa$$ **Step 7:** For small deflections, $\kappa \approx \dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}}$, giving the **Moment–Curvature Equation**: $$\boxed{\;M = E\,I\,\frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}}\;}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Sign-Check:** If $M > 0$ (sagging), then $y'' > 0$ (concave up). This is mathematically consistent and is the entire reason TPSP is preferred. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Stress Sign Convention on an Inclined Plane For a 2-D stress element, the stresses on a plane whose normal makes angle $\theta$ with the $x$-axis are obtained from **force equilibrium** of a small wedge. With the standard **tension-positive, CCW-shear-positive** convention: $$\sigma_{\theta} = \frac{\sigma_{x}+\sigma_{y}}{2} + \frac{\sigma_{x}-\sigma_{y}}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$$ $$\tau_{\theta} = -\frac{\sigma_{x}-\sigma_{y}}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta$$ These equations are valid **only** when the above sign convention is honored throughout; flipping any sign without flipping the equations leads to erroneous principal-stress calculations — a common pitfall in robot link failure analysis. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Flow — Applying Sign Convention in a Robot Link Analysis ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Identify Member Type] --> B{Axial Bar?} B -- Yes --> C[Apply TPSP: Tension = Positive] B -- No --> D{Beam Element?} D -- Yes --> E[Left-face Up V = Positive
Sagging M = Positive] D -- No --> F{Space Frame / Robotic Link?} F -- Yes --> G[Use Right-Hand Rule
Vector Convention] C --> H[Equilibrium dV/dx = -w
dM/dx = V] E --> H G --> I[Write Equilibrium in
Matrix Form F=Ku] H --> J[Solve for Internal Forces
and Deformations] I --> J J --> K[Verify with Boundary
Conditions and Units] ``` ### 4.2 Sign Convention Mapping for Different Robot Components ```mermaid graph LR subgraph ROBOTPARTS[ROBOTIC STRUCTURAL COMPONENTS] dir1[Linear Actuator Link] dir2[Robot Arm Cantilever] dir3[Wrist Joint Shaft] dir4[Gripper Finger] end dir1 --> sig1[Tension Positive
N = + Force] dir2 --> sig2[Shear V and
Sagging M Positive] dir3 --> sig3[Torsion T Positive
via Right-Hand Rule] dir4 --> sig4[Bending M and
Transverse Shear Positive] sig1 --> out[Unified Equilibrium
and Compatibility] sig2 --> out sig3 --> out sig4 --> out out --> result[Internal Force
Distribution] ``` ### 4.3 Free-Body Sign-Convention Reference (Axial Member) ``` +P (Tension) -P (Compression) ↓ ↑ ┌───────────┐ ┌───────────┐ │ │ │ │ │ Member │ PULL │ Member │ PUSH │ │ │ │ └───────────┘ └───────────┘ ↓ ↑ +P (Tension) -P (Compression) RESULT: Member ELONGATES (ΔL>0) Member SHORTENS (ΔL<0) ``` ### 4.4 Free-Body Sign-Convention Reference (Beam — Sagging vs Hogging) ``` SAGGING MOMENT (M > 0) HOGGING MOMENT (M < 0) ┌───────────────┐ ┌───────────────┐ │ ↓ ↓ │ │ ↑ ↑ │ │ W W W │ │ W W W │ │ │ │ │ │ ) ) ) │ bottom in │ ( ( ( │ top in │ ) ) ) │ tension (+) │ ( ( ( │ tension (+) └───────────────┘ └───────────────┘ Concave UP Concave DOWN y'' > 0 y'' < 0 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State and explain the sign convention used in structural analysis for (i) bending moment, and (ii) shear force in a beam. Draw a neat sketch to support your answer.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **(i) Bending Moment:** A bending moment is taken as **positive** when it causes the beam to **sag** (concave upward), producing **tension in the bottom fiber** and **compression in the top fiber**. Conversely, a **hogging** moment (concave downward, tension on top) is **negative**. - **(ii) Shear Force:** Shear force is taken as **positive** when the **left portion of the section experiences an upward force** and the **right portion experiences a downward force**, producing a **clockwise rotation** of the infinitesimal element. - **Sketch** of a simply supported beam with a central point load, showing sagging and the corresponding positive $M$ and $V$ regions. *(Award: 1 mark per correct definition + 1 mark for the sketch.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *Differentiate between the **tension-positive** convention and the **sagging-positive** convention. Why is it important to maintain a single sign convention throughout an analysis?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Tension-positive convention:** Applied to axial members; a force pulling outward is $+$, pushing inward is $-$. Used for bars, ties, columns. - **Sagging-positive convention:** Applied to beam bending; a moment producing sagging curvature is $+$, hogging is $-$. Used for beams and frames. - **Importance of uniformity:** A single convention guarantees the differential relations $dV/dx = -w$ and $EI\,y'' = M$ hold without sign-flips. Mixing conventions causes sign-errors in SFD/BMD and incorrect deflection results — particularly dangerous in robotic link design where miscalculated deflections lead to end-effector positioning errors. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) --- #### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** A simply supported beam of span **$L = 6$ m** carries a **uniformly distributed load (UDL) of intensity $w = 10$ kN/m** over its entire length and a **point load of $P = 20$ kN** at the mid-span. **(a)** *(7 Marks)* Determine the **support reactions** at both ends using equilibrium and the sign convention, and draw the **Shear Force Diagram (SFD)**. **(b)** *(7 Marks)* Draw the **Bending Moment Diagram (BMD)** and locate the section of **maximum bending moment** with its value. --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Reactions and SFD** By symmetry of geometry and loading about the mid-span ($x = 3$ m): $$R_A = R_B = \frac{wL}{2} + \frac{P}{2}$$ Step 1: Substitute numerical values. $$R_A = R_B = \frac{10 \times 6}{2} + \frac{20}{2} = 30 + 10 = 40 \text{ kN}$$ Step 2: Shear force at distance $x$ from left support (for $0 \le x \le 3$ m, *left of point load*): $$V(x) = R_A - w\,x = 40 - 10x \;\; \text{kN}$$ Step 3: At $x = 0$: $V = +40$ kN. At $x = 3^{-}$ (just before mid-span): $$V(3^{-}) = 40 - 10(3) = +10 \text{ kN}$$ Step 4: Just after the point load, the shear drops by $P$: $$V(3^{+}) = 10 - 20 = -10 \text{ kN}$$ Step 5: For $3 \le x \le 6$ m, by symmetry the shear at the right end is $-40$ kN. The SFD is **linear** on both sides of mid-span, with a **discontinuity of 20 kN** at $x = 3$ m. ``` SFD (kN) +40 ┤■■■■■■■■■ │ ■ │ ■ +10 ┤ ■■ (slope -10 kN/m) │ ─────┼────────────────── x 0 │ 3 6 │ ■■ │ ■ │ ■ -10 ┤ ■■ -40 ┤■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■ ``` **Part (b) — BMD and Maximum Moment** Step 1: Bending moment at distance $x$ from left support (for $0 \le x \le 3$): $$M(x) = R_A\,x - \frac{w\,x^{2}}{2} = 40x - 5x^{2}$$ Step 2: Differentiate and set to zero to find the extremum (where $V = 0$): $$\frac{dM}{dx} = V(x) = 40 - 10x = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad x = 4 \text{ m}$$ But $x = 4$ m lies **outside** the interval $[0, 3]$; therefore the maximum in this segment occurs at the **boundary** $x = 3$ m. Step 3: Moment at mid-span ($x = 3$ m): $$M_{\max} = 40(3) - 5(3)^{2} = 120 - 45 = 75 \text{ kN·m}$$ Step 4: Verify by the right segment (for $3 \le x \le 6$): $$M(x) = R_B(6-x) - \frac{w(6-x)^{2}}{2}$$ At $x = 3$: $M = 40(3) - 5(3)^{2} = 75$ kN·m ✓ (matches) Step 5: Moment at the supports is $M(0) = M(6) = 0$, so the BMD is a **parabola** on each half, peaking at the centre. ``` BMD (kN·m) ┌─── 75 ───┐ ╱ ╲ ╱ ╲ ╱ ╲ 0╱ ╲0 ─┼─────────┼────────── x 0 3 6 ``` **Maximum Bending Moment:** $\boxed{M_{\max} = 75 \text{ kN·m at } x = 3 \text{ m (mid-span)}}$ --- #### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]** A cantilever beam of length **$L = 4$ m** is fixed at the **left end (A)** and free at the **right end (B)**. It carries a **point load $P = 30$ kN** downward at the free end **B** and a **uniformly distributed load of $w = 8$ kN/m** over its entire span. **(a)** *(7 Marks)* Draw the **Shear Force Diagram (SFD)** and **Bending Moment Diagram (BMD)** with correct sign convention. **(b)** *(7 Marks)* Compute the **maximum bending stress** in the beam if the cross-section is a **rectangle 100 mm wide × 200 mm deep**, and state whether the top or bottom fibre is in tension. --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — SFD and BMD** Fix a coordinate $x$ from the **fixed end A** ($x = 0$) to the free end B ($x = 4$ m). Step 1: Shear force at any section $x$ (taking the right-side free body, where downward loads appear as **negative** $V$ because they rotate the element CCW as viewed from the right side): $$V(x) = -P - w(L - x) = -30 - 8(4 - x) = -30 - 32 + 8x = -62 + 8x \;\;\text{kN}$$ Step 2: Compute end-values: - At $x = 0$ (fixed end): $V(0) = -62$ kN - At $x = 4$ (free end): $V(4) = -62 + 32 = -30$ kN ✓ (matches the applied $P$) Step 3: Bending moment at any section $x$ (using right-hand free body; moments causing **hogging** are **negative**): $$M(x) = -P(L - x) - \frac{w(L - x)^{2}}{2} = -30(4-x) - 4(4-x)^{2}$$ Step 4: End-values: - $M(0) = -30(4) - 4(16) = -120 - 64 = -184$ kN·m - $M(4) = 0$ ✓ (free end) The BMD is a **parabola** that is entirely **negative** (hogging) — the top fibre is in tension throughout the span. ``` SFD (kN) 0 ┤■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■ +x │ -30┤ ■ (free end, B) │ ╱ -62┤■■■■■■■■■■ (fixed end, A) 0 4 BMD (kN·m) 0 ┤■■■■■■■■■■■■ (free end, B) │ ■ │ ■ │ ■ │ ■ -184┤■■■■■■■■■■■■■ (fixed end, A) 0 4 ``` **Part (b) — Maximum Bending Stress** Step 1: Section properties for a rectangular cross-section $b = 100$ mm, $d = 200$ mm: $$I = \frac{b\,d^{3}}{12} = \frac{100 \times 200^{3}}{12} = \frac{100 \times 8 \times 10^{6}}{12} = 6.667 \times 10^{7} \text{ mm}^{4}$$ Step 2: Section modulus: $$Z = \frac{I}{d/2} = \frac{6.667 \times 10^{7}}{100} = 6.667 \times 10^{5} \text{ mm}^{3}$$ Step 3: Maximum bending moment is at the fixed end: $$|M_{\max}| = 184 \text{ kN·m} = 184 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}$$ Step 4: Maximum bending stress (using $\sigma = M/Z$): $$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{184 \times 10^{6}}{6.667 \times 10^{5}} = 276 \text{ N/mm}^{2} = 276 \text{ MPa}$$ Step 5: Sign of stress: Since the moment is **hogging** ($M < 0$, concave down), the **top fibre is in tension** and the **bottom fibre is in compression**. $$\boxed{\sigma_{\max} = 276 \text{ MPa (tension at top fibre)}}$$ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Sign Convention Module** > 1. **Forgetting to invert the sign for hogging cantilevers** — Students often draw a positive BMD for a cantilever, losing 2–3 marks. *Always check: free end has $M = 0$, fixed end has the maximum negative (hogging) moment.* > 2. **Confusing the two shear signs** — In TPSP, the *left-face-up* convention is positive. Flipping the FBD direction flips the sign of every shear value. State your convention *first*. > 3. **Sign error in the moment–curvature equation** — Writing $M = -EI\,y''$ when TPSP is used is incorrect and costs full marks on derivation questions. > 4. **Unit inconsistency** — Mixing kN and N, or m and mm, is the #1 silent killer in bending-stress sub-parts. Convert *before* substituting. > 5. **Skipping the sign in the final answer** — $\sigma = 276$ MPa with no sign loses 1 mark. The sign of stress indicates the physical state (tension/compression), which is critical for material selection in robot parts. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Sign Convention = Algebraic Direction.** Choose TPSP (tension positive, sagging positive) and stick to it. - **The three differential relations to memorize:** $$\frac{dV}{dx} = -w, \quad \frac{dM}{dx} = V, \quad EI\,\frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = M$$ - **Cantilever beams always have hogging (negative) BMD** that is maximum at the fixed end and zero at the free end. - **Simply supported beams under symmetric downward loading always have sagging (positive) BMD** that is maximum near the centre. - **Sign of stress = Sign of physical state:** $(+)$ tension, $(-)$ compression. Top fibre in tension → sagging moment $(+)$; top fibre in compression → hogging moment $(-)$. - **In robotics:** Sign convention underpins the **stiffness matrix $[K]$** formulation used in FEM analysis of manipulator links — without it, $[K]$ is non-symmetric and the simulation is meaningless. - **Always declare the convention** at the start of every answer — KTU examiners award at least **½ to 1 mark** for explicitly stating the sign rule used. - **Units check:** Force (N or kN), Moment (N·m or kN·m), Stress (Pa or N/mm² = MPa), Strain (dimensionless), Length (m or mm). Convert *all* quantities to a single system before calculation.

topics.title: Shear Force and Bending Moment Diagrams for Cantilever
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT525 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS OF ROBOTIC PARTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Shear Force and Bending Moment Diagrams for Cantilever

# Module 2: Shear Force and Bending Moment Diagrams for Cantilever Beams

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **cantilever beam** is a rigid structural member that is **fixed (built-in) at one end** and **completely free at the opposite end**. The fixed end provides three reactions—**vertical force, horizontal force, and a fixing moment (couple)**—which together restrain the beam against translation and rotation. This makes it a **statically determinate** element (when the axial force is ignored) whose internal force distribution can be fully obtained from equilibrium alone.

In the context of **robotics**, cantilever beams model robotic arms, gripper fingers, link members, and tool extensions where one end is welded/bolted to a rigid base and the other end is free to carry a payload or interact with the environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Students must be able to:
> 1. State and apply the standard sign convention for Shear Force (SF) and Bending Moment (BM).
> 2. Derive SF and BM equations for a cantilever carrying a **point load at the free end**, a **point load at an intermediate section**, a **uniformly distributed load (UDL)**, and a **uniformly varying load (UVL)**.
> 3. Sketch the Shear Force Diagram (SFD) and Bending Moment Diagram (BMD) qualitatively and quantitatively.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Diving Board

Imagine standing at the edge of a swimming pool on a **diving board**. One end of the board is firmly clamped into the concrete deck (the **fixed end**), while the other end projects freely into the air (the **free end**). When a diver walks out along the board:

- The free end deflects **downward** under the load.
- The board experiences maximum **bending stress at the fixed end**, precisely where it is bolted down.
- If you cut the board near the fixed end and isolate the free portion, you would find:
  - A vertical shear trying to slide one face past the other → **Shear Force**.
  - A twisting tendency that wants to rotate the cut face → **Bending Moment**.

This real-world intuition is the entire basis of the SFD and BMD. The **sign** tells you the direction of internal stress; the **magnitude** tells you how strong the beam material must be.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics & Conventions:**
> - Length: **L (m)**
> - Point Load: **P or W (N or kN)**
> - UDL intensity: **w (N/m or kN/m)**
> - UVL maximum intensity: **w (N/m)**
> - Shear Force: **V or SF (N)**
> - Bending Moment: **M or BM (N·m)**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**

### 1.3 The Standard Sign Convention (Must Be Memorized)

| Internal Action | Positive Sense | Visual Cue |
|---|---|---|
| **Shear Force (V)** | Left portion pushed **UP**, right portion pushed **DOWN** | Clockwise rotation tendency on the element |
| **Bending Moment (M)** | **Sagging** — bottom fibre in **tension**, top fibre in compression | Concave-up ("smile") shape on BMD |
| **Negative Moment** | **Hogging** — top fibre in tension, bottom fibre in compression | Concave-down ("frown") shape on BMD |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a cantilever loaded downward, the bending moment is **always NEGATIVE (hogging)** because the top fibres are stretched and the bottom fibres are compressed throughout the span. This is one of the most common sign-convention mistakes in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic for Drawing SFD and BMD of a Cantilever

The procedure is a fixed algorithm. Follow these four steps in order:

1. **Step 1 — Compute Reactions at the Fixed End (A)**
   - Vertical reaction $R_A$ (upward, positive).
   - Fixing moment $M_A$ (counter-clockwise, positive).
   - Use $\sum F_y = 0$ and $\sum M_A = 0$.

2. **Step 2 — Section the Beam at a Generic Distance $x$ from the Free End (B)**
   - Choose the **right-hand segment** (free end to the cut) for analysis. This is the **golden rule** for cantilevers because the right segment carries no reactions and the equations become simple.

3. **Step 3 — Write the Shear Force Equation**
   - $V(x) = \sum (\text{upward forces on the right segment})$
   - $V(x) = -\sum (\text{downward forces on the right segment})$

4. **Step 4 — Write the Bending Moment Equation**
   - $M(x) = \sum (\text{moments of forces on the right segment about the cut section})$
   - Use the rule: a downward force on the right segment produces a **negative (hogging)** moment.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below assume a **cantilever of span $L$**, fixed at the **left end A** and **free at the right end B**. Take origin at the **free end B** for convenience; $x$ is measured from B towards A.

| S.No. | Loading Type | Shear Force $V(x)$ | Bending Moment $M(x)$ | $V_{max}$ | $M_{max}$ (at fixed end) | Diagram Shape |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Point Load $W$ at free end B** | $V(x) = -W$ (constant) | $M(x) = -W \cdot x$ | $\vert W \vert$ at fixed end | $-W L$ | SFD: rectangle; BMD: triangle |
| 2 | **Point Load $P$ at distance $a$ from fixed end** | $V = -P$ (constant for $x \le L-a$) | $M(x) = -P \cdot x$ (for $x \le L-a$); $M = 0$ beyond | $\vert P \vert$ | $-P(L-a)$ | SFD: rectangle; BMD: triangle (only up to load) |
| 3 | **UDL $w$ over entire span** | $V(x) = -w \cdot x$ | $M(x) = -\dfrac{w \cdot x^2}{2}$ | $-w L$ at fixed end | $-\dfrac{w L^2}{2}$ | SFD: triangle; BMD: second-degree parabola |
| 4 | **UVL: zero at free end, $w$ at fixed end** | $V(x) = -\dfrac{w \cdot x^2}{2L}$ | $M(x) = -\dfrac{w \cdot x^3}{6L}$ | $-\dfrac{wL}{2}$ at fixed end | $-\dfrac{w L^2}{6}$ | SFD: second-degree parabola; BMD: cubic curve |
| 5 | **UVL: $w$ at free end, zero at fixed end** | $V(x) = -w \left(x - \dfrac{x^2}{2L}\right)$ | $M(x) = -w\left(\dfrac{x^2}{2} - \dfrac{x^3}{6L}\right)$ | $-\dfrac{wL}{2}$ at fixed end | $-\dfrac{w L^2}{3}$ | SFD: parabola; BMD: cubic curve |

### 2.3 Why These Formulas Matter in Robotic Structural Analysis

In robotic manipulator design, links are treated as **cantilever beams** projecting from a rigid hub. The SFD and BMD tell the design engineer:

- **Where to place the maximum-section reinforcement** — always at the fixed (root) end of a cantilever.
- **How much the tip deflection will be** — the integral of the moment curve gives the slope, and the double integral gives the deflection.
- **Fatigue-critical locations** — the point of contraflexure (where moment changes sign) and the section of maximum moment.

These formulas are not just academic; they are used in **SolidWorks Simulation, ANSYS, and MATLAB robotics toolboxes** as analytical benchmarks against which finite element results are validated.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Point Load $W$ at the Free End (B)

**Given:** Cantilever AB of span $L$, fixed at A, point load $W$ acting downward at the free end B.

**Step 1 — Reactions at A**

$$
\sum F_y = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; R_A - W = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; R_A = W \quad (\uparrow)
$$

$$
\sum M_A = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; M_A - W \cdot L = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; M_A = W \cdot L \quad (\text{counter-clockwise})
$$

**Step 2 — Section at distance $x$ from free end B (i.e., $0 \le x \le L$)**

Consider the **right segment** (length $x$). The only force acting on it is the downward point load $W$ at the free end.

**Step 3 — Shear Force Equation**

For equilibrium of the right segment, the internal shear $V(x)$ on the cut face must balance $W$:

$$
V(x) + (\text{downward force on right}) = 0
$$

$$
V(x) - W = 0 \quad \text{(taking downward as negative on right segment)}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{V(x) = -W \quad \text{(constant for all } x \text{)}}
$$

The negative sign indicates the shear acts **downward on the right face of the element**.

**Step 4 — Bending Moment Equation**

Sum of moments about the cut section, considering only the right segment. The load $W$ acts at distance $x$ from the cut:

$$
M(x) = -W \cdot x
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{M(x) = -W \cdot x \quad \text{(linear in } x \text{)}}
$$

**Step 5 — Key Values**

- At free end ($x = 0$): $V = -W$, $M = 0$.
- At fixed end ($x = L$): $V = -W$, $M = -W L$.

**Step 6 — Diagram Interpretation**

The SFD is a **horizontal rectangle** at ordinate $-W$ from $x=0$ to $x=L$. The BMD is a **straight line** rising linearly from $0$ at the free end to $-WL$ at the fixed end (i.e., drawn below the baseline because it is negative).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL) $w$ over the Entire Span

**Given:** Cantilever AB of span $L$, UDL of intensity $w$ (N/m) over the whole span, fixed at A.

**Step 1 — Reactions at A**

$$
\sum F_y = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; R_A = w L \quad (\uparrow)
$$

$$
\sum M_A = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; M_A = w L \cdot \dfrac{L}{2} = \dfrac{w L^2}{2} \quad (\text{counter-clockwise})
$$

**Step 2 — Section at distance $x$ from free end B**

Consider the right segment of length $x$. The load on this segment is a UDL of intensity $w$, whose **total resultant** is $w \cdot x$ acting at the **centroid** of the segment, which is at a distance $x/2$ from the cut.

**Step 3 — Shear Force Equation**

The total downward force on the right segment is $w \cdot x$. For equilibrium:

$$
V(x) - w \cdot x = 0
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{V(x) = -w \cdot x \quad \text{(linear in } x \text{)}}
$$

**Step 4 — Bending Moment Equation**

The resultant $w \cdot x$ acts at a distance $x/2$ from the cut, producing a moment:

$$
M(x) = -(w \cdot x) \cdot \left(\dfrac{x}{2}\right) = -\dfrac{w \cdot x^2}{2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{M(x) = -\dfrac{w \cdot x^2}{2} \quad \text{(parabolic in } x \text{)}}
$$

**Step 5 — Key Values**

- At free end ($x = 0$): $V = 0$, $M = 0$.
- At fixed end ($x = L$): $V = -wL$, $M = -\dfrac{wL^2}{2}$.

**Step 6 — Diagram Interpretation**

The SFD is a **straight line** rising linearly from $0$ at the free end to $-wL$ at the fixed end. The BMD is a **second-degree parabola** starting at $0$ (with slope $= 0$ at the free end) and reaching $-wL^2/2$ at the fixed end. The parabola opens downward (since the second derivative of $M$ is $-w$, which is negative).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Uniformly Varying Load (UVL): Zero at B, $w$ at A

**Given:** Cantilever AB of span $L$, load intensity varying linearly from $0$ at free end B to $w$ at fixed end A.

**Step 1 — Load Intensity as a Function of $x$**

Measuring $x$ from the free end B, the load intensity at distance $x$ is:

$$
w(x) = \dfrac{w \cdot x}{L}
$$

This is because the intensity grows linearly from $0$ at $x=0$ to $w$ at $x=L$.

**Step 2 — Section at distance $x$ from free end B**

Consider the right segment. The load distribution on this segment is a **triangle** with:
- Base along the segment (length $x$).
- Zero intensity at the free end ($x=0$).
- Intensity $w(x) = wx/L$ at the cut.

**Step 3 — Total Load on Right Segment**

The total load is the area of the triangle:

$$
W_{\text{seg}} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot x \cdot \left(\dfrac{w \cdot x}{L}\right) = \dfrac{w \cdot x^2}{2L}
$$

**Step 4 — Location of Centroid from the Cut**

For a right-triangular distribution (zero at the free end, max at the cut), the centroid lies at a distance of $\dfrac{2x}{3}$ from the **zero end** (i.e., from B) and therefore at $\dfrac{x}{3}$ from the **cut** (the maximum end).

**Step 5 — Shear Force Equation**

$$
V(x) = -\dfrac{w \cdot x^2}{2L}
$$

**Step 6 — Bending Moment Equation**

$$
M(x) = -W_{\text{seg}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{x}{3}\right) = -\dfrac{w \cdot x^2}{2L} \cdot \dfrac{x}{3} = -\dfrac{w \cdot x^3}{6L}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{V(x) = -\dfrac{w \cdot x^2}{2L} \quad \text{;}\quad M(x) = -\dfrac{w \cdot x^3}{6L}}
$$

**Step 7 — Key Values**

- At free end ($x = 0$): $V = 0$, $M = 0$.
- At fixed end ($x = L$): $V = -\dfrac{wL}{2}$, $M = -\dfrac{wL^2}{6}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** Total load on the beam = area of triangle = $\frac{1}{2} \cdot w \cdot L = \frac{wL}{2}$, which equals $|V_{\max}|$ at the fixed end. ✓ The total moment about A due to this load = $\frac{wL}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{3} = \frac{wL^2}{6}$, which equals $|M_{\max}|$. ✓

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Point Load $P$ at Distance $a$ from the Fixed End

**Given:** Cantilever AB of span $L$, point load $P$ acting downward at a section located at distance $a$ from the fixed end A. Equivalently, the load is at distance $(L-a)$ from the free end B.

**Step 1 — Reactions at A**

$$
R_A = P \quad (\uparrow) \quad ; \quad M_A = P \cdot a \quad (\text{counter-clockwise})
$$

**Step 2 — Section at distance $x$ from the free end B**

Consider two regions:

**Region 1 — $0 \le x \le (L - a)$** (between the free end and the load): No forces act on the right segment, so:

$$
V(x) = 0 \quad ; \quad M(x) = 0
$$

**Region 2 — $(L - a) \le x \le L$** (between the load and the fixed end): The point load $P$ now lies on the right segment, at a distance $x - (L - a)$ from the cut. Therefore:

$$
V(x) = -P \quad ; \quad M(x) = -P \cdot [x - (L - a)]
$$

**Step 3 — Key Values**

- At free end ($x = 0$): $V = 0$, $M = 0$.
- Just to the right of the load ($x = L - a$): $V = 0$, $M = 0$ (still no force on right segment of length $< L-a$).
- Just to the left of the load ($x = (L - a)^+$): $V = -P$, $M = 0$.
- At fixed end ($x = L$): $V = -P$, $M = -P \cdot a$.

**Step 4 — Diagram Interpretation**

The SFD is **zero** from $B$ up to the load, then jumps to $-P$ and remains constant up to $A$. The BMD is **zero** from $B$ up to the load, then rises linearly to $-Pa$ at $A$. The diagram is thus a **triangle of base $a$** plotted below the baseline.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cantilever Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Identify Cantilever Geometry: Span L, Fixed End A, Free End B] --> B0[Compute Reactions at Fixed End]
    B0 --> B1[Vertical Reaction RA from sum Fy = 0]
    B0 --> B2[Fixing Moment MA from sum MA = 0]
    B1 --> C0[Choose Generic Section at distance x from Free End B]
    B2 --> C0
    C0 --> D0[Analyze Right Hand Segment of the Beam]
    D0 --> E0[Write Shear Force Equation V of x]
    D0 --> F0[Write Bending Moment Equation M of x]
    E0 --> G0[Evaluate V at key points x = 0 and x = L]
    F0 --> G0
    G0 --> H0[Plot Shear Force Diagram SFD]
    G0 --> I0[Plot Bending Moment Diagram BMD]
    H0 --> J0[Identify Vmax location and magnitude]
    I0 --> K0[Identify Mmax location and magnitude]
    J0 --> L0[Design Check: Beam strength vs Vmax and Mmax]
    K0 --> L0
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cantilever Loading Cases and Their Signatures

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SC[Standard Cantilever Loading Cases]
        direction TB
        SC1[Case 1: Point Load W at Free End]
        SC2[Case 2: Point Load P at Intermediate Section]
        SC3[Case 3: UDL w over Entire Span]
        SC4[Case 4: UVL Zero at Free End, w at Fixed End]
        SC5[Case 5: UVL w at Free End, Zero at Fixed End]
    end
    SC1 --> SF1[SFD: Rectangle at minus W]
    SC1 --> BM1[BMD: Triangle peaking at minus WL]
    SC2 --> SF2[SFD: Zero then Step down to minus P]
    SC2 --> BM2[BMD: Zero then Triangle peaking at minus Pa]
    SC3 --> SF3[SFD: Triangle from 0 to minus wL]
    SC3 --> BM3[BMD: Parabola peaking at minus wL squared by 2]
    SC4 --> SF4[SFD: Parabola peaking at minus wL by 2]
    SC4 --> BM4[BMD: Cubic Curve peaking at minus wL squared by 6]
    SC5 --> SF5[SFD: Parabola peaking at minus wL by 2]
    SC5 --> BM5[BMD: Cubic Curve peaking at minus wL squared by 3]
```

### 4.3 ASCII Schematic — Cantilever with UDL

```
   Fixed End A                                          Free End B
       ||==========================================================
       || <------------------- Span L ----------------------->
       ||                                                          
       ||  R_A = w*L (↑)                                            
       ||  M_A = wL²/2 (↺)                                          
       ||                                                          
   ----||---------------------------------------------------------|
   |   ||   ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░  |
   |   ||   ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░ UDL of intensity w (N/m) ░░░░░░░░░░░  |
   ----||---------------------------------------------------------|
       ||                              |x|
       ||                              |<->  (measured from B)
       ||                              ▼
       ||                         [Cut Section]
```

### 4.4 Typical SFD and BMD Shapes (Block-Level Visualization)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SFD[Shear Force Diagram - Below Baseline means Negative]
        SFV[Vertical axis: V in N] --> SFH[Horizontal axis: x measured from free end B]
        SFH --> SH1[Rectangle for Point Load Case]
        SFH --> SH2[Triangle for UDL Case]
        SFH --> SH3[Parabola for UVL Case]
    end
    subgraph BMD[Bending Moment Diagram - All Negative for Cantilever]
        BMV[Vertical axis: M in N.m] --> BMH[Horizontal axis: x measured from free end B]
        BMH --> BH1[Triangle peaking at minus WL for Point Load Case]
        BMH --> BH2[Parabola peaking at minus wL squared by 2 for UDL Case]
        BMH --> BH3[Cubic curve peaking at minus wL squared by 6 for UVL Case]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a cantilever beam. State the sign convention for shear force and bending moment.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A cantilever beam is a structural member **fixed at one end and free at the other**. The fixed end provides vertical, horizontal, and moment reactions.
- **Sign convention for Shear Force (V):** Positive when the left portion of the beam is pushed upward (or equivalently, the right portion is pushed downward), causing a clockwise rotation of the element.
- **Sign convention for Bending Moment (M):** Positive when it causes **sagging** (concave up), producing tension in the bottom fibres and compression in the top fibres. Negative moment corresponds to **hogging**.

---

**Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**For a cantilever of span $L$ carrying a point load $W$ at the free end, write the expressions for shear force and bending moment at a section $x$ from the free end.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Taking origin at the free end:
$$V(x) = -W \quad \text{(constant, negative)}$$

$$M(x) = -W \cdot x \quad \text{(linear, negative)}$$

At the fixed end ($x = L$): $V_{\max} = -W$ and $M_{\max} = -WL$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question B1 — Choice A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**A cantilever beam AB of span 4 m is fixed at A and carries a uniformly distributed load of intensity 5 kN/m over the entire span. Draw the Shear Force Diagram (SFD) and Bending Moment Diagram (BMD). Also determine the magnitude and location of maximum bending moment.**

**Sub-part (a) — Derive the SF and BM equations and calculate reactions. (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Reactions at Fixed End A:**

Total load = $w L = 5 \times 4 = 20$ kN, acting at mid-span.
