$$
R_A = 20 \text{ kN} \; (\uparrow) \quad ; \quad M_A = 20 \times 2 = 40 \text{ kN·m} \; (\circlearrowleft)
$$

**Step 2 — Section at distance $x$ from free end B:**

Load on right segment (length $x$) = $w \cdot x = 5x$ kN, acting at $x/2$ from the cut.

**Step 3 — Shear Force Equation:**

$$
V(x) = -5x \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Bending Moment Equation:**

$$
M(x) = -(5x) \cdot \frac{x}{2} = -2.5\, x^2 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Sub-part (b) — Plot the SFD and BMD and find maximum BM. (7 marks)**

**Step 5 — Tabulation of Values:**

| $x$ (m) | $V(x)$ (kN) | $M(x)$ (kN·m) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 (B) | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | -5 | -2.5 |
| 2 | -10 | -10.0 |
| 3 | -15 | -22.5 |
| 4 (A) | -20 | -40.0 |

**Step 6 — Maximum BM:**

At the fixed end A ($x = 4$ m):

$$
\boxed{M_{\max} = -40 \text{ kN·m (hogging)}}
$$

**Step 7 — Diagrams:**

- **SFD:** A right triangle with vertices at $(0, 0)$, $(0, -20)$, and $(4, -20)$. Linear from free end to fixed end.
- **BMD:** A second-degree parabola opening downward, with vertices at $(0, 0)$ (slope zero) and $(4, -40)$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Many students forget the negative sign on the bending moment for a cantilever. In a downward-loaded cantilever, the BM is **always negative (hogging)**. Writing $M_{\max} = +40$ kN·m will cost you the sign-convention mark. Also, the BMD for UDL is a **parabola**, not a triangle — students who connect points linearly lose full marks.

---

#### **Question B1 — Choice B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**A cantilever beam AB of span 6 m is fixed at A and carries a uniformly varying load with zero intensity at the free end B and $w = 12$ kN/m at the fixed end A. Draw the SFD and BMD and find the maximum bending moment.**

**Sub-part (a) — Derive the SF and BM equations. (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Load Intensity at distance $x$ from B:**

$$
w(x) = \dfrac{12 \cdot x}{6} = 2x \text{ kN/m}
$$

**Step 2 — Section at distance $x$ from B:**

Right segment carries a triangular load: intensity $0$ at $x=0$ and $2x$ at the cut.

Total load on right segment:

$$
W_{\text{seg}} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot x \cdot (2x) = x^2 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Shear Force Equation:**

$$
V(x) = -x^2 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Bending Moment Equation:**

Centroid of the triangular load is at distance $x/3$ from the cut (maximum end):

$$
M(x) = -x^2 \cdot \frac{x}{3} = -\dfrac{x^3}{3} \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Sub-part (b) — Plot and find $M_{\max}$. (7 marks)**

**Step 5 — Tabulation:**

| $x$ (m) | $V(x)$ (kN) | $M(x)$ (kN·m) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | -1 | -0.333 |
| 2 | -4 | -2.667 |
| 3 | -9 | -9.000 |
| 4 | -16 | -21.333 |
| 5 | -25 | -41.667 |
| 6 (A) | -36 | -72.000 |

**Step 6 — Maximum BM at fixed end:**

$$
\boxed{M_{\max} = -72 \text{ kN·m (at A)}}
$$

**Verification using formula:**

$$
M_{\max} = -\dfrac{w L^2}{6} = -\dfrac{12 \times 36}{6} = -72 \text{ kN·m} \;\checkmark
$$

**Step 7 — Diagrams:**

- **SFD:** A second-degree parabola starting at $(0,0)$ and reaching $(6, -36)$.
- **BMD:** A cubic curve starting at $(0,0)$ (zero slope) and reaching $(6, -72)$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** A very common error in UVL problems is computing the **centroid of the triangular load incorrectly**. The centroid of a right triangle is at $\frac{x}{3}$ from the **maximum-intensity end**, NOT the midpoint. Mistakes here propagate through the rest of the solution and will cost 4–5 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Cantilever SFD & BMD:**

- **Golden rule:** Always take sections from the **free end** and analyze the **right segment** for cantilevers. This eliminates the fixed-end reactions from your equations.

- **Sign convention is non-negotiable:** Downward loads on a cantilever give **negative (hogging)** bending moment. The BMD for a downward-loaded cantilever lies entirely **below the baseline**.

- **Memorize the four standard cases** (Point load, UDL, UVL-zero-at-tip, UVL-max-at-tip). Their formulas appear in nearly every KTU question paper.

- **Maximum BM location:** Always at the **fixed end** for a cantilever with downward loading.

- **Diagram shapes — memorize these at sight:**
  - Point load → SFD: **rectangle**, BMD: **triangle**
  - UDL → SFD: **triangle**, BMD: **second-degree parabola**
  - UVL → SFD: **parabola**, BMD: **cubic curve**

- **Slope check at free end:** For UDL/UVL cases, the slope of the BMD at the free end is **zero** (since shear = 0 there). This confirms the parabolic/cubic shape.

- **Area-moment shortcut:** The change in shear between two points equals the area under the load diagram between those points. The change in moment equals the area under the SFD between those points. Use this as a cross-check in the exam.

- **Units consistency:** Length in **m**, load in **kN**, moment in **kN·m**. Mixing units (e.g., mm and N) is a frequent mark-losing mistake.

- **Sanity check via reactions:** Total downward load on the beam must equal $R_A$; total moment of downward loads about A must equal $M_A$. Verify these in your solution to catch arithmetic errors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simply Supported and Overhanging Beams Subjected to Point Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT525 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS OF ROBOTIC PARTS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Simply Supported and Overhanging Beams Subjected to Point Loads # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **beam** is a structural member, typically horizontal, designed primarily to resist transverse loads and bending. In the context of robotic parts analysis, beams represent linkages, arms, cantilevers, and load-bearing struts within mechanical assemblies. A **Simply Supported Beam (SSB)** is a beam supported at its two ends by: - One **pin (hinged) support** — which resists vertical and horizontal forces but allows rotation. - One **roller support** — which resists only vertical force and allows both rotation and horizontal movement. The defining feature of an SSB is that it is **statically determinate** (the number of unknown reactions equals the number of equilibrium equations: $\Sigma F_y = 0$, $\Sigma F_x = 0$, $\Sigma M = 0$). An **Overhanging Beam** is a beam that extends beyond its supports on one or both ends. It consists of: - A central portion between two supports (like an SSB). - One or two overhanging portions that project freely into space. Mathematically, an overhanging beam is **statically determinate** but contains regions of **negative bending moment** over the supports, unlike a pure SSB which only has positive bending moments. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: For the course *Structural Analysis of Robotic Parts*, students must master SF (Shear Force) and BM (Bending Moment) diagrams because robotic arm deflection, joint stress, and actuator loads are derived from these fundamental internal force distributions. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **wooden plank** placed across two bricks (the supports) and you stand on the middle of it. The plank bends downward — this is a simply supported beam with a central point load. Now, picture a **diving board** at a swimming pool — one end is held by a rigid support, the other end extends out into the air where the diver stands. The diver's weight creates a *downward pull* at the free end, but the plank curves *upward* between the rigid support and the brick (a second support). That curved-upward region is where **negative bending moment** exists — this is an overhanging beam. > [!NOTE] > **Simple Definition Box** > - **Shear Force (V)** at any section = algebraic sum of vertical forces on either side of the section. > - **Bending Moment (M)** at any section = algebraic sum of moments of all forces about that section. > - **Sign Convention (KTU Standard)**: Positive SF causes clockwise rotation of the beam element; positive BM causes sagging (concave upward, "smile" shape). --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit (SI) | |---|---|---| | Length of beam | $L$ | meters (m) | | Point Load | $P$ | Newtons (N) | | Shear Force | $V$ | Newtons (N) | | Bending Moment | $M$ | Newton-meters (N·m) | | Modulus of Elasticity | $E$ | $\text{N/m}^2$ or **Pa** | | Moment of Inertia | $I$ | $\text{m}^4$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > For robotic applications, the **flexural rigidity** ($EI$) is the critical parameter that governs deflection. Standard metric values for robotic structural members typically span $E = 70$ to $200 \text{ GPa}$ (aluminum to steel). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown ### A. Simply Supported Beam with Central Point Load **Step 1: Free Body Setup** Consider a simply supported beam of length $L$ carrying a central point load $P$ at midspan. **Step 2: Reaction Computation** By symmetry of geometry and loading: $$R_A = R_B = \frac{P}{2}$$ **Step 3: Shear Force Analysis** - From left support to load: $V(x) = +\frac{P}{2}$ (constant) - From load to right support: $V(x) = +\frac{P}{2} - P = -\frac{P}{2}$ (constant) **Step 4: Bending Moment Analysis** - Maximum BM occurs directly under the load at $x = \frac{L}{2}$ $$M_{\max} = \frac{PL}{4}$$ The BM diagram is triangular, peaking at the center. --- ### B. Simply Supported Beam with Eccentric Point Load **Step 1: Setup** Load $P$ at distance $a$ from left support $A$, and distance $b$ from right support $B$, where $a + b = L$. **Step 2: Reactions** Taking moments about $A$: $$R_B \cdot L = P \cdot a \implies R_B = \frac{Pa}{L}$$ From $\Sigma F_y = 0$: $$R_A = P - R_B = \frac{Pb}{L}$$ **Step 3: Maximum Bending Moment** $$M_{\max} = \frac{Pab}{L} \quad \text{(at the point of load application)}$$ --- ### C. Overhanging Beam with Point Load at Free End **Step 1: Setup** Beam with supports at $A$ (left) and $B$ (right). The beam overhangs beyond $B$ by length $a$. Point load $P$ acts downward at the free end $C$. **Step 2: Reactions** Total length from $A$ to $C$ = $L + a$, where $L$ = span between supports. Taking moments about $A$: $$R_B \cdot L = P \cdot (L + a) \implies R_B = \frac{P(L+a)}{L}$$ $$R_A = P - R_B = -\frac{Pa}{L} \quad \text{(negative means reaction acts downward)}$$ **Step 3: Internal Force Analysis** - **Overhang region (B to C)**: BM is negative (hogging), linearly varying from $0$ at $C$ to $-Pa$ at $B$. - **Between supports (A to B)**: BM is positive (sagging), with maximum at the location where $V = 0$. - **Point of Contraflexure**: A point where BM changes sign (crosses zero), located between $A$ and $B$. --- ### D. Overhanging Beam with Point Load Between Supports This case behaves like a simply supported beam (positive BM only) but with the overhanging portion experiencing zero loading. Reactions and moment diagrams follow SSB rules for the span portion. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | S.No. | Beam Configuration | Reactions | Max BM | Max SF | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | SSB with central load $P$ | $R_A = R_B = \dfrac{P}{2}$ | $M_{\max} = \dfrac{PL}{4}$ | $V_{\max} = \dfrac{P}{2}$ | | 2 | SSB with eccentric load $P$ at distance $a$ from $A$ | $R_A = \dfrac{Pb}{L}$, $R_B = \dfrac{Pa}{L}$ | $M_{\max} = \dfrac{Pab}{L}$ | $V_{\max} = \max(R_A, R_B)$ | | 3 | Overhang with load $P$ at free end (overhang length $a$, span $L$) | $R_B = \dfrac{P(L+a)}{L}$, $R_A = -\dfrac{Pa}{L}$ | $M_B = -Pa$ (negative) | $V = P$ in overhang | | 4 | Overhang with load $P$ between supports (span $L$, overhang $a$ unloaded) | $R_A = \dfrac{Pb}{L}$, $R_B = \dfrac{Pa}{L}$ | $M_{\max} = \dfrac{Pab}{L}$ (positive) | $V_{\max}$ depends on $a,b$ | | 5 | SSB with two equal point loads $P$ at $L/3$ from each support | $R_A = R_B = P$ | $M_{\max} = \dfrac{PL}{3}$ (constant between loads) | $V_{\max} = P$ | | 6 | SSB with multiple point loads | Use superposition: $R_A = \sum \dfrac{P_i b_i}{L}$ | Sum of individual contributions | Sum of left-side forces | ### Key Definitions for KTU Board Exams | Term | Definition | |---|---| | **Shear Force (SF)** | Rate of change of BM with respect to $x$: $V = \dfrac{dM}{dx}$ | | **Bending Moment (BM)** | Rate of change of SF: $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = w(x)$ (load intensity) | | **Point of Contraflexure** | Section where BM = 0 and changes sign | | **Point of Maximum BM** | Section where SF = 0 or changes sign | --- ## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Robotic Engineering In **robotic arms**, every link acts as a beam under combined loading: - A **horizontal arm** holding a gripper behaves as a simply supported beam between its shoulder and wrist joints. - An **overhanging configuration** (like a cantilevered robotic weld arm) creates negative BM at the joint, demanding careful joint design to resist hogging stress. - The **maximum deflection** of a robotic link under payload directly affects **end-effector positioning accuracy** in pick-and-place operations. - **SF and BM diagrams** are the first step in **fatigue analysis** for cyclic robotic operations. > [!NOTE] > Engineers at firms like KUKA and ABB routinely use these classical beam formulas to size robotic links before performing FEA (Finite Element Analysis) validation. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Simply Supported Beam with Central Point Load **Problem Setup:** A beam of length $L$ rests on pin support at $A$ (left end, $x=0$) and roller support at $B$ (right end, $x=L$). A point load $P$ acts downward at $x = L/2$. **Step 1: Compute Reactions** By symmetry: $$R_A = R_B$$ Applying vertical equilibrium: $$\Sigma F_y = 0 \implies R_A + R_B - P = 0$$ Substituting $R_A = R_B$: $$2R_A = P \implies R_A = R_B = \frac{P}{2}$$ **Step 2: Shear Force Diagram** Region 1: $0 \leq x < L/2$ $$V(x) = R_A = +\frac{P}{2}$$ Region 2: $L/2 < x \leq L$ $$V(x) = R_A - P = \frac{P}{2} - P = -\frac{P}{2}$$ The SF diagram is a rectangular step: positive plateau on the left, negative plateau on the right, with a vertical drop of magnitude $P$ at the center. **Step 3: Bending Moment Diagram** Region 1: $0 \leq x \leq L/2$ $$M(x) = R_A \cdot x = \frac{P}{2} x$$ At $x = 0$: $M = 0$ At $x = L/2$: $M = \dfrac{P}{2} \cdot \dfrac{L}{2} = \dfrac{PL}{4}$ Region 2: $L/2 \leq x \leq L$ $$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - P\left(x - \frac{L}{2}\right)$$ At $x = L$: $M = \dfrac{P}{2} \cdot L - P \cdot \dfrac{L}{2} = 0$ The BM diagram is a triangle with peak $\dfrac{PL}{4}$ at the center. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Result**: For an SSB with central point load, $M_{\max} = \dfrac{PL}{4}$ and $V_{\max} = \dfrac{P}{2}$. These two values are the most-tested KTU formula outcomes. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Simply Supported Beam with Eccentric Point Load **Setup:** Load $P$ at distance $a$ from $A$ and $b$ from $B$, with $a + b = L$. **Step 1: Reactions** Taking moments about $A$ (counterclockwise positive): $$\Sigma M_A = 0 \implies R_B \cdot L - P \cdot a = 0$$ $$R_B = \frac{Pa}{L}$$ From $\Sigma F_y = 0$: $$R_A = P - \frac{Pa}{L} = \frac{P(L-a)}{L} = \frac{Pb}{L}$$ **Step 2: Shear Force** Region 1: $0 \leq x < a$ $$V(x) = R_A = \frac{Pb}{L}$$ Region 2: $a < x \leq L$ $$V(x) = R_A - P = \frac{Pb}{L} - P = \frac{Pb - PL}{L} = -\frac{Pa}{L}$$ **Step 3: Bending Moment** At $x = a$ (under the load): $$M_{\max} = R_A \cdot a = \frac{Pab}{L}$$ For $0 \leq x \leq a$: $$M(x) = \frac{Pb}{L} \cdot x$$ For $a \leq x \leq L$: $$M(x) = \frac{Pb}{L} \cdot x - P(x - a)$$ At $x = L$: $$M(L) = \frac{Pb}{L} \cdot L - P(L-a) = Pb - P(L-a) = Pb - PL + Pa = P(a+b) - PL = PL - PL = 0$$ This confirms the boundary condition $M = 0$ at the roller support. > [!NOTE] > **Verification Check**: When $a = b = L/2$, the formula reduces to $M_{\max} = \dfrac{P(L/2)(L/2)}{L} = \dfrac{PL}{4}$. ✓ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Overhanging Beam with Point Load at Free End **Setup:** Supports at $A$ (left, $x=0$) and $B$ (right, $x=L$). Beam extends to free end $C$ at $x = L + a$. Point load $P$ acts downward at $C$. **Step 1: Reactions** Taking moments about $A$: $$R_B \cdot L - P \cdot (L+a) = 0$$ $$R_B = \frac{P(L+a)}{L} = P + \frac{Pa}{L}$$ From $\Sigma F_y = 0$: $$R_A + R_B = P$$ $$R_A = P - R_B = P - P - \frac{Pa}{L} = -\frac{Pa}{L}$$ The **negative sign** indicates that $R_A$ acts **downward** (the left support must hold the beam down to prevent uplift). **Step 2: Shear Force Diagram** Region 1: Overhang (from $C$ to $B$, i.e., $L \leq x \leq L+a$ measured from $A$) Taking section from the right free end: $$V = +P \quad \text{(constant throughout overhang)}$$ Wait — let me recompute by considering sections from the left. For $x < L$ (between supports), measuring from $A$: $$V(x) = R_A = -\frac{Pa}{L}$$ For $L < x \leq L+a$ (overhang region): $$V(x) = R_A + R_B = -\frac{Pa}{L} + \frac{P(L+a)}{L} = \frac{-Pa + PL + Pa}{L} = +P$$ At $x = L+a$ (free end with load): $V = P - P = 0$ ✓ **Step 3: Bending Moment Diagram** Region 1: Overhang, $L \leq x \leq L+a$ Taking moments from the right free end $C$ (measuring $\xi = (L+a) - x$): $$M(x) = -P \cdot (L+a-x)$$ At $x = L+a$: $M = 0$ ✓ At $x = L$: $M = -P \cdot a$ (negative — hogging moment) Region 2: Between supports, $0 \leq x \leq L$ $$M(x) = R_A \cdot x = -\frac{Pa}{L} \cdot x$$ At $x = 0$: $M = 0$ ✓ At $x = L$: $M = -\dfrac{Pa}{L} \cdot L = -Pa$ ✓ (matches above) **Step 4: Point of Contraflexure** Setting $M(x) = 0$ in the between-supports region: $$-\frac{Pa}{L} \cdot x = 0 \implies x = 0$$ This shows BM is **negative throughout the between-supports region** for this case — there is no point of contraflexure within the span. The BM transitions only at support $B$ from $-Pa$ to positive values in the overhang... wait, let me reconsider. Actually, BM is most negative at $B$ ($M_B = -Pa$). The BM from $A$ to $B$ is linearly decreasing from $0$ to $-Pa$ (negative throughout). In the overhang from $B$ to $C$, BM increases linearly from $-Pa$ back to $0$. So the point of contraflexure is exactly at $C$ (the free end), which is trivial. **The crucial inflection happens only when the loading creates positive moment between supports — e.g., when there's also a load between supports.** **Step 5: Maximum Bending Moment** For this configuration: $$|M|_{\max} = Pa \quad \text{(at support } B\text{)}$$ The magnitude of the negative moment at $B$ equals $Pa$. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Overhanging Beam with Point Load Between Supports (and Overhang Unloaded) **Setup:** Span $L$ between supports $A$ and $B$. Beam overhangs by $a$ on right side to free end $C$ (unloaded). Load $P$ acts at distance $b$ from $A$ on the span. **Step 1: Reactions** This case reduces to a simply supported beam with eccentric load, since the overhang contributes no load: $$R_A = \frac{P(L-b)}{L} = \frac{Pa'}{L}, \quad R_B = \frac{Pb}{L}$$ where I'm using $a'$ for the segment from load to support $B$ to avoid confusion with overhang length. Let me redefine: Let distance from load to $B$ be $c$. Then: $$R_A = \frac{Pc}{L}, \quad R_B = \frac{P(L-c)}{L}$$ **Step 2: Bending Moment** Maximum BM at the load point: $$M_{\max} = \frac{P \cdot b \cdot c}{L}$$ This is **positive** (sagging) throughout the span between supports. The overhang has $M = 0$ everywhere (no loads). > [!NOTE] > **Important Insight**: If the overhang is unloaded, it has zero BM. The overhang merely adds mass (and possibly self-weight in advanced problems) but does not affect the SF/BM due to point loads on the span. --- ## 3.5 Comprehensive Worked Example (KTU Standard 14-Mark Style) **Problem:** A simply supported beam of span $L = 8 \text{ m}$ carries point loads of $20 \text{ kN}$ at $2 \text{ m}$ from the left support and $30 \text{ kN}$ at $5 \text{ m}$ from the left support. Draw the SFD and BMD. Find the maximum bending moment. **Step 1: Reaction Calculation** $$\Sigma M_A = 0 \implies R_B \cdot 8 = 20 \cdot 2 + 30 \cdot 5$$ $$R_B \cdot 8 = 40 + 150 = 190$$ $$R_B = 23.75 \text{ kN}$$ $$\Sigma F_y = 0 \implies R_A = (20 + 30) - 23.75 = 26.25 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 2: Shear Force Calculations** | Location | Calculation | SF (kN) | |---|---|---| | Just right of $A$ | $+R_A$ | $+26.25$ | | Just left of 20 kN load | $R_A$ | $+26.25$ | | Just right of 20 kN load | $26.25 - 20$ | $+6.25$ | | Just left of 30 kN load | $6.25$ | $+6.25$ | | Just right of 30 kN load | $6.25 - 30$ | $-23.75$ | | Just left of $B$ | $-23.75$ | $-23.75$ | | Just right of $B$ | $-23.75 + 23.75 = 0$ | $0$ ✓ | **Step 3: Bending Moment Calculations** | Location | Calculation | BM (kN·m) | |---|---|---| | At $A$ ($x=0$) | $0$ | $0$ | | At 20 kN load ($x=2$) | $26.25 \times 2$ | $+52.5$ | | At 30 kN load ($x=5$) | $52.5 + 6.25 \times 3$ | $+71.25$ | | At $B$ ($x=8$) | $71.25 + (-23.75) \times 3$ | $0$ ✓ | **Step 4: Identify Maximum** Since SF changes from $+6.25$ to $-23.75$ at $x = 5 \text{ m}$ (passing through zero), the maximum BM occurs at $x = 5 \text{ m}$: $$M_{\max} = 71.25 \text{ kN·m}$$ **Verification using superposition:** $$M_{\max} = \frac{20 \times 2 \times 6}{8} + \frac{30 \times 5 \times 3}{8} = \frac{240}{8} + \frac{450}{8} = 30 + 56.25 = 86.25$$ Wait — this needs rechecking. Let me recompute. The maximum moment might not be at $x = 5$ if SF doesn't change sign there. Let me verify: SF just left of $x=5$ is $+6.25$ (positive), and just right of $x=5$ is $-23.75$ (negative). So SF **does** change sign, and maximum BM is indeed at $x = 5 \text{ m}$. Recomputing using superposition formula correctly: - Load 1: $P_1 = 20$, $a_1 = 2$, $b_1 = 6$ - Load 2: $P_2 = 30$, $a_2 = 5$, $b_2 = 3$ At $x = 5$: $$M = \frac{P_1 a_1 b_1}{L} + \frac{P_2 a_2 b_2}{L} = \frac{20 \cdot 2 \cdot 6}{8} + \frac{30 \cdot 5 \cdot 3}{8}$$ $$= \frac{240}{8} + \frac{450}{8} = 30 + 56.25 = 86.25 \text{ kN·m}$$ There's a discrepancy with the earlier direct calculation. Let me recheck the direct calculation. Direct method: - At $x = 5$: We need moment considering forces to the left of $x = 5$ - $M(5) = R_A \cdot 5 - 20 \cdot (5-2)$ - $M(5) = 26.25 \times 5 - 20 \times 3 = 131.25 - 60 = 71.25 \text{ kN·m}$ But superposition gives 86.25. There's an inconsistency. Let me check. **Verification of superposition formula application:** The formula $M = \frac{Pab}{L}$ gives BM at the location of the load when it's the only load. When multiple loads exist, we sum the BM contributions at each point. At $x = 5$ (where load 2 is): - Contribution from load 1 alone: $M_1 = \frac{20 \cdot 2 \cdot 6}{8} = 30 \text{ kN·m}$ (this is the BM that load 1 alone would create at $x=5$) - Contribution from load 2 alone: $M_2 = \frac{30 \cdot 5 \cdot 3}{8} = 56.25 \text{ kN·m}$ (this is the BM that load 2 alone would create at its own location $x=5$) Total: $30 + 56.25 = 86.25 \text{ kN·m}$ But direct calculation from the left gives: $M(5) = R_A \cdot 5 - 20 \cdot (5-2) = 26.25 \times 5 - 60 = 131.25 - 60 = 71.25$ This must equal the moment computed from the right: $M(5) = R_B \cdot 3 = 23.75 \times 3 = 71.25 \text{ kN·m}$ ✓ (matches from right) So direct calculation gives 71.25, but superposition gives 86.25. Which is correct? Let me recheck superposition. The superposition formula $\frac{Pab}{L}$ applies when the BM is calculated **at the point of load application** for a single load. For load 2 at $x=5$, the contribution is correctly $\frac{30 \cdot 5 \cdot 3}{8} = 56.25$. But for load 1's contribution at $x=5$ (which is **not** at load 1's location $x=2$), we cannot use the same formula directly. For load 1 alone, at section $x = 5$ (which is in the right segment, $x > a_1 = 2$): $$M_1(5) = R_A^{(1)} \cdot 5 - P_1 \cdot (5-2) = \frac{P_1 b_1}{L} \cdot 5 - P_1 \cdot 3$$ $$M_1(5) = \frac{20 \cdot 6}{8} \cdot 5 - 20 \cdot 3 = \frac{120}{8} \cdot 5 - 60 = 15 \cdot 5 - 60 = 75 - 60 = 15 \text{ kN·m}$$ So the correct contribution from load 1 at $x=5$ is **15**, not 30. The formula $\frac{Pab}{L}$ only gives the moment **at the load location**, not at arbitrary sections. Total: $15 + 56.25 = 71.25 \text{ kN·m}$ ✓ **Correction noted**: This is a common KTU exam pitfall — using $\frac{Pab}{L}$ at locations other than the load point. The correct method is to use either direct integration from one end, or compute each load's contribution at the desired section using reactions specific to that section. So the final answer is: $$M_{\max} = 71.25 \text{ kN·m at } x = 5 \text{ m}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Error**: Students often incorrectly apply the formula $\frac{Pab}{L}$ at every section. This formula gives BM **only at the load point**. For other sections, you must use $M(x) = \sum (\text{force} \times \text{distance})$ from one side. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1: Simply Supported Beam with Central Point Load — Free Body, SFD, BMD ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FBD[Free Body Diagram] A1[Pin Support A
RA upward] --> B1[Beam length L
Load P at center
midspan x = L/2] B1 --> C1[Roller Support B
RB upward] end subgraph SFD[Shear Force Diagram] A2[x = 0
V = +P/2] --> B2[x = L/2
Vertical drop of P] B2 --> C2[x = L
V = -P/2] end subgraph BMD[Bending Moment Diagram] A3[x = 0
M = 0] --> B3[x = L/2
Peak Mmax = PL/4] B3 --> C3[x = L
M = 0] end ``` **Visual Description**: - **FBD**: Horizontal line representing beam, with upward arrows $R_A$ and $R_B$ at the ends, downward arrow $P$ at midspan. - **SFD**: Rectangular pulse — positive constant $P/2$ from $A$ to midspan, then negative constant $-P/2$ from midspan to $B$. - **BMD**: Triangular shape, zero at supports, peak $PL/4$ at midspan (sagging). --- ## 4.2 Diagram 2: Overhanging Beam with Load at Free End — SF and BM ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph OVFBD[Overhanging Beam FBD] SA[Pin A] --> SB[Support B at x=L] SB --> SC[Free end C at x = L+a] SC --> LD[Downward load P at C] RA[RA acts DOWN
magnitude Pa/L] -.-> SA RB[RB acts UP
magnitude PL+Pa over L] -.-> SB end subgraph OVBMD[BMD for Overhang] ptA[M = 0 at A] --> ptMid[Linear decrease
to M = -Pa at B] ptMid --> ptC[M = 0 at C
linear recovery] end ``` **Visual Description**: - **Beam**: Extends from $A$ to $B$ (span $L$), then continues to $C$ (overhang $a$). - **Loads**: $P$ downward at free end $C$. - **Reactions**: $R_A$ acts **downward** (anchoring force), $R_B$ acts upward and is larger than $P$. - **SFD**: Between supports, $V = -\frac{Pa}{L}$ (negative constant). In overhang, $V = +P$ (positive constant, since only $P$ is to the right). - **BMD**: Linear from $M = 0$ at $A$ down to $M = -Pa$ at $B$, then linear from $-Pa$ at $B$ back to $0$ at $C$. **All negative (hogging) between supports**. --- ## 4.3 Diagram 3: Block Architecture — Robotic Arm as a Simply Supported Beam ```mermaid flowchart TB SH[Shoulder Joint
Pin Support
Acts as RA] -->|Arm Link
Beam Length L| WR[Wrist Joint
Roller Support
Acts as RB] WR --> EF[End Effector
at midspan
Acts as load P] SF1[SFD shows peak V = P/2
at both supports] -.-> SH SF2[BMD shows peak M = PL/4
at end effector] -.-> EF DEF[Deflection at end effector
delta_max = PL^3 / 48EI] -.-> WR ``` **Interpretation**: In a robotic pick-and-place arm, the shoulder and wrist joints act as supports. The payload at the end effector is the central load. Maximum BM occurs at the end effector location, governing the design of the arm cross-section to prevent yielding. --- ## 4.4 Diagram 4: Comparison Table of Beam Configurations (Block Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR SSB[SSB with Central Load] -->|Mmax = PL/4
Vmax = P/2| SSBresult[Sagging BM only
Triangular BMD] SSBECC[SSB with Eccentric Load] -->|Mmax = Pab/L| SSBeccresult[Triangular BMD
peak at load point] OV[Overhang with Load at Tip] -->|Mmax = Pa| OVresult[Hogging BM at B
Negative between supports] OVMID[Overhang with Load Between Supports] -->|Mmax = Pab/L| OVMIDresult[Positive BM in span
Zero BM in overhang] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: Define "Point of Contraflexure" in a beam. Under what loading conditions does it occur in an overhanging beam?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **point of contraflexure** (also called a point of inflection) is a section in a beam where the **bending moment changes sign** — i.e., the curvature of the beam changes from sagging (concave up) to hogging (concave down), or vice versa. Mathematically, it is the location where $M(x) = 0$ and $\dfrac{dM}{dx}$ changes sign. In an **overhanging beam**, a point of contraflexure occurs in the span between supports when there are **simultaneous loads**: - A load between the supports (causing positive/sagging BM), AND - A load on the overhang (causing negative/hogging BM at the support) This creates a region where BM transitions from negative (near the loaded support) to positive (under the span load), passing through zero at the **point of contraflexure**. *(Award: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for sign change condition, 1 mark for loading scenario)* --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: A simply supported beam of span 6 m carries a point load of 30 kN at 2 m from the left support. Calculate the maximum bending moment.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Given: $L = 6 \text{ m}$, $P = 30 \text{ kN}$, $a = 2 \text{ m}$, $b = L - a = 4 \text{ m}$. **Step 1: Reactions** $$R_A = \frac{Pb}{L} = \frac{30 \times 4}{6} = 20 \text{ kN}$$ $$R_B = \frac{Pa}{L} = \frac{30 \times 2}{6} = 10 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 2: Maximum BM (at load point)** $$M_{\max} = \frac{Pab}{L} = \frac{30 \times 2 \times 4}{6} = \boxed{40 \text{ kN·m}}$$ *(Award: 1 mark for reaction calculation, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for final answer)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: A simply supported beam AB of span 10 m carries two point loads of 40 kN and 60 kN at distances of 3 m and 7 m from the left support A, respectively. Draw the Shear Force Diagram (SFD) and Bending Moment Diagram (BMD). Also find the position and magnitude of the maximum bending moment.** **Model Solution (Step-by-Step, 14 marks):** **Part (a): Reaction Calculation [4 marks]** Taking moments about $A$: $$R_B \times 10 = 40 \times 3 + 60 \times 7$$ $$R_B \times 10 = 120 + 420 = 540$$ $$R_B = 54 \text{ kN}$$ Vertical equilibrium: $$R_A = (40 + 60) - 54 = 46 \text{ kN}$$ **Part (b): Shear Force Calculation [4 marks]** | Section | Calculation | SF (kN) | |---|---|---| | Just right of $A$ | $R_A$ | $+46$ | | Just left of 40 kN | $46$ | $+46$ | | Just right of 40 kN | $46 - 40$ | $+6$ | | Just left of 60 kN | $6$ | $+6$ | | Just right of 60 kN | $6 - 60$ | $-54$ | | At $B$ | $-54 + 54 = 0$ | $0$ ✓ | **Part (c): Bending Moment Calculation [4 marks]** | Section | Calculation | BM (kN·m) | |---|---|---| | At $A$ ($x=0$) | $0$ | $0$ | | At 40 kN ($x=3$) | $46 \times 3$ | $+138$ | | At 60 kN ($x=7$) | $138 + 6 \times 4$ | $+162$ | | At $B$ ($x=10$) | $162 + (-54) \times 3$ | $0$ ✓ | **Part (d): Maximum BM Identification [2 marks]** Since SF changes sign from $+6$ to $-54$ at $x = 7 \text{ m}$, the maximum BM occurs at $x = 7 \text{ m}$. $$\boxed{M_{\max} = 162 \text{ kN·m at } x = 7 \text{ m from } A}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: An overhanging beam ABC has supports at A and B, with A and B separated by 6 m. The beam overhangs beyond B by 2 m to the free end C. A point load of 50 kN acts downward at the free end C, and another point load of 30 kN acts downward at 4 m from A (i.e., 2 m to the left of B). Draw the SFD and BMD. Locate the point of contraflexure, if any.** **Model Solution (Step-by-Step, 14 marks):** **Part (a): Reaction Calculation [4 marks]** Total span $A$ to $B$ = 6 m, overhang $B$ to $C$ = 2 m. Total length from $A$ to $C$ = 8 m. Taking moments about $A$: $$R_B \times 6 = 30 \times 4 + 50 \times 8$$ $$R_B \times 6 = 120 + 400 = 520$$ $$R_B = \frac{520}{6} = 86.67 \text{ kN}$$ Vertical equilibrium: $$R_A = (30 + 50) - 86.67 = 80 - 86.67 = -6.67 \text{ kN}$$ (Negative sign means $R_A$ acts **downward**, i.e., the support holds the beam down.) **Part (b): Shear Force Calculation [5 marks]** | Section | Calculation | SF (kN) | |---|---|---| | Just right of $A$ | $R_A$ | $-6.67$ | | Just left of 30 kN | $-6.67$ | $-6.67$ | | Just right of 30 kN | $-6.67 - 30$ | $-36.67$ | | Just left of $B$ | $-36.67$ | $-36.67$ | | Just right of $B$ | $-36.67 + 86.67$ | $+50$ | | At $C$ | $50 - 50 = 0$ | $0$ ✓ | **Part (c): Bending Moment Calculation [3 marks]** | Section | Calculation | BM (kN·m) | |---|---|---| | At $A$ ($x=0$) | $0$ | $0$ | | At 30 kN ($x=4$) | $-6.67 \times 4$ | $-26.67$ | | At $B$ ($x=6$) | $-26.67 + (-36.67) \times 2$ | $-100$ | | At $C$ ($x=8$) | $-100 + 50 \times 2$ | $0$ ✓ | **Part (d): Point of Contraflexure [2 marks]** In the region between $A$ and $B$, BM varies linearly from $0$ at $A$ to $-26.67$ at $x=4$, then continues to $-100$ at $B$. Since BM is negative throughout, **there is no point of contraflexure between the supports** in this configuration (the 30 kN load is not strong enough to overcome the negative moment caused by the 50 kN overhang load). **Summary**: - $\boxed{M_{\max} = -100 \text{ kN·m at support } B \text{ (hogging)}}$ - $\boxed{V_{\max} = +50 \text{ kN in overhang region}}$ - No point of contraflexure exists in the span. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Sign Convention Mistakes**: Students frequently forget that overhanging beam supports can have **downward reactions**. Always show the direction in your FBD. > 2. **BMD Continuity**: BMD must have zero slope at free ends and free slope (linear) where point loads exist. Discontinuous slopes mean calculation errors. > 3. **Point of Contraflexure**: Don't confuse with "point of maximum BM." The former is where $M = 0$ with sign change; the latter is where $V = 0$. > 4. **Multiple Loads**: For SSBs with multiple loads, plot SF and BM at **every load location and at every support** — missing sections lose 2–3 marks. > 5. **Units**: Always include units (kN, kN·m) in the final answer. Missing units costs 0.5 marks per occurrence. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exams** - [ ] **Sign Convention**: Positive SF → clockwise rotation tendency; Positive BM → sagging (smile shape, concave up). - [ ] **SSB Reactions**: Always sum to total downward load ($R_A + R_B = \sum P$). - [ ] **SSB with Central Load**: $M_{\max} = \dfrac{PL}{4}$, $V_{\max} = \dfrac{P}{2}$. - [ ] **SSB with Eccentric Load**: $R_A = \dfrac{Pb}{L}$, $R_B = \dfrac{Pa}{L}$, $M_{\max} = \dfrac{Pab}{L}$ **at the load point**. - [ ] **Overhang with Load at Free End**: $R_A$ acts **downward** if $a > 0$; $M_B = -Pa$ (hogging). - [ ] **Key Relationships**: $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$ and $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V(x)$ (with appropriate sign convention). - [ ] **Maximum BM Location**: At the section where SF changes sign (crosses zero). - [ ] **Point of Contraflexure**: Section where BM = 0 and changes sign — occurs only with mixed positive/negative loading. - [ ] **BMD Verification**: Always check $M = 0$ at free ends and roller supports; check $M$ values match from both sides at any section. - [ ] **SFD Verification**: SF must return to zero at the rightmost free end (or end of beam). - [ ] **Robotic Engineering Context**: Beam theory underpins robotic arm link design, joint stress analysis, and end-effector deflection calculations. - [ ] **Common Pitfall**: The formula $\dfrac{Pab}{L}$ gives BM **only at the load point** — not at arbitrary sections. Use direct moment summation for general sections. - [ ] **Units Mastery**: $P$ in kN, $L$ in m, $V$ in kN, $M$ in kN·m. Mixing units loses marks.

topics.title: Uniformly Distributed Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT525 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS OF ROBOTIC PARTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Uniformly Distributed Loads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL)** is a loading condition in structural mechanics where the magnitude of the applied force remains **constant per unit length** along the entire span (or over a defined region) of a structural member. In the context of *Structural Analysis of Robotic Parts*, UDLs typically model the **self-weight of manipulator links**, payloads spread across gripper jaws, distributed cable/harness loads on robot arms, and inertial loads due to gravity acting on long, slender robot links.

Mathematically, the intensity of the UDL is expressed as:

$$w = \frac{dW}{dx} = \text{constant} \quad [\text{N/m}]$$

where **w** is the load intensity (force per unit length), **W** is the total resultant load, and **x** is the position along the member.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** UDLs form the foundational loading case for deriving shear force and bending moment diagrams (SFBD & BMD) in robotic link analysis. Mastery of UDL analysis is a prerequisite for tackling combined, triangular, and trapezoidal loadings in subsequent modules.

> [!NOTE]
> **S.I. Unit Reminder:** The standard unit for UDL intensity is **N/m (Newton per meter)** in the SI system. In some KTU problems, **kN/m** is also used for convenience. Always keep units consistent when computing reactions and internal forces.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine holding a **long, hollow cardboard tube horizontally with both hands** (similar to a robot arm in its extended position). The weight of the cardboard is not concentrated at a single point; instead, every small segment of the tube pulls downward with its own tiny weight. If you were to "sum" all these tiny weights across the entire length, you would get a **single equivalent downward force** acting at the **midpoint** of the tube.

This is exactly the philosophy behind a UDL:

- **Physically:** Gravity acts on every infinitesimal slice of the robot link.
- **Mathematically:** We replace this continuous distribution with a **single resultant point load** for easier computation.
- **Position:** The resultant acts at the **centroid** of the load diagram — for a uniform rectangle, this is the geometric center.

A robotic manipulator arm in a static, horizontal pose experiences an almost perfect UDL due to its self-weight. This is why understanding UDLs is critical for designing **deflection-resistant, vibration-stable** robot structures.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Robotic Application:** Industrial robotic arms (e.g., KUKA, ABB, FANUC) often have their link cross-sections engineered as **box girders** or **I-beams** specifically to resist the UDL created by their own self-weight during horizontal extension. Failure to account for UDL-induced bending moments leads to **tip deflection errors** and reduced positional accuracy.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Breaking Down the UDL

When analyzing a UDL on a structural member (beam/link), the analysis follows a structured five-step logical framework:

- **Step 1 — Identify the Load Function:**
  Determine whether the load is truly uniform (`w = constant`) or if it varies (triangular, trapezoidal). For pure UDL, the load function is a **horizontal line** on the loading diagram.

- **Step 2 — Compute the Resultant Force:**
  The total equivalent point load is the **area under the load-intensity diagram**:
  $$W = w \times L$$
  This is essentially integrating the constant load function over the length `L`.

- **Step 3 — Locate the Centroid (Line of Action):**
  For a uniform rectangular load diagram, the centroid lies at the **geometric center** of the loaded region:
  $$\bar{x} = \frac{L}{2} \quad (\text{measured from either support})$$

- **Step 4 — Apply Equilibrium Equations:**
  Use **ΣF_y = 0** and **ΣM = 0** to determine support reactions. For a simply supported beam:
  $$R_A = R_B = \frac{wL}{2}$$

- **Step 5 — Derive Internal Force Functions:**
  Cut the beam at a section `x` and apply equilibrium to the free body. This yields:
  - Shear Force: $V(x) = R_A - wx$
  - Bending Moment: $M(x) = R_A \cdot x - \frac{wx^2}{2}$

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind the Steps

- **Why replace UDL with a point load?** → Because the resultant force and its location completely capture the **external loading effect** on the structure for static analysis.
- **Why centroid at L/2?** → Symmetry of the rectangular load diagram implies the "balance point" of all the tiny loads is the midpoint.
- **Why is V(x) linear and M(x) parabolic?** → The shear force is the integral of the load function (constant integrated = linear), and the bending moment is the integral of shear (linear integrated = parabolic). This relationship, $\frac{dV}{dx} = -w$ and $\frac{dM}{dx} = V$, is a **cornerstone of KTU structural analysis problems**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The table below contains every UDL-related equation you will need for PERAT525 Module 2. Memorize the *form* of each equation — the numerical substitutions are mechanical.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Applicable Condition |
|---|----------|---------|-------|----------------------|
| 1 | Load Intensity | $w = \text{constant}$ | N/m | Full span UDL |
| 2 | Resultant Force | $W = w \cdot L$ | N | Total downward force |
| 3 | Centroid Location | $\bar{x} = L / 2$ | m | Symmetric UDL on simply supported beam |
| 4 | Reaction (Simply Supported) | $R_A = R_B = wL / 2$ | N | Both ends pinned/roller |
| 5 | Reaction (Cantilever, fixed at A) | $R_A = wL, M_A = wL^2 / 2$ | N, N·m | Fixed-free beam |
| 6 | Shear Force at section x | $V(x) = w(L/2 - x)$ | N | Simply supported, x from left |
| 7 | Bending Moment at section x | $M(x) = \frac{wx}{2}(L - x)$ | N·m | Simply supported |
| 8 | Max Bending Moment | $M_{\max} = wL^2 / 8$ | N·m | At midspan (x = L/2) |
| 9 | Max Shear Force | $V_{\max} = wL / 2$ | N | At supports |
| 10 | Slope at Supports (SS beam) | $\theta = wL^3 / 24EI$ | rad | Elastic curve analysis |
| 11 | Deflection at Midspan (SS beam) | $\delta_{\max} = 5wL^4 / 384EI$ | m | Elastic curve analysis |
| 12 | Strain Energy Stored | $U = w^2 L^5 / 240EI$ | J | Castigliano's theorem applications |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Convention:** Always label diagrams showing `R_A`, `R_B`, the UDL rectangle, and the equivalent point load `W = wL` at the **midspan**. Examiners award partial marks specifically for **clean, well-labeled free body diagrams (FBDs)**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **robotic structural design**, UDL analysis directly informs:

- **Link Sizing:** Determines the cross-sectional moment of inertia `I` required to keep deflection within ±0.1 mm tolerance.
- **Servo Motor Selection:** The bending moment `M_max` translates into the torque the joint motor must counteract.
- **Fatigue Life Prediction:** Repeated lifting cycles create *cyclic* UDLs; analysis feeds into S-N curve-based life estimation.
- **Vibration Analysis:** The natural frequency of a robotic link under UDL is $f_n = \frac{4.73^2}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{EI}{wL^4}}$, critical for avoiding resonance.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Reactions for a Simply Supported Beam under Full-Span UDL

**Problem Setup:** A horizontal robotic link of length `L` is simply supported at `A` (x = 0) and `B` (x = L). A uniform load `w` (N/m) acts downward over the entire span.

**Free Body Diagram (Conceptual):** Vertical reactions `R_A` (upward) at A and `R_B` (upward) at B balance the UDL.

**Step 1 — Apply Vertical Force Equilibrium (ΣF_y = 0):**

$$\sum F_y = 0 \implies R_A + R_B - wL = 0$$

This says: total upward forces must equal total downward load (which is the area `w × L` of the UDL rectangle).

**Step 2 — Apply Moment Equilibrium about Point A (ΣM_A = 0):**

Taking counterclockwise as positive, and recalling the UDL resultant `wL` acts at `L/2`:

$$\sum M_A = 0 \implies R_B \cdot L - (wL) \cdot \frac{L}{2} = 0$$

Solving for `R_B`:

$$R_B \cdot L = \frac{wL^2}{2}$$

$$R_B = \frac{wL}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Back to Find R_A:**

$$R_A = wL - R_B = wL - \frac{wL}{2} = \frac{wL}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** By symmetry, both reactions are equal. This is expected because the loading is symmetric about the midspan.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Shear Force Equation V(x)

**Step 1 — Make a Section Cut at Distance x from A:**

Consider the left portion of the beam (from 0 to x). On the free body, forces acting are:
- Upward reaction `R_A = wL/2`
- Downward UDL over length `x`, with resultant `w·x` acting at `x/2`
- Internal shear force `V(x)` acting on the cut face

**Step 2 — Apply Vertical Equilibrium to the Left FBD:**

$$R_A - wx - V(x) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for V(x):**

$$V(x) = R_A - wx = \frac{wL}{2} - wx$$

$$V(x) = w\left(\frac{L}{2} - x\right)$$

**Step 4 — Identify Key Values:**

- At $x = 0$: $V(0) = wL/2 = V_{\max}^+$
- At $x = L$: $V(L) = wL/2 - wL = -wL/2 = V_{\max}^-$
- At $x = L/2$: $V(L/2) = 0$ (point of contraflexure... wait, no, point of **zero shear**)

> [!TIP]
> The location of **zero shear** is where **maximum bending moment** occurs. For UDL on a simply supported beam, this is always at midspan.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Bending Moment Equation M(x)

**Step 1 — Sum Moments about the Section Cut (x) for the Left FBD:**

Taking counterclockwise moments about the cut section:

$$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - (wx) \cdot \frac{x}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute R_A = wL/2:**

$$M(x) = \frac{wL}{2} \cdot x - \frac{wx^2}{2}$$

$$M(x) = \frac{w}{2}\left(Lx - x^2\right)$$

$$M(x) = \frac{wx}{2}(L - x)$$

**Step 3 — Find Maximum Moment (dM/dx = 0):**

Differentiating:

$$\frac{dM}{dx} = \frac{w}{2}(L - 2x) = 0$$

$$L - 2x = 0 \implies x = \frac{L}{2}$$

Substituting back:

$$M_{\max} = \frac{w}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{2} \cdot \left(L - \frac{L}{2}\right) = \frac{w}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{2}$$

$$M_{\max} = \frac{wL^2}{8}$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Cantilever Beam with Full UDL (Robotic Arm Fixed at Base)

This is **highly relevant** for robotic arms where the base is fixed (like a bolted mounting).

**Setup:** Fixed at A (x = 0), free at B (x = L), UDL `w` over full length.

**Step 1 — Reactions at Fixed End:**

Vertical equilibrium: $R_A = wL$

Moment equilibrium about A: $M_A = wL \cdot \frac{L}{2} = \frac{wL^2}{2}$

**Step 2 — Shear Force at Section x (measuring from free end for simplicity):**

Let `x'` be distance from the free end. Then:

$$V(x') = wx'$$

**Step 3 — Bending Moment at Section x':**

$$M(x') = -\frac{w(x')^2}{2}$$

(Negative sign indicates **hogging moment**, characteristic of cantilevers.)

**Step 4 — Maximum Values (at fixed end, x' = L):**

$$V_{\max} = wL$$

$$M_{\max} = -\frac{wL^2}{2}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often forget the sign convention for cantilever moments. A UDL on a cantilever produces a **hogging (negative) moment** at the fixed end, which is *opposite* in sign to a simply supported beam. Always show the sagging/hogging curvature in your sketch.

### 3.5 Summary Matrix of Results

| Beam Type | Reaction(s) | Max Shear | Max Moment | Location of M_max |
|-----------|-------------|-----------|------------|-------------------|
| Simply Supported (full UDL) | $R_A = R_B = wL/2$ | $wL/2$ | $wL^2/8$ | At midspan (x = L/2) |
| Cantilever (full UDL, fixed at A) | $R_A = wL$, $M_A = wL^2/2$ | $wL$ | $wL^2/2$ | At fixed end A |
| Propped Cantilever (full UDL) | $R_A = 3wL/8$, $R_B = 5wL/8$ | $5wL/8$ | $9wL^2/128$ | At x = 3L/8 from A |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: UDL Analysis Workflow for Robotic Link

The following flowchart illustrates the complete analytical pipeline a KTU student should follow when given a robotic link under a UDL.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Identify Beam Type and Supports] --> B[Step 2: Plot the UDL Diagram w over L]
    B --> C[Step 3: Compute Resultant W = w times L]
    C --> D[Step 4: Locate Centroid at L by 2]
    D --> E[Step 5: Draw Free Body Diagram with Reactions]
    E --> F[Step 6: Apply Sigma F vertical equals 0]
    F --> G[Step 7: Apply Sigma M equals 0]
    G --> H[Step 8: Solve for Support Reactions R sub A and R sub B]
    H --> I[Step 9: Make Section Cut at distance x]
    I --> J[Step 10: Derive V of x and M of x]
    J --> K[Step 11: Identify V max and M max]
    K --> L[Step 12: Plot SFD and BMD]
    L --> M[Step 13: Check Deflection if Required]
    M --> N[Step 14: Validate Robotic Link Design]
    N --> O[OUTPUT: Final Reaction and Moment Values]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of UDL in a Robotic Manipulator

This block diagram maps how a UDL manifests in a physical robot system and how each subsystem responds to it.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STRUCTURAL_INPUT[STRUCTURAL INPUT ZONE]
        A1[Robot Link Beam Model]
        A2[Payload at End Effector]
        A3[Self Weight Distribution]
    end

    subgraph UDL_PROCESSING[UDL PROCESSING ZONE]
        B1[Compute Load Intensity w in N per m]
        B2[Integrate to find W]
        B3[Locate Centroid L by 2]
    end

    subgraph INTERNAL_FORCE_ZONE[INTERNAL FORCE ZONE]
        C1[Reactions R sub A and R sub B]
        C2[Shear Force V of x]
        C3[Bending Moment M of x]
    end

    subgraph DESIGN_OUTPUT[DESIGN OUTPUT ZONE]
        D1[Motor Torque Selection]
        D2[Cross Section Sizing]
        D3[Deflection and Stiffness Check]
        D4[Fatigue Life Estimation]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    B3 --> C3
    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> D2
    C3 --> D3
    C2 --> D4
    C3 --> D4
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Cantilever Robotic Arm Under UDL

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Fixed Base Mounting Point A] --> P2[Vertical Reaction R sub A equals wL upward]
    P1 --> P3[Fixing Moment M sub A equals wL squared by 2]
    P4[UDL w N per m] --> P5[Acts on entire link length L]
    P5 --> P6[Resultant W equals wL at L by 2]
    P6 --> P7[Free End B experiences Max Shear wL]
    P6 --> P8[Fixed End A experiences Max Moment wL squared by 2]
    P7 --> P9[Critical Section for Shear at Fixed End]
    P8 --> P10[Critical Section for Moment at Fixed End]
    P9 --> P11[Design Hub Bolts and Motor Bracket]
    P10 --> P11
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to Read These Diagrams:** Each rectangular block represents a computational or physical entity. Arrows denote data/force flow. The three-zone architecture (Input → Processing → Output) is a **standard systems-engineering pattern** and aligns with the NEP 2020 outcome-based education framework used by KTU.

---
