# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the terms 'point of inflexion' and 'point of contraflexure' as applied to the elastic curve of a beam. How are they related to the bending moment diagram?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **point of inflexion** is a point on the elastic (deflected) shape of a beam where the curvature of the curve changes sign; mathematically, the second derivative of the deflection $y$ with respect to $x$ is zero and reverses sign, i.e., $\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = 0$ with a sign change. **(1 mark)**

A **point of contraflexure** is a point on the beam where the bending moment changes its algebraic sign, i.e., the bending-moment diagram crosses the zero line, indicating a transition from sagging to hogging curvature or vice-versa. **(1 mark)**

**Relationship:** Since the governing equation is $M(x) = EI\,\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}}$, every point where the BM changes sign corresponds to a point where the elastic-curve curvature changes sign. Therefore, every point of contraflexure is also a point of inflexion, and the two concepts coincide at points where the BM diagram crosses the axis in fixed or continuous beams. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**A simply supported beam of span 6 m carries a uniformly distributed load of 20 kN/m over its entire length. State with reasons whether the beam has any point of contraflexure.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement:** The simply supported beam has **no point of contraflexure**. **(1 mark)**

**Reasoning:** For a simply supported beam with UDL, the bending-moment expression is $M(x) = \dfrac{wx}{2}(L - x)$, which is a single-signed positive (sagging) parabola reaching a maximum of $wL^{2}/8$ at mid-span. **(1 mark)**

Since the BM does not change sign anywhere in the span (it is zero only at the two simple supports, where there is no reversal of the curvature), the condition for a point of contraflexure — $M = 0$ with a *sign change* — is not satisfied in the interior. Hence, the beam exhibits a single-curvature deflected shape and has no point of contraflexure. **(1 mark)**

---

## Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Note (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Module 2 carries 50 marks (typically one 14-mark question + an 8-mark question). The 14-mark question below is *internal-choice* style: students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**A fixed beam AB of span 6 m is subjected to a uniformly distributed load of 30 kN/m over the entire span. (a) Determine the fixed-end moments and the reactions. (b) Locate the points of contraflexure and the points of maximum bending moment, and sketch the bending moment diagram.**

#### Part (a) — Fixed-End Moments and Reactions (7 marks)

**Step 1:** By symmetry, $V_A = V_B$ and the fixed-end moments are equal in magnitude and opposite in sense.

$$
V_A = V_B = \dfrac{wL}{2} = \dfrac{30 \times 6}{2} = 90 \text{ kN} \uparrow
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** Standard formula for FEM of a fixed beam with UDL:

$$
M_{\text{FAB}} = -\dfrac{wL^{2}}{12} = -\dfrac{30 \times 6^{2}}{12} = -90 \text{ kN·m}
$$

$$
M_{\text{FBA}} = +\dfrac{wL^{2}}{12} = +90 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**(2 marks)** *(negative indicates hogging at A; positive indicates sagging-reaction moment at B)*

**Step 3:** Reactions: $R_A = R_B = 90$ kN upward. End moments are as above. **(4 marks for full setup, sign convention, and stating the FEM formula used)**

#### Part (b) — Location of Points of Contraflexure and Maximum BM (7 marks)

**Step 1:** BM at any section $x$ from A:

$$
M(x) = M_A + V_A \cdot x - \dfrac{wx^{2}}{2} = -90 + 90x - 15x^{2} \quad (\text{kN·m})
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 2:** Set $M(x) = 0$:

$$
-90 + 90x - 15x^{2} = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 15x^{2} - 90x + 90 = 0
$$

$$
x^{2} - 6x + 6 = 0
$$

$$
x = \dfrac{6 \pm \sqrt{36 - 24}}{2} = \dfrac{6 \pm \sqrt{12}}{2} = 3 \pm \sqrt{3}
$$

$$
x_{1} = 3 - 1.732 = 1.268 \text{ m}, \quad x_{2} = 6 - 1.268 = 4.732 \text{ m}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 3:** Verify by symmetry: $x_1 + x_2 = 6$ m ✓ and $x_2 - x_1 = 3.464 \approx 2\sqrt{3}$ ✓.

> **The points of contraflexure are at $x = 1.268$ m and $x = 4.732$ m from A.** **(1 mark)**

**Step 4:** Maximum sagging BM occurs at midspan where $dM/dx = 0$ (i.e., $V = 0$):

$$
\dfrac{dM}{dx} = 90 - 30x = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad x = 3 \text{ m}
$$

$$
M_{\max,\text{sag}} = -90 + 90(3) - 15(3)^{2} = -90 + 270 - 135 = +45 \text{ kN·m}
$$

Maximum hogging BM at supports: $|M_{\text{support}}| = 90$ kN·m. **(1 mark)**

**Step 5:** BMD sketch — parabolic, hogging at both ends ($\pm 90$ kN·m), sagging in the middle ($+45$ kN·m), zero crossings at $x = 1.268$ m and $x = 4.732$ m. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**A beam of span 8 m is fixed at end A and simply supported at end B (propped cantilever). It carries a uniformly distributed load of 20 kN/m over the entire span. (a) Determine the fixed-end moment at A and the reaction at B. (b) Locate the point of contraflexure and compute the maximum positive and negative bending moments in the span.**

#### Part (a) — Fixed-End Moment at A and Reaction at B (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Use the standard formula for a propped cantilever with UDL. The fixed-end moment at A (hogging) is:

$$
M_A = -\dfrac{wL^{2}}{8} = -\dfrac{20 \times 8^{2}}{8} = -160 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 2:** Reaction at B (prop) using equilibrium $\sum M_A = 0$:

$$
R_B \cdot L - wL \cdot \dfrac{L}{2} - |M_A| = 0
$$

$$
R_B \cdot 8 = 20 \times 8 \times 4 - 160
$$

$$
R_B = \dfrac{640 - 160}{8} = 60 \text{ kN} \uparrow
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 3:** Reaction at A:

$$
R_A = wL - R_B = 20 \times 8 - 60 = 100 \text{ kN} \uparrow
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 4:** Verification of vertical equilibrium: $100 + 60 = 160 = 20 \times 8$ ✓. Moment equilibrium about B also satisfies the boundary condition. **(2 marks)**

#### Part (b) — Point of Contraflexure and Maximum Moments (7 marks)

**Step 1:** BM expression measured from the fixed end A:

$$
M(x) = M_A + R_A \cdot x - \dfrac{wx^{2}}{2} = -160 + 100x - 10x^{2} \quad (\text{kN·m})
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 2:** Set $M(x) = 0$:

$$
10x^{2} - 100x + 160 = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad x^{2} - 10x + 16 = 0
$$

$$
x = \dfrac{10 \pm \sqrt{100 - 64}}{2} = \dfrac{10 \pm 6}{2}
$$

$$
x_{1} = 2 \text{ m}, \quad x_{2} = 8 \text{ m (the prop itself, not interior)}
$$

**(2 marks)**

> **The point of contraflexure lies at $x = 2$ m from the fixed end A.** **(1 mark)**

**Step 3:** Maximum sagging BM where $V = 0$:

$$
\dfrac{dM}{dx} = 100 - 20x = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad x = 5 \text{ m}
$$

$$
M_{\max,\text{sag}} = -160 + 100(5) - 10(25) = -160 + 500 - 250 = +90 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 4:** Maximum hogging BM at the fixed end A: $|M_A| = 160$ kN·m. The BM at the prop B is zero by definition of simple support. **(1 mark)**

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "point of zero BM" with "point of contraflexure".** A point of zero BM at a *simple support* or *free end* is **NOT** a contraflexure unless the BM *changes sign* across it. Examiners explicitly deduct 1 mark for this error. **(High-frequency mistake)**
>
> 2. **Using the approximate value $L/4$ for fixed-beam-with-UDL contraflexure.** The *exact* KTU-expected value is $0.2113L$. Using $L/4 = 0.25L$ is acceptable only if the problem says "approximate" — otherwise, expect a 0.5–1 mark deduction.
>
> 3. **Sign-convention error for $M_A$ of fixed beam.** Writing $M_A = +wL^{2}/12$ instead of $-wL^{2}/12$ (hogging) flips the entire BMD and the location of contraflexures. Always state: *sagging is positive, hogging is negative*.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to check that the root lies *within* the span.** Sometimes the quadratic gives a root outside $[0, L]$ — that is a mathematical root but not a *physical* point of contraflexure. Always state the range check.
>
> 5. **Not verifying the sign reversal.** Setting $M = 0$ is necessary but not sufficient. Show $M(0)$ and $M(L/2)$ with opposite signs to *prove* the contraflexure exists.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Point of Inflexion** = geometric event on the elastic curve where $d^{2}y/dx^{2} = 0$ and changes sign.
- **Point of Contraflexure** = static event on the BMD where $M = 0$ and changes sign.
- **Bridge Equation:** $M = EI\,y''$ links both; a contraflexure is *always* an inflexion, but not vice-versa.
- **Existence Criterion:** Contraflexure requires the BMD to cross the zero axis in the *interior* of the span — typical of fixed, propped, and continuous beams.
- **Simply supported and cantilever beams (with single-sign loading) have NO contraflexure.**
- **Standard KTU results to memorise:**
  - Fixed beam + UDL → $x_c = 0.2113L$ and $0.7887L$ (exact); $\approx L/4$ and $3L/4$ (approximate).
  - Fixed beam + central point load → $x_c = L/4$ and $3L/4$ (exact).
  - Propped cantilever + UDL → $x_c = 0.5785L$ from fixed end (i.e., $L(1 - 1/\sqrt{5})$).
  - Two-span continuous + UDL → four contraflexures at $0.207L$, $0.5L$, $0.5L$, $0.793L$ from outer ends.
- **Slope at contraflexure is extremum** (max or min of $dy/dx$) — a useful verification.
- **At a contraflexure, $M = 0 \Rightarrow$ strain $\varepsilon = 0$** at the extreme fibre level; useful for sensor placement avoidance.
- **Engineering applications:** plastic-hinge design, crash energy absorption, tapered robotic links, manipulator compliance modelling.
- **Always show:** BM expression, $M = 0$ quadratic/linear solution, sign-change verification, and physical interpretation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Theories of Failure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT525 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS OF ROBOTIC PARTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Theories of Failure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theories of Failure (Static Failure Theories / Strength Theories):**
> A set of hypothesis-based engineering criteria that predict the conditions under which a ductile or brittle material, subjected to a complex multi-axial state of stress, will either yield (begin permanent plastic deformation) or fracture (completely separate). Since uniaxial tensile test data is the easiest to obtain experimentally, these theories mathematically extrapolate that single-axis strength data to predict the safe working stresses under **combined / multi-axial loading** — exactly the kind of loading found at robot joints, gear teeth, link pins, gripper fingers, and wrist actuators.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, these theories are mapped to **Course Outcome CO2**: *"Apply the concepts of stress, strain and failure theories to analyse robotic structural components under combined loading."*

### 1.2 The Need for Theories of Failure — The Engineering Problem

Most **Strength of Materials** experiments are conducted using simple uniaxial tension or compression tests on standard specimens (cylinders, flat bars). From these tests, we obtain only **one** material property per test:

| Test Performed | Property Obtained | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Uniaxial Tension | Ultimate Tensile Strength | $\sigma_t$ |
| Uniaxial Compression | Ultimate Compressive Strength | $\sigma_c$ |
| Uniaxial Tension | Yield Strength | $\sigma_y$ or $S_y$ |
| Uniaxial Tension | Elastic Modulus & Poisson's Ratio | $E,\ \mu$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **But real robotic components never experience pure uniaxial stress!** A robot arm link carries bending + torsion simultaneously. A gripper jaw experiences combined bending + direct shear. We **cannot** test every possible combination. So engineers devised mathematical hypotheses that connect *multi-axial stress* back to *uniaxial test data*.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Banana Test" 🍌

Imagine you have a bunch of bananas. You want to know when they will **fail (split open or bruise)**.

- You could simply pull one end (uniaxial tension) → tells you the *pulling* strength.
- You could twist one end (pure torsion) → tells you the *twisting* strength.
- But what if you **pull AND twist** at the same time? Will the banana fail faster? How much faster?

**Theories of failure are like "calibrated recipes"** that tell us: *"If I know how the banana breaks under simple pulling, and I know the combined pull+twist forces I am applying right now, I can predict whether it will fail."* Each theory is a different recipe — some focus on the maximum pulling force inside (Rankine), some on the maximum twist (Tresca), some on the total energy stored (Total Strain Energy).

### 1.4 Classification of Static Failure Theories

The KTU syllabus classifies them into two broad material families:

**A. Failure Theories for Ductile Materials (e.g., structural steel, aluminium alloys used in robot links):**
1. Maximum Principal Stress Theory (Rankine, 1858)
2. Maximum Principal Strain Theory (Saint-Venant, 1837)
3. Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca, 1864)
4. Total Strain Energy Theory (Haigh, 1924)
5. Distortion Energy Theory (von Mises, 1913 / Hencky, 1924)

**B. Failure Theories for Brittle Materials (e.g., cast iron, ceramic gears):**
1. Coulomb-Mohr Theory (modified Mohr)
2. Modified Mohr Theory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for Robot Designers:** Robot arms and grippers are mostly made of ductile alloys (Al 6061-T6, AISI 4140 steel). Therefore, for **PERAT525**, the ductile failure theories — especially **Maximum Shear Stress (Tresca)** and **Distortion Energy (von Mises)** — are the most heavily tested in KTU exams.

### 1.5 Standard Symbols Used Throughout

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value (Steel) |
|---|---|---|
| $\sigma_1,\ \sigma_2,\ \sigma_3$ | Principal stresses (algebraically ordered) | MPa |
| $\sigma_t$ | Ultimate tensile strength | 400–600 MPa |
| $\sigma_c$ | Ultimate compressive strength | ≈ $\sigma_t$ for ductile |
| $\sigma_y$ or $S_y$ | Yield strength in tension | 250 MPa (mild steel) |
| $n$ | Factor of Safety (FoS) | 1.5 – 4 (robotics) |
| $E$ | Young's Modulus | 200 GPa |
| $\mu$ | Poisson's Ratio | 0.3 (steel) |
| $\tau_y$ | Yield strength in pure shear | $\approx 0.577 \sigma_y$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Pre-Requisite: The Stress Element

Consider a 2-D stress element taken from a robot arm at a critical cross-section (say, near a bolted flange).

The element has:
- Normal stresses: $\sigma_x,\ \sigma_y$
- Shear stress: $\tau_{xy}$

By solving the **characteristic equation** of the stress tensor, the **principal stresses** are obtained:

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}
$$

In 3-D, an additional principal stress $\sigma_3$ exists (out-of-plane). Algebraic ordering convention:

$$
\sigma_1 \ge \sigma_2 \ge \sigma_3
$$

And the **maximum shear stress** in 3-D is:

$$
\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}
$$

## 2.2 Theory 1: Maximum Principal Stress Theory (Rankine's Theory)

### Core Hypothesis
> *"Failure (yield or fracture, whichever occurs first) occurs when the maximum principal stress in a complex stress system reaches the strength limit obtained from a simple uniaxial test."*

### Failure Criterion

$$
\sigma_1 \ge \sigma_t \quad \text{or} \quad \vert \sigma_3 \vert \ge \sigma_c
$$

### Design Equation (with Factor of Safety $n$)

$$
\sigma_1 \le \frac{\sigma_t}{n} \quad \text{and} \quad \vert \sigma_3 \vert \le \frac{\sigma_c}{n}
$$

### Step-by-Step Logic
1. Compute all three principal stresses from the applied loading.
2. Identify $\sigma_1$ (largest tensile) and $\sigma_3$ (smallest / most compressive).
3. Compare each independently against the material's tensile/compressive limits.
4. The material is **safe** if **both** inequalities are satisfied.

### Why / How It Works
- It is the **oldest** and **simplest** theory — essentially says "tension cracks open, compression crushes."
- Best suited for **brittle materials** (cast iron, concrete, ceramics).
- **Disadvantage:** It completely ignores the intermediate principal stress $\sigma_2$ and the shear stresses — yet experiments show shears play a huge role in ductile yielding.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget to apply Rankine to **both** tensile and compressive sides. For a ductile material where $\sigma_t = \sigma_c$, this is fine. But for **cast iron** used in some robot gear housings, $\sigma_c \gg \sigma_t$, and the compressive side governs.

## 2.3 Theory 2: Maximum Principal Strain Theory (Saint-Venant's Theory)

### Core Hypothesis
> *"Failure occurs when the maximum principal strain in the complex state reaches the strain at failure in uniaxial testing."*

### Strain Expressions (generalised Hooke's law, 3-D)

$$
\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_1 - \mu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)\right]
$$
$$
\varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_2 - \mu(\sigma_3 + \sigma_1)\right]
$$
$$
\varepsilon_3 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_3 - \mu(\sigma_1 + \sigma_2)\right]
$$

### Failure Criterion

$$
\varepsilon_1 \ge \varepsilon_{tu} \quad \text{or} \quad \vert \varepsilon_3 \vert \ge \varepsilon_{cu}
$$

where $\varepsilon_{tu} = \sigma_t / E$ and $\varepsilon_{cu} = \sigma_c / E$.

### Design Equation (with FoS $n$)

$$
\sigma_1 - \mu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3) \le \frac{\sigma_t}{n}
$$
$$
\sigma_3 - \mu(\sigma_1 + \sigma_2) \ge -\frac{\sigma_c}{n}
$$

### Limitations
- Over-predicts failure strength for ductile materials.
- Not commonly used in modern design but appears in KTU theory questions.

## 2.4 Theory 3: Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca's Theory) ⭐ KTU Favourite

### Core Hypothesis
> *"Failure by yielding occurs when the maximum shear stress in the complex state equals the maximum shear stress at yielding in a uniaxial tension test."*

### Geometric Insight
The maximum shear stress in 3-D occurs on planes oriented at 45° to $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_3$, and equals half the difference of these two extreme principal stresses.

### Failure Criterion

$$
\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} \ge \frac{\sigma_y}{2}
$$

### Design Equation (with FoS $n$)

$$
\frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} \le \frac{\sigma_y}{2n} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \sigma_1 - \sigma_3 \le \frac{\sigma_y}{n}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $\sigma_3$ and not $\sigma_2$?** Because Tresca's 3-D hypothesis explicitly considers only the **algebraically largest** and **algebraically smallest** principal stresses. The middle stress $\sigma_2$ is **completely ignored** — this is the theory's main weakness compared to von Mises.

### Application Example (Robotics)
A robot wrist pin (made of AISI 1045 steel, $\sigma_y = 310$ MPa) carries a combined stress state with $\sigma_1 = 180$ MPa, $\sigma_2 = 50$ MPa, $\sigma_3 = -60$ MPa. Required FoS = 2.

By Tresca:
$$
\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = 180 - (-60) = 240 \text{ MPa}
$$
$$
\sigma_y / n = 310 / 2 = 155 \text{ MPa}
$$

Since $240 > 155$ → **UNSAFE.** Designer must increase pin diameter.

## 2.5 Theory 4: Total Strain Energy Theory (Haigh's Theory)

### Core Hypothesis
> *"Failure occurs when the total strain energy per unit volume in the complex stress state equals the strain energy per unit volume at yielding in uniaxial tension."*

### Strain Energy Expression
The total strain energy density in 3-D principal stresses:

$$
U = \frac{1}{2E}\left[\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\mu(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1)\right]
$$

### Failure Criterion

$$
U \ge \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}
$$

### Design Equation (with FoS $n$)

$$
\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\mu(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1) \le \left(\frac{\sigma_y}{n}\right)^2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention Note:** In the above, we have used the principal stresses as scalars (signs preserved). The original Haigh formulation works with both tensile and compressive yield. Strictly, the uniaxial strain energy $\sigma_y^2/2E$ assumes the uniaxial test is in tension (positive).

## 2.6 Theory 5: Distortion Energy Theory (von Mises / Hencky) ⭐⭐ MOST IMPORTANT

### Core Hypothesis
> *"Failure occurs when the distortion strain energy (the energy associated with shape change only, excluding volume change) equals the distortion strain energy at yielding in uniaxial tension."*

### Why This Theory Wins
The total strain energy has **two parts**:
1. **Volumetric (dilatational) energy** — energy stored due to change in *volume*.
2. **Distortional energy** — energy stored due to change in *shape*.

For ductile metals, **only the distortional energy causes yielding.** Volume change alone (pure hydrostatic pressure) does not cause yielding — even at enormous pressures, a steel sphere does not yield permanently.

This makes the von Mises criterion **physically the most accurate** for ductile materials.

### Failure Criterion (3-D)

$$
\frac{1}{2}\left[(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2\right] \ge \sigma_y^2
$$

### Design Equation (with FoS $n$)

$$
(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2 \le 2\left(\frac{\sigma_y}{n}\right)^2
$$

### Simplified 2-D Form (Most Used in KTU Problems)

$$
\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 - \sigma_x \sigma_y + \sigma_y^2 + 3\tau_{xy}^2}
$$

Design Equation:

$$
\sigma_{VM} \le \frac{\sigma_y}{n}
$$

### Special Cases of von Mises

| Loading | von Mises Equivalent Stress |
|---|---|
| Uniaxial Tension ($\sigma_1 = \sigma$) | $\sigma_1$ |
| Pure Shear ($\tau$) | $\sqrt{3}\,\tau$ |
| Biaxial Equal ($\sigma_1 = \sigma_2 = \sigma$) | $\sigma$ |
| Biaxial Opposite ($\sigma_1 = -\sigma_2 = \sigma$) | $\sqrt{3}\,\sigma$ |

## 2.7 Theory 6: Mohr's Theory (Modified Mohr)

### Core Hypothesis
> *"Failure occurs when, on Mohr's circle, the largest stress circle is tangent to, or exceeds, the failure envelope of the material drawn from uniaxial tension and compression tests."*

### Failure Criterion

$$
\frac{\sigma_1}{\sigma_t} - \frac{\sigma_3}{\sigma_c} \ge \frac{1}{n}
$$

### Design Equation

$$
\frac{\sigma_1}{\sigma_t} - \frac{\sigma_3}{\sigma_c} \le \frac{1}{n}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For ductile materials where $\sigma_t = \sigma_c$, Mohr's theory **reduces exactly to Tresca's theory.** For brittle materials where $\sigma_c > \sigma_t$, it is more accurate than Rankine. This makes it a useful **unified criterion** spanning both material families.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Theory Name | Design Equation (with FoS $n$) | Best For | Material Class |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rankine (Max Principal Stress) | $\sigma_1 \le \dfrac{\sigma_t}{n}$ and $\vert\sigma_3\vert \le \dfrac{\sigma_c}{n}$ | Brittle | Brittle |
| 2 | Saint-Venant (Max Principal Strain) | $\sigma_1 - \mu(\sigma_2+\sigma_3) \le \dfrac{\sigma_t}{n}$ | Brittle | Brittle |
| 3 | **Tresca (Max Shear Stress)** | $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 \le \dfrac{\sigma_y}{n}$ | Ductile | Ductile |
| 4 | Haigh (Total Strain Energy) | $\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\mu(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1) \le \left(\dfrac{\sigma_y}{n}\right)^2$ | Ductile (over-conservative) | Ductile |
| 5 | **von Mises (Distortion Energy)** | $\dfrac{1}{2}\left[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\right] \le 2\left(\dfrac{\sigma_y}{n}\right)^2$ | Ductile (most accurate) | Ductile |
| 6 | Mohr (Unified) | $\dfrac{\sigma_1}{\sigma_t} - \dfrac{\sigma_3}{\sigma_c} \le \dfrac{1}{n}$ | Both | Brittle + Ductile |

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility (Robotics Context)

In a **6-DOF industrial robot arm**, every link experiences a unique multi-axial stress state depending on its pose and payload. Failure theories enable the design engineer to:

1. **Size cross-sections** of links, pins, shafts, and bolts under combined bending + torsion.
2. **Select appropriate FoS** — typically 1.5 to 3 for steady loads, 4 to 6 for cyclic/fatigue.
3. **Choose materials** — ductile alloys allow the use of von Mises (lighter designs); brittle castings require Rankine/Mohr (more conservative).
4. **Validate FEA results** — ANSYS outputs von Mises equivalent stress by default; engineers compare this to $\sigma_y/n$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of von Mises Criterion from Strain Energy Decomposition

This is the most rigorous derivation and is a **high-yield topic** for KTU 14-mark questions.

### Starting Point: Total Strain Energy Density in 3-D Principal Coordinates

The total strain energy per unit volume in a linear elastic material under principal stresses is:

$$
U_{total} = \frac{1}{2E}\left[\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\mu(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1)\right]
$$

### Step 1 — Decompose Into Mean (Hydrostatic) + Deviatoric Stresses

Define the **mean stress** (pure volumetric component):

$$
\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3}{3}
$$

Define the **stress deviators** (pure shape-change components):

$$
\sigma_1' = \sigma_1 - \sigma_m, \quad \sigma_2' = \sigma_2 - \sigma_m, \quad \sigma_3' = \sigma_3 - \sigma_m
$$

By construction, the deviators sum to zero:

$$
\sigma_1' + \sigma_2' + \sigma_3' = (\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3) - 3\sigma_m = 0
$$

### Step 2 — Volumetric Strain Energy Density

A pure hydrostatic stress $\sigma_m$ acting on all three axes causes only volumetric strain:

$$
U_v = \frac{3(1-2\mu)}{2E}\sigma_m^2
$$

### Step 3 — Distortional Strain Energy Density

The distortional energy is the total minus the volumetric:

$$
U_d = U_{total} - U_v
$$

After algebraic expansion (which we do explicitly below):

$$
U_{total} = \frac{1}{2E}\left[\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\mu(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1)\right]
$$

Expanding $U_v$ in terms of principal stresses:

$$
U_v = \frac{1-2\mu}{6E}\left(\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3\right)^2
$$

Subtracting:

$$
U_d = U_{total} - U_v = \frac{1+\mu}{3E}\left[\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - \sigma_1\sigma_2 - \sigma_2\sigma_3 - \sigma_3\sigma_1\right]
$$

The full algebraic reduction:

$$
U_d = \frac{1+\mu}{6E}\left[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\right]
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Yielding Hypothesis

For uniaxial tension at yield: $\sigma_1 = \sigma_y$, $\sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$.

The distortional energy at yield:

$$
U_{d,yield} = \frac{1+\mu}{6E}\left[(\sigma_y-0)^2 + (0-0)^2 + (0-\sigma_y)^2\right] = \frac{1+\mu}{6E}\left[2\sigma_y^2\right] = \frac{1+\mu}{3E}\sigma_y^2
$$

### Step 5 — Failure Criterion

Setting $U_d = U_{d,yield}$:

$$
\frac{1+\mu}{6E}\left[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\right] = \frac{1+\mu}{3E}\sigma_y^2
$$

Canceling $\dfrac{1+\mu}{E}$ from both sides:

$$
\frac{1}{6}\left[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\right] = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{3}
$$

Multiplying both sides by 6:

$$
(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2 = 2\sigma_y^2
$$

### Step 6 — Design Equation with Factor of Safety $n$

Replacing $\sigma_y$ with $\sigma_y/n$:

$$
(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2 \le 2\left(\frac{\sigma_y}{n}\right)^2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intermediate algebraic step verified:** The expansion $(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2 = 2(\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - \sigma_1\sigma_2 - \sigma_2\sigma_3 - \sigma_3\sigma_1)$ is a standard identity. This is what enabled the cancellation in Step 5.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of 2-D von Mises Formula

For a **plane stress** element with $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, $\tau_{xy}$, the third principal stress $\sigma_3 = 0$ (out-of-plane, no stress).

The other two principal stresses (from the characteristic equation):

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}
$$

Plugging $\sigma_3 = 0$ into the 3-D von Mises formula:

$$
(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_1^2 = 2\sigma_y^2
$$

Expanding the left side:

$$
\sigma_1^2 - 2\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 = 2\sigma_y^2
$$
$$
2\sigma_1^2 + 2\sigma_2^2 - 2\sigma_1\sigma_2 = 2\sigma_y^2
$$
$$
\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - \sigma_1\sigma_2 = \sigma_y^2
$$

Now, using the identity $\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y$ and $\sigma_1\sigma_2 = \dfrac{(\sigma_x+\sigma_y)^2 - (\sigma_x-\sigma_y)^2}{4} - \tau_{xy}^2$, after reduction:

$$
\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - \sigma_1\sigma_2 = \sigma_x^2 - \sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y^2 + 3\tau_{xy}^2
$$

Therefore:

$$
\sigma_x^2 - \sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y^2 + 3\tau_{xy}^2 = \sigma_y^2
$$

The **von Mises equivalent stress** in 2-D:

$$
\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 - \sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y^2 + 3\tau_{xy}^2}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — KTU Typical 14-Mark Pattern

**Problem:** A robot manipulator link made of mild steel has the following state of stress at the critical point: $\sigma_x = 80$ MPa, $\sigma_y = -40$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = 30$ MPa. Yield strength $\sigma_y = 250$ MPa.

Determine the factor of safety using:
**(a)** Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca)
**(b)** Distortion Energy Theory (von Mises)

### Step A — Compute Principal Stresses

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}
$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{80 + (-40)}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{80 - (-40)}{2}\right)^2 + (30)^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{40}{2} \pm \sqrt{(60)^2 + (30)^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = 20 \pm \sqrt{3600 + 900}
$$

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = 20 \pm \sqrt{4500}
$$

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = 20 \pm 67.082
$$

Final principal stresses:

$$
\sigma_1 = 20 + 67.082 = 87.082 \text{ MPa}
$$
$$
\sigma_2 = 0 \text{ MPa (plane stress, out-of-plane)}
$$
$$
\sigma_3 = 20 - 67.082 = -47.082 \text{ MPa}
$$

Algebraic ordering: $\sigma_1 \ge \sigma_2 \ge \sigma_3$ ⇒ $87.082 \ge 0 \ge -47.082$ ✓

### Part (a) — Tresca Solution

Tresca design equation:

$$
\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \frac{\sigma_y}{n}
$$

$$
87.082 - (-47.082) = \frac{250}{n}
$$

$$
134.164 = \frac{250}{n}
$$

$$
n_{Tresca} = \frac{250}{134.164} = 1.863
$$

### Part (b) — von Mises Solution

Two methods — using 2-D formula directly (since $\sigma_3 = 0$ plane stress is implicit):

Method 1 — 2-D Direct:

$$
\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 - \sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y^2 + 3\tau_{xy}^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{(80)^2 - (80)(-40) + (-40)^2 + 3(30)^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{6400 + 3200 + 1600 + 2700}
$$

$$
\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{13900} = 117.898 \text{ MPa}
$$

Then:

$$
\sigma_{VM} = \frac{\sigma_y}{n_{VM}} \Rightarrow n_{VM} = \frac{250}{117.898} = 2.120
$$

Method 2 — 3-D Formula verification:

$$
\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\frac{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2}{2}}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{\frac{(87.082)^2 + (47.082)^2 + (134.164)^2}{2}}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{\frac{7583.27 + 2216.71 + 17999.97}{2}} = \sqrt{13900} = 117.898 \text{ MPa} \checkmark
$$

### Comparison Table

| Theory | FoS Computed | Comment |
|---|---|---|
| Tresca | **1.863** | More conservative |
| von Mises | **2.120** | More accurate, less conservative |
| Difference | ~13.8% | von Mises allows ~14% higher load |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** In KTU exam answers, always state **"von Mises gives a higher (more economical) factor of safety than Tresca. Since von Mises is more accurate for ductile materials, we adopt $n = 2.12$."**

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Theory Selection Decision Flowchart Logic

| Question | Decision Path |
|---|---|
| Is the material **ductile** (mild steel, Al, Cu)? | → Use **von Mises** (preferred) or Tresca |
| Is the material **brittle** (cast iron, ceramic)? | → Use **Rankine** or **Coulomb-Mohr** |
| Is the loading **uniaxial** only? | → Any theory gives same answer; skip theory |
| Is $\sigma_2 = 0$ (plane stress)? | → Use the **2-D von Mises** formula directly |
| Is the loading **cyclic/fatigue**? | → Static theories are **not valid**; use Soderberg/Goodman |
| Is the FoS computed **< 1**? | → **IMMEDIATE FAILURE** — redesign required |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Master Schematic — All Failure Theories in One View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Complex Multi-Axial Stress State<br/>sigma_x, sigma_y, tau_xy]) --> Compute[Compute Principal Stresses<br/>sigma1, sigma2, sigma3]
    
    Compute --> Material{Material Type?}
    
    Material -->|Ductile| Ductile[Use Ductile Theories]
    Material -->|Brittle| Brittle[Use Brittle Theories]
    
    Ductile --> D1[Rankine Theory<br/>sigma1 ≤ sigma_t/n]
    Ductile --> D2[Saint-Venant<br/>Max Strain]
    Ductile --> D3[Tresca<br/>sigma1 - sigma3 ≤ sigma_y/n]
    Ductile --> D4[Haigh<br/>Total Strain Energy]
    Ductile --> D5[von Mises<br/>Distortion Energy ⭐]
    
    Brittle --> B1[Rankine Theory<br/>sigma1 ≤ sigma_t/n]
    Brittle --> B2[Coulomb-Mohr<br/>sigma1/sigma_t - sigma3/sigma_c ≤ 1/n]
    
    D1 --> Compare1[Compare Each Result<br/>with Allowable Stress]
    D2 --> Compare1
    D3 --> Compare1
    D4 --> Compare1
    D5 --> Compare1
    B1 --> Compare1
    B2 --> Compare1
    
    Compare1 --> Safety{Is FoS > Required?}
    Safety -->|Yes| Safe([SAFE - Design Acceptable])
    Safety -->|No| Fail([UNSAFE - Redesign Required])
    
    style Start fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D5 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px
    style D3 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px

    style Safe fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style Fail fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENVELOPE[Material Failure Envelope in sigma-tau Plane]
        direction TB
        T[Ultimate Tensile Strength sigma_t] --- P1((sigma_t, 0))
        P1 --- AR1[Rankine Tangent Line]
        AR1 --- P2((sigma_c, 0))
        P2 --- C[Ultimate Compressive Strength sigma_c]
        AR1 -.->|Mohr Criterion| M1((sigma1, tau1))
        M1 -.->|Operating Mohr Circle| M2((sigma3, 0))
    end

```

## 4.3 Von Mises vs Tresca — Yield Surface Comparison (2-D Plot Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph YIELD[Yield Locus in sigma1-sigma2 Plane]
        direction LR
        HM[Hydrostatic Axis<br/>sigma1 = sigma2]
        VM[von Mises Yield Locus<br/>Ellipse<br/>sigma1^2 - sigma1 sigma2 + sigma2^2 = sigma_y^2]
        TR[tresca Yield Locus<br/>Hexagon<br/>Inscribed in von Mises Ellipse]
    end
    
    TR -.->|Tresca inside ellipse| VM
    VM -.->|von Mises is the smooth<br/>outer boundary| TR
    
    style VM fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px
    style TR fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
    style HM fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The von Mises ellipse is the **outer boundary** of the elastic region; Tresca's hexagon is **inscribed within it.** This geometric fact is the visual proof that **von Mises always predicts a higher (less conservative) failure load than Tresca** — the ellipse encloses more "safe" stress combinations.

## 4.4 Robot Component — Application Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROBOT[6-DOF Industrial Robot] --> BASE[Base Mount]
    ROBOT --> J1[Shoulder Joint J1]

    ROBOT --> LINK2[Forearm Link]
    ROBOT --> WRIST[Wrist + End Effector]
    
    BASE -.->|Combined: Bending + Direct Stress| FT1[Use von Mises<br/>FoS = 2.5]
    J1 -.->|Torsion + Axial| FT2[Use Tresca<br/>FoS = 3.0]
    LINK1 -.->|Bending + Torsion + Axial| FT3[Use von Mises<br/>FoS = 2.0]
    J2 -.->|Pin Shear + Bearing| FT4[Use Tresca<br/>FoS = 4.0]
    LINK2 -.->|Buckling + Bending| FT5[Use Euler + von Mises]
```
