# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**"State the Distortion Energy Theory of failure and write its design equation for a 3-D stress state with factor of safety $n$."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement:** Failure by yielding occurs when the distortion strain energy (energy associated with shape change) per unit volume in a complex stress state equals the distortion strain energy per unit volume at yielding in a uniaxial tensile test.

**Design Equation (3-D, with FoS $n$):**

$$
(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2 \le 2\left(\frac{\sigma_y}{n}\right)^2
$$

[1 mark statement + 2 marks equation — full marks]

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
**"Differentiate between the Maximum Shear Stress Theory and the Distortion Energy Theory of failure."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Tresca (Max Shear Stress) | von Mises (Distortion Energy) |
|---|---|---|
| Hypothesis | Based on max shear stress in element | Based on distortion strain energy |
| Considers $\sigma_2$? | **No** — ignores middle principal stress | **Yes** — uses all three principal stresses |
| Yield surface | Hexagonal (6-sided) | Smooth ellipse |
| Conservatism | More conservative (lower FoS) | Less conservative (higher FoS) |
| Accuracy | ~15% over-conservative for ductile | Closely matches experimental data |
| Formula | $\sigma_1-\sigma_3 \le \sigma_y/n$ | Complex (see Q1) |

[1 mark per major distinguishing point × 3 = full marks]

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students must answer ONE full question from a choice of TWO. Each question is 14 marks with internal sub-parts of 7+7 marks.**

---

### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

**A robot manipulator link made of mild steel ($\sigma_y = 250$ MPa) is subjected at a critical section to the following biaxial stress state: $\sigma_x = 120$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 60$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = 45$ MPa.**

**(a) [7 marks]** Determine the factor of safety using **(i) Maximum Shear Stress Theory** and **(ii) Distortion Energy Theory**.

**(b) [7 marks]** If the material were changed to a brittle cast iron ($\sigma_t = 200$ MPa, $\sigma_c = 600$ MPa), determine the factor of safety using **Coulomb-Mohr Theory** and compare with the Rankine Theory.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

### Part (a) — Solution

**Step 1: Compute principal stresses**

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{120 + 60}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{120 - 60}{2}\right)^2 + 45^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = 90 \pm \sqrt{30^2 + 45^2} = 90 \pm \sqrt{900 + 2025} = 90 \pm \sqrt{2925}
$$

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = 90 \pm 54.083
$$

$$
\sigma_1 = 144.083 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 = 35.917 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_3 = 0 \text{ MPa (plane stress)}
$$

**Step 2(a-i): Tresca Theory**

$$
\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = 144.083 - 0 = 144.083 \text{ MPa} = \frac{250}{n_{Tresca}}
$$

$$
n_{Tresca} = \frac{250}{144.083} = \boxed{1.735}
$$

**Step 2(a-ii): von Mises Theory (2-D direct formula)**

$$
\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 - \sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y^2 + 3\tau_{xy}^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{120^2 - (120)(60) + 60^2 + 3(45)^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{14400 - 7200 + 3600 + 6075}
$$

$$
\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{16875} = 129.904 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
n_{vonMises} = \frac{250}{129.904} = \boxed{1.924}
$$

**Comparison:** von Mises gives higher FoS (1.924) than Tresca (1.735). Since von Mises is more accurate for ductile mild steel, we adopt $n = 1.924$. **Design is SAFE** for a typical robotics FoS requirement of 1.5.

### Part (b) — Solution

Now the material is **brittle cast iron**: $\sigma_t = 200$ MPa, $\sigma_c = 600$ MPa.

**Note:** $\sigma_c/\sigma_t = 3$, so the strength asymmetry matters!

**Step 1: Coulomb-Mohr Theory**

$$
\frac{\sigma_1}{\sigma_t} - \frac{\sigma_3}{\sigma_c} \le \frac{1}{n}
$$

For plane stress, $\sigma_3 = 0$ (out-of-plane). Therefore:

$$
\frac{\sigma_1}{\sigma_t} \le \frac{1}{n}
$$

$$
n_{Mohr} = \frac{\sigma_t}{\sigma_1} = \frac{200}{144.083} = \boxed{1.388}
$$

**Step 2: Rankine Theory**

Rankine uses **both** tensile and compressive limits independently:

Tensile side check:
$$
\sigma_1 \le \frac{\sigma_t}{n} \Rightarrow n_t = \frac{\sigma_t}{\sigma_1} = \frac{200}{144.083} = 1.388
$$

Compressive side check:
$$
\sigma_3 \le \frac{\sigma_c}{n} \Rightarrow n_c = \frac{\sigma_c}{\vert\sigma_3\vert} = \frac{600}{0} = \infty \text{ (not governing)}
$$

(Plane stress has $\sigma_3 = 0$, so compression side never governs.)

Therefore $n_{Rankine} = 1.388$.

**Comparison Table (Part b):**

| Theory | FoS |
|---|---|
| Coulomb-Mohr | 1.388 |
| Rankine | 1.388 |
| (Tresca from part a, for reference) | 1.735 |

**Conclusion:** For brittle cast iron under this loading, both Rankine and Coulomb-Mohr predict the same FoS (since $\sigma_3 = 0$). The FoS is much lower than for ductile mild steel (1.735), reflecting the lower tensile strength of cast iron.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. Students frequently forget the **algebraic ordering** of principal stresses ($\sigma_1 \ge \sigma_2 \ge \sigma_3$) before plugging into Tresca. Reversing $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_3$ will give the **wrong sign** and a wrong FoS.
> 2. Many students apply von Mises 3-D formula incorrectly when given 2-D data. **Always check if $\sigma_3 = 0$ (plane stress) — use the simplified 2-D formula directly.**
> 3. In Coulomb-Mohr, the signs matter! $\sigma_3$ must be entered with its algebraic sign (negative for compression). Using magnitudes will give incorrect results.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE): **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain **Saint-Venant's Maximum Principal Strain Theory** of failure. A steel component in a robot gripper has a 2-D stress state with $\sigma_x = 100$ MPa, $\sigma_y = -50$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = 0$ MPa. Given $E = 200$ GPa, $\mu = 0.3$, $\sigma_t = 400$ MPa, $\sigma_c = 400$ MPa. Find the FoS.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the **Distortion Energy Theory (von Mises)** criterion for a general 3-D stress state, starting from the total strain energy density expression. Clearly state all assumptions.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

### Part (a) — Solution

**Statement of Saint-Venant's Theory:**

> Failure occurs when the maximum principal strain in the complex state equals the strain at failure in a uniaxial test.

**Design Equations:**

$$
\varepsilon_1 \le \frac{\varepsilon_{tu}}{n} \Rightarrow \sigma_1 - \mu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3) \le \frac{\sigma_t}{n}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_3 \ge -\frac{\varepsilon_{cu}}{n} \Rightarrow \sigma_3 - \mu(\sigma_1 + \sigma_2) \ge -\frac{\sigma_c}{n}
$$

**Step 1: Since $\tau_{xy} = 0$, the principal stresses are simply the normal stresses:**

$$
\sigma_1 = 100 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 = 0 \text{ MPa (plane stress)}, \quad \sigma_3 = -50 \text{ MPa}
$$

Verification of ordering: $100 \ge 0 \ge -50$ ✓

**Step 2: Apply tensile side equation:**

$$
\sigma_1 - \mu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3) \le \frac{\sigma_t}{n}
$$

$$
100 - 0.3(0 + (-50)) \le \frac{400}{n}
$$

$$
100 + 15 \le \frac{400}{n}
$$

$$
115 \le \frac{400}{n} \Rightarrow n \le \frac{400}{115} = 3.478
$$

**Step 3: Apply compressive side equation:**

$$
\sigma_3 - \mu(\sigma_1 + \sigma_2) \ge -\frac{\sigma_c}{n}
$$

$$
-50 - 0.3(100 + 0) \ge -\frac{400}{n}
$$

$$
-50 - 30 \ge -\frac{400}{n}
$$

$$
-80 \ge -\frac{400}{n} \Rightarrow 80 \le \frac{400}{n} \Rightarrow n \le 5.000
$$

**Step 4: Governing FoS**

The **lower** of the two governs:

$$
n_{SV} = \min(3.478,\ 5.000) = \boxed{3.478}
$$

### Part (b) — Solution (Von Mises Derivation Summary)

This is the complete derivation as in Section 3.1. The student must present:

1. **Starting Equation** — Total strain energy in 3-D principal stresses.
2. **Hydrostatic Decomposition** — Define $\sigma_m$ and deviatoric stresses.
3. **Compute $U_v$** — Show the volumetric energy term.
4. **Compute $U_d$** — Algebraic reduction to get $U_d$.
5. **Evaluate $U_{d,yield}$** — Substitute $\sigma_1 = \sigma_y$, $\sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$.
6. **Equate** $U_d = U_{d,yield}$ and simplify to obtain:

$$
(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2 = 2\sigma_y^2
$$

7. **Final Design Equation** with FoS $n$:

$$
(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2 \le 2\left(\frac{\sigma_y}{n}\right)^2
$$

**Assumptions:**
- Material is linear elastic, isotropic, homogeneous.
- Material is ductile (yields before fracture).
- Volume change does not contribute to yielding.
- No residual stresses.
- Static (not fatigue) loading.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning for Part (b):**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention the assumption** that volumetric strain energy does not cause yielding. This is the **physical heart** of von Mises theory. 2 marks lost if missed.
> 2. **Skipping the algebraic cancellation step** — Examiners expect to see Step 4 and Step 5 worked out explicitly.
> 3. **Final equation must be the design form** with $(1/n^2)$ factor, not the failure form. Many students write the failure equation and forget the FoS.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER ⚡

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

- [ ] Always compute and **algebraically order** principal stresses ($\sigma_1 \ge \sigma_2 \ge \sigma_3$) **before** applying any theory.
- [ ] **Plane stress** assumption: $\sigma_3 = 0$ — use the simplified 2-D von Mises formula:
  $$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 - \sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y^2 + 3\tau_{xy}^2}$$
- [ ] **Rankine** is for **brittle materials**; **Tresca & von Mises** are for **ductile materials**.
- [ ] **Von Mises is the most accurate** ductile failure theory and the **most frequently tested**.
- [ ] **Tresca ignores $\sigma_2$** — gives **lower** (more conservative) FoS than von Mises.
- [ ] **Mohr's Theory** unifies brittle & ductile cases: for $\sigma_t = \sigma_c$, it reduces to Tresca.
- [ ] **Factor of Safety $n$ is applied to the strength, not the load.** Compute $(σ_{1} - σ_{3})$, then divide $\sigma_y$ by $n$ (NOT multiply the stresses by $n$).
- [ ] **Strain Energy Theories assume linear elastic behaviour** — they are not valid for stresses above the proportional limit.
- [ ] **For robotics components** (ductile alloys), always prefer **von Mises** in your final answer.
- [ ] **Cast iron / ceramics** (brittle) — use **Rankine** or **Coulomb-Mohr**; $\sigma_c \gg \sigma_t$.
- [ ] The **sign convention for σ₃ in Coulomb-Mohr** must be algebraic: tensile = positive, compressive = negative. Plug the magnitude of $\sigma_3/\sigma_c$ with the minus sign as written in the formula.
- [ ] **Two-dimensional Mohr's circle transformation equations** can be used to verify principal stress calculations.
- [ ] **FoS < 1** means the component will fail in service. FoS between 1 and 1.5 is risky. **Aim for 2.0 to 4.0** in robotic structural design (lower for steady loads, higher for fatigue/impact).
- [ ] The **5 theories** (Rankine, Saint-Venant, Tresca, Haigh, von Mises) correspond to 5 different hypotheses about what *triggers* failure: maximum stress, maximum strain, maximum shear, total energy, distortion energy.
- [ ] In KTU exams, **state the hypothesis first**, then write the **design equation**, then show **step-by-step substitution** — this matches the official valuation key structure.
- [ ] Final answer must always include an **engineering interpretation sentence**: "Since von Mises is more accurate for ductile materials, we adopt $n = 2.12$. The design is safe / unsafe for the given loading."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maximum Principal Stress Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT525 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS OF ROBOTIC PARTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Maximum Principal Stress Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximum Principal Stress Theory (MPST)**, also historically known as **Rankine's Theory of Failure** (proposed by William John Macquorn Rankine in **1858**), is a classical brittle-failure hypothesis used in the design of robotic components and load-bearing machine parts. It postulates that failure of a structural element occurs when the **maximum (algebraically largest) principal stress** developed at any internal point in the component reaches a critical threshold equal to the **ultimate tensile strength ($\sigma_{ut}$)** of the material under a uniaxial tensile test.

Mathematically, the failure criterion is expressed as:

$$\sigma_1 \;\geq\; \sigma_{ut}$$

Where $\sigma_1$ is the **largest principal stress** (algebraic maximum) acting at a critical point in the part. For safe design, the inequality is reversed and a factor of safety $n$ is introduced:

$$\sigma_1 \;\leq\; \frac{\sigma_{ut}}{n} \;=\; \sigma_{allowable}$$

In the context of **PERAT525 – Structural Analysis of Robotic Parts**, this theory is applied to components such as **gripper jaws, ceramic sensor housings, gear teeth, fiber-reinforced composite linkages, and brittle actuator casings** where catastrophic fracture rather than yielding is the governing design concern.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Glass Cup Under Squeeze**
>
> Imagine pressing a thick ceramic coffee mug between your palms. The ceramic does not "bend" or "yield" like a metal spoon would. Instead, the moment the **single largest internal pull (tensile stress)** in the wall exceeds the bonding strength of the ceramic grains, a crack shoots through the wall and the mug shatters without warning.
>
> Rankine's Maximum Principal Stress Theory treats the robot part exactly like this ceramic mug. It does **not** care about the smaller stresses acting in other directions, nor about the shear stresses. It simply asks: *"What is the single biggest tensile pull inside the part right now?"* If that value crosses the material's ultimate limit, fracture is assumed imminent.

### 1.3 Salient Constants and Metrics

- **Ultimate Tensile Strength ($\sigma_{ut}$)** — measured in **MPa** or **N/mm²**.
- **Factor of Safety ($n$)** — a dimensionless integer typically between **1.5** and **4** for static robotic applications.
- **Principal Stress Magnitudes** — $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$ (algebraic ordering; $\sigma_3$ may be compressive).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> The theory is strictly applicable to **brittle materials** (cast iron, concrete, ceramics, brittle polymers, and unidirectional fiber composites). It is **not** recommended for ductile materials like mild steel or aluminum alloys — for those, the *Distortion Energy Theory* or *Maximum Shear Stress Theory* govern.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logic of the Theory — Step-by-Step Reasoning

1. **Step 1 — Stress Tensor Reduction:** Any 3D stress state at a point inside a robot part can be represented by a symmetric $3 \times 3$ stress tensor $\sigma_{ij}$.

2. **Step 2 — Eigenvalue Problem:** Principal stresses are the **eigenvalues** of this tensor. They are the normal stresses acting on planes where the **shear stress vanishes**.

3. **Step 3 — Algebraic Ranking:** Once the three principal stresses $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$ are computed, they are algebraically ordered. For example, if computed values are $\sigma_x = 80\,\text{MPa}, \sigma_y = -20\,\text{MPa}, \tau_{xy} = 30\,\text{MPa}$, then the roots yield $\sigma_1 = 87.6\,\text{MPa}$ and $\sigma_2 = -27.6\,\text{MPa}$.

4. **Step 4 — Maximum Identification:** The algebraically largest value is isolated. The other two stresses are **completely ignored**, regardless of their magnitude or sign.

5. **Step 5 — Comparison to Strength:** This single maximum value is compared with the material's **ultimate tensile strength** $\sigma_{ut}$ (obtained from a uniaxial tensile test).

6. **Step 6 — Failure Decision:** If $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_{ut}$, the theory declares **fracture / brittle failure**. If $\sigma_1 < \sigma_{ut}$, the part is **assumed safe** under the applied load system.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for solving KTU 2024 ESE questions on this topic. Memorize the variable definitions and units.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Principal stresses in 2D | $\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau_{xy}^{2}}$ | MPa | Real roots always exist for real stress tensors |
| 2 | Maximum principal stress | $\sigma_1 = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau_{xy}^{2}}$ | MPa | Algebraically largest value |
| 3 | Principal angle | $\tan 2\theta_p = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ | radians | Orientation of principal plane |
| 4 | Characteristic (cubic) equation for 3D | $\sigma^{3} - I_1\sigma^{2} + I_2\sigma - I_3 = 0$ | MPa | $I_1, I_2, I_3$ are stress invariants |
| 5 | First stress invariant | $I_1 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z$ | MPa | Independent of orientation |
| 6 | Second stress invariant | $I_2 = \sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y\sigma_z + \sigma_z\sigma_x - \tau_{xy}^{2} - \tau_{yz}^{2} - \tau_{zx}^{2}$ | MPa² | Independent of orientation |
| 7 | Third stress invariant | $I_3 = \det[\sigma_{ij}]$ | MPa³ | Independent of orientation |
| 8 | MPST failure criterion | $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_{ut}$ | MPa | Rankine fracture hypothesis |
| 9 | Safe design inequality | $\sigma_1 \leq \dfrac{\sigma_{ut}}{n}$ | MPa | $n$ is the factor of safety |
| 10 | Factor of safety (MPST) | $n = \dfrac{\sigma_{ut}}{\sigma_{1,\text{working}}}$ | dimensionless | Working value of $\sigma_1$ under design load |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility in Robotics

- **Design of ceramic end-effector gripper pads** where contact stresses are triaxial and brittle fracture is the concern.
- **Static strength validation of composite robot arm links** (carbon-fiber reinforced polymers) where matrix cracking initiates at the principal tensile stress.
- **Failure analysis of cast iron gearbox housings** in industrial robotic joints.
- **Sizing of brittle force/torque sensor bodies** in haptic feedback assemblies.
- **Code-compliant design** under standards such as **IS 2825**, **ASME BPVC Section VIII**, and **AGMA** for brittle materials.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** MPST is **conservative for uniaxial tension** but **non-conservative (unsafe) in triaxial tensile states** because it ignores the strengthening contribution of confining stresses. For complex 3D loading in robot wrists or wrists of manipulator arms, engineers typically verify the design with **modified Mohr's theory** or **Bresler–Pister criterion** as a cross-check.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the 2D Principal Stress Formula

Consider a 2D stress element in a robot linkage, with normal stresses $\sigma_x, \sigma_y$ on faces perpendicular to $x$ and $y$ axes, and complementary shear stress $\tau_{xy}$ on those faces.

**Step 1 — Stress transformation equations** (from equilibrium of an inclined plane at angle $\theta$ to the $x$-axis):

$$
\sigma_{\theta} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta
$$

$$
\tau_{\theta} = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate $\sigma_{\theta}$ with respect to $\theta$** to find the extremum (where the shear on the inclined face becomes zero).

$$
\frac{d\sigma_{\theta}}{d\theta} = -(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\sin 2\theta + 2\tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $2\theta_p$ (the principal angle)**:

$$
\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}
$$

**Step 4 — Use the trigonometric identity** $\cos 2\theta_p = \pm \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \tan^{2} 2\theta_p}}$ and $\sin 2\theta_p = \pm \dfrac{\tan 2\theta_p}{\sqrt{1 + \tan^{2} 2\theta_p}}$.

Let the denominator be:

$$
R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau_{xy}^{2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back into the transformation equation for $\sigma_{\theta}$**:

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm R
$$

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau_{xy}^{2}}
$$

**Step 6 — Identify $\sigma_1$** as the value with the $+$ sign (provided that the term inside the square root is real, which it always is for real stresses by construction of the Cauchy stress tensor).

$$
\sigma_1 = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau_{xy}^{2}}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the 3D Characteristic Equation

The 3D stress tensor is:

$$
[\sigma] = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x & \tau_{xy} & \tau_{xz} \\ \tau_{xy} & \sigma_y & \tau_{yz} \\ \tau_{xz} & \tau_{yz} & \sigma_z \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 1 — Principal stresses are eigenvalues** of this tensor. They satisfy the characteristic equation $\det([\sigma] - \sigma[I]) = 0$.

**Step 2 — Expand the determinant** using cofactor expansion along the first row:

$$
\det \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x - \sigma & \tau_{xy} & \tau_{xz} \\ \tau_{xy} & \sigma_y - \sigma & \tau_{yz} \\ \tau_{xz} & \tau_{yz} & \sigma_z - \sigma \end{bmatrix} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Sarrus' rule or cofactor expansion**, collect powers of $\sigma$:

$$
\sigma^{3} - (\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)\sigma^{2} + (\sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y\sigma_z + \sigma_z\sigma_x - \tau_{xy}^{2} - \tau_{yz}^{2} - \tau_{zx}^{2})\sigma
$$

$$
- \det[\sigma] = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Identify stress invariants** (these do not change with rotation of the element):

$$
I_1 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z
$$

$$
I_2 = \sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y\sigma_z + \sigma_z\sigma_x - \tau_{xy}^{2} - \tau_{yz}^{2} - \tau_{zx}^{2}
$$

$$
I_3 = \det[\sigma]
$$

**Step 5 — Final compact cubic**:

$$
\sigma^{3} - I_1\sigma^{2} + I_2\sigma - I_3 = 0
$$

The three real roots of this cubic are $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example — Design of a Robot Gripper Link

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Typical ESE-Level Problem Pattern**

**Problem Statement:**
A point on the cast-iron gripper link of an industrial pick-and-place robot experiences the following 2D stress state under operational load:

$$
\sigma_x = 60\;\text{MPa}, \quad \sigma_y = 20\;\text{MPa}, \quad \tau_{xy} = 25\;\text{MPa}
$$

The cast iron grade has an ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{ut} = 200\;\text{MPa}$ and ultimate compressive strength $\sigma_{uc} = 600\;\text{MPa}$. Using the **Maximum Principal Stress Theory**, determine:
- (i) The maximum and minimum principal stresses.
- (ii) The orientation of the principal planes.
- (iii) The factor of safety based on MPST.

**Step 1 — Substitute into the principal stress formula:**

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{60 + 20}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{60 - 20}{2}\right)^{2} + 25^{2}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = 40 \pm \sqrt{20^{2} + 25^{2}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = 40 \pm \sqrt{400 + 625} = 40 \pm \sqrt{1025}
$$

$$
\sqrt{1025} = 32.0156\;\text{MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve:**

$$
\sigma_1 = 40 + 32.0156 = 72.0156\;\text{MPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_2 = 40 - 32.0156 = 7.9844\;\text{MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Principal angle:**

$$
\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} = \frac{2 \times 25}{60 - 20} = \frac{50}{40} = 1.25
$$

$$
2\theta_p = \tan^{-1}(1.25) = 51.34^{\circ}
$$

$$
\theta_p = 25.67^{\circ}
$$

The orientation of the plane on which $\sigma_1$ acts is **$25.67^{\circ}$** counter-clockwise from the $x$-axis. The plane for $\sigma_2$ is perpendicular to it, at $\theta_p + 90^{\circ} = 115.67^{\circ}$.

**Step 4 — Apply MPST failure criterion:**

The maximum principal stress is $\sigma_1 = 72.02\;\text{MPa}$. The ultimate tensile strength is $\sigma_{ut} = 200\;\text{MPa}$.

Since $\sigma_1 = 72.02\;\text{MPa} < \sigma_{ut} = 200\;\text{MPa}$, the part is **safe under MPST**.

**Step 5 — Compute factor of safety:**

$$
n = \frac{\sigma_{ut}}{\sigma_1} = \frac{200}{72.0156} = 2.777
$$

$$
n \approx 2.78
$$

**Step 6 — Verification using the trace invariant:**

The sum $\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = 72.0156 + 7.9844 = 80\;\text{MPa} = \sigma_x + \sigma_y$ ✓ (validates computation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on the Compressive Strength Term:** Strictly speaking, classical Rankine's theory only uses $\sigma_{ut}$. However, when $\sigma_1$ turns out to be compressive (algebraically negative) and $\sigma_2$ or $\sigma_3$ is tensile, some textbooks use a two-equation form involving both $\sigma_{ut}$ and $\sigma_{uc}$. KTU 2024 typically tests the **simple $\sigma_1 \leq \sigma_{ut}$** form, but the examiner may award bonus marks if you mention the modified criterion.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Algorithmic Decision Flow of MPST

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below shows the **complete decision flow** that a design engineer follows when applying Maximum Principal Stress Theory to a robot component.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Critical Point on Robot Part] --> B[Extract Stress Tensor sigma_ij from FEA or Hand Calc]
    B --> C{Dimensionality of Stress State}
    C -->|2D Plane Stress| D[Apply 2D Principal Stress Formula]
    C -->|3D General| E[Solve Cubic Characteristic Equation]
    D --> F[Compute Sigma1 and Sigma2]
    E --> G[Identify Three Real Roots Sigma1, Sigma2, Sigma3]
    F --> H[Algebraically Rank: Sigma1 Greater than or Equal to Sigma2]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Isolate Maximum Principal Stress Sigma1max]
    I --> J[Retrieve Material Ultimate Tensile Strength sigma_ut from Test Data]
    J --> K{Is Sigma1max Less than or Equal to sigma_ut divided by n}
    K -->|Yes| L[SAFE: Part Passes MPST Design Check]
    K -->|No| M[UNSAFE: Brittle Fracture Predicted, Redesign Required]
    M --> N[Redesign Options: Increase Cross Section, Change Material, Reduce Load]
    N --> B
    L --> O[Compute Factor of Safety n = sigma_ut divided by Sigma1max]
    O --> P[Document Result and End]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Stress Analysis in a Robot Link

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[External Load Source] --> A2[Force and Moment Inputs on Robotic Link]
    A2 --> A3[Section Property Database]
    A3 --> A4[Internal Stress Tensor Computer]
    A4 --> A5[Principal Stress Solver]
    A5 --> A6[MPST Failure Comparator]
    M1[Material Property Database sigma_ut] --> A6
    A6 --> A7{Failure Decision}
    A7 --> A8[Pass: Continue to Manufacturing]
    A7 --> A9[Fail: Loop Back to Design]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Chart — MPST vs Other Classical Theories

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Classical Failure Theories in Robotics] --> T1[Maximum Principal Stress Theory]
    Start --> T2[Maximum Shear Stress Theory Tresca]
    Start --> T3[Distortion Energy Theory Von Mises]
    Start --> T4[Modified Mohr Theory]
    T1 --> C1[Best for Brittle Materials Cast Iron Ceramics Composites]
    T2 --> C2[Conservative for Ductile Materials]
    T3 --> C3[Most Accurate for Ductile Materials]
    T4 --> C4[Best for Brittle with Both Tension and Compression Strengths]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**

**Q1.** Define the **Maximum Principal Stress Theory** of failure. State **two limitations** of this theory.

**Model Answer:**
Maximum Principal Stress Theory, also known as Rankine's Theory, states that a brittle material fails when the maximum principal tensile stress ($\sigma_1$) at any point in the component reaches the ultimate tensile strength ($\sigma_{ut}$) of the material, i.e., $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_{ut}$. It is widely used in design of cast iron and ceramic components in robotic systems.

*Two limitations:*
1. The theory ignores the effect of **all other principal stresses** except the largest one, which can lead to unsafe predictions in triaxial tensile states.
2. The theory is **inapplicable to ductile materials** where yielding — not brittle fracture — is the dominant failure mode.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**

**Q2.** Differentiate between the **Maximum Principal Stress Theory** and the **Maximum Shear Stress Theory** in **two points** relevant to robotic part design.

**Model Answer:**

| Comparison Axis | Maximum Principal Stress Theory | Maximum Shear Stress Theory |
|-----------------|--------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Material suitability | Brittle materials (ceramics, cast iron, composites) | Ductile materials (mild steel, aluminum alloys) |
| Governing quantity | Maximum normal (principal) stress $\sigma_1$ | Maximum shear stress $\tau_{max} = (\sigma_1 - \sigma_3)/2$ |
| Failure equation | $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_{ut}$ | $(\sigma_1 - \sigma_3)/2 \geq \sigma_{yt}/2$ or $\tau_y$ |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the **2D principal stress formula** starting from the basic stress transformation equations. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Start from the stress transformation equation for a plane inclined at angle $\theta$ to the $x$-axis on a 2D element:

$$
\sigma_{\theta} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta
$$

**Step 2:** For principal stress, the shear stress on the inclined plane must vanish. The maximum of $\sigma_{\theta}$ is found by differentiation:

$$
\frac{d\sigma_{\theta}}{d\theta} = -(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\sin 2\theta + 2\tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta = 0
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for the principal angle:

$$
\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}
$$

**Step 4:** Use the identity $\cos 2\theta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1+\tan^{2} 2\theta}}$ and $\sin 2\theta = \frac{\tan 2\theta}{\sqrt{1+\tan^{2} 2\theta}}$. Define:

$$
R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau_{xy}^{2}}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back:

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau_{xy}^{2}}
$$

The **maximum principal stress** is:

$$
\sigma_1 = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau_{xy}^{2}}
$$

---

**(b)** At a critical point in a robot arm component, the stresses are $\sigma_x = 90\;\text{MPa}, \sigma_y = -30\;\text{MPa}, \tau_{xy} = 40\;\text{MPa}$. The material is **grey cast iron** with $\sigma_{ut} = 210\;\text{MPa}$ and $\sigma_{uc} = 700\;\text{MPa}$. Using **MPST**, calculate the **factor of safety**. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the average stress:

$$
\bar{\sigma} = \frac{90 + (-30)}{2} = 30\;\text{MPa}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the radius term:

$$
R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{90 - (-30)}{2}\right)^{2} + 40^{2}} = \sqrt{60^{2} + 40^{2}} = \sqrt{3600 + 1600} = \sqrt{5200}
$$

$$
R = 72.111\;\text{MPa}
$$

**Step 3:** Principal stresses:

$$
\sigma_1 = 30 + 72.111 = 102.111\;\text{MPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_2 = 30 - 72.111 = -42.111\;\text{MPa}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply MPST. Since $\sigma_1$ is tensile and positive, compare with $\sigma_{ut} = 210\;\text{MPa}$.

$$
\sigma_1 = 102.111\;\text{MPa} < \sigma_{ut} = 210\;\text{MPa} \quad \text{(Safe)}
$$

**Step 5:** Factor of safety:

$$
n = \frac{\sigma_{ut}}{\sigma_1} = \frac{210}{102.111} = 2.057
$$

$$
\boxed{n \approx 2.06}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **stress invariants** in a 3D stress state. Why are they called "invariants"? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

In a 3D stress state, the stress tensor has three scalar quantities that **do not change** when the coordinate system is rotated. These are called **stress invariants**.

**First Invariant (Linear Invariant) $I_1$:**

$$
I_1 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z = \sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3
$$

**Second Invariant (Quadratic Invariant) $I_2$:**

$$
I_2 = \sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y\sigma_z + \sigma_z\sigma_x - \tau_{xy}^{2} - \tau_{yz}^{2} - \tau_{zx}^{2}
$$

**Third Invariant (Cubic Invariant) $I_3$:**

$$
I_3 = \det[\sigma] = \sigma_x\sigma_y\sigma_z + 2\tau_{xy}\tau_{yz}\tau_{zx} - (\sigma_x\tau_{yz}^{2} + \sigma_y\tau_{zx}^{2} + \sigma_z\tau_{xy}^{2})
$$

**Why are they called invariants?** Because the **trace**, the sum of principal $2\times2$ minors, and the determinant of a tensor are mathematically invariant under orthogonal coordinate transformations. Physically, this means the *sum* of normal stresses, *sum of products*, and *product* of principal stresses represent intrinsic physical properties of the stress field that are **independent of the observer's coordinate orientation**. In robotic part analysis, this is crucial because the choice of reference axes for a robot arm should not affect the design conclusions.

---

**(b)** A spherical pressure vessel in a hydraulic robot actuator has inner diameter $d = 200\;\text{mm}$ and wall thickness $t = 6\;\text{mm}$. It is subjected to an internal pressure $p = 4\;\text{MPa}$. The material is a **brittle composite** with $\sigma_{ut} = 90\;\text{MPa}$. Using **MPST**, determine the **factor of safety**. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** For a thin-walled spherical pressure vessel, the membrane (hoop) stress is uniform and equal in all directions:

$$
\sigma = \frac{pd}{4t} = \frac{4 \times 200}{4 \times 6} = \frac{800}{24} = 33.33\;\text{MPa}
$$

**Step 2:** The stress state is **biaxial and equal**: $\sigma_1 = \sigma_2 = 33.33\;\text{MPa}$, $\sigma_3 = 0$ (neglecting radial stress for thin wall).

**Step 3:** Maximum principal stress:

$$
\sigma_1 = 33.33\;\text{MPa}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply MPST:

$$
n = \frac{\sigma_{ut}}{\sigma_1} = \frac{90}{33.33} = 2.70
$$

$$
\boxed{n \approx 2.70}
$$

The vessel is safe under MPST with a safety factor of approximately 2.70.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Algebraic sign confusion:** Students often forget that the "+" sign gives $\sigma_1$ (the larger root), and the "−" sign gives $\sigma_2$ (the smaller root). This is true *regardless* of the signs of $\sigma_x$ and $\sigma_y$. Always verify by checking that $\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y$.
>
> 2. **Units mismatch:** Mixing $\sigma_x$ in MPa with $\sigma_{ut}$ in N/mm² without conversion. (1 MPa = 1 N/mm², but always write the units explicitly.)
>
> 3. **Ignoring factor of safety in the design equation:** Students write $\sigma_1 \leq \sigma_{ut}$ instead of $\sigma_1 \leq \sigma_{ut}/n$. The factor of safety must be included in the safe design inequality, not just in the final step.
>
> 4. **Applying MPST to ductile materials:** This is a conceptual error. KTU examiners specifically check whether the student can *justify* the choice of theory based on material behavior.
>
> 5. **Skipping the orientation calculation:** A full 14-mark solution should include the principal angle $\theta_p$. Leaving it out typically costs 2 marks.
>
> 6. **Drawing Mohr's circle wrong:** When asked for Mohr's circle, students often mislabel the center or forget to indicate the radius $R$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE**

- ✅ **Theory name:** Rankine's Theory / Maximum Principal Stress Theory (MPST), year **1858**.
- ✅ **Governing equation:** $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_{ut}$ for failure; $\sigma_1 \leq \sigma_{ut}/n$ for safe design.
- ✅ **Applicable to:** Brittle materials — cast iron, ceramics, glass, concrete, CFRP, brittle polymers.
- ✅ **Not applicable to:** Ductile materials — use Tresca or Von Mises instead.
- ✅ **2D formula:** $\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau_{xy}^{2}}$.
- ✅ **3D formula:** Solve $\sigma^{3} - I_1\sigma^{2} + I_2\sigma - I_3 = 0$ where $I_1, I_2, I_3$ are stress invariants.
- ✅ **Principal angle:** $\tan 2\theta_p = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$.
- ✅ **Factor of safety:** $n = \sigma_{ut} / \sigma_1$.
- ✅ **Stress invariants:** $I_1, I_2, I_3$ are **coordinate-independent** — they remain the same in all rotated frames.
- ✅ **Verification trick:** $\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y$ (always true for 2D).
- ✅ **Limitation:** Ignores all other principal stresses and triaxial confinement effects.
- ✅ **Engineering use in robotics:** Gripper pads, ceramic sensor housings, cast iron gearboxes, composite robot arms.
- ✅ **Relation to Mohr's circle:** Center at $\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}, 0\right)$, radius $R = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau_{xy}^{2}}$.
- ✅ **Modified version:** When $\sigma_1$ is compressive, the modified Mohr's theory uses both $\sigma_{ut}$ and $\sigma_{uc}$.

> **End of KTU 2024 Module 2 — Maximum Principal Stress Theory Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maximum Principal Strain Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT525 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS OF ROBOTIC PARTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Maximum Principal Strain Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Maximum Principal Strain Theory**, also historically known as **Saint-Venant's Theory** (proposed in 1837), is one of the classical theories of failure for ductile materials subjected to complex states of stress. It postulates that a material element will yield (or fail) when the **maximum principal strain** in the element reaches the strain value at the yield point as determined from a simple tension test on the same material.

Mathematically, the theory asserts that failure occurs when the maximum tensile (or compressive) principal strain $\varepsilon_1$ (or $\varepsilon_3$, whichever is larger in absolute value) equals the strain corresponding to the yield point of the material in uniaxial tension $\varepsilon_Y$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This theory is an extension of the Maximum Normal Stress Theory (Rankine's Theory) to include the effect of Poisson's ratio. Unlike Rankine's theory which ignores lateral deformations, Saint-Venant recognized that under triaxial stress, lateral strains play a critical role in failure initiation, particularly in brittle materials and composite robotic parts.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a robotic gripper arm made of an aluminum alloy. When it lifts a heavy payload, the material at the critical cross-section experiences forces in multiple directions simultaneously, not just along the lifting axis. If we only check the maximum stress (Rankine's approach), we might miss failure scenarios where the material stretches sideways (or compresses sideways) until tiny micro-cracks form and propagate.

**Think of it this way:** Imagine stretching a rubber band. The rubber band not only becomes longer (axial strain) but also becomes thinner (lateral strain due to Poisson's effect). The **Maximum Principal Strain Theory** says the rubber band will tear when the **total deformation**, in any of the three principal directions, exceeds a critical value. It is the "deformation-based" cousin of the stress-based theories.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** While stress-based theories (like Tresca and Von Mises) ask "How much force per unit area?", strain-based theories ask "How much does the material physically distort?" before failure. For robotics, where precise dimensional tolerances are critical, strain-based criteria often provide more realistic safety predictions.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Strain at Yield Point** ($\varepsilon_Y$) — dimensionless quantity, typically in the range $0.001$ to $0.01$ for engineering metals.
- **Poisson's Ratio** ($\nu$ or $\mu$) — ranges from $0$ (cork) to $0.5$ (rubber); for most metals $\nu \approx 0.3$.
- **Young's Modulus** ($E$) — measured in **GPa** (Gigapascals) or **N/m²**.
- **Yield Strength** ($\sigma_Y$ or $S_{yt}$) — measured in **MPa** (Megapascals).
- **Factor of Safety** ($FOS$ or $N$) — a dimensionless ratio $\geq 1$ for safe design.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Logic Breakdown

The Maximum Principal Strain Theory operates on the following structured logic:

- **Step 1 — Identify the State of Stress:** Determine the principal stresses $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$ from the given 2D or 3D stress state using Mohr's circle or eigenvalue methods.
- **Step 2 — Apply Generalized Hooke's Law:** Compute the principal strains using the relationships:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_2 - \nu(\sigma_1 + \sigma_3)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_3 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_3 - \nu(\sigma_1 + \sigma_2)\right]$$

- **Step 3 — Identify the Critical Strain:** The maximum principal strain is the strain with the largest absolute value:

$$\varepsilon_{max} = \max\left(\vert \varepsilon_1 \vert, \vert \varepsilon_2 \vert, \vert \varepsilon_3 \vert\right)$$

- **Step 4 — Compare to Yield Strain:** Calculate the yield strain from the uniaxial tension test:

$$\varepsilon_Y = \frac{\sigma_Y}{E}$$

- **Step 5 — Apply Failure Criterion:** Failure occurs when $\varepsilon_{max} \geq \varepsilon_Y$. For design with a safety factor $N$:

$$\varepsilon_{max} \leq \frac{\varepsilon_Y}{N}$$

- **Step 6 — Compute Factor of Safety:** The design Factor of Safety is derived as:

$$N = \frac{\varepsilon_Y}{\varepsilon_{max}} = \frac{\sigma_Y}{E \cdot \varepsilon_{max}}$$

## 2.2 Why Poisson's Ratio Matters — The "How" Behind the Theory

The genius of Saint-Venant's theory lies in recognizing that **lateral strains are not free**. When a robotic link is stretched axially, it contracts laterally. This contraction is **resisted** by any constraints (geometric or material continuities), creating **secondary stresses** in the lateral directions. The Maximum Principal Strain Theory captures this by including the $\nu$ term in the strain equations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Implication for Robotics:** In a robotic manipulator arm, links are often constrained at joints. The Maximum Principal Strain Theory is therefore more accurate than the Maximum Normal Stress Theory for analyzing failure in these constrained geometries, particularly for **fiber-reinforced composite links** and **3D-printed lattice structures** used in modern lightweight robots.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\varepsilon_1 = \dfrac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)\right]$ | Principal strain 1 (3D) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\varepsilon_2 = \dfrac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_2 - \nu(\sigma_1 + \sigma_3)\right]$ | Principal strain 2 (3D) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\varepsilon_3 = \dfrac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_3 - \nu(\sigma_1 + \sigma_2)\right]$ | Principal strain 3 (3D) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\varepsilon_Y = \dfrac{\sigma_Y}{E}$ | Yield strain (uniaxial) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $N = \dfrac{\sigma_Y}{E \cdot \varepsilon_{max}}$ | Factor of Safety | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3) = \pm \dfrac{\sigma_Y}{N}$ | Design equation (3D) | MPa |
| 7 | $\sigma_1 - \nu \cdot \sigma_2 = \pm \dfrac{\sigma_Y}{N}$ | Design equation (2D, plane stress) | MPa |
| 8 | $\varepsilon_1 = \dfrac{1}{E}(\sigma_1 - \nu \sigma_2)$ | 2D plane stress principal strain | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In 2D problems (plane stress), only $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ exist, and $\sigma_3 = 0$. Always substitute $\sigma_3 = 0$ into Formula 1 before applying it. The **sign convention** matters: tensile principal stresses give positive strains (elongation), while compressive principal stresses give negative strains (contraction).

## 2.4 Comparison with Other Classical Theories

| Theory | Critical Quantity | Best Suited For |
|--------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Maximum Normal Stress (Rankine) | $\sigma_1$ or $\sigma_3$ | Brittle materials |
| Maximum Shear Stress (Tresca) | $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3$ | Ductile materials, conservative |
| Distortion Energy (Von Mises) | $\sqrt{\sigma_1^2 - \sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2^2}$ | Ductile materials, accurate |
| **Maximum Principal Strain (Saint-Venant)** | $\varepsilon_1$ or $\varepsilon_3$ | **Brittle materials, constrained parts** |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Robotics

The Maximum Principal Strain Theory is particularly valuable in:

- **Robotic actuator design** — predicting deformation in servo-driven linkages.
- **Carbon-fiber composite grippers** — where strain-based criteria outperform stress-based ones.
- **Soft robotics** — hyperelastic materials with large deformations follow strain-based failure better.
- **Micro-electromechanical systems (MEMS)** — strain limits govern fatigue life.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Maximum Principal Strain Criterion

We start from the generalized Hooke's Law for a 3D linear elastic isotropic material. The strain in the $x$-direction due to stresses in all three principal directions is:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$

In the principal coordinate system (where shear stresses vanish), the principal strains become:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_2 - \nu(\sigma_1 + \sigma_3)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_3 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_3 - \nu(\sigma_1 + \sigma_2)\right]$$

For a **uniaxial tension test** at yield, the stress state is $\sigma_1 = \sigma_Y$ and $\sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$. Substituting:

$$\varepsilon_Y = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_Y - \nu(0 + 0)\right] = \frac{\sigma_Y}{E}$$

The **Maximum Principal Strain Theory** states that failure occurs when:

$$\varepsilon_{max} = \max\left(\vert \varepsilon_1 \vert, \vert \varepsilon_2 \vert, \vert \varepsilon_3 \vert\right) = \varepsilon_Y = \frac{\sigma_Y}{E}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Factor of Safety

Starting from the failure criterion:

$$\vert \varepsilon_{max} \vert = \frac{\sigma_Y}{E}$$

If the principal stresses are $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$ and we assume $\varepsilon_1$ is the maximum principal strain:

$$\frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)\right] = \frac{\sigma_Y}{E}$$

Multiplying both sides by $E$:

$$\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3) = \sigma_Y$$

If the same stresses are scaled by a factor $N$ to reach failure, then $\sigma_1' = N\sigma_1$, $\sigma_2' = N\sigma_2$, $\sigma_3' = N\sigma_3$:

$$N\sigma_1 - \nu(N\sigma_2 + N\sigma_3) = \sigma_Y$$

$$N\left[\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)\right] = \sigma_Y$$

Solving for the Factor of Safety:

$$N = \frac{\sigma_Y}{\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)}$$

For design with a safety factor $N$ built-in:

$$\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3) = \frac{\sigma_Y}{N}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Negative Sign Caution:** If the left-hand side is **negative**, the maximum principal strain in absolute value is $\vert \varepsilon_3 \vert$. In that case, the formula becomes:
> $$\nu(\sigma_1 + \sigma_2) - \sigma_3 = \frac{\sigma_Y}{N}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A robotic link in a 6-DOF manipulator experiences a 2D plane stress state with $\sigma_x = 80 \text{ MPa}$ (tensile) and $\sigma_y = -40 \text{ MPa}$ (compressive). The material has $\sigma_Y = 250 \text{ MPa}$ and $\nu = 0.3$. Determine the Factor of Safety using the Maximum Principal Strain Theory.

**Step 1: Compute Principal Stresses (2D Mohr's Circle).**

$$\sigma_{avg} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{80 + (-40)}{2} = 20 \text{ MPa}$$

$$R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{80 - (-40)}{2}\right)^2 + 0^2} = 60 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_{avg} + R = 20 + 60 = 80 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_2 = \sigma_{avg} - R = 20 - 60 = -40 \text{ MPa}$$

(With no shear stress and the convention that $\sigma_x > \sigma_y$, the principal stresses are simply the given normal stresses here.)

**Step 2: Apply Maximum Principal Strain Criterion (Plane Stress, $\sigma_3 = 0$).**

For $\sigma_1$ (tensile — positive):
$$\sigma_1 - \nu \cdot \sigma_2 = 80 - (0.3)(-40) = 80 + 12 = 92 \text{ MPa}$$

For $\sigma_2$ (compressive — check if absolute value is larger):
$$\nu \cdot \sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = (0.3)(80) - (-40) = 24 + 40 = 64 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Identify the Critical (Maximum) Quantity.**

$$\sigma_1 - \nu \sigma_2 = 92 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{(larger)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \varepsilon_1 \text{ is critical}$$

**Step 4: Compute Factor of Safety.**

$$N = \frac{\sigma_Y}{\sigma_1 - \nu \sigma_2} = \frac{250}{92} \approx 2.717$$

**Answer:** $N \approx 2.72$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The robotic link has a safety factor of **2.72** against strain-based failure. For comparison, the Maximum Normal Stress Theory would give $N = 250/80 = 3.125$, while Tresca would give $N = 250/(80-(-40)) = 2.083$. The Saint-Venant theory lies between, which is typical for moderately ductile materials.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix for 2D Plane Stress Cases

| Stress Case | $\sigma_1 - \nu\sigma_2$ | $\nu\sigma_1 - \sigma_2$ | Critical Quantity |
|-------------|-------------------------|--------------------------|-------------------|
| Uniaxial Tension ($\sigma_1, 0$) | $\sigma_1$ | $\nu\sigma_1$ | $\sigma_1$ (always larger) |
| Equal Biaxial Tension ($\sigma, \sigma$) | $\sigma(1-\nu)$ | $\sigma(\nu-1)$ | Equal magnitude |
| Pure Shear ($\tau$) | $2\tau$ | $2\tau$ | Equal magnitude |
| Tension + Compression | Depends on magnitudes | Depends on magnitudes | Requires computation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Pure Shear:** For pure shear, the Maximum Principal Strain Theory predicts the same critical stress as the Maximum Shear Stress Theory: $\sigma_1 = \tau$, and $N = \sigma_Y / (2\tau)$. This is because in pure shear, the principal strains are equal in magnitude, so the theories coincide.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the decision flow for applying the Maximum Principal Strain Theory in a robotic part design workflow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Stress State of Robotic Part] --> B{Identify Loading Type}
    B -- Uniaxial --> C[sigma_1, sigma_2=0, sigma_3=0]
    B -- Biaxial --> D[sigma_1, sigma_2 given, sigma_3=0]
    B -- Triaxial --> E[All three principal stresses given]
    C --> F[Compute Principal Strains using Hooke's Law]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Compute epsilon_1, epsilon_2, epsilon_3]
    G --> H{Find Maximum |epsilon|}
    H --> I[Compute Yield Strain: epsilon_Y = sigma_Y / E]
    I --> J[Apply Failure Criterion: |epsilon_max| >= epsilon_Y ?]
    J -- Yes --> K[FAILURE - Redesign Required]
    J -- No --> L[Compute Factor of Safety: N = epsilon_Y / epsilon_max]
    L --> M{Is N >= N_required?}
    M -- Yes --> N[SAFE DESIGN - Approve]
    M -- No --> O[UNSAFE - Increase Material or Reduce Load]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Mohr's Circle with Saint-Venant Envelope

The following diagram shows how the Maximum Principal Strain Theory is graphically represented as an envelope around the Mohr's circle in the stress space.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Uniaxial Yield Test: sigma_1 = sigma_Y] --> B[Convert to Strain: epsilon_Y = sigma_Y/E]
    B --> C[Plot Envelope Lines in 2D: sigma_1 - nu*sigma_2 = ± sigma_Y]
    C --> D[Draw Mohr's Circle for Given Stress State]
    D --> E{Circle Touches Envelope?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Failure Point Reached]
    E -- No --> G[Safe State]
    F --> H[Compute N = sigma_Y / Critical_Quantity]
    G --> H
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Comparison of Failure Envelopes in 2D Stress Space

The following block diagram shows how the Maximum Principal Strain envelope differs from the other classical failure theories geometrically.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Classical Failure Theories in 2D] --> B[Maximum Normal Stress Theory]
    A --> C[Maximum Shear Stress Theory]
    A --> D[Von Mises Theory]
    A --> E[Maximum Principal Strain Theory]
    B --> B1[Vertical and Horizontal Lines: sigma_1 = ±sigma_Y]
    C --> C1[Inclined Lines at 45 deg: sigma_1 - sigma_3 = ±sigma_Y]
    D --> D1[Elliptical Envelope]
    E --> E1[Inclined Lines at angle: sigma_1 - nu*sigma_2 = ±sigma_Y]
    E1 --> F[Note: Saint-Venant Envelope is NOT a rotated square]
    F --> G[It accounts for Poisson coupling between axes]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Distinction:** The Maximum Principal Strain envelope is **not** rotated by 45° like the Tresca envelope. It is rotated by an angle determined by Poisson's ratio. As $\nu \to 0$, it approaches the Rankine envelope (axis-aligned square). As $\nu \to 0.5$, it approaches a 45°-rotated square (similar to Tresca).

## 4.4 Decision Tree: When to Use Saint-Venant's Theory

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Failure Theory for Robotic Part] --> B{Material Type?}
    B -- Ductile Metal --> C{Triaxial Stress State?}
    B -- Brittle Composite --> D[Use Maximum Principal Strain]
    B -- Hyperelastic Polymer --> E[Use Strain-Based Theory]
    C -- Yes --> F[Use Von Mises]
    C -- No --> G[Use Tresca or Von Mises]
    D --> H[Saint-Venant Theory Recommended]
    E --> H
    H --> I[For Soft Robotics, MEMS, Composite Grippers]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Definition
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**

**Q:** State the Maximum Principal Strain Theory of failure. Mention the condition under which it is preferred over the Maximum Shear Stress Theory.

**Model Answer:**

The **Maximum Principal Strain Theory**, also known as **Saint-Venant's Theory**, states that failure of a material element occurs when the maximum principal strain reaches a value equal to the strain at the yield point in a simple uniaxial tension test. Mathematically, $\varepsilon_{max} = \sigma_Y / E$, where $\varepsilon_{max}$ is the absolute maximum of the three principal strains.

**Condition for preference:** It is preferred over the Maximum Shear Stress Theory in the following scenarios:

- When the material is **brittle** (e.g., cast iron, ceramics, fiber-reinforced composites used in robotic end-effectors).
- When the structure is **constrained**, causing significant lateral strains due to Poisson's effect.
- When analyzing **2D stress states** where one principal stress is compressive and the other is tensile (mixed mode), as the theory properly accounts for the opposing effects of lateral contraction and expansion.

It is **not** recommended for fully ductile materials under predominantly uniaxial loading, where the Tresca or Von Mises theories are more accurate.

---

### Question 2: Formula-Based Short Answer
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]**

**Q:** A steel component of a robot arm is in a state of plane stress with $\sigma_x = 120 \text{ MPa}$, $\sigma_y = 30 \text{ MPa}$, and $\tau_{xy} = 0$. For the material, $\sigma_Y = 240 \text{ MPa}$ and $\nu = 0.3$. Determine the Factor of Safety using the Maximum Principal Strain Theory.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1:** Since $\tau_{xy} = 0$, the principal stresses are the given normal stresses:
- $\sigma_1 = 120 \text{ MPa}$
- $\sigma_2 = 30 \text{ MPa}$

**Step 2:** For plane stress, evaluate both possible maximum strains:

For $\sigma_1$ (tensile):
$$\sigma_1 - \nu \sigma_2 = 120 - (0.3)(30) = 120 - 9 = 111 \text{ MPa}$$

For $\sigma_2$ (compressive check):
$$\nu \sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = (0.3)(120) - 30 = 36 - 30 = 6 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3:** The critical quantity is $111 \text{ MPa}$ (from $\sigma_1$).

**Step 4:** Factor of Safety:
$$N = \frac{\sigma_Y}{\sigma_1 - \nu \sigma_2} = \frac{240}{111} \approx 2.162$$

**Answer:** $N \approx 2.16$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Main Examination]**

**Q (a)** [7 Marks] Derive the expression for the Factor of Safety using the Maximum Principal Strain Theory for a 2D plane stress state. Clearly state all assumptions made.

**Q (b)** [7 Marks] A robotic gripper finger is made of an aluminum alloy with $\sigma_Y = 320 \text{ MPa}$ and $\nu = 0.25$. At a critical section, the stress state is $\sigma_x = 150 \text{ MPa}$, $\sigma_y = -80 \text{ MPa}$, and $\tau_{xy} = 50 \text{ MPa}$. Determine the Factor of Safety using: (i) Maximum Normal Stress Theory, (ii) Maximum Principal Strain Theory, and (iii) comment on which theory is more conservative.

**Model Solution for Q (a):**

**Assumptions:**

1. The material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** up to failure.
2. Failure is assumed to be **initiated by the maximum tensile or compressive principal strain**, whichever has the greater magnitude.
3. The yield strain $\varepsilon_Y$ is obtained from a **uniaxial tension test** on the same material.
4. Plane stress conditions apply: $\sigma_3 = 0$, and only $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ are nonzero.

**Derivation:**

For a 2D plane stress state, the principal strains in the principal directions are given by Hooke's Law:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_1 - \nu \sigma_2\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_2 - \nu \sigma_1\right]$$

At yield in uniaxial tension: $\varepsilon_Y = \sigma_Y / E$.

According to the Maximum Principal Strain Theory, failure occurs when:

$$\varepsilon_{max} = \max\left(\vert \varepsilon_1 \vert, \vert \varepsilon_2 \vert\right) = \frac{\sigma_Y}{E}$$

If $\sigma_1$ is the maximum tensile principal stress, the failure condition becomes:

$$\sigma_1 - \nu \sigma_2 = \sigma_Y$$

If the principal stresses are scaled by $N$ at failure ($\sigma_1' = N\sigma_1, \sigma_2' = N\sigma_2$):

$$N\sigma_1 - N\nu\sigma_2 = \sigma_Y$$

$$N(\sigma_1 - \nu\sigma_2) = \sigma_Y$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{N = \frac{\sigma_Y}{\sigma_1 - \nu \sigma_2}}$$

**Model Solution for Q (b):**

**Given:**
- $\sigma_x = 150 \text{ MPa}$, $\sigma_y = -80 \text{ MPa}$, $\tau_{xy} = 50 \text{ MPa}$
- $\sigma_Y = 320 \text{ MPa}$, $\nu = 0.25$

**Step 1: Compute Principal Stresses.**

$$\sigma_{avg} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{150 + (-80)}{2} = 35 \text{ MPa}$$

$$R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{150 - (-80)}{2}\right)^2 + (50)^2}$$

$$R = \sqrt{(115)^2 + (50)^2} = \sqrt{13225 + 2500} = \sqrt{15725} \approx 125.40 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_1 = 35 + 125.40 = 160.40 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_2 = 35 - 125.40 = -90.40 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 (i): Maximum Normal Stress Theory (MNST).**

$$N_{MNST} = \frac{\sigma_Y}{\sigma_1} = \frac{320}{160.40} \approx 1.995$$

$$\boxed{N_{MNST} \approx 1.99}$$

**Step 3 (ii): Maximum Principal Strain Theory (MPST).**

Evaluate the critical quantity. Since $\sigma_1$ is positive and $\sigma_2$ is negative, the term $\sigma_1 - \nu \sigma_2$ will be amplified:

$$\sigma_1 - \nu \sigma_2 = 160.40 - (0.25)(-90.40) = 160.40 + 22.60 = 183.00 \text{ MPa}$$

Check the other candidate:
$$\nu \sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = (0.25)(160.40) - (-90.40) = 40.10 + 90.40 = 130.50 \text{ MPa}$$

Since $183.00 > 130.50$, the critical quantity is $183.00 \text{ MPa}$.

$$N_{MPST} = \frac{\sigma_Y}{\sigma_1 - \nu\sigma_2} = \frac{320}{183.00} \approx 1.749$$

$$\boxed{N_{MPST} \approx 1.75}$$

**Step 4 (iii): Comparison and Comment.**

Comparing the two safety factors:

- MNST: $N = 1.99$
- MPST: $N = 1.75$

The **Maximum Principal Strain Theory is more conservative** (gives a lower Factor of Safety). This is because it accounts for the additional lateral strain caused by the Poisson effect. When $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ have **opposite signs** (as in this case, one tensile and one compressive), the term $-\nu\sigma_2$ becomes positive, **increasing the effective strain** and reducing the safety margin. For robotic grippers experiencing such mixed loading, the MPST provides a more realistic and safer design criterion.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Supplementary Examination]**

**Q (a)** [7 Marks] A hydraulic actuator rod in a robotic excavator is subjected to an internal pressure creating a 2D stress state: $\sigma_x = 200 \text{ MPa}$ and $\sigma_y = 100 \text{ MPa}$. The material is a high-strength steel with $\sigma_Y = 500 \text{ MPa}$ and $\nu = 0.3$. Using the Maximum Principal Strain Theory, find the Factor of Safety. Compare it with the Tresca theory.

**Q (b)** [7 Marks] With the help of a neat sketch, explain the Mohr's circle representation of the Maximum Principal Strain Theory. State two limitations of the theory.

**Model Solution for Q (a):**

**Given:** $\sigma_x = 200 \text{ MPa}$, $\sigma_y = 100 \text{ MPa}$, $\tau_{xy} = 0$, $\sigma_Y = 500 \text{ MPa}$, $\nu = 0.3$

**Step 1:** Since $\tau_{xy} = 0$, principal stresses are:
- $\sigma_1 = 200 \text{ MPa}$
- $\sigma_2 = 100 \text{ MPa}$

**Step 2:** Apply Maximum Principal Strain Theory.

For $\sigma_1$ (tensile):
$$\sigma_1 - \nu \sigma_2 = 200 - (0.3)(100) = 200 - 30 = 170 \text{ MPa}$$

For $\sigma_2$ (tensile — check):
$$\nu \sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = (0.3)(200) - 100 = 60 - 100 = -40 \text{ MPa}$$

Absolute values: $\vert 170 \vert = 170$, $\vert -40 \vert = 40$.

Critical quantity: $170 \text{ MPa}$ (from $\sigma_1$).

**Step 3:** Factor of Safety:
$$N_{MPST} = \frac{\sigma_Y}{170} = \frac{500}{170} \approx 2.941$$

**Step 4:** Compare with Tresca Theory.

For Tresca, $\sigma_3 = 0$ in plane stress, so the maximum shear stress is:
$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \frac{200 - 0}{2} = 100 \text{ MPa}$$

Failure occurs when $\tau_{max} = \sigma_Y / 2$:
$$N_{Tresca} = \frac{\sigma_Y}{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3} = \frac{500}{200} = 2.5$$

**Step 5: Comparison.**

- $N_{MPST} = 2.94$
- $N_{Tresca} = 2.50$

The MPST gives a **higher** (less conservative) Factor of Safety than Tresca in this case. This is because both principal stresses are tensile and of the same sign, so the Poisson coupling **reduces** the effective maximum strain (as $\nu\sigma_2$ is subtracted). For **same-sign** biaxial stress states, the MPST is **less conservative** than Tresca.

$$\boxed{N_{MPST} \approx 2.94, \quad N_{Tresca} = 2.50}$$

**Model Solution for Q (b):**

**Mohr's Circle Representation:**

In Mohr's circle representation, the Maximum Principal Strain Theory is depicted as two straight lines passing through the origin of the $\sigma_1$-$\sigma_2$ stress plane, with the equation:

$$\sigma_1 - \nu\sigma_2 = \pm\sigma_Y$$

These lines are inclined at an angle $\theta$ to the $\sigma_1$-axis, where:

$$\tan\theta = \nu$$

The Mohr's circle of the given stress state must remain entirely within this envelope for the design to be safe. Failure is reached when the circle **touches** either of the two lines.

**Limitations of the Maximum Principal Strain Theory:**

1. **Inaccurate for fully plastic deformation:** The theory assumes linear elastic behavior up to failure and does not account for plastic flow in ductile materials. In metallic robotic arms that undergo significant plastic deformation, the theory over-predicts strength.

2. **Does not predict fracture under combined stresses accurately:** The theory fails to capture the effect of **intermediate principal stress** on failure. For triaxial stress states, it can give unconservative results because it ignores the hydrostatic component that suppresses ductile failure.

3. **Sensitive to Poisson's ratio:** Small errors in measuring $\nu$ (especially for anisotropic composite materials used in robotics) lead to large errors in the predicted Factor of Safety.

4. **No distinction between yielding and fracture:** The theory does not differentiate between brittle fracture and ductile yielding, making it unsuitable for materials that exhibit different behaviors in tension and compression.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Compressive Case:** The most common mistake is forgetting to flip the sign when the maximum principal stress is **compressive**. Always use $\nu(\sigma_1 + \sigma_2) - \sigma_3 = \sigma_Y / N$ when the critical strain is $\varepsilon_3$.
> 2. **Plane Stress vs. Plane Strain Confusion:** In 2D problems, students often incorrectly treat it as plane strain, leading to a missing factor of $\nu$ in the strain equations. Always assume **plane stress** ($\sigma_3 = 0$) unless explicitly stated otherwise.
> 3. **Mixing Theories:** Do not mix the failure formulas of Tresca or Von Mises with the Maximum Principal Strain criterion — each theory has its own unique mathematical form.
> 4. **Units:** $\sigma_Y$ should be in the same units as the computed stress quantities. Mixing MPa with N/m² is a guaranteed **0.5 mark deduction**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Theory Name:** Maximum Principal Strain Theory, also called **Saint-Venant's Theory** (1837).
- **Core Statement:** Failure occurs when the maximum absolute principal strain $\varepsilon_{max}$ equals the yield strain $\varepsilon_Y = \sigma_Y / E$ obtained from a uniaxial tension test.
- **Key Formula (3D):** $\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3) = \sigma_Y$ (for tensile $\sigma_1$).
- **Key Formula (2D):** $\sigma_1 - \nu \sigma_2 = \sigma_Y$ (for tensile $\sigma_1$).
- **Factor of Safety:** $N = \sigma_Y / \left[\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)\right]$.
- **Best Suited For:** Brittle materials (cast iron, ceramics), composite robotic parts, soft robotics, and constrained structures.
- **Not Recommended For:** Fully ductile materials under predominantly uniaxial loading.
- **Geometric Distinction:** The MPST envelope in 2D stress space is a parallelogram with sides inclined at $\arctan(\nu)$ to the $\sigma_1$ axis, **not** a square like Rankine or a rotated square like Tresca.
- **Special Case:** For pure shear, MPST gives the same result as Tresca: $N = \sigma_Y / (2\tau)$.
- **Poisson's Ratio Effect:** The sign of $\nu\sigma_2$ amplifies the strain when $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ have **opposite signs** (mixed mode) and reduces it when they have the **same sign** (uniaxial or equibiaxial tension).
- **Historical Note:** Saint-Venant proposed this theory in 1837, making it one of the earliest attempts to extend the uniaxial yield criterion to multiaxial stress states.
- **Comparison Anchor:** MPST is **less conservative** than Tresca for same-sign biaxial tension, but **more conservative** for mixed-sign stress states.
- **Always** compute the principal stresses first using Mohr's circle or eigenvalue methods before applying the criterion.
- **Always** check both $\varepsilon_1$ and $\varepsilon_3$ (or $\varepsilon_2$ in 2D) to identify the critical maximum strain.
- **Always** state the value of $\nu$ clearly and check if the material is given as isotropic; for anisotropic composites, modified formulas with directional Poisson's ratios must be used.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strain Energy and Shear Strain Energy Theory (Von Mises Theory)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT525 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS OF ROBOTIC PARTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Strain Energy and Shear Strain Energy Theory (Von Mises Theory)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Strain Energy — Formal Definition

**Strain Energy (U)** is the internal work done by the internal forces of a deformable body against its deformation, stored as potential energy within the volume of the body. It is mathematically defined as the work done by external forces when a body is loaded gradually from an unloaded state to a loaded state, assuming the loading is **quasi-static** and **elastic**.

$$U = \int_{0}^{\delta} P \, d\delta$$

where $P$ is the gradually applied load and $\delta$ is the corresponding displacement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Strain energy is the energy absorbed by a member due to elastic deformation. For robotic structural components, this stored energy is critical because sudden release (e.g., in a brittle fracture) can cause catastrophic failure of grippers, links, and joints.

The **Strain Energy Density (u)** is the strain energy per unit volume:

$$u = \frac{U}{V} \quad \left[ \text{Units: N·m/m}^3 = \text{J/m}^3 \right]$$

---

### 1.2 Shear Strain Energy (Distortion Energy) — Formal Definition

**Shear Strain Energy (Distortion Energy, $U_d$)** is the portion of the total strain energy that is responsible for **shape change** (distortion) of the material element, *excluding* the energy associated with volumetric change (dilation).

For a 3D stress state, total strain energy is split into two parts:

$$U_{total} = U_{volumetric} + U_{distortion}$$

$$U_{total} = \frac{1}{2E} \left( \sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_z^2 \right) - \frac{\nu}{E} \left( \sigma_x \sigma_y + \sigma_y \sigma_z + \sigma_z \sigma_x \right) + \frac{1}{2G} \left( \tau_{xy}^2 + \tau_{yz}^2 + \tau_{zx}^2 \right)$$

The **distortion energy component** alone is:

$$u_d = \frac{1+\nu}{6E} \left[ (\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + (\sigma_y - \sigma_z)^2 + (\sigma_z - \sigma_x)^2 + 6(\tau_{xy}^2 + \tau_{yz}^2 + \tau_{zx}^2) \right]$$

---

### 1.3 Von Mises Theory — Formal Definition

**Von Mises Theory (Distortion Energy Theory / Max Distortion Energy Criterion)** states that a ductile material begins to yield when the **distortion strain energy** in the stressed element reaches a value equal to the distortion strain energy at the yield point in a simple tension test conducted on the same material.

The **Von Mises Stress (Equivalent Stress, $\sigma_{vm}$ or $\sigma_e$)** is a scalar representation of the 3D stress state:

$$\sigma_{vm} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2} \left[ (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2 \right]}$$

The **Yield Criterion** is:

$$\sigma_{vm} \leq \sigma_y \quad (\text{for safety})$$

where $\sigma_y$ is the **yield stress** of the material, typically a value like **250 MPa** for mild steel or **6061-T6 aluminum**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Von Mises" for Robots?** Robotic arms and joints are subjected to complex 3D stress states (combined bending, torsion, and axial loads). A single Von Mises scalar value allows engineers to compare the actual stress to material yield strength using a single number — perfect for FEA (Finite Element Analysis) of robot links, gear shafts, and gripper fingers.

---

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

**Analogy 1 — The Stretched Rubber Band:**
Imagine pulling a rubber band slowly. You are doing work against the elastic forces of the rubber. If you release the band, the energy you put in is given back — this is **strain energy**. The harder you pull, the more energy is stored. If you pull too hard (beyond the elastic limit), the band yields and deforms permanently. **Von Mises theory** says a material "yields" (deforms permanently) when the *shape-changing* portion of this stored energy becomes too large.

**Analogy 2 — The Inflated Balloon Under Squeeze:**
Hold a balloon in your hand and squeeze it. The air inside gets compressed (volumetric change — does NOT cause yield) while the balloon's shape distorts (shape change — DOES cause yield). Von Mises theory only cares about the **shape change part** because metals and alloys yield due to shear-driven slip along crystal planes, not due to uniform compression. This is the **"Distortion Energy"** concept.

**Analogy 3 — Robotic Gripper:**
A robot gripper holding a payload experiences bending in the finger, torsion at the wrist, and axial force in the arm. Each of these stores different forms of strain energy. The Von Mises criterion combines them into a single equivalent stress that can be compared to the material's yield strength — a fundamental check in robotic structural design.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** Hydrostatic pressure (equal compression on all sides) does NOT cause yielding of ductile materials. Only the *distortional* component does. This is why Von Mises (and Tresca) focus on deviatoric stress, not hydrostatic stress.

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Values

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value (Mild Steel) | Typical Value (Al 6061-T6) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Young's Modulus | $E$ | **200 GPa** | **69 GPa** |
| Poisson's Ratio | $\nu$ | **0.30** | **0.33** |
| Shear Modulus | $G$ | **77 GPa** | **26 GPa** |
| Yield Strength | $\sigma_y$ | **250 MPa** | **240 MPa** |
| Ultimate Tensile Strength | $\sigma_u$ | **410 MPa** | **310 MPa** |
