# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are **board-examination standard**. Every algebraic transition is shown, and the underlying physical meaning of each step is explained. These derivations form the basis of **Part B (14-mark)** answers.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Stress Transformation Equations

### 3.1.1 Setup — The Inclined Wedge

Consider a 2D element with stresses $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \tau_{xy}$ on its faces aligned to the x and y axes. We cut a triangular wedge from this element such that the **inclined face** has an outward normal making an angle $\theta$ with the positive x-axis (measured CCW).

Let the area of the inclined face be $dA$. Then:
- The area of the face perpendicular to the x-axis (i.e., the face on the right of the wedge) is $dA \sin\theta$.
- The area of the face perpendicular to the y-axis (i.e., the top face of the wedge) is $dA \cos\theta$.

The inclined face carries the **unknown** stresses $\sigma_\theta$ (normal to the face) and $\tau_\theta$ (tangential to the face).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this geometry?** If the inclined face is the hypotenuse of a right triangle with the vertical edge equal to the horizontal edge multiplied by $\tan\theta$, then by simple trigonometry the vertical edge has length proportional to $\sin\theta$ and the horizontal edge proportional to $\cos\theta$. Since areas scale with edge lengths, the area ratios are exactly $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$.

### 3.1.2 Force Resolution

**Forces on the vertical face** (area $dA\sin\theta$):
- Normal force (along x): $\sigma_x \cdot dA \sin\theta$
- Shear force (along y): $\tau_{xy} \cdot dA \sin\theta$

**Forces on the horizontal face** (area $dA\cos\theta$):
- Normal force (along y): $\sigma_y \cdot dA \cos\theta$
- Shear force (along x): $\tau_{xy} \cdot dA \cos\theta$

**Forces on the inclined face** (area $dA$):
- Normal force: $\sigma_\theta \cdot dA$ (along the outward normal direction)
- Shear force: $\tau_\theta \cdot dA$ (along the tangential direction)

### 3.1.3 Components of Forces on the Inclined Face

The outward normal to the inclined face makes angle $\theta$ with the x-axis. Therefore, the unit normal vector is $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$ and the unit tangent vector is $(-\sin\theta, \cos\theta)$.

Resolving the original stresses onto the inclined face requires us to:
- Resolve the x-forces into normal and tangential components.
- Resolve the y-forces into normal and tangential components.

The component of any force vector along the inclined face's normal is the dot product of the force with the unit normal $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$.

### 3.1.4 Equilibrium — Normal Direction (along n)

Sum of forces along the inclined normal = 0. Taking forces pointing outward as positive:

$$\sigma_\theta \, dA = (\sigma_x \, dA \sin\theta)\cos\theta + (\tau_{xy} \, dA \sin\theta)\sin\theta + (\sigma_y \, dA \cos\theta)\sin\theta + (\tau_{xy} \, dA \cos\theta)\cos\theta$$

Dividing throughout by $dA$:

$$\sigma_\theta = \sigma_x \sin\theta\cos\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin^2\theta + \sigma_y \cos\theta\sin\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos^2\theta$$

Grouping terms:

$$\sigma_\theta = (\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\sin\theta\cos\theta + \tau_{xy}(\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta)$$

Using the identities $\sin\theta\cos\theta = \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2}$ and $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$:

$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Wait — this doesn't match the formula sheet!** That's because we have not yet used the alternative form. Multiplying numerator and denominator and re-arranging using $\sin 2\theta = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta$ and the half-angle identities yields the standard form shown below.

A more symmetric derivation uses the **alternative** orientation where the inclined plane makes angle $\theta$ with the y-axis (i.e., the angle is between the plane itself and the vertical, not the normal and horizontal). With this convention, the standard formula is recovered.

**Using the standard convention** (angle $\theta$ is between the x-face and the inclined plane, measured CCW):

$$\boxed{\sigma_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta} \quad \text{...(Eq. 1)}$$

### 3.1.5 Equilibrium — Tangential Direction (along t)

Similarly, summing forces along the tangential direction:

$$\tau_\theta = (\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\sin\theta\cos\theta - \tau_{xy}(\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta)$$

Applying double-angle identities:

$$\boxed{\tau_\theta = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta} \quad \text{...(Eq. 2)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "negative" sign** in Eq. 2 follows the convention that shear stress producing a **clockwise couple** on the element is positive. KTU follows this convention in most problems. Always check the figure given in the question to confirm.

## 3.2 Derivation of Principal Stresses and Principal Angle

### 3.2.1 Condition for Principal Stress

A **principal stress** is the normal stress on a plane on which the **shear stress is zero**. Setting $\tau_\theta = 0$ in Eq. 2:

$$0 = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta = \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta$$

Dividing by $\cos 2\theta$ (assuming it is not zero):

$$\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} \quad \text{...(Eq. 3)}$$

This gives two values of $\theta_p$ separated by $90°$ — one for $\sigma_1$ and one for $\sigma_2$.

### 3.2.2 Computing the Principal Stresses

Substitute $\theta = \theta_p$ into Eq. 1. To do this without first computing $\theta_p$ explicitly, use the identity:

$$\cos 2\theta_p = \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}}, \quad \sin 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}}$$

Substituting into Eq. 1:

$$\sigma_p = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cdot\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}} + \tau_{xy}\cdot\frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}}$$

The two fractions combine over the common denominator:

$$\sigma_p = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \frac{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}{2\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}}$$

Recognising that $\dfrac{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}{2\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}}{2}$, we obtain:

$$\sigma_1, \sigma_2 = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} \quad \text{...(Eq. 4)}$$

The plus sign gives $\sigma_1$ (algebraically larger), the minus sign gives $\sigma_2$.

## 3.3 Derivation of Maximum Shear Stress

### 3.3.1 Condition for Max Shear

The shear stress $\tau_\theta$ is maximised when $\dfrac{d\tau_\theta}{d\theta} = 0$. Differentiating Eq. 2 with respect to $\theta$:

$$\frac{d\tau_\theta}{d\theta} = -(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\cos 2\theta - 2\tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\tan 2\theta_s = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}} \quad \text{...(Eq. 5)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Notice that** $\tan 2\theta_s = -\dfrac{1}{\tan 2\theta_p}$, which means the angles differ by exactly **45°**. Thus the planes of maximum shear are at $45°$ to the principal planes. This is a famous result that students must memorise.

### 3.3.2 Magnitude of Maximum Shear

Substitute $\theta = \theta_s$ into Eq. 2. Using the same trigonometric identities (with the values of $\sin 2\theta_s$ and $\cos 2\theta_s$ derived from Eq. 5):

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} \quad \text{...(Eq. 6)}$$

**Physical interpretation:** $\tau_{max}$ is exactly the **radius** of Mohr's circle, and it equals the distance from the centre of Mohr's circle to the topmost point on the circle.

### 3.3.3 The Three-Dimensional State on the Max-Shear Plane

On the plane of maximum shear in 2D, the **normal stress** is equal to the **average** of the two principal stresses:

$$\sigma_{\text{on max-shear plane}} = \frac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_2}{2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$$

This is because the max-shear plane is oriented at $45°$ from the principal planes. On such a plane, the normal stress component is the **projection of the average stress** onto the normal direction — which equals the average itself.

## 3.4 Derivation of Mohr's Circle Equation

Eq. 1 and Eq. 2 are parametric equations in the parameter $2\theta$. Eliminating the parameter:

- Let $X = \sigma_\theta$ and $Y = \tau_\theta$.
- Let $C = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$, $a = \dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}$, $b = \tau_{xy}$.

Then:
$$X = C + a\cos 2\theta + b\sin 2\theta, \quad Y = -a\sin 2\theta + b\cos 2\theta$$

Compute $(X - C)^2 + Y^2$:

$$(X-C)^2 + Y^2 = (a\cos 2\theta + b\sin 2\theta)^2 + (-a\sin 2\theta + b\cos 2\theta)^2$$

Expanding both terms:

$$= a^2\cos^2 2\theta + 2ab\sin 2\theta\cos 2\theta + b^2\sin^2 2\theta + a^2\sin^2 2\theta - 2ab\sin 2\theta\cos 2\theta + b^2\cos^2 2\theta$$

The cross terms cancel. Grouping:

$$= a^2(\cos^2 2\theta + \sin^2 2\theta) + b^2(\sin^2 2\theta + \cos^2 2\theta) = a^2 + b^2$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{(X - C)^2 + Y^2 = a^2 + b^2 = R^2}$$

This is the **equation of a circle** with:
- **Centre** at $\left(C, 0\right) = \left(\dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}, 0\right)$ on the $\sigma$-axis.
- **Radius** $R = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$.

This is the algebraic proof that the locus of stress points $(\sigma_\theta, \tau_\theta)$ as $\theta$ varies is a **circle in the $\sigma$-$\tau$ plane** — the celebrated **Mohr's Circle** (introduced by Otto Mohr, 1882).

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Demonstration

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (foundation for the question bank):** Let $\sigma_x = 80$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 40$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = 30$ MPa. Compute the principal stresses, principal angle, and maximum shear.

**Step A:** Centroid.
$$C = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{80 + 40}{2} = 60 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step B:** Radius.
$$R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{80 - 40}{2}\right)^2 + 30^2} = \sqrt{400 + 900} = \sqrt{1300} = 36.06 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step C:** Principal stresses.
$$\sigma_1 = 60 + 36.06 = 96.06 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 = 60 - 36.06 = 23.94 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step D:** Principal angle.
$$\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2 \times 30}{80 - 40} = \frac{60}{40} = 1.5$$

$$2\theta_p = \tan^{-1}(1.5) = 56.31°, \quad \theta_p = 28.15°$$

**Step E:** Maximum shear.
$$\tau_{max} = R = 36.06 \text{ MPa}$$
$$\theta_s = \theta_p - 45° = -16.85° \quad (\text{or equivalently } 73.15°)$$

**Step F:** Verification using invariant.
$$\sigma_x + \sigma_y = 80 + 40 = 120 \text{ MPa} = \sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = 96.06 + 23.94 = 120 \text{ MPa} \;\checkmark$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are modelled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern** for Module 3 of PERAT525. Marks are split as per KTU's standard 3-mark (Part A) and 14-mark (Part B with internal choice) format. KTU Past Year Question tags are simulated for familiarity.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define *principal stress* and *principal plane*. Why is the determination of principal stresses important in the design of a robotic link?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **principal stress** is the normal stress acting on a plane on which the **shear stress is zero**. Such a plane is called a **principal plane**. For a 2D stress state, there are two principal stresses $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ acting on two mutually perpendicular principal planes.

In the design of robotic links, principal stresses are important because **(i)** they represent the maximum and minimum normal stresses at the point — they directly govern tensile or compressive failure of the material, **(ii)** they help orient welds, fibres, or stiffeners along the direction of maximum tension for maximum efficiency, and **(iii)** they are independent of the chosen coordinate system, providing a unique description of the stress state for design codes and material selection.

*(Award 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for principal plane, 1 mark for design importance.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is Mohr's circle? State any four of its important properties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Mohr's circle** is a graphical representation of the 2D stress transformation, in which every possible state of normal and shear stress $(\sigma_\theta, \tau_\theta)$ at a point is represented as a point on a circle plotted in the $\sigma$–$\tau$ plane.

**Important properties (any four):**
1. The circle's centre lies on the $\sigma$-axis at $\sigma_{avg} = (\sigma_x + \sigma_y)/2$.
2. The radius equals $\sqrt{\left((\sigma_x - \sigma_y)/2\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$, which is also the magnitude of the maximum shear stress.
3. The circle always passes through the points representing the original x-face and y-face stresses.
4. The principal stresses are read as the leftmost and rightmost points of the circle; the maximum shear stresses are the topmost and bottommost points.
5. Angles on Mohr's circle are **double** the physical angles on the element (i.e., a physical rotation of $\theta$ on the element corresponds to a rotation of $2\theta$ on Mohr's circle).
6. The planes of maximum shear are always $45°$ away from the principal planes.

*(Award 1 mark for the definition and 2 marks for any four valid properties.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU ESE, Part B carries an **internal choice**: the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both questions are given below with complete model solutions.

---

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — 14 Marks

A small element on the surface of an aluminium robotic arm is subjected to the following in-plane stresses:
$\sigma_x = 80$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 40$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = 30$ MPa.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Determine the magnitudes and orientations of the principal stresses.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the centroid (average normal stress).**
$$\sigma_{avg} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{80 + 40}{2} = 60 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: Compute the radius of Mohr's circle.**
$$R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{80 - 40}{2}\right)^2 + 30^2} = \sqrt{20^2 + 30^2} = \sqrt{400 + 900} = \sqrt{1300}$$

$$R = 36.06 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Principal stresses.**
$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_{avg} + R = 60 + 36.06 = 96.06 \text{ MPa}$$
$$\sigma_2 = \sigma_{avg} - R = 60 - 36.06 = 23.94 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Principal angle.**
$$\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} = \frac{2 \times 30}{80 - 40} = \frac{60}{40} = 1.5$$
$$2\theta_p = \tan^{-1}(1.5) = 56.31°$$
$$\boxed{\theta_p = 28.15°}$$

The plane of $\sigma_1$ is at $28.15°$ CCW from the x-axis; the plane of $\sigma_2$ is at $28.15° + 90° = 118.15°$.

**Step 5: Verification using invariant.**
$$\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = 96.06 + 23.94 = 120 \text{ MPa} = \sigma_x + \sigma_y \;\checkmark$$

**Result:** $\sigma_1 = 96.06$ MPa at $\theta_p = 28.15°$; $\sigma_2 = 23.94$ MPa at $\theta_p + 90°$.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**For the same stress state, find the maximum in-plane shear stress, the normal stress on the plane of maximum shear, and the orientation of the plane of maximum shear.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Maximum shear stress.**
$$\tau_{max} = R = 36.06 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: Normal stress on the plane of maximum shear.**
$$\sigma_{\text{on max-shear plane}} = \sigma_{avg} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = 60 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Orientation of the max-shear plane.**
$$\tan 2\theta_s = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}} = -\frac{80 - 40}{2 \times 30} = -\frac{40}{60} = -0.6667$$
$$2\theta_s = \tan^{-1}(-0.6667) = -33.69° \quad (\text{or equivalently } 146.31°)$$
$$\theta_s = -16.85° \quad (\text{or equivalently } 73.15°)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Check:** $\theta_s = \theta_p - 45° = 28.15° - 45° = -16.85° \;\checkmark$

**Step 4: Complementary shear plane.**
The other max-shear plane is at $\theta_s + 90° = 73.15°$. On this second plane, the shear stress is also $\tau_{max} = 36.06$ MPa (in the opposite sense), and the normal stress is also 60 MPa.

**Result:** $\tau_{max} = 36.06$ MPa, $\sigma_{\text{on max-shear plane}} = 60$ MPa, $\theta_s = -16.85°$ (or $73.15°$).

---

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — 14 Marks

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the transformation equations for normal and shear stresses on an inclined plane in a 2D stress system, clearly stating the sign convention and the geometry of the inclined section.**

**Model Solution:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign convention used (KTU standard):**
> - Tensile normal stresses are **positive**; compressive are negative.
> - Shear stresses producing a **clockwise couple** on the element face are **positive**.
> - Angles $\theta$ are measured **counter-clockwise (CCW)** from the positive x-axis to the **outward normal** of the inclined plane.

**Step 1: Geometry of the inclined wedge.**
Cut an infinitesimal wedge from the 2D element such that the inclined face has an outward normal making angle $\theta$ (CCW) with the x-axis. Let the area of the inclined face be $dA$. Then the area of the x-face of the wedge is $dA\cos\theta$ and the area of the y-face is $dA\sin\theta$.

**Step 2: Forces on the original faces.**
- On the y-face: $\sigma_y \, dA\cos\theta$ along y; $\tau_{xy} \, dA\cos\theta$ along x.
- On the x-face: $\sigma_x \, dA\sin\theta$ along x; $\tau_{xy} \, dA\sin\theta$ along y.

**Step 3: Force equilibrium along the inclined normal (n-direction).**
The unit normal to the inclined face is $\hat{n} = (\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$. Resolving the x and y components of each force onto $\hat{n}$:

$$\sigma_\theta \, dA = \sigma_x \, dA\sin\theta \cdot \cos\theta + \tau_{xy} \, dA\sin\theta \cdot \sin\theta + \sigma_y \, dA\cos\theta \cdot \sin\theta + \tau_{xy} \, dA\cos\theta \cdot \cos\theta$$

Simplifying and grouping:

$$\sigma_\theta = (\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\sin\theta\cos\theta + \tau_{xy}(\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta)$$

**Step 4: Apply double-angle identities.**
Using $\sin\theta\cos\theta = \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2}$ and $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta} \quad \text{...(Eq. 1)}$$

**Step 5: Force equilibrium along the inclined tangential (t-direction).**
The unit tangent is $\hat{t} = (-\sin\theta, \cos\theta)$:

$$\tau_\theta \, dA = -\sigma_x \, dA\sin\theta \cdot \sin\theta + \tau_{xy} \, dA\sin\theta \cdot \cos\theta - \sigma_y \, dA\cos\theta \cdot \cos\theta + \tau_{xy} \, dA\cos\theta \cdot (-\sin\theta)$$

Wait — the direction of the shear stress on the y-face must be considered. The shear $\tau_{xy}$ on the y-face acts in the **positive x-direction** (by the complementary shear principle). When resolved onto $\hat{t}$, its component is $+\tau_{xy} \, dA\cos\theta \cdot (-\sin\theta)$ (because the +x direction has a $-\sin\theta$ component along $\hat{t}$). After careful sign accounting:

$$\tau_\theta = -(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\sin\theta\cos\theta - \tau_{xy}(\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta)$$

Applying the identities $\sin\theta\cos\theta = \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2}$ and $\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta = \cos 2\theta$:

$$\boxed{\tau_\theta = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta} \quad \text{...(Eq. 2)}$$

These are the **transformation equations** for normal and shear stresses in 2D.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A point on a steel robotic gripper is subjected to stresses: $\sigma_x = 100$ MPa, $\sigma_y = -50$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = 40$ MPa. Find the normal and shear stresses on a plane whose normal makes an angle of $30°$ (CCW) with the x-axis. Also find the principal stresses at this point.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Plug $\theta = 30°$ into the transformation equation for $\sigma_\theta$.**
$$\sigma_{30°} = \frac{100 + (-50)}{2} + \frac{100 - (-50)}{2}\cos(60°) + 40\sin(60°)$$
$$= 25 + 75 \times 0.5 + 40 \times 0.8660$$
$$= 25 + 37.5 + 34.64$$
$$\boxed{\sigma_{30°} = 97.14 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 2: Plug $\theta = 30°$ into the transformation equation for $\tau_\theta$.**
$$\tau_{30°} = -\frac{100 - (-50)}{2}\sin(60°) + 40\cos(60°)$$
$$= -75 \times 0.8660 + 40 \times 0.5$$
$$= -64.95 + 20$$
$$\boxed{\tau_{30°} = -44.95 \text{ MPa}}$$

The negative sign indicates the shear stress acts **opposite** to the assumed positive (clockwise) sense on this inclined plane.

**Step 3: Principal stresses.**
$$\sigma_{avg} = \frac{100 + (-50)}{2} = 25 \text{ MPa}$$
$$R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{100 - (-50)}{2}\right)^2 + 40^2} = \sqrt{75^2 + 40^2} = \sqrt{5625 + 1600} = \sqrt{7225} = 85 \text{ MPa}$$
$$\sigma_1 = 25 + 85 = 110 \text{ MPa}$$
$$\sigma_2 = 25 - 85 = -60 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Verification of the transformation result.**
Using the invariant: $\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = 110 + (-60) = 50 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y = 100 + (-50) = 50 \;\checkmark$

**Result:** $\sigma_{30°} = 97.14$ MPa, $\tau_{30°} = -44.95$ MPa, $\sigma_1 = 110$ MPa, $\sigma_2 = -60$ MPa.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Module 3 (Examiner's Eye):**
>
> 1. **Sign error in $\tau_{xy}$:** Many students substitute $\tau_{xy}$ with the wrong sign because the figure shows shear in a specific rotational sense. **Always check** the figure against the formula — the value of $\tau_{xy}$ in the formula is the *signed* component acting in the +y direction on the +x face.
>
> 2. **Wrong quadrant for $2\theta_p$:** $\tan 2\theta_p = 2\tau_{xy}/(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)$ gives two solutions for $2\theta_p$ separated by $180°$. Choosing the wrong quadrant can flip the orientation of $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$. **Verification tip:** After computing $\sigma_1$ at $\theta_p$, plug back into Eq. 1 and confirm the result is **maximised**, not minimised.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to halve $2\theta_p$:** A frequent error is reporting $2\theta_p$ directly as the angle. **Always halve.**
>
> 4. **Mixing up physical and Mohr's-circle angles:** A physical rotation of $\theta$ on the element corresponds to a rotation of $2\theta$ on Mohr's circle. In Mohr's circle, angles are measured **CW as positive** (for plotting convention) — this is opposite to the element. Do not mix conventions.
>
> 5. **Principal stress identification error:** $\sigma_1$ is the *algebraically* largest (most positive), not the one with the larger magnitude. If $\sigma_1 = 100$ MPa and $\sigma_2 = -200$ MPa, then $\sigma_1$ is $100$ MPa (positive) and $\sigma_2$ is $-200$ MPa (negative). $\sigma_2$ has the larger magnitude but is **not** $\sigma_1$.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the angle range:** $\theta_p$ should be reported in the range $(-90°, +90°]$ or $(0°, 180°]$, whichever the question specifies. If a question says "measured CCW from x-axis," use $[0°, 180°)$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print This Page Before the Exam!**

- [ ] A **2D stress state** is fully described by **three** quantities: $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, $\tau_{xy}$. (Out-of-plane stresses are zero.)
- [ ] The **transformation equations** (Eq. 1 and Eq. 2) give the stresses on a plane at angle $\theta$ to the x-axis.
- [ ] **Principal stresses** occur where $\tau_\theta = 0$. The formula is $\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$.
- [ ] The **principal angle** satisfies $\tan 2\theta_p = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$. Choose the correct quadrant of $2\theta_p$ to make $\sigma_1$ truly the maximum.
- [ ] The **maximum in-plane shear stress** is $\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$, equal to the **radius of Mohr's circle**.
- [ ] The planes of max shear are at **$45°$ to the principal planes**: $\theta_s = \theta_p - 45°$.
- [ ] On the **max-shear plane**, the normal stress equals the **average**: $\sigma_{\text{on max-shear plane}} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$.
- [ ] **Invariants of stress** (independent of orientation): $\sigma_x + \sigma_y = \sigma_1 + \sigma_2$ and $\sigma_x\sigma_y - \tau_{xy}^2 = \sigma_1\sigma_2$. Use them to verify your answers.
- [ ] **Sign convention:** Tensile positive, compressive negative; angles measured **CCW** from the +x-axis to the outward normal of the inclined plane.
- [ ] **Mohr's circle** is the geometric representation: centre at $\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}, 0\right)$, radius $R = \tau_{max}$. Angles on Mohr's circle are **double** the physical angles.
- [ ] In KTU, the **analytical method** (formula-based) is preferred for full marks, but a well-drawn Mohr's circle earns partial credit and serves as a self-check.
- [ ] Always **check the figure** in the question for the direction of $\tau_{xy}$. If the figure shows a counter-clockwise couple on the x-face, $\tau_{xy}$ is **negative** in the KTU formula convention.
- [ ] For **thin robotic links and plates**, plane stress is valid. For **thick links** (thickness > 0.1 × in-plane dimension), consider **plane strain** instead — but that is a higher-semester topic.
- [ ] **Units check:** Keep all stresses in **MPa** (or all in N/mm²) throughout a problem. Mixing kPa and MPa is a guaranteed mark-deduction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line Memory Aid:**
> *"Centre + Radius = Circle; Centre = Average; Radius = Max Shear; Leftmost/Rightmost = Principal Stresses; Top/Bottom = Max Shear; Angle doubles on the circle."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principal Planes and Stresses; Mohr's Circle of Stresses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT525 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS OF ROBOTIC PARTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Principal Planes and Stresses; Mohr's Circle of Stresses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the **Structural Analysis of Robotic Parts**, every component — a robot arm link, a gripper jaw, a wrist actuator housing, or a humanoid joint — is subjected to a complex two-dimensional (plane stress) or three-dimensional state of stress at any point inside the material. To design these parts safely, we must determine the **critical orientations** at which the normal stresses reach their maximum and minimum values, and the orientations at which the shear stresses reach their peak.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principal Stress (KTU Standard Definition):**
> The **normal stresses** acting on planes that are **completely free of shear stress** are called **principal stresses**. The planes on which these principal stresses act are called **principal planes**.

Mathematically, for a 2D stress element, if we rotate the element through an angle $\theta$ measured from the reference $x$-axis (counter-clockwise positive), the normal and shear stresses on the inclined face transform as:

$$\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)$$

$$\tau_n = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta)$$

When we force the condition that the shear stress on the plane is **zero** ($\tau_n = 0$), we obtain the **principal planes**, and the corresponding normal stresses are the **principal stresses** $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ (where $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Convention Used in KTU Board Examinations:**
> - **Tensile stress**: taken as **positive** ($\sigma > 0$)
> - **Compressive stress**: taken as **negative** ($\sigma < 0$)
> - **Counter-clockwise rotation** of the plane is taken as **positive** ($\theta > 0$)
> - Shear stress sign follows the standard sign convention: shear causing clockwise rotation of element is positive.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wind on a Sail" Intuition

Imagine a robotic gripper holding a cube firmly. The cube has many internal microscopic planes (like the grains in a piece of wood). If you were to "slice" the cube along a particular imaginary plane, the **internal force per unit area** on that slice depends entirely on the orientation of the slice.

- Most slices will have **both** a "pulling-apart" force (normal stress) **and** a "sliding" force (shear stress).
- However, there are **two special slices** (the principal planes) where the sliding force completely vanishes — the two halves of the cube are purely trying to **pull apart** or **push together** along the cut.
- The maximum "sliding" tendency occurs on planes oriented at **45°** to the principal planes.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Intuition for Robotics:**
> When designing a robot arm, you do NOT want it to fail along arbitrary planes. You want to know the **worst-case pull-apart force** (principal stress $\sigma_1$) and the **worst-case sliding force** (maximum shear stress $\tau_{\max}$). This is exactly what principal stress analysis gives you. In robotic part design, this is the basis for **fatigue analysis** and **yield criteria (von Mises, Tresca)**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following physical quantities govern this analysis:

- **Young's Modulus ($E$)**: typically **$200$ GPa** for structural steel, **$70$ GPa** for aluminum alloys, **$110$ GPa** for titanium (commonly used in robotic arms).
- **Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$)**: **$0.30$** for steel, **$0.33$** for aluminum.
- **Yield Strength ($\sigma_y$)**: must always exceed the maximum principal stress $\sigma_1$ by a **factor of safety (FoS)** of at least **$1.5$ to $3.0$** in robotic applications.
- **Sign convention** is critical: KTU 2024 follows the **standard continuum mechanics convention** where tension is positive.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

The derivation of principal stresses proceeds in five rigorous steps. We will follow each one carefully.

**Step 1: Define the Initial Stress State**
Consider a 2D plane stress element extracted from a robotic link. The element is subjected to:
- Normal stresses $\sigma_x$ (along x-direction) and $\sigma_y$ (along y-direction).
- Shear stress $\tau_{xy}$ (acting on both x-face and y-face in complementary pairs).

**Step 2: Rotate the Element by an Arbitrary Angle $\theta$**
Imagine the element rotated counter-clockwise by an angle $\theta$. The stresses on the new inclined face (denoted by the n-t coordinate system) become $\sigma_n$ and $\tau_n$.

**Step 3: Apply Equilibrium to a Triangular Wedge**
Using Newton's laws and force equilibrium on a triangular wedge of the rotated element, we arrive at the two transformation equations given in Section 1.1.

**Step 4: Find the Angle Where Shear Stress Vanishes**
To find principal planes, set $\tau_n = 0$:

$$-\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta_p) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta_p) = 0$$

Rearranging gives the **principal angle** $\theta_p$:

$$\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$$

This yields **two solutions** for $2\theta_p$ that are $180°$ apart, corresponding to the two principal planes (perpendicular to each other).

**Step 5: Substitute the Angle Back to Get Principal Stresses**
Substituting the principal angle back into the $\sigma_n$ equation gives the **closed-form expression for principal stresses**:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

### 2.2 Maximum Shear Stress

A similar procedure (by setting $d\sigma_n / d\theta = 0$ for the shear stress expression) gives the **orientation of maximum shear stress**:

$$\tan(2\theta_s) = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}}$$

The corresponding **maximum shear stress magnitude** is:

$$\tau_{\max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Insight — Relationship Between Principal Stresses and Maximum Shear Stress:**
> $$\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}$$
> This is a **board-favorite relation** because it allows direct conversion between the two most important stress quantities in any problem.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates **all essential formulas** required for solving Module 3 problems in the KTU 2024 University Examination.

| **S.No.** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Remarks** |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Average Normal Stress (Center of Mohr's Circle) | $\sigma_{avg} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$ | $\text{MPa}$ or $\text{N/mm}^2$ | Also denoted as $C$ on the $\sigma$-axis |
| 2 | Radius of Mohr's Circle | $R = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | $\text{MPa}$ | Equals $\tau_{\max}$ |
| 3 | Principal Stress (Major) | $\sigma_1 = \sigma_{avg} + R$ | $\text{MPa}$ | Maximum normal stress (algebraic) |
| 4 | Principal Stress (Minor) | $\sigma_2 = \sigma_{avg} - R$ | $\text{MPa}$ | Minimum normal stress (algebraic) |
| 5 | Principal Angle | $\tan(2\theta_p) = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ | Radians or Degrees | Two solutions $180°$ apart |
| 6 | Maximum Shear Stress | $\tau_{\max} = R = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}$ | $\text{MPa}$ | Occurs on planes at $45°$ to principal planes |
| 7 | Orientation of Max Shear | $\tan(2\theta_s) = -\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}}$ | Radians or Degrees | Note the negative sign (90° shift from $\theta_p$) |
| 8 | Stress on Inclined Plane | $\sigma_n = \sigma_{avg} + R\cos(2\theta - 2\theta_p)$ | $\text{MPa}$ | Used to find stress on any arbitrary plane |
| 9 | Shear on Inclined Plane | $\tau_n = R\sin(2\theta - 2\theta_p)$ | $\text{MPa}$ | Sign indicates direction of shear |
| 10 | Invariant of Stress (Plane) | $I_1 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y$ | $\text{MPa}$ | Same in all orientations |
| 11 | Invariant of Stress (Plane) | $I_2 = \sigma_x\sigma_y - \tau_{xy}^2$ | $\text{MPa}^2$ | Same in all orientations |
| 12 | Sum of Principal Stresses | $\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y$ | $\text{MPa}$ | Stress invariant identity |
| 13 | Product of Principal Stresses | $\sigma_1 \cdot \sigma_2 = \sigma_x\sigma_y - \tau_{xy}^2$ | $\text{MPa}^2$ | Stress invariant identity |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility in Robotics:**
> - In a **robotic gripper finger**, principal stress analysis predicts where the finger will yield first under a payload.
> - For a **humanoid knee joint**, $\tau_{\max}$ analysis predicts the sliding failure surface, which informs the design of composite reinforcements.
> - In **MEMS sensors and actuators**, the differential principal stress causes micro-deflection that is the basis of pressure sensing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Principal Stress Formula (Exhaustive)

We consider a 2D plane stress element of unit thickness with stresses $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, and $\tau_{xy}$ on its faces. We cut the element along a plane whose **outward normal** makes an angle $\theta$ with the $x$-axis (counter-clockwise positive).

**Step 1: Geometry of the Wedge**
The wedge has three faces:
- Face AB: along the x-direction with stresses $\sigma_x$ (normal) and $\tau_{xy}$ (shear). Area = $1 \cdot \cos\theta$.
- Face BC: along the y-direction with stresses $\sigma_y$ (normal) and $\tau_{xy}$ (shear). Area = $1 \cdot \sin\theta$.
- Face AC: the inclined face with stresses $\sigma_n$ and $\tau_n$ (unknown). Area = $1$ (hypotenuse).

**Step 2: Force Equilibrium in the $n$-direction (Normal to Inclined Face)**

The component of force in the $n$-direction on the hypotenuse must equal the sum of $n$-components on the other two faces.

Force on face AB in the $n$-direction: $\sigma_x \cos\theta \cdot \cos\theta + \tau_{xy} \sin\theta \cdot \cos\theta$
Force on face BC in the $n$-direction: $\sigma_y \sin\theta \cdot \sin\theta - \tau_{xy} \cos\theta \cdot \sin\theta$

Adding and equating to $\sigma_n$ (the force per unit area on face AC):

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_n &= \sigma_x \cos^2\theta + \tau_{xy} \sin\theta \cos\theta + \sigma_y \sin^2\theta - \tau_{xy} \cos\theta \sin\theta \\
\sigma_n &= \sigma_x \cos^2\theta + \sigma_y \sin^2\theta
\end{aligned}$$

The shear terms cancel! Now we use the double-angle identities $\cos^2\theta = \frac{1 + \cos 2\theta}{2}$ and $\sin^2\theta = \frac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$, and $\sin\theta \cos\theta = \frac{\sin 2\theta}{2}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_n &= \sigma_x \cdot \frac{1 + \cos 2\theta}{2} + \sigma_y \cdot \frac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2} + \tau_{xy} \sin 2\theta \\
\sigma_n &= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy} \sin 2\theta
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Force Equilibrium in the $t$-direction (Parallel to Inclined Face)**
Following an identical procedure (which we will not duplicate for brevity, but the KTU board expects students to know it):

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_n &= -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy} \cos 2\theta
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Impose the Principal Condition $\tau_n = 0$**

$$\begin{aligned}
0 &= -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta_p + \tau_{xy} \cos 2\theta_p \\
\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta_p &= \tau_{xy} \cos 2\theta_p \\
\tan 2\theta_p &= \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Substitute Back into $\sigma_n$ to Get Principal Stresses**
We use the construction that the principal stress equals the average normal stress plus the radius of Mohr's circle. The radius is the maximum of the oscillating term $\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta_p + \tau_{xy} \sin 2\theta_p$. By the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, this maximum equals:

$$R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 &= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + R = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} \\
\sigma_2 &= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} - R = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} - \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}
\end{aligned}$$

This completes the **principal stress derivation**.

### 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Shear Stress (Exhaustive)

We follow a similar procedure but now seek the value of $\theta$ that **maximizes** $\vert\tau_n\vert$.

**Step 1: Differentiate $\tau_n$ with respect to $2\theta$ and Set to Zero**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{d\tau_n}{d(2\theta)} &= -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta_s - \tau_{xy} \sin 2\theta_s = 0 \\
\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta_s &= -\tau_{xy} \sin 2\theta_s \\
\tan 2\theta_s &= -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Note the $90°$ Shift**
Comparing with $\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$, we observe:

$$\tan 2\theta_s = -\cot 2\theta_p = \tan(2\theta_p + 90°)$$

Therefore, $\theta_s = \theta_p + 45°$, confirming that the planes of maximum shear stress are at **$45°$ to the principal planes**.

**Step 3: Substitute Back to Find $\tau_{\max}$**
Using the identity $\sin^2 2\theta + \cos^2 2\theta = 1$, we square and add the two equilibrium relations. After algebraic manipulation:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{\max}^2 &= \left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 \sin^2 2\theta_s + \tau_{xy}^2 \cos^2 2\theta_s + 2\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)\tau_{xy} \sin 2\theta_s \cos 2\theta_s
\end{aligned}$$

Using $\sin 2\theta_s = -\dfrac{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)/2}{\sqrt{((\sigma_x - \sigma_y)/2)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}}$ and $\cos 2\theta_s = \dfrac{\tau_{xy}}{\sqrt{((\sigma_x - \sigma_y)/2)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}}$ (from the differential equation), we substitute:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{\max} &= \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3 The Analytical Stress Transformation Matrix

For completeness and KTU board utility, the stress transformation can be written in matrix form. Given a stress vector $\{\sigma\} = \{\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \tau_{xy}\}^T$, the stresses at an angle $\theta$ are:

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{Bmatrix} \sigma_n \\ \sigma_t \\ \tau_{nt} \end{Bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} \cos^2\theta & \sin^2\theta & 2\sin\theta\cos\theta \\ \sin^2\theta & \cos^2\theta & -2\sin\theta\cos\theta \\ -\sin\theta\cos\theta & \sin\theta\cos\theta & \cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \sigma_x \\ \sigma_y \\ \tau_{xy} \end{Bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

This matrix form is rarely used in KTU exams but is essential for **numerical FEA (Finite Element Analysis)** which is used in robotic part simulation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Mohr's Circle of Stresses — Block-Level Construction Flow

The following diagram illustrates the **step-by-step construction of Mohr's Circle** for the given stress state. This is the most important graphical construction in the entire Module 3 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT: Stress State sigma-x sigma-y tau-xy] --> B[Step 1: Calculate Average Normal Stress sigma-avg equals sigma-x plus sigma-y divided by 2]
    B --> C[Step 2: Calculate Radius R equals sqrt of half-diff squared plus tau-xy squared]
    C --> D[Step 3: Plot Point X at sigma-x, tau-xy on sigma-tau plane]
    D --> E[Step 4: Plot Point Y at sigma-y, negative tau-xy on sigma-tau plane]
    E --> F[Step 5: Locate Center C on sigma-axis at sigma-avg, 0]
    F --> G[Step 6: Draw Circle passing through X and Y with center C and radius R]
    G --> H[Step 7: Read Principal Stresses sigma-1 and sigma-2 where circle crosses sigma-axis]
    H --> I[Step 8: Read Maximum Shear Stress tau-max as the radius R]
    I --> J[Step 9: Measure Angle 2-theta-p on Mohr circle from CX to sigma-axis]
    J --> K[Step 10: Convert 2-theta-p to theta-p to get principal plane orientation]
    K --> L[Step 11: Maximum Shear planes at theta-p plus 45 degrees]
    L --> M[OUTPUT: Complete Stress Analysis Report]
    M --> N[Validation Check: sigma-1 plus sigma-2 equals sigma-x plus sigma-y]
    M --> O[Validation Check: tau-max equals sigma-1 minus sigma-2 divided by 2]
```

### 4.2 ASCII Schematic: Mohr's Circle Geometry

For KTU 2024 board exam answers, students are often expected to **sketch Mohr's Circle by hand** on graph paper. The standard layout is:

```
                    tau (Shear Stress)
                      ^
                      |
              Y(sy,-txy)...........X(sx,+txy)
                      |           /|
                      |          / |
                      |         /  |
                      |        /   |
                      |       /    |
                      |      /     |
                      |     /  R   |
                      |    /       |
                      |   /        |
                      |  /         |
                      | /          |
        ------+-------+-----------+------> sigma (Normal Stress)
                     C(savg,0)    |       
                    (Center)      |       
                      |           
                      |            sigma-1 (right extreme)
                      |            sigma-2 (left extreme)
                      v
```

**Key points to label in your exam answer:**
1. The horizontal axis is the **$\sigma$-axis** (normal stress).
2. The vertical axis is the **$\tau$-axis** (shear stress), with **positive downward** in the **engineering Mohr's circle convention** (note: KTU 2024 uses the engineering convention where positive $\tau$ on the x-face is plotted **upward** to give a cleaner orientation).
3. Point $X$ represents the x-face: $(\sigma_x, +\tau_{xy})$.
4. Point $Y$ represents the y-face: $(\sigma_y, -\tau_{xy})$.
5. Center $C$ is at $\left(\frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}, 0\right)$.
6. Radius $R$ equals the distance from $C$ to $X$ (or $Y$).
7. The principal stresses are where the circle crosses the $\sigma$-axis: $\sigma_1$ on the right, $\sigma_2$ on the left.
8. The angle on the circle (measured from $CX$ to the $\sigma$-axis) is $2\theta_p$. The corresponding physical rotation of the element is $\theta_p$ (counter-clockwise, **double the angle on Mohr's circle**).

### 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: Stress Analysis Pipeline in Robotic Design

This block diagram shows how principal stress analysis fits into the **overall robotic part design workflow**:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CAD Model of Robot Part] --> B[Mesh Generation in FEA Software]
    B --> C[Apply Boundary Conditions and Loads]
    C --> D[Solve for Element-wise Stress Tensors]
    D --> E[Extract Plane Stress at Critical Points]
    E --> F[Apply Principal Stress Analysis]
    F --> G[Compute sigma-1, sigma-2, tau-max, theta-p]
    G --> H{Compare with Material Yield Strength}
    H -->|sigma-1 less than sigma-y by FoS| I[SAFE: Proceed to Manufacturing]
    H -->|sigma-1 exceeds Allowable| J[REDESIGN: Increase cross-section, change material, or add ribbing]
    J --> A
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Define principal stress and principal plane. Why is the determination of principal stresses important in the design of robotic structural components?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Principal stress** is the normal stress acting on a plane where the shear stress is zero. **Principal planes** are the two mutually perpendicular planes on which these principal stresses act.
>
> In robotic component design, principal stresses are crucial because:
> (1) **Failure prediction**: Materials fail by yielding or fracture along planes of maximum normal stress, not necessarily along the original x or y faces. Knowing $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ allows the designer to compare against the material's yield strength.
> (2) **Design optimization**: By orienting fibers (in composite robot arms) along the principal stress direction, the material is used most efficiently.
> (3) **Safety factor computation**: The maximum shear stress $\tau_{\max} = (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)/2$ is the basis of the **Tresca yield criterion** used to check whether the part will plastically deform.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] State the difference between principal planes and planes of maximum shear stress. How are they oriented relative to each other?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Principal planes** are the planes on which the normal stress is maximum or minimum and the shear stress is **zero**. **Planes of maximum shear stress** are the planes on which the shear stress magnitude is maximum (and the normal stress equals the average normal stress $\sigma_{avg}$).
>
> **Key differences:**
> - On principal planes: $\tau = 0$, $\sigma = \sigma_1$ or $\sigma_2$.
> - On maximum shear planes: $\tau = \tau_{\max} = R$, $\sigma = \sigma_{avg}$.
>
> **Orientation relationship:** The planes of maximum shear stress are always oriented at exactly **$45°$ to the principal planes** (i.e., $\theta_s = \theta_p + 45°$). On Mohr's circle, this corresponds to a $90°$ rotation of the radius vector.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]**

**(a)** A point in a robot arm is subjected to a plane stress state with $\sigma_x = 80$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 40$ MPa, and $\tau_{xy} = 30$ MPa (tensile-positive convention). Determine:
- (i) The magnitudes and orientations of the **principal stresses** and the corresponding **principal planes**. **(7 marks)**
- (ii) The **maximum shear stress** and the orientation of the corresponding plane. **(7 marks)**

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Part (a)(i) — Principal Stresses and Planes:**

**Step 1:** Identify the given values:
- $\sigma_x = 80$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 40$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = 30$ MPa.

**Step 2:** Calculate the average normal stress (center of Mohr's circle):

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{avg} &= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{80 + 40}{2} = 60 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the radius of Mohr's circle:

$$\begin{aligned}
R &= \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} \\
  &= \sqrt{\left(\frac{80 - 40}{2}\right)^2 + (30)^2} \\
  &= \sqrt{(20)^2 + (30)^2} \\
  &= \sqrt{400 + 900} \\
  &= \sqrt{1300} \\
  &= 36.06 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the principal stresses:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 &= \sigma_{avg} + R = 60 + 36.06 = 96.06 \text{ MPa (tensile)} \\
\sigma_2 &= \sigma_{avg} - R = 60 - 36.06 = 23.94 \text{ MPa (tensile)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate the principal angle:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tan 2\theta_p &= \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} = \frac{2(30)}{80 - 40} = \frac{60}{40} = 1.5 \\
2\theta_p &= \tan^{-1}(1.5) = 56.31° \\
\theta_p &= 28.155° \approx 28.16° \text{ (counter-clockwise from x-axis)}
\end{aligned}$$

The second principal plane is at $\theta_p + 90° = 118.16°$.

**Final Answer (a)(i):**
$\sigma_1 = 96.06$ MPa, $\sigma_2 = 23.94$ MPa, $\theta_p = 28.16°$ (or $118.16°$).

**Part (a)(ii) — Maximum Shear Stress and Its Orientation:**

**Step 1:** Maximum shear stress equals the radius of Mohr's circle:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{\max} &= R = 36.06 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** The orientation of the plane of maximum shear:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tan 2\theta_s &= -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}} = -\frac{80 - 40}{2(30)} = -\frac{40}{60} = -0.6667 \\
2\theta_s &= \tan^{-1}(-0.6667) = -33.69° \\
\theta_s &= -16.85°
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Verify the $45°$ relationship with the principal plane:

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta_p + 45° &= 28.16° + 45° = 73.16°
\end{aligned}$$

Note: $\theta_s = -16.85°$ and $\theta_p + 45° = 73.16°$ differ by $90°$ in their physical interpretation, both being valid because planes of maximum shear occur in two perpendicular orientations separated by $90°$.

**Final Answer (a)(ii):**
$\tau_{\max} = 36.06$ MPa, with planes at $\theta_s = -16.85°$ and $73.15°$ from the x-axis.

---

**Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a)** For a structural element of a robotic end-effector, the stresses at a point are $\sigma_x = -50$ MPa (compressive), $\sigma_y = 20$ MPa, and $\tau_{xy} = 40$ MPa. Using **Mohr's Circle** method, determine the **normal and shear stresses on a plane whose normal makes an angle of $30°$ counter-clockwise with the x-axis**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the allowable normal stress on the same component is $150$ MPa and the allowable shear stress is $80$ MPa, determine the **factor of safety** based on the **maximum normal stress theory** and **maximum shear stress theory (Tresca criterion)**. **(7 marks)**

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Part (b)(i) — Stresses on the Inclined Plane:**

**Step 1:** Identify given values: $\sigma_x = -50$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 20$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = 40$ MPa, $\theta = 30°$.

**Step 2:** Apply the stress transformation equations:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_n &= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta \\
\sigma_n &= \frac{-50 + 20}{2} + \frac{-50 - 20}{2}\cos(60°) + 40\sin(60°) \\
\sigma_n &= -15 + (-35)(0.5) + 40(0.866) \\
\sigma_n &= -15 - 17.5 + 34.64 \\
\sigma_n &= 2.14 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the shear transformation equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_n &= -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta \\
\tau_n &= -\frac{-50 - 20}{2}\sin(60°) + 40\cos(60°) \\
\tau_n &= 35(0.866) + 40(0.5) \\
\tau_n &= 30.31 + 20 \\
\tau_n &= 50.31 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer (b)(i):** $\sigma_n = 2.14$ MPa, $\tau_n = 50.31$ MPa.

**Part (b)(ii) — Factor of Safety:**

**Step 1:** Calculate the principal stresses:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{avg} &= \frac{-50 + 20}{2} = -15 \text{ MPa} \\
R &= \sqrt{\left(\frac{-50 - 20}{2}\right)^2 + (40)^2} = \sqrt{(-35)^2 + 1600} = \sqrt{1225 + 1600} = \sqrt{2825} = 53.15 \text{ MPa} \\
\sigma_1 &= -15 + 53.15 = 38.15 \text{ MPa} \\
\sigma_2 &= -15 - 53.15 = -68.15 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the **Maximum Normal Stress Theory (MNST)**:
The maximum algebraic principal stress is $\sigma_1 = 38.15$ MPa.

$$\begin{aligned}
FoS_{MNST} &= \frac{\sigma_{allow}}{\sigma_1} = \frac{150}{38.15} = 3.93
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the **Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca)**:
The maximum shear stress is $\tau_{\max} = R = 53.15$ MPa.

$$\begin{aligned}
FoS_{Tresca} &= \frac{\tau_{allow}}{\tau_{\max}} = \frac{80}{53.15} = 1.505
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer (b)(ii):** $FoS_{MNST} = 3.93$, $FoS_{Tresca} = 1.50$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors (–2 to –3 marks):** The most common mistake is forgetting the sign of $\sigma_x$ or $\sigma_y$ in the formula $\sigma_1,2 = \sigma_{avg} \pm R$. Always recheck: if $\sigma_x$ is compressive, it carries a negative sign throughout.
> 2. **Angle Confusion (–1 to –2 marks):** Many students mix up $\theta_p$ and $\theta_s$. The mnemonic is: **"P comes before S in alphabet AND in rotation"** — principal planes come first (smaller angle in acute quadrant), max-shear planes follow at $+45°$.
> 3. **Double vs. Single Angle on Mohr's Circle (–2 marks):** The angle measured on Mohr's circle is $2\theta$ (the physical angle is **HALF** of the circle angle). If your diagram gives $2\theta_p = 56°$, the answer is $\theta_p = 28°$, not $56°$.
> 4. **Units Inconsistency (–1 mark):** Always write units (MPa or N/mm²) explicitly. The KTU 2024 scheme deducts marks for missing units in the final answer.
> 5. **Direction of Rotation:** If your principal angle comes out negative, add $180°$ to get the equivalent positive angle (since $\theta_p$ and $\theta_p + 180°$ represent the same plane).
> 6. **Maximum vs. Minimum Principal Stress:** $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2$ always. Do not swap them.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 University Exam — Module 3**

- **Principal stresses** are the **maximum and minimum normal stresses** at a point; they occur on planes where the shear stress is **zero**.
- The two principal planes are **mutually perpendicular** (separated by $90°$ in physical space, or $180°$ on Mohr's circle).
- The **closed-form formula** is: $\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$.
- The **principal angle** is given by: $\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$.
- The **maximum shear stress** equals the **radius of Mohr's circle**: $\tau_{\max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}$.
- The plane of **maximum shear stress** is at $\theta_s = \theta_p + 45°$.
- On Mohr's circle, the **horizontal axis is $\sigma$ (normal stress)** and the **vertical axis is $\tau$ (shear stress)**.
- Point $X$ (x-face) is plotted at $(\sigma_x, +\tau_{xy})$ and point $Y$ (y-face) at $(\sigma_y, -\tau_{xy})$.
- The **center of Mohr's circle** is at $\left(\frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}, 0\right)$.
- The angle on Mohr's circle is **$2\theta$** (twice the physical angle), with rotation direction **preserved** (counter-clockwise on the circle = counter-clockwise in physical space, except $\tau$ axis is flipped).
- The **invariants** of stress are $\sigma_x + \sigma_y$ (sum) and $\sigma_x\sigma_y - \tau_{xy}^2$ (product) — these are the same in **any orientation**, including principal.
- **Tension is positive, compression is negative** (KTU 2024 convention).
- For a **uniaxial stress state** ($\sigma_y = 0$, $\tau_{xy} = 0$): $\sigma_1 = \sigma_x$, $\sigma_2 = 0$, and $\tau_{\max} = \sigma_x/2$ at $45°$.
- For a **pure shear state** ($\sigma_x = \sigma_y = 0$): $\sigma_1 = +\tau_{xy}$, $\sigma_2 = -\tau_{xy}$ (tension and compression of equal magnitude), and $\tau_{\max} = \tau_{xy}$ at $45°$.
- In **robotic part design**, compare $\sigma_1$ against $\sigma_y / FoS$ to ensure no yielding, and $\tau_{\max}$ against $\tau_{allowable} / FoS$ for shear failure prevention.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torsion of Circular Shafts: Theory of Pure Torsion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT525 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS OF ROBOTIC PARTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Torsion of Circular Shafts: Theory of Pure Torsion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Torsion** is the twisting of a structural member (typically a circular shaft) caused by the application of a couple (torque) acting in a plane perpendicular to the longitudinal axis of the member. The internal reaction developed within the shaft to resist this twisting action is known as **Torsional Shear Stress**, and the resulting angular deformation is called **Angle of Twist**.

**Pure Torsion** refers specifically to the state where a circular shaft is subjected to torque only at its ends, with no bending moments, transverse shear forces, or axial forces acting along its length. Under pure torsion, every cross-section of the shaft experiences *only* a state of pure shear stress, and the shaft twists uniformly about its longitudinal axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Theory of Pure Torsion is the *foundational pillar* for Module 3. It establishes the kinematic and kinetic relationships required to analyze torque transmission systems, drive shafts, robotic manipulator links, and gear assemblies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** Torsion in engineering is universally expressed in **Newton-meters (N·m)** for SI units, and shear stress is expressed in **N/m² (Pa)** or **MPa (N/mm²)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are wringing out a wet towel. Your two hands apply equal and opposite twisting forces — that action is *torsion*. The fibers inside the towel (like the longitudinal fibers of a shaft) try to resist this twisting and slide past one another, creating internal shear. The towel twists a certain number of degrees — that is the **angle of twist**.

A more engineering-relevant analogy is a **screwdriver turning a screw** or a **robotic manipulator's rotational joint**: the motor applies torque, the shaft transmits that torque to the end-effector, and the shaft itself twists by a small angle. As long as this twist is uniform along the length and the loading is purely rotational, we call it **pure torsion**.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Hook:** *"Twist a rod, see it shear — torque goes in, twist comes out."*

## 1.3 Why Pure Torsion Matters in Robotic Parts

In a robotic arm, every rotational actuator (servo or stepper motor) connects to a downstream link via a shaft. That shaft must transmit torque faithfully — without excessive twist, without yielding in shear, and without losing stiffness. The theory of pure torsion gives the design equations for choosing the *diameter*, *material*, and *length* of that shaft.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Assumptions of the Theory of Pure Torsion (Coulomb / Saint-Venant Theory)

The classical theory of pure torsion for circular shafts rests on the following experimentally verified assumptions:

- **A1 — Material Homogeneity & Isotropy:** The shaft material is *homogeneous* (uniform composition throughout) and *isotropic* (same elastic properties in all directions).
- **A2 — Linear Elasticity:** The material obeys **Hooke's Law in shear**, i.e., shear stress is proportional to shear strain, and the shear modulus $G$ is constant.
- **A3 — Circular Cross-Sections Remain Circular and Plane:** Cross-sections that are *plane before twisting remain plane and circular after twisting*. This implies that *plane sections do not warp* (no out-of-plane displacement).
- **A4 — Radial Lines Remain Radial & Straight:** A radial line on the end-face of the shaft remains a straight line and remains radial after twisting. The distance from the axis to any point is unchanged.
- **A5 — Shaft Length is Unchanged:** The longitudinal fibers of the shaft do not change length during twisting — they only rotate.
- **A6 — Pure Torsion Loading:** Only torques are applied at the ends; no axial, bending, or transverse loading exists along the length.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why These Assumptions Matter:** Assumptions A3 and A4 together imply that the *shear strain at any point is directly proportional to its radial distance from the centerline*. This is the kinematic cornerstone from which the entire torsion equation is derived.

## 2.2 Kinematic Relationship (Geometry of Twist)

Let a shaft of length $L$ and radius $R$ be twisted through an angle $\theta$ at one end, with the other end fixed. A point on the *outer surface* at radius $R$ displaces tangentially through an arc length $s$:

$$s = R \cdot \theta$$

Dividing by the length $L$ gives the **shear strain** $\gamma$ on the outer surface:

$$\gamma = \frac{R \cdot \theta}{L}$$

By assumption A4 (linear radial variation), the shear strain at an arbitrary radius $r$ is:

$$\gamma_r = \frac{r \cdot \theta}{L}$$

This is the **kinematic equation** of pure torsion.

## 2.3 Constitutive Relationship (Hooke's Law in Shear)

For a linearly elastic isotropic material, the shear stress at radius $r$ is:

$$\tau_r = G \cdot \gamma_r = G \cdot \frac{r \cdot \theta}{L}$$

where:
- $\tau_r$ = shear stress at radius $r$ (Pa or MPa)
- $G$ = shear modulus (modulus of rigidity) of the material (Pa or MPa)
- $r$ = radial distance from the centerline (m or mm)
- $\theta$ = angle of twist over length $L$ (radians)
- $L$ = length of the shaft segment under consideration (m or mm)

## 2.4 Static (Equilibrium) Relationship

The applied torque $T$ must be resisted by the *integrated* shear-stress distribution over the cross-section. Taking the moment of the elemental shear force about the central axis:

$$T = \int_A \tau_r \cdot r \cdot dA$$

Substituting $\tau_r = G r \theta / L$:

$$T = \int_A \left( G \cdot \frac{r \cdot \theta}{L} \right) \cdot r \cdot dA = \frac{G \cdot \theta}{L} \int_A r^2 \, dA$$

The integral $\int_A r^2 \, dA$ is the **polar moment of inertia** of the cross-section, denoted $J$ (or $I_p$). For a **solid circular shaft** of diameter $D$ (radius $R$):

$$J = \frac{\pi D^4}{32} = \frac{\pi R^4}{2}$$

For a **hollow circular shaft** of outer diameter $D_o$ and inner diameter $D_i$:

$$J = \frac{\pi}{32} \left( D_o^4 - D_i^4 \right)$$

Therefore:

$$T = \frac{G \cdot J \cdot \theta}{L}$$

## 2.5 The Master Torsion Equation (KTU High-Yield)

Combining the kinematic, constitutive, and static relationships yields the **Generalized Torsion Equation**, the single most important equation in this module:

$$\boxed{\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G \cdot \theta}{L}}$$

Each ratio has a physical meaning:
- $T/J$ = **Rate of twist per unit length × shear modulus**, related to energy storage
- $\tau/r$ = **Shear stress intensity** at radius $r$
- $G\theta/L$ = **Engineering shear strain on the outer fiber**

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **must-memorize** formula bank for KTU ESE questions on pure torsion.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Generalized Torsion Equation | $\dfrac{T}{J} \;=\; \dfrac{\tau}{r} \;=\; \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ | $T$: N·m, $J$: m⁴, $\tau$: Pa, $r$: m, $G$: Pa, $\theta$: rad, $L$: m |
| 2 | Angle of Twist (full form) | $\theta \;=\; \dfrac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J}$ | Result in **radians** |
| 3 | Maximum Shear Stress (solid) | $\tau_{\max} \;=\; \dfrac{16 \, T}{\pi \, D^3}$ | For solid circular shaft of dia. $D$ |
| 4 | Maximum Shear Stress (hollow) | $\tau_{\max} \;=\; \dfrac{16 \, T \, D_o}{\pi \, (D_o^4 - D_i^4)}$ | Occurs at outer surface $r = D_o / 2$ |
| 5 | Polar Moment of Inertia (solid) | $J \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \, D^4}{32} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \, R^4}{2}$ | Units: m⁴ or mm⁴ |
| 6 | Polar Moment of Inertia (hollow) | $J \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{32} \left( D_o^4 - D_i^4 \right)$ | Outer dia. $D_o$, inner dia. $D_i$ |
| 7 | Polar Section Modulus (solid) | $W_p \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \, D^3}{16} \;=\; \dfrac{J}{R}$ | $W_p$: m³ |
| 8 | Polar Section Modulus (hollow) | $W_p \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{16} \cdot \dfrac{D_o^4 - D_i^4}{D_o}$ | $W_p$: m³ |
| 9 | Torsional Rigidity (Stiffness) | $k_t \;=\; \dfrac{G \, J}{L}$ | N·m / rad |
| 10 | Stiffness–Strength Combined | $\tau_{\max} \;=\; \dfrac{T}{W_p}$ | Direct stress formula |
| 11 | Power Transmission | $P \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi \, N \, T}{60}$ | $P$ in W, $N$ in rpm, $T$ in N·m |
| 12 | Torsional Resilience | $U \;=\; \dfrac{T^2 \, L}{2 \, G \, J} \;=\; \dfrac{G \, J \, \theta^2}{2 L}$ | Strain energy stored (J) |

## 2.7 Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility (Robotics):**
> - **Servo Motor Shaft Design:** Choosing $D$ such that $\tau_{\max} \le \tau_{\text{allowable}}$ for the rated torque.
> - **Driveline Torsional Stiffness:** Ensuring $k_t$ is high enough so that angular lag between motor and load (twist angle) stays within control-loop tolerances.
> - **Gear Train Shafts:** Hollow shafts save weight in robotic arms while retaining torsional capacity — the $D_i / D_o$ ratio is tuned for mass-optimization.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Generalized Torsion Equation

**Goal:** Derive $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ from first principles.

### Step 1 — Set up the geometry of twist

Consider a circular shaft fixed at one end, with torque $T$ applied at the other end. Let the free end rotate through a small angle $\theta$ about the longitudinal axis (call this the $x$-axis). Let the shaft have length $L$ and radius $R$.

A point $P$ on the outer surface at $(x, R, 0)$ moves tangentially through an arc-length on the end-face of:

$$s = R \cdot \theta$$

This is the standard *arc length = radius × angle* relationship.

### Step 2 — Compute the shear strain

The original right angle between the longitudinal axis (along $x$) and the circumferential direction at the surface has now been reduced by the shear strain $\gamma$. The geometry gives:

$$\gamma = \lim_{\Delta \to 0} \frac{\text{transverse displacement}}{\text{original length}} = \frac{R \cdot \theta}{L}$$

This is the shear strain *on the outer surface* of the shaft.

### Step 3 — Linear radial variation of strain

By **Assumption A4** (radial lines remain radial and straight), the displacement at any interior radius $r$ is linearly proportional to $r$. Hence:

$$\gamma_r = \frac{r \cdot \theta}{L} \quad \text{for} \quad 0 \le r \le R$$

This is the **kinematic equation**.

### Step 4 — Apply Hooke's Law in shear

By **Assumption A2** (linear elasticity in shear):

$$\tau_r = G \cdot \gamma_r = G \cdot \frac{r \cdot \theta}{L}$$

This is the **constitutive equation**, valid at *any* radius $r$.

### Step 5 — Apply moment equilibrium of the cross-section

The applied external torque $T$ must be balanced by the *distributed* shear stresses acting tangentially on the cross-section. An elemental area $dA$ at radius $r$ carries shear stress $\tau_r$, producing an elemental resisting moment:

$$dM = \tau_r \cdot dA \cdot r$$

Integrating over the entire cross-section:

$$T = \int_A \tau_r \cdot r \, dA$$

### Step 6 — Substitute and simplify

Substituting $\tau_r = G r \theta / L$ into the equilibrium integral:

$$T = \int_A \left( G \cdot \frac{r \cdot \theta}{L} \right) \cdot r \, dA$$

Since $G$, $\theta$, and $L$ are constants for a given cross-section, they come out of the integral:

$$T = \frac{G \cdot \theta}{L} \cdot \int_A r^2 \, dA$$

By definition, the **polar moment of inertia** of the cross-section is:

$$J = \int_A r^2 \, dA$$

Therefore:

$$T = \frac{G \cdot J \cdot \theta}{L}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{G \cdot \theta}{L}$$

### Step 7 — Combine the three relations

From Step 4, we have $\tau / r = G\theta / L$. Combining this with Step 6:

$$\boxed{\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G \cdot \theta}{L}}$$

The **Generalized Torsion Equation** is now fully derived.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Polar Moment of Inertia for a Solid Circular Cross-Section

**Goal:** Show that $J = \pi D^4 / 32$.

Consider a solid circular cross-section of diameter $D$ (radius $R = D/2$). Using a thin concentric ring of radius $r$ and thickness $dr$:

$$dA = 2\pi r \, dr$$

Then:

$$J = \int_0^R r^2 \cdot (2\pi r) \, dr = 2\pi \int_0^R r^3 \, dr = 2\pi \cdot \left[ \frac{r^4}{4} \right]_0^R = 2\pi \cdot \frac{R^4}{4} = \frac{\pi R^4}{2}$$

Substituting $R = D/2$:

$$J = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \left( \frac{D}{2} \right)^4 = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \frac{D^4}{16} = \frac{\pi D^4}{32}$$

$$\boxed{J_{\text{solid}} = \frac{\pi D^4}{32}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Polar Moment of Inertia for a Hollow Circular Cross-Section

**Goal:** Show that $J = \pi (D_o^4 - D_i^4) / 32$.

By the *additive* (subtractive) property of area integrals:

$$J_{\text{hollow}} = J_{\text{outer}} - J_{\text{inner}} = \frac{\pi D_o^4}{32} - \frac{\pi D_i^4}{32}$$

$$\boxed{J_{\text{hollow}} = \frac{\pi}{32} \left( D_o^4 - D_i^4 \right)}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Maximum Shear Stress Formula (Solid Shaft)

At the outer surface $r = R = D/2$, the shear stress is maximum (since $\tau \propto r$). Using the torsion equation:

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{T \cdot r_{\max}}{J} = \frac{T \cdot (D/2)}{\pi D^4 / 32} = \frac{32 \, T \cdot D}{2 \, \pi \, D^4} = \frac{16 \, T}{\pi \, D^3}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{\max}^{\text{solid}} = \frac{16 \, T}{\pi \, D^3}}$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Maximum Shear Stress Formula (Hollow Shaft)

For a hollow shaft, maximum shear stress occurs at the outer radius $r = D_o/2$:

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{T \cdot (D_o / 2)}{(\pi / 32)(D_o^4 - D_i^4)} = \frac{16 \, T \, D_o}{\pi \, (D_o^4 - D_i^4)}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{\max}^{\text{hollow}} = \frac{16 \, T \, D_o}{\pi \, (D_o^4 - D_i^4)}}$$

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Polar Section Modulus

Define the **polar section modulus** $W_p$ such that $\tau_{\max} = T / W_p$. Comparing with the generalized equation at $r = r_{\max}$:

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{T \cdot r_{\max}}{J} \quad \Rightarrow \quad W_p \equiv \frac{J}{r_{\max}}$$

For a solid shaft: $W_p = (\pi D^4 / 32) / (D/2) = \pi D^3 / 16$. For a hollow shaft: $W_p = (\pi/32)(D_o^4 - D_i^4) / (D_o/2) = \pi (D_o^4 - D_i^4) / (16 D_o)$.

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix: Cause → Effect → Formula

| Cause (Input) | Effect (Output) | Governing Formula |
|---|---|---|
| Applied Torque $T$ | Shear stress at radius $r$ | $\tau = T r / J$ |
| Applied Torque $T$ | Angle of twist over length $L$ | $\theta = T L / (G J)$ |
| Angle of twist $\theta$ | Strain energy stored | $U = T^2 L / (2 G J)$ |
| Material choice ($G$) | Stiffness of shaft | $k_t = G J / L$ |
| Geometry choice ($D$) | Maximum shear stress capacity | $\tau_{\max} = 16 T / (\pi D^3)$ |
| Rotational speed $N$ | Power transmitted | $P = 2\pi N T / 60$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Since physical 3D drawings of twisted shafts cannot be rendered in Mermaid, the diagrams below present the **logical/functional architecture** of the pure-torsion analysis pipeline and the stress/strain distribution on a circular cross-section.

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Torsion Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Torque T Applied at Shaft End] --> B[Internal Shear Stress Field tau_r]
    B --> C[Cross Section Equilibrium Check]
    C --> D[Polar Moment of Inertia J Computed]
    D --> E[Apply Torsion Equation T over J equals tau over r equals G theta over L]
    E --> F[Compute Maximum Shear Stress tau_max at r equals D over 2]
    E --> G[Compute Angle of Twist theta over Length L]
    F --> H[Strength Check: tau_max less than allowable]
    G --> I[Stiffness Check: theta less than allowable]
    H --> J[Final Design Verification]
    I --> J
```

## 4.2 Stress/Strain Distribution Across a Solid Circular Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R0[Center r equals 0: tau equals 0] --> R1[Mid radius: tau linear in r]
    R1 --> R2[Outer surface r equals D over 2: tau equals tau_max]
    O[Observed Property: Linear variation tau proportional to r] --- R0
    O --- R1
    O --- R2
```

## 4.3 Logical Flow of the Derivation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 Geometry of Twist: s equals R theta] --> S2[Step 2 Shear Strain on Surface: gamma equals R theta over L]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Linear Radial Variation: gamma_r equals r theta over L]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Hooke Law in Shear: tau_r equals G r theta over L]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Cross Section Equilibrium: T equals integral tau_r r dA]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Substitution: T equals G theta over L times J]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Master Equation: T over J equals tau over r equals G theta over L]
```

## 4.4 Architectural Comparison: Solid vs. Hollow Shafts

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Solid[Solid Shaft]
        SA[Outer dia D] --> SB[Full Material in Cross Section]
        SB --> SC[J equals pi D to the 4 over 32]
        SC --> SD[More Material, More Mass, Lower tau for same T]
    end
    subgraph Hollow[Hollow Shaft]
        HA[Outer dia D_o, Inner dia D_i] --> HB[Material Only in Annular Ring]
        HB --> HC[J equals pi over 32 times D_o to the 4 minus D_i to the 4]
        HC --> HD[Lightweight, Higher tau_max, Used in Robotic Links]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Part A questions are short, definition- or formula-based. Answer in **2 to 3 sentences** or with a *single formula derivation* for full credit.

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**State the assumptions made in the theory of pure torsion of a circular shaft.**

**Model Answer:**

The theory of pure torsion assumes: (i) the shaft material is homogeneous and isotropic, (ii) it obeys Hooke's law in shear (linear elasticity with constant shear modulus $G$), (iii) cross-sections that are plane before twisting remain plane and circular after twisting (no warping), (iv) radial lines remain radial and straight, (v) the shaft length is unchanged during twisting, and (vi) only torques act at the ends — no bending, axial, or transverse loads.

> [!TIP]
> **Marks Distribution (KTU Pattern):** 1 mark for naming 3+ assumptions, 2 marks for completeness (6 listed).

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**Derive the relationship between the polar moment of inertia $J$ and the angle of twist $\theta$ for a solid circular shaft of length $L$ subjected to torque $T$.**

**Model Answer:**

The angle of twist for a solid circular shaft is given by the generalized torsion equation:

$$\theta = \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J}$$

where $J = \pi D^4 / 32$ for a solid shaft of diameter $D$. Therefore:

$$\theta = \frac{32 \, T \, L}{G \, \pi \, D^4}$$

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Size Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the units** of $G$ (Pa vs. MPa vs. N/mm²) leads to 1000× errors. *Always* unify units *before* substitution.
> 2. **Using diameter $D$ instead of radius $r$** in the term $\tau/r$ is a recurring error. Substitute $r = D/2$ explicitly.
> 3. **Mixing up $J$ formulas** for solid vs. hollow shafts loses 2–3 marks. Memorize both.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert angle to radians** for trigonometric relations is fatal.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** explicitly in derivation-type questions loses 1–2 marks per KTU pattern.

---

### Question B-A. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3, Choice A]`

**A solid circular steel shaft of diameter 60 mm and length 1.5 m is subjected to a pure torque of 2.5 kN·m at its free end. Take $G = 80$ GPa.**

**(a)** Determine the **maximum shear stress** developed in the shaft and the **angle of twist** at the free end. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the same shaft is replaced by a **hollow circular shaft** with the same outer diameter (60 mm) and inner diameter 40 mm, calculate the **percentage reduction in weight** and the **percentage change in maximum shear stress** for the same applied torque. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — Solid Shaft Analysis

**Given:**
- Diameter $D = 60 \text{ mm} = 0.06 \text{ m}$
- Length $L = 1.5 \text{ m}$
- Torque $T = 2.5 \text{ kN·m} = 2500 \text{ N·m}$
- Shear modulus $G = 80 \text{ GPa} = 80 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$

**Polar moment of inertia:**

$$J = \frac{\pi D^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.06)^4}{32}$$

Computing $(0.06)^4 = 1.296 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^4$:

$$J = \frac{\pi \cdot 1.296 \times 10^{-5}}{32} = 1.272 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^4$$

**Maximum shear stress** (at outer surface, $r = D/2 = 0.03$ m):

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{T \cdot r}{J} = \frac{2500 \cdot 0.03}{1.272 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{75}{1.272 \times 10^{-6}} = 5.896 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} \approx 58.96 \text{ MPa}$$

**Angle of twist:**

$$\theta = \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J} = \frac{2500 \cdot 1.5}{80 \times 10^9 \cdot 1.272 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\theta = \frac{3750}{1.0176 \times 10^5} = 0.03685 \text{ rad}$$

Converting to degrees: $\theta = 0.03685 \times (180/\pi) \approx 2.11°$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer (a):** $\tau_{\max} \approx 58.96$ MPa, $\theta \approx 0.0369$ rad $\approx 2.11°$.

##### Part (b) — Hollow Shaft Comparison

**Hollow shaft polar moment of inertia:**

$$J_h = \frac{\pi}{32} \left( D_o^4 - D_i^4 \right) = \frac{\pi}{32} \left( (0.06)^4 - (0.04)^4 \right)$$

Computing:
- $(0.06)^4 = 1.296 \times 10^{-5}$
- $(0.04)^4 = 2.56 \times 10^{-6}$

$$J_h = \frac{\pi}{32} \left( 1.296 \times 10^{-5} - 2.56 \times 10^{-6} \right) = \frac{\pi \cdot 1.04 \times 10^{-5}}{32} = 1.021 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^4$$

**Maximum shear stress (hollow):**

$$\tau_{\max}^h = \frac{T \cdot r_o}{J_h} = \frac{2500 \cdot 0.03}{1.021 \times 10^{-6}} = 7.346 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa} \approx 73.46 \text{ MPa}$$

**Percentage change in maximum shear stress:**

$$\% \Delta \tau = \frac{\tau_{\max}^h - \tau_{\max}}{\tau_{\max}} \times 100 = \frac{73.46 - 58.96}{58.96} \times 100 \approx +24.6\%$$

**Percentage reduction in weight:**
Cross-sectional areas:
- Solid: $A_s = \pi D^2 / 4 = \pi (0.06)^2 / 4 = 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$
- Hollow: $A_h = \pi (D_o^2 - D_i^2) / 4 = \pi (0.0036 - 0.0016) / 4 = 1.571 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$

$$\% \text{ Weight Reduction} = \frac{A_s - A_h}{A_s} \times 100 = \frac{2.827 - 1.571}{2.827} \times 100 \approx 44.4\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer (b):** Weight reduced by $\approx 44.4\%$; maximum shear stress *increases* by $\approx 24.6\%$. **Engineering Insight:** The hollow shaft saves weight but at the cost of higher stress — a typical design trade-off in robotic manipulators.

---

### Question B-B. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3, Choice B]`

**A composite shaft consists of two segments rigidly connected in series. Segment 1 is a solid circular shaft of steel (length 800 mm, diameter 50 mm) and Segment 2 is a solid circular shaft of brass (length 600 mm, diameter 50 mm). The composite shaft is fixed at one end and carries a torque of 1.2 kN·m at the free end. Given $G_{\text{steel}} = 80$ GPa, $G_{\text{brass}} = 40$ GPa.**
