**(a)** Determine the **total angle of twist** of the composite shaft. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the torque is reversed in direction, determine the **maximum shear stress** in each segment and the **polar section modulus** required to keep the shear stress below 60 MPa. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — Total Angle of Twist

**Given:**
- $L_1 = 0.8 \text{ m}$, $D_1 = 0.05 \text{ m}$, $G_1 = 80$ GPa (steel)
- $L_2 = 0.6 \text{ m}$, $D_2 = 0.05 \text{ m}$, $G_2 = 40$ GPa (brass)
- $T = 1200$ N·m (same in both segments, by equilibrium of a series shaft)

**Polar moment of inertia** (same for both, since diameters are equal):

$$J = \frac{\pi D^4}{32} = \frac{\pi (0.05)^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \cdot 6.25 \times 10^{-6}}{32} = 6.136 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4$$

**Angle of twist — Segment 1 (steel):**

$$\theta_1 = \frac{T \cdot L_1}{G_1 \cdot J} = \frac{1200 \cdot 0.8}{80 \times 10^9 \cdot 6.136 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$\theta_1 = \frac{960}{4.909 \times 10^4} = 0.01956 \text{ rad}$$

**Angle of twist — Segment 2 (brass):**

$$\theta_2 = \frac{T \cdot L_2}{G_2 \cdot J} = \frac{1200 \cdot 0.6}{40 \times 10^9 \cdot 6.136 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$\theta_2 = \frac{720}{2.454 \times 10^4} = 0.02933 \text{ rad}$$

**Total angle of twist:**

$$\theta_{\text{total}} = \theta_1 + \theta_2 = 0.01956 + 0.02933 = 0.04889 \text{ rad}$$

In degrees: $\theta_{\text{total}} \approx 2.80°$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer (a):** Total angle of twist $\approx 0.0489$ rad $\approx 2.80°$.

##### Part (b) — Maximum Shear Stress and Section Modulus

**Maximum shear stress — Segment 1 (steel), at $r = D/2 = 0.025$ m:**

$$\tau_{\max,1} = \frac{T \cdot r}{J} = \frac{1200 \cdot 0.025}{6.136 \times 10^{-7}} = 4.888 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa} \approx 48.88 \text{ MPa}$$

**Maximum shear stress — Segment 2 (brass), at $r = D/2 = 0.025$ m:**

$$\tau_{\max,2} = \frac{T \cdot r}{J} = \frac{1200 \cdot 0.025}{6.136 \times 10^{-7}} = 48.88 \text{ MPa}$$

(The geometric formula gives the same value because $D$ is the same — but strain and stored energy differ because $G$ differs.)

**Polar section modulus required** to keep $\tau \le 60$ MPa = $60 \times 10^6$ Pa:

$$W_p = \frac{T}{\tau_{\max}} = \frac{1200}{60 \times 10^6} = 2.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3 = 20{,}000 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Check** with current $W_p$:
$$W_p^{\text{current}} = \frac{\pi D^3}{16} = \frac{\pi (0.05)^3}{16} = 2.454 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3 = 24{,}544 \text{ mm}^3$$

Since $W_p^{\text{current}} = 24{,}544 \text{ mm}^3 > 20{,}000 \text{ mm}^3 = W_p^{\text{required}}$, the existing shaft is **safe**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer (b):** $\tau_{\max,1} = \tau_{\max,2} \approx 48.88$ MPa (both segments); $W_p^{\text{required}} = 20{,}000$ mm³ (current is $24{,}544$ mm³ — safe).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Error to Avoid:** Students often compute the *new* diameter from the $W_p$ formula and forget to take the cube root, or they confuse $W_p$ (polar) with $Z$ (elastic section modulus for bending). Always re-check that you are using the **polar** form: $W_p = \pi D^3 / 16$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- **Master Equation:** $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ — memorize verbatim.
- **Polar Moment of Inertia:**
    - Solid: $J = \pi D^4 / 32$
    - Hollow: $J = \pi (D_o^4 - D_i^4) / 32$
- **Polar Section Modulus:**
    - Solid: $W_p = \pi D^3 / 16$
    - Hollow: $W_p = \pi (D_o^4 - D_i^4) / (16 D_o)$
- **Angle of Twist Formula:** $\theta = T L / (G J)$ — units of $\theta$ are **radians**, not degrees.
- **Maximum Shear Stress** always occurs at the **outermost fiber** ($r_{\max} = D_o / 2$).
- **Assumption Anchors:** Six assumptions — Homogeneous, Isotropic, Hooke's law, Plane sections remain plane, Radial lines straight, Length unchanged.
- **Power Equation:** $P = 2\pi N T / 60$ — use when $N$ is in rpm and $P$ in watts.
- **Strain Energy:** $U = T^2 L / (2 G J) = G J \theta^2 / (2 L)$.
- **Unit Hygiene:** $G$ in Pa (or MPa), $D$ in m (or mm), $T$ in N·m — *never* mix.
- **Composite Shaft Rule:** Torque is *the same* through all series segments; total twist is the *sum* of individual twists: $\theta_{\text{total}} = \sum (T_i L_i / G_i J_i)$.
- **Hollow vs. Solid:** Hollow shafts save weight but have *higher* $\tau_{\max}$ for the same $T$ and $D_o$ — a deliberate design trade-off.
- **Stiffness vs. Strength:** $k_t = GJ/L$ governs stiffness (deflection); $W_p$ governs strength (stress).

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always draw a free-body diagram of the shaft first, label the internal torque, and *then* apply the formula. This habit alone prevents 80% of the mistakes examiners penalize.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Derivation of Torsion Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT525 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS OF ROBOTIC PARTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Derivation of Torsion Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Torsion** is the twisting of a structural member (such as a shaft, robotic arm link, or drive axis) about its longitudinal axis when subjected to a **Torque** (Twisting Moment) acting in a plane perpendicular to that axis. The **Torsion Equation** is the fundamental mathematical relationship that governs the resulting **shear stress distribution** and **angle of twist** across a circular cross-section.

For a circular shaft of **polar moment of inertia $J$** subjected to an external torque $T$:

$$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\,\theta}{L}$$

where:
- $T$ = Applied torque (N·m)
- $J$ = Polar moment of inertia of the cross-section (m⁴)
- $\tau$ = Shear stress at radius $r$ (Pa or N/m²)
- $r$ = Radial distance from the center of the shaft (m)
- $G$ = Modulus of rigidity of the material (Pa or N/m²)
- $\theta$ = Angle of twist over length $L$ (radians)
- $L$ = Length of the shaft under torsion (m)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (For KTU Students):**
> Imagine you are wringing a wet towel with both hands. Your left hand holds one end fixed, while your right hand twists the other end. The fibers of the towel (which represent the longitudinal elements of a shaft) that are farther from the central axis experience a **greater shear deformation** than those near the center. The outermost fibers "travel" the longest distance for the same angular rotation.
>
> In a **robotic manipulator arm**, when the motor at the shoulder applies a torque to rotate the forearm, the shaft inside the joint experiences exactly this twisting action. The torque equation tells us **how much the shaft will twist** and **where the maximum stress concentration** will occur (always at the outer surface, $r = R$).

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Modulus of Rigidity ($G$):** Steel ≈ **79.3 GPa**, Aluminum ≈ **26.5 GPa**, Cast Iron ≈ **41–54 GPa**.
- **Polar Moment of Inertia for Solid Shaft:** $J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$
- **Polar Moment of Inertia for Hollow Shaft:** $J = \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}$
- **Shear Modulus is related to Young's Modulus ($E$) and Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$):** $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** In the analysis of robotic parts, torsion equations are the foundation for **drive shaft design**, **wrist joint stiffness modeling**, and **twist-induced positional error compensation** in serial-link manipulators.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Assumptions for Torsion Equation Derivation

For the classical torsion equation $\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\theta}{L}$ to be valid, the following assumptions must hold:

1. **Material is Homogeneous and Isotropic** – Properties are uniform and identical in all directions.
2. **Shaft Cross-Section is Circular** (Solid or Hollow) – Ensures a pure torsional deformation without warping irregularities.
3. **Cross-Sections Remain Plane and Perpendicular to the Axis** – Even after twisting, sections do **not warp** (Saint-Venant's principle for circular sections).
4. **Radial Lines Remain Radial and Straight** – After deformation, radial lines on the cross-section stay radial but rotate through angle $\theta$.
5. **Distance between adjacent cross-sections remains constant** – The shaft length $L$ does not change.
6. **Twisting is within the Elastic Limit** – Linear stress-strain behavior (Hooke's Law applies).
7. **Shaft is Subjected to Pure Torsion** – No bending, axial, or transverse loading is present.

### 2.2 Logical Breakdown — The "Why" and "How"

- **Why do cross-sections rotate by angle $\theta$?** Because a torque applied to one end of a clamped shaft causes every cross-section to undergo a relative angular displacement about the longitudinal axis.
- **How does shear stress vary with radius?** The outer fibers undergo maximum shear strain. Since shear stress is proportional to shear strain (Hooke's Law: $\tau = G\gamma$), stress must also vary **linearly** with $r$.
- **How is the angle of twist related to torque?** The internal torque $T$ resists the twisting; equilibrium of moments on any cross-section gives the first ratio $T/J$. Compatibility (geometry of deformation) gives $G\theta/L$. Equating the shear stress from both gives the master relationship.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Torsion Formula Reference Table (KTU 2024)**

| **Formula** | **Description** | **SI Units** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $T = \dfrac{G J \theta}{L}$ | Torque-Angle relationship (Stiffness form) | N·m |
| $\tau = \dfrac{T r}{J}$ | Shear stress at radial distance $r$ | Pa (N/m²) |
| $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{16\,T}{\pi\,d^{3}}$ | Max shear stress in **solid** shaft | Pa |
| $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{16\,T\,D}{\pi(D^{4}-d^{4})}$ | Max shear stress in **hollow** shaft | Pa |
| $J_{\text{solid}} = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{32}$ | Polar moment of inertia — solid | m⁴ |
| $J_{\text{hollow}} = \dfrac{\pi(D^{4}-d^{4})}{32}$ | Polar moment of inertia — hollow | m⁴ |
| $\theta = \dfrac{T L}{G J}$ | Total angle of twist over length $L$ | rad |
| $\dfrac{\theta}{L} = \dfrac{T}{G J}$ | Angle of twist per unit length (twist rate) | rad/m |
| $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ | Relation between elastic constants | Pa |
| $I_{p} = I_{xx} + I_{yy}$ | Polar moment = sum of perpendicular planar moments | m⁴ |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute value / magnitude expressions use standard math notation. Never use the vertical pipe `|` inside a table cell.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility in Robotics

- **Drive Train Sizing:** Design of motor-to-gearbox coupling shafts in robotic joints.
- **Stiffness Analysis:** A shaft with low torsional stiffness ($GJ/L$) causes angular lag, leading to **positional errors** in robotic arms.
- **Material Selection:** Choosing high-$G$ materials (like carbon steel) for stiffness-critical drive shafts.
- **Failure Prediction:** The maximum shear stress criterion is used to prevent yielding at the outer surface.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the Torsion Equation

We consider a **solid circular shaft** of radius $R$, length $L$, fixed at one end and subjected to torque $T$ at the free end.

**STEP 1 — Geometry of Deformation (Kinematic Relationship)**

Consider two cross-sections, $XX$ and $YY$, originally separated by a distance $dx$ along the axis. After twisting, section $XX$ rotates through an angle $\theta$ relative to section $YY$. An outer fiber at radius $R$ on the surface of the shaft moves from point $A$ to point $A'$, describing an arc.

The arc length traveled by the surface fiber is:
$$
\text{Arc} = R \, d\theta
$$

The original length of the fiber segment between the two cross-sections is $dx$. The shear strain $\gamma$ for the surface fiber is therefore:
$$
\gamma = \frac{R \, d\theta}{dx}
$$

Since $\gamma$ is in radians (dimensionless, since it's a ratio of two lengths), and for small deformations, we can write:
$$
\gamma = R \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dx}
$$

> **Textual Explanation:** This step uses the geometric definition of shear strain as the change in angle of a right-angled element. The fiber at radius $R$ slides on the cross-section $YY$ by an amount $R\,d\theta$ over a length $dx$.

**STEP 2 — Generalization to Any Radius $r$**

For any fiber at a generic radius $r$ (where $0 \le r \le R$), the shear strain is:
$$
\gamma_r = r \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dx}
$$

> **Textual Explanation:** Because the cross-section rotates as a rigid disc, the arc length is proportional to the radius. Fibers closer to the center experience less shear strain.

**STEP 3 — Hooke's Law in Shear (Material Relationship)**

Within the elastic limit, shear stress is proportional to shear strain:
$$
\tau_r = G \cdot \gamma_r
$$

Substituting $\gamma_r$ from Step 2:
$$
\tau_r = G \cdot r \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dx}
$$

Therefore:
$$
\frac{\tau_r}{r} = G \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dx}
$$

> **Textual Explanation:** This is the **second ratio** in the master equation. It links shear stress to the twist rate $d\theta/dx$.

**STEP 4 — Equilibrium of Torque (Static Relationship)**

Consider an elemental ring on the cross-section at radius $r$ with thickness $dr$ and area $dA = 2\pi r \, dr$. The shear stress $\tau_r$ acting on this elemental area creates a differential torque $dT$ about the shaft's longitudinal axis:

$$
dT = \tau_r \cdot r \cdot dA
$$

The total resisting torque at the cross-section must equal the applied torque $T$:
$$
T = \int_{0}^{R} \tau_r \cdot r \cdot dA
$$

Substitute $dA = 2\pi r \, dr$:
$$
T = \int_{0}^{R} \tau_r \cdot r \cdot (2\pi r) \, dr = 2\pi \int_{0}^{R} \tau_r \, r^{2} \, dr
$$

Now substitute $\tau_r$ from Step 3:
$$
T = 2\pi \int_{0}^{R} \left(G \cdot r \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dx}\right) \cdot r^{2} \, dr
$$

$$
T = 2\pi \, G \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dx} \int_{0}^{R} r^{3} \, dr
$$

Evaluating the integral:
$$
\int_{0}^{R} r^{3} \, dr = \left[\frac{r^{4}}{4}\right]_{0}^{R} = \frac{R^{4}}{4}
$$

Therefore:
$$
T = 2\pi \, G \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dx} \cdot \frac{R^{4}}{4} = \frac{\pi R^{4}}{2} \cdot G \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dx}
$$

The **Polar Moment of Inertia** of a solid circular cross-section is:
$$
J = \frac{\pi R^{4}}{2} = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{32}
$$

So:
$$
T = J \cdot G \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dx}
$$

Rearranging:
$$
\frac{T}{J} = G \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dx}
$$

> **Textual Explanation:** This is the **first ratio** in the master equation. It links the internal torque to the polar moment of inertia and the twist rate.

**STEP 5 — Combining the Two Ratios**

From **Step 3**, we have:
$$
\frac{\tau_r}{r} = G \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dx}
$$

From **Step 4**, we have:
$$
\frac{T}{J} = G \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dx}
$$

Equating both expressions for $G \cdot \dfrac{d\theta}{dx}$:
$$
\boxed{\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = G \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dx}}
$$

For a uniform shaft with constant $T$ and $J$, integrating over the length $L$ gives:
$$
\frac{d\theta}{dx} = \frac{\theta}{L}
$$

And the complete torsion equation becomes:
$$
\boxed{\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G \theta}{L}}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation Summary Matrix

| **Step** | **Principle Used** | **Result Obtained** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. Kinematic | Geometry of small deformation | $\gamma = R \cdot \dfrac{d\theta}{dx}$ |
| 2. Generalization | Rigid cross-section rotation | $\gamma_r = r \cdot \dfrac{d\theta}{dx}$ |
| 3. Constitutive | Hooke's Law in shear | $\dfrac{\tau}{r} = G \cdot \dfrac{d\theta}{dx}$ |
| 4. Equilibrium | Moment equilibrium + integration | $\dfrac{T}{J} = G \cdot \dfrac{d\theta}{dx}$ |
| 5. Combination | Equating the two expressions | $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Derivation Logic Flow

The following block diagram visualizes the **logical flow** of the torsion equation derivation, mapping the three core principles (Kinematics, Constitutive, Equilibrium) to the unified master equation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Shaft Fixed at One End Torque T Applied at Other End"] --> nodeB["Step 1 KINEMATIC Geometry of Deformation"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Surface fiber arc R d theta over length dx"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Shear strain gamma r d theta by dx"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Step 2 CONSTITUTIVE Hookes Law in Shear"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["tau r equals G times r times d theta by dx"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Ratio One tau by r equals G d theta by dx"]
    nodeB --> nodeH["Step 3 EQUILIBRIUM Moment Equilibrium on Cross Section"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Elemental area dA equals 2 pi r dr"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Integral T equals integral tau r dA from 0 to R"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Substitute tau r to get T equals J G d theta by dx"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["Ratio Two T by J equals G d theta by dx"]
    nodeG --> nodeM["Equate Both Expressions for G d theta by dx"]
    nodeL --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeN["MASTER TORSION EQUATION T by J equals tau by r equals G theta by L"]
    nodeN --> nodeO["Engineering Application Shaft Design and Robotic Joint Stiffness"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Torsion Equation Architecture

This second diagram illustrates the **three-part architecture** of the master equation, showing how each ratio originates from a distinct engineering principle.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP["APPLIED TORQUE T"] --> nodeQ["POLAR MOMENT J"]
    nodeQ --> nodeR["Ratio T by J STATIC"]
    nodeS["SHEAR STRESS tau"] --> nodeT["RADIUS r"]
    nodeT --> nodeU["Ratio tau by r KINEMATIC"]
    nodeV["MODULUS G"] --> nodeW["THETA by L"]
    nodeW --> nodeX["Ratio G theta by L CONSTITUTIVE"]
    nodeR --> nodeY["MASTER EQUATION"]
    nodeU --> nodeY
    nodeX --> nodeY
    nodeY --> nodeZ["OUTPUT Stress and Twist Prediction"]
```

### 4.3 Physical Visualization — Stress Distribution Across Radius

> [!NOTE]
> **Stress Distribution Concept (Verbal Schematic):**
> - **Center ($r = 0$):** $\tau = 0$ (No shear stress on the axis)
> - **Mid-radius ($r = R/2$):** $\tau = \tau_{\max}/2$ (Linear variation)
> - **Outer surface ($r = R$):** $\tau = \tau_{\max}$ (Maximum shear stress — critical for design)
>
> The shear stress distribution across the cross-section is **linear** with radius, forming a cone when visualized in 3D along the length of the shaft. The angle of twist $\theta$ increases **linearly** along the length $L$ for a constant torque.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **State the torsion equation and explain the meaning of each term used.**

**Model Answer:**

The torsion equation is:
$$
\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\theta}{L}
$$

Where:
- $T$ = Twisting moment (torque) applied to the shaft (N·m)
- $J$ = Polar moment of inertia of the cross-section (m⁴)
- $\tau$ = Shear stress induced at a fiber located at radius $r$ (Pa)
- $r$ = Radial distance from the center of the shaft to the fiber (m)
- $G$ = Modulus of rigidity of the shaft material (Pa)
- $\theta$ = Angle of twist in radians over length $L$
- $L$ = Length of the shaft over which the twist is measured (m)

The equation relates the external torque to the internal shear stress and angular deformation of a circular shaft under pure torsion.

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **List any four assumptions made in the derivation of the torsion equation.**

**Model Answer:**

1. The shaft material is **homogeneous and isotropic**.
2. The shaft has a **uniform circular cross-section** (solid or hollow) along its length.
3. Cross-sections **remain plane and perpendicular** to the axis of the shaft after twisting (no warping).
4. The shaft is subjected to **pure torsion only** (no bending or axial loading).
5. The shaft material obeys **Hooke's Law** — deformations are within the elastic limit.
6. **Radial lines** on the cross-section remain radial and straight after twisting.

*(Any four will receive full 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Pattern (KTU 2024 ESE Module 3):** Answer ONE full question. Each question has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Derive the torsion equation $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ for a solid circular shaft, stating all assumptions clearly.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's Law.
2. Shaft has a uniform solid circular cross-section.
3. Cross-sections remain plane, perpendicular, and do not warp.
4. Radial lines remain radial and straight after twisting.
5. Distance between adjacent cross-sections remains unchanged.
6. Shaft is subjected to pure torsion within the elastic limit.

**Derivation:**

Consider a shaft of radius $R$ and length $L$ fixed at one end, with torque $T$ applied at the other end. Let two cross-sections be separated by a small length $dx$. The relative rotation between them is $d\theta$.

**Shear strain at radius $r$:**
$$
\gamma = r \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dx}
$$

**Hooke's Law in shear:**
$$
\tau = G\gamma = G \cdot r \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dx} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\tau}{r} = G \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dx} \quad \text{...(1)}
$$

**Equilibrium of torque on the cross-section** (using elemental ring of area $dA = 2\pi r\,dr$):
$$
T = \int_0^R \tau \cdot r \cdot dA = \int_0^R G \cdot r \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dx} \cdot r \cdot 2\pi r \, dr
$$
$$
T = 2\pi G \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dx} \int_0^R r^3 \, dr = 2\pi G \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dx} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4}
$$
$$
T = \frac{\pi R^4}{2} \cdot G \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dx}
$$

Substituting $J = \dfrac{\pi R^4}{2}$:
$$
\frac{T}{J} = G \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dx} \quad \text{...(2)}
$$

**Equating (1) and (2):**
$$
\boxed{\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\theta}{L}}
$$
(for uniform torque, $\dfrac{d\theta}{dx} = \dfrac{\theta}{L}$).

---

**(b) A solid steel shaft of diameter 50 mm transmits 2 kW of power at 200 rpm. The modulus of rigidity is 80 GPa. Determine:** **(7 Marks)**
1. The torque transmitted.
2. The maximum shear stress induced.
3. The angle of twist per metre length.

**Given:** $d = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m, $P = 2$ kW $= 2000$ W, $N = 200$ rpm, $G = 80 \times 10^9$ Pa.

**Solution:**

**(i) Torque transmitted:**
$$
T = \frac{60\,P}{2\pi N} = \frac{60 \times 2000}{2\pi \times 200}
$$
$$
T = \frac{120000}{1256.64} = 95.49 \text{ N·m}
$$

**(ii) Polar moment of inertia:**
$$
J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32} = \frac{\pi (0.05)^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{-6}}{32} = 6.136 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4
$$

**(iii) Maximum shear stress (at $r = d/2 = 0.025$ m):**
$$
\tau_{\max} = \frac{T \cdot r}{J} = \frac{95.49 \times 0.025}{6.136 \times 10^{-7}} = 3.89 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 3.89 \text{ MPa}
$$

**(iv) Angle of twist per metre length:**
$$
\frac{\theta}{L} = \frac{T}{GJ} = \frac{95.49}{80 \times 10^{9} \times 6.136 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{95.49}{49088.6}
$$
$$
\frac{\theta}{L} = 1.945 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad/m} = 0.1115^\circ/\text{m}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Derive the expression for the polar moment of inertia of a hollow circular shaft with outer diameter $D$ and inner diameter $d$.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A hollow circular shaft has outer radius $R_o = D/2$ and inner radius $R_i = d/2$. The polar moment of inertia is the second moment of area about the longitudinal (centroidal) axis.

**Elemental ring approach:** Consider a thin ring of radius $r$ and thickness $dr$.
$$
dA = 2\pi r \, dr
$$
$$
dJ = r^2 \, dA = r^2 \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = 2\pi r^3 \, dr
$$

**Integrate from $R_i$ to $R_o$:**
$$
J = \int_{R_i}^{R_o} 2\pi r^3 \, dr = 2\pi \left[\frac{r^4}{4}\right]_{R_i}^{R_o} = \frac{\pi}{2}\left(R_o^4 - R_i^4\right)
$$

**Substitute $R_o = D/2$ and $R_i = d/2$:**
$$
J = \frac{\pi}{2}\left[\left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^4 - \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^4\right] = \frac{\pi}{2 \cdot 16}\left(D^4 - d^4\right)
$$
$$
\boxed{J = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}}
$$

**Verification:** For a solid shaft, $d = 0$, so $J = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{32} = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$ (using $D$ as the diameter). This is consistent with the standard formula.

---

**(b) A hollow shaft with outer diameter 60 mm and inner diameter 40 mm is required to transmit 30 kW at 180 rpm. If the permissible shear stress is 60 MPa and the modulus of rigidity is 80 GPa, find:** **(7 Marks)**
1. The torque transmitted.
2. The safety check on shear stress.
3. The angle of twist per metre length.

**Given:** $D = 0.06$ m, $d = 0.04$ m, $P = 30{,}000$ W, $N = 180$ rpm, $\tau_{\text{perm}} = 60 \times 10^6$ Pa, $G = 80 \times 10^9$ Pa.

**Solution:**

**(i) Torque transmitted:**
$$
T = \frac{60\,P}{2\pi N} = \frac{60 \times 30000}{2\pi \times 180} = \frac{1{,}800{,}000}{1130.97} = 1591.55 \text{ N·m}
$$

**(ii) Polar moment of inertia:**
$$
J = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32} = \frac{\pi \left[(0.06)^4 - (0.04)^4\right]}{32}
$$
$$
J = \frac{\pi \left[1.296 \times 10^{-5} - 2.56 \times 10^{-6}\right]}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 1.04 \times 10^{-5}}{32} = 1.021 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^4
$$

**(iii) Maximum shear stress:**
$$
\tau_{\max} = \frac{T \cdot (D/2)}{J} = \frac{1591.55 \times 0.03}{1.021 \times 10^{-6}} = 46.77 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 46.77 \text{ MPa}
$$

Since $\tau_{\max} = 46.77$ MPa $< \tau_{\text{perm}} = 60$ MPa, the shaft is **SAFE**.

**(iv) Angle of twist per metre length:**
$$
\frac{\theta}{L} = \frac{T}{GJ} = \frac{1591.55}{80 \times 10^9 \times 1.021 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1591.55}{81680} = 0.01949 \text{ rad/m}
$$
$$
\frac{\theta}{L} \approx 1.117^\circ/\text{m}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing mm and m in the same calculation. **Always convert all dimensions to meters** before substituting into formulas.
> 2. **Power Formula Error:** Forgetting the **$60$** in $T = \dfrac{60P}{2\pi N}$. Some students write $T = \dfrac{P}{2\pi N}$, which is dimensionally wrong.
> 3. **Confusing $\tau_{\max}$ and $\tau_r$:** $\tau_{\max}$ is **always** at the outer radius $R = D/2$. Substituting a smaller $r$ gives a smaller, incorrect stress.
> 4. **Polar Moment of Inertia Confusion:** Hollow shaft $J = \dfrac{\pi(D^4 - d^4)}{32}$ — many students incorrectly write $J = \dfrac{\pi(D^2 - d^2)}{32}$, which is dimensionally and numerically wrong.
> 5. **Angle of Twist Units:** Final answer for $\theta$ should be in **radians**; convert to degrees only if explicitly asked.
> 6. **Forgetting Assumptions:** The KTU answer key **always** awards marks for stating assumptions clearly in derivation questions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Save This for Last-Minute KTU Prep!):**

- The torsion equation is: $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$.
- It is valid **only for circular cross-sections** (solid or hollow) and **within the elastic limit**.
- The derivation rests on **three pillars**: Geometry (kinematics), Hooke's Law (constitutive), and Moment Equilibrium (statics).
- **Polar moment of inertia** $J$ is the torsional analog of the second moment of area used in bending.
- For a **solid shaft**: $J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$.
- For a **hollow shaft**: $J = \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}$.
- **Maximum shear stress** always occurs at the **outer surface** ($r = R$).
- **Angle of twist per unit length** is given by $\dfrac{\theta}{L} = \dfrac{T}{GJ}$.
- **Power-torque relationship**: $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ (with $P$ in Watts, $N$ in rpm, $T$ in N·m).
- **Stiffness** of a shaft under torsion is $\dfrac{GJ}{L}$; higher $GJ/L$ means lower angular twist and higher positional accuracy in robotic joints.
- In robotic applications, torsional stiffness directly affects the **dynamic response and precision** of manipulator arms.
- **Units Convention (KTU Standard):** Use SI units throughout — meters, Newtons, Pascals, Watts.
- **Exam Tip:** Always state the **assumptions first** in derivation questions — it is a guaranteed 2-mark grab in KTU answer scripts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torsional Rigidity and Polar Modulus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT525 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS OF ROBOTIC PARTS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Torsional Rigidity and Polar Modulus # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Torsion — The Engineering Phenomenon **Torsion** is the twisting of a structural member (shaft) caused by the application of two equal and opposite **torques (couples)** acting in planes perpendicular to the longitudinal axis of the member. In robotics, torsion is encountered in: - **Drive shafts** of robot joints - **Robot arm cross-sections** transmitting rotational motion - **Wrist and gripper shafts** under payload-induced twist > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Torsion* is the state of loading in which a shaft is subjected to twisting moments (torques) about its longitudinal axis, producing shear stresses that vary linearly with the radial distance from the center. ## 1.2 What is Torsional Rigidity? **Torsional Rigidity (k or GJ)** is defined as the product of the **Modulus of Rigidity (G)** and the **Polar Moment of Inertia (J)** of the cross-section. It quantifies the resistance offered by a shaft against angular deformation. $$k = G \cdot J \quad \text{[N·m}^2 \text{ or N·mm}^2\text{]}$$ > [!NOTE] > The **higher** the torsional rigidity of a shaft, the **smaller** will be the angle of twist for a given torque — which is a critical design requirement in precision robotic joints. ## 1.3 What is Polar Modulus? **Polar Modulus (Z_p or Z_t)** is the ratio of the **Polar Moment of Inertia (J)** to the **outer radius (R)** of the cross-section. $$Z_p = \frac{J}{R}$$ It is analogous to the **section modulus** used in bending and is the geometric property used to compute the **maximum shear stress** in a shaft under torsion: $$\tau_{\max} = \frac{T}{Z_p}$$ ## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The Twisted Rubber Rod Imagine holding a soft rubber rod and twisting both ends in **opposite directions** with your hands. The rod twists by some angle θ. Now: - If the rod is **thicker** (larger J) → it resists twisting more → **higher torsional rigidity** - If the rod is made of a **stiffer material** (higher G, e.g., steel vs. rubber) → again resists more → **higher torsional rigidity** - The **maximum stress** (resistance to tearing) depends on how far the outermost fibre is from the center → this is captured by **polar modulus Z_p** In a **robotic arm**, the motor torque (T) tries to twist the shaft, and the shaft's ability to resist this twist without excessive deformation or failure is governed entirely by **GJ** and **Z_p**. ## 1.5 Key Physical Constants (Standard Values) | Material | Modulus of Rigidity **G** (GPa) | |---|---| | Steel | **80** | | Cast Iron | **40 – 50** | | Aluminium | **27** | | Copper | **42 – 45** | | Bronze | **36 – 44** | > [!IMPORTANT] > Always state **units of G in N/mm² or GPa** consistently in KTU numerical problems. **1 GPa = 1000 N/mm² = 10³ MPa**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Assumptions of Pure Torsion (Saint-Venant's Theory) For a circular shaft under pure torsion, the following assumptions are valid: 1. The cross-sections of the shaft, which were plane and perpendicular to the axis **before loading, remain plane and perpendicular** to the axis after loading (no warping). 2. **Radial lines** in the cross-section remain radial and **straight** after deformation. 3. The twist is **uniform** along the length of the shaft. 4. The material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's Law** within the elastic limit. 5. The **distance between any two cross-sections remains unchanged** (fibres do not change length, only rotate). ## 2.2 The Two Torsion Equations (Cornerstone of Module 3) From the geometry of deformation and Hooke's Law, two relationships emerge: ### Equation 1 — Shear Stress Distribution $$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r}$$ - $T$ = Applied Torque (N·mm) - $J$ = Polar Moment of Inertia (mm⁴) - $\tau$ = Shear stress at radius $r$ (N/mm² or MPa) - $r$ = Radial distance from center (mm) ### Equation 2 — Angle of Twist $$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{G \cdot \theta}{L}$$ - $G$ = Modulus of Rigidity (N/mm²) - $\theta$ = Angle of twist (radians) - $L$ = Length of the shaft (mm) ### Combined Final Form $$\tau_{\max} = \frac{T \cdot R}{J} = \frac{T}{Z_p} \quad \text{and} \quad \theta = \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J}$$ ## 2.3 Polar Moment of Inertia (J) for Standard Cross-Sections ### Solid Circular Shaft (Diameter d) $$J = \frac{\pi \cdot d^4}{32}$$ ### Hollow Circular Shaft (Outer Dia D, Inner Dia d) $$J = \frac{\pi \cdot (D^4 - d^4)}{32}$$ > [!NOTE] > For thin-walled tubes where $D > 10d$, a simplified approximation $J \approx \pi D^3 t / 8$ is used (where $t$ is wall thickness). KTU typically tests the **exact form** unless specified. ## 2.4 Polar Modulus (Z_p) for Standard Cross-Sections ### Solid Circular Shaft $$Z_p = \frac{J}{R} = \frac{\pi d^4 / 32}{d/2} = \frac{\pi \cdot d^3}{16}$$ ### Hollow Circular Shaft $$Z_p = \frac{J}{R} = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4) / 32}{D/2} = \frac{\pi \cdot (D^4 - d^4)}{16 \cdot D}$$ ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Revision Table) | # | Quantity | Solid Shaft (d) | Hollow Shaft (D, d) | Unit | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Polar Moment of Inertia **J** | $\pi d^4 / 32$ | $\pi(D^4 - d^4) / 32$ | mm⁴ | | 2 | Polar Modulus **Z_p** | $\pi d^3 / 16$ | $\pi(D^4 - d^4) / 16D$ | mm³ | | 3 | Max Shear Stress **τ_max** | $16T / \pi d^3$ | $16TD / \pi(D^4 - d^4)$ | N/mm² | | 4 | Angle of Twist **θ** | $32TL / (\pi d^4 G)$ | $32TL / [\pi(D^4 - d^4)G]$ | radians | | 5 | Torsional Rigidity **GJ** | $G \pi d^4 / 32$ | $G \pi(D^4 - d^4) / 32$ | N·mm² | | 6 | Torque Capacity (given τ_allow) | $\tau_{allow} \cdot \pi d^3 / 16$ | $\tau_{allow} \cdot \pi(D^4 - d^4) / 16D$ | N·mm | | 7 | Power Transmission | $P = 2\pi N T / 60$ where N = rpm | Same | W or kW | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always convert **angle from degrees to radians** before substituting into the angle-of-twist formula. **180° = π rad**. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Field | Application of Torsional Rigidity & Polar Modulus | |---|---| | **Robotics** | Designing joint drive shafts, harmonic reducers, gripper couplings | | **Automotive** | Crankshafts, propeller shafts, half-shafts in differentials | | **Aerospace** | Helicopter rotor shafts, turbine drive shafts | | **Mechanical Power Transmission** | Couplings, gearbox input/output shafts | The **selection of shaft diameter in any robotic manipulator** is fundamentally a trade-off between **strength (governed by Z_p)** and **stiffness/precision (governed by GJ)**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I — Polar Modulus from First Principles **Objective:** Derive the polar modulus $Z_p$ for a solid circular shaft starting from the shear stress formula. ### Given Data / Setup - Consider a solid circular shaft of diameter $d$ (radius $R = d/2$). - Subjected to a torque $T$ at the free end. - Cross-section has polar moment of inertia $J$. ### Step 1 — Recall the Shear Stress Distribution Equation The shear stress at any radius $r$ is given by the first torsion equation: $$\tau = \frac{T \cdot r}{J}$$ ### Step 2 — Identify Where Maximum Stress Occurs Shear stress $\tau$ is **directly proportional to radial distance $r$** (linear variation). Therefore, maximum shear stress occurs at the **outermost fibre**, i.e., at $r = R$: $$\tau_{\max} = \frac{T \cdot R}{J}$$ ### Step 3 — Define Polar Modulus By definition, the **polar modulus** $Z_p$ is defined as the ratio $J/R$: $$Z_p = \frac{J}{R} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{Z_p} = \frac{R}{J}$$ ### Step 4 — Substitute into the Maximum Stress Equation Replace the $R/J$ factor in the maximum shear stress expression: $$\tau_{\max} = \frac{T \cdot R}{J} = T \cdot \left(\frac{R}{J}\right) = T \cdot \left(\frac{1}{Z_p}\right) = \frac{T}{Z_p}$$ > **Result:** $\tau_{\max} = T / Z_p$ — *the fundamental design equation of shafts in torsion.* ### Step 5 — Derive the Explicit Expression for a Solid Shaft Substitute $J = \pi d^4 / 32$ and $R = d/2$: $$\begin{aligned} Z_p &= \frac{J}{R} = \frac{\pi d^4 / 32}{d / 2} \\ &= \frac{\pi d^4}{32} \times \frac{2}{d} \\ &= \frac{\pi d^3}{16} \end{aligned}$$ > **Result:** $Z_p = \pi d^3 / 16$ for a solid circular shaft. ### Step 6 — Derive the Explicit Expression for a Hollow Shaft For a hollow shaft with outer diameter $D$ and inner diameter $d$: $$J = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}, \quad R = \frac{D}{2}$$ $$\begin{aligned} Z_p &= \frac{J}{R} = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4) / 32}{D / 2} \\ &= \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32} \times \frac{2}{D} \\ &= \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{16 D} \end{aligned}$$ > **Result:** $Z_p = \pi (D^4 - d^4) / (16 D)$ for a hollow circular shaft. --- ## 3.2 Derivation II — Relationship Between Angle of Twist and Strain **Objective:** Prove $\theta = T L / (G J)$ from first principles. ### Step 1 — Consider Geometry of Deformation Let a shaft of length $L$ be twisted by an angle $\theta$ at the free end. A fibre originally at the surface (radius $R$) moves through an arc length: $$\text{Arc} = R \cdot \theta$$ ### Step 2 — Compute Shear Strain γ Shear strain is the ratio of lateral displacement to the original length: $$\gamma = \frac{R \cdot \theta}{L}$$ ### Step 3 — Apply Hooke's Law in Shear $$\tau = G \cdot \gamma = \frac{G \cdot R \cdot \theta}{L}$$ ### Step 4 — Equate to the Torsion Equation From the shear stress distribution, at the outer fibre ($r = R$): $$\tau = \frac{T \cdot R}{J}$$ Setting the two expressions for $\tau$ equal: $$\frac{T \cdot R}{J} = \frac{G \cdot R \cdot \theta}{L}$$ ### Step 5 — Cancel R and Rearrange $$\begin{aligned} \frac{T}{J} &= \frac{G \cdot \theta}{L} \\ \theta &= \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J} \end{aligned}$$ > **Result:** $\theta = T L / (G J)$ — *the angle-of-twist formula.* ### Step 6 — Recognize Torsional Rigidity Rearranging the same equation: $$T = \left(\frac{G \cdot J}{L}\right) \cdot \theta \quad \Rightarrow \quad T = k_t \cdot \theta, \quad \text{where } k_t = \frac{G \cdot J}{L}$$ The term $GJ$ is the **torsional rigidity** (independent of length), and $GJ/L$ is the **torsional stiffness** of the shaft. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical — KTU 2024 Pattern Problem ### Problem Statement A solid steel shaft of diameter **60 mm** transmits a torque of **2 kN·m**. The modulus of rigidity for steel is **80 GPa**. **(a)** Find the maximum shear stress in the shaft. **(b)** Find the angle of twist in degrees over a length of **1.5 m**. ### Given Data - Diameter $d = 60$ mm → $R = 30$ mm - Torque $T = 2$ kN·m $= 2 \times 10^6$ N·mm - Length $L = 1.5$ m $= 1500$ mm - $G = 80$ GPa $= 80 \times 10^3$ N/mm² ### Part (a) — Maximum Shear Stress **Step 1:** Compute Polar Modulus. $$Z_p = \frac{\pi d^3}{16} = \frac{\pi (60)^3}{16} = \frac{\pi \times 216000}{16} = 13500\pi \approx 42411.5 \text{ mm}^3$$ **Step 2:** Compute Maximum Shear Stress. $$\tau_{\max} = \frac{T}{Z_p} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{42411.5} \approx 47.15 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 47.15 \text{ MPa}$$ ### Part (b) — Angle of Twist **Step 1:** Compute Polar Moment of Inertia. $$J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32} = \frac{\pi (60)^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 12960000}{32} = 405000\pi \approx 1.272 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$ **Step 2:** Compute Angle in Radians. $$\theta = \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J} = \frac{(2 \times 10^6)(1500)}{(80 \times 10^3)(1.272 \times 10^6)}$$ $$\begin{aligned} \theta &= \frac{3 \times 10^9}{1.0176 \times 10^{11}} \\ &= 0.02948 \text{ rad} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3:** Convert to Degrees. $$\theta = 0.02948 \times \frac{180}{\pi} \approx 1.689°$$ ### Final Answers (Boxed) $$\boxed{\tau_{\max} \approx 47.15 \text{ MPa} \quad ; \quad \theta \approx 1.69°}$$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Flow — Torsion Analysis Pipeline The following block diagram maps the **sequential logic** a KTU student must follow when solving any torsion problem in Module 3. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[INPUT DATA
diameter d or D d
torque T
length L
modulus G] --> B[IDENTIFY SHAFT TYPE
Solid or Hollow] B --> C[SOLID SHAFT PATH] B --> D[HOLLOW SHAFT PATH] C --> E[COMPUTE J
J = pi d4 over 32] D --> F[COMPUTE J
J = pi D4 minus d4 over 32] E --> G[COMPUTE POLAR MODULUS Zp
Zp = pi d3 over 16] F --> H[COMPUTE POLAR MODULUS Zp
Zp = pi D4 minus d4 over 16D] G --> I[COMPUTE MAX SHEAR STRESS
tau max = T over Zp] H --> I I --> J[COMPUTE ANGLE OF TWIST
theta = T L over G J] J --> K[CONVERT theta TO DEGREES
multiply by 180 over pi] K --> L[VALIDATE AGAINST ALLOWABLE
tau max less than tau allow] L --> M[FINAL DESIGN OUTPUT] ``` ## 4.2 Stress Distribution Schematic (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Cross section Center
r = 0
tau = 0] --> P2[Mid radius
r = R by 2
tau = half tau max] P2 --> P3[Outer surface
r = R
tau = tau max LINEAR] style P1 fill:#e0f7fa style P2 fill:#fff9c4 style P3 fill:#ffcdd2 ``` > [!NOTE] > The shear stress varies **linearly** from **zero at the center** to **maximum at the outer surface** of the shaft. This is a frequently tested KTU conceptual question. ## 4.3 Robotic Joint Drive Shaft — Application Block ```mermaid flowchart LR M[MOTOR] --> T[TORQUE T] --> S[DRIVE SHAFT] --> J[ROBOTIC JOINT] S --> R1[Resistance from GJ
Torsional Rigidity] S --> R2[Resistance from Zp
Polar Modulus] R1 --> A1[Angle of twist theta] R2 --> A2[Max shear stress tau] ``` > The **Motor** delivers torque $T$ to the drive shaft of a robotic joint. The shaft's **GJ** resists the angular twist $\theta$, while its **Z_p** limits the maximum shear stress $\tau_{max}$ that develops. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define the term Polar Modulus. Derive its expression for a solid circular shaft of diameter d.** #### Model Answer (3 marks) **Definition (1 mark):** Polar Modulus $Z_p$ is the ratio of the polar moment of inertia ($J$) to the outer radius ($R$) of the cross-section. Mathematically, $Z_p = J / R$. It is used to compute maximum shear stress in a shaft under torsion as $\tau_{\max} = T / Z_p$. **Derivation (2 marks):** For a solid circular shaft of diameter $d$ (radius $R = d/2$): $$J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}$$ $$Z_p = \frac{J}{R} = \frac{\pi d^4 / 32}{d / 2} = \frac{\pi d^3}{16}$$ --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **State the two fundamental torsion equations and explain the physical significance of Torsional Rigidity.** #### Model Answer (3 marks) **Torsion Equations (2 marks):** 1. $\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r}$ — relates torque to shear stress distribution 2. $\frac{T}{J} = \frac{G \theta}{L}$ — relates torque to angle of twist **Physical Significance of Torsional Rigidity (1 mark):** Torsional Rigidity $GJ$ represents the ability of a shaft to resist twisting deformation. A higher value of $GJ$ means the shaft will undergo a smaller angle of twist for a given applied torque. It is the rotational analogue of axial rigidity $AE$ in tension/compression problems. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]` **(a)** A solid circular shaft of diameter **80 mm** is required to transmit **300 kW** at **200 rpm**. If the modulus of rigidity of the shaft material is **80 GPa** and the allowable shear stress is **60 MPa**, determine: 1. The torque transmitted. 2. The actual maximum shear stress in the shaft. 3. The angle of twist per metre length of the shaft. **(b)** A hollow circular shaft has an outer diameter of **100 mm** and an inner diameter of **60 mm**. Compare its **polar modulus** and **torsional rigidity** with a solid shaft of the same material and weight, transmitting the same torque. Assume $G = 80$ GPa. --- #### Model Solution for Question A ### Part (a) — Solid Shaft Analysis **Given:** - $d = 80$ mm, $P = 300$ kW $= 300 \times 10^3$ W, $N = 200$ rpm - $G = 80 \times 10^3$ N/mm², $\tau_{allow} = 60$ MPa **Step 1 — Torque Transmitted (3 marks):** Using the power equation $P = 2\pi N T / 60$: $$\begin{aligned} T &= \frac{60 \cdot P}{2 \pi N} = \frac{60 \times 300 \times 10^3}{2 \pi \times 200} \\ &= \frac{18 \times 10^6}{400 \pi} = \frac{18000000}{1256.64} \\ &\approx 14324 \text{ N·m} = 14.324 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Polar Modulus and Maximum Shear Stress (2 marks):** $$Z_p = \frac{\pi d^3}{16} = \frac{\pi (80)^3}{16} = \frac{\pi \times 512000}{16} = 32000\pi \approx 100530.96 \text{ mm}^3$$ $$\tau_{\max} = \frac{T}{Z_p} = \frac{14.324 \times 10^6}{100530.96} \approx 142.48 \text{ MPa}$$ > Since $\tau_{\max} = 142.48$ MPa $> \tau_{allow} = 60$ MPa, the **shaft is unsafe** in its current diameter. **Step 3 — Angle of Twist per Metre (2 marks):** $$J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32} = \frac{\pi (80)^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 40960000}{32} = 1280000\pi \approx 4.021 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$ For $L = 1$ m $= 1000$ mm: $$\theta = \frac{T L}{G J} = \frac{(14.324 \times 10^6)(1000)}{(80 \times 10^3)(4.021 \times 10^6)}$$ $$\theta = \frac{1.4324 \times 10^{10}}{3.217 \times 10^{11}} \approx 0.04452 \text{ rad/m}$$ Converting to degrees: $$\theta = 0.04452 \times \frac{180}{\pi} \approx 2.55°/\text{m}$$ --- ### Part (b) — Hollow vs Solid Shaft Comparison **Step 1 — Solid Shaft Properties (3 marks):** For the solid shaft of the **same weight as the hollow shaft**, equate the cross-sectional areas: $$\frac{\pi d_s^2}{4} = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d_i^2)$$ $$d_s^2 = D^2 - d_i^2 = 100^2 - 60^2 = 10000 - 3600 = 6400$$ $$d_s = 80 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(the same solid reference)}$$ **Step 2 — Polar Modulus of Hollow Shaft (2 marks):** $$Z_{p,\text{hollow}} = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d_i^4)}{16 D} = \frac{\pi (100^4 - 60^4)}{16 \times 100}$$ $$= \frac{\pi (10^8 - 1.296 \times 10^6)}{1600} = \frac{\pi \times 98.704 \times 10^6}{1600}$$ $$Z_{p,\text{hollow}} \approx 193828 \text{ mm}^3$$ **Step 3 — Polar Modulus of Solid Shaft (1 mark):** $$Z_{p,\text{solid}} = \frac{\pi (80)^3}{16} = 32000\pi \approx 100531 \text{ mm}^3$$ **Step 4 — Ratio and Conclusion (1 mark):** $$\frac{Z_{p,\text{hollow}}}{Z_{p,\text{solid}}} = \frac{193828}{100531} \approx 1.928$$ > **Conclusion:** The hollow shaft has a **~93% higher** polar modulus (strength) than the solid shaft of equal weight. It is therefore **far more efficient** for power transmission — a key reason hollow shafts are preferred in robotic manipulators and aerospace drive systems. --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3 Alternative]` **(a)** Derive the expression for the **angle of twist** of a circular shaft of length $L$ subjected to a torque $T$, starting from the basic definition of shear strain. **(b)** A composite shaft consists of a **steel core of diameter 40 mm** surrounded by a **bronz e sleeve of outer diameter 80 mm**. The shaft is fixed at both ends and carries a torque of **5 kN·m** at the junction. Find: 1. The torque shared by each material. 2. The maximum shear stress in steel and bronze. Take $G_{steel} = 80$ GPa and $G_{bronze} = 40$ GPa. --- #### Model Solution for Question B ### Part (a) — Derivation of Angle of Twist (7 marks) **Step 1 (1 mark):** Consider a solid circular shaft of length $L$ and radius $R$ fixed at one end and subjected to torque $T$ at the free end. After twisting, the free end rotates by angle $\theta$. **Step 2 (1 mark):** A line on the surface originally parallel to the axis tilts to form helix. The arc length traversed by a point at radius $R$ is: $$s = R \cdot \theta$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** The shear strain $\gamma$ at the surface (radius $R$) is: $$\gamma = \frac{s}{L} = \frac{R \theta}{L}$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Apply Hooke's Law in shear: $$\tau = G \gamma = \frac{G R \theta}{L}$$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** From the first torsion equation, shear stress at outer fibre: $$\tau = \frac{T R}{J}$$ **Step 6 (1 mark):** Equating the two expressions for $\tau$: $$\frac{T R}{J} = \frac{G R \theta}{L}$$ **Step 7 (1 mark):** Cancel $R$ and solve for $\theta$: $$\boxed{\theta = \frac{T L}{G J}}$$ --- ### Part (b) — Composite Shaft (Fixed-Fixed with Torque at Junction) **Given:** - Steel core: $d_s = 40$ mm → $R_s = 20$ mm, $G_s = 80 \times 10^3$ N/mm² - Bronze sleeve: $D_b = 80$ mm, $d_b = 40$ mm → $R_b = 40$ mm, $G_b = 40 \times 10^3$ N/mm² - Applied torque at junction: $T = 5$ kN·m $= 5 \times 10^6$ N·mm **Step 1 — Polar Moments of Inertia (2 marks):** $$J_s = \frac{\pi d_s^4}{32} = \frac{\pi (40)^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 2.56 \times 10^6}{32} = 80000\pi \approx 2.513 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^4$$ $$J_b = \frac{\pi (D_b^4 - d_b^4)}{32} = \frac{\pi [(80)^4 - (40)^4]}{32}$$ $$= \frac{\pi (40.96 \times 10^6 - 0.256 \times 10^6)}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 40.704 \times 10^6}{32} = 1272000\pi \approx 3.996 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$ **Step 2 — Compatibility (Equal Angle of Twist) (2 marks):** Since the shaft is fixed at both ends, the angle of twist of steel and bronze segments must be equal: $$\theta_s = \theta_b \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{T_s L_s}{G_s J_s} = \frac{T_b L_b}{G_b J_b}$$ Since $L_s = L_b$ (junction condition), the torques are inversely proportional to $GJ$: $$\frac{T_s}{T_b} = \frac{G_s J_s}{G_b J_b} = \frac{(80 \times 10^3)(2.513 \times 10^5)}{(40 \times 10^3)(3.996 \times 10^6)} = \frac{2.010 \times 10^{10}}{1.598 \times 10^{11}} = 0.1258$$ **Step 3 — Equilibrium (1 mark):** $$T_s + T_b = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$ Solving simultaneously: $$T_s = 0.1258 \cdot T_b \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.1258 T_b + T_b = 5 \times 10^6$$ $$1.1258 \cdot T_b = 5 \times 10^6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_b = 4.441 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm} \approx 4.44 \text{ kN·m}$$ $$T_s = 0.1258 \times 4.441 \times 10^6 = 5.587 \times 10^5 \text{ N·mm} \approx 0.559 \text{ kN·m}$$ **Step 4 — Maximum Shear Stresses (2 marks):** $$\tau_{s,\max} = \frac{T_s \cdot R_s}{J_s} = \frac{(5.587 \times 10^5)(20)}{2.513 \times 10^5} = \frac{1.1174 \times 10^7}{2.513 \times 10^5} \approx 44.47 \text{ MPa}$$ $$\tau_{b,\max} = \frac{T_b \cdot R_b}{J_b} = \frac{(4.441 \times 10^6)(40)}{3.996 \times 10^6} = \frac{1.7764 \times 10^8}{3.996 \times 10^6} \approx 44.45 \text{ MPa}$$ > **Observation:** Maximum shear stresses are nearly equal — a design feature of composite shafts that ensures both materials yield simultaneously, optimising material usage. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in Torsion Problems (KTU 2024):** > > 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing $G$ in GPa with $J$ in mm⁴ and $T$ in N·m. Always convert to a consistent system: $G$ in N/mm², dimensions in mm, $T$ in N·mm. > 2. **Forgetting to Convert Radians to Degrees:** KTU often asks for $\theta$ in degrees. Multiplying by $180/\pi$ is mandatory. > 3. **Using Diameter Instead of Radius:** Shear stress $\tau = Tr/J$ needs the **outer radius $R$**, not the diameter $D$. Substituting $D$ instead of $R$ gives a 100% error. > 4. **Confusing Polar Modulus with Section Modulus (Bending):** Polar modulus applies to **torsion**, and its unit is **mm³**, not mm³/m. The formula $\tau_{max} = T/Z_p$ is distinct from $\sigma_{max} = M/Z$. > 5. **Hollow Shaft — Wrong D Substitution:** For hollow shafts, the denominator is $16D$ (not $16d$). $D$ is the **outer diameter**. > 6. **Power-Torque Conversion Error:** Forgetting the factor of 60 in $P = 2\pi N T / 60$ is a classic 1-mark loss. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Torsional Rigidity and Polar Modulus** - **Torsion** = Twisting of a shaft due to torque about its longitudinal axis. Cross-sections remain **plane and undistorted** (Saint-Venant's theory for circular shafts). - **Two Torsion Equations** to memorise verbatim: - $T / J = \tau / r$ (stress distribution) - $T / J = G \theta / L$ (angle of twist) - **Polar Moment of Inertia (J):** - Solid: $J = \pi d^4 / 32$ - Hollow: $J = \pi (D^4 - d^4) / 32$ - **Polar Modulus (Z_p):** - Solid: $Z_p = \pi d^3 / 16$ - Hollow: $Z_p = \pi (D^4 - d^4) / (16 D)$ - **Torsional Rigidity:** $k = G J$ (in N·mm²) — resistance to angular deformation. - **Angle of Twist:** $\theta = T L / (G J)$ in **radians**. Convert to degrees: $\theta° = \theta \times 180/\pi$. - **Maximum Shear Stress:** $\tau_{\max} = T / Z_p = T R / J$. Occurs at the **outermost fibre**. - **Power-Torque Relation:** $P = 2\pi N T / 60$, where $N$ is rpm and $P$ is in watts. - **Hollow shafts are stronger and stiffer** than solid shafts of equal weight — preferred in robotic joints, aerospace, and automotive drive systems. - **Composite shafts:** Use **compatibility** (equal angle of twist) and **equilibrium** ($\Sigma T = 0$) to find torque distribution between materials. - Always verify that the **calculated $\tau_{\max}$** does not exceed the **allowable shear stress** for the material.

topics.title: Assumptions Made in the Theory of Pure Torsion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT525 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS OF ROBOTIC PARTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Assumptions Made in the Theory of Pure Torsion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pure Torsion** is defined as the state of loading in a structural member (typically a shaft) where it is subjected exclusively to **twisting moments (torques)** acting about its longitudinal centroidal axis, with no accompanying bending moments, transverse shear forces, or axial forces.

The **Theory of Pure Torsion** (commonly called the **Saint-Venant's Theory of Torsion** for circular shafts) provides the mathematical framework to determine the shear stress distribution, angle of twist, and stiffness of a prismatic circular shaft under such loading. For a KTU 2024 scheme perspective applied to robotic components (driveshafts, motor output shafts, joint pins, wrist actuators), this theory is the foundational tool for **sizing and validating** rotating/transmitting members.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The assumptions discussed in this section are the bedrock on which the standard torsion equation $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ is mathematically derived. A question on "state the assumptions" is a guaranteed 5–7 marker in every KTU Board Examination cycle.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are **wringing water out of a wet towel** by twisting it from both ends:
- The towel fibers (cross-sectional material) rotate relative to each other.
- The longer you twist, the more rotation accumulates (analogous to *angle of twist θ*).
- The towel resists your twisting; some parts resist more than others.
- If you twist too hard, the towel tears along a spiral path — this spiral is at **45°** to the axis, and represents the **principal stress trajectory**.

Similarly, the output shaft of a **robotic arm's motor** twists as the motor delivers torque. The cross-sections rotate infinitesimally relative to each other, generating shear stresses. The assumptions of pure torsion are the *idealized rules* that allow us to compute these stresses accurately.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants in Torsion Theory (MUST be memorized in bold):**
> - **G (Shear Modulus / Modulus of Rigidity)** — units: **GPa** or **N/mm²**
> - **J (Polar Moment of Inertia)** — units: **mm⁴** or **m⁴**
> - **τ (Shear Stress)** — units: **MPa** or **N/mm²**
> - **θ (Angle of Twist)** — units: **radians** (always convert degrees to radians)
> - **T (Applied Torque)** — units: **N·mm** or **N·m**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Complete Set of Assumptions in Pure Torsion Theory

The theory of pure torsion for a circular shaft rests on the following **eleven fundamental assumptions**. KTU examiners frequently ask students to list and briefly explain at least 5–7 of these.

### Assumption 1: Homogeneous and Isotropic Material
The shaft material is **homogeneous** (same composition at every point) and **isotropic** (same mechanical properties in all directions at any given point).
- *Why:* Ensures a **single, constant value of G** (Shear Modulus) throughout the volume. Without this, the relationship $\tau = G\gamma$ would be position-dependent and unsolvable in closed form.

### Assumption 2: Hooke's Law is Valid
The material behaves **elastically** and obeys **Hooke's Law in shear**: the shear stress is directly proportional to the shear strain.
$$\tau = G \gamma$$
- *Why:* This linear constitutive relation is the *only* bridge that links geometry (strain) to strength (stress). If the material yields or behaves plastically, this linear relation fails.

### Assumption 3: Circular Cross-Section Remains Circular
After twisting, the cross-section of a circular shaft **does not distort**; it remains circular.
- *Why:* This is unique to circular geometry. Non-circular sections (rectangular, I-section) **warp** (their cross-sections do not remain plane), invalidating this assumption.

### Assumption 4: Plane Cross-Sections Remain Plane and Perpendicular to the Axis
Cross-sections initially perpendicular to the longitudinal axis **rotate as rigid discs** and **remain plane** after deformation (Saint-Venant's kinematic hypothesis for circular sections).
- *Why:* This allows us to define a single angle of rotation $\phi$ for the entire cross-section at any station *x*.

### Assumption 5: Radial Lines Remain Radial and Straight
A line drawn on the end face from the center to the circumference **remains straight** and **remains radial** after twisting.
- *Why:* The radius vector does not bend or change length. This implies that **the distance of any particle from the axis remains constant** during deformation.

### Assumption 6: The Length of the Shaft is Unchanged
Under pure torsion, the **axial length of the shaft is constant** (no extension or contraction).
- *Why:* Pure twist produces no axial strain. This decouples the torsion problem from axial loading.

### Assumption 7: Small Deformations (Small Angle of Twist)
The angle of twist $\theta$ is **small** (typically $\theta < 0.2$ rad, i.e., < 11.5°).
- *Why:* Small-angle approximations like $\tan\theta \approx \theta$ and $\sin\theta \approx \theta$ are used. This linearizes the problem.

### Assumption 8: Shear Strain Varies Linearly with Radial Distance
The shear strain $\gamma$ at any point is **directly proportional** to its radial distance *r* from the axis.
$$\gamma \propto r \quad \Rightarrow \quad \gamma = \dfrac{r \theta}{L}$$
- *Why:* This follows from the rigid-disc rotation geometry. Particles farther from the axis move through a larger arc, hence strain more.

### Assumption 9: Shear Stress is Directed Tangentially (Perpendicular to the Radius)
At any point in the cross-section, the shear stress $\tau$ acts **tangentially** — that is, perpendicular to the radius vector at that point.
- *Why:* The complementary nature of shear stresses (acting on mutually perpendicular faces) demands this orientation.

### Assumption 10: Cross-Sectional Dimensions are Uniform (Prismatic Shaft)
The shaft is **prismatic** — its cross-section is **constant** along the entire length.
- *Why:* This makes the polar moment of inertia $J$ a constant, allowing the differential equation to be integrated cleanly.

### Assumption 11: Shaft is Loaded by Torques Only (Pure Torsion)
The shaft is subjected **only to twisting moments** at its ends, with **no transverse loads, no bending, and no axial force**.
- *Why:* Any other loading superimposes additional stresses (bending, axial), invalidating the "pure" torsion framework.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight for Robotics:** In a real robotic arm, the wrist actuator shaft may simultaneously experience **bending + torsion**. In such cases, the pure torsion assumption must be abandoned, and **combined loading theories** (e.g., Maximum Shear Stress or von Mises) are used.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat-Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning | SI Units |
|---|---------|---------|----------|
| 1 | $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r}$ | Torsion shear stress relation | T in N·mm, J in mm⁴ |
| 2 | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{T \cdot R}{J}$ | Maximum shear stress (at outer surface) | N/mm² = MPa |
| 3 | $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{G \theta}{L}$ | Stiffness / angle of twist relation | θ in radians |
| 4 | $\theta = \dfrac{T L}{G J}$ | Angle of twist of a shaft | radians |
| 5 | $\phi = \dfrac{\theta}{L} = \dfrac{T}{G J}$ | Rate of twist per unit length | rad/mm |
| 6 | $J = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{32}$ for solid shaft | Polar moment (solid) | mm⁴ |
| 7 | $J = \dfrac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{32}$ for hollow shaft | Polar moment (hollow) | mm⁴ |
| 8 | $\gamma = \dfrac{r \theta}{L}$ | Shear strain at radius *r* | dimensionless |
| 9 | $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60{,}000}$ for motors | Power transmitted (N in rpm, T in N·m) | kW |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Reminder:** In every KTU numerical, the angle of twist $\theta$ **MUST** be in **radians**. If the angle is given in degrees, convert using $\theta_{\text{rad}} = \theta_{\text{deg}} \times \dfrac{\pi}{180}$.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Application | Why Torsion Theory is Used |
|-------------|----------------------------|
| **Robotic Drive Shafts** | Sizing for torque transmission from motor to gearbox |
| **Wrist & Elbow Joints of Manipulators** | Designing couplings that transmit rotation without slip |
| **Drill Bits & End-Effectors** | Calculating twist and failure torque |
| **Automobile Axles & Propeller Shafts** | Combined speed-torque-power analysis |
| **Helical Springs** | Energy storage under twist (Bredt's first formula is the basis) |
| **Bone Screws in Surgical Robotics** | Biocompatible shafts must be validated for torsional yield |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Linear Variation of Shear Strain with Radius

**Starting Geometry:** Consider a circular shaft of length $L$ and radius $R$, fixed at one end and twisted through a small angle $\theta$ at the free end.

**Step 1:** Consider two cross-sections separated by an infinitesimal distance $\mathrm{d}x$. The relative rotation between them is $\mathrm{d}\phi$ (a small twist of the disc).

**Step 2:** A fiber located at radial distance $r$ from the centerline moves through an arc of length on the surface of the shaft.
$$\text{Arc length} = r \cdot \mathrm{d}\phi$$

**Step 3:** The original length of this fiber (between the two infinitesimally close cross-sections) is $\mathrm{d}x$. Therefore, the **shear strain** (small angular distortion) is:
$$\gamma = \dfrac{\text{transverse displacement}}{\text{axial length}} = \dfrac{r \cdot \mathrm{d}\phi}{\mathrm{d}x}$$

**Step 4:** Integrating over the full length, the total angle of twist is $\theta = \int_0^L \mathrm{d}\phi$. Since $J$ and $G$ are uniform (prismatic assumption), $\dfrac{\mathrm{d}\phi}{\mathrm{d}x}$ is constant, giving:
$$\dfrac{\mathrm{d}\phi}{\mathrm{d}x} = \dfrac{\theta}{L}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting back, we obtain the **linear shear strain distribution**:
$$\boxed{\gamma = \dfrac{r \theta}{L}}$$

This proves **Assumption 8** mathematically: shear strain is **directly proportional to r** (linear with radial distance).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Torsion Equation $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$

**Step 1 — From Geometry and Hooke's Law:**
Using $\tau = G\gamma$ and $\gamma = \dfrac{r\theta}{L}$:
$$\tau = G \cdot \dfrac{r\theta}{L} = r \cdot \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the Pattern:**
This shows that the **shear stress at any radius r** is:
$$\dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L} = \text{constant for a given shaft}$$

**Step 3 — Use Moment Equilibrium:**
The applied torque $T$ must be resisted by the distributed shear stresses over the entire cross-section. The moment of the shear stress about the centroidal axis is:
$$T = \int_A (\tau \cdot r) \cdot \mathrm{d}A$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\tau$ in terms of r:**
Since $\tau = r \cdot \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$, we get:
$$T = \int_A \left(r \cdot \dfrac{G\theta}{L}\right) \cdot r \cdot \mathrm{d}A = \dfrac{G\theta}{L} \int_A r^{2} \, \mathrm{d}A$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the Polar Moment of Inertia:**
By definition, $J = \int_A r^{2} \, \mathrm{d}A$. Therefore:
$$T = \dfrac{G\theta}{L} \cdot J \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{G\theta}{L} = \dfrac{T}{J}$$

**Step 6 — Final Unified Torsion Equation:**
$$\boxed{\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}}$$

This single equation encodes **all three** of the most important torsion quantities (torque capacity, stress, deformation) in one elegant form.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Assumptions to Mathematical Steps

| Assumption | Mathematical Step Where It Is Used | Consequence if Violated |
|------------|------------------------------------|--------------------------|
| Homogeneous & Isotropic | G is a single constant | G varies with position — nonlinear PDE |
| Hooke's Law Valid | $\tau = G\gamma$ | Plastic deformation — no closed-form stress |
| Circular Cross-Section | Radii remain straight (r = const) | Cross-section warps — no plane-section assumption |
| Plane Sections Remain Plane | One $\theta$ defines whole section | Different $\theta(x,y)$ at each point |
| Radial Lines Stay Radial | Length of radius unchanged | Distance *r* changes; $\gamma$ not linear |
| Constant Length L | Decouples axial from torsional | Axial–torsional coupling must be considered |
| Small Deformations | $\tan\theta \approx \theta$ | Large twist — non-linear geometric effects |
| Shear Strain $\propto r$ | $\gamma = r\theta/L$ | Strain distribution is non-linear |
| Shear Stress Tangential | $\tau$ is perpendicular to *r* | Stress has a radial component — invalid |
| Prismatic Shaft | $J$ = constant | $J(x)$ — requires integration of varying section |
| Pure Torsion Only | No bending stress superimposed | Combined loading — must superimpose stresses |

## 3.4 Worked Example Problem

**Problem:** A solid circular shaft of diameter $d = 50$ mm and length $L = 1.5$ m transmits a torque of $T = 1.2$ kN·m. Take $G = 80$ GPa. Find: (a) Maximum shear stress, (b) Angle of twist in degrees.

**Step 1 — Calculate Polar Moment of Inertia:**
$$J = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{32} = \dfrac{\pi (50)^{4}}{32} = 6.136 \times 10^{5} \text{ mm}^{4}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Shear Stress (at r = R = 25 mm):**
$$\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{T \cdot R}{J} = \dfrac{(1.2 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}) \cdot 25 \text{ mm}}{6.136 \times 10^{5} \text{ mm}^{4}}$$

Performing the division:
$$\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{3.0 \times 10^{7}}{6.136 \times 10^{5}} = 48.89 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \approx 48.9 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Angle of Twist (in radians):**
$$\theta = \dfrac{T L}{G J} = \dfrac{(1.2 \times 10^{6}) \cdot 1500}{(80 \times 10^{3}) \cdot (6.136 \times 10^{5})}$$

Computing the numerator and denominator:
$$\theta = \dfrac{1.8 \times 10^{9}}{4.909 \times 10^{10}} = 0.03666 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Degrees:**
$$\theta_{\text{deg}} = 0.03666 \times \dfrac{180}{\pi} = 2.10^{\circ}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** $\tau_{\max} \approx 48.9$ MPa and $\theta \approx 2.10°$. This is well within the small-angle assumption ($\theta < 11.5°$), validating our use of linear torsion theory.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hierarchy of Assumptions Feeding into the Torsion Equation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Material Assumptions] --> A1[Homogeneous and Isotropic]
    A --> A2[Hookes Law Valid tau equals G times gamma]
    A --> A3[Elastic Limit Not Exceeded]
    B[Geometric Assumptions] --> B1[Prismatic Circular Cross Section]
    B --> B2[Plane Sections Remain Plane]
    B --> B3[Radial Lines Stay Radial and Straight]
    B --> B4[Cross Section Remains Circular]
    C[Deformation Assumptions] --> C1[Small Angle of Twist less than 0.2 rad]
    C --> C2[Shaft Length Unchanged]
    C --> C3[Shear Strain Varies Linearly with r]
    D[Loading Assumption] --> D1[Pure Torsion Only No Bending or Axial]
    E[Stress Orientation] --> E1[Shear Stress Acts Tangentially]
    A1 --> F[Torsion Equation T over J equals tau over r equals G theta over L]
    A2 --> F
    A3 --> F
    B1 --> F
    B2 --> F
    B3 --> F
    B4 --> F
    C1 --> F
    C2 --> F
    C3 --> F
    D1 --> F
    E1 --> F
    F --> G[Engineering Output 1 Maximum Shear Stress]
    F --> H[Engineering Output 2 Angle of Twist]
    F --> I[Engineering Output 3 Torsional Stiffness GJ over L]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Application Flow in Robotic Component Design

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Motor Output Torque T in N m] --> Q1[Apply Torsion Equation]
    P2[Shaft Geometry d or D] --> Q2[Compute Polar Moment J]
    P3[Material Selection Steel Alloy Titanium] --> Q3[Identify G value]
    Q1 --> R{Assumptions Validated}
    Q2 --> R
    Q3 --> R
    R -- Yes --> S1[Calculate tau max less than tau allowable]
    R -- No --> T1[Switch to Combined Loading Theory]
    S1 --> U1[Design is SAFE]
    S2[Compute theta in radians] --> U2[Check against deflection limit]
    U1 --> V[Final Robotic Shaft Design]
    U2 --> V
    T1 --> V
```

## 4.3 Schematic Description: Torsion of a Circular Shaft

For a KTU answer-sheet sketch, draw the following:

1. A horizontal **prismatic shaft** of length $L$.
2. A **fixed support** (hatching) at the left end.
3. A **twisting moment $T$** (curved arrow) at the right end.
4. **Two cross-sections** A–A and B–B separated by a small distance $\mathrm{d}x$.
5. A **radial line** drawn on the end face from center *O* to a point *P* on the circumference.
6. After twisting, the radial line at section B has rotated through $\mathrm{d}\phi$ relative to section A. The point *P* moves to *P'*, sweeping out a small arc.
7. The **shear strain** $\gamma$ at radius $r$ is the angle between the original radius and the deformed position of the fiber.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This figure is the **single most important sketch** for a 7-mark derivation question. Drawing it correctly can fetch full marks even if the algebraic derivation has a minor slip.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]
**State any five assumptions made in the theory of pure torsion of a circular shaft.**

**Model Answer:**
The following are the key assumptions in the pure torsion theory:
1. The material is **homogeneous, isotropic**, and obeys **Hooke's law**.
2. The shaft has a **uniform circular cross-section** that remains circular after twisting.
3. **Cross-sections remain plane and perpendicular** to the axis, and **rotate as rigid discs**.
4. **Radial lines** on the cross-section remain **straight and radial**; the radius vector is unchanged in length.
5. The angle of twist is **small** (small deformation theory applies).
6. The shear stress at any point acts **tangentially**, perpendicular to the radius at that point.
7. The shaft is subjected to **pure torsion only** (no bending, axial, or transverse loading).
*(Any five well-explained assumptions fetch full 3 marks.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between pure torsion and bending in a circular shaft.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Pure Torsion | Bending |
|--------|--------------|---------|
| Loading | Twisting moment about longitudinal axis | Transverse load or bending moment |
| Stress produced | Pure shear stress only | Normal (bending) stress + shear |
| Deformation | Cross-sections rotate relative to each other | Cross-sections tilt relative to axis |
| Maximum stress location | Outer surface (at radius R) | Extreme fibers (top/bottom) |
| Governing equation | $T/J = \tau/r = G\theta/L$ | $\sigma/y = M/I = E/R$ |

*(3 marks for a well-tabulated answer with at least 4 distinguishing points.)*

---

## PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Similar Model]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the torsion equation $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ for a solid circular shaft, listing all assumptions.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Assumptions (3 marks for listing):**
- Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and elastic (Hooke's law valid).
- Shaft is prismatic with a uniform circular cross-section.
- Plane cross-sections remain plane and perpendicular to the axis.
- Radial lines remain radial and straight.
- The length and radius of the shaft remain unchanged.
- Deformations are small (angle of twist < 0.2 rad).
- Shear stress acts tangentially on the cross-section.

**Step 2 — Geometry (2 marks):**
Consider two cross-sections A and B separated by $\mathrm{d}x$. A point *P* at radius $r$ on section B moves through arc $r \cdot \mathrm{d}\phi$ relative to section A. Shear strain:
$$\gamma = \dfrac{r \cdot \mathrm{d}\phi}{\mathrm{d}x} = \dfrac{r\theta}{L}$$

**Step 3 — Hooke's Law (1 mark):**
$$\tau = G \gamma = \dfrac{G r \theta}{L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$$

**Step 4 — Moment Equilibrium (1 mark):**
$$T = \int_A \tau \cdot r \cdot \mathrm{d}A = \dfrac{G\theta}{L} \int_A r^{2} \mathrm{d}A = \dfrac{G\theta J}{L}$$

**Step 5 — Final Equation:**
$$\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L} \quad \blacksquare$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A solid steel shaft of diameter 60 mm transmits 5 kW at 200 rpm. Take $G = 80$ GPa. Find the angle of twist per metre length and the maximum shear stress.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the Torque from Power:**
Using $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60{,}000}$ (P in kW, N in rpm, T in N·m):
$$5 = \dfrac{2 \pi (200) T}{60{,}000} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T = \dfrac{5 \times 60{,}000}{2 \pi \times 200}$$

$$T = \dfrac{300{,}000}{1256.64} = 238.73 \text{ N·m} = 238{,}732 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Polar Moment of Inertia:**
$$J = \dfrac{\pi (60)^{4}}{32} = \dfrac{\pi \times 12{,}960{,}000}{32} = 1.2723 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{4}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Shear Stress:**
$$\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{T R}{J} = \dfrac{238{,}732 \times 30}{1.2723 \times 10^{6}} = 5.63 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Angle of Twist per Metre:**
For L = 1000 mm:
$$\theta = \dfrac{T L}{G J} = \dfrac{238{,}732 \times 1000}{80{,}000 \times 1.2723 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$\theta = \dfrac{2.387 \times 10^{8}}{1.018 \times 10^{11}} = 0.002345 \text{ rad/m}$$

Converting to degrees:
$$\theta = 0.002345 \times \dfrac{180}{\pi} = 0.1343^{\circ}/\text{m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - **Do not confuse power units.** When P is in **kW**, use the formula $T = \dfrac{60{,}000 \cdot P}{2 \pi N}$. When P is in **watts**, use $T = \dfrac{60 \cdot P}{2 \pi N}$.
> - Always keep T in **N·mm** when working in mm units to avoid catastrophic unit mismatch.
> - Do **not** write $\theta$ in degrees directly in the formula; radians first, then convert.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**A hollow shaft has an outer diameter of 80 mm and an inner diameter of 50 mm. It is subjected to a torque of 5 kN·m. Find the maximum and minimum shear stresses in the shaft.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Polar Moment of Inertia:**
$$J = \dfrac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{32} = \dfrac{\pi (80^{4} - 50^{4})}{32}$$

Computing the terms:
$$80^{4} = 4.096 \times 10^{7} \text{ mm}^{4}, \quad 50^{4} = 6.25 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{4}$$

Difference: $4.096 \times 10^{7} - 6.25 \times 10^{6} = 3.471 \times 10^{7} \text{ mm}^{4}$

$$J = \dfrac{\pi \times 3.471 \times 10^{7}}{32} = 3.407 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{4}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Shear Stress (at outer radius R = 40 mm):**
$$\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{T R}{J} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{6} \times 40}{3.407 \times 10^{6}} = 58.7 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Minimum Shear Stress (at inner radius r = 25 mm):**
$$\tau_{\min} = \dfrac{T r}{J} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{6} \times 25}{3.407 \times 10^{6}} = 36.7 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Stress Ratio:**
$$\dfrac{\tau_{\max}}{\tau_{\min}} = \dfrac{R}{r} = \dfrac{40}{25} = 1.6$$

> This confirms Assumption 9: shear stress varies **linearly with radial distance**.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Why is a hollow circular shaft preferred over a solid circular shaft for the same torque capacity? Justify with a comparison of strength-to-weight ratio and material economy.**

**Model Answer (in bullet form for KTU marking):**

1. **Strength-to-Weight Advantage:** A hollow shaft has material only at the periphery where shear stress is **maximum**. The inner core (low-stress region) is removed, drastically reducing weight.
2. **Larger $J$ for Same Mass:** For equal mass (and hence equal cross-sectional area), a hollow shaft has a **larger polar moment of inertia** $J$ than a solid shaft.
3. **Stiffer Under Torsion:** Since $J$ is larger, the angle of twist $\theta = TL/GJ$ is **smaller** for the same torque and length.
4. **Material Economy:** Cost savings for the same performance — critical in robotics where weight directly affects payload and energy consumption.
5. **Quantitative Comparison:**
   - Solid shaft of diameter *d* has $J_s = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{32}$.
   - Hollow shaft with $D = 1.2 d$ and $d_i = 0.6 d$ has $J_h = \dfrac{\pi (1.2^{4} - 0.6^{4}) d^{4}}{32} = \dfrac{\pi (2.0736 - 0.1296) d^{4}}{32} = \dfrac{1.944 \pi d^{4}}{32}$, which is **~94% larger** $J$ than the solid shaft for only **51% more cross-sectional area** (and hence a small weight increase).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in KTU Valuation:**
> - Students often forget to list assumptions in derivation questions. **Always begin with the assumption block.**
> - For hollow shafts, do not use the solid-shaft $J = \pi d^{4}/32$ formula. Use the difference formula.
> - In comparison questions, you must provide **numbers** or formulas, not just qualitative reasoning.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- **Pure Torsion** = shaft loaded by twisting moments only, no other forces.
- The **Theory of Pure Torsion** is built on **11 assumptions**: homogeneous/isotropic material, Hooke's law, circular section, plane sections remain plane, radial lines stay straight, small deformations, constant length, prismatic geometry, tangential stress, and pure torsion loading.
- The **Torsion Equation** is the master formula: $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$.
- **Polar Moment of Inertia:**
  - Solid: $J = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{32}$
  - Hollow: $J = \dfrac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{32}$
- **Maximum shear stress occurs at the outer surface** (r = R).
- **Minimum shear stress** in a hollow shaft occurs at the **inner surface** (r = d/2).
- **Angle of twist $\theta$ must be in radians** in all formulas.
- **Hollow shafts are preferred** for torque transmission due to better strength-to-weight ratio and higher $J$ per unit mass.
- **Power-Torque relation:** $T = \dfrac{60{,}000 \cdot P_{\text{kW}}}{2 \pi N_{\text{rpm}}}$.
- **Real-world applications** in robotics: motor output shafts, wrist/elbow joint couplings, drive trains, end-effectors.
