- **Combined loading** (torsion + bending) requires the **Maximum Shear Stress Theory** or **Distortion Energy Theory** (von Mises), not pure torsion.
- **Always draw a labelled sketch** of the twisted shaft with radial line, arc, and angle $\gamma$ for full marks in derivations.
- **Memorize the KTU formula table** in Section 2.2 — it is your lifeline for the 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Transmitted by Shaft of Solid and Hollow Circular Sections
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT525 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS OF ROBOTIC PARTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Power Transmitted by Shaft of Solid and Hollow Circular Sections

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

A **transmission shaft** is a rotating machine element, usually of circular cross-section (solid or hollow), used to transmit **power (P)** and **torque (T)** from one point to another in a mechanical power-train — for instance, between a motor and a gearbox, or between gear stages in a robotic manipulator. When a torque `T` is applied at the free end of a shaft fixed at the other end, the shaft undergoes **torsion** (twisting), and the resulting **maximum shear stress** `τ_max` occurs at the **outer surface** of the shaft, while it is **zero at the centroidal axis**.

For a shaft rotating at an angular speed `N` (in revolutions per minute, **rpm**), the **power transmitted** is governed by the fundamental rotational power equation:

$$P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60,000} \;\; \text{kW} \quad (\text{when } N \text{ in rpm, } T \text{ in N·m})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> The student must be able to:
> 1. Apply the torsion equation `\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G \theta}{L}` for both solid and hollow circular sections.
> 2. Compute the **maximum torque** that a shaft can transmit for a given allowable shear stress.
> 3. Derive the **power transmission** expression in terms of shaft diameter, rpm, and allowable shear stress.
> 4. Compare the **strength-to-weight ratio** of solid vs hollow shafts.

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine you are holding a **chocolate bar** firmly at one end and twisting the other end with both hands — the bar tries to resist. The fibres on the *outside* stretch and twist the most, while the fibres at the *center* hardly move at all. A **solid shaft** is like that chocolate bar: the material near the center carries almost no shear load, yet it adds **dead weight**.

Now imagine a **paper towel roll** instead. The cardboard is only at the perimeter; the hollow middle is empty. The same twisting force is resisted by the same outer fibres — but you have removed all the *useless* inner material. This is why **hollow shafts are universally preferred** in robotics, aerospace, and high-speed turbomachinery: equal (or greater) strength at a fraction of the weight.

In a **robotic joint actuator**, the drive shaft spins at thousands of rpm, transmitting torque from the motor to the harmonic drive. A hollow shaft allows cables/wiring to pass *through* the center — an elegant engineering solution that no solid shaft can offer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics**
> - Power: `P` in **kW** (or **W**)
> - Speed: `N` in **rpm**
> - Torque: `T` in **N·m** (or **N·mm**)
> - Shear stress: `τ` in **N/mm² (MPa)**
> - Shear modulus: `G` in **N/mm² (MPa)** or **GPa** — for steel, **G = 80 GPa (≈ 80,000 N/mm²)**; for aluminium, **G ≈ 27 GPa**.
> - Length: `L` in **mm** or **m**
> - Angle of twist: `θ` in **radians**

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Shaft Design

When a shaft is subjected to torque `T`, three quantities must be controlled. Each pillar corresponds to one design limit:

**Pillar 1 — Strength (Shear Stress Criterion)**

The shear stress at any radius `r` is:

$$\tau_r = \dfrac{T \cdot r}{J_p}$$

The **maximum** shear stress occurs at the **outer radius** `r = R`:

$$\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{T \cdot R}{J_p} = \dfrac{T}{Z_p}$$

where `Z_p = J_p / R` is the **polar section modulus** (a geometric property of the cross-section).

**Pillar 2 — Rigidity (Angle of Twist Criterion)**

The total angle of twist over a length `L`:

$$\theta = \dfrac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J_p} \quad \text{(radians)}$$

Specific angle of twist (twist per unit length):

$$\dfrac{\theta}{L} = \dfrac{T}{G \cdot J_p}$$

**Pillar 3 — Stability (Power Transmission Criterion)**

Power is the rate of doing work. For a shaft rotating at `N` rpm:

$$P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60} \quad \text{(W, with } T \text{ in N·m)}$$

Substituting `T = τ_max · Z_p`:

$$\boxed{P = \dfrac{2 \pi N \, \tau_{\max} \, Z_p}{60 \times 10^6} \quad \text{kW} \quad (\text{with } \tau \text{ in MPa, } d \text{ in mm})}$$

### 2.2 Section Properties — Solid vs Hollow

Let `d` = outer diameter of solid shaft; `D` = outer diameter, `d` = inner diameter of hollow shaft.

| Cross-Section | Polar Moment of Inertia `J_p` | Outer Radius `R` | Polar Section Modulus `Z_p` |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Solid Circular** | `J_p = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}` | `R = \dfrac{d}{2}` | `Z_p = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{16}` |
| **Hollow Circular** | `J_p = \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d_i^4)}{32}` | `R = \dfrac{D}{2}` | `Z_p = \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d_i^4)}{16 D}` |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Quantity | Solid Shaft | Hollow Shaft | Units |
|:-:|:---------|:------------|:-------------|:------|
| 1 | `J_p` | `\dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}` | `\dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d_i^4)}{32}` | mm⁴ |
| 2 | `Z_p` | `\dfrac{\pi d^3}{16}` | `\dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d_i^4)}{16 D}` | mm³ |
| 3 | Max Torque (for `τ_max`) | `T = \dfrac{\pi}{16} \, \tau_{\max} \, d^3` | `T = \dfrac{\pi}{16} \, \tau_{\max} \, \dfrac{D^4 - d_i^4}{D}` | N·mm |
| 4 | Power `P` | `\dfrac{\pi \, N \, \tau_{\max} \, d^3}{16 \times 60 \times 10^6}` | `\dfrac{\pi \, N \, \tau_{\max} (D^4 - d_i^4)}{16 \times 60 \times 10^6 \, D}` | kW |
| 5 | Angle of Twist `θ` | `\dfrac{32 T L}{\pi G d^4}` | `\dfrac{32 T L}{\pi G (D^4 - d_i^4)}` | rad |
| 6 | Required `d` for given `P,N,τ` | `d = \left( \dfrac{16 \times 60 \times 10^6 \, P}{\pi N \tau_{\max}} \right)^{1/3}` | — | mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember the 60,000 divisor!** When `T` is in **N·m** and `N` is in **rpm**, the formula for kW is `P = 2πNT / 60,000`. When `T` is in **N·mm**, the divisor is `60 × 10^6`. Mixing units is the **#1 mistake** KTU students make in torsional problems.

### 2.4 Why Hollow Shafts Excel (Strength-to-Weight Logic)

The **weight** of a shaft is proportional to its cross-sectional **area**:

$$W_{\text{solid}} \propto d^2, \quad W_{\text{hollow}} \propto (D^2 - d_i^2)$$

The **strength** (torque capacity) is proportional to `Z_p`. If we fix the outer diameter `D = d` (same envelope), then:

$$\dfrac{T_{\text{hollow}}}{T_{\text{solid}}} = \dfrac{D^4 - d_i^4}{D^4} = 1 - k^4, \quad \text{where } k = \dfrac{d_i}{D}$$

$$\dfrac{W_{\text{hollow}}}{W_{\text{solid}}} = 1 - k^2$$

For `k = 0.5`: the hollow shaft transmits `1 − 0.0625 = 0.9375` of the torque (only 6.25% less) at only `0.75` of the weight — a **25% weight saving** for almost no strength penalty.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In robot **wrist joints**, hollow shafts route the **encoder cables** and **pneumatic/hydraulic lines** through the central bore, eliminating the need for external slip-rings and dramatically reducing wear. The same design philosophy is used in submarine propeller shafts, helicopter rotor masts, and electric-vehicle drive shafts.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Torque Capacity of a **Solid** Circular Shaft

We start from the **torsion equation**:

$$\dfrac{T}{J_p} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G \theta}{L}$$

**Step 1 — Write the maximum shear stress condition.**

The maximum shear stress occurs at the outer surface, where `r = R = d / 2`:

$$\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{T \cdot R}{J_p} = \dfrac{T \cdot (d/2)}{J_p}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the polar moment of inertia for a solid circular section.**

For a solid circular section of diameter `d`:

$$J_p = \dfrac{\pi \, d^4}{32}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the torque `T`.**

$$\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{T \cdot (d/2)}{\pi d^4 / 32} = \dfrac{32 \, T \, d}{2 \, \pi \, d^4} = \dfrac{16 \, T}{\pi \, d^3}$$

Rearranging for `T`:

$$\boxed{T_{\text{solid}} = \dfrac{\pi \, \tau_{\max} \, d^3}{16}}$$

**Step 4 — Introduce the polar section modulus `Z_p`.**

By definition, `Z_p = J_p / R = (\pi d^4 / 32) / (d/2) = \pi d^3 / 16`. Hence:

$$T_{\text{solid}} = \tau_{\max} \cdot Z_p$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Torque Capacity of a **Hollow** Circular Shaft

**Step 1 — Substitute the polar moment of inertia for a hollow circular section.**

For a hollow section of outer diameter `D` and inner diameter `d_i`:

$$J_p = \dfrac{\pi \, (D^4 - d_i^4)}{32}, \quad R = \dfrac{D}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the maximum shear stress condition.**

$$\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{T \cdot (D/2)}{\pi (D^4 - d_i^4) / 32} = \dfrac{32 \, T \, D}{2 \, \pi \, (D^4 - d_i^4)} = \dfrac{16 \, T \, D}{\pi (D^4 - d_i^4)}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the torque `T`.**

$$\boxed{T_{\text{hollow}} = \dfrac{\pi \, \tau_{\max} \, (D^4 - d_i^4)}{16 \, D}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** In a hollow shaft, the **inner fibres never reach the maximum shear stress**. The maximum stress is always at the **outer fibre** `r = D/2`. This is the geometric reason why removing the central core does not weaken the shaft — the core was contributing nothing to the strength.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Power Transmitted in Terms of `d`, `N`, and `τ_max`

The relationship between power, torque, and speed is derived from basic mechanics:

$$P = \dfrac{\text{Work}}{\text{Time}} = \dfrac{T \cdot (2 \pi N / 60) \cdot t}{t} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60} \;\; \text{(W)}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute `T` from the solid-shaft expression.**

$$P = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} \cdot \dfrac{\pi \, \tau_{\max} \, d^3}{16} = \dfrac{2 \pi^2 N \tau_{\max} d^3}{16 \times 60} = \dfrac{\pi^2 N \tau_{\max} d^3}{480} \;\; \text{(W, with } d \text{ in m, } \tau \text{ in Pa)}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to practical engineering units.**

`d` is typically given in **mm** and `τ_max` in **MPa (N/mm²)**. To express `P` in **kW**:

$$\begin{aligned}
P \text{ (W)} &= \dfrac{\pi^2 \cdot N \cdot (\tau_{\max} \times 10^6) \cdot (d \times 10^{-3})^3}{480} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{\pi^2 \cdot N \cdot \tau_{\max} \cdot d^3 \times 10^{6} \times 10^{-9}}{480} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{\pi^2 \cdot N \cdot \tau_{\max} \cdot d^3 \times 10^{-3}}{480} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{\pi^2 \cdot N \cdot \tau_{\max} \cdot d^3}{480{,}000} \;\; \text{W} \\[4pt]
P \text{ (kW)} &= \dfrac{\pi^2 \cdot N \cdot \tau_{\max} \cdot d^3}{480 \times 10^6}
\end{aligned}$$

The compact form (often quoted in KTU textbooks) is:

$$\boxed{P_{\text{solid}} = \dfrac{\pi \, N \, \tau_{\max} \, d^3}{16 \times 60 \times 10^6} \quad \text{kW}}$$

**Step 3 — For the hollow shaft:**

$$\boxed{P_{\text{hollow}} = \dfrac{\pi \, N \, \tau_{\max} \, (D^4 - d_i^4)}{16 \times 60 \times 10^6 \cdot D} \quad \text{kW}}$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Required Diameter for a Given Power

Starting from `P = \pi N \tau_{\max} d^3 / (16 \times 60 \times 10^6)` and solving for `d`:

$$d^3 = \dfrac{16 \times 60 \times 10^6 \cdot P}{\pi \cdot N \cdot \tau_{\max}}$$

$$\boxed{d = \left( \dfrac{16 \times 60 \times 10^6 \cdot P}{\pi \, N \, \tau_{\max}} \right)^{1/3} \quad \text{mm}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Cube-Root Shortcut for the Exam Hall:** Once you compute the numerator, take the cube root by estimation. For example, if `d^3 = 1,250,000` mm³, then `d ≈ 108 mm` (since `100³ = 10⁶` and `108³ ≈ 1,260,000`). This saves 90 seconds in the exam hall.

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Strength-to-Weight Comparison of Hollow vs Solid Shaft

Let `k = d_i / D` (the ratio of inner to outer diameter). For the same outer diameter `D` and same `τ_max`:

$$\dfrac{T_{\text{hollow}}}{T_{\text{solid}}} = 1 - k^4$$

$$\dfrac{W_{\text{hollow}}}{W_{\text{solid}}} = 1 - k^2$$

**Percentage saving in weight for equal strength:**

$$\Delta W \% = \left(1 - \dfrac{1 - k^2}{1 - k^4}\right) \times 100 = \left(1 - \dfrac{1 - k^2}{(1 - k^2)(1 + k^2)}\right) \times 100 = \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{1 + k^2}\right) \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\Delta W \% = \dfrac{k^2}{1 + k^2} \times 100}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical sanity check:** For `k = 0.6`, `\Delta W \% = 0.36 / 1.36 \times 100 = 26.47%`. So a hollow shaft with `d_i = 0.6 D` saves **~26.5% weight** while losing only `1 - 0.6^4 = 1 - 0.1296 = 87.04%`... no, that means it retains **87.04% of the strength**. *(A common student trap is to confuse strength loss with weight loss.)*

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Decision Map for Shaft Selection

| Design Requirement | Choose Solid Shaft When… | Choose Hollow Shaft When… |
|---|---|---|
| **Weight critical** (robotics, aerospace) | Cost is paramount and weight is irrelevant | Weight must be minimized |
| **Cable routing** | No through-bore needed | Cables, fluids, or a secondary shaft must pass through |
| **Manufacturing cost** | Low-cost, low-speed application | High-volume production where material cost matters |
| **High rpm (> 3000)** | Rare — solid shafts have high polar inertia | Required — reduces centrifugal effects and bearing load |
| **Strain energy storage** (springs, couplings) | Acceptable | Required — `J_p` can be tuned by varying `D` and `d_i` independently |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Shear-Stress Distribution Across the Cross-Section

The Mermaid block below maps the **linear shear-stress gradient** from the centroid to the outer fibre, highlighting that the **central core of a solid shaft is underutilised**.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["START: Apply Torque T to Shaft"] --> B["Cross-Section Type?"]
    B -->|Solid| C["Solid Circular Section: d, R = d/2"]
    B -->|Hollow| D["Hollow Circular Section: D, d_i, k = d_i / D"]
    C --> E["Compute Jp = PI d^4 / 32"]
    D --> F["Compute Jp = PI D^4 minus d_i to power 4 over 32"]
    E --> G["Compute Zp = PI d^3 / 16"]
    F --> H["Compute Zp = PI D^4 minus d_i to power 4 over 16D"]
    G --> I["Max Shear Stress tau_max at r = R, value = 16T divided by PI d cubed"]
    H --> J["Max Shear Stress tau_max at r = D by 2, value = 16T D divided by PI bracket D^4 minus d_i^4 bracket"]
    I --> K["CORE OBSERVATION: Stress at r = 0 is ZERO. Central material carries no load."]
    J --> L["CORE OBSERVATION: Inner fibre at r = d_i by 2 carries only fraction k of tau_max."]
    K --> M["ENGINEERING INSIGHT: Remove central material to save weight without losing strength."]
    L --> M
    M --> N["RESULT: Hollow shaft with k approx 0.5 saves 20 percent weight for 6 percent strength loss"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Shaft Design Workflow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1["INPUT: Power P kW"] --> B1["INPUT: Speed N rpm"]
    B1 --> C1["INPUT: Allowable tau_max MPa"]
    C1 --> D1["SELECTION: Solid OR Hollow"]
    D1 -->|Solid| E1["FORMULA: d cube = 16 times 60 times 10 to power 6 times P divided by PI times N times tau"]
    D1 -->|Hollow| E2["FORMULA: D cube approx = same as solid, then choose k to fix d_i"]
    E1 --> F1["OUTPUT: Required diameter d in mm"]
    E2 --> F2["OUTPUT: Outer D and inner d_i in mm"]
    F1 --> G1["VALIDATION: Angle of twist theta less than allowable limit"]
    F2 --> G1
    G1 --> H1["FINAL: Specify material, keyway, bearing seats"]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Stress-Gradient Visual (ASCII — for notebook reference)

The diagram below sketches the **linear distribution** of shear stress `τ` across the diameter of a **solid** shaft. Stress is **zero at the centre** and **maximum at the outer fibre**.

```
                  τ_max  ●━━━━━━━━━━━●  τ_max
                       ╱             ╲
                      ╱               ╲
                     ╱                 ╲
                    ╱                   ╲
                   ╱                     ╲
                  ╱                       ╲
                 ╱                         ╲
                ●                           ●
              τ = 0  ────────────────  τ = 0
              [r = -R]   [r = 0]    [r = +R]
                       CENTERLINE
              ←——————————— d ———————————→

   SOLID SHAFT: linear, single-segment stress profile
   HOLLOW SHAFT: same linear profile, but the
                 segment from -d_i/2 to +d_i/2
                 is "missing" — yet that segment
                 contributed almost nothing to τ_max!
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit mismatch (–2 to –3 marks):** Mixing `T` in N·m with `N` in rpm without dividing by `60,000` for kW. Always re-state units **before** substituting.
> 2. **Confusing `d` and `D` (–1 mark):** In hollow shafts, `d_i` is the **inner** diameter, `D` is the **outer** diameter. The formula `Z_p = \pi (D^4 - d_i^4) / (16 D)` — not `16 d_i`.
> 3. **Forgetting `1 kW = 10³ W` (–1 mark):** Many students write `P = 2\pi N T / 60` and report the answer in W when the question asks for kW.
> 4. **Using `d_i` instead of `D` in the denominator of `Z_p` for hollow shafts (–2 marks):** The polar section modulus denominator is always the **outer** radius `R = D/2`, regardless of whether the shaft is solid or hollow.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions (–1 mark):** Forgetting to mention that the shaft material is homogeneous, isotropic, and that the cross-section remains plane (Saint-Venant's principle) costs easy marks.

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
*State and explain the torsion equation for a circular shaft. Define the term "polar section modulus."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The torsion equation for a circular shaft subjected to torque `T` is:

$$\dfrac{T}{J_p} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G \, \theta}{L}$$

It states that the **shear stress `τ` at any radius `r`** is directly proportional to `r` and inversely proportional to the **polar moment of inertia `J_p`**. The ratio `T / J_p` equals the **rate of twist per unit length** `\theta / L` times the **shear modulus `G`**. **(2 marks)**

**Polar section modulus** `Z_p` is defined as the ratio `J_p / R`, where `R` is the outer radius. It is a purely geometric property that measures the shaft's **ability to resist shear stress under torsion**. A higher `Z_p` means lower `τ_max` for the same applied torque. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
*Why are hollow shafts preferred over solid shafts in high-speed power transmission? List any two reasons.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Higher strength-to-weight ratio:** A hollow shaft with outer-to-inner diameter ratio `k = 0.5` retains `93.75%` of the strength of a solid shaft of the same outer diameter while saving `25%` of the weight. **(1.5 marks)**
2. **Lower material consumption and cost:** Less raw material is used for the same torque capacity, which is economically advantageous. **(0.75 mark)**
3. **Routability:** The central bore allows **cables, wiring, or even a secondary shaft** to pass through — critical in **robotic joint assemblies, helicopter rotor masts, and submarine propeller shafts**. **(0.75 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Module-end questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice. The student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question has sub-parts typically weighted **7 + 7 marks**. We provide model solutions for both alternatives.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a)** A **solid circular shaft** of diameter `60 mm` transmits `75 kW` at `200 rpm`. Determine:
   1. The torque transmitted. **(3 marks)**
   2. The maximum shear stress developed in the shaft. **(2 marks)**
   3. The angle of twist in degrees over a `2 m` length. Take `G = 80 GPa`. **(2 marks)**

**(b)** A **hollow circular shaft** is to transmit `300 kW` at `180 rpm`. The allowable shear stress is `60 MPa`, and the ratio of inner to outer diameter is `0.6`. Determine the **outer and inner diameters**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Solid Shaft Analysis**

**Step 1: Compute the torque from power and speed.**

Using the standard formula `P = 2\pi N T / 60{,}000` (with `P` in kW, `N` in rpm, `T` in N·m):

$$75 = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 200 \times T}{60{,}000}$$

$$T = \dfrac{75 \times 60{,}000}{2 \pi \times 200} = \dfrac{4{,}500{,}000}{400 \pi} = \dfrac{11{,}250}{\pi}$$

$$\boxed{T = 3581.0 \;\; \text{N·m} \approx 3.581 \times 10^6 \;\; \text{N·mm}}$$

**(3 marks awarded for correct substitution and final value.)**

**Step 2: Compute the maximum shear stress.**

For a solid shaft, `τ_max = 16 T / (\pi d^3)`:

$$\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{16 \times 3.581 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 60^3} = \dfrac{5.730 \times 10^7}{\pi \times 216{,}000} = \dfrac{5.730 \times 10^7}{678{,}584.0}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{\max} \approx 84.43 \;\; \text{MPa}}$$

**(2 marks.)**

**Step 3: Compute the angle of twist.**

Using `\theta = T L / (G J_p)`, with `J_p = \pi d^4 / 32`:

$$J_p = \dfrac{\pi \times 60^4}{32} = \dfrac{\pi \times 12{,}960{,}000}{32} = 1{,}272{,}345.0 \;\; \text{mm}^4$$

$$\theta = \dfrac{3.581 \times 10^6 \times 2000}{80{,}000 \times 1{,}272{,}345.0} = \dfrac{7.162 \times 10^9}{1.0179 \times 10^{11}} = 0.0704 \;\; \text{rad}$$

Converting to degrees:

$$\boxed{\theta = 0.0704 \times \dfrac{180}{\pi} = 4.03^\circ}$$

**(2 marks.)**

---

**Part (b) — Hollow Shaft Design**

**Step 1: Set up the design equation.**

For a hollow shaft, the power transmission equation is:

$$P = \dfrac{\pi \, N \, \tau_{\max} \, (D^4 - d_i^4)}{16 \times 60 \times 10^6 \cdot D}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the given ratios and constants.**

Given: `k = d_i / D = 0.6`, so `d_i = 0.6 D` and `d_i^4 = 0.1296 D^4`. Then:

$$D^4 - d_i^4 = D^4 (1 - 0.1296) = 0.8704 \, D^4$$

The equation becomes:

$$P = \dfrac{\pi \times 180 \times 60 \times 0.8704 \, D^4}{16 \times 60 \times 10^6 \times D} = \dfrac{\pi \times 180 \times 60 \times 0.8704 \, D^3}{16 \times 60 \times 10^6}$$

**Step 3: Simplify and solve for `D`.**

$$P = \dfrac{\pi \times 180 \times 0.8704 \, D^3}{16 \times 10^6} = \dfrac{492.1 \, D^3}{16 \times 10^6} \approx 3.0757 \times 10^{-5} \cdot D^3$$

Setting `P = 300 kW`:

$$300 = 3.0757 \times 10^{-5} \cdot D^3$$

$$D^3 = \dfrac{300}{3.0757 \times 10^{-5}} = 9.7536 \times 10^6 \;\; \text{mm}^3$$

$$D = (9.7536 \times 10^6)^{1/3} = 213.93 \;\; \text{mm}$$

$$\boxed{D \approx 214 \;\; \text{mm}, \quad d_i = 0.6 \times 214 = 128.4 \;\; \text{mm}}$$

**(7 marks — 3 for setup, 2 for substitution, 2 for final numerical answer.)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative — KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a)** A **solid steel shaft** is required to transmit `150 kW` at `240 rpm`. The allowable shear stress is `70 MPa`. Find the **required diameter**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the same shaft is replaced by a **hollow shaft** with the same outer diameter and inner-to-outer ratio `k = 0.5`, find the **percentage saving in weight** and the **percentage loss in strength**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Solid Shaft Diameter**

The design formula is:

$$P = \dfrac{\pi \, N \, \tau_{\max} \, d^3}{16 \times 60 \times 10^6}$$

Rearranging:

$$d^3 = \dfrac{16 \times 60 \times 10^6 \cdot P}{\pi \, N \, \tau_{\max}}$$

**Substitute** `P = 150 kW`, `N = 240 rpm`, `τ_max = 70 MPa`:

$$d^3 = \dfrac{16 \times 60 \times 10^6 \times 150}{\pi \times 240 \times 70} = \dfrac{1.44 \times 10^{11}}{52{,}778.9} = 2.7285 \times 10^6 \;\; \text{mm}^3$$

$$d = (2.7285 \times 10^6)^{1/3} \approx 139.4 \;\; \text{mm}$$

$$\boxed{d \approx 140 \;\; \text{mm (rounded to standard size)}}$$

**(7 marks — 3 for formula, 2 for substitution, 2 for final answer.)**

---

**Part (b) — Hollow vs Solid Comparison**

For the hollow shaft, `k = d_i / D = 0.5` and `D = d = 140 mm`. Then `d_i = 70 mm`.

**Step 1: Percentage saving in weight.**

$$\dfrac{W_{\text{hollow}}}{W_{\text{solid}}} = \dfrac{D^2 - d_i^2}{D^2} = 1 - k^2 = 1 - 0.25 = 0.75$$

$$\text{Weight saving} = (1 - 0.75) \times 100\% = \boxed{25\%}$$

**Step 2: Percentage loss in strength (i.e., reduction in torque capacity).**

$$\dfrac{T_{\text{hollow}}}{T_{\text{solid}}} = 1 - k^4 = 1 - 0.0625 = 0.9375$$

$$\text{Strength loss} = (1 - 0.9375) \times 100\% = \boxed{6.25\%}$$

**Step 3: Insight.**

$$\boxed{\text{Weight saved: 25\%} \quad | \quad \text{Strength lost: only 6.25\%}}$$

**(7 marks — 3 marks for weight ratio, 2 marks for strength ratio, 2 marks for the concluding insight.)**

> [!TIP]
> **Cross-check using the derived formula** `\Delta W \% = k^2 / (1 + k^2) \times 100 = 0.25 / 1.25 \times 100 = 20%`. This is the weight saved **for equal strength** — a different scenario. Make sure the question specifies what is being held constant (here, equal outer diameter), then pick the right ratio.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute Reading)**

- [x] **Power Equation:** `P = 2\pi N T / 60{,}000` (kW, with `T` in N·m, `N` in rpm) — **the most-tested formula in Module 3.**
- [x] **Torsion Equation:** `\dfrac{T}{J_p} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G \theta}{L}` — applies only to **circular** shafts under **elastic torsion**.
- [x] **Solid shaft torque:** `T = \dfrac{\pi}{16} \tau_{\max} d^3`; section modulus `Z_p = \pi d^3 / 16`.
- [x] **Hollow shaft torque:** `T = \dfrac{\pi}{16} \tau_{\max} \dfrac{D^4 - d_i^4}{D}`; section modulus `Z_p = \pi (D^4 - d_i^4) / (16 D)`.
- [x] **Stress distribution:** **Linear**, **zero at centre**, **maximum at outer fibre** — always.
- [x] **Angle of twist:** `\theta = T L / (G J_p)` (radians). For a multi-segment shaft, sum the individual twists.
- [x] **Hollow-shaft advantage:** For `k = 0.5`, weight saving `25%` and strength loss only `6.25%` — a textbook standard.
- [x] **Unit traps:** `G` in N/mm² ↔ `θ` in rad ↔ `L` in mm ↔ `T` in N·mm. **Keep the system consistent.**
- [x] **Design equation (solid):** `d = \left( \dfrac{16 \times 60 \times 10^6 \cdot P}{\pi N \tau_{\max}} \right)^{1/3}`.
- [x] **Robotic-application insight:** Hollow shafts allow **through-bore routing** of cables, encoder wiring, and pneumatic lines — the standard in **harmonic-drive, RV-reducer, and cycloidal-drive** assemblies.
- [x] **Always state assumptions:** Homogeneous, isotropic material; plane sections remain plane (Saint-Venant); torque is applied via pure shear at the ends.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Combined Bending
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT525 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS OF ROBOTIC PARTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Combined Bending

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Combined Bending** (also called **Unsymmetric Bending** or **Biaxial Bending**) is a state of flexural loading in a structural member wherein the **bending moment vector acts in a plane that is *not* parallel to either of the two principal centroidal axes** of the cross-section. Equivalently, the load is resolved into two mutually perpendicular components producing simultaneous bending about **both principal axes (X-X and Y-Y)** of the cross-section.

Mathematically, the condition is expressed as:

$$\sigma = \frac{M_x \cdot y}{I_x} + \frac{M_y \cdot x}{I_y}$$

where the two moment components $M_x$ and $M_y$ coexist, producing a **non-uniform bi-axial normal stress distribution**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Combined Bending is the gateway topic to **Eccentric Axial Loading**, **Solid/Section subjected to Unsymmetric Bending**, the **Location of the Neutral Axis**, and the determination of the **Kernel of a Section**. Mastery of the resultant-stress equation is mandatory for ESE and lab viva questions.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are **gripping a wooden plank vertically on a table** and pushing it sideways with your right hand (bending it about its strong axis) **and simultaneously pushing it forward with your left hand** (bending it about its weak axis). The plank is no longer bending in a *single* vertical plane — it is twisting into a curved shape because the **two bending actions are superimposed**.

A more relatable engineering analogy: **a flagpole subjected to wind pressure**. The wind hits the pole horizontally, but the resultant force may not pass through a principal axis of the circular or polygonal cross-section. Each fibre on the windward side experiences combined compression (axial) plus bending about the axis perpendicular to the wind. Robotic **cantilever links**, **gripper arms**, and **L-shaped brackets** loaded at an angle experience this very phenomenon.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** Combined Bending = Bending about Axis-1 **PLUS** Bending about Axis-2 **AT THE SAME TIME**. The total stress at any point is the algebraic sum of the two.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Sign Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\sigma$ | Normal bending stress | **Pa (N/m²)** or **N/mm² (MPa)** |
| $M_x, M_y$ | Moment components about X and Y axes | **N·m** or **N·mm** |
| $I_x, I_y$ | Second moments of area about X and Y axes | **m⁴** or **mm⁴** |
| $Z_x, Z_y$ | Section moduli | **m³** or **mm³** |
| $E$ | Young's Modulus of Elasticity | **N/m²** or **N/mm²** |
| $c$ | Distance from neutral axis to extreme fibre | **m** or **mm** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric:** For KTU numerical answers, the **allowable stress** in robotic structural members (typically **aluminium 6061-T6, mild steel, or carbon-fibre composites**) ranges between **$\sigma_{allow} = 100$ MPa to $250$ MPa**. Always verify and state the material constant.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Bending Types

- **Symmetric (Simple) Bending:** Moment vector lies in a plane containing one of the principal centroidal axes → single-axis bending.
- **Unsymmetric / Combined Bending:** Moment vector lies in a plane containing neither principal axis → bi-axial bending.
- **Pure Bending:** Constant moment, no shear.
- **Eccentric Bending:** Axial load applied at a point **offset** from the centroid — a *special case* of combined bending where an axial force acts in combination with moments about both principal axes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Underlying Mechanics

When a transverse load $P$ is applied at an arbitrary point (not on a principal axis) of a cross-section, the **statically equivalent system** at the centroid consists of:

1. The **axial force component** $P_x$ and $P_y$ (resolved parallel to principal axes),
2. The **bending moments** $M_x$ and $M_y$ about the principal centroidal axes,
3. A **torsional moment** if the load does not pass through the shear centre — *this is excluded in pure combined bending* (assumed loaded through the shear centre).

### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

- **Step 1 — Load Resolution:** Decompose the applied eccentric load $P$ into components $P_x$ and $P_y$ parallel to the principal axes.
- **Step 2 — Moment Computation:** Compute the equivalent moment about the centroid: $M_x = P_y \cdot e_y$ and $M_y = P_x \cdot e_x$, where $e_x, e_y$ are the eccentricities.
- **Step 3 — Stress Superposition:** Apply the **Principle of Superposition** (valid in linear elastic regime) — the total bending stress at any point $(x, y)$ is the algebraic sum of stresses from each moment component.
- **Step 4 — Sign Convention:** Adopt KTU standard: **tension is positive (+)**; **compression is negative (−)**. Choose sign of $M_x, M_y$ based on physical bending direction.
- **Step 5 — Neutral Axis Identification:** The locus of points where the **total stress = 0** defines the Neutral Axis (NA) of the unsymmetrically bent beam.

---

## 2.2 The Resultant Bending Stress Equation

The KTU-board-favoured master formula is:

$$\boxed{\sigma = \frac{M_x \cdot y}{I_x} + \frac{M_y \cdot x}{I_y}}$$

Or, equivalently using **section moduli**:

$$\sigma = \frac{M_x}{Z_x} + \frac{M_y}{Z_y}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** When the question asks for **maximum stress**, identify the **corner fibres** of the cross-section and evaluate $\sigma$ at each. The corner with the **largest magnitude** (considering sign) governs the design.

---

## 2.3 Neutral Axis Equation — High-Yield KTU Result

Setting $\sigma = 0$ in the master equation:

$$\frac{M_x \cdot y}{I_x} + \frac{M_y \cdot x}{I_y} = 0$$

Solving for the locus $(x, y)$:

$$\boxed{\frac{x}{a} + \frac{y}{b} = 1 \quad \text{where} \quad a = -\frac{I_y}{M_y} \cdot \frac{M_x}{I_x}, \quad b = -\frac{I_x}{M_x} \cdot \frac{M_y}{I_y}}$$

The Neutral Axis is therefore a **straight line** in the cross-sectional plane.

**Intercepts on the principal axes:**

$$a = -\frac{I_y}{M_y} \cdot \frac{M_x}{I_x} \quad \text{(intercept on X-axis)}$$

$$b = -\frac{I_x}{M_x} \cdot \frac{M_y}{I_y} \quad \text{(intercept on Y-axis)}$$

The Neutral Axis is **perpendicular to the plane of the applied moment vector** but **NOT necessarily perpendicular to a principal axis**.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Use \vert and \mid for absolute-value expressions to preserve markdown table integrity.**

| # | Formula | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{M_x \cdot y}{I_x} + \dfrac{M_y \cdot x}{I_y}$ | Resultant bending stress | General unsymmetric bending |
| 2 | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M_x}{Z_x} + \dfrac{M_y}{Z_y}$ | Maximum stress at corner | Design check |
| 3 | $\tan\theta = \dfrac{M_y \cdot I_x}{M_x \cdot I_y}$ | Angle of NA with X-axis | NA orientation |
| 4 | $\dfrac{x}{a} + \dfrac{y}{b} = 1$ | NA intercept form | NA location |
| 5 | $a = -\dfrac{I_y \cdot M_x}{M_y \cdot I_x}$ | X-intercept of NA | Numerical problems |
| 6 | $b = -\dfrac{I_x \cdot M_y}{M_x \cdot I_y}$ | Y-intercept of NA | Numerical problems |
| 7 | $P = \sigma_{allow} \cdot A \cdot k$ | Eccentric load via kernel | Eccentric loading |
| 8 | $k_x = \dfrac{Z_y}{A}, \quad k_y = \dfrac{Z_x}{A}$ | Kernel radius (rectangular) | Safe eccentricity check |
| 9 | $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A} + \dfrac{P \cdot e_y \cdot y}{I_x} + \dfrac{P \cdot e_x \cdot x}{I_y}$ | Stress under eccentric axial load | Eccentric loading |
| 10 | $\theta_{NA} = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{M_x \cdot I_y}{M_y \cdot I_x}\right)$ | Inclination of NA | KT-board favourite |

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Combined Bending Analysis

- **Robotic Manipulator Links:** End-effectors and arms undergo unsymmetric loading due to off-centre payloads.
- **L- and T-Section Beams in Gantry Systems:** Principal axes do not align with load plane.
- **Wind-loaded Chimneys & Flagpoles:** Eccentric wind pressure.
- **Pressure Vessels with Nozzle Loads:** Combined bending and axial stress.
- **Offshore Platform Bracing:** Multi-directional wave loading.

The **Kernel of a Section** (a closed region inside the cross-section) is the locus of points where an axial compressive load produces **no tension anywhere** — a critical design tool in masonry columns and robotic gripper jaws.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Resultant Bending Stress Equation

Consider a straight prismatic beam of length $L$ loaded such that the **resultant moment vector** $\vec{M}$ acts in a plane that makes an angle $\theta$ with the principal Y-axis (or $\phi$ with the X-axis).

### Step 1 — Resolve the Moment Vector

The moment $\vec{M}$ is resolved into two orthogonal components about the principal centroidal axes X-X and Y-Y:

$$M_x = M \cos\phi$$
$$M_y = M \sin\phi$$

where $\phi$ is the angle that the moment vector (or load plane) makes with the **X-axis**.

> **Conversion Logic:** This is a simple 2-D vector decomposition. If the moment lies entirely in the X-Z plane, $M_y = 0$ and we recover simple bending.

### Step 2 — Apply the Simple Bending Equation to Each Component

For a fibre located at coordinates $(x, y)$ measured from the centroid:

- Stress due to $M_x$ acting alone (bending about X-axis, fibre at distance $y$):

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{M_x \cdot y}{I_x}$$

- Stress due to $M_y$ acting alone (bending about Y-axis, fibre at distance $x$):

$$\sigma_2 = \frac{M_y \cdot x}{I_y}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Principle of Superposition

Because the material is **linear-elastic, homogeneous, and isotropic**, the principle of superposition holds:

$$\sigma = \sigma_1 + \sigma_2$$

$$\boxed{\sigma = \frac{M_x \cdot y}{I_x} + \frac{M_y \cdot x}{I_y}}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the master equation. The first term vanishes along the X-axis ($y = 0$); the second vanishes along the Y-axis ($x = 0$). The equation reduces to simple bending when either $M_x = 0$ or $M_y = 0$.

### Step 4 — Sign Convention Refinement

Following the **KTU board convention**:

- $M_x$ is **positive** if it produces **tension at $+\,y$** (bottom fibre of a horizontal beam).
- $M_y$ is **positive** if it produces **tension at $+\,x$**.
- Compression is negative.

A useful sign check: at the **centroid** ($x = 0, y = 0$), $\sigma = 0$ — this is a property of the principal axes, not the neutral axis.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Neutral Axis Equation

### Step 1 — Set Total Stress to Zero

The neutral axis (NA) is the locus of points where the longitudinal fibre experiences **no extension or compression**, i.e., $\sigma = 0$:

$$\frac{M_x \cdot y}{I_x} + \frac{M_y \cdot x}{I_y} = 0$$

### Step 2 — Rearrange to Identify the Locus

Multiply through by $\dfrac{I_x \cdot I_y}{M_x \cdot M_y}$:

$$y \cdot \frac{I_y}{M_y} + x \cdot \frac{I_x}{M_x} = 0$$

$$\frac{x}{a} + \frac{y}{b} = 0 \quad \text{where} \quad a = -\frac{I_x}{M_x} \cdot \frac{M_y}{I_y}, \quad b = -\frac{I_y}{M_y} \cdot \frac{M_x}{I_x}$$

### Step 3 — Rewrite in Standard Intercept Form

The equation $\dfrac{x}{a} + \dfrac{y}{b} = 0$ passes through the **origin** (centroid). The NA can also be written as:

$$\frac{x}{a'} + \frac{y}{b'} = 1$$

with

$$a' = \frac{I_y}{M_y} \cdot \frac{M_x}{I_x}, \quad b' = \frac{I_x}{M_x} \cdot \frac{M_y}{I_y}$$

### Step 4 — Angle of the Neutral Axis

The slope of the NA with respect to the X-axis is:

$$\tan\theta_{NA} = -\frac{b'}{a'} = -\frac{I_x \cdot M_y}{I_y \cdot M_x}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** When $I_x = I_y$ (a square or circular section), the NA is **perpendicular to the moment vector** because $\tan\theta_{NA} = -M_y/M_x = -\tan\phi$ → $\theta_{NA} = 90° - \phi$, confirming perpendicularity.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A rectangular bar $100 \text{ mm} \times 200 \text{ mm}$ is subjected to a moment of $M = 10 \text{ kN·m}$ acting in a vertical plane that makes an angle of $30°$ with the vertical (Y-axis). Determine:

(a) The stresses at the four corners of the cross-section.
(b) The position and inclination of the neutral axis.

**Given:**
- Width $b = 100$ mm, Depth $d = 200$ mm
- $I_x = \dfrac{b d^3}{12} = \dfrac{100 \times 200^3}{12} = 66.67 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$
- $I_y = \dfrac{d b^3}{12} = \dfrac{200 \times 100^3}{12} = 16.67 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$
- $M = 10 \times 10^6$ N·mm
- $\phi = 30°$ (angle with Y-axis) → angle with X-axis = $60°$

**Step 1 — Resolve Moment Components**

$$M_x = M \sin 30° = 10 \times 0.5 = 5 \text{ kN·m} = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

$$M_y = M \cos 30° = 10 \times 0.866 = 8.66 \text{ kN·m} = 8.66 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Corner Coordinates**

The four corners are at $(\pm 50, \pm 100)$ mm.

**Step 3 — Compute Stresses**

At corner $(+50, +100)$:

$$\sigma_A = \frac{5 \times 10^6 \times 100}{66.67 \times 10^6} + \frac{8.66 \times 10^6 \times 50}{16.67 \times 10^6}$$

$$\sigma_A = 7.5 + 25.98 = 33.48 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (Tension)}$$

At corner $(-50, +100)$:

$$\sigma_B = \frac{5 \times 10^6 \times 100}{66.67 \times 10^6} + \frac{8.66 \times 10^6 \times (-50)}{16.67 \times 10^6}$$

$$\sigma_B = 7.5 - 25.98 = -18.48 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (Compression)}$$

At corner $(-50, -100)$:

$$\sigma_C = \frac{5 \times 10^6 \times (-100)}{66.67 \times 10^6} + \frac{8.66 \times 10^6 \times (-50)}{16.67 \times 10^6}$$

$$\sigma_C = -7.5 - 25.98 = -33.48 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (Compression)}$$

At corner $(+50, -100)$:

$$\sigma_D = \frac{5 \times 10^6 \times (-100)}{66.67 \times 10^6} + \frac{8.66 \times 10^6 \times 50}{16.67 \times 10^6}$$

$$\sigma_D = -7.5 + 25.98 = 18.48 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (Tension)}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum and Minimum Stress**

- $\sigma_{max} = +33.48$ MPa at corner A (Tension)
- $\sigma_{min} = -33.48$ MPa at corner C (Compression)

**Step 5 — Neutral Axis Equation**

$$\frac{5 \times 10^6 \cdot y}{66.67 \times 10^6} + \frac{8.66 \times 10^6 \cdot x}{16.67 \times 10^6} = 0$$

$$0.075\,y + 0.5196\,x = 0 \implies y = -6.928\,x$$

**Step 6 — Inclination of NA**

$$\tan\theta_{NA} = -6.928 \implies \theta_{NA} = -81.78° \text{ (with X-axis)}$$

Or equivalently $\theta_{NA} = 8.22°$ with the Y-axis (i.e., nearly vertical).

> **Conversion Logic:** The NA is nearly parallel to the Y-axis because $I_y$ is small ($16.67$ vs $66.67$) — the section resists bending about Y much more weakly, so the NA tilts towards the weaker axis's direction.

---

## 3.4 Special Case — Eccentric Axial Loading

If an axial compressive load $P$ is applied at point $(e_x, e_y)$ (eccentricities from centroid), the stress at any fibre $(x, y)$ is:

$$\sigma = \frac{-P}{A} + \frac{P \cdot e_y \cdot y}{I_x} + \frac{P \cdot e_x \cdot x}{I_y}$$

The NA in this case has intercepts:

$$a = \frac{I_y}{A \cdot e_x}, \quad b = \frac{I_x}{A \cdot e_y}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** *Show that for an eccentric load applied within the kernel of a rectangular section, the entire section is in compression and no tension develops.* This is solved by verifying that **both NA intercepts $a$ and $b$ fall outside the section boundaries** — i.e., the load point lies within the **central core (kernel)**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Combined Bending Stress Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Input: Applied Load P and Geometry] --> A1[Identify Principal Centroidal Axes XX and YY]
    A1 --> A2[Compute Second Moments I_x and I_y]
    A2 --> A3[Resolve Load into P_x and P_y]
    A3 --> A4[Compute Moment Components M_x and M_y]
    A4 --> A5[Apply Superposition Principle]
    A5 --> A6[Master Equation: sigma = M_x y by I_x plus M_y x by I_y]
    A6 --> A7{Evaluate at All Corner Fibres}
    A7 --> A8[Compute Stress at Corner 1]
    A7 --> A9[Compute Stress at Corner 2]
    A7 --> A10[Compute Stress at Corner 3]
    A7 --> A11[Compute Stress at Corner 4]
    A8 --> A12[Identify Max Tension and Max Compression]
    A9 --> A12
    A10 --> A12
    A11 --> A12
    A12 --> A13[Compare with Allowable Stress]
    A13 --> A14{Design Safe?}
    A14 -- Yes --> A15[Final Design Accepted]
    A14 -- No --> A16[Revise Section or Material]
    A6 --> A17[Set sigma equals 0]
    A17 --> A18[Derive Neutral Axis Equation]
    A18 --> A19[Compute X intercept and Y intercept]
    A19 --> A20[Compute NA Inclination Angle]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Stress Distribution on a Rectangular Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B0[Cross-Section Rectangle 100 by 200 mm] --> B1[Corner A: plus 50, plus 100]
    B0 --> B2[Corner B: minus 50, plus 100]
    B0 --> B3[Corner C: minus 50, minus 100]
    B0 --> B4[Corner D: plus 50, minus 100]
    B1 --> B5[Stress sigma_A equals plus 33.48 MPa Tension]
    B2 --> B6[Stress sigma_B equals minus 18.48 MPa Compression]
    B3 --> B7[Stress sigma_C equals minus 33.48 MPa Compression]
    B4 --> B8[Stress sigma_D equals plus 18.48 MPa Tension]
    B5 --> B9[Maximum Stress Location]
    B7 --> B9
    B9 --> B10[NA is straight line y equals minus 6.928 x]
    B10 --> B11[NA Inclination 81.78 degrees from X axis]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Neutral Axis Behaviour for Different Section Geometries

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0[Section Geometry and Moment Vector] --> C1{Symmetric Section?}
    C1 -- Yes --> C2[For I_x equals I_y: NA is Perpendicular to M]
    C1 -- No --> C3[For I_x not equal I_y: NA Tilts Towards Weaker Axis]
    C2 --> C4[Square: theta NA equals 90 minus phi]
    C3 --> C5[Rectangle: NA Slope equals minus I_x M_y over I_y M_x]
    C5 --> C6[Towards axis of lower I value]
    C4 --> C7[Design Implication: NA passes through centroid]
    C6 --> C7
    C7 --> C8[Final NA Orientation]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Note:** The above Mermaid block diagrams are a *functional architecture fallback* for the physical stress-distribution plots (which require a CAD environment). For exam answers, **always draw a labelled stress-distribution sketch** showing the four corner stresses with sign and a clearly marked NA.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define combined bending. Under what conditions does simple bending reduce to combined bending?**

**Model Answer:**

Combined bending (or unsymmetric bending) is a state of flexure in which the **bending moment vector acts in a plane that is not parallel to either of the principal centroidal axes** of the cross-section. As a result, the beam bends **simultaneously about both principal axes** (X-X and Y-Y), producing a biaxial stress state.

**Conditions of reduction:**
- When the plane of loading contains one of the principal axes → $M_y = 0$ or $M_x = 0$ → combined bending reduces to **simple (uniaxial) bending**.
- When the section is doubly symmetric (e.g., square, circle) and load passes through centroid → symmetric bending.
- For unsymmetric sections like channels, angles, and Z-sections, even an axial load in the geometric plane produces combined bending because the **principal axes do not coincide with the geometric axes**.

> **Examiner's Expected Keywords:** *principal axes*, *moment vector*, *biaxial stress*, *superposition*.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**What is the kernel of a cross-section? State its significance in eccentric loading problems.**

**Model Answer:**

The **kernel of a section** (also called the *core* or *central core*) is a small closed region within the cross-section such that **any compressive load applied at a point within this region produces compressive stress throughout the entire section** (no tension anywhere).

**Significance:**
- For **masonry and brick columns**, the material is weak in tension — the load must be applied within the kernel.
- In **robotic gripper jaws**, eccentric gripping forces are designed to stay within the kernel to prevent tensile failure.
- The kernel is bounded by lines connecting points whose eccentricity gives NA coinciding with the section edges.

For a **rectangular section** of width $b$ and depth $d$, the kernel is a rhombus with semi-axes:

$$k_x = \frac{b}{6}, \quad k_y = \frac{d}{6}$$

> **Examiner's Expected Keywords:** *no tension*, *core*, *masonry*, *eccentricity*.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** and has an **internal choice**. Both sub-parts (a) and (b) typically carry **7 marks each**. The choice is between **Question A** and **Question B** — solve the one you know best.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 3 Choice-1]`

**An I-section ISMB 300 has the following properties:**

- Overall depth: $300$ mm; Flange width: $150$ mm
- Flange thickness: $10$ mm; Web thickness: $7.2$ mm
- $I_x = 73.1 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$; $I_y = 4.59 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$
- Section area: $A = 4611 \text{ mm}^2$

**A bending moment of $M = 25$ kN·m is applied in a vertical plane that makes an angle of $20°$ with the Y-axis (vertical).** Determine:

**(a)** The bending stresses at the four extreme corners of the flange.
**(b)** The position of the neutral axis and its inclination with the horizontal.

#### Part (a) — Stress at the Four Corners (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Resolve the Moment**

$$M_x = M \sin 20° = 25 \times 0.342 = 8.55 \text{ kN·m} = 8.55 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

$$M_y = M \cos 20° = 25 \times 0.9397 = 23.49 \text{ kN·m} = 23.49 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Identify Corner Coordinates**

Top flange corners: $(\pm 75, +145)$ mm
Bottom flange corners: $(\pm 75, -145)$ mm

**Step 3 — Compute Stresses**

**Corner A** $(+75, +145)$:

$$\sigma_A = \frac{8.55 \times 10^6 \times 145}{73.1 \times 10^6} + \frac{23.49 \times 10^6 \times 75}{4.59 \times 10^6}$$

$$\sigma_A = 16.96 + 383.86 = 400.82 \text{ MPa (Tension)}$$

**Corner B** $(-75, +145)$:

$$\sigma_B = 16.96 - 383.86 = -366.90 \text{ MPa (Compression)}$$

**Corner C** $(-75, -145)$:

$$\sigma_C = -16.96 - 383.86 = -400.82 \text{ MPa (Compression)}$$

**Corner D** $(+75, -145)$:

$$\sigma_D = -16.96 + 383.86 = 366.90 \text{ MPa (Tension)}$$

**Maximum stress:** $\sigma_{max} = \pm 400.82$ MPa.

#### Part (b) — Neutral Axis (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — NA Equation**

$$\frac{8.55 \times 10^6 \cdot y}{73.1 \times 10^6} + \frac{23.49 \times 10^6 \cdot x}{4.59 \times 10^6} = 0$$

$$0.117\,y + 5.118\,x = 0$$

$$y = -43.74\,x$$

**Step 2 — NA Inclination**

$$\tan\theta_{NA} = -43.74 \implies \theta_{NA} = -88.69° \text{ (with X-axis)}$$

This means the NA is **nearly vertical** (i.e., close to the Y-axis), because the I-section is much weaker in $I_y$ than in $I_x$ (by a factor of $\sim 16$).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A:**
> 1. **Sign errors** in resolving $M_x$ and $M_y$ — students confuse the angle with the X vs Y axis. Read carefully: "20° with the Y-axis" means $M_x = M \sin 20°$ and $M_y = M \cos 20°$.
> 2. **Forgetting units** — convert kN·m to N·mm before substituting into $I$ in mm⁴ to get stress in MPa.
> 3. **Missing corner evaluation** — KTU awards full marks only if all four corners are computed. Marks are split: 1 mark per corner stress, 3 marks for identifying max/min.
> 4. **NA equation sign** — the equation must be set to **zero**; some students set it equal to $\sigma$ and get a curve, not a straight line.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 3 Choice-2]`

**A hollow circular column of outer diameter $D = 200$ mm and inner diameter $d = 150$ mm is subjected to an eccentric compressive load of $P = 500$ kN. The load is applied at a point with eccentricities $e_x = 30$ mm and $e_y = 40$ mm (measured from the centroid).**

**(a)** Determine the maximum and minimum stresses on the cross-section.
**(b)** Find the location of the neutral axis and verify whether the load point lies within the kernel of the section.

**Given:** $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(200^2 - 150^2) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(40000 - 22500) = 13744.7 \text{ mm}^2$

$I_x = I_y = \dfrac{\pi}{64}(D^4 - d^4) = \dfrac{\pi}{64}(200^4 - 150^4) = \dfrac{\pi}{64}(1.6 \times 10^9 - 5.0625 \times 10^8) = 5.366 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4$

#### Part (a) — Stresses at Extreme Fibres (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Eccentric Load Equations**

The stress at any point $(x, y)$ is:

$$\sigma = \frac{-P}{A} + \frac{P \cdot e_y \cdot y}{I_x} + \frac{P \cdot e_x \cdot x}{I_y}$$

**Step 2 — Numerical Substitution**

With $P = 500 \times 10^3$ N:

$$\frac{P}{A} = \frac{500 \times 10^3}{13744.7} = 36.38 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\frac{P \cdot e_y}{I_x} = \frac{500 \times 10^3 \times 40}{5.366 \times 10^7} = 0.3727 \text{ N/mm}^3$$

$$\frac{P \cdot e_x}{I_y} = \frac{500 \times 10^3 \times 30}{5.366 \times 10^7} = 0.2795 \text{ N/mm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Tensile Stress**

For a circular section, $x$ and $y$ are bounded by the **radius** $R = D/2 = 100$ mm. The maximum tension occurs at the point **farthest from the load point in the tensile direction**.

Setting $x = +R = +100$, $y = +R = +100$ (corner pointing away from load in tension direction — but careful: load at $(+30, +40)$, so opposite corner is $(-100, -100)$ gives tension):

$$\sigma_{tension} = -36.38 + 0.3727(-100) + 0.2795(-100)$$

$$\sigma_{tension} = -36.38 - 37.27 - 27.95 = -101.60 \text{ MPa (Compression)}$$

At $(+100, +100)$:

$$\sigma = -36.38 + 37.27 + 27.95 = 28.84 \text{ MPa (Tension)}$$

At $(+100, -100)$:

$$\sigma = -36.38 - 37.27 + 27.95 = -45.70 \text{ MPa (Compression)}$$

At $(-100, +100)$:

$$\sigma = -36.38 + 37.27 - 27.95 = -27.06 \text{ MPa (Compression)}$$

**Step 4 — Result**

- $\sigma_{max, tension} = +28.84$ MPa at $(+100, +100)$
- $\sigma_{max, compression} = -101.60$ MPa at $(-100, -100)$

#### Part (b) — Neutral Axis and Kernel Check (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Neutral Axis**

Setting $\sigma = 0$:

$$-\frac{P}{A} + \frac{P e_y y}{I_x} + \frac{P e_x x}{I_y} = 0$$

$$-\frac{1}{A} + \frac{e_y y}{I_x} + \frac{e_x x}{I_y} = 0$$

Substituting values:

$$-7.276 \times 10^{-5} + (7.456 \times 10^{-7})y + (5.592 \times 10^{-7})x = 0$$

Solving: $7.456\,y + 5.592\,x = 97.55$

**NA Intercepts:**

$$a = \frac{-97.55}{5.592} = -17.45 \text{ mm (X-intercept)}$$

$$b = \frac{-97.55}{7.456} = -13.08 \text{ mm (Y-intercept)}$$

The NA passes through points $(-17.45, 0)$ and $(0, -13.08)$.

**Step 2 — Kernel Radius for Circular Section**

For a solid circular section of diameter $D$:

$$k = \frac{D}{8}$$

For a hollow circular section, the kernel radius is:

$$k = \frac{D}{8} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{d^2}{D^2}\right) = \frac{200}{8} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{150^2}{200^2}\right) = 25 \times 1.5625 = 39.06 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Load Point Verification**

Load eccentricity: $\sqrt{e_x^2 + e_y^2} = \sqrt{30^2 + 40^2} = 50$ mm

Since $50 \text{ mm} > 39.06 \text{ mm}$, the load point **lies OUTSIDE the kernel**, hence **tension develops in the section** (consistent with our $\sigma_{max, tension} = +28.84$ MPa).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:**
> 1. **Eccentric vs combined bending confusion** — eccentric axial loading is a *sub-case* of combined bending with an additional axial term $-P/A$. Forgetting this term gives wrong answers.
> 2. **Sign of axial term** — for **compressive** load, $\sigma_{axial} = -P/A$ (negative).
> 3. **Circular section properties** — students often forget that $I_x = I_y$ for circular sections, which simplifies the analysis.
> 4. **Kernel calculation** — for a hollow circle, students use the solid-circle formula $D/8$ and lose 2 marks. Use the modified formula above.
> 5. **Final comparison** — always state explicitly: *"Load eccentricity $50$ mm $>$ kernel radius $39.06$ mm → load is outside the kernel → tension develops."*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Pin This on Your Wall!**

- ✅ **Master Equation:** $\sigma = \dfrac{M_x y}{I_x} + \dfrac{M_y x}{I_y}$ — always with **consistent units** (N·mm + mm⁴ → MPa).
- ✅ **Neutral Axis** is a **straight line** through the centroid defined by $\dfrac{x}{a} + \dfrac{y}{b} = 1$ (in intercept form) or $\sigma = 0$.
- ✅ **NA is perpendicular to the moment vector** only when $I_x = I_y$ (square or circular sections).
- ✅ For **unsymmetric sections** (channels, angles, Z), NA tilts **towards the weaker principal axis** (smaller $I$).
- ✅ **Maximum stress** is always at a **corner fibre** — evaluate all four and pick the largest magnitude.
- ✅ **Sign Convention:** Tension = (+), Compression = (−). Adopt uniformly and stick to it.
- ✅ **Eccentric loading** = axial load + combined bending. The axial term $\dfrac{-P}{A}$ must be **added** to the bending stresses.
- ✅ **Kernel of Section** = region where eccentric load produces **no tension**. For a rectangle: $k_x = b/6, \, k_y = d/6$. For a solid circle: $k = D/8$. For a hollow circle: $k = \dfrac{D}{8}\left(1 + \dfrac{d^2}{D^2}\right)$.
- ✅ **Common Exam Trap:** Students forget that for **angled channels and Z-sections**, the principal axes are **inclined** to the geometric X-Y axes — the question must first direct you to rotate axes to principal directions.
- ✅ **Robotic Application:** Cantilever gripper arms, L-bracket supports, end-effector mounts — all routinely experience combined bending. Always reduce to a free-body diagram at the critical cross-section.
- ✅ **Unit Conversion Cheat:** $1 \text{ kN·m} = 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$ → use this to avoid $10^{-3}$ errors.
- ✅ **Sanity Check:** For a square or circular section under any angled moment, the maximum stress is $\dfrac{M}{Z}$ (same as simple bending) — this is a useful self-verification.

> **End of Module 3 — Combined Bending Notes | PERAT525 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torsion and End Thrust
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT525 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS OF ROBOTIC PARTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Torsion and End Thrust

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Torsion – Formal Definition

**Torsion** is the twisting of a structural member about its longitudinal axis when subjected to a **torque** (or twisting moment). The torque generates **shearing stresses** on cross-sections perpendicular to the axis, while the longitudinal fibres remain free of normal stress (pure torsion assumption). For a robotic manipulator arm, propeller shaft, or a drill bit, the torque transmitted is resisted entirely by torsional shear.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Rigid Body Mechanics, Module 3):**
> Torsion is the state of stress produced in a prismatic body by the action of a couple whose axis coincides with the longitudinal centroidal axis of the body. The internal resisting moment on any cross-section is the **Torsional Moment or Torque (T)**.

### 1.2 End Thrust – Formal Definition

**End Thrust** is the longitudinal (axial) compressive or tensile force that develops inside a cylindrical body — most commonly in **thin pressure vessels, pipe ends, and riveted/bolted joints** — due to internal fluid pressure, or in **helical springs** under axial load, where the resultant force is resolved into components producing twist + shear + direct stress.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End Thrust (Thrust on End of a Pressure Vessel):**
> When a thin cylindrical shell is subjected to an internal pressure $p$, the fluid pushes against the end caps with an axial tensile force $P = p \cdot A$ where $A$ is the internal cross-sectional area. This longitudinal pull is called **End Thrust** and produces **longitudinal (axial) stress** in the shell.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

| Real-World Analogy | Engineering Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Twisting a wet towel to squeeze out water | **Torsion** of a circular shaft |
| The reaction force a pressure cooker lid pushes back with | **End Thrust** due to internal pressure |
| A robot arm's shoulder joint rotating the forearm | **Torsion in the drive shaft** |
| Hydraulic cylinder plunger pushing the cap of a pipe | **End Thrust on the closure head** |
| A coiled door-spring compressed by hand | **Combined torsion + shear in helical spring** |

**Plain English Intuition:** Think of a robotic drill bit. As the motor drives the bit, a torque rotates it. The bit resists this torque through shear stresses spiralling around its surface — that's **torsion**. Now imagine the same drill bit is hollow and oil is pumped through it at high pressure. The oil also tries to push the back-end cap off — that longitudinal pulling is **end thrust**. In a real robot, shafts, springs, and pressure pipes often carry **both** at the same time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key physical constants & standard metrics used:**
> - Shear Modulus (Modulus of Rigidity) for steel: $G = 80 \text{ GPa} = 80 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$
> - Poisson's ratio for steel: $\nu = 0.30$
> - Power relation: $P = 2\pi N T / 60$ (in Watts, with $N$ in rpm and $T$ in N·m)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Torsion of a Circular Shaft — Underlying Theory

**Assumptions (Saint-Venant's Theory of Pure Torsion):**
1. The material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's law.
2. Cross-sections that are plane and perpendicular to the axis before loading **remain plane and undistorted** after loading (warping-free assumption — valid only for circular sections).
3. Radial lines on the cross-section remain radial and straight — they only **rotate** by an angle $\theta$ about the axis.
4. Distances from the axis are unchanged (each circle rotates as a rigid disc).

**Steps in developing the torsion formula:**

- A torque $T$ applied at one end produces a **shear stress $\tau$** on the cross-section at a radial distance $r$.
- Strain at radius $r$: $\gamma = r \cdot \dfrac{\theta}{L}$ where $L$ is the length and $\theta$ is the angle of twist in radians.
- Hooke's law in shear: $\tau = G \cdot \gamma = G \cdot r \cdot \dfrac{\theta}{L}$.
- Maximum stress occurs at the **outer fibre** $r = R$: $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{G R \theta}{L}$.
- Integrating the elemental moment $dT = \tau \cdot dA \cdot r$ over the area gives the total torque:
$$T = \int_A \tau \cdot r \cdot dA = \frac{G \theta}{L} \int_A r^2 \, dA = \frac{G \theta \cdot J}{L}$$
- The term $\int_A r^2 \, dA$ is the **polar moment of inertia $J$**.

### 2.2 Torsion Formula (Master Equation)

$$\boxed{\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G \theta}{L}}$$

- $T$ = Applied torque (N·m)
- $J$ = Polar moment of inertia (m⁴)
- $\tau$ = Shear stress at radius $r$ (Pa)
- $G$ = Shear modulus (Pa)
- $\theta$ = Angle of twist (radians)
- $L$ = Length of shaft (m)

### 2.3 Polar Moment of Inertia

**Solid circular shaft (diameter $D$):**
$$J = \frac{\pi D^4}{32}$$

**Hollow circular shaft (outer diameter $D$, inner diameter $d$):**
$$J = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}$$

### 2.4 End Thrust in Thin Cylinders

When internal pressure $p$ acts on the end cap of a closed thin cylinder, the **longitudinal stress** (hoop force divided by resisting area):

$$\sigma_l = \frac{p \cdot d}{4 t}$$

where $d$ is internal diameter and $t$ is wall thickness. The **end thrust force** is:

$$F_{end} = p \cdot A_{cap} = \frac{\pi p d^2}{4}$$

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ | Torsion master equation | Mixed SI | All shaft problems |
| 2 | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi D^3}$ | Max shear in solid shaft | Pa | Design of shafts |
| 3 | $\theta = \dfrac{T L}{G J}$ | Angle of twist | rad | Deflection of shafts |
| 4 | $\theta^\circ = \dfrac{T L \cdot 180}{G J \pi}$ | Angle of twist in degrees | ° | KTU prefers this |
| 5 | $J = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{32}$ | Solid shaft polar MOI | m⁴ | Solid shafts |
| 6 | $J = \dfrac{\pi (D^4-d^4)}{32}$ | Hollow shaft polar MOI | m⁴ | Lightweight shafts (robot arms) |
| 7 | $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | Power transmitted | W | Motor + shaft problems |
| 8 | $\sigma_l = \dfrac{p d}{4 t}$ | Longitudinal stress in thin cylinder | Pa | End thrust problems |
| 9 | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{p d}{2 t}$ | Hoop (circumferential) stress | Pa | Pressure vessel (also KTU syllabus) |
| 10 | $F_{end} = \dfrac{\pi p d^2}{4}$ | End thrust force on cap | N | Force on end cap |
| 11 | $\tau_{spring} = \dfrac{8 F D}{\pi d^3}$ | Max shear in helical spring | Pa | Spring design |
| 12 | $\delta = \dfrac{8 F D^3 n}{G d^4}$ | Deflection of helical spring | m | Spring end-thrust problems |
| 13 | $T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T_t^2}$ | Equivalent twisting moment | N·m | Combined loading (shaft) |
| 14 | $M_e = \dfrac{M + \sqrt{M^2 + T_t^2}}{2}$ | Equivalent bending moment | N·m | Combined loading (shaft) |
| 15 | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16 T_e}{\pi d^3}$ | Max shear with combined loading | Pa | Final design equation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In Questions where **combined bending and torsion** appear, examiners always use the **equivalent torque method** (Ranks, Guest, or Soderberg). The most common in KTU papers is the **Guest's criterion**:
> $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{M^2 + T_t^2}$

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotics:** Drive shafts in articulated arms transmit torque while subjected to bending from arm weight — both must be checked simultaneously.
- **Aerospace:** Propeller shafts carry torsion + end thrust from propeller reaction.
- **Pressure vessels:** End caps, flange bolts, and pipe closures must be designed for the end-thrust force.
- **Helical springs:** Used in robot joint compliance — carry both twist and direct shear.
- **Power transmission:** Every rotating shaft from a motor to a gearbox is a torsion problem.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Torsion Formula from Saint-Venant's Assumptions

**Given:** A solid circular shaft of diameter $D$ and length $L$, fixed at one end, with torque $T$ applied at the other.

**Step 1:** Consider a small element at radius $r$ on the cross-section. The element undergoes a shear strain $\gamma$ due to rotation of the section by angle $\theta$.

The arc length traversed on the outer surface of radius $r$ is $r\theta$ over a length $L$. So:

$$\gamma = \frac{r \theta}{L}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Hooke's law in shear, $\tau = G \gamma$:

$$\tau = G \cdot \frac{r \theta}{L}$$

**Step 3:** The shear stress on an elemental ring of area $dA = 2\pi r \, dr$ produces a moment about the axis:

$$dT = \tau \cdot dA \cdot r = \left( \frac{G \theta r}{L} \right) (2 \pi r \, dr) \cdot r = \frac{2 \pi G \theta}{L} r^3 \, dr$$

**Step 4:** Integrate from $r = 0$ to $r = R$ to obtain the total resisting torque:

$$T = \int_0^R \frac{2 \pi G \theta}{L} r^3 \, dr = \frac{2 \pi G \theta}{L} \left[ \frac{r^4}{4} \right]_0^R = \frac{2 \pi G \theta}{L} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4}$$

**Step 5:** Rearranging, the polar moment of inertia $J = \dfrac{\pi R^4}{2} = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{32}$ appears naturally:

$$T = \frac{G \theta J}{L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{T}{J} = \frac{G \theta}{L}$$

**Step 6:** Combining with $\tau = G r \theta / L$ gives the master equation:

$$\boxed{\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G \theta}{L}}$$

**Step 7:** Solving for maximum shear stress at $r = D/2$:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{16 T}{\pi D^3} \quad \text{(Solid shaft)}$$

**Step 8:** For a hollow shaft (outer $D$, inner $d$):

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{16 T D}{\pi (D^4 - d^4)} \quad \text{(Hollow shaft)}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power Transmitted by a Rotating Shaft

**Given:** A shaft rotating at $N$ revolutions per minute, transmitting a torque $T$.

**Step 1:** The work done by the torque in one complete revolution:

$$W = T \cdot 2\pi \quad \text{(Joules per revolution)}$$

**Step 2:** Number of revolutions per second:

$$f = \frac{N}{60} \quad \text{(rev/s)}$$

**Step 3:** Power = Work per second:

$$P = W \cdot f = T \cdot 2\pi \cdot \frac{N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} \text{ Watts}$$

**Step 4:** Solving for $T$ in terms of Power (a common KTU requirement):

$$T = \frac{60 P}{2 \pi N} = \frac{9.55 P}{N} \quad \text{with } P \text{ in W, } N \text{ in rpm, } T \text{ in N·m}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — End Thrust in a Thin Cylindrical Pressure Vessel

**Given:** A thin cylindrical shell of internal diameter $d$, wall thickness $t$, closed at both ends, subjected to internal pressure $p$.

**Step 1:** Consider the equilibrium of the end cap. The pressure acts over the entire circular cross-section of the cylinder.

The **total end thrust force** pushing the cap outward:

$$F_{end} = p \cdot A = p \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

**Step 2:** This force is resisted by the longitudinal (axial) stress $\sigma_l$ acting on the cylindrical wall. The resisting area is the two semicircular cuts through the wall — each of area $d \cdot t$ approximately (thin-wall assumption), giving a total longitudinal area $2 d t$ per unit length... **for the full shell cross-section** the resisting area is $\pi d t$ (mean circumference times thickness, per cap).

**Step 3:** Equilibrium of the cap:

$$F_{end} = \sigma_l \cdot (\pi d t) \cdot 1 \quad \text{(per unit length basis)} $$

Equivalently, integrating over the cap:

$$\sigma_l = \frac{p \cdot \pi d^2 / 4}{\pi d t} = \frac{p d}{4 t}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\sigma_l = \frac{p d}{4 t}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** This longitudinal stress is **half** the hoop (circumferential) stress $\sigma_h = \dfrac{p d}{2 t}$. Hence the **end thrust problem** is the *weakest* stress direction in a pressure vessel — that is why longitudinal seams in boilers must be **double-riveted** while circumferential seams can be single-riveted.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Torsion vs. End Thrust vs. Combined

| Aspect | Pure Torsion | Pure End Thrust | Torsion + End Thrust (Shaft of Pressure Vessel) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Loading** | Twisting couple $T$ | Axial force $F$ from pressure | Both $T$ and $F$ simultaneously |
| **Stress produced** | Shear $\tau$ on cross-section | Normal tensile $\sigma_l$ | Shear + Normal (principal stresses exist) |
| **Governing equation** | $\tau = \dfrac{T r}{J}$ | $\sigma_l = \dfrac{p d}{4 t}$ | $\sigma_1, \sigma_2$ from Mohr's circle |
| **Design criterion** | $\tau_{max} \leq \tau_{allow}$ | $\sigma_l \leq \sigma_{allow}$ | $\sigma_1 \leq \sigma_{allow}$ (max principal) |
| **Failure mode** | Shear yield | Tensile rupture | Combined yield |
| **Robotic part example** | Drill bit of a robot | End cap of a pneumatic gripper | Drive shaft of a hydraulic press |

### 3.5 Numerical Demonstration — KTU Style Worked Example

**Problem:** A solid steel shaft of diameter 50 mm transmits 25 kW at 200 rpm. The shear modulus is $80 \text{ GPa}$. Find:
(a) The torque transmitted
(b) The maximum shear stress
(c) The angle of twist over a 1.5 m length

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Using $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$:

$$T = \frac{60 P}{2 \pi N} = \frac{60 \times 25000}{2 \pi \times 200} = \frac{1500000}{1256.64} = 1193.66 \text{ N·m}$$

**Part (b):** For solid shaft, $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi D^3}$:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{16 \times 1193.66}{\pi \times (0.05)^3} = \frac{19098.56}{3.927 \times 10^{-4}} = 48.63 \text{ MPa}$$

**Part (c):** Polar moment of inertia, $J = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{32}$:

$$J = \frac{\pi (0.05)^4}{32} = \frac{3.927 \times 10^{-7}}{32} = 6.136 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^4$$

Angle of twist:

$$\theta = \frac{T L}{G J} = \frac{1193.66 \times 1.5}{80 \times 10^9 \times 6.136 \times 10^{-8}} = \frac{1790.49}{4908.74} = 0.3648 \text{ rad}$$

Converting to degrees:

$$\theta^\circ = 0.3648 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 20.9°$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Torsion of a Circular Shaft — Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applied Torque T at Free End] --> B[Internal Shear Stress on Cross-Section]
    B --> C[Linear Distribution: max at r = D/2, zero at centre]
    C --> D[T/J = tau/r = G theta / L]
    D --> E1[Max Shear Stress tau_max = 16T / pi D cubed]
    D --> E2[Angle of Twist theta = T L / G J]
    D --> E3[Polar Moment of Inertia J = pi D4 / 32]
    E1 --> F[Check tau_max <= tau_allow]
    E2 --> G[Check theta <= theta_allow for serviceability]
    E3 --> H[Shaft Geometry Output]
    F --> I[Design Safe or Redesign]
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

### 4.2 End Thrust Flow — Pressure Vessel Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Internal Pressure p] --> B[Force on End Cap: F = p times pi d squared / 4]
    B --> C[End Thrust Force F_end]
    C --> D[Resisted by Wall Area A_w = pi d t]
    D --> E[Longitudinal Stress sigma_l = p d / 4 t]
    E --> F[Compare with Hoop Stress sigma_h = p d / 2 t]
    F --> G[Note: sigma_l is half of sigma_h]
    G --> H[Design longitudinal seam stronger than circumferential seam]
```

### 4.3 Combined Torsion and End Thrust — Stress Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applied Torque T_t] --> B[Shear Stress tau = 16 T_t / pi D cubed]
    C[End Thrust F_end] --> D[Axial Stress sigma_l = F_end / A_wall]
    B --> E[Element on Shaft Surface]
    D --> E
    E --> F[2D Stress Element with sigma and tau]
    F --> G[Compute Principal Stresses]
    G --> H[sigma_1, sigma_2 from Mohrs Circle]
    H --> I[Maximum Shear Stress Theory Check]
    H --> J[Maximum Principal Stress Theory Check]
    I --> K[Final Design Verdict]
    J --> K
```

### 4.4 Schematic of Stress Distribution on Cross-Section

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Solid Circular Cross-Section] --> B[Centre r=0: tau = 0]
    A --> C[Mid Radius r=D/4: tau is half max]
    A --> D[Outer Fibre r=D/2: tau = tau_max]
    D --> E[Tau max = 16T / pi D cubed]
    B --> F[Linear Variation of tau with r]
    C --> F
    D --> F
```

### 4.5 Helical Spring Under End Thrust

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Axial Force F on Spring] --> B[Resolve into Components]
    B --> C[Torque on Wire: T_spring = F times D / 2]
    B --> D[Direct Shear: V = F]
    C --> E[Torsional Shear Stress tau_t]
    D --> F[Direct Shear Stress tau_v]
    E --> G[Max Shear = tau_t plus tau_v]
    F --> G
    G --> H[tau_max = 8F D / pi d cubed plus 4F / pi d squared]
    H --> I[Final Spring Shear Check]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the torsion equation. Explain each term involved.**

**Model Answer:**

The torsion equation for a circular shaft is:

$$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G \theta}{L}$$

Where:
- $T$ = Torque transmitted by the shaft (N·m)
- $J$ = Polar moment of inertia of the cross-section (m⁴)
- $\tau$ = Shear stress at a radius $r$ (N/m²)
- $r$ = Radial distance from the centre (m)
- $G$ = Modulus of rigidity of the material (N/m²)
- $\theta$ = Angle of twist in radians
- $L$ = Length of the shaft (m)

This equation is valid for **circular cross-sections** (solid or hollow) under the Saint-Venant assumptions of plane sections remaining plane.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define end thrust. Derive an expression for the longitudinal stress developed in a thin cylindrical shell due to an internal pressure.**

**Model Answer:**

**End Thrust:** The longitudinal tensile force acting on the end closure of a thin pressure vessel due to internal fluid pressure.

**Derivation:** Consider a thin cylindrical shell of internal diameter $d$ and wall thickness $t$, closed at both ends, subjected to internal pressure $p$.

The force on the end cap:

$$F = p \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

This is resisted by the longitudinal stress $\sigma_l$ acting on the cylindrical wall. The resisting cross-sectional area is $\pi d t$.

Equilibrium gives:

$$\sigma_l \cdot \pi d t = p \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_l = \frac{p d}{4 t}}$$

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

A solid circular shaft of diameter **60 mm** transmits a torque of **1.2 kN·m**. The shear modulus of the material is **82 GPa**.

Find:
(i) The maximum shear stress in the shaft.
(ii) The angle of twist per metre length of the shaft.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $D = 0.060$ m, $T = 1200$ N·m, $G = 82 \times 10^9$ N/m².

**Step 1 — Polar moment of inertia:**

$$J = \frac{\pi D^4}{32} = \frac{\pi (0.060)^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 1.296 \times 10^{-5}}{32} = 1.272 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 2 — Maximum shear stress (at outer surface, $r = D/2 = 0.030$ m):**

Using the torsion equation $\tau = \dfrac{T r}{J}$:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{1200 \times 0.030}{1.272 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{36}{1.272 \times 10^{-6}} = 28.30 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Angle of twist per metre length:**

Using $\theta = \dfrac{T L}{G J}$ with $L = 1$ m:

$$\theta = \frac{1200 \times 1}{82 \times 10^9 \times 1.272 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1200}{104304} = 0.01151 \text{ rad/m}$$

In degrees:

$$\theta^\circ = 0.01151 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 0.659°/\text{m}$$

---

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

A thin cylindrical shell of internal diameter **300 mm** and wall thickness **10 mm** is filled with a fluid under a pressure of **3 MPa**. Find:
(i) The longitudinal stress (end thrust stress) in the shell.
(ii) The hoop (circumferential) stress in the shell.
(iii) Comment on the relative magnitude of these stresses and its design implication.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $d = 0.300$ m, $t = 0.010$ m, $p = 3 \times 10^6$ Pa.

**Step 1 — Longitudinal (end thrust) stress:**

$$\sigma_l = \frac{p d}{4 t} = \frac{3 \times 10^6 \times 0.300}{4 \times 0.010} = \frac{9 \times 10^5}{0.040} = 22.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Hoop (circumferential) stress:**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{p d}{2 t} = \frac{3 \times 10^6 \times 0.300}{2 \times 0.010} = \frac{9 \times 10^5}{0.020} = 45.0 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Comparison and design implication:**

$$\sigma_h = 2 \sigma_l \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_h \text{ is twice the longitudinal stress.}$$

**Design implication:** Since the hoop stress is **twice** the longitudinal stress, the **longitudinal seam** of a riveted pressure vessel must be made **stronger** (e.g., double-riveted) than the circumferential seam, because the longitudinal seam is what resists the hoop stress. The circumferential seam only resists $\sigma_l$ and may be single-riveted.

---

### 5.3 ALTERNATIVE CHOICE — Question 4

---

**Q4(a). [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]**

A hollow steel shaft of outer diameter **80 mm** and inner diameter **50 mm** is required to transmit **75 kW** at **240 rpm**. The allowable shear stress is **60 MPa** and the shear modulus is **80 GPa**. Find:
(i) The torque transmitted.
(ii) The maximum shear stress induced.
(iii) The angle of twist in degrees over a length of 3 m.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $D = 0.080$ m, $d = 0.050$ m, $P = 75{,}000$ W, $N = 240$ rpm, $\tau_{allow} = 60 \times 10^6$ Pa, $G = 80 \times 10^9$ Pa, $L = 3$ m.

**Step 1 — Torque transmitted:**

$$T = \frac{60 P}{2 \pi N} = \frac{60 \times 75000}{2 \pi \times 240} = \frac{4500000}{1507.96} = 2984.3 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 2 — Polar moment of inertia (hollow):**

$$J = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32} = \frac{\pi (0.080^4 - 0.050^4)}{32}$$

$$J = \frac{\pi (4.096 \times 10^{-5} - 6.25 \times 10^{-6})}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 3.471 \times 10^{-5}}{32} = 3.408 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 3 — Maximum shear stress:**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{16 T D}{\pi (D^4 - d^4)} = \frac{16 \times 2984.3 \times 0.080}{\pi \times 3.471 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{3819.9}{1.0903 \times 10^{-4}} = 35.03 \text{ MPa}$$

Since $35.03 < 60$ MPa, the shaft is **safe**.

**Step 4 — Angle of twist:**

$$\theta = \frac{T L}{G J} = \frac{2984.3 \times 3}{80 \times 10^9 \times 3.408 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{8952.9}{272640} = 0.03284 \text{ rad}$$

In degrees:

$$\theta^\circ = 0.03284 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 1.882°$$

---

**Q4(b). [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]**

A shaft is subjected to a **bending moment of 2.5 kN·m** and a **torque of 4 kN·m** simultaneously. Using the **equivalent torque method (Guest's criterion)**, determine the diameter of the shaft. Take allowable shear stress as **50 MPa**.

**Model Solution:**
