**Given:** $M = 2500$ N·m, $T_t = 4000$ N·m, $\tau_{allow} = 50 \times 10^6$ Pa.

**Step 1 — Compute the equivalent twisting moment (Guest's criterion):**

$$T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T_t^2} = \sqrt{2500^2 + 4000^2} = \sqrt{6250000 + 16000000} = \sqrt{22250000}$$

$$T_e = 4717.0 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 2 — Apply torsion formula for solid shaft:**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{16 T_e}{\pi D^3} \leq \tau_{allow}$$

Solving for $D$:

$$D^3 \geq \frac{16 T_e}{\pi \tau_{allow}} = \frac{16 \times 4717.0}{\pi \times 50 \times 10^6}$$

$$D^3 \geq \frac{75472}{1.5708 \times 10^8} = 4.805 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

$$D \geq (4.805 \times 10^{-4})^{1/3} = 0.0782 \text{ m} = 78.2 \text{ mm}$$

**Adopt $D = 80$ mm** as the next standard size.

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Board Pattern):**
> 
> 1. **Mixing up polar and area moments of inertia:** Students often confuse $J = \pi D^4 / 32$ (polar) with $I = \pi D^4 / 64$ (area moment of inertia about a diameter). These differ by a factor of **2** — losing 2–3 marks for this alone.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the $\dfrac{180}{\pi}$ conversion** when answering "angle of twist in degrees." The master formula gives $\theta$ in **radians**. KTU boards deduct 1 mark if the final answer is left in radians when degrees are asked.
> 
> 3. **Hollow shaft $J$ formula forgotten:** For hollow shafts, the formula is $J = \pi(D^4 - d^4) / 32$, **not** $\pi(D - d)^4 / 32$. A 4-mark error.
> 
> 4. **End thrust vs hoop stress mix-up:** The end-thrust formula has a denominator of $4t$ (giving $\sigma_l$), while hoop stress has $2t$. Many students write both with the same denominator. This is a guaranteed **2-mark** loss.
> 
> 5. **Power-Torque unit conversion errors:** $P = 2\pi N T / 60$ requires $P$ in **Watts**, $N$ in **rpm**, $T$ in **N·m**. Mixing kW with N·mm or rpm with rad/s is a common slip.
> 
> 6. **Guest vs Rankine criterion confusion:** For combined loading, **Guest's criterion** uses $T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T_t^2}$ for *ductile* materials (steel). For *brittle* materials, **Rankine's** $M_e$ is used. KTU mostly tests Guest for steel shafts.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Torsion equation** $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ is the single most important formula for Module 3.
- **Maximum shear stress in a solid shaft:** $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16T}{\pi D^3}$.
- **Maximum shear stress in a hollow shaft:** $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16TD}{\pi(D^4 - d^4)}$.
- **Polar moment of inertia** is always about the **centroidal axis** — for circular sections, $J = 2I$ where $I$ is the area moment about a diameter.
- **End thrust force** on the cap of a pressure vessel = $p \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$; **end-thrust stress** in the wall = $\dfrac{pd}{4t}$.
- **Hoop stress is always twice the longitudinal (end-thrust) stress** for a thin cylinder: $\sigma_h = 2 \sigma_l$.
- **Angle of twist** in radians is $\theta = \dfrac{TL}{GJ}$; multiply by $\dfrac{180}{\pi}$ for degrees.
- **Power-Torque-Speed** relationship: $P = \dfrac{2\pi NT}{60}$ (W, N·m, rpm) — circular saw blades, drill bits, and robot drive shafts.
- **For combined bending + torsion**, use **Guest's criterion** with equivalent torque $T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T_t^2}$ for ductile materials.
- **Helical springs** under axial load carry **torsion + direct shear** in the wire; the dominant term is torsion $\tau_t = \dfrac{8FD}{\pi d^3}$.
- **Assumptions of pure torsion** (Saint-Venant): plane sections remain plane, radial lines remain radial — valid only for **circular** cross-sections.
- **Thin-cylinder assumption** is that $d/t \geq 20$; otherwise the formulas need correction factors.
- For robotic parts, **hollow shafts** are preferred over solid ones because they offer higher $J/$mass$ ratio.
- **Always recheck units** — KTU boards reward unit consistency and penalise dimensional errors heavily.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Theory of Simple Bending
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT525 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS OF ROBOTIC PARTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Theory of Simple Bending

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theory of Simple Bending** is a fundamental principle in *Strength of Materials / Mechanics of Solids* that establishes the relationship between the **bending moment** acting on a structural element (beam) and the resulting **bending stress** distributed across its cross-section, assuming the material behaves within its **elastic limit** and obeys **Hooke's Law**.

The classical bending equation, often called the **Flexure Formula** or **Euler-Bernoulli Beam Equation**, is universally expressed as:

$$
\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}
$$

where:
- $M$ = Bending Moment at the section (N·m)
- $I$ = Second Moment of Area / Moment of Inertia of the cross-section (m⁴ or mm⁴)
- $\sigma$ = Bending Stress at a distance $y$ from the Neutral Axis (N/m² or Pa)
- $y$ = Perpendicular distance from the Neutral Axis (m or mm)
- $E$ = Young's Modulus of Elasticity of the material (N/m² or Pa)
- $R$ = Radius of Curvature of the bent beam at that section (m or mm)

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

**Plain English Explanation:**

Imagine you are holding a **flat steel ruler** flat on a table with one end pinned down, and you press down firmly on the free end. The ruler bends into a gentle curve. The top surface of the ruler **stretches** (experiences *tensile stress*) and the bottom surface **compresses** (experiences *compressive stress*). Somewhere in the middle, there is a layer that does **neither** — neither stretches nor compresses. This imaginary layer is called the **Neutral Axis**, and it is the cornerstone of the theory of simple bending.

In **robotic structural parts**, this concept is critical. For example:
- A **robotic arm link** lifting a payload behaves exactly like our ruler — it bends under load.
- The **end-effector mounting flange** of a robot manipulator experiences bending when gripping an object.
- A **gripper finger** holding a workpiece undergoes simple bending during grasping.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Robotic Applications:** When designing a robot arm link, the engineer must ensure that the maximum bending stress ($\sigma_{max} = M \cdot c / I$, where $c$ is the distance to the extreme fibre) never exceeds the **Allowable Stress** of the material — otherwise the arm will permanently deform or fracture during operation.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Young's Modulus ($E$):**
  - Structural Steel: $E = 2 \times 10^{5}$ **N/mm²** (or **200 GPa**)
  - Aluminium Alloy: $E = 0.7 \times 10^{5}$ **N/mm²** (or **70 GPa**)
  - Carbon Fibre Reinforced Polymer (CFRP): $E = 1.4 \times 10^{5}$ **N/mm²** (or **140 GPa**)
- **Standard Section Modulus ($Z$):** Units — mm³ or m³
- **Radius of Curvature ($R$):** Units — mm or m

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students are expected to derive the bending equation, identify the assumptions governing the theory, and apply it to standard cross-sections (rectangular, circular, I-section, T-section) commonly used in robotic structural frames.

---

### 1.4 Pure Bending vs. Simple Bending

| Feature | Pure Bending | Simple Bending |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Bending moment is **constant** along the length; shear force is **zero** | Bending moment **varies** along the length; shear force is **non-zero** |
| **Stress Condition** | Only bending (normal) stress exists | Bending stress + Shear stress both exist |
| **Example** | Central portion of a beam under two equal point loads | Cantilever beam with a point load at the free end |
| **Validity of Bending Equation** | **Exactly valid** | **Approximately valid** (shear deformation neglected) |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU examinations, the *Theory of Simple Bending* refers to the general case where the bending equation is applied — the derivation itself assumes pure bending for mathematical simplicity, but the equation is extended to simple bending as a first-order engineering approximation.

---

### 1.5 Neutral Axis & Neutral Layer — Conceptual Foundation

- **Neutral Axis:** The line of intersection of the neutral layer with the cross-section.
- **Neutral Layer (or Surface):** The longitudinal plane in the beam where fibres experience **zero longitudinal strain** and therefore **zero bending stress**.
- For a **symmetric cross-section**, the neutral axis passes through the **centroid** of the cross-section.
- For an **unsymmetric cross-section** (e.g., a T-beam or a robot L-bracket), the neutral axis still passes through the centroid — but the distances to the extreme fibres ($c_{top}$ and $c_{bottom}$) will be different.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** In a KTU exam, if asked "Where does the neutral axis pass?" — the correct answer is **always "through the centroid of the cross-section"**, NOT through the geometric centre for unsymmetric sections. This is a frequent mark-deduction point.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Fundamental Assumptions of the Theory of Simple Bending

The bending equation is derived under the following **idealized assumptions**:

1. **Homogeneous Material:** The beam material has uniform composition and identical elastic properties at every point (no voids, no inclusions).
2. **Isotropic Material:** Elastic properties are the same in all directions at any given point.
3. **Linear Elastic Behaviour:** The material obeys **Hooke's Law** up to the proportional limit — the stress-strain relationship is perfectly linear.
4. **Plane Sections Remain Plane:** Cross-sections that were originally plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis *remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis* after bending (Euler-Bernoulli hypothesis). This implies that **shear deformation is neglected**.
5. **No Initial Curvature:** The beam is **initially straight** before loading.
6. **Pure Bending Condition:** The bending moment is constant (or, for simple bending, the shear force contribution to deformation is negligibly small).
7. **No Lateral Buckling / Torsion:** The beam does not twist or buckle sideways; only pure flexural deformation occurs.
8. **Small Deflections:** The deflection of the beam is very small compared to its length, so that the geometry of the deformed beam can be approximated using the original (undeformed) configuration.
9. **Constant Cross-Section:** The cross-sectional geometry does not vary along the length of the beam (prismatic beam). *Note:* For non-prismatic beams, the theory is applied locally at each section.
10. **Self-weight Neglected** (in standard textbook derivations).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Question like *"State the assumptions of the theory of simple bending"* is a **favourite 5-mark question** in KTU Board Exams. Memorize all 10 points — they are asked verbatim almost every cycle.

---

### 2.2 Why These Assumptions Matter — The "How" Behind Each Step

- **Assumption 1-2 (Homogeneous + Isotropic):** Allows us to define a single value of $E$ for the entire beam. For anisotropic materials like fibre-reinforced composites, the bending equation needs modification (use of *bending modulus*).
- **Assumption 4 (Plane Sections):** This is the **most critical assumption**. It allows the strain $\epsilon$ at a distance $y$ from the neutral axis to be expressed purely as a function of the curvature: $\epsilon = y / R$. Without this, the bending equation would have a more complex form.
- **Assumption 6 (Pure Bending):** When shear force exists, additional shear stresses develop, causing cross-sections to *warp* (no longer remain plane). The simple bending equation neglects this warping.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Mastery Tip:** The following table contains the entire formula set needed for any KTU numerical problem on simple bending. Memorize this — derivation steps are tested separately, but application problems require these directly.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ | **Bending Equation** (Universal Flexure Formula) | Dimensionless ratios |
| 2 | $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Bending stress at distance $y$ from NA | N/m² (Pa) |
| 3 | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Maximum bending stress ($Z = I / c$) | N/m² (Pa) |
| 4 | $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$ | Section Modulus | m³ or mm³ |
| 5 | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ | Curvature of the bent beam | 1/m or 1/mm |
| 6 | $M = EI \cdot \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2}$ | Moment-Curvature relationship (for beams) | N·m |
| 7 | $\sigma = \dfrac{E \cdot y}{R}$ | Pure flexure stress (bending stress) | N/m² (Pa) |
| 8 | $\epsilon = \dfrac{y}{R}$ | Strain at distance $y$ from NA | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $I_{rectangle} = \dfrac{b d^3}{12}$ | Moment of Inertia (rectangular section, $b$ = width, $d$ = depth) | m⁴ or mm⁴ |
| 10 | $I_{circle} = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{64}$ | Moment of Inertia (solid circular section, $D$ = diameter) | m⁴ or mm⁴ |
| 11 | $I_{hollow\,circle} = \dfrac{\pi \left( D^4 - d^4 \right)}{64}$ | Moment of Inertia (hollow circular section) | m⁴ or mm⁴ |
| 12 | $I_{I\text{-}section} = \dfrac{B D^3}{12} - \dfrac{b d^3}{12}$ | Moment of Inertia (I-section, flange $B \times t_f$, web $b \times d$) | m⁴ or mm⁴ |
| 13 | $I_{T\text{-}section}$ | $\dfrac{B D^3}{12} - \dfrac{(B-b)(D-t_f)^3}{12} + B D \left( y_c - \dfrac{D}{2} \right)^2 - (B-b)(D-t_f) \left( y_c - \dfrac{D - t_f}{2} \right)^2$ (with NA at centroid) | m⁴ or mm⁴ |
| 14 | $\theta = \dfrac{M L}{EI}$ | Slope at free end of cantilever (length $L$, point load at tip) | Radians |
| 15 | $\delta = \dfrac{W L^3}{3EI}$ | Deflection at free end of cantilever (point load $W$ at tip) | m or mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Table Convention:** Where absolute value signs appear in derivations (e.g., $|y|$), they have been written as $y$ for brevity — physically, distance is always non-negative. KTU does not test this distinction.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Bending Theory |
|---|---|
| **Robotics** | Design of manipulator links, gripper arms, end-effector flanges |
| **Civil Engineering** | Design of building beams, bridge girders, slabs |
| **Mechanical Engineering** | Crankshafts, leaf springs, machine tool beds |
| **Aerospace** | Wing spars of aircraft, helicopter rotor blades |
| **Biomedical** | Orthopaedic bone plates, prosthetic limb sockets |
| **Automotive** | Chassis frame rails, suspension leaf springs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For PERAT525 (Structural Analysis of Robotic Parts):** When a robot manipulator is under load, every link experiences bending. The **first link** (closest to the base) experiences the maximum bending moment because it must support the weight of all the downstream links plus the payload. This is why robot links are often **tapered** (larger cross-section near the base, smaller near the end-effector) — to optimize material usage and resist bending efficiently.

---

### 2.5 Section Modulus for Common Robotic Cross-Sections

For a robotic arm link of standard **rectangular cross-section** ($b \times d$):

$$
Z = \frac{I}{c} = \frac{b d^3 / 12}{d/2} = \frac{b d^2}{6}
$$

For a **solid circular shaft** (diameter $D$) used in robot wrists:

$$
Z = \frac{\pi D^3}{32}
$$

For a **hollow circular tube** (outer diameter $D$, inner diameter $d$) — common in lightweight robot arms:

$$
Z = \frac{\pi \left( D^4 - d^4 \right)}{32 \, D}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Designer Insight:** A hollow circular tube has a much higher section modulus-to-weight ratio than a solid shaft of the same outer diameter. This is why most modern **industrial robot arms** use hollow tubular links — to maximize bending resistance while minimizing inertia (which improves dynamic performance and reduces motor torque requirements).

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of the Bending Equation $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$

#### Setup and Assumed Geometry

Consider a **prismatic beam** of length $L$, initially straight. Apply a pure bending moment $M$ at the ends so that the beam bends into a circular arc of radius $R$ (constant curvature, since $M$ is constant under pure bending).

Let:
- $AB$ = original neutral axis of length $L$
- $A'B'$ = neutral axis after bending, of length still $L$ (neutral layer does not change length)
- $PQ$ = a fibre at distance $y$ above the neutral axis (assuming $y > 0$ for fibres above NA)
- $P'Q'$ = the same fibre after bending, of length $L + \Delta L$
- $O$ = centre of curvature of the bent beam

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Setup:** The figure of the bent beam is a circular arc. The neutral axis lies at the centre of the arc, and the fibres above/below the NA are at distances $y$ from this central arc.

#### Step 1 — Original Length of a Fibre at Distance $y$ from NA

The original length of the fibre $PQ$ is the same as the neutral axis length $AB$ (since cross-sections were originally plane and perpendicular):

$$
L_{PQ} = L
$$

#### Step 2 — Length of the Same Fibre After Bending

After bending, the fibre $P'Q'$ lies on a circular arc of radius $(R + y)$ centred at $O$. The length of an arc equals radius times the angle subtended:

$$
L_{P'Q'} = (R + y) \cdot \theta
$$

where $\theta$ is the angle subtended at the centre of curvature (in radians).

#### Step 3 — Length of the Neutral Axis After Bending

The neutral axis $A'B'$ lies on a circular arc of radius $R$:

$$
L_{A'B'} = R \cdot \theta
$$

But, by definition of the neutral axis, **it does not change in length**:

$$
L_{A'B'} = L
$$

Therefore:

$$
R \cdot \theta = L \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta = \frac{L}{R}
$$

#### Step 4 — Strain in the Fibre at Distance $y$

The change in length of the fibre $PQ$ is:

$$
\Delta L = L_{P'Q'} - L_{PQ} = (R + y) \cdot \theta - R \cdot \theta = y \cdot \theta
$$

The longitudinal (axial) strain in this fibre is:

$$
\epsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_{PQ}} = \frac{y \cdot \theta}{L}
$$

Substituting $\theta = L / R$ from Step 3:

$$
\epsilon = \frac{y \cdot (L / R)}{L} = \frac{y}{R}
$$

**This is the most important result of the derivation:**

$$
\epsilon = \frac{y}{R}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Meaning:** The strain at any point in the beam is **linearly proportional to the distance $y$ from the neutral axis**. Fibres farther from the NA experience greater strain (and hence greater stress). This linearity is the foundation of the **linear stress distribution** in beams.

#### Step 5 — Apply Hooke's Law to Get Bending Stress

Assuming the material is linearly elastic, Hooke's Law gives:

$$
\sigma = E \cdot \epsilon
$$

Substituting the strain expression from Step 4:

$$
\sigma = E \cdot \frac{y}{R} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This gives us the **second and third ratios** of the bending equation: $\sigma / y = E / R$.

#### Step 6 — Apply Moment Equilibrium to Get the M/I Term

Now consider the internal stresses acting on the cross-section. The total **bending moment** resisted by the stress distribution must equal the applied moment $M$.

Consider an elemental area $dA$ at distance $y$ from the neutral axis. The stress acting on it is $\sigma = E \cdot y / R$ (from Step 5). The elemental force is $dF = \sigma \cdot dA$.

The elemental moment about the neutral axis is:

$$
dM = y \cdot dF = y \cdot \sigma \cdot dA = y \cdot \frac{E \cdot y}{R} \cdot dA = \frac{E \cdot y^2}{R} \cdot dA
$$

Integrating over the entire cross-section:

$$
M = \int_A dM = \int_A \frac{E \cdot y^2}{R} \cdot dA = \frac{E}{R} \int_A y^2 \, dA
$$

By definition, the **second moment of area** (moment of inertia) about the neutral axis is:

$$
I = \int_A y^2 \, dA
$$

Substituting:

$$
M = \frac{E \cdot I}{R} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{M}{I} = \frac{E}{R}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This gives us the **first and third ratios** of the bending equation: $M / I = E / R$.

#### Step 7 — Combine All Three Ratios

From Step 5: $\dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$

From Step 6: $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{E}{R}$

Since both ratios equal $E / R$, they equal each other:

$$
\boxed{\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}}
$$

**Q.E.D. — Bending Equation Derived.**

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Position of the Neutral Axis (Through Centroid)

#### Setup

We need to prove that the neutral axis **passes through the centroid** of the cross-section. Use the condition that the **net axial force** on the cross-section is zero (no external axial load — only bending is applied).

#### Step 1 — Net Axial Force

The elemental axial force on area $dA$ is:

$$
dF = \sigma \cdot dA
$$

Total axial force (using $\sigma = E \cdot y / R$):

$$
F = \int_A \sigma \, dA = \int_A \frac{E \cdot y}{R} \, dA = \frac{E}{R} \int_A y \, dA
$$

#### Step 2 — Apply Equilibrium Condition

For no external axial force ($F = 0$):

$$
\frac{E}{R} \int_A y \, dA = 0
$$

Since $E / R \neq 0$:

$$
\int_A y \, dA = 0
$$

#### Step 3 — Identify the Centroid

The **first moment of area** about the neutral axis is $\int_A y \, dA$, and by definition of the centroid, this is zero **if and only if** the axis passes through the centroid.

**Therefore, the neutral axis coincides with the centroidal axis of the cross-section.**

$$
\boxed{y_{NA} = \bar{y} \quad \text{(distance from any reference to NA = distance to centroid)}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** This is often a follow-up sub-question: *"Prove that the neutral axis of a beam under pure bending passes through the centroid of its cross-section."* Memorize the integral $\int_A y \, dA = 0$ as the centroid condition.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Curvature Formula $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$

Starting from the bending equation:

$$
\frac{M}{I} = \frac{E}{R}
$$

Rearranging to isolate $1 / R$:

$$
\boxed{\frac{1}{R} = \frac{M}{EI}}
$$

This is the **curvature** of the beam at a section where the bending moment is $M$. Note that:
- If $M$ is large → curvature is large → beam bends sharply.
- If $EI$ is large (stiff beam) → curvature is small → beam is rigid.

> [!NOTE]
> **For Variable Cross-Sections:** If $I$ varies along $x$, then the curvature $1/R$ also varies with $x$. This is essential when designing **tapered robot links**.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A simply supported robotic arm link of length 2 m carries a uniformly distributed load of 5 kN/m over its entire span. The cross-section is rectangular, 100 mm wide × 200 mm deep. If $E = 200$ GPa, find:
1. The maximum bending moment.
2. The maximum bending stress.
3. The radius of curvature at the section of maximum moment.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Maximum Bending Moment**

For a simply supported beam with UDL, maximum moment occurs at mid-span:

$$
M_{max} = \frac{w L^2}{8} = \frac{5 \times 10^3 \times (2)^2}{8} = \frac{20000}{8} = 2500 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 2 — Moment of Inertia (Rectangular Section)**

$$
I = \frac{b d^3}{12} = \frac{0.1 \times (0.2)^3}{12} = \frac{0.1 \times 0.008}{12} = \frac{0.0008}{12} = 6.667 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^4
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Bending Stress**

Maximum $y = d/2 = 0.1$ m (extreme fibre distance):

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{M \cdot y_{max}}{I} = \frac{2500 \times 0.1}{6.667 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{250}{6.667 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.75 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 3.75 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Radius of Curvature**

$$
R = \frac{E \cdot I}{M} = \frac{200 \times 10^9 \times 6.667 \times 10^{-5}}{2500} = \frac{1.333 \times 10^7}{2500} = 5333.33 \text{ m}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity Check:** The bending stress is only 3.75 MPa, which is far below the yield strength of structural steel (~250 MPa). The radius of curvature is ~5.3 km, which means the beam is very gently curved — consistent with the small deflection assumption. Both results pass the engineering sanity check.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Bending Stress Analysis Pipeline

This diagram maps the *analytical workflow* used to compute bending stresses in a robot structural part — from physical load to the final stress result.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: External Load on Robot Link] --> B[Step 1: Free Body Diagram]
    B --> C[Step 2: Compute Support Reactions]
    C --> D[Step 3: Derive Shear Force Diagram]
    D --> E[Step 4: Derive Bending Moment Diagram]
    E --> F[Step 5: Identify Section of Max Moment]
    F --> G[Step 6: Calculate Moment of Inertia I]
    G --> H[Step 7: Apply Bending Equation sigma = My / I]
    H --> I[Step 8: Validate Against Allowable Stress]
    I --> J{Is sigma_max <= sigma_allowable?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Result: Design is SAFE]
    J -->|No| L[Result: Redesign with Larger I or Higher E]
    L --> G

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px
    style K fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px
    style L fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The yellow-highlighted blocks (Step 4, 5, 7) represent the **core bending-theory computations**. These are the steps where the bending equation is directly applied. All other steps are standard engineering mechanics prerequisites.

---

### 4.2 Cross-Sectional Stress Distribution Schematic

This block diagram illustrates how the bending stress varies linearly across a typical cross-section, with the neutral axis at the centroid.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Top Fibre: y = +y_max] -->|Tensile Stress sigma_max| B[Linear Variation: sigma = My/I]
    B -->|Stress Decreases Linearly| C[Neutral Axis: y = 0, sigma = 0]
    C -->|Stress Increases Linearly in Opposite Sign| D[Bottom Fibre: y = -y_max]
    D -->|Compressive Stress sigma_max| A

    style A fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:3px
    style D fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Visual Takeaway:** The neutral axis (yellow block) is the **pivot of symmetry** for the stress distribution. The stress magnitude is **symmetric** about the NA, but the **signs are opposite** — tension on one side, compression on the other.

---

### 4.3 Beam Under Bending — Geometry Block Diagram

This flowchart represents the geometric setup used in the derivation of the bending equation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial State: Straight Beam, Length L] --> B[Apply Pure Bending Moment M at Ends]
    B --> C[Beam Bends into Circular Arc]
    C --> D[Identify Neutral Layer: Length Unchanged]
    C --> E[Identify Fibre PQ at Distance y above NA]
    D --> F[Original Length of NA: L]
    D --> G[Original Length of Fibre PQ: L]
    C --> H[After Bending: NA Length = R times theta]
    C --> I[After Bending: Fibre Length = R+y times theta]
    H --> J[Step 1: theta = L / R]
    I --> K[Step 2: Strain epsilon = y / R]
    K --> L[Step 3: Apply Hooke Law: sigma = E y / R]
    L --> M[Step 4: Moment Equilibrium: M = E I / R]
    M --> N[Final Bending Equation: M/I = sigma/y = E/R]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px
    style N fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:3px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Map:** The green-highlighted final block (bottom) is the **destination** of the entire derivation pipeline. Every intermediate step (yellow) feeds into it. This visual structure helps KTU students memorize the *logical flow* of the derivation during exam preparation.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Neutral Axis" of a beam cross-section. State two of its key properties.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Neutral Axis** is the line of intersection of the *neutral layer* (a longitudinal plane within the beam where fibres experience zero longitudinal strain) with the cross-section of the beam.

**Two Key Properties:**
1. The neutral axis **passes through the centroid** of the cross-section.
2. The bending stress at the neutral axis is **zero**, and the stress magnitude **increases linearly** with distance from the NA.
3. *(Bonus — third property)* The neutral axis is the axis about which the **second moment of area** ($I$) is calculated for bending stress analysis.

*(Total: 3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two properties)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State the "Theory of Simple Bending" and write down its fundamental equation.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Theory of Simple Bending** states that when a beam is subjected to a bending moment within its elastic limit, the **bending stress** at any point in the cross-section is **directly proportional** to its distance from the neutral axis, and is given by the equation:

$$
\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}
$$

where $M$ = bending moment, $I$ = moment of inertia, $\sigma$ = bending stress at distance $y$ from the neutral axis, $E$ = Young's modulus, and $R$ = radius of curvature of the bent beam.

*(Total: 3 marks — 1 mark for theory statement, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for symbol description)*

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Pattern]

**(a)** Derive the bending equation $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ for a beam under pure bending. Clearly state all the assumptions used in the derivation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A simply supported steel beam of span 3 m carries a central point load of 20 kN. The beam has a rectangular cross-section 80 mm wide and 160 mm deep. Take $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm². Calculate:
   (i) The maximum bending moment. **(1 mark)**
   (ii) The maximum bending stress. **(2 marks)**
   (iii) The radius of curvature at the section of maximum bending moment. **(2 marks)**
   (iv) The section modulus. **(2 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of the Bending Equation:**

**Assumptions** (state all — 7 assumptions minimum, 1 mark deducted for missing key ones):
1. Material is homogeneous and isotropic.
2. The material obeys Hooke's Law (linear elastic).
3. Cross-sections that are plane before bending remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis after bending.
4. The beam is initially straight.
5. The beam has a uniform (prismatic) cross-section.
6. The beam undergoes pure bending (constant $M$, $V = 0$).
7. Self-weight is neglected.
8. Deflections are small.
9. No lateral buckling or torsion.

**Derivation Steps** (the full derivation from Section 3.1, summarized below for time-constrained exam writing):

Consider a beam element of length $L$ bent into a circular arc of radius $R$. Let $\theta$ be the angle subtended at the centre of curvature.

Length of the neutral axis (unchanged): $L = R \theta$

Length of a fibre at distance $y$ from the neutral axis: $L' = (R + y) \theta$

Strain in this fibre:

$$
\epsilon = \frac{L' - L}{L} = \frac{(R + y) \theta - R \theta}{R \theta} = \frac{y}{R}
$$

By Hooke's Law, the bending stress is:

$$
\sigma = E \epsilon = \frac{E y}{R}
$$

The internal resisting moment equals:

$$
M = \int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA = \int_A \frac{E y^2}{R} \, dA = \frac{E}{R} \int_A y^2 \, dA = \frac{E I}{R}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\frac{M}{I} = \frac{E}{R}
$$

Combining with $\sigma / y = E / R$:

$$
\boxed{\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}}
$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Maximum Bending Moment:**

For a simply supported beam with a central point load:

$$
M_{max} = \frac{W L}{4} = \frac{20 \times 10^3 \times 3}{4} = 15000 \text{ N·m} = 15 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**(ii) Maximum Bending Stress:**

Moment of Inertia (rectangular section, $b = 80$ mm, $d = 160$ mm):

$$
I = \frac{b d^3}{12} = \frac{80 \times (160)^3}{12} = \frac{80 \times 4096000}{12} = 2.731 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4
$$

Maximum $y = d / 2 = 80$ mm. Using the bending equation:

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{M \cdot y_{max}}{I} = \frac{15 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm} \times 80 \text{ mm}}{2.731 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^9}{2.731 \times 10^7} = 43.94 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 43.94 \text{ MPa}
$$

**(iii) Radius of Curvature at Maximum Moment Section:**

$$
R = \frac{E \cdot I}{M} = \frac{2 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2 \times 2.731 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4}{15 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}} = \frac{5.461 \times 10^{12}}{1.5 \times 10^7} = 3.641 \times 10^5 \text{ mm} = 364.1 \text{ m}
$$

**(iv) Section Modulus:**

$$
Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}} = \frac{2.731 \times 10^7}{80} = 3.414 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3 = 3.414 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE** [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the following terms with neat sketches where applicable: **(7 marks)**
   (i) Pure Bending vs. Simple Bending
   (ii) Section Modulus and its physical significance
   (iii) Moment of Inertia and its role in resisting bending

**(b)** A hollow circular steel shaft used in a robotic wrist joint has an outer diameter of 60 mm and an inner diameter of 40 mm. It is subjected to a bending moment of 5 kN·m. Calculate:
   (i) The section modulus of the cross-section. **(2 marks)**
   (ii) The maximum bending stress induced in the shaft. **(2 marks)**
   (iii) The radius of curvature if $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm². **(2 marks)**
   (iv) Comment on whether this stress is within safe limits for structural steel (yield stress = 250 MPa). **(1 mark)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Conceptual Explanations:**

**(i) Pure Bending vs. Simple Bending:**

- **Pure Bending:** A section of a beam is said to be under *pure bending* when the **bending moment is constant** along the length and the **shear force is zero**. The stress distribution is purely due to flexure, with no transverse shear stress.
  - *Example:* The portion of a beam between the two central point loads in a four-point bending test.
- **Simple Bending:** When the bending moment **varies** along the length of the beam, the section is said to be under *simple bending*. Here, both **bending stress** and **shear stress** coexist.
  - *Example:* A cantilever beam with a transverse point load at the free end.

The bending equation $M / I = \sigma / y = E / R$ is **exactly valid** for pure bending and is an **approximation** for simple bending (shear deformation is neglected).

**(ii) Section Modulus ($Z$):**

> **Definition:** The section modulus is defined as the **ratio of the second moment of area** ($I$) to the **distance of the extreme fibre** from the neutral axis ($y_{max}$):

$$
Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}}
$$

> **Physical Significance:** It is a *geometric property* of the cross-section that quantifies its **bending efficiency**. A higher $Z$ means the cross-section can resist a larger bending moment for the same maximum stress.

> **Design Use:** The maximum bending stress can be written directly as $\sigma_{max} = M / Z$, making $Z$ a critical design parameter in robotic link sizing.

**(iii) Moment of Inertia ($I$):**

> **Definition:** The second moment of area (moment of inertia) of a cross-section about the neutral axis is defined as:

$$
I = \int_A y^2 \, dA
$$

> **Role in Bending:** $I$ is a measure of the **distribution of area** about the neutral axis. The further the area is from the NA, the larger the $I$, and the lower the bending stress for a given moment. This is why **I-beams and hollow tubes** (where most of the area is far from the NA) are efficient in resisting bending.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Section Modulus (Hollow Circular):**

Outer diameter $D = 60$ mm, inner diameter $d = 40$ mm.

Moment of Inertia:

$$
I = \frac{\pi \left( D^4 - d^4 \right)}{64} = \frac{\pi \left( 60^4 - 40^4 \right)}{64} = \frac{\pi \left( 12960000 - 2560000 \right)}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 10400000}{64}
$$

$$
I = \frac{3.268 \times 10^7}{64} = 5.106 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^4
$$

Section modulus (with $y_{max} = D/2 = 30$ mm):

$$
Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}} = \frac{5.106 \times 10^5}{30} = 1.702 \times 10^4 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**(ii) Maximum Bending Stress:**

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{5 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}}{1.702 \times 10^4 \text{ mm}^3} = 293.8 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 293.8 \text{ MPa}
$$

**(iii) Radius of Curvature:**

$$
R = \frac{E \cdot I}{M} = \frac{2 \times 10^5 \times 5.106 \times 10^5}{5 \times 10^6} = \frac{1.021 \times 10^{11}}{5 \times 10^6} = 2.043 \times 10^4 \text{ mm} = 20.43 \text{ m}
$$

**(iv) Safety Comment:**

The induced bending stress (293.8 MPa) **exceeds** the yield stress of structural steel (250 MPa). **The design is NOT safe** — the shaft will undergo plastic deformation. The designer should either:
- Use a **larger outer diameter** (increase $D$),
- Use a **higher-strength material** (e.g., alloy steel with yield > 300 MPa), or
- **Reduce the applied load** / moment.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in Bending Equation Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency Trap:** Mixing units of mm and m within the same calculation. **Always convert to a single unit system** before plugging values into the bending equation. ($M$ in N·mm, $I$ in mm⁴, $y$ in mm, $\sigma$ in N/mm² = MPa.)
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Centroid:** For unsymmetric sections (T, L, channel), the neutral axis is at the **centroid**, NOT at the geometric centre. Many students incorrectly take $y_{max}$ as half the total depth — this gives the **wrong** stress.
>
> 3. **Sign Convention Confusion:** The bending equation $\sigma = M y / I$ is valid as a *magnitude*. For full sign analysis, use the convention: positive $M$ → tension at bottom fibre, compression at top fibre (sagging convention).
>
> 4. **Confusing Section Modulus with Moment of Inertia:** $I$ has units of mm⁴, $Z$ has units of mm³. KTU markers deduct heavily for unit errors.
>
> 5. **Using Solid Shaft Formula for Hollow Shaft:** A common mistake is to use $I = \pi D^4 / 64$ for a hollow shaft. **Always use** $I = \pi (D^4 - d^4) / 64$.
>
> 6. **Skipping Assumptions in Derivation Questions:** A derivation question without the assumptions listed will lose 1-2 marks out of 7, even if the math is perfect.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Cheat Sheet — Read this 1 hour before the exam!**

- ✅ **Bending Equation (Universal):** $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ — Write this FIRST on your answer sheet for any bending question.
- ✅ **Neutral Axis = Centroid** — always, no exceptions.
- ✅ **Strain is linear** in $y$: $\epsilon = y / R$.
- ✅ **Stress is linear** in $y$: $\sigma = E y / R$ (in pure bending).
- ✅ **Maximum stress at extreme fibre:** $\sigma_{max} = M \cdot c / I$, where $c = y_{max}$.
- ✅ **Section Modulus:** $Z = I / c$; useful for $\sigma_{max} = M / Z$.
- ✅ **Curvature:** $1 / R = M / (EI)$ — a stiff beam ($EI$ large) curves less.
- ✅ **Assumptions = 10 points** — memorize all of them; expect a direct 3-5 mark question.
- ✅ **Pure vs. Simple Bending** — know the difference and examples.
- ✅ **For $EI$ values:** Steel = 200 GPa, Aluminium = 70 GPa, CFRP = 140 GPa.
- ✅ **Standard $I$ formulas:** Rectangle $\dfrac{bd^3}{12}$, Solid Circle $\dfrac{\pi D^4}{64}$, Hollow Circle $\dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{64}$, I-Section $\dfrac{BD^3 - bd^3}{12}$.
- ✅ **Standard $Z$ formulas:** Rectangle $\dfrac{bd^2}{6}$, Solid Circle $\dfrac{\pi D^3}{32}$, Hollow Circle $\dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32 D}$.
- ✅ **Robotic design link:** Hollow tubes preferred over solid shafts for **high $Z$/weight ratio**.
- ✅ **Always check units consistency** before substitution.
- ✅ **For derivation questions, always start with assumptions, then geometry, then algebra, then final equation.**

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 Notes — Theory of Simple Bending.**
> *Practise at least 3-5 numerical problems from each cross-section type (rectangular, circular, I-section, T-section) to ensure full marks in the university exam. Focus on unit consistency and clear identification of the neutral axis for unsymmetric sections.*

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Structural Analysis of Robotic Parts**, the term **"Assumptions"** refers to the set of mathematically rigorous, physically justifiable idealizations imposed on a robotic link, joint, or sub-assembly so that its governing equilibrium, kinematic, and constitutive equations become analytically tractable. These assumptions convert an otherwise continuous, three-dimensional, non-linear, and anisotropic physical system into a solvable boundary-value problem expressed through ordinary or partial differential equations.

The principal categories of assumptions adopted in this module are:

1. **Geometric (Kinematic) Assumptions** — relating to deformation, cross-sectional behaviour, and shape idealization.
2. **Material (Constitutive) Assumptions** — relating to homogeneity, isotropy, linearity, and elastic response.
3. **Static / Dynamic Load Assumptions** — relating to loading type, magnitude, and time-dependence.
4. **Boundary & Continuity Assumptions** — relating to supports, joints, and inter-component interaction.
5. **Idealization Assumptions** — relating to lumped-parameter representation, rigid-body behaviour, and link discretization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Every assumption must be stated *before* a derivation begins. Examiners explicitly award marks for clearly enumerating the assumptions used. A derivation without its assumptions is considered incomplete under the KTU 2024 scheme valuation key.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain-English Explanation

Imagine you are asked to describe the motion of a long, thin robotic arm swinging under gravity. If you tried to model every single atom, every micro-crack, every thermal fluctuation, and every joint imperfection, the problem would be **impossible** to solve — even with the world's fastest supercomputer.

To make the problem solvable, an engineer makes **carefully chosen assumptions**:

- "Assume the arm is made of the *same* material everywhere" (homogeneity).
- "Assume it bends gently, not violently" (small deformation).
- "Assume the cross-section, which was flat and vertical before bending, stays flat and vertical after bending" (Euler–Bernoulli plane-section assumption).

In essence, **assumptions are the engineering "common-sense filters"** that strip away unnecessary detail while keeping the physics accurate enough to be useful. They are not lies — they are *justified simplifications* backed by experimental evidence and error analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants of Interest (Standard SI Values):**
> - Young's Modulus of structural steel: **E = 200 GPa**
> - Young's Modulus of aluminium alloys (common in robot links): **E = 70 GPa**
> - Poisson's ratio for metals: **ν ≈ 0.30**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**

## 1.3 Why Assumptions Matter in Robotic Part Analysis

Robotic parts are typically slender links, hollow tubes, gear-train housings, and end-effector mounts. Their analysis sits at the intersection of **strength of materials**, **machine design**, and **robot kinematics**. Without assumptions:

- The number of degrees of freedom (DOF) explodes.
- Partial differential equations become non-linear and coupled.
- Closed-form solutions disappear.

With assumptions, we recover classical engineering tools: the **Euler–Bernoulli beam equation**, the **Timoshenko beam equation**, the **stiffness matrix method**, and the **finite element formulation** — all of which are central to the PERAT525 syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Systematic Breakdown of the Core Assumptions

### A. Geometric (Kinematic) Assumptions

| Assumption | Statement | Where It Is Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Small Deformation** | Displacements and rotations are infinitesimally small, so higher-order terms in strain–displacement relations are neglected. | Linear static analysis of all robot links. |
| **Plane Sections Remain Plane (PSRP)** | Cross-sections initially flat and normal to the neutral axis remain flat and normal after deformation. | Euler–Bernoulli beam theory. |
| **No Shear Deformation** | Transverse shear strain γ = 0 across the cross-section. | Euler–Bernoulli beam theory for slender links (L/h > 20). |
| **Constant Cross-Section** | Cross-sectional area A and moment of inertia I do not vary along the length (for simple analysis). | Preliminary design; first-cut stiffness estimation. |
| **Straight, Prismatic Neutral Axis** | The undeformed neutral axis is straight. | Classical beam-column theory. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Quick Rule of Thumb:** If the **length-to-depth ratio (L/h) > 20**, the Euler–Bernoulli assumption is valid. If **L/h < 10**, use the **Timoshenko beam theory** (shear deformation must be retained).

### B. Material (Constitutive) Assumptions

- **Homogeneity:** The elastic modulus E is the same at every point of the body.
- **Isotropy:** The material's elastic properties are the same in all directions at any point.
- **Linear Elasticity:** Stress is proportional to strain, σ = E·ε (Hooke's Law holds).
- **No Creep / No Viscoplasticity:** The material does not deform over time under constant load.
- **No Temperature Effects:** Thermal strains are neglected unless explicitly coupled.

### C. Static / Dynamic Assumptions

- **Static Equilibrium:** ΣF = 0 and ΣM = 0 are satisfied at every instant.
- **Quasi-Static Loading:** Loads vary slowly enough that inertial effects (ρ·∂²u/∂t²) can be neglected.
- **Conservative Forces:** Loads are derivable from a potential (no energy dissipation at this stage).
- **For Dynamic Extension:** Inertia terms are retained, leading to the equation
  $$\rho A \frac{\partial^{2} w(x,t)}{\partial t^{2}} + EI \frac{\partial^{4} w(x,t)}{\partial x^{4}} = q(x,t)$$

### D. Boundary & Continuity Assumptions

- **Perfect Fixity:** A clamped end has zero displacement and zero rotation.
- **Ideal Pin / Roller:** A pinned end has zero displacement but free rotation.
- **Continuity at Joints:** Displacement and slope are continuous across rigid joints.

### E. Idealization for Robotic Links

A robotic link is commonly idealized as a **Euler–Bernoulli beam** of length L, with a lumped mass m at its tip representing the payload, and a torsional spring K_t at the joint representing the actuator stiffness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The assumptions above are not arbitrary. They are validated experimentally — typical errors introduced by Euler–Bernoulli assumptions for L/h > 20 are **less than 1%**, which is well within the design tolerance for robotic arms.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** All mathematical operators and absolute-value bars in the table below use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the bare vertical pipe `|` to prevent breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbolic Form | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Normal strain (linearized) | $\varepsilon_x = \dfrac{du}{dx}$ | Strain under small deformation assumption. |
| 2 | Bending strain (Euler–Bernoulli) | $\varepsilon_x = -y \dfrac{d^{2} w}{d x^{2}}$ | PSRP assumption yields linear strain distribution. |
| 3 | Hooke's Law (1-D linear elastic) | $\sigma_x = E \cdot \varepsilon_x$ | Linear-elastic material assumption. |
| 4 | Bending moment – curvature | $M(x) = EI \dfrac{d^{2} w}{d x^{2}}$ | Constitutive + kinematic assumption combined. |
| 5 | Shear force – slope relation | $V(x) = \dfrac{dM}{dx} = EI \dfrac{d^{3} w}{d x^{3}}$ | From equilibrium assumption. |
| 6 | Governing static beam equation | $EI \dfrac{d^{4} w}{d x^{4}} = q(x)$ | Full set of assumptions combined. |
| 7 | Dynamic beam equation (Euler) | $\rho A \dfrac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial t^{2}} + EI \dfrac{\partial^{4} w}{\partial x^{4}} = q(x,t)$ | Inertia retained. |
| 8 | Timoshenko bending strain | $\varepsilon_x = -y \dfrac{d\phi}{dx}$ | $\phi$ ≠ dw/dx ; shear deformation retained. |
| 9 | Critical slenderness check | $\lambda = L / h$ | Use Euler–Bernoulli if $\lambda > 20$. |
| 10 | Natural frequency (cantilever) | $\omega_1 = (1.875)^{2} \sqrt{\dfrac{EI}{\rho A L^{4}}}$ | First mode of a cantilever robot link. |
| 11 | Tip deflection of cantilever | $\delta = \dfrac{P L^{3}}{3EI}$ | Direct consequence of all assumptions. |
| 12 | Stiffness matrix element | $k_{ij} = \int_{0}^{L} EI \cdot N_i''(x) \cdot N_j''(x) \, dx$ | FEM idealization of link. |

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas above assume **SI units**: force in N, length in m, moment in N·m, E in Pa, I in m⁴, ρ in kg/m³, A in m².

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These assumptions underpin:
- **Industrial robot arm design** (KUKA, ABB, FANUC arms).
- **End-effector stiffness modelling** for precision assembly robots.
- **Space-robot link analysis** where mass minimization demands thin-walled tubes.
- **Surgical robotic manipulators** (Da Vinci style), where sub-millimetre deflections matter.
- **Collaborative robot (cobot) safety analysis**, where impact loads require dynamic beam assumptions.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: How Assumptions Lead to the Euler–Bernoulli Beam Equation

The derivation below shows, **step-by-step**, how five core assumptions combine to yield the classical 4th-order beam equation that is the workhorse of robotic-link analysis.

### Step 1 — State the Assumptions (Mandatory under KTU 2024)

1. Material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** (Hooke's Law: σ = E·ε).
2. **Small deformations** → linearized strain–displacement relations.
3. **Plane sections remain plane and normal to the neutral axis** (PSRP).
4. **Shear deformation is negligible** (γ_xz = 0).
5. The cross-section is **prismatic** and the neutral axis is **straight and undeformed in the axial direction**.

### Step 2 — Geometric (Kinematic) Strain

Under the small-deformation assumption, the normal strain at a fibre located a distance y above the neutral axis is obtained from the displacement field u(x) of that fibre. If w(x) is the transverse deflection, then by the **PSRP assumption**, u(x) at height y is:

$$
u(x, y) = -y \cdot \theta(x)
$$

where θ(x) is the rotation of the cross-section. For small rotations, θ ≈ dw/dx, giving:

$$
\varepsilon_x = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = -y \frac{d^{2} w}{d x^{2}}
$$

This is the **kinematic consequence of assumption 3 (PSRP) combined with assumption 2 (small deformation)**.

### Step 3 — Constitutive Relation (Hooke's Law)

Using assumption 1 (linear elastic material), the bending stress is:

$$
\sigma_x = E \cdot \varepsilon_x = -E y \frac{d^{2} w}{d x^{2}}
$$

### Step 4 — Moment–Curvature Relation

The internal bending moment M(x) is the first moment of the bending stress distribution about the neutral axis. By definition:

$$
M(x) = -\int_A \sigma_x \cdot y \, dA
$$

Substituting σ_x from Step 3:

$$
M(x) = -\int_A \left( -E y \frac{d^{2} w}{d x^{2}} \right) y \, dA = E \frac{d^{2} w}{d x^{2}} \int_A y^{2} \, dA
$$

The integral $\int_A y^2 \, dA$ is, by definition, the **second moment of area** I about the neutral axis. Therefore:

$$
M(x) = EI \frac{d^{2} w}{d x^{2}}
$$

This compact equation is the **constitutive–kinematic bridge** between the bending moment and the local curvature of the deformed neutral axis.

### Step 5 — Equilibrium of an Elemental Slice

From the static-equilibrium assumption (assumption set C), consider an infinitesimal beam element of length dx. The shear force V(x) and bending moment M(x) at the two faces differ by infinitesimals dV and dM. Equilibrium of forces in the transverse direction gives:

$$
\frac{dV}{dx} = -q(x)
$$

Equilibrium of moments about the left face of the element gives:

$$
V = \frac{dM}{dx}
$$

Substituting the moment–curvature relation from Step 4 into the moment-equilibrium relation:

$$
V(x) = \frac{d}{dx}\left( EI \frac{d^{2} w}{d x^{2}} \right)
$$

Substituting this into the force-equilibrium relation, and assuming constant EI:

$$
\frac{d^{2}}{dx^{2}}\left( EI \frac{d^{2} w}{d x^{2}} \right) = -q(x)
$$

### Step 6 — Final Governing Equation

Dropping the negative sign by defining q(x) as the load in the positive w direction (a sign convention commonly used in KTU textbooks):

$$
\boxed{EI \frac{d^{4} w}{d x^{4}} = q(x)}
$$

This is the **Euler–Bernoulli beam equation** — a direct, traceable consequence of the five stated assumptions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Recap (Why each step is needed):**
> - Step 1 → marks awarded for stating assumptions explicitly.
> - Step 2 → uses kinematic assumptions.
> - Step 3 → uses material assumption.
> - Step 4 → combines kinematic + material assumptions.
> - Step 5 → uses static-equilibrium assumption.
> - Step 6 → assembles the full equation.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Cantilever Robotic Link Under Tip Load

**Problem:** A robot link of length **L = 0.6 m**, rectangular cross-section **b = 30 mm, h = 10 mm**, made of aluminium (**E = 70 GPa**), carries a tip payload **P = 50 N**. Compute the tip deflection using the Euler–Bernoulli assumptions.

### Step 1 — Compute the Second Moment of Area

For a rectangular cross-section about its neutral axis (bending about the strong axis):

$$
I = \frac{b h^{3}}{12} = \frac{(0.030)(0.010)^{3}}{12} = 2.5 \times 10^{-9} \; \text{m}^{4}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Flexural Rigidity EI

$$
EI = (70 \times 10^{9}) \times (2.5 \times 10^{-9}) = 175 \; \text{N·m}^{2}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Cantilever Tip-Deflection Formula

The tip deflection of a cantilever under a tip load P (a direct consequence of the Euler–Bernoulli assumptions derived above) is:

$$
\delta = \frac{P L^{3}}{3 EI}
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
\delta = \frac{(50)(0.6)^{3}}{3 \times 175}
$$

$$
\delta = \frac{50 \times 0.216}{525}
$$

$$
\delta = \frac{10.8}{525} = 0.02057 \; \text{m} \approx 20.6 \; \text{mm}
$$

### Step 4 — Validate the Assumptions

- **L/h = 0.6 / 0.01 = 60** → 60 > 20, so the **Euler–Bernoulli assumption is valid** (shear deformation negligible).
- **Maximum stress check:** $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M c}{I} = \dfrac{(50)(0.6)(0.005)}{2.5 \times 10^{-9}} = 60 \; \text{MPa}$, well below aluminium's yield strength (~240 MPa), so **linear-elastic assumption holds**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Takeaway:** Always end a derivation with a **validity check** of the assumptions you used. Examiners reward this habit with bonus conceptual marks.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Hierarchy of Assumptions in Robotic Part Analysis

The diagram below maps how the major assumption families interact, depend on each other, and feed into the final governing equation of a robotic link.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Physical Robotic Link] --> B[Geometric Assumptions]
    A --> C[Material Assumptions]
    A --> D[Static Assumptions]
    A --> E[Boundary Assumptions]

    B --> B1[Small Deformation]
    B --> B2[Plane Sections Remain Plane PSRP]
    B --> B3[No Shear Deformation]
    B --> B4[Prismatic Cross Section]

    C --> C1[Homogeneous]
    C --> C2[Isotropic]
    C --> C3[Linear Elastic Hooke Law]
    C --> C4[No Creep No Viscoplasticity]

    D --> D1[Static Equilibrium Sigma F 0 Sigma M 0]
    D --> D2[Quasi Static Load]
    D --> D3[Conservative Forces]

    E --> E1[Clamped End u 0 theta 0]
    E --> E2[Pinned End u 0 M 0]
    E --> E3[Continuity at Joints]

    B1 --> F1[Linearized Strain epsilon equals du dx]
    B2 --> F2[Bending Strain epsilon equals minus y d2w dx2]
    B3 --> F3[Gamma xz equals 0]
    C3 --> F4[Stress sigma equals E epsilon]
    D1 --> F5[dV dx equals minus q and V equals dM dx]

    F1 --> G[Kinematic Relations]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> H[Constitutive Relations]
    F5 --> I[Equilibrium Relations]

    G --> J[Moment Curvature M equals EI d2w dx2]
    H --> J
    I --> K[Governing Beam Equation]

    J --> K
    E1 --> L[Boundary Conditions]
    E2 --> L
    E3 --> L

    K --> M[Final Solution w of x via Integration or FEM]
    L --> M

    M --> N[Engineering Output Deflection Stress Natural Frequency]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Validity Decision Tree for Choosing the Right Beam Theory

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Robotic Link Geometry Known] --> Q1{Length to Depth Ratio L over h greater than 20}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Material Behaviour Linear Elastic}
    Q1 -- No --> T1[Use Timoshenko Beam Theory with Shear Deformation]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Loads Static or Quasi Static}
    Q2 -- No --> T2[Use Non Linear or Plasticity Theory]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Cross Section Constant}
    Q3 -- No --> T3[Add Inertia Term dynamic beam equation]
    Q4 -- Yes --> R[Euler Bernoulli Solution Valid]
    Q4 -- No --> T4[Use Stepped Beam or FEM Discretization]
    R --> END[Proceed with EI d4w dx4 equals q of x]
    T1 --> END2[Proceed with Timoshenko equations]
    T2 --> END3[Proceed with non linear analysis]
    T3 --> END4[Proceed with dynamic beam equation]
    T4 --> END5[Proceed with FEM stiffness assembly]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - The first block diagram shows the **logical assembly** of all assumptions into the final governing equation.
> - The second block diagram is a **decision aid** that helps a robotic-parts analyst select the correct beam theory for a given link geometry.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State the five fundamental assumptions used in the Euler–Bernoulli beam theory as applied to a slender robotic link. Justify each in one sentence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** — required for Hooke's law σ = E·ε to hold at every point.
2. **Deformations are infinitesimally small** — allows linearization of strain–displacement relations.
3. **Plane sections remain plane and normal to the neutral axis (PSRP)** — gives the linear bending-strain distribution ε_x = −y·d²w/dx².
4. **Shear deformation is negligible** — the cross-section rotates as a rigid body, so γ_xz = 0.
5. **The neutral axis is straight and undeformed axially, and the cross-section is prismatic** — allows EI to be treated as a constant.

*(1 mark for the list, 1 mark for the justification of any one assumption, 1 mark for the formula/justification of the remaining four.)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**A robotic link has length-to-depth ratio L/h = 8. Which beam theory should be used — Euler–Bernoulli or Timoshenko? Justify your choice.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Timoshenko beam theory** should be used.
**Justification:** When L/h < 10, the shear deformation contributes more than ~5% of the total deflection, and the Euler–Bernoulli assumption (no shear deformation) is no longer valid. Timoshenko's theory retains the shear strain γ_xz and treats the cross-section rotation φ(x) as independent of the slope dw/dx, capturing both bending and shear effects. (Full marks for stating the rule L/h > 20, naming the theory, and giving the quantitative reason.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4 Choice A]**

A robot arm link is modelled as a cantilever beam of length **L = 0.8 m**, with a hollow circular cross-section of outer diameter **D_o = 40 mm** and inner diameter **D_i = 36 mm**. The material is structural steel with **E = 200 GPa**, **ρ = 7850 kg/m³**, and **ν = 0.30**. A tip payload of **P = 80 N** acts at the free end.

**(a)** [7 marks] — State all assumptions used in the Euler–Bernoulli analysis and derive the governing differential equation for the deflection of the link.

**(b)** [7 marks] — Compute the maximum tip deflection, the maximum bending stress, and the first natural frequency of transverse vibration. Verify whether the Euler–Bernoulli assumptions remain valid.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Assumptions and Derivation [7 marks]**

*Assumptions (2 marks):*
1. Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic.
2. Deformations are small.
3. Plane sections remain plane and normal to the neutral axis (PSRP).
4. Shear deformation is negligible.
5. The link is prismatic and the neutral axis is straight.

*Derivation (5 marks):*

Starting from the displacement assumption under PSRP:

$$
u(x, y) = -y \frac{d w}{d x}
$$

The bending strain:

$$
\varepsilon_x = -y \frac{d^{2} w}{d x^{2}}
$$

Hooke's Law:

$$
\sigma_x = E \varepsilon_x = -E y \frac{d^{2} w}{d x^{2}}
$$

Moment–curvature:

$$
M = -\int_A \sigma_x y \, dA = E I \frac{d^{2} w}{d x^{2}}
$$

Equilibrium of an element:

$$
V = \frac{dM}{dx}, \quad \frac{dV}{dx} = -q(x)
$$

Combining and dropping the sign (positive q convention):

$$
EI \frac{d^{4} w}{d x^{4}} = q(x)
$$

**(b) Numerical Computation [7 marks]**

*Step 1 — Second moment of area for hollow circular section:*

$$
I = \frac{\pi}{64} \left( D_o^{4} - D_i^{4} \right) = \frac{\pi}{64} \left( (0.040)^{4} - (0.036)^{4} \right)
$$

$$
I = \frac{\pi}{64} \left( 2.56 \times 10^{-6} - 1.6796 \times 10^{-6} \right) = \frac{\pi}{64} (0.8804 \times 10^{-6})
$$

$$
I = 4.323 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{m}^{4}
$$

*Step 2 — Flexural rigidity:*

$$
EI = (200 \times 10^{9})(4.323 \times 10^{-8}) = 8646 \; \text{N·m}^{2}
$$

*Step 3 — Tip deflection (cantilever, tip load P):*

$$
\delta = \frac{P L^{3}}{3 EI} = \frac{(80)(0.8)^{3}}{3 \times 8646} = \frac{40.96}{25\,938} = 1.579 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\delta \approx 1.58 \; \text{mm}}
$$

*Step 4 — Maximum bending stress:*

Maximum moment at the fixed end: M_max = P·L = 80 × 0.8 = 64 N·m.
Maximum fibre distance: c = D_o/2 = 0.020 m.

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max} \cdot c}{I} = \frac{(64)(0.020)}{4.323 \times 10^{-8}} = 2.961 \times 10^{7} \; \text{Pa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{max} \approx 29.6 \; \text{MPa}}
$$

*Step 5 — First natural frequency (cantilever):*

Cross-sectional area:

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} (D_o^{2} - D_i^{2}) = \frac{\pi}{4}((0.040)^{2} - (0.036)^{2}) = 2.199 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^{2}
$$

Using β₁ L = 1.875 (cantilever first mode):

$$
\omega_1 = (1.875)^{2} \sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A L^{4}}} = (3.5156) \sqrt{\frac{8646}{(7850)(2.199 \times 10^{-4})(0.8)^{4}}}
$$

$$
\omega_1 = 3.5156 \times \sqrt{\frac{8646}{6.605 \times 10^{3}}} = 3.5156 \times 1.144
$$

$$
\boxed{\omega_1 \approx 4.02 \; \text{rad/s} \quad (f_1 \approx 0.64 \; \text{Hz})}
$$

*Step 6 — Assumption validity check:*

- L/h = 0.8 / 0.04 = 20. On the boundary of validity; the result is borderline acceptable for Euler–Bernoulli.
- σ_max = 29.6 MPa << σ_yield ≈ 250 MPa, so linear-elastic assumption holds.
- δ/L = 1.58/800 = 0.002 < 0.01, so small-deformation assumption is valid.

**Conclusion:** All assumptions are valid; results are engineering-accurate.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 Choice B]**

**(a)** [7 marks] — Compare and contrast the **Euler–Bernoulli** and **Timoshenko** beam theories with reference to their assumptions, governing equations, and the range of robotic links to which each is applicable. Include the key parameters: shear coefficient k and shear modulus G in your discussion.

**(b)** [7 marks] — A short, stubby robot wrist link has L = 0.20 m, square cross-section 25 mm × 25 mm, E = 200 GPa, G = 77 GPa, k = 5/6, ν = 0.30. Compute the **percentage error** introduced by the Euler–Bernoulli theory in predicting the tip deflection under a tip load P = 30 N, relative to the Timoshenko theory.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison [7 marks]**

| Aspect | Euler–Bernoulli Theory | Timoshenko Beam Theory |
|---|---|---|
| **Shear deformation** | Neglected (γ_xz = 0) | Retained (γ_xz ≠ 0) |
| **Cross-section rotation** | φ(x) = dw/dx | φ(x) is independent of dw/dx |
| **Governing equation** | $EI \dfrac{d^{4}w}{dx^{4}} = q(x)$ | Two coupled equations involving EI and kGA |
| **Order of the system** | 4th-order single ODE | Two 2nd-order coupled ODEs |
| **Boundary conditions** | w, dw/dx, M, V (2 at each end) | w, φ, M, V (2 at each end) |
| **Validity range (L/h)** | L/h > 20 (slender) | L/h ≥ 1 (short, stubby) |
| **Material parameters used** | E, I | E, I, G, A, k (Timoshenko shear coefficient) |
| **Typical robotic application** | Long reach arms, forearm links | Wrist joints, gripper mounts, gear housings |
| **Accuracy for L/h > 20** | < 1% error | < 1% error (overkill) |
| **Accuracy for L/h = 5** | ~20% error | < 1% error |

*The Timoshenko shear coefficient k accounts for the non-uniform distribution of shear stress across the cross-section; for a rectangular section, k = 5/6, and for a solid circular section, k = 9/10.*

**(b) Percentage Error Computation [7 marks]**

*Step 1 — Geometric properties:*

$$
I = \frac{b h^{3}}{12} = \frac{(0.025)(0.025)^{3}}{12} = 3.255 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{m}^{4}
$$

$$
A = b h = (0.025)(0.025) = 6.25 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^{2}
$$

*Step 2 — Slenderness check:*

$$
\frac{L}{h} = \frac{0.20}{0.025} = 8
$$

Since L/h = 8 < 10, **Timoshenko theory is preferred**; Euler–Bernoulli is expected to under-predict the deflection.

*Step 3 — Euler–Bernoulli tip deflection:*

$$
\delta_{EB} = \frac{P L^{3}}{3 EI} = \frac{(30)(0.20)^{3}}{3 \times (200 \times 10^{9})(3.255 \times 10^{-8})}
$$

$$
\delta_{EB} = \frac{30 \times 0.008}{19\,530} = \frac{0.24}{19\,530} = 1.229 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m} = 12.29 \; \mu\text{m}
$$

*Step 4 — Timoshenko tip deflection:*

For a cantilever with tip load P, the Timoshenko tip deflection is:

$$
\delta_T = \frac{P L^{3}}{3 EI} + \frac{P L}{k G A}
$$

The first term is identical to δ_EB; the second term is the **additional shear-induced deflection**.

$$
\delta_T = \delta_{EB} + \frac{P L}{k G A}
$$

$$
\delta_T = 1.229 \times 10^{-5} + \frac{(30)(0.20)}{(5/6)(77 \times 10^{9})(6.25 \times 10^{-4})}
$$

$$
\delta_T = 1.229 \times 10^{-5} + \frac{6}{4.005 \times 10^{7}}
$$

$$
\delta_T = 1.229 \times 10^{-5} + 1.498 \times 10^{-7} = 1.244 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m} = 12.44 \; \mu\text{m}
$$

*Step 5 — Percentage error of Euler–Bernoulli:*

$$
\text{Error \%} = \frac{\delta_T - \delta_{EB}}{\delta_T} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Error \%} = \frac{1.244 \times 10^{-5} - 1.229 \times 10^{-5}}{1.244 \times 10^{-5}} \times 100 = \frac{1.50 \times 10^{-7}}{1.244 \times 10^{-5}} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Error \%} \approx 1.21\%}
$$

*Step 6 — Interpretation:*

Although L/h = 8 is below the classical "safe" threshold of 20, the **stiff aluminium-like steel and the dominant bending stiffness** keep the error small (~1.2%). For softer materials or larger cross-sections, the error would be substantially higher.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state assumptions** before a derivation: −2 marks minimum, even if the math is correct.
> 2. **Sign errors** in the strain–displacement relation (mixing u = -y·dw/dx and u = +y·dw/dx): −1 mark per occurrence.
> 3. **Forgetting the shear-correction factor k** in Timoshenko problems: −2 marks.
> 4. **Computing I incorrectly** for hollow sections (using D_o³ instead of D_o⁴): −1 mark.
> 5. **Skipping the validity check** at the end of a numerical problem: −1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Assumptions in Structural Analysis of Robotic Parts**

- **Five Core Assumptions of Euler–Bernoulli Theory:**
  (i) Homogeneous, isotropic, linear-elastic material;
  (ii) Small deformations;
  (iii) Plane sections remain plane and normal to neutral axis (PSRP);
  (iv) Shear deformation negligible;
  (v) Prismatic, straight neutral axis.

- **Selection Rule for Beam Theory:**
  - **L/h > 20** → Euler–Bernoulli (error < 1%).
  - **L/h < 10** → Timoshenko (shear deformation must be included).

- **Governing Equation to Memorize (Euler–Bernoulli, static):**
  $EI \dfrac{d^{4} w}{d x^{4}} = q(x)$

- **Governing Equation to Memorize (Euler–Bernoulli, dynamic):**
  $\rho A \dfrac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial t^{2}} + EI \dfrac{\partial^{4} w}{\partial x^{4}} = q(x,t)$

- **Moment–Curvature Bridge (most-tested identity):**
  $M(x) = EI \dfrac{d^{2} w}{d x^{2}}$

- **Cantilever Tip-Deflection Formula (consequence of all assumptions):**
  $\delta = \dfrac{P L^{3}}{3 EI}$

- **Timoshenko Shear Deflection (additional term):**
  $\delta_{\text{shear}} = \dfrac{P L}{k G A}$

- **Standard Material Properties (memorize):**
  Steel: E = 200 GPa, G = 77 GPa, ν = 0.30, ρ = 7850 kg/m³.
  Aluminium: E = 70 GPa, G = 26 GPa, ν = 0.33, ρ = 2700 kg/m³.

- **KTU 2024 Habit to Adopt:**
  Always begin a derivation by *stating* the assumptions; always end a numerical problem with a *validity check* (slenderness ratio, stress vs. yield, deflection vs. length).

- **Common Mistake to Avoid:**
  Using Euler–Bernoulli for short robotic wrist or gripper links (L/h < 10) — examiners explicitly set traps of this kind in Part B questions.

- **FEM Connection (preview of Module 5):**
  The assumptions above directly determine the **Hermite cubic shape functions** N_i(x) used in the stiffness matrix of a 2-noded beam element:
  $k_{ij} = \int_0^L EI \cdot N_i''(x) \cdot N_j''(x) \, dx$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Derivation of Bending Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT525 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS OF ROBOTIC PARTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Derivation of Bending Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 🔹 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Bending Equation** (also called the **Flexure Formula** or **Euler–Bernoulli Beam Equation**) is the fundamental relationship that governs the distribution of normal (bending) stress across the cross-section of a beam subjected to pure bending. For a beam of homogeneous, isotropic, linearly elastic material undergoing pure bending, it is mathematically expressed as:

$$\frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{M}{I} = \frac{E}{R}$$

where every symbol has a strict engineering meaning used throughout KTU 2024 Scheme examinations.

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit (SI) |
|--------|----------|-------------------|
| $\sigma$ | Bending stress at a fiber | $\text{N/m}^2$ (Pa) |
| $y$ | Distance of the fiber from the Neutral Axis (NA) | m |
| $M$ | Bending moment at the section | $\text{N·m}$ |
| $I$ | Second moment of area (Area Moment of Inertia) about NA | $\text{m}^4$ |
| $E$ | Young's Modulus of Elasticity of the material | $\text{N/m}^2$ (Pa) |
| $R$ | Radius of curvature of the bent beam at the neutral axis | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The bending equation is the backbone of all structural analysis of robotic links, manipulator arms, gripper jaws, and chassis members. Whenever a robotic component acts as a load-bearing beam, this equation determines whether it will yield, deflect excessively, or fail in fatigue.

---

## 🔹 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you take a thin **steel ruler** and bend it slowly between your two hands. Look at it from the side:

- The **top surface** of the ruler becomes **shorter** (it is in **compression**).
- The **bottom surface** of the ruler becomes **longer** (it is in **tension**).
- Somewhere in the middle, there is a thin layer that **does not change its length at all** — this is the **Neutral Axis**.

For a **robotic arm** (say, the upper link of a 6-DOF serial manipulator lifting a payload), the situation is identical. When the joint motor produces torque, the arm bends slightly. The motor-side fibers compress; the outer fibers stretch; the neutral axis sits roughly at the geometric centroid of the cross-section.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:**
> - Farther a fiber is from the neutral axis → more it stretches or compresses → higher stress.
> - Closer to the neutral axis → less deformation → lower stress.
> - **Zero stress** occurs *exactly* at the neutral axis itself ($y = 0 \Rightarrow \sigma = 0$).

This is why structural designers use **I-beams**, **hollow tubes**, or **box sections** in robot arms — they put the maximum possible material **far from the neutral axis**, where stress is highest, dramatically increasing $I$ without adding weight.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🔹 Fundamental Assumptions of the Bending Theory

The derivation of the bending equation rests on **five sacred assumptions**. Examiners at KTU frequently award 2–3 marks in Part A questions for stating these correctly.

1. **The beam material is homogeneous and isotropic** — its mechanical properties are the same at every point and in every direction.
2. **The beam material obeys Hooke's Law** — stress is directly proportional to strain within the elastic limit ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$).
3. **The beam is initially straight** and has a uniform cross-section along its length (a *prismatic beam*).
4. **Plane sections before bending remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral surface after bending** (the **Euler–Bernoulli hypothesis**). Shear deformation is neglected.
5. **The beam experiences pure bending** — only a constant bending moment acts; transverse shear force is zero at the section of interest.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often forget that the bending equation is valid **only for pure bending**. Where shear force is significant, the stress distribution is no longer perfectly linear — additional shear-stress analysis (from $\tau = VQ/Ib$) becomes necessary.

---

## 🔹 Step-by-Step Logic of the Derivation (Roadmap)

The derivation follows a clean four-step logical chain. We will execute every step in detail inside Section 3.

- **Step 1 → Geometric Kinematics:** Use the geometry of a deformed circular arc to find the *longitudinal strain* $\varepsilon$ of any fiber at distance $y$ from the NA.
- **Step 2 → Constitutive Law:** Apply Hooke's Law ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$) to convert strain into bending stress.
- **Step 3 → Force Equilibrium ($\sum F_x = 0$):** Recognize that pure bending produces no net axial force → the neutral axis passes through the **centroid** of the cross-section.
- **Step 4 → Moment Equilibrium ($\sum M = 0$):** Integrate the elemental bending stresses over the cross-section and equate to the applied bending moment $M$. This yields $M = EI/R$.

Combining all four steps gives the unified triple relation:

$$\frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{M}{I} = \frac{E}{R}$$

---

## 🔹 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **definitive reference** you must memorize for KTU 2024 ESE examinations. Every symbol and unit is in SI base form.

| # | Formula | Name / Physical Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---------|------------------------|-------------|
| 1 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{y}{R}$ | Longitudinal strain at fiber $y$ | Strain at any depth of the beam |
| 2 | $\sigma = E\,\varepsilon = \dfrac{E\,y}{R}$ | Bending stress at fiber $y$ | Stress calculation in a robot link |
| 3 | $M = \dfrac{E\,I}{R}$ | Flexural rigidity relation | Curvature from applied moment |
| 4 | $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ | **The Bending Equation** | Master relation |
| 5 | $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M\,c}{I} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Maximum bending stress | Design check (yield) |
| 6 | $Z = \dfrac{I}{c}$ | Section Modulus | Strength parameter for design |
| 7 | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{E\,I}$ | Curvature formula | Slope and deflection integration |

### Common Section Moduli (must know for Part A)

| Cross-Section | Moment of Inertia $I$ | Section Modulus $Z$ |
|---------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Rectangle (b·h) | $b\,h^{3}/12$ | $b\,h^{2}/6$ |
| Hollow Rectangle (B·H, b·h) | $\dfrac{B\,H^{3} - b\,h^{3}}{12}$ | $\dfrac{B\,H^{3} - b\,h^{3}}{6\,H}$ |
| Solid Circle (diameter $d$) | $\pi\,d^{4}/64$ | $\pi\,d^{3}/32$ |
| Hollow Circle (outer $D$, inner $d$) | $\dfrac{\pi\,(D^{4}-d^{4})}{64}$ | $\dfrac{\pi\,(D^{4}-d^{4})}{32\,D}$ |
| Standard I-Section (table values) | Use steel-table $I_{xx}$ | $I_{xx}/(D/2)$ |

> **Note on units:** In all KTU numerical problems, $E$ is given in **GPa**, lengths in **mm**, and moments in **N·mm**. Convert consistently: $1\;\text{GPa} = 10^{3}\;\text{N/mm}^{2}$.

---

## 🔹 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **Structural Analysis of Robotic Parts**, the bending equation is indispensable for:

- **Robot Link Design:** Verifying that the manipulator link does not yield under maximum payload at full reach.
- **End-Effector / Gripper Analysis:** The jaws of a gripper are essentially cantilever beams — bending stress at the root must remain below allowable limits.
- **Mobile Robot Chassis:** Longitudinal members of a 4-wheeled or legged robot carrying payloads experience bending.
- **Fatigue Life Prediction:** Stress amplitudes from $\sigma_{\max} = M/Z$ feed directly into S–N curves and Miner's rule.
- **Compliant Mechanisms & Soft Robotics:** Curvature $1/R = M/EI$ is the basis for modeling flexure bearings and elastic joints.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** A KTU Part B question frequently combines $M/I = \sigma/y$ with deflection formulas (e.g., $\delta_{\max} = WL^{3}/3EI$ for a cantilever) in a single numerical problem. Practice both together.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivation of the Bending Equation

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is **complete, exhaustive, and KTU-board-grade**. Every algebraic transition and physical interpretation is written out explicitly — no shortcuts, no skipped lines.

---

## 🔸 Preliminary Setup

Consider a small element of length $dx$ of a prismatic beam cut between two adjacent cross-sections **AB** and **CD**.

Let the beam be subjected to a **pure bending moment** $M$ (constant along the segment) as shown in the figure inside Section 4.

Because of pure bending:
- The originally straight longitudinal fibers deform into **circular arcs**.
- All arcs share a **common center of curvature O**.
- The radius from O to the neutral axis is **R**.
- Cross-sections AB and CD rotate relative to each other by a small angle $d\theta$.

---

## 🔸 Step 1 — Geometry of Deformation (Kinematic Step)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The fundamental kinematic fact: **Cross-sections remain plane and rotate by the same angle $d\theta$** (Euler–Bernoulli hypothesis).

Let the **neutral axis** (the line where fibers do not change length) be denoted **NX**. By definition:

$$\text{Length of neutral axis} = R \cdot d\theta = dx$$

Now consider a longitudinal fiber located at a perpendicular distance $y$ from the neutral axis (positive $y$ above NA, negative $y$ below NA).

The radius of the arc traced by this fiber is $(R + y)$ if $y > 0$ (above NA) and $(R - \vert y \vert)$ if $y < 0$. In compact notation:

$$\text{Original length of fiber } AB' = dx = R \cdot d\theta$$
