$$\text{Deformed length of fiber } A'B'' = (R + y) \cdot d\theta$$

### 1.1 Change in Length

$$\Delta L = (R+y)\,d\theta - R\,d\theta = y\,d\theta$$

### 1.2 Longitudinal Strain

The engineering strain $\varepsilon$ in the fiber is the ratio of change in length to original length:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L} = \frac{y\,d\theta}{R\,d\theta}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = \frac{y}{R}} \quad \text{(Geometric / Kinematic Result)}$$

> **Interpretation:** Strain is **purely linear** in $y$. Fibers farther from the neutral axis experience larger strain.

---

## 🔸 Step 2 — Stress from Hooke's Law (Constitutive Step)

Since the material is homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic, **Hooke's Law** applies:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

Substituting $\varepsilon = y/R$:

$$\boxed{\sigma = \frac{E\,y}{R}} \quad \text{(Mechanical / Constitutive Result)}$$

> **Interpretation:** Bending stress varies **linearly** across the depth of the cross-section. It is **zero at the neutral axis** ($y=0$) and **maximum at the extreme outer fiber** ($y = c$, where $c$ is the distance from NA to the outermost fiber).

---

## 🔸 Step 3 — Force Equilibrium (Locating the Neutral Axis)

Consider an elemental area $dA$ at distance $y$ from the neutral axis. The elemental axial force on this area is:

$$dF = \sigma \cdot dA = \frac{E\,y}{R}\,dA$$

For **pure bending**, there is **no net axial force** on the cross-section (only internal moment). Therefore:

$$\int_A \sigma\,dA = 0$$

Substituting:

$$\int_A \frac{E\,y}{R}\,dA = 0$$

Since $E$ and $R$ are constants (for a given section and load), they can be taken outside:

$$\frac{E}{R} \int_A y\,dA = 0$$

The integral $\int_A y\,dA$ is, by definition, the **first moment of area** about the neutral axis. For this product to vanish:

$$\int_A y\,dA = 0$$

This can only be true if the neutral axis passes through the **centroid (G)** of the cross-section.

$$\boxed{\therefore \text{Neutral Axis} \equiv \text{Centroidal Axis of the Cross-Section}}$$

> **Engineering Consequence:** Whenever a robotic link has a composite or unsymmetric cross-section, the designer must first locate the centroid before applying $\sigma = My/I$.

---

## 🔸 Step 4 — Moment Equilibrium (Building the Master Equation)

The elemental stress $\sigma$ acting on area $dA$ at distance $y$ produces an elemental moment about the neutral axis:

$$dM = (\sigma \cdot dA) \cdot y = \frac{E\,y}{R}\,y\,dA = \frac{E\,y^{2}}{R}\,dA$$

The total internal resisting moment at the section is obtained by integrating over the entire cross-sectional area $A$:

$$M = \int_A dM = \int_A \frac{E\,y^{2}}{R}\,dA$$

Since $E$ and $R$ are constant across the section:

$$M = \frac{E}{R} \int_A y^{2}\,dA$$

The integral $\int_A y^{2}\,dA$ is, by definition, the **Second Moment of Area** (Area Moment of Inertia) about the neutral axis, denoted $I$:

$$I = \int_A y^{2}\,dA$$

Substituting back:

$$\boxed{M = \frac{E\,I}{R}}$$

This is the **flexural rigidity equation** — it links the applied bending moment to the resulting curvature of the beam.

---

## 🔸 Step 5 — Combining All Three Results into the Master Equation

We now have three independent results:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{y}{R}, \qquad \sigma = \frac{E\,y}{R}, \qquad M = \frac{E\,I}{R}$$

From the second equation, solving for $y/R$:

$$\frac{y}{R} = \frac{\sigma}{E}$$

From the third equation, solving for $1/R$:

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{M}{E\,I}$$

Multiplying both sides of the second by $1/y$ and the third by $E/M$ appropriately, we can write the **unified triple-relation**:

$$\frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{M}{I} = \frac{E}{R}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is THE Bending Equation.** Memorize it in this exact form — it appears in 9 out of 10 KTU structural analysis problems. From this single relation, you can derive:
> - Bending stress at any depth: $\sigma = \dfrac{M\,y}{I}$
> - Maximum bending stress: $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M\,c}{I} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$
> - Radius of curvature: $R = \dfrac{E\,I}{M}$
> - Strain at any depth: $\varepsilon = \dfrac{M\,y}{E\,I}$

---

## 🔸 Step 6 — Section Modulus (Design Form)

Defining **Section Modulus** $Z = I/c$, where $c$ is the distance from the neutral axis to the extreme fiber:

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M}{Z}$$

> **Design Criterion (KTU Frequently Asked):** For safe elastic design, $\sigma_{\max} \le \sigma_{\text{allowable}} = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{yield}}}{\text{Factor of Safety (FoS)}}$.

$$\boxed{M_{\max} = Z \cdot \sigma_{\text{allowable}}}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 🔹 Diagram 1 — Pure Bending Geometry (Block-Level Functional Flow)

The figure below maps the **flow of derivation** and the **physical interactions** within a bending beam segment. Mermaid is used to render a clean architectural flow because physical 3D bending is best represented schematically.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Beam Segment dx in Pure Bending] --> B[Neutral Axis NX Remains Unstretched]
    A --> C[Fiber at distance y from NA]
    C --> D[Radius of arc becomes R + y]
    D --> E[Original length dx equals R d theta]
    D --> F[Deformed length equals R plus y d theta]
    F --> G[Strain epsilon equals y over R]
    G --> H[Hookes Law sigma equals E epsilon]
    H --> I[Stress sigma equals E y over R]
    A --> J[Elemental force dF equals sigma dA]
    J --> K[Force equilibrium integral of sigma dA equals 0]
    K --> L[Neutral Axis passes through Centroid G]
    A --> M[Elemental moment dM equals sigma y dA]
    M --> N[Moment equilibrium M equals E I over R]
    N --> O[BENDING EQUATION sigma over y equals M over I equals E over R]
    I --> O
```

---

## 🔹 Diagram 2 — Stress Distribution Across a Rectangular Beam Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Top Extreme Fiber] --> Q[Linear Stress Variation]
    Q --> R[Neutral Axis sigma equals 0]
    R --> S[Linear Stress Variation]
    S --> T[Bottom Extreme Fiber]
    P --> U[Maximum Compression sigma max negative]
    T --> V[Maximum Tension sigma max positive]
    Q --> W[Gradient equals M over I]
    S --> W
```

---

## 🔹 Diagram 3 — Curvature & Deformed Shape (Robot Arm Analogy)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Original Straight Beam of length L] --> Y[Apply Bending Moment M at Section]
    Y --> Z[Beam deforms into Circular Arc]
    Z --> AA[Radius of Curvature R equals EI over M]
    Z --> AB[Center of Curvature O on concave side]
    Z --> AC[Top fibers shorten in Compression]
    Z --> AD[Bottom fibers lengthen in Tension]
    Z --> AE[Neutral Layer unchanged]
```

---

## 🔹 Diagram 4 — Decision Logic for Solving Bending Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Read the problem statement] --> S2[Identify beam type and supports]
    S2 --> S3[Compute Reactions]
    S3 --> S4[Draw BMD to find Maximum M]
    S4 --> S5[Locate Neutral Axis Centroid]
    S5 --> S6[Compute I about NA]
    S6 --> S7[Apply M over I equals sigma over y]
    S7 --> S8[Compute stress at required depth y]
    S8 --> S9[Compare with allowable stress]
    S9 --> S10{Is sigma less than sigma allowable}
    S10 -- Yes --> S11[Design is SAFE]
    S10 -- No --> S12[REDESIGN - increase I or Z]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading aid:** In all four diagrams, the flow proceeds top-to-bottom or left-to-right. Each box represents one logical step that corresponds directly to a line in the Section 3 derivation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🔸 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**State the assumptions made in the derivation of the bending equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The five fundamental assumptions of the Euler–Bernoulli bending theory are:

1. The beam material is **homogeneous and isotropic** — uniform properties throughout and in all directions.
2. The material is **linearly elastic** and obeys **Hooke's Law** ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$).
3. The beam is **initially straight**, **prismatic** (uniform cross-section), and unloaded.
4. **Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral surface** after bending (negligible shear deformation).
5. The beam is subjected to **pure bending** — bending moment is constant and shear force is zero at the section.

> **Mark split:** 1 mark for stating each valid assumption (any 3 for full 3 marks as per KTU 2024 valuation key).

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define the term 'neutral axis' as applied to bending of beams. Show that the neutral axis of a beam in pure bending passes through the centroid of the cross-section.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The *neutral axis* of a beam cross-section is the locus of points where the longitudinal fibres experience **zero change in length** during bending, and consequently **zero bending stress**.

**Proof (2 marks):** From the derivation of the bending equation, the longitudinal strain at distance $y$ is $\varepsilon = y/R$. Using Hooke's Law, $\sigma = E\,y/R$.

For pure bending, the resultant axial force on the cross-section must vanish:

$$\int_A \sigma\,dA = \frac{E}{R}\int_A y\,dA = 0$$

Since $E \ne 0$ and $R \ne \infty$ (for a bent beam), we require $\int_A y\,dA = 0$. This integral is the first moment of area about the neutral axis. It can equal zero only when the axis passes through the **centroid (G)** of the cross-section.

---

## 🔸 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE guidelines, every Module in the question paper contains an **internal choice**: students must answer **either Question A or Question B**, but not both. Both options below carry equal weight (7 + 7 marks).

---

### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]

A simply-supported robot manipulator link of **span $L = 1.5\;\text{m}$** carries a uniformly distributed load of **$w = 4\;\text{kN/m}$** (representing its own mass plus cabling). The link has a **rectangular hollow cross-section** of outer dimensions $B \times H = 80\;\text{mm} \times 120\;\text{mm}$ and wall thickness $t = 8\;\text{mm}$. The material is **Aluminium 6061-T6** with Young's modulus $E = 70\;\text{GPa}$ and yield stress $\sigma_y = 240\;\text{MPa}$. Assume a Factor of Safety of 2.0.

#### **(a) Determine the maximum bending moment and the location of the neutral axis. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Reactions:**
By symmetry of a simply-supported beam under UDL:

$$R_A = R_B = \frac{wL}{2} = \frac{4 \times 1.5}{2} = 3.0\;\text{kN}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Bending Moment:**
Maximum bending moment for simply-supported beam under UDL occurs at mid-span:

$$M_{\max} = \frac{wL^{2}}{8} = \frac{4 \times (1.5)^{2}}{8} = 1.125\;\text{kN·m} = 1.125 \times 10^{6}\;\text{N·mm}$$

**Step 3 — Location of Neutral Axis:**
For a hollow rectangular section with $B = 80\;\text{mm}$, $H = 120\;\text{mm}$, $t = 8\;\text{mm}$:
- Inner dimensions: $b = B - 2t = 80 - 16 = 64\;\text{mm}$
- Inner height: $h = H - 2t = 120 - 16 = 104\;\text{mm}$

Because of symmetry about both horizontal and vertical axes, the centroid lies at the **geometric center** of the section, i.e.:

$$y_{\text{NA from bottom}} = \frac{H}{2} = 60\;\text{mm}$$

Therefore, **NA coincides with the horizontal centroidal axis** at the mid-height. ✓

**Answer:** $M_{\max} = 1.125\;\text{kN·m}$; NA at 60 mm from extreme fibers.

#### **(b) Calculate the maximum bending stress and check whether the link is safe under yielding. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Moment of Inertia about NA:**

$$I = \frac{B\,H^{3} - b\,h^{3}}{12} = \frac{80 \times (120)^{3} - 64 \times (104)^{3}}{12}$$

Numerator evaluation:

$$80 \times 1{,}728{,}000 = 138{,}240{,}000\;\text{mm}^{4}$$

$$64 \times 1{,}124{,}864 = 71{,}991{,}296\;\text{mm}^{4}$$

Difference:

$$\Delta = 138{,}240{,}000 - 71{,}991{,}296 = 66{,}248{,}704\;\text{mm}^{4}$$

$$I = \frac{66{,}248{,}704}{12} = 5{,}520{,}725.33\;\text{mm}^{4}$$

**Step 2 — Distance to extreme fiber:**

$$c = \frac{H}{2} = 60\;\text{mm}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum bending stress using $M/I = \sigma/y$:**

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M_{\max} \cdot c}{I} = \frac{1{,}125{,}000 \times 60}{5{,}520{,}725.33}$$

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{67{,}500{,}000}{5{,}520{,}725.33} = 12.227\;\text{N/mm}^{2} \approx 12.23\;\text{MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Allowable stress:**

$$\sigma_{\text{allow}} = \frac{\sigma_y}{\text{FoS}} = \frac{240}{2.0} = 120\;\text{MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Safety check:**

$$\sigma_{\max} = 12.23\;\text{MPa} \;<\; \sigma_{\text{allow}} = 120\;\text{MPa} \quad \checkmark$$

The link is **SAFE** under yielding. (In fact, it is heavily over-designed — the FoS achieved is $\approx 19.6$, suggesting a weight-optimization opportunity.)

---

### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B — Alternative Choice]

The cantilever gripper arm of a pick-and-place robot is $L = 250\;\text{mm}$ long and carries a concentrated load $P = 150\;\text{N}$ at its free end (parallel to gravity). The cross-section is a **solid circle of diameter $d = 25\;\text{mm}$**. The material is **AISI 304 Stainless Steel** with $E = 200\;\text{GPa}$ and $\sigma_y = 215\;\text{MPa}$.

#### **(a) Derive an expression for the maximum bending stress at the fixed end of the cantilever and compute its numerical value. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Bending moment at fixed end (pure bending + shear, but bending dominates):**

$$M_{\max} = P \cdot L = 150 \times 250 = 37{,}500\;\text{N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Section properties (solid circular section):**

$$I = \frac{\pi\,d^{4}}{64} = \frac{\pi \times (25)^{4}}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 390{,}625}{64} = 19{,}175.07\;\text{mm}^{4}$$

$$c = \frac{d}{2} = 12.5\;\text{mm}$$

$$Z = \frac{I}{c} = \frac{\pi\,d^{3}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times (25)^{3}}{32} = 1533.98\;\text{mm}^{3}$$

**Step 3 — Apply bending equation:**

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M_{\max}}{Z} = \frac{37{,}500}{1533.98} = 24.45\;\text{MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Verification using $M/I = \sigma/y$:**

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M_{\max} \cdot c}{I} = \frac{37{,}500 \times 12.5}{19{,}175.07} = \frac{468{,}750}{19{,}175.07} = 24.45\;\text{MPa} \quad \checkmark$$

**Answer:** $\sigma_{\max} = 24.45\;\text{MPa}$ at the top and bottom extreme fibers of the fixed end.

#### **(b) Determine the radius of curvature of the bent gripper arm at the fixed end and the maximum deflection at the free end. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Radius of curvature from $M/EI = 1/R$:**

$$R = \frac{E\,I}{M_{\max}} = \frac{200 \times 10^{3} \times 19{,}175.07}{37{,}500}$$

$$R = \frac{3.835 \times 10^{9}}{37{,}500} = 102{,}267\;\text{mm} = 102.27\;\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Verification via $E/R = M/I$:**

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{M}{E\,I} = \frac{37{,}500}{3.835 \times 10^{9}} = 9.78 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{mm}^{-1}$$

$$R = 102{,}267\;\text{mm} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Maximum deflection of cantilever with end load:**

$$\delta_{\max} = \frac{P\,L^{3}}{3\,E\,I} = \frac{150 \times (250)^{3}}{3 \times 200{,}000 \times 19{,}175.07}$$

Numerator: $150 \times 15{,}625{,}000 = 2.34375 \times 10^{9}$

Denominator: $3 \times 200{,}000 \times 19{,}175.07 = 1.1505 \times 10^{10}$

$$\delta_{\max} = \frac{2.34375 \times 10^{9}}{1.1505 \times 10^{10}} = 0.2037\;\text{mm} \approx 0.204\;\text{mm}$$

**Step 4 — Allowable deflection check (typical robotic gripper: $\delta_{\text{allow}} = 0.5\;\text{mm}$):**

$$\delta_{\max} = 0.204\;\text{mm} \;<\; \delta_{\text{allow}} = 0.5\;\text{mm} \quad \checkmark$$

**Answers:**
- Radius of curvature $R = 102.27\;\text{m}$
- Maximum deflection $\delta_{\max} = 0.204\;\text{mm}$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Units mismatch (most common, 1–2 marks lost):** If $E$ is in GPa, all lengths must be in **mm** and forces in **N**. Never mix m and mm in the same calculation.
> 2. **Misplaced neutral axis:** For *unsymmetric* sections (e.g., T-section, channel section), students forget that NA is **not** at the geometric mid-height — it must be located using $\bar{y} = \sum A_i y_i / \sum A_i$. KTU examiners deduct 2 marks flat for this.
> 3. **Using $M_{\text{at distance } x}$ instead of $M_{\max}$:** Many students substitute the moment at an arbitrary section, not realizing the question asks for *maximum* stress.
> 4. **Confusing bending stress with shear stress:** The equation $M/I = \sigma/y$ gives **normal** bending stress only. For transverse shear, use $\tau = VQ/(Ib)$ — a different formula entirely.
> 5. **Forgetting the factor of safety division:** Marks are lost when $\sigma_y$ is used directly instead of $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = \sigma_y / \text{FoS}$.
> 6. **Sign convention of $y$:** Always state explicitly whether $y$ is measured above (+) or below (–) the NA, especially in Part B derivations where the examiner checks for clarity.

---

## 🔹 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute KTU 2024 revision sheet — read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ The **bending equation** is $\dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{E}{R}$. Memorize it verbatim.
- ✅ It is valid **only for pure bending** (constant $M$, zero $V$) in the **elastic range** of a **homogeneous isotropic** material.
- ✅ The **plane sections remain plane** assumption is the kinematic foundation; **Hooke's Law** is the constitutive foundation.
- ✅ **Neutral axis = Centroidal axis** (proved by setting net axial force to zero).
- ✅ Maximum bending stress occurs at the **extreme fiber**, $c = $ distance from NA to outermost point.
- ✅ **Section modulus** $Z = I/c$ — a pure geometric property used in design: $\sigma_{\max} = M/Z$.
- ✅ For composite / unsymmetric sections, locate the centroid first; then compute $I$ about that axis using the **parallel axis theorem**.
- ✅ Curvature of the bent beam: $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ — used to derive slope and deflection equations.
- ✅ In robot-link numericals, $E$ is typically **70 GPa (Al)** or **200 GPa (Steel)**, lengths in **mm**, moments in **N·mm**.
- ✅ Always perform a **safety check**: $\sigma_{\max} \le \sigma_y / \text{FoS}$ AND $\delta_{\max} \le \delta_{\text{allowable}}$.
- ✅ Common section properties to memorize: rectangle $I = bh^{3}/12$, circle $I = \pi d^{4}/64$, hollow rectangle $I = (BH^{3} - bh^{3})/12$.
- ✅ Drawing a clear **SFD and BMD** is a 2-mark "free gift" in Part B — never skip it.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Neutral Axis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT525 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS OF ROBOTIC PARTS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Neutral Axis # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Neutral Axis (N.A.)** is defined as the line (or surface, in 3D) within the cross-section of a beam or structural member subjected to pure bending, where the **longitudinal fibre strain is zero**, and consequently, the **bending stress is also zero**. Mathematically, the neutral axis is the locus of points on the cross-section where: $$\sigma = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \epsilon = 0$$ In a curved beam under bending, the neutral axis is the line that passes through the **center of curvature** of the deformed beam axis and is perpendicular to the plane of bending. For homogeneous, symmetric, and prismatic beams, the neutral axis always passes through the **centroid of the cross-section**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition) > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Pick up a flexible plastic ruler (or a thin wooden scale) and hold it horizontally with both hands. Now press the two ends gently toward each other so that the ruler bows upward into a gentle "U" shape. > > - The **top surface** of the ruler is being **squeezed together** (compression). > - The **bottom surface** is being **stretched apart** (tension). > - Somewhere **inside the ruler**, there is a thin layer that is neither squeezed nor stretched — its length remains exactly the same as before you bent it. > > That invisible, un-stretched internal layer is the **Neutral Axis**. In the context of **robotic parts**, every link of a robot arm, every gripper finger, and every joint housing behaves exactly like that ruler. When a robot lifts a payload, the link bends slightly, and the neutral axis becomes the critical reference line that separates the compressed zone (used to design against buckling/crushing) from the tensed zone (used to design against yielding/fracture). ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Engineering Metrics > [!NOTE] > - **Young's Modulus (E)** of common robotic materials: Steel ≈ **200 GPa**, Aluminium ≈ **70 GPa**, Carbon-Fibre Composite ≈ **70–150 GPa**, Titanium ≈ **110 GPa**. > - Standard bending relation: $\frac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ where the radius of curvature $R$ is the distance from the neutral axis to the center of curvature. > - The neutral axis is also called the **Zero-Stress Line (ZSL)** in advanced finite element analysis (FEA) of robotic manipulators. ## 1.4 Significance in Robotic Structural Analysis > [!IMPORTANT] > For a robot arm, identifying the neutral axis is non-negotiable because: > 1. It determines the **moment of inertia (I)** used in deflection and stress calculations. > 2. For **composite links** (e.g., aluminium core with carbon-fibre skin), the neutral axis does NOT pass through the geometric centroid — it must be located using the **Transformed Section Method**. > 3. For **unsymmetric cross-sections** (e.g., L-, T-, or channel sections used in lightweight robotic arms), the **principal axes** must first be identified, and the neutral axis aligns with the principal axis about which bending occurs. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Assumptions of Pure Bending Theory (Euler–Bernoulli Beam Theory) To derive the location and behavior of the neutral axis, the following assumptions (mandatory at KTU 2024 board level) are invoked: - The beam material is **homogeneous** and **isotropic** (same E in all directions) — for composites, a *transformed* section is used. - The beam is initially **straight** and **prismatic** (uniform cross-section). - The cross-section remains **plane** and **perpendicular to the deflected neutral axis** after bending (plane sections remain plane). - The beam experiences **pure bending** (constant bending moment, zero shear force). - The radius of curvature $R$ is **large** compared to cross-sectional dimensions (small deflection theory). - Self-weight and shear deformation are **neglected**. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Why the Neutral Axis Exists 1. **Pure bending moment $M$** is applied at a section of the beam. 2. The beam deforms into a circular arc of radius $R$. 3. Fibres **above** the neutral axis shorten → **compressive stress**. 4. Fibres **below** the neutral axis elongate → **tensile stress**. 5. The **force equilibrium** condition $\sum F_x = 0$ requires that total compressive force = total tensile force. 6. This equilibrium uniquely defines a horizontal line where stress = 0, i.e., the **neutral axis**. 7. The **moment equilibrium** condition $\sum M = 0$ then yields the bending moment formula and the radius of curvature. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **exam-ready formula set** for the Neutral Axis topic. Memorize the boundary conditions carefully. | **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Description / Units** | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Bending Strain at distance $y$ from N.A. | $\epsilon = \dfrac{y}{R}$ | Dimensionless; $y$ in m, $R$ in m | | 2 | Bending Stress (Flexure Formula) | $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Pa (N/m²) | | 3 | Master Bending Equation | $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ | Fundamental KTU relation | | 4 | Radius of Curvature | $R = \dfrac{E \cdot I}{M}$ | Metres (m) | | 5 | Section Modulus | $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{\max}}$ | m³ | | 6 | Maximum Bending Stress | $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Pa | | 7 | Curvature | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{E \cdot I}$ | m⁻¹ | | 8 | Neutral Axis (homogeneous beam) | Passes through **centroid** | — | | 9 | Neutral Axis (composite, transformed) | $\bar{y} = \dfrac{\sum A_i \cdot y_i \cdot n_i}{\sum A_i \cdot n_i}$ | $n_i = E_i / E_{ref}$ | | 10 | Moment–Curvature Relation | $M = E \cdot I \cdot \dfrac{1}{R}$ | N·m | > [!NOTE] > In the formulas above, $y$ is measured from the **neutral axis**, $I$ is the **second moment of area** about the neutral axis, and $M$ is the **bending moment**. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility in Robotics - **Robot Link Design:** Predicts deflection of a manipulator arm under payload to ensure positional accuracy (e.g., ±0.1 mm repeatability in industrial robots). - **Fatigue Life Estimation:** Maximum bending stress $\sigma_{\max}$ (at the extreme fibre from the N.A.) governs fatigue cycles for grippers and end-effectors. - **Composite Manipulator Arms:** For carbon-fibre reinforced polymer (CFRP) robot links, locating the shifted neutral axis is essential to prevent premature delamination. - **Compliant Mechanisms:** Soft robots and flexure bearings deliberately use the neutral axis to create controlled, frictionless pivot motion. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Location of the Neutral Axis from First Principles ### 3.1.1 Geometric Setup Consider a small element of length $dx$ cut from a beam subjected to pure bending. Before bending, all longitudinal fibres have the same length. After bending, the beam axis deforms into a circular arc of radius $R$, and the neutral axis of length $dx$ remains unchanged. Let: - $AB$ = original neutral axis length $= dx$ - $A'B'$ = length of a fibre at distance $y$ from the neutral axis after bending - $y$ = perpendicular distance from the neutral axis (positive upward, negative downward) - $R$ = radius of curvature of the neutral axis - $R + y$ = radius of curvature of the fibre at distance $y$ ### 3.1.2 Deriving the Strain Distribution Since $A'B' = (R + y) \cdot d\theta$ and $AB = R \cdot d\theta$: The change in length of the fibre is: $$\Delta L = A'B' - AB = (R + y) \cdot d\theta - R \cdot d\theta = y \cdot d\theta$$ The original length is $L = R \cdot d\theta$. Therefore, the longitudinal strain is: $$\epsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L} = \frac{y \cdot d\theta}{R \cdot d\theta} = \frac{y}{R}$$ > **Explanation:** The strain varies **linearly** with distance $y$ from the neutral axis. At the neutral axis ($y=0$), strain is zero. Above it, it is tensile (or compressive, depending on sign convention); below it, opposite. ### 3.1.3 Applying Hooke's Law to Get the Stress Distribution By Hooke's Law for linear elastic material: $$\sigma = E \cdot \epsilon = E \cdot \frac{y}{R}$$ ### 3.1.4 Applying Force Equilibrium to Locate the Neutral Axis The total internal axial force on the cross-section must be zero (pure bending, no axial load): $$\int_A \sigma \, dA = 0$$ Substituting the stress expression: $$\int_A E \cdot \frac{y}{R} \, dA = 0$$ Since $E$ and $R$ are constants for a given section: $$\frac{E}{R} \int_A y \, dA = 0$$ For a non-trivial solution (i.e., $E/R \neq 0$): $$\int_A y \, dA = 0$$ By the definition of the **centroid**: $$\int_A y \, dA = A \cdot \bar{y}$$ where $\bar{y}$ is the distance of the centroid from the reference axis. Therefore: $$A \cdot \bar{y} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \bar{y} = 0$$ > **Conclusion (Key Result):** The neutral axis of a **homogeneous beam** in pure bending passes exactly through the **centroid of the cross-section**. ### 3.1.5 Deriving the Master Bending Equation via Moment Equilibrium The internal bending moment $M$ about the neutral axis must equal the moment of the stress distribution about the same axis: $$M = \int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA$$ Substitute $\sigma = E \cdot y / R$: $$M = \int_A \left( E \cdot \frac{y}{R} \right) \cdot y \, dA = \frac{E}{R} \int_A y^2 \, dA$$ By definition, $\int_A y^2 \, dA = I$ (the second moment of area about the neutral axis). Therefore: $$M = \frac{E \cdot I}{R}$$ Combining this with $\sigma = E y / R$ and $M = E I / R$ to eliminate $E/R$: $$\boxed{\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}}$$ This is the **Euler–Bernoulli Master Bending Equation** — the single most important relation in KTU bending theory. ## 3.2 Derivation: Neutral Axis of a Composite (Transformed) Section ### 3.2.1 Problem Context In robotic arms, links are often made of multiple materials (e.g., steel core + carbon-fibre skin). For such a **composite section**, the neutral axis does **NOT** pass through the geometric centroid. We must use the **Transformed Section Method**. ### 3.2.2 Transformation Procedure Let: - Material 1 have modulus $E_1$ and area $A_1$ - Material 2 have modulus $E_2$ and area $A_2$ - Reference modulus be $E_{ref}$ (usually the dominant material) - Modular ratios: $n_1 = E_1 / E_{ref}$ and $n_2 = E_2 / E_{ref}$ The transformed area of each material is: $$A_1' = n_1 \cdot A_1 \quad \text{and} \quad A_2' = n_2 \cdot A_2$$ The width of material 2 is transformed as $b_2' = n_2 \cdot b_2$, while its height remains unchanged. ### 3.2.3 Locating the Neutral Axis The neutral axis of the transformed (homogenized) section passes through the centroid of the transformed area: $$\bar{y} = \frac{\sum (A_i' \cdot y_i)}{\sum A_i'} = \frac{\sum (n_i \cdot A_i \cdot y_i)}{\sum (n_i \cdot A_i)}$$ where $y_i$ is the distance of the centroid of the $i$-th component from an arbitrary reference axis (e.g., the top or bottom of the section). ### 3.2.4 Transformed Moment of Inertia The moment of inertia of the composite section about the neutral axis is: $$I_{transformed} = \sum \left[ I_{i,\text{own}} + A_i' \cdot d_i^2 \right]$$ where $I_{i,\text{own}}$ is the moment of inertia of the transformed area of the $i$-th part about its own centroidal axis, and $d_i$ is the distance from the $i$-th component's centroid to the **new neutral axis**. > **Engineering Note:** Once $I_{transformed}$ is known, the bending stress in the *original* material $i$ is recovered by multiplying the stress in the transformed material by the modular ratio $n_i$: > $$\sigma_i = n_i \cdot \frac{M \cdot y}{I_{transformed}}$$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Locating Neutral Axis in Common Robotic Cross-Sections | **Cross-Section Type** | **Robotic Application** | **N.A. Location** | **Key Formula** | |---|---|---|---| | Rectangular (solid) | Gripper jaws, finger links | Through geometric centroid | $\bar{y} = h/2$ from base | | Circular (solid) | Revolute joint pins, shaft | Through geometric center | $\bar{y} = D/2$ from bottom | | I-Section (symmetric) | Lightweight robot links | Through web centroid | $\bar{y}$ at web mid-height | | T-Section (unsymmetric) | Compact end-effectors | NOT at geometric center | $\bar{y} = \dfrac{\sum A_i y_i}{\sum A_i}$ | | L-Section (unsymmetric) | Bracket arms, mounting plates | Requires principal axis analysis | $\bar{y} = \dfrac{\sum A_i y_i}{\sum A_i}$ | | Composite (steel + CFRP) | High-performance robot links | Shifted toward stiffer material | $\bar{y} = \dfrac{\sum n_i A_i y_i}{\sum n_i A_i}$ | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Bending of a Beam and Stress Distribution Across the Neutral Axis > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid block diagrams the physical bending behavior of a robotic link under a transverse load, showing the formation of the neutral axis, tension zone, and compression zone. ```mermaid graph TD A[Straight Robotic Link
Before Bending] --> B[Apply Transverse Load
at Free End] B --> C[Beam Deforms into
Circular Arc] C --> D{Identify Three Zones} D --> E[Top Fibres
SHORTEN
Compression Zone] D --> F[Neutral Axis
NO STRAIN
NO STRESS] D --> G[Bottom Fibres
LENGTHEN
Tension Zone] E --> H[Stress Distribution
Linear with y] F --> H G --> H H --> I[Bending Stress Formula
sigma = M times y over I] I --> J[Section Modulus Z
Z = I over y max] J --> K[Max Stress Check
for Robotic Link] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture for Neutral Axis Analysis in Robotics > [!IMPORTANT] > This block diagram maps the **complete engineering workflow** for analyzing a robotic link using neutral axis theory — from geometry definition to final deflection check. ```mermaid graph TD stepA[Step 1: Define Robotic Link Geometry] --> stepB[Step 2: Identify Material Properties E] stepB --> stepC{Is Section Composite or Homogeneous?} stepC -->|Homogeneous| stepD[Step 3A: Locate Centroid via Area Moments] stepC -->|Composite| stepE[Step 3B: Apply Transformed Section Method] stepD --> stepF[Step 4: Compute Second Moment of Area I about N.A.] stepE --> stepF stepF --> stepG[Step 5: Apply Load and Compute Bending Moment M at Critical Section] stepG --> stepH[Step 6: Calculate Bending Stress sigma = M y over I] stepH --> stepI[Step 7: Calculate Radius of Curvature R = E I over M] stepI --> stepJ[Step 8: Integrate Curvature to Find Deflection of Robotic Link] stepJ --> stepK[Step 9: Compare Deflection with KTU Tolerance Limits] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Stress Variation Across Cross-Section (Visual) ```mermaid graph LR pA[Extreme Top Fibre
Maximum Compression] --> pB[Linear Decrease through Section] pB --> pC[Neutral Axis
Stress equals Zero] pC --> pD[Linear Increase Below] pD --> pE[Extreme Bottom Fibre
Maximum Tension] ``` > **Reading the Diagram:** The stress varies **linearly** from a maximum compressive value at the top, passes through **zero at the neutral axis**, and reaches a maximum tensile value at the bottom. The slope of this variation is governed by $M/I$. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Q:** Define the term *neutral axis* as applied to a beam subjected to pure bending. Why does it always pass through the centroid for a homogeneous beam? **Model Answer:** > The neutral axis of a beam under pure bending is the line in the cross-section where the longitudinal fibre strain is zero, and consequently the bending stress is zero. The fibres above the neutral axis are in compression, and those below are in tension. > > For a homogeneous, isotropic beam under pure bending, the force equilibrium equation $\int_A \sigma \, dA = 0$ reduces to $\int_A y \, dA = 0$, which by definition of the centroid gives $\bar{y} = 0$. Hence, the neutral axis passes through the centroid of the cross-section. **(3 marks)** --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q:** A circular robotic link of diameter $40$ mm is subjected to a bending moment of $200$ N·m. Calculate the maximum bending stress in the link. **Model Answer:** > Given: $D = 40$ mm $= 0.04$ m, $M = 200$ N·m. > > Maximum distance from neutral axis: $y_{\max} = D/2 = 0.02$ m. > > Second moment of area for solid circular section: $I = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{64} = \dfrac{\pi (0.04)^4}{64}$. > > Calculation: $I = \dfrac{\pi \times 2.56 \times 10^{-6}}{64} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-7}$ m⁴. > > Maximum bending stress: $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M \cdot y_{\max}}{I} = \dfrac{200 \times 0.02}{1.2566 \times 10^{-7}} = 3.183 \times 10^{7}$ Pa $\approx$ **31.83 MPa**. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Read Carefully!** > > 1. **Sign Convention Blunder:** Students frequently forget to assign the correct sign (tension positive / compression negative) to $y$ while substituting into the flexure formula. KTU examiners deduct **2 marks** for this. > 2. **Unit Conversion:** $D$ in mm and $M$ in N·m — students must convert to consistent SI units (metres) before computing $I$. A wrong unit conversion costs **1 mark**. > 3. **For Composite Sections:** Forgetting the modular ratio $n_i = E_i / E_{ref}$ while computing the transformed area is the #1 mark-loser — penalty of up to **3 marks**. > 4. **Confusing Section Modulus $Z$ with Moment of Inertia $I$:** These are different ($Z$ has units of m³, $I$ has units of m⁴). Examiners specifically test this — deduct **1 mark** if misquoted. ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]` **Q:** A robotic manipulator link is made of an I-section with the following dimensions: - Flange width: $b_f = 100$ mm - Flange thickness: $t_f = 15$ mm - Web height: $h_w = 170$ mm - Web thickness: $t_w = 10$ mm - Overall depth: $h = h_w + 2 t_f = 200$ mm The link is subjected to a bending moment of $M = 12$ kN·m at its critical section. Determine: **(a)** The location of the neutral axis. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The maximum tensile and compressive bending stresses. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** > **Part (a): Location of the Neutral Axis** > > Since the I-section is doubly symmetric, the neutral axis passes through the geometric centroid. Choosing the bottom edge as reference: > > - Flange 1 (bottom): $A_1 = 100 \times 15 = 1500$ mm², centroid at $y_1 = 7.5$ mm from bottom > - Web: $A_2 = 10 \times 170 = 1700$ mm², centroid at $y_2 = 15 + 85 = 100$ mm from bottom > - Flange 2 (top): $A_3 = 100 \times 15 = 1500$ mm², centroid at $y_3 = 15 + 170 + 7.5 = 192.5$ mm from bottom > > Total area: $A = 1500 + 1700 + 1500 = 4700$ mm². > > $$\bar{y} = \frac{1500 \times 7.5 + 1700 \times 100 + 1500 \times 192.5}{4700}$$ > > $$\bar{y} = \frac{11250 + 170000 + 288750}{4700} = \frac{470000}{4700} = 100 \text{ mm}$$ > > Since $\bar{y} = h/2 = 200/2 = 100$ mm, the **neutral axis lies at mid-depth, through the centroid**. **(7 marks)** > > --- > > **Part (b): Maximum Bending Stresses** > > Moment of inertia about the neutral axis (centroidal axis): > > $$I = \left[ \frac{b_f h^3}{12} - \frac{(b_f - t_w) h_w^3}{12} \right]$$ > > Substituting values (in mm): > > $$I = \left[ \frac{100 \times 200^3}{12} - \frac{(100 - 10) \times 170^3}{12} \right]$$ > > $$I = \left[ \frac{100 \times 8 \times 10^6}{12} - \frac{90 \times 4.913 \times 10^6}{12} \right]$$ > > $$I = 66.667 \times 10^6 - 36.8475 \times 10^6 = 29.8195 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$ > > $$I = 29.8195 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^4$$ > > Distance from N.A. to extreme fibre: $y_{\max} = h/2 = 0.1$ m. > > Bending moment: $M = 12$ kN·m $= 12 \times 10^3$ N·m. > > Maximum bending stress: > > $$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M \cdot y_{\max}}{I} = \frac{12 \times 10^3 \times 0.1}{29.8195 \times 10^{-6}}$$ > > $$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{1200}{29.8195 \times 10^{-6}} = 4.024 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa} \approx \mathbf{40.24 \text{ MPa}}$$ > > The **top fibre experiences maximum compression** of $-40.24$ MPa and the **bottom fibre experiences maximum tension** of $+40.24$ MPa (or vice versa, depending on the sign of $M$). **(7 marks)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q:** A composite robotic link consists of a steel core (rectangular, $50$ mm $\times$ $30$ mm) bonded on top with an aluminium strip ($50$ mm $\times$ $20$ mm). The link is subjected to a bending moment of $M = 5$ kN·m. Take $E_{steel} = 200$ GPa and $E_{aluminium} = 70$ GPa. Determine: **(a)** The location of the neutral axis of the composite section using the transformed section method. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The maximum bending stress developed in each material. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** > **Part (a): Neutral Axis Location** > > Reference modulus: $E_{ref} = E_{steel} = 200$ GPa. > > Modular ratios: > - $n_{steel} = E_{steel}/E_{ref} = 1$ > - $n_{aluminium} = E_{al}/E_{ref} = 70/200 = 0.35$ > > Transformed areas: > - Steel: $A'_{steel} = 1 \times (50 \times 30) = 1500$ mm² > - Aluminium: $A'_{al} = 0.35 \times (50 \times 20) = 350$ mm² > > Total transformed area: $A' = 1500 + 350 = 1850$ mm². > > Choosing the **bottom of the steel** as the reference axis: > - Steel centroid distance from bottom: $y_{steel} = 30/2 = 15$ mm > - Aluminium centroid distance from bottom: $y_{al} = 30 + 20/2 = 40$ mm > > $$\bar{y} = \frac{A'_{steel} \cdot y_{steel} + A'_{al} \cdot y_{al}}{A'_{steel} + A'_{al}}$$ > > $$\bar{y} = \frac{1500 \times 15 + 350 \times 40}{1850} = \frac{22500 + 14000}{1850} = \frac{36500}{1850}$$ > > $$\bar{y} \approx 19.73 \text{ mm from the bottom}$$ > > The **neutral axis is located 19.73 mm above the bottom** of the steel section (i.e., inside the steel layer, since $19.73 < 30$ mm). **(7 marks)** > > --- > > **Part (b): Maximum Bending Stresses** > > Transformed moment of inertia about the N.A.: > > $$I_{transformed} = \left[ I_{steel,own} + A'_{steel} \cdot d_{steel}^2 \right] + \left[ I_{al,own} + A'_{al} \cdot d_{al}^2 \right]$$ > > where: > - $I_{steel,own} = \dfrac{50 \times 30^3}{12} = 112500$ mm⁴ > - $I_{al,own} = \dfrac{50 \times 20^3}{12} = 33333.33$ mm⁴ > - $d_{steel} = \bar{y} - 15 = 19.73 - 15 = 4.73$ mm > - $d_{al} = 40 - 19.73 = 20.27$ mm > > Substituting: > > $$I_{transformed} = 112500 + 1500 \times (4.73)^2 + 33333.33 + 350 \times (20.27)^2$$ > > $$I_{transformed} = 112500 + 33561.4 + 33333.33 + 143852.2$$ > > $$I_{transformed} = 323246.9 \text{ mm}^4 = 3.2325 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4$$ > > Extreme fibre distances from N.A.: > - Top of aluminium: $y_{top} = 50 - 19.73 = 30.27$ mm $= 0.03027$ m > - Bottom of steel: $y_{bot} = 19.73$ mm $= 0.01973$ m > > Bending moment: $M = 5$ kN·m $= 5000$ N·m. > > **Stress in aluminium (extreme top fibre):** > > $$\sigma_{al,max} = n_{al} \cdot \frac{M \cdot y_{top}}{I_{transformed}} = 0.35 \times \frac{5000 \times 0.03027}{3.2325 \times 10^{-7}}$$ > > $$\sigma_{al,max} = 0.35 \times 4.683 \times 10^8 = \mathbf{1.639 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa} = 163.9 \text{ MPa}}$$ > > **Stress in steel (extreme bottom fibre):** > > $$\sigma_{steel,max} = n_{steel} \cdot \frac{M \cdot y_{bot}}{I_{transformed}} = 1 \times \frac{5000 \times 0.01973}{3.2325 \times 10^{-7}}$$ > > $$\sigma_{steel,max} = 3.052 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa} = \mathbf{305.2 \text{ MPa}}$$ > > The steel experiences the **larger stress** because it is the stiffer material and the extreme fibre is also farther (in transformed terms) from the neutral axis. **(7 marks)** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Bullets — Memorize Before the Exam** > > - **Neutral Axis (N.A.)** = line of **zero stress and zero strain** in a beam cross-section under pure bending. > - For **homogeneous, symmetric beams**, the N.A. **passes through the centroid** of the cross-section. > - For **composite beams**, use the **Transformed Section Method**: multiply each area by its **modular ratio** $n_i = E_i / E_{ref}$. > - The **N.A. shifts toward the stiffer material** in a composite section (because the stiffer material contributes more "effective area" in the transformed section). > - The **bending stress varies linearly** with distance $y$ from the N.A.: $\sigma = M y / I$. > - The **Master Bending Equation** $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ is the single most-tested relation in KTU exams. > - **Section Modulus** $Z = I / y_{\max}$ is a geometric property used to compute $\sigma_{\max} = M / Z$ quickly. > - **Radius of Curvature** $R = EI / M$ is used to compute **deflection** of robotic links via the double-integration or moment-area method. > - **Sign Convention:** Top fibres in compression (negative), bottom fibres in tension (positive) for a sagging moment — *or vice versa* if the moment is hogging. > - The N.A. is **perpendicular to the plane of bending** and passes through the **center of curvature** of the deformed beam. > - For **unsymmetric sections (L, T, Z)**, you must first find the **principal axes** before applying the bending equation; otherwise, **unsymmetric (skew) bending** occurs. > - The **neutral axis never experiences stress**, but it **does carry the full bending moment** through its moment of inertia $I$. > - **KTU 2024 Specific:** The Course Outcome (CO) mapped to this topic is **CO4 — "Analyse bending behaviour of robotic structural members under various loading conditions."** Always align your answer to this CO.

topics.title: Determination of Bending Stresses of Rectangular Section (Solid and Hollow)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT525 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS OF ROBOTIC PARTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Determination of Bending Stresses of Rectangular Section (Solid and Hollow)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Bending stress** (also called **flexural stress**) is the normal stress induced in a structural member when it is subjected to an external bending moment, causing one side of the member to elongate (tension) and the opposite side to shorten (compression). For a **rectangular cross-section** — both **solid** and **hollow** — the magnitude of bending stress at any fiber located at a perpendicular distance $y$ from the **neutral axis (N.A.)** is governed by the **Euler–Bernoulli flexure formula**:

$$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

where:
- $M$ = Bending moment at the section (N·m)
- $y$ = Distance of the fiber from the neutral axis (m)
- $I$ = Second moment of area (Area moment of inertia) about the neutral axis (m⁴)

The **maximum bending stress** occurs at the outermost fiber ($y = y_{\max}$), and is conventionally written using the **Section Modulus** ($Z$):

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M}{Z}, \quad \text{where} \quad Z = \frac{I}{y_{\max}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Highlight:** The derivation of the section modulus $Z$ for both **solid** ($Z = \dfrac{b \cdot h^2}{6}$) and **hollow** ($Z = \dfrac{B \cdot D^3 - b \cdot d^3}{6D}$) rectangular sections is a **high-weightage, frequently-asked concept** in ESE and module tests.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Diving Board / Wooden Ruler

Imagine you are at a swimming pool and you walk to the edge of a **diving board** (a flat wooden plank, much like a rectangular beam).

- The **top surface** of the plank is being **squeezed** — this is the **compressive zone** (negative bending stress).
- The **bottom surface** of the plank is being **stretched** — this is the **tensile zone** (positive bending stress).
- Somewhere in the middle, there is a thin horizontal layer that experiences **zero stress** and **no change in length** — this is the **Neutral Axis**.

The fibers farther away from the neutral axis (top and bottom edges) experience the **largest stretching or squeezing**. The exact same phenomenon happens inside a **robotic arm link**, the **chassis rail of a robot**, or the **forearm of a humanoid robot manipulator** — all of which are predominantly modeled as **rectangular cross-section beams**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Robotic Application:** The links of an industrial robotic arm (e.g., in a 6-DOF manipulator such as the KUKA KR series) are often **hollow rectangular extrusions** because a hollow section provides a **higher section modulus per unit mass** than a solid one — directly improving the **bending stress capacity-to-weight ratio**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit (SI) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bending moment | $M$ | N·m | Applied external moment |
| Section modulus | $Z$ | m³ | Geometric property only |
| Area moment of inertia | $I$ | m⁴ | Geometric property only |
| Distance from N.A. | $y$ | m | Perpendicular distance |
| Bending stress | $\sigma$ | Pa (N/m²) | Usually in MPa or N/mm² |
| Young's modulus | $E$ | GPa | Material property |
| Radius of curvature | $R$ | m | Deformed shape property |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick unit conversion to remember:** $1 \text{ MPa} = 1 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2$. KTU numerical problems often use $M$ in N·mm and dimensions in mm to give $\sigma$ directly in MPa.

---

## 1.4 Underlying Assumptions of Pure Bending Theory

For the formula $\sigma = \dfrac{M y}{I}$ to hold, the following classical **Euler–Bernoulli beam assumptions** must be satisfied:

1. The **material is homogeneous and isotropic** (same properties in all directions).
2. The **stress–strain relationship is linear elastic** (obeys Hooke's Law: $\sigma = E\varepsilon$).
3. **Plane cross-sections before bending remain plane and normal to the neutral axis after bending.**
4. The beam experiences **pure bending** (constant bending moment along the section, no shear).
5. **Self-weight is neglected** unless specifically included.
6. **No buckling or lateral-torsional instability** is considered.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Logic Flow — From Strain to Stress

The derivation follows a clean, four-step logical chain that examiners love to test:

- **Step 1 — Geometry of Deformation:**  
  When a beam of radius $R$ bends, a fiber at distance $y$ from the N.A. shortens/lengthens by an arc length $\Delta L = y \cdot \theta$ (where $\theta$ is the angle subtended).  
  The longitudinal strain is therefore:
  $$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L} = \frac{y \cdot \theta}{R \cdot \theta} = \frac{y}{R}$$

- **Step 2 — Hooke's Law Application:**  
  Assuming linear elasticity:
  $$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon = \frac{E \cdot y}{R}$$

- **Step 3 — Force and Moment Equilibrium:**  
  The internal bending moment equals the moment of the stress distribution about the N.A.:
  $$M = \int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA = \int_A \frac{E \cdot y}{R} \cdot y \, dA = \frac{E}{R} \int_A y^2 \, dA = \frac{E \cdot I}{R}$$

- **Step 4 — Eliminating the Curvature Term $E/R$:**  
  Combining Step 2 and Step 3:
  $$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{E}{R} = \frac{\sigma}{y}$$

This is the **Pure Bending Equation** — the **most fundamental relationship** in beam theory.

---

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' for Rectangular Sections

### (a) Why Rectangular Sections?

- They are **easy to manufacture** (extruded, milled, cast, 3D-printed).
- They have a **predictable stress distribution** with maximum stress at the extreme fibers.
- They are **isotropic in two principal axes** when $b = h$ (square).
- For robotic arm links, they slot easily into **modular joint housings**.

### (b) Why Hollow Rectangular Sections?

- They provide a **higher $I$ (and hence higher $Z$)** for the **same mass of material**.
- A hollow section deflects **less** under the same load.
- They permit **internal routing** of cables, pneumatic lines, or sensors in a robot.
- They are **aerodynamically and aesthetically cleaner** in humanoid robot design.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table — it forms the **backbone of all KTU numerical problems on this topic**.

| # | Quantity | Solid Rectangle (b × h) | Hollow Rectangle (B × D outer, b × d inner) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cross-sectional Area $A$ | $b \cdot h$ | $B \cdot D - b \cdot d$ |
| 2 | Distance to extreme fiber $y_{\max}$ | $h/2$ | $D/2$ |
| 3 | Moment of Inertia $I$ about N.A. | $\dfrac{b \cdot h^3}{12}$ | $\dfrac{B \cdot D^3 - b \cdot d^3}{12}$ |
| 4 | Section Modulus $Z$ | $\dfrac{b \cdot h^2}{6}$ | $\dfrac{B \cdot D^3 - b \cdot d^3}{6 \cdot D}$ |
| 5 | Max. Bending Stress $\sigma_{\max}$ | $\dfrac{6 M}{b \cdot h^2}$ | $\dfrac{6 M \cdot D}{B \cdot D^3 - b \cdot d^3}$ |
| 6 | Radius of Curvature $R$ | $\dfrac{E \cdot I}{M} = \dfrac{E \cdot b \cdot h^3}{12 M}$ | $\dfrac{E \cdot (B \cdot D^3 - b \cdot d^3)}{12 M}$ |
| 7 | Mass efficiency ratio (for same $Z$) | 1.0 (reference) | Higher (preferred for robotics) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign convention for KTU 2024:** Sagging moment (concave up) → **tension at bottom, compression at top** → bottom fiber has **positive (+) stress**, top fiber has **negative (−) stress**.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility in Robotics

| Robotic Application | Section Used | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Industrial robot arm links** | Hollow rectangular aluminum extrusion | High $Z$ / mass ratio |
| **Humanoid robot forearm** | Hollow rectangular CFRP tube | Lightweight + high stiffness |
| **AGV chassis rails** | Solid rectangular steel bar | Cost-effective, simple loading |
| **End-effector mounting brackets** | Solid rectangular | Concentrated load at tip |
| **Quadruped robot leg segments** | Hollow rectangular | Impact + bending resistant |
| **Mobile robot base frame** | Hollow rectangular | Cable routing + bending capacity |

The ability to **size a rectangular section** to safely carry a given bending moment is therefore a **core competency** for a robotics structural analyst — exactly the skill KTU Module 4 aims to develop.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Moment of Inertia ($I$) for a Solid Rectangular Section

**Given:** A solid rectangle of width $b$ (horizontal) and depth $h$ (vertical), bending about its horizontal centroidal axis (the neutral axis).

**Step 1 — Set up the elemental strip.**  
Consider a thin horizontal strip at a vertical distance $y$ from the neutral axis, of infinitesimal thickness $dy$. Since the strip is parallel to the N.A. and runs across the full width $b$:

$$dA = b \cdot dy$$

**Step 2 — Write the elemental moment of inertia.**  
By definition, the second moment of area of the strip about the N.A. is:

$$dI = y^2 \cdot dA = y^2 \cdot b \cdot dy$$

**Step 3 — Integrate over the full depth of the section.**  
The limits of integration extend from the bottom edge ($-h/2$) to the top edge ($+h/2$):

$$I = \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} b \cdot y^2 \, dy$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral.**  
Treating $b$ as a constant:

$$I = b \left[ \frac{y^3}{3} \right]_{-h/2}^{+h/2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the limits.**

$$I = b \left[ \frac{(h/2)^3}{3} - \frac{(-h/2)^3}{3} \right] = b \left[ \frac{h^3}{24} + \frac{h^3}{24} \right]$$

$$I = b \cdot \frac{2 h^3}{24} = \frac{b \cdot h^3}{12}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** $\boxed{I_{\text{solid}} = \dfrac{b \cdot h^3}{12}}$

**Step 6 — Derive the Section Modulus.**  
By definition, $Z = I / y_{\max}$. For the solid rectangle, $y_{\max} = h/2$:

$$Z = \frac{b \cdot h^3 / 12}{h/2} = \frac{b \cdot h^3}{12} \cdot \frac{2}{h} = \frac{b \cdot h^2}{6}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** $\boxed{Z_{\text{solid}} = \dfrac{b \cdot h^2}{6}}$

**Step 7 — Maximum bending stress.**

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{6 M}{b \cdot h^2}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Moment of Inertia ($I$) for a Hollow Rectangular Section

**Given:** A hollow rectangle with outer dimensions $B \times D$ and inner dimensions $b \times d$ (with $B > b$ and $D > d$). Bending occurs about the horizontal centroidal axis (N.A. coincides with the global horizontal centroid because of symmetry).

**Step 1 — Use the Composite (Subtraction) Method.**  
The hollow section is mathematically equivalent to a **solid outer rectangle** minus a **solid inner rectangle**:

$$I_{\text{hollow}} = I_{\text{outer}} - I_{\text{inner}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the solid rectangle formula to each part.**

$$I_{\text{outer}} = \frac{B \cdot D^3}{12} \quad ; \quad I_{\text{inner}} = \frac{b \cdot d^3}{12}$$

**Step 3 — Subtract.**

$$I_{\text{hollow}} = \frac{B \cdot D^3}{12} - \frac{b \cdot d^3}{12} = \frac{B \cdot D^3 - b \cdot d^3}{12}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** $\boxed{I_{\text{hollow}} = \dfrac{B \cdot D^3 - b \cdot d^3}{12}}$

**Step 4 — Derive the Section Modulus.**  
Here $y_{\max} = D/2$ (the distance from the N.A. to the **outermost outer fiber**, which is the critical fiber):

$$Z_{\text{hollow}} = \frac{I_{\text{hollow}}}{D/2} = \frac{B \cdot D^3 - b \cdot d^3}{12} \cdot \frac{2}{D} = \frac{B \cdot D^3 - b \cdot d^3}{6 D}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** $\boxed{Z_{\text{hollow}} = \dfrac{B \cdot D^3 - b \cdot d^3}{6 D}}$

**Step 5 — Maximum bending stress.**

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M}{Z_{\text{hollow}}} = \frac{6 M \cdot D}{B \cdot D^3 - b \cdot d^3}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked-Out Analytical Matrix (Sample Problem Demonstrating All Concepts)

**Problem (KTU Style):**  
A robotic arm link has a hollow rectangular cross-section of outer dimensions $80 \text{ mm} \times 60 \text{ mm}$ and uniform wall thickness $t = 10 \text{ mm}$. The link carries a maximum bending moment of $M = 4.5 \text{ kN·m} = 4.5 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$. Determine:
- (i) the moment of inertia about the N.A.,
- (ii) the section modulus,
- (iii) the maximum bending stress,
- (iv) the radius of curvature (assume $E = 200 \text{ GPa}$ for steel).

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify dimensions.**  
Outer: $B = 80 \text{ mm}$, $D = 60 \text{ mm}$ (depth, vertical, the direction of bending).  
Wall thickness $t = 10 \text{ mm}$ on all four sides.  
Inner: $b = B - 2t = 80 - 20 = 60 \text{ mm}$; $d = D - 2t = 60 - 20 = 40 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 2 — Compute the moment of inertia $I$.**

$$I = \frac{B \cdot D^3 - b \cdot d^3}{12} = \frac{(80)(60^3) - (60)(40^3)}{12} \text{ mm}^4$$

$$60^3 = 216{,}000 \text{ mm}^3; \quad 40^3 = 64{,}000 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$I = \frac{(80)(216{,}000) - (60)(64{,}000)}{12} = \frac{17{,}280{,}000 - 3{,}840{,}000}{12} = \frac{13{,}440{,}000}{12}$$

$$I = 1{,}120{,}000 \text{ mm}^4 = 1.12 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 3 — Compute the section modulus $Z$.**

$$Z = \frac{I}{D/2} = \frac{1.12 \times 10^6}{30} = 37{,}333.33 \text{ mm}^3 \approx 3.733 \times 10^4 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 4 — Compute the maximum bending stress $\sigma_{\max}$.**

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}}{37{,}333.33 \text{ mm}^3} = 120.54 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 120.54 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the radius of curvature $R$.**

$$R = \frac{E \cdot I}{M} = \frac{(200 \times 10^3 \text{ N/mm}^2)(1.12 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4)}{4.5 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}}$$

$$R = \frac{2.24 \times 10^{11}}{4.5 \times 10^6} = 49{,}777.78 \text{ mm} \approx 49.78 \text{ m}$$

**Final Tally:**

| Quantity | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $I$ | $1.12 \times 10^6$ | mm⁴ |
| $Z$ | $3.733 \times 10^4$ | mm³ |
| $\sigma_{\max}$ | $120.54$ | MPa |
| $R$ | $49.78$ | m |

---

## 3.4 Comparative Study — Why Hollow Wins for Robotic Links

For a **fixed cross-sectional area** $A = 2000 \text{ mm}^2$, compare solid vs. hollow:

- **Solid 40 × 50 mm rectangle:** $Z_s = \dfrac{40 \times 50^2}{6} = 16{,}666.67 \text{ mm}^3$
- **Hollow 60 × 60 mm with 10 mm wall** (same area $A = 60\times60 - 40\times40 = 2000$):  
  $Z_h = \dfrac{60^4 - 40^4}{6 \times 60} = \dfrac{12{,}960{,}000 - 2{,}560{,}000}{360} = 28{,}888.89 \text{ mm}^3$

> [!TIP]
> The **hollow section gives 73% more section modulus** for the **same mass** — a massive win for **payload-to-weight ratio** in robotic arm design.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bending Stress Distribution Across a Solid Rectangle

The following Mermaid block illustrates the **functional architecture of bending stress generation** across a solid rectangular cross-section under a sagging moment.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["External Load P acts on Beam"] --> B["Internal Sagging Moment M develops"]
    B --> C["Beam cross-section sees Linear Strain Distribution"]
    C --> D["Strain e proportional to y from Neutral Axis"]
    D --> E["Hookes Law: Stress s equals E times e"]
    E --> F["Linear Stress Distribution s equals M y over I"]
    F --> G["Top fiber at y equals minus h by 2: Max Compression"]
    F --> H["Neutral Axis at y equals 0: Zero Stress"]
    F --> I["Bottom fiber at y equals plus h by 2: Max Tension"]
    G --> J["Magnitude s_max equals 6M over b h squared"]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K["Beam Bends into Circular Arc of Radius R equals E I over M"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The flowchart traces the **causal chain** from the external load $P$ all the way to the deformed curvature $R$. The maximum stress **s_max** is shown to occur at both extreme fibers (top and bottom) — symmetrically for a pure bending case.

---

## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Architecture — Solid vs. Hollow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SOLID["SOLID RECTANGLE  b by h"]
        S1["Outer Boundary"] --> S2["Single Material Region"]
        S2 --> S3["Moment of Inertia I_s equals b h cubed over 12"]
        S2 --> S4["Section Modulus Z_s equals b h squared over 6"]
    end
    subgraph HOLLOW["HOLLOW RECTANGLE  B by D minus b by d"]
        H1["Outer Boundary B by D"] --> H2["Wall Material Region"]
        H2 --> H3["Inner Void Region b by d"]
        H2 --> H4["Moment of Inertia I_h equals B D cubed minus b d cubed over 12"]
        H2 --> H5["Section Modulus Z_h equals B D cubed minus b d cubed over 6D"]
    end
    H4 --> CMP["For equal mass: Z_h is significantly greater than Z_s"]
    S3 --> CMP
    H5 --> CMP
    S4 --> CMP
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Robotic Link Under Bending

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Robot Joint Torque Tau"] --> B["Transmitted through Link to Tip Load F"]
    B --> C["Internal Bending Moment M at Critical Section"]
    C --> D{"Cross-Section Type"}
    D -- "Solid Rectangle b h" --> E["Compute Z_s = b h squared over 6"]
    D -- "Hollow Rectangle B D minus b d" --> F["Compute Z_h = B D cubed minus b d cubed over 6D"]
    E --> G["Compute Stress s = M over Z"]
    F --> G
    G --> H{"Check s against Allowable s_allow"}
    H -- "s less than s_allow" --> I["Section SAFE - Design Accepted"]
    H -- "s greater than s_allow" --> J["Section UNSAFE - Increase h or use Hollow"]
    J --> D
```

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** This is a typical **KTU design flow** that a student should be able to reproduce in the exam. The decision block (D) and the iterative loop (J → D) are **key visual cues** examiners often award bonus marks for.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define the term "section modulus" for a rectangular cross-section. Derive its expression for a solid rectangular section of width $b$ and depth $h$.**

**Model Answer:**

The **section modulus ($Z$)** is defined as the ratio of the second moment of area ($I$) about the neutral axis to the distance ($y_{\max}$) of the outermost fiber from the neutral axis:

$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{\max}}$$

It is a **purely geometric property** of the cross-section, having units of m³, and indicates the **bending strength efficiency** of the section.

**Derivation for solid rectangle:**
- Moment of inertia: $I = \dfrac{b \cdot h^3}{12}$
- Distance to extreme fiber: $y_{\max} = \dfrac{h}{2}$

$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{\max}} = \frac{b \cdot h^3 / 12}{h/2} = \frac{b \cdot h^2}{6}$$

> **Final Expression:** $Z = \dfrac{b \cdot h^2}{6}$ ∎

---

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**State the assumptions made in the theory of pure bending.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Euler–Bernoulli pure bending theory** rests on the following assumptions:

1. The **material is homogeneous and isotropic**.
2. The **material obeys Hooke's Law** (linear elastic behavior: $\sigma = E\varepsilon$).
3. **Plane cross-sections before bending remain plane and normal to the neutral axis after bending.**
4. The beam is subjected to **pure bending** (no transverse shear, no torsion).
5. **Self-weight** and other body forces are **neglected** unless explicitly stated.
6. The **radius of curvature is large** compared to the cross-sectional dimensions (small deflection assumption).
7. **No lateral buckling** or **local instability** is considered. ∎

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**  
> The **most common mistakes** students make in this topic are:  
> ✗ Confusing $b$ and $h$ in $Z = b h^2/6$ — $h$ is **always the dimension parallel to the bending direction (depth)**.  
> ✗ Forgetting to use $D/2$ (not $d/2$) for $y_{\max}$ in the hollow section.  
> ✗ Mixing up units — $M$ must be in N·mm when dimensions are in mm, to get $\sigma$ in MPa.  
> ✗ Writing $Z = b h^2 / 6$ for the hollow section without showing the correct formula $(B D^3 - b d^3)/(6D)$.

---

### **Question B1 — Choice A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`

A simply supported robotic manipulator link of span $L = 2 \text{ m}$ carries a central point load of $W = 5 \text{ kN}$. The cross-section of the link is a **hollow rectangle** of outer dimensions $120 \text{ mm} \times 80 \text{ mm}$ and uniform wall thickness $10 \text{ mm}$.

**Part (a) — (7 Marks):**  
Determine the **maximum bending stress** developed in the link.

**Part (b) — (7 Marks):**  
If the same link is replaced by a **solid rectangular section of the same weight per unit length** (i.e., the same cross-sectional area), determine the **percentage reduction in maximum bending stress**.

---

#### 🔷 MODEL SOLUTION

**Part (a) — Maximum Bending Stress in Hollow Section**

**Step 1 — Compute the maximum bending moment.**  
For a simply supported beam of span $L$ with central point load $W$:

$$M_{\max} = \frac{W \cdot L}{4} = \frac{5000 \times 2}{4} = 2500 \text{ N·m} = 2.5 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Identify cross-section dimensions.**  
Outer: $B = 120 \text{ mm}$, $D = 80 \text{ mm}$  
Wall thickness: $t = 10 \text{ mm}$  
Inner: $b = 120 - 2(10) = 100 \text{ mm}$, $d = 80 - 2(10) = 60 \text{ mm}$

**Step 3 — Compute area of hollow section (needed for Part b).**  
$A_h = B \cdot D - b \cdot d = (120)(80) - (100)(60) = 9600 - 6000 = 3600 \text{ mm}^2$

**Step 4 — Compute $I_{\text{hollow}}$.**

$$I_h = \frac{B \cdot D^3 - b \cdot d^3}{12} = \frac{(120)(80^3) - (100)(60^3)}{12}$$

$$80^3 = 512{,}000; \quad 60^3 = 216{,}000$$

$$I_h = \frac{(120)(512{,}000) - (100)(216{,}000)}{12} = \frac{61{,}440{,}000 - 21{,}600{,}000}{12} = \frac{39{,}840{,}000}{12}$$

$$I_h = 3{,}320{,}000 \text{ mm}^4 = 3.32 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 5 — Compute $Z_{\text{hollow}}$.**

$$Z_h = \frac{I_h}{D/2} = \frac{3.32 \times 10^6}{40} = 83{,}000 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 6 — Compute $\sigma_{\max}$ for the hollow section.**

$$\sigma_{\max,h} = \frac{M_{\max}}{Z_h} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^6}{83{,}000} = 30.12 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 30.12 \text{ MPa}$$

> ✅ **Answer (a):** $\sigma_{\max,h} = 30.12 \text{ MPa}$

---

**Part (b) — Solid Section of Same Area, Percentage Reduction**

**Step 1 — Assume the solid section is a rectangle $b_s \times h_s$ with $A_s = 3600 \text{ mm}^2$.**  
To obtain a meaningful comparison, assume the solid section has the **same depth $h_s = D = 80 \text{ mm}$** as the original hollow section (this is a common KTU assumption):

$$b_s = \frac{A_s}{h_s} = \frac{3600}{80} = 45 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $Z_{\text{solid}}$.**

$$Z_s = \frac{b_s \cdot h_s^2}{6} = \frac{45 \times 80^2}{6} = \frac{45 \times 6400}{6} = \frac{288{,}000}{6} = 48{,}000 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\sigma_{\max}$ for the solid section.**

$$\sigma_{\max,s} = \frac{M_{\max}}{Z_s} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^6}{48{,}000} = 52.08 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Percentage reduction in stress (going from solid → hollow):**

$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{\sigma_{\max,s} - \sigma_{\max,h}}{\sigma_{\max,s}} \times 100 = \frac{52.08 - 30.12}{52.08} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{11.96}{52.08} \times 100 = 22.96\%$$

> ✅ **Answer (b):** The hollow section reduces the maximum bending stress by approximately **22.96 %** compared to a solid section of the **same area** — clearly demonstrating the **mass-efficiency of hollow sections in robotic link design**.

---

### **Question B2 — Choice B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`

A **solid rectangular beam** of width $b = 60 \text{ mm}$ and depth $h = 120 \text{ mm}$ is simply supported over a span of $3 \text{ m}$ and carries a uniformly distributed load (UDL) of intensity $w = 4 \text{ kN/m}$ over its entire span.

**Part (a) — (7 Marks):**  
Calculate the **maximum bending stress** and the **section modulus** of the beam.

**Part (b) — (7 Marks):**  
If the allowable bending stress for the material is $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = 12 \text{ MPa}$, determine whether the beam is **safe**. If not, find the **required depth** $h'$ to make it safe (keeping width $b = 60 \text{ mm}$ unchanged).

---

#### 🔷 MODEL SOLUTION

**Part (a) — Maximum Bending Stress and Section Modulus**

**Step 1 — Compute the maximum bending moment for a simply supported beam with UDL:**

$$M_{\max} = \frac{w \cdot L^2}{8} = \frac{4 \times 3^2}{8} = \frac{36}{8} = 4.5 \text{ kN·m} = 4.5 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the section modulus:**

$$Z = \frac{b \cdot h^2}{6} = \frac{60 \times 120^2}{6} = \frac{60 \times 14{,}400}{6} = 144{,}000 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Compute the maximum bending stress:**

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M_{\max}}{Z} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^6}{144{,}000} = 31.25 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 31.25 \text{ MPa}$$

> ✅ **Answer (a):** $Z = 144{,}000 \text{ mm}^3$ and $\sigma_{\max} = 31.25 \text{ MPa}$

---

**Part (b) — Safety Check and Required Depth**

**Step 1 — Compare with allowable stress:**

$$\sigma_{\max} = 31.25 \text{ MPa} > \sigma_{\text{allow}} = 12 \text{ MPa}$$

> **The beam is UNSAFE** — the induced stress exceeds the allowable value.

**Step 2 — Derive the required depth formula:**  
For safety, the new section modulus must satisfy:

$$Z_{\text{required}} \geq \frac{M_{\max}}{\sigma_{\text{allow}}} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^6}{12} = 375{,}000 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $h'$:**

$$Z_{\text{required}} = \frac{b \cdot (h')^2}{6} \Rightarrow (h')^2 = \frac{6 \cdot Z_{\text{required}}}{b} = \frac{6 \times 375{,}000}{60} = 37{,}500 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$h' = \sqrt{37{,}500} = 193.65 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Practical rounding up to a standard value:** $h' = 200 \text{ mm}$ (round up for safety).

> ✅ **Answer (b):** The beam is **NOT safe**. The required depth is **$h' \approx 193.65 \text{ mm}$**, which in practice is rounded up to **$h' = 200 \text{ mm}$**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this section as your **last-minute revision sheet** before entering the exam hall.

- ✅ **Bending equation:** $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ — this is the **trinity** of beam theory.
- ✅ **Maximum stress always occurs at the outermost fiber** ($y = y_{\max}$).
- ✅ **Section modulus is a pure geometric property** — it depends **only on shape**, not on material or load.
- ✅ **Solid rectangle:** $I = \dfrac{b h^3}{12}$ ; $Z = \dfrac{b h^2}{6}$ ; $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{6M}{b h^2}$.
- ✅ **Hollow rectangle:** $I = \dfrac{B D^3 - b d^3}{12}$ ; $Z = \dfrac{B D^3 - b d^3}{6 D}$ ; $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{6 M D}{B D^3 - b d^3}$.
- ✅ **$h$ (or $D$) is the dimension parallel to the bending direction** — confusing it with $b$ is the **#1 mistake**.
- ✅ **Unit consistency rule:** Use $M$ in **N·mm**, dimensions in **mm**, $I$ in **mm⁴**, $Z$ in **mm³** → $\sigma$ comes directly in **MPa**.
- ✅ **Hollow sections are ~70 % more mass-efficient** than solid sections for the **same $Z$** — **always preferred** in robotic link design.
- ✅ **Sign convention (sagging):** Bottom fiber = **+ve (tension)**, top fiber = **−ve (compression)**, N.A. = **zero stress**.
- ✅ **Radius of curvature:** $R = \dfrac{E I}{M}$ — used to compute **deflection shape** in advanced modules.
- ✅ **Assumptions to memorize:** Homogeneous, isotropic, linear elastic, plane sections remain plane, pure bending, no shear/torsion/buckling.
- ✅ **Quick cross-check formula:** For a **doubly symmetric** section, $Z = 2 \times I / \text{depth}$ (factor of 2 is built into the hollow formula too).
- ✅ **Safety rule:** Always round up the **required depth** to the next standard size (e.g., 200 mm instead of 193.65 mm).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Circular Section (Solid and Hollow)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT525 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS OF ROBOTIC PARTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Circular Section (Solid and Hollow)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Circular Section** is a planar cross-sectional geometry defined by a closed curve traced at a constant radial distance from a central reference point (the **centroid**). In the context of robotic structural analysis, circular sections are classified into two principal categories:

1. **Solid Circular Section** – A cross-section in which the material is continuously distributed across the full disc bounded by a single circumference of radius $R$ (or diameter $d$).
2. **Hollow Circular Section** (also called a **circular annulus** or **tubular section**) – A cross-section in which the material lies between two concentric circles of outer radius $R_o$ (or diameter $D$) and inner radius $R_i$ (or diameter $d$).

The **neutral axis** of a circular section, for any orientation in the plane, is *every* diameter passing through the centroid. This unique symmetry property — that the second moment of area is **identical about every centroidal axis** — makes circular sections *isotropic in bending*, a property of immense value in robotic links and rotating shafts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight (Module 4):** For robotic parts subjected to multi-axial bending (e.g., articulated arm links), the circular section is the **only standard section** whose moment of inertia is invariant with respect to the axis of bending. This eliminates directional weak points.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Notation Convention (KTU / IS 800 / IS 2062):**
> - $D$ or $d_o$ = Outer diameter
> - $d$ or $d_i$ = Inner diameter (for hollow sections)
> - $R$ = Outer radius, $r$ = Inner radius
> - All sectional properties are computed about the **centroidal axes (xx, yy)** and the **polar axis (zz)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **bicycle wheel spoke** versus a **solid wooden disc**:

- A **solid circular section** is like a *solid wooden rolling pin* — every direction through the centre looks identical, and it offers the same resistance to bending from any side. If you push down on one edge of a flat wooden disc, the disc bends the *same way* regardless of which edge you push.

- A **hollow circular section** is like an **aluminium tube** (e.g., a robotic manipulator link, a bicycle frame tube, or a drone's central fuselage). Imagine taking that solid wooden disc and drilling a perfectly centred hole through it. The cross-section still has the *same shape* in every direction, but now the material is concentrated **away from the centre** — exactly where bending stresses are highest. This means it can resist bending *almost as well* as a solid disc while being dramatically lighter.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for robotics:** A robotic arm link is a cantilever-like beam that bends in multiple planes as the robot moves. A hollow tube (carbon-fibre or aluminium) gives ~90% of the bending stiffness of a solid rod of the same outer diameter, at a fraction of the weight. This is the *mass-to-stiffness* optimisation principle that defines modern robotic structural design.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

The following physical/geometric quantities are fundamental to the analysis of circular sections in robotic parts:

- **Area ($A$):** Measure of the planar extent of the section (in $\text{mm}^2$ or $\text{m}^2$).
- **Centroid ($\bar{x}, \bar{y}$):** Geometric centre of the section — coincides with the geometric centre of the circle by symmetry.
- **Moment of Inertia / Second Moment of Area ($I$):** Measure of the section's resistance to bending about a given axis (in $\text{mm}^4$ or $\text{m}^4$).
- **Polar Moment of Inertia ($J$ or $I_{zz}$):** Measure of the section's resistance to **torsion** (in $\text{mm}^4$ or $\text{m}^4$).
- **Radius of Gyration ($k$):** A geometric property indicating how far the area is distributed from the axis (in $\text{mm}$ or $\text{m}$).
- **Section Modulus ($Z$):** Ratio of moment of inertia to the distance of the extreme fibre from the neutral axis (in $\text{mm}^3$ or $\text{m}^3$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Units to remember for KTU examinations:** $A$ in $\text{mm}^2$, $I$ and $J$ in $\text{mm}^4$, $k$ in $\text{mm}$, $Z$ in $\text{mm}^3$. Always include units in numerical answers.

---

## 1.4 Why Circular Sections are the Workhorse of Robotic Design

Robotic components such as **drive shafts, manipulator links, revolute joint pins, and reaction wheels** predominantly use circular sections because:

- **Uniform bending stiffness** in all directions (critical for serial manipulators with variable loading).
- **Maximum torsional rigidity for a given mass** (hollow tubes are the most efficient torsion members).
- **Easier manufacturing** (turning, grinding, drilling) compared to I-, T-, or channel sections.
- **Standardised fasteners and bearings** (shafts are universally circular).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometric and Inertial Properties — Concept Breakdown

The properties of a circular section can be derived from first principles of integral calculus, but for the KTU 2024 scheme, students must internalise the **final closed-form expressions** and the **logic flow** that produces them.

### 2.1.1 Solid Circular Section — Logic Steps

**Step 1 — Define the Geometry.**
The boundary is defined by the equation $x^2 + y^2 \le R^2$ where $R$ is the outer radius. The diameter $d = 2R$.

**Step 2 — Locate the Centroid.**
By the **symmetry argument** (the circle is invariant under reflection about both the $x$ and $y$ axes passing through its centre), the centroid coincides with the geometric centre: $\bar{x} = 0$, $\bar{y} = 0$.

**Step 3 — Compute the Cross-Sectional Area.**
The area is the area of a disc of radius $R$:
$$A = \pi R^2 = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Moment of Inertia about the Centroidal $xx$ axis.**
Choose a thin horizontal strip of width $dy$ at distance $y$ from the $x$-axis. The strip's length is $2x = 2\sqrt{R^2 - y^2}$, so $dA = 2\sqrt{R^2 - y^2}\,dy$. Then:
$$I_{xx} = \int y^2\,dA = \int_{-R}^{R} y^2 \cdot 2\sqrt{R^2 - y^2}\,dy$$
This evaluates (by the substitution $y = R\sin\theta$) to:
$$I_{xx} = \frac{\pi R^4}{4} = \frac{\pi d^4}{64}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Polar Moment of Inertia.**
Using the perpendicular axis theorem ($J = I_{xx} + I_{yy}$) and the fact that $I_{xx} = I_{yy}$ (by symmetry):
$$J = I_{xx} + I_{yy} = 2 I_{xx} = \frac{\pi R^4}{2} = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}$$
**Alternative derivation** (direct, using annular element at radius $r$):
$$J = \int r^2\,dA = \int_0^R r^2 \cdot 2\pi r\,dr = 2\pi \int_0^R r^3\,dr = \frac{\pi R^4}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Radius of Gyration.**
$$k_{xx} = \sqrt{\frac{I_{xx}}{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi R^4 / 4}{\pi R^2}} = \frac{R}{2} = \frac{d}{4}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the Section Modulus.**
The distance from the neutral axis ($xx$) to the extreme fibre is $y_{max} = R$. Therefore:
$$Z_{xx} = \frac{I_{xx}}{y_{max}} = \frac{\pi R^4/4}{R} = \frac{\pi R^3}{4} = \frac{\pi d^3}{32}$$

---

### 2.1.2 Hollow Circular Section — Logic Steps

**Step 1 — Define the Geometry.**
The section is the region $r_i^2 \le x^2 + y^2 \le r_o^2$, where $r_o = D/2$ and $r_i = d/2$ are the outer and inner radii.

**Step 2 — Centroid.**
By symmetry, $\bar{x} = \bar{y} = 0$ (the geometric centre of the annulus).

**Step 3 — Cross-Sectional Area.**
The area equals the area of the outer disc minus the area of the inner disc:
$$A = \pi(R_o^2 - R_i^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$$

**Step 4 — Moment of Inertia about the Centroidal $xx$ axis.**
