Using the difference of solid-section inertias (or the direct annular-strip method):
$$I_{xx} = \frac{\pi}{4}(R_o^4 - R_i^4) = \frac{\pi}{64}(D^4 - d^4)$$

**Step 5 — Polar Moment of Inertia.**
$$J = I_{xx} + I_{yy} = 2I_{xx} = \frac{\pi}{2}(R_o^4 - R_i^4) = \frac{\pi}{32}(D^4 - d^4)$$

**Step 6 — Radius of Gyration.**
$$k_{xx} = \sqrt{\frac{I_{xx}}{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi(R_o^4 - R_i^4)/4}{\pi(R_o^2 - R_i^2)}} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{R_o^2 + R_i^2} = \frac{1}{4}\sqrt{D^2 + d^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Aid:** For hollow tubes, the radius of gyration depends on the **arithmetic mean of squared radii**. Many students mistakenly write $k = (D-d)/4$ — that is the *mean radius*, not the *radius of gyration*. They are different.

**Step 7 — Section Modulus.**
$$Z_{xx} = \frac{I_{xx}}{y_{max}} = \frac{I_{xx}}{R_o} = \frac{\pi(R_o^4 - R_i^4)}{4R_o} = \frac{\pi(D^4 - d^4)}{32D}$$

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **board-essential**. Memorise the **d-form** (most commonly used in KTU numerical problems) and be able to **derive** the R-form during the exam.

| Property | Solid Circular Section | Hollow Circular Section |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **Area, $A$** | $\dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$ | $\dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$ |
| **Centroidal Moment of Inertia, $I_{xx}$** | $\dfrac{\pi d^4}{64}$ | $\dfrac{\pi}{64}(D^4 - d^4)$ |
| **Centroidal Moment of Inertia, $I_{yy}$** | $\dfrac{\pi d^4}{64}$ | $\dfrac{\pi}{64}(D^4 - d^4)$ |
| **Polar Moment of Inertia, $J$** | $\dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$ | $\dfrac{\pi}{32}(D^4 - d^4)$ |
| **Radius of Gyration, $k_{xx}$** | $\dfrac{d}{4}$ | $\dfrac{1}{4}\sqrt{D^2 + d^2}$ |
| **Section Modulus, $Z_{xx}$** | $\dfrac{\pi d^3}{32}$ | $\dfrac{\pi(D^4 - d^4)}{32D}$ |
| **Perimeter (for solid/mean)** | $\pi d$ | $\pi(D + d)$ |
| **Relation: $I$ to $J$** | $J = 2I$ | $J = 2I$ |
| **Relation: $Z$ to $I$** | $Z = I \cdot 4 / d$ | $Z = I \cdot 4 / D$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Identity:** $I_{xx} \times 32 = J \times 16 = \pi d^4 / 2$ for solid sections. Cross-checking answers using $J = 2I$ is a fast way to catch sign errors in the exam hall.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The formulas above are foundational for the design and analysis of:

- **Robotic drive shafts** (solid sections for small actuators; hollow for high-power servo-driven joints).
- **Manipulator links** of serial and parallel robots (hollow aluminium or carbon-fibre tubes for high stiffness-to-weight ratio).
- **Gear and bearing housings** (where polar moment of inertia governs torsional deflection).
- **Reaction wheels and flywheels** in mobile robots and drones (where $J$ governs angular acceleration).
- **Revolute joint pins** (solid sections carrying shear and bending in articulated robots).
- **Tubular robot chassis** (hollow sections for crashworthiness and structural rigidity).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $J$ matters in robotics:** A robot arm driving a load through space experiences **torsion** in the wrist and forearm. The polar moment of inertia $J$ of the cross-section determines how much the shaft **twists per unit torque** ($\theta = TL / JG$). For a hollow shaft, $J \propto D^4 - d^4$ — even a modest increase in $D$ dramatically reduces twist, which is why industrial robot shafts are almost always hollow.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In the KTU 2024 scheme, derivations are tested under Part B (14-mark questions). The student is expected to show the **complete integral set-up** and the **substitution/execution** steps. Skipping the integral set-up costs **3–4 marks** in valuation.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Moment of Inertia of a Solid Circular Section

**Objective:** Prove that $I_{xx} = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{64}$ for a solid disc of diameter $d$.

**Step 1 — Reference Frame and Geometry.**
Place the origin at the centroid of the circle. Let the $x$-axis and $y$-axis be centroidal axes (perpendicular diameters). The boundary of the section is defined by:
$$x^2 + y^2 = R^2$$
where $R$ is the radius.

**Step 2 — Choose an Elemental Area.**
Select a thin horizontal strip at a vertical distance $y$ from the $x$-axis, of infinitesimal thickness $dy$. The length of this strip is determined by the intersection of the horizontal line $y = \text{const}$ with the circle:

From $x^2 + y^2 = R^2 \Rightarrow x = \sqrt{R^2 - y^2}$.

The strip extends from $x = -\sqrt{R^2 - y^2}$ to $x = +\sqrt{R^2 - y^2}$, so its length is:
$$2x = 2\sqrt{R^2 - y^2}$$

Therefore, the elemental area is:
$$dA = 2\sqrt{R^2 - y^2}\,dy$$

**Step 3 — Set up the Integral.**
The second moment of area about the $x$-axis is:
$$I_{xx} = \int y^2\,dA = \int_{-R}^{R} y^2 \cdot 2\sqrt{R^2 - y^2}\,dy$$
$$I_{xx} = 2\int_{-R}^{R} y^2 \sqrt{R^2 - y^2}\,dy$$

**Step 4 — Trigonometric Substitution.**
Let $y = R \sin\theta$. Then:
$$dy = R\cos\theta\,d\theta$$
$$\sqrt{R^2 - y^2} = \sqrt{R^2 - R^2\sin^2\theta} = R\cos\theta$$
$$y^2 = R^2 \sin^2\theta$$

**Limits of integration:**
- When $y = -R$: $\sin\theta = -1 \Rightarrow \theta = -\pi/2$
- When $y = +R$: $\sin\theta = +1 \Rightarrow \theta = +\pi/2$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the Integral.**
$$I_{xx} = 2\int_{-\pi/2}^{\pi/2} \left(R^2\sin^2\theta\right)\left(R\cos\theta\right)\left(R\cos\theta\,d\theta\right)$$
$$I_{xx} = 2\int_{-\pi/2}^{\pi/2} R^4 \sin^2\theta \cos^2\theta\,d\theta$$
$$I_{xx} = 2R^4 \int_{-\pi/2}^{\pi/2} \sin^2\theta \cos^2\theta\,d\theta$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Identity $\sin 2\theta = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta$.**
$$\sin^2\theta\cos^2\theta = \left(\frac{\sin 2\theta}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{\sin^2 2\theta}{4}$$
$$I_{xx} = 2R^4 \int_{-\pi/2}^{\pi/2} \frac{\sin^2 2\theta}{4}\,d\theta = \frac{R^4}{2}\int_{-\pi/2}^{\pi/2} \sin^2 2\theta\,d\theta$$

**Step 7 — Use the Half-Angle Identity $\sin^2\alpha = \dfrac{1 - \cos 2\alpha}{2}$.**
Here $\alpha = 2\theta$, so $\cos 2\alpha = \cos 4\theta$:
$$\sin^2 2\theta = \frac{1 - \cos 4\theta}{2}$$
$$I_{xx} = \frac{R^4}{2} \int_{-\pi/2}^{\pi/2} \frac{1 - \cos 4\theta}{2}\,d\theta = \frac{R^4}{4}\int_{-\pi/2}^{\pi/2} (1 - \cos 4\theta)\,d\theta$$

**Step 8 — Evaluate the Integral.**
$$I_{xx} = \frac{R^4}{4}\left[\theta - \frac{\sin 4\theta}{4}\right]_{-\pi/2}^{\pi/2}$$
$$I_{xx} = \frac{R^4}{4}\left[\left(\frac{\pi}{2} - \frac{\sin 2\pi}{4}\right) - \left(-\frac{\pi}{2} - \frac{\sin(-2\pi)}{4}\right)\right]$$
$$I_{xx} = \frac{R^4}{4}\left[\frac{\pi}{2} - 0 + \frac{\pi}{2} - 0\right] = \frac{R^4}{4} \cdot \pi = \frac{\pi R^4}{4}$$

**Step 9 — Convert to Diameter Form.**
Substituting $R = d/2$:
$$I_{xx} = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^4 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{d^4}{16} = \frac{\pi d^4}{64}$$

$$\boxed{I_{xx} = I_{yy} = \frac{\pi d^4}{64}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Polar Moment of Inertia of a Solid Circular Section

**Objective:** Prove that $J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$.

**Step 1 — Choose an Elemental Area.**
Use a thin concentric ring (annulus) of radius $r$ and thickness $dr$. The circumference of the ring is $2\pi r$, so:
$$dA = 2\pi r\,dr$$

**Step 2 — Set up the Integral.**
$$J = \int r^2\,dA = \int_0^R r^2 \cdot 2\pi r\,dr = 2\pi \int_0^R r^3\,dr$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**
$$J = 2\pi \cdot \left[\frac{r^4}{4}\right]_0^R = 2\pi \cdot \frac{R^4}{4} = \frac{\pi R^4}{2} = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}$$

$$\boxed{J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}}$$

**Verification via Perpendicular Axis Theorem:**
$$J = I_{xx} + I_{yy} = \frac{\pi d^4}{64} + \frac{\pi d^4}{64} = \frac{\pi d^4}{32} \checkmark$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Moment of Inertia of a Hollow Circular Section

**Objective:** Prove that $I_{xx} = \dfrac{\pi}{64}(D^4 - d^4)$.

**Step 1 — Method: Difference of Solid Discs.**
The hollow section can be visualised as a **large solid disc of diameter $D$** minus a **small solid disc of diameter $d$**, both sharing the same centroid.

**Step 2 — Apply the Subtraction Principle.**
$$I_{xx}^{\text{hollow}} = I_{xx}^{\text{outer disc}} - I_{xx}^{\text{inner disc}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Solid Disc Formula.**
$$I_{xx}^{\text{hollow}} = \frac{\pi D^4}{64} - \frac{\pi d^4}{64} = \frac{\pi}{64}(D^4 - d^4)$$

$$\boxed{I_{xx} = \frac{\pi}{64}(D^4 - d^4)}$$

**Step 4 — Verification via Direct Annular Integration.**
Using an annular element of radius $r$ and thickness $dr$ between $r_i$ and $r_o$:
$$I_{xx} = \int_{r_i}^{r_o} r^2 \cdot 2\pi r\,dr \cdot \frac{1}{2} \quad \text{(factor } 1/2 \text{ accounts for half-plane)}$$

Wait — the direct method yields $J$ first, so $I_{xx} = J/2$:
$$J = \int_{r_i}^{r_o} r^2 \cdot 2\pi r\,dr = 2\pi \cdot \frac{r_o^4 - r_i^4}{4} = \frac{\pi}{2}(R_o^4 - R_i^4)$$
$$I_{xx} = \frac{J}{2} = \frac{\pi}{4}(R_o^4 - R_i^4) = \frac{\pi}{64}(D^4 - d^4) \checkmark$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Radius of Gyration of a Hollow Circular Section

**Objective:** Prove that $k_{xx} = \dfrac{1}{4}\sqrt{D^2 + d^2}$.

**Step 1 — Apply the Definition.**
$$k_{xx} = \sqrt{\frac{I_{xx}}{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi(D^4 - d^4)/64}{\pi(D^2 - d^2)/4}}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the Fraction.**
$$k_{xx} = \sqrt{\frac{D^4 - d^4}{16(D^2 - d^2)}}$$

**Step 3 — Factor the Difference of Fourth Powers.**
Recall the identity:
$$D^4 - d^4 = (D^2 - d^2)(D^2 + d^2)$$

**Step 4 — Cancel and Take the Square Root.**
$$k_{xx} = \sqrt{\frac{(D^2 - d^2)(D^2 + d^2)}{16(D^2 - d^2)}} = \sqrt{\frac{D^2 + d^2}{16}} = \frac{1}{4}\sqrt{D^2 + d^2}$$

$$\boxed{k_{xx} = \frac{\sqrt{D^2 + d^2}}{4}}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Section Modulus of a Hollow Circular Section

**Objective:** Prove that $Z_{xx} = \dfrac{\pi(D^4 - d^4)}{32D}$.

**Step 1 — Apply the Definition.**
The extreme fibre distance from the neutral axis is $y_{max} = D/2$.
$$Z_{xx} = \frac{I_{xx}}{y_{max}} = \frac{\pi(D^4 - d^4)/64}{D/2} = \frac{\pi(D^4 - d^4)}{64} \cdot \frac{2}{D}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify.**
$$Z_{xx} = \frac{\pi(D^4 - d^4)}{32D}$$

$$\boxed{Z_{xx} = \frac{\pi(D^4 - d^4)}{32D}}$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Comparison Summary

> [!NOTE]
> The following **summary matrix** is excellent for KTU Part A 3-mark questions where a comparison or identification is asked.

| Property | Solid Circle | Hollow Circle | Ratio (Hollow / Solid, same $D$) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $A$ | $\pi D^2 / 4$ | $\pi(D^2 - d^2)/4$ | $1 - (d/D)^2$ |
| $I_{xx}$ | $\pi D^4 / 64$ | $\pi(D^4 - d^4)/64$ | $1 - (d/D)^4$ |
| $J$ | $\pi D^4 / 32$ | $\pi(D^4 - d^4)/32$ | $1 - (d/D)^4$ |
| $k_{xx}$ | $D/4$ | $\sqrt{D^2 + d^2}/4$ | $> 1$ (always) |
| $Z_{xx}$ | $\pi D^3 / 32$ | $\pi(D^4 - d^4)/(32D)$ | $1 - (d/D)^4$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** A hollow tube with inner-to-outer diameter ratio $d/D = 0.8$ retains $1 - 0.8^4 = 1 - 0.4096 = 0.5904 \approx 59\%$ of the bending stiffness of a solid bar of the same outer diameter, while weighing only $1 - 0.8^2 = 36\%$ as much. This is the **mass-stiffness efficiency** that drives robotic structural design.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Geometry of Solid vs Hollow Circular Sections

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A["SOLID CIRCULAR SECTION"]
        A1["Centroid O at origin (0,0)"]
        A2["Boundary: x² + y² = R²"]
        A3["Diameter d = 2R"]
        A4["Cross-section: filled disc"]
    end

    subgraph B["HOLLOW CIRCULAR SECTION"]
        B1["Centroid O at origin (0,0)"]
        B2["Outer boundary: x² + y² = R²_o"]
        B3["Inner boundary: x² + y² = R²_i"]
        B4["Outer diameter D, inner diameter d"]
        B5["Cross-section: annular ring (tubular)"]
    end

    subgraph C["AXIS CONVENTIONS"]
        C1["xx axis: horizontal centroidal diameter"]
        C2["yy axis: vertical centroidal diameter"]
        C3["zz axis (polar): perpendicular to plane through O"]
        C4["All three axes pass through the centroid"]
    end

    A --> C
    B --> C
```

**Textual Schematic Description (for the student to draw in the exam):**

- **Solid Circle:** Draw a circle of radius $R$. Mark the centre as $O$. Draw a horizontal line $x-x$ and a vertical line $y-y$ through $O$, plus a small circle-dot at $O$ pointing out of the page labelled $z-z$ (polar axis).
- **Hollow Circle:** Draw two concentric circles — outer of radius $R_o$, inner of radius $R_i$. Label the outer and inner diameters as $D$ and $d$. Mark the centre $O$ and the axes $x-x$, $y-y$, $z-z$ as before.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture: Sectional Property Derivation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["INPUT: Geometry of Section<br>(diameter D, inner d for hollow)"] --> B["STEP 1: Define Boundary Equation<br>x² + y² = R²"]
    B --> C["STEP 2: Choose Elemental Area dA<br>Solid: horizontal strip<br>Hollow: annular ring"]
    C --> D["STEP 3: Set up Integral<br>∫ y² dA or ∫ r² dA"]
    D --> E["STEP 4: Apply Limits<br>y: -R to +R<br>or r: R_i to R_o"]
    E --> F["STEP 5: Execute Integration<br>using trig sub or power rule"]
    F --> G["STEP 6: Apply Perpendicular Axis Theorem<br>J = I_xx + I_yy = 2I_xx"]
    G --> H["STEP 7: Derive Secondary Properties<br>k = sqrt(I/A)<br>Z = I/y_max"]
    H --> I["OUTPUT: Closed-Form Expressions<br>I = πd⁴/64 or π(D⁴-d⁴)/64<br>J = πd⁴/32 or π(D⁴-d⁴)/32"]

    style A fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0284C7

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The questions below are modelled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern. Marks are distributed as: **Part A (3 marks each)** and **Part B (14 marks, with internal choice)**. The expected model answers follow KTU's valuation key norms (methodology, formulae, substitution, units, and final answer carry marks).

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: State the perpendicular axis theorem. Using it, derive the polar moment of inertia of a solid circular section of diameter $d$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — State the Theorem (1 mark):**
The perpendicular axis theorem states that for any planar (lamina) cross-section, the moment of inertia about an axis perpendicular to the plane of the section (polar axis) is equal to the sum of the moments of inertia about any two mutually perpendicular axes lying in the plane of the section and intersecting at the point where the polar axis passes.

Mathematically:
$$I_{zz} = I_{xx} + I_{yy}$$

**Step 2 — Apply to a Solid Circle (1 mark):**
For a solid circular section, by symmetry:
$$I_{xx} = I_{yy} = \frac{\pi d^4}{64}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Polar Moment (1 mark):**
$$J = I_{xx} + I_{yy} = \frac{\pi d^4}{64} + \frac{\pi d^4}{64} = \frac{2\pi d^4}{64} = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}$$

$$\boxed{J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}}$$

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Distinguish between the moment of inertia and the section modulus of a hollow circular section of outer diameter $D$ and inner diameter $d$. Write the expression for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — Define Moment of Inertia (1 mark):**
The moment of inertia (second moment of area) of a section about a centroidal axis is the integral $\int y^2 dA$, representing the section's resistance to bending. It has units of $\text{mm}^4$ or $\text{m}^4$.

For a hollow circular section:
$$I_{xx} = \frac{\pi}{64}(D^4 - d^4)$$

**Step 2 — Define Section Modulus (1 mark):**
The section modulus is the ratio of the moment of inertia to the distance of the extreme fibre from the neutral axis ($y_{max} = D/2$). It directly gives the bending stress when multiplied by the bending moment ($\sigma = M/Z$). It has units of $\text{mm}^3$ or $\text{m}^3$.

For a hollow circular section:
$$Z_{xx} = \frac{I_{xx}}{y_{max}} = \frac{\pi(D^4 - d^4)}{32D}$$

**Step 3 — Key Distinction (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Moment of Inertia, $I$ | Section Modulus, $Z$ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Definition | $\int y^2 dA$ | $I / y_{max}$ |
| Physical meaning | Resistance to bending | Bending stress per unit moment |
| Units | $\text{mm}^4$ | $\text{mm}^3$ |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B questions carry **14 marks each** with internal choice. The 14 marks are typically split as **Part (a): 7 marks** and **Part (b): 7 marks**. A common 14-mark pattern involves a derivation (7 marks) + a numerical application (7 marks), or two separate numerical/design problems.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: A hollow circular shaft of outer diameter $D = 80\,\text{mm}$ and inner diameter $d = 50\,\text{mm}$ is used as a drive shaft in a robotic manipulator. Determine:**

**(a)** The cross-sectional area, the centroidal moment of inertia, and the polar moment of inertia of the section. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The section modulus and the radius of gyration. If the shaft transmits a torque of $T = 1500\,\text{N·m}$, find the angle of twist per metre length, given the modulus of rigidity $G = 80\,\text{GPa}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

#### Part (a) — Geometric and Inertial Properties (7 marks)

**Given:** $D = 80\,\text{mm} = 0.08\,\text{m}$, $d = 50\,\text{mm} = 0.05\,\text{m}$.

**Step 1 — Cross-Sectional Area (2 marks):**
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(80^2 - 50^2)\,\text{mm}^2$$
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(6400 - 2500) = \frac{\pi}{4}(3900) = 975\pi\,\text{mm}^2$$
$$\boxed{A \approx 3063.05\,\text{mm}^2}$$

**Step 2 — Centroidal Moment of Inertia (2 marks):**
$$I_{xx} = \frac{\pi}{64}(D^4 - d^4) = \frac{\pi}{64}(80^4 - 50^4)\,\text{mm}^4$$
$$80^4 = 40{,}960{,}000,\quad 50^4 = 6{,}250{,}000$$
$$D^4 - d^4 = 40{,}960{,}000 - 6{,}250{,}000 = 34{,}710{,}000\,\text{mm}^4$$
$$I_{xx} = \frac{\pi \times 34{,}710{,}000}{64} = 1{,}703{,}565.6\,\text{mm}^4$$
$$\boxed{I_{xx} \approx 1.704 \times 10^6\,\text{mm}^4}$$

**Step 3 — Polar Moment of Inertia (3 marks):**
$$J = \frac{\pi}{32}(D^4 - d^4) = 2 \times I_{xx} = 2 \times 1{,}703{,}565.6$$
$$\boxed{J \approx 3.407 \times 10^6\,\text{mm}^4}$$

Alternatively, $J = \dfrac{\pi}{32}(34{,}710{,}000) = 3{,}407{,}131.3\,\text{mm}^4$.

---

#### Part (b) — Section Modulus, Radius of Gyration, and Angle of Twist (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Section Modulus (2 marks):**
$$Z_{xx} = \frac{\pi(D^4 - d^4)}{32D} = \frac{\pi \times 34{,}710{,}000}{32 \times 80}$$
$$Z_{xx} = \frac{109{,}029{,}000}{2560} = 42{,}589.45\,\text{mm}^3$$
$$\boxed{Z_{xx} \approx 4.259 \times 10^4\,\text{mm}^3}$$

**Step 2 — Radius of Gyration (2 marks):**
$$k_{xx} = \frac{\sqrt{D^2 + d^2}}{4} = \frac{\sqrt{80^2 + 50^2}}{4} = \frac{\sqrt{6400 + 2500}}{4} = \frac{\sqrt{8900}}{4}$$
$$k_{xx} = \frac{94.34}{4} = 23.585\,\text{mm}$$
$$\boxed{k_{xx} \approx 23.58\,\text{mm}}$$

**Step 3 — Angle of Twist (3 marks):**
The torsional formula:
$$\frac{\theta}{L} = \frac{T}{JG}$$

Convert units:
- $T = 1500\,\text{N·m} = 1.5 \times 10^6\,\text{N·mm}$
- $J = 3.407 \times 10^6\,\text{mm}^4$
- $G = 80\,\text{GPa} = 80 \times 10^3\,\text{N/mm}^2 = 80{,}000\,\text{MPa}$
- $L = 1\,\text{m} = 1000\,\text{mm}$

$$\frac{\theta}{L} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^6}{3.407 \times 10^6 \times 80{,}000}$$
$$\frac{\theta}{L} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^6}{2.726 \times 10^{11}} = 5.503 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{rad/mm}$$

For $L = 1\,\text{m} = 1000\,\text{mm}$:
$$\theta = 5.503 \times 10^{-6} \times 1000 = 5.503 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{rad}$$
$$\boxed{\theta \approx 5.50 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{rad/m} \approx 0.315^\circ/\text{m}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: A robotic arm link is constructed from a hollow aluminium tube of outer diameter $60\,\text{mm}$ and wall thickness $t = 8\,\text{mm}$.**

**(a)** Calculate the inner diameter, cross-sectional area, and the second moment of inertia of the cross-section. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If this link is subjected to a bending moment of $M = 400\,\text{N·m}$ in a vertical plane, determine the maximum bending stress. Also, calculate the percentage saving in material (cross-sectional area) if a solid rod of the same outer diameter were used instead. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

#### Part (a) — Inner Diameter, Area, and Moment of Inertia (7 marks)

**Given:** $D = 60\,\text{mm}$, $t = 8\,\text{mm}$ (wall thickness).

**Step 1 — Inner Diameter (2 marks):**
$$d = D - 2t = 60 - 2(8) = 60 - 16 = 44\,\text{mm}$$
$$\boxed{d = 44\,\text{mm}}$$

**Step 2 — Cross-Sectional Area (2 marks):**
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(60^2 - 44^2)$$
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(3600 - 1936) = \frac{\pi}{4}(1664) = 416\pi$$
$$\boxed{A \approx 1306.90\,\text{mm}^2}$$

**Step 3 — Second Moment of Inertia (3 marks):**
$$I_{xx} = \frac{\pi}{64}(D^4 - d^4) = \frac{\pi}{64}(60^4 - 44^4)$$
$$60^4 = 12{,}960{,}000,\quad 44^4 = 3{,}748{,}096$$
$$D^4 - d^4 = 12{,}960{,}000 - 3{,}748{,}096 = 9{,}211{,}904\,\text{mm}^4$$
$$I_{xx} = \frac{\pi \times 9{,}211{,}904}{64} = 452{,}119.6\,\text{mm}^4$$
$$\boxed{I_{xx} \approx 4.521 \times 10^5\,\text{mm}^4}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Maximum Bending Stress and Material Saving (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Maximum Bending Stress (3 marks):**
Convert units: $M = 400\,\text{N·m} = 400{,}000\,\text{N·mm}$, $y_{max} = D/2 = 30\,\text{mm}$.

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M \cdot y_{max}}{I_{xx}} = \frac{M}{Z_{xx}}$$

Section modulus:
$$Z_{xx} = \frac{I_{xx}}{y_{max}} = \frac{452{,}119.6}{30} = 15{,}070.65\,\text{mm}^3$$

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{400{,}000}{15{,}070.65} = 26.54\,\text{N/mm}^2 = 26.54\,\text{MPa}$$
$$\boxed{\sigma_{max} \approx 26.54\,\text{MPa}}$$

**Step 2 — Area of Equivalent Solid Rod (2 marks):**
For a solid rod of diameter $D = 60\,\text{mm}$:
$$A_{solid} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 60^2}{4} = 900\pi \approx 2827.43\,\text{mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Percentage Material Saving (2 marks):**
$$\% \text{ Saving} = \frac{A_{solid} - A_{hollow}}{A_{solid}} \times 100$$
$$= \frac{2827.43 - 1306.90}{2827.43} \times 100 = \frac{1520.53}{2827.43} \times 100$$
$$\boxed{\% \text{ Saving} \approx 53.78\%}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The hollow tube saves ~54% of material (and hence ~54% of mass, assuming uniform density) while still providing usable bending stiffness. This is precisely why robotic arms and aerospace structures use hollow tubes.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $64$ in the denominator of $I$:** Students often write $I = \pi d^4 / 4$ (the disc area formula) instead of $I = \pi d^4 / 64$. This single error loses **3–4 marks**.
>
> 2. **Confusing $I$ with $J$:** Polar moment $J = 2I$. Mixing them up in torsion problems (Part B) leads to a wrong angle of twist.
>
> 3. **Wrong $y_{max}$ for hollow sections:** For a hollow section, $y_{max} = D/2$ (the **outer** radius), not $(D-d)/2$ (the mean radius). The extreme fibre is on the outer surface.
>
> 4. **Radius of gyration confusion:** The radius of gyration $k$ is **not** the mean radius. It is defined as $k = \sqrt{I/A}$. For a hollow section, $k = \frac{1}{4}\sqrt{D^2 + d^2}$, **not** $(D+d)/4$.
>
> 5. **Unit inconsistency:** $M$ in N·m vs. N·mm, $I$ in $\text{mm}^4$ vs. $\text{m}^4$ — always convert to **consistent units** before substitution.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the $1/2$ in $I_{xx} = J/2$:** When deriving $I_{xx}$ from the annular element integration, the result gives $J$ first, not $I_{xx}$. Divide by 2 to get $I_{xx}$.
>
> 7. **Skipping the integral set-up in derivations:** In KTU 2024 ESE, the line $\displaystyle I_{xx} = \int y^2 dA = \int_{-R}^{R} y^2 \cdot 2\sqrt{R^2 - y^2}\,dy$ is worth **1 mark on its own**. Skipping directly to the answer costs this mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print/Bookmark this section before exams.**

### Core Formulas (Both Solid & Hollow)

- **Area:** $A_{\text{solid}} = \pi d^2/4$, $\quad A_{\text{hollow}} = \pi(D^2 - d^2)/4$
- **Centroidal $I$:** $I_{\text{solid}} = \pi d^4/64$, $\quad I_{\text{hollow}} = \pi(D^4 - d^4)/64$
- **Polar $J$:** $J_{\text{solid}} = \pi d^4/32$, $\quad J_{\text{hollow}} = \pi(D^4 - d^4)/32$
- **Radius of Gyration:** $k_{\text{solid}} = d/4$, $\quad k_{\text{hollow}} = \sqrt{D^2 + d^2}/4$
- **Section Modulus:** $Z_{\text{solid}} = \pi d^3/32$, $\quad Z_{\text{hollow}} = \pi(D^4 - d^4)/(32D)$

### Key Identities & Theorems

- **Perpendicular Axis Theorem:** $J = I_{xx} + I_{yy}$. For a circle, $I_{xx} = I_{yy} = J/2$.
- **Symmetry Property:** $I_{xx} = I_{yy}$ for a circular section about *any* centroidal axis.
- **Hollow = Outer − Inner:** $I_{\text{hollow}} = I_{\text{outer disc}} - I_{\text{inner disc}}$.

### Common Engineering Applications in Robotics

- **Solid shafts:** Small actuators, low-torque drive shafts, revolute joint pins.
- **Hollow tubes:** Manipulator links, drone booms, high-torque drive shafts (saves mass while retaining stiffness).
- **Polar $J$ governs:** Torsional rigidity, angle of twist, flywheel angular momentum.

### Exam Strategy Points

- Always state the **given data** with units.
- Always **state the formula** before substitution.
- **Show the integration set-up** in derivation questions — it carries independent marks.
- **Check units** before and after substitution.
- For hollow sections, the **inner diameter** is $d = D - 2t$ where $t$ is the wall thickness.
- **Sanity check:** $I_{\text{hollow}} < I_{\text{solid of same } D}$; $k_{\text{hollow}} > k_{\text{solid of same } D}$.

# I-Section: Definition, Geometry & Intuitive Engineering Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **I-Section** (also universally known as an **I-beam**, **H-section**, **W-section** in American practice, or **Universal Beam / UB** in British/European practice) is a structural cross-sectional profile whose geometric shape resembles the capital letter "**I**" or "**H**". It consists of two horizontal parallel components called **flanges** connected by a single vertical component called the **web**.

In the context of **robotic structural analysis**, I-sections are extensively employed as the cross-section for:
- **Robotic manipulator link members** (rigid links connecting joints)
- **Base frames and pedestals** of industrial robotic arms
- **End-effector structural housings**
- **Gantry robot support beams**
- **Linear motion guide rails** in Cartesian and SCARA robots

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The I-section is the *most efficient* and *most frequently tested* standard rolled-steel section in structural analysis. Mastery of its centroidal axis location, moment of inertia (second moment of area), section modulus, and bending stress distribution is **mandatory** for any KTU examination under Module 4.

### 1.2 Standard Geometrical Parameters

For a standard symmetric I-section, the following geometrical parameters are defined:

| Parameter | Symbol | Description |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Overall Depth | $h$ | Total vertical height of the section |
| Flange Width | $b_f$ | Horizontal width of each flange |
| Web Thickness | $t_w$ | Thickness of the central vertical web |
| Flange Thickness | $t_f$ | Thickness of each flange |
| Root Radius | $r_1$ | Inside fillet radius between web and flange |
| Toe Radius | $r_2$ | Outer fillet radius at flange tip |
| Cross-sectional Area | $A$ | Total area of the section |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symmetry Property:** A standard I-section has **two axes of symmetry**: a horizontal axis (x-x, the major principal axis) and a vertical axis (y-y, the minor principal axis). This symmetry is critical because it ensures the **centroid (G)** lies exactly at the geometric intersection of these axes.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "Skyscraper of Steel"

Imagine a tall, slender **human skeletal bone** — specifically the **femur (thigh bone)**. Nature did not design the femur as a solid rod of bone. Instead, it evolved a **hollow, flanged cross-section** that places material far from the central (neutral) axis. Why? Because the femur (and all bending-dominant members) experiences the **largest stresses at the outermost fibers** (top and bottom) and almost zero stress at the neutral axis.

An **I-section is the engineering embodiment of this biological principle**:

- The **flanges** (top and bottom horizontal plates) carry nearly all of the **bending moment** (like the strong outer cortical bone of the femur).
- The **web** (vertical middle plate) primarily resists **shear force** and maintains the separation between the flanges (analogous to the trabecular bone along the bone's central axis).
- Material near the **neutral axis** is intentionally "missing" — saving **40–60% weight** compared to a solid rectangular section of equal bending strength.

> [!TIP]
> **Robotic Application Intuition:** When a robotic arm lifts a payload, its link members experience large bending moments at the joints. Using an I-section allows the link to be **lightweight (low mass → low inertia → fast acceleration)** yet **extremely stiff in bending (high moment of inertia)**. This is exactly why industrial robot manufacturers like KUKA, FANUC, and ABB heavily use I-section or hollow I-section profiles in their manipulator links.

### 1.4 Why I-Sections Dominate Robotic Structural Design

Three engineering reasons make I-sections the default choice for bending-dominant robotic components:

1. **Maximum Stiffness-to-Weight Ratio:** The moment of inertia $I_{xx}$ of an I-section about its strong axis is comparable to a solid rectangle but with **far less mass**.

2. **Directional Optimization:** Most robotic links bend predominantly in one plane (the vertical plane of motion). The I-section's strong axis ($x-x$) is oriented to resist this dominant bending direction, while the weak axis ($y-y$) resists lateral loads.

3. **Manufacturing & Standardization:** I-sections are mass-produced as standard rolled-steel sections (e.g., ISMB, ISJB, ISWB in Indian Standards; UB, UC in British Standards; W, S, M in American Standards), making them **cheap, readily available, and easily welded** to joint fixtures.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Centroid & Symmetry Argument

For a **symmetric I-section**, locating the centroid is trivial due to **double symmetry**:

$$ \bar{x} = \frac{h}{2}, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{b_f}{2} $$

The **centroid $G$** coincides with the intersection of the two axes of symmetry, so it is located at the **geometric center** of the section.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why double symmetry matters:** If a cross-section has two axes of symmetry, the centroid MUST lie on both axes. The intersection point of these axes is uniquely determined, eliminating the need for any centroidal calculation. This is a guaranteed time-saver in KTU exams.

---

## 2.2 Area of an I-Section

The cross-sectional area is computed by summing the **three rectangular components** (two flanges + one web), then **subtracting the overlapping regions** (or by simple addition of disjoint rectangles):

$$ A = 2 \cdot (b_f \cdot t_f) + (h - 2t_f) \cdot t_w $$

$$ A = 2 b_f t_f + t_w (h - 2 t_f) $$

> The second form avoids double-counting by computing the web height as $(h - 2 t_f)$.

---

## 2.3 Moment of Inertia (Second Moment of Area)

This is the **most critical property** for bending analysis in robotic links. Because of symmetry, the moment of inertia is computed by summing contributions from each rectangle.

### 2.3.1 About the Strong Axis ($x-x$ axis, parallel to flanges)

The strong axis moment of inertia $I_{xx}$ governs **vertical bending** — the dominant loading in most robotic links.

$$ I_{xx} = I_{xx, \text{flanges}} + I_{xx, \text{web}} $$

**Flange contribution (each flange, using parallel axis theorem):**

$$ I_{xx, \text{flange}} = \frac{b_f \cdot t_f^3}{12} + b_f \cdot t_f \cdot \left(\frac{h - t_f}{2}\right)^2 $$

**Web contribution (web is centered, so no parallel axis shift):**

$$ I_{xx, \text{web}} = \frac{t_w \cdot (h - 2t_f)^3}{12} $$

**Total $I_{xx}$:**

$$ I_{xx} = \frac{2 b_f t_f^3}{12} + 2 b_f t_f \left(\frac{h - t_f}{2}\right)^2 + \frac{t_w (h - 2 t_f)^3}{12} $$

Simplifying the dominant flange term:

$$ I_{xx} \approx \frac{b_f (h - t_f)^3 - (b_f - t_w)(h - 2 t_f)^3}{12} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Approximation:** Since $t_f \ll h$ and $t_w \ll b_f$, the dominant contribution to $I_{xx}$ comes from the **flanges**, which are far from the neutral axis. The flanges contribute roughly **85–95%** of the total moment of inertia. The web contributes the remaining small fraction.

### 2.3.2 About the Weak Axis ($y-y$ axis, perpendicular to flanges)

$$ I_{yy} = \frac{t_w^3 \cdot (h - 2t_f)}{12} + 2 \cdot \left[\frac{t_f \cdot b_f^3}{12}\right] $$

$$ I_{yy} = \frac{t_w^3 (h - 2 t_f) + 2 t_f b_f^3}{12} $$

> Since $t_w$ is typically very small, the $t_w^3$ term is often negligible, leaving:
> $$ I_{yy} \approx \frac{t_f \cdot b_f^3}{6} $$

---

## 2.4 Radius of Gyration

The radius of gyration measures how "spread out" the area is from the centroidal axis:

$$ r_{xx} = \sqrt{\frac{I_{xx}}{A}}, \quad r_{yy} = \sqrt{\frac{I_{yy}}{A}} $$

For a standard I-section: $r_{xx} \gg r_{yy}$, confirming that the section is much stiffer about the strong axis.

---

## 2.5 Section Modulus

The section modulus $Z$ converts the moment of inertia into the **maximum bending stress capacity**:

$$ Z_{xx} = \frac{I_{xx}}{y_{\max}} = \frac{I_{xx}}{h/2} = \frac{2 I_{xx}}{h} $$

$$ Z_{yy} = \frac{I_{yy}}{x_{\max}} = \frac{I_{yy}}{b_f/2} = \frac{2 I_{yy}}{b_f} $$

---

## 2.6 Bending Stress Distribution

For a beam/link subjected to a bending moment $M$ about the $x-x$ axis, the bending stress at any fiber distance $y$ from the neutral axis is given by the **flexure formula**:

$$ \sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I_{xx}} $$

The **maximum bending stress** occurs at the **outermost fiber**:

$$ \sigma_{\max} = \frac{M}{Z_{xx}} = \frac{M \cdot h}{2 I_{xx}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention:** Tension (positive) occurs at the bottom fiber (below neutral axis for sagging moment); compression (negative) at the top fiber.

---

## 2.7 Shear Stress Distribution

The transverse shear stress at any vertical distance $y$ from the neutral axis:

$$ \tau = \frac{V \cdot Q_{xx}}{I_{xx} \cdot t} $$

where:
- $V$ = transverse shear force
- $Q_{xx}$ = first moment of area of the portion of section beyond (above or below) the level at which shear stress is computed, about the neutral axis
- $t$ = thickness at that level

**Maximum shear stress** in an I-section occurs at the **neutral axis** (in the web):

$$ \tau_{\max} = \frac{V \cdot Q_{\max}}{I_{xx} \cdot t_w} $$

where $Q_{\max}$ = first moment of the area of one flange about the neutral axis:

$$ Q_{\max} = b_f \cdot t_f \cdot \left(\frac{h - t_f}{2}\right) $$

---

## 2.8 Polar Moment of Inertia

For torsional analysis of robotic links (relevant when links transmit torque about their own axis):

$$ I_p = I_{xx} + I_{yy} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Caution:** The polar moment of inertia of the cross-section ($I_p = I_{xx} + I_{yy}$) is a **geometric property of the cross-section**, NOT the polar moment of inertia used in shaft torsion (which is $J$). For non-circular sections like I-sections, **Saint-Venant's torsion constant $J$** is computed differently using warping functions and is generally much smaller than $I_p$.

---

## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Compilation for KTU 2024 — I-Section**

| # | Property | Formula | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Cross-sectional Area | $A = 2 b_f t_f + t_w (h - 2 t_f)$ | $\text{mm}^2$ or $\text{m}^2$ |
| 2 | Strong-axis MOI | $I_{xx} = \dfrac{2 b_f t_f^3}{12} + 2 b_f t_f \left(\dfrac{h - t_f}{2}\right)^2 + \dfrac{t_w (h - 2t_f)^3}{12}$ | $\text{mm}^4$ or $\text{m}^4$ |
| 3 | Weak-axis MOI | $I_{yy} = \dfrac{t_w^3 (h - 2t_f)}{12} + \dfrac{2 t_f b_f^3}{12}$ | $\text{mm}^4$ or $\text{m}^4$ |
| 4 | Strong-axis Radius of Gyration | $r_{xx} = \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{xx}}{A}}$ | mm or m |
| 5 | Weak-axis Radius of Gyration | $r_{yy} = \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{yy}}{A}}$ | mm or m |
| 6 | Strong-axis Section Modulus | $Z_{xx} = \dfrac{I_{xx}}{h/2} = \dfrac{2 I_{xx}}{h}$ | $\text{mm}^3$ or $\text{m}^3$ |
| 7 | Weak-axis Section Modulus | $Z_{yy} = \dfrac{2 I_{yy}}{b_f}$ | $\text{mm}^3$ or $\text{m}^3$ |
| 8 | Maximum Bending Stress | $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M}{Z_{xx}} = \dfrac{M h}{2 I_{xx}}$ | $\text{N/mm}^2$ (MPa) |
| 9 | Bending Stress at Fiber $y$ | $\sigma = \dfrac{M y}{I_{xx}}$ | MPa |
| 10 | Maximum Shear Stress | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{V \cdot b_f t_f (h - t_f)/2}{I_{xx} \cdot t_w}$ | MPa |
| 11 | General Shear Stress | $\tau = \dfrac{V Q}{I_{xx} \cdot t}$ | MPa |
| 12 | Polar MOI (cross-section) | $I_p = I_{xx} + I_{yy}$ | $\text{mm}^4$ |

---

## 2.10 Engineering Utility in Robotic Structural Design

| Application | Property Used | Design Rationale |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Robot arm link (bending-dominant) | $I_{xx}, Z_{xx}$ | Maximize bending stiffness while minimizing link mass |
| End-effector mounting bracket | $I_{yy}, Z_{yy}$ | Resist lateral/torsional loads from payload |
| Robot base pedestal | $A, r_{xx}, r_{yy}$ | Prevent buckling under compressive reaction loads |
| Gantry crane beam | $I_{xx}, \tau_{\max}$ | Resist combined bending and high shear at supports |
| Cantilevered gripper arm | $\sigma_{\max}$ | Ensure payload-induced stress stays below yield |

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Moment of Inertia of an I-Section about the Strong Axis ($I_{xx}$)

### Step 1: Subdivision of the Cross-Section

An I-section is decomposed into **three simple rectangles** to facilitate integration:

- **Top flange:** Rectangle 1, dimensions $b_f \times t_f$, located at the top.
- **Bottom flange:** Rectangle 2, dimensions $b_f \times t_f$, located at the bottom.
- **Web:** Rectangle 3, dimensions $t_w \times (h - 2 t_f)$, located in the middle.

### Step 2: Establish the Centroidal Reference Frame

Because the section is doubly symmetric, place the origin at the **centroid G** (geometric center). The $x$-axis lies along the neutral axis (horizontal), and the $y$-axis is vertical through the centroid.

### Step 3: Apply the Parallel Axis Theorem

For any rectangle with local centroidal MOI $\bar{I}$ and area $A$, located at distance $d$ from the global centroidal axis:

$$ I = \bar{I} + A d^2 $$

**For each flange (top and bottom are symmetric):**

Local MOI of a flange about its own centroidal horizontal axis:

$$ \bar{I}_{xx, \text{flange}} = \frac{b_f \cdot t_f^3}{12} $$

Distance from flange centroid to global neutral axis:

$$ d_{\text{flange}} = \frac{h}{2} - \frac{t_f}{2} = \frac{h - t_f}{2} $$

Area of one flange:

$$ A_{\text{flange}} = b_f \cdot t_f $$

MOI of one flange about global $x$-axis (parallel axis theorem):

$$ I_{xx, \text{flange}} = \frac{b_f t_f^3}{12} + b_f t_f \cdot \left(\frac{h - t_f}{2}\right)^2 $$

**For the web:**

Local MOI of the web about its own centroidal horizontal axis (web is centered on $x$-axis, so no shift):

$$ I_{xx, \text{web}} = \frac{t_w \cdot (h - 2 t_f)^3}{12} $$

### Step 4: Sum the Contributions

Since both flanges contribute equally:

$$ I_{xx} = 2 \left[ \frac{b_f t_f^3}{12} + b_f t_f \cdot \left(\frac{h - t_f}{2}\right)^2 \right] + \frac{t_w (h - 2 t_f)^3}{12} $$

### Step 5: Algebraic Simplification

Expanding the dominant flange term:

$$ 2 b_f t_f \cdot \left(\frac{h - t_f}{2}\right)^2 = 2 b_f t_f \cdot \frac{(h - t_f)^2}{4} = \frac{b_f t_f (h - t_f)^2}{2} $$

So:

$$ I_{xx} = \frac{b_f t_f^3}{6} + \frac{b_f t_f (h - t_f)^2}{2} + \frac{t_w (h - 2 t_f)^3}{12} $$

### Step 6: Engineering-Useful Compact Form

By factoring and combining, a frequently tabulated compact form emerges:

$$ I_{xx} = \frac{1}{12} \left[ b_f \cdot h^3 - (b_f - t_w)(h - 2 t_f)^3 \right] $$

This compact form is **derived by treating the I-section as a solid rectangle of width $b_f$ and depth $h$, then subtracting two side rectangles of width $(b_f - t_w)/2$ on each side of the web**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Bending Stress in a Robotic Link

### Step 1: Identify the Loading Condition

Consider a robotic manipulator link modeled as a cantilever beam of length $L$, fixed at the robot joint (proximal end) and free at the payload end (distal end). A payload of weight $W$ acts at the free end, creating a bending moment that varies linearly along the span:

$$ M(x) = W \cdot (L - x) $$

where $x$ is measured from the fixed end. The maximum bending moment occurs at the fixed end ($x = 0$):

$$ M_{\max} = W \cdot L $$

### Step 2: Apply the Flexure Formula

The bending stress at any cross-section at distance $x$:

$$ \sigma(x, y) = \frac{M(x) \cdot y}{I_{xx}} $$

The maximum bending stress in the link occurs at the **fixed end, at the outermost fiber** ($y = h/2$):

$$ \sigma_{\max} = \frac{M_{\max} \cdot (h/2)}{I_{xx}} = \frac{W L}{I_{xx} / (h/2)} = \frac{W L}{Z_{xx}} $$

### Step 3: Safety Check Against Yield

The link is safe if:

$$ \sigma_{\max} \leq \frac{\sigma_{\text{yield}}}{\text{FoS}} $$

where $\text{FoS}$ = Factor of Safety (typically 1.5 to 3 in robotic design).

### Step 4: Design Equation for Required Section Modulus

Rearranging:

$$ Z_{xx, \text{required}} = \frac{W \cdot L \cdot \text{FoS}}{\sigma_{\text{yield}}} $$

This is the equation used to **select an appropriate standard I-section** from a steel section table.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example Problem (KTU-Style)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A robot manipulator link is modeled as a cantilever of length $L = 800 \text{ mm}$ carrying an end payload of $W = 1.5 \text{ kN}$. The link uses a standard I-section with the following dimensions:
>
> $h = 200 \text{ mm}$, $b_f = 100 \text{ mm}$, $t_f = 10 \text{ mm}$, $t_w = 6 \text{ mm}$.
>
> **Compute:** (i) Cross-sectional area, (ii) Moment of inertia $I_{xx}$, (iii) Section modulus $Z_{xx}$, (iv) Maximum bending stress, (v) Maximum shear stress.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**(i) Cross-sectional Area:**

$$ A = 2 b_f t_f + t_w (h - 2 t_f) $$

$$ A = 2 \times 100 \times 10 + 6 \times (200 - 20) $$

$$ A = 2000 + 6 \times 180 = 2000 + 1080 $$

$$ \boxed{A = 3080 \text{ mm}^2 = 3.08 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2} $$

**(ii) Moment of Inertia $I_{xx}$:**

Using the compact form:

$$ I_{xx} = \frac{1}{12} \left[ b_f \cdot h^3 - (b_f - t_w)(h - 2 t_f)^3 \right] $$

Computing each term:

$$ b_f \cdot h^3 = 100 \times (200)^3 = 100 \times 8{,}000{,}000 = 800 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4 $$

$$ (b_f - t_w) = 100 - 6 = 94 \text{ mm} $$

$$ (h - 2 t_f) = 200 - 20 = 180 \text{ mm} $$

$$ (h - 2 t_f)^3 = (180)^3 = 5{,}832{,}000 \text{ mm}^3 $$

$$ (b_f - t_w)(h - 2 t_f)^3 = 94 \times 5{,}832{,}000 = 548{,}208{,}000 \text{ mm}^4 $$

Therefore:

$$ I_{xx} = \frac{1}{12} \left[ 800 \times 10^6 - 548.208 \times 10^6 \right] $$

$$ I_{xx} = \frac{1}{12} \times 251.792 \times 10^6 $$

$$ \boxed{I_{xx} = 20.9827 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4 = 2.098 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^4} $$

**(iii) Section Modulus $Z_{xx}$:**

$$ Z_{xx} = \frac{2 I_{xx}}{h} = \frac{2 \times 20.9827 \times 10^6}{200} $$

$$ \boxed{Z_{xx} = 209{,}827 \text{ mm}^3 = 2.098 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3} $$

**(iv) Maximum Bending Stress:**

$$ M_{\max} = W \cdot L = 1.5 \times 10^3 \text{ N} \times 0.8 \text{ m} = 1200 \text{ N·m} $$

$$ \sigma_{\max} = \frac{M_{\max}}{Z_{xx}} = \frac{1200}{2.098 \times 10^{-4}} $$

$$ \boxed{\sigma_{\max} = 5.72 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2 = 5.72 \text{ MPa}} $$

**(v) Maximum Shear Stress:**

$$ V_{\max} = W = 1500 \text{ N} $$

$$ Q_{\max} = b_f t_f \cdot \frac{(h - t_f)}{2} = 100 \times 10 \times \frac{(200 - 10)}{2} $$

$$ Q_{\max} = 1000 \times 95 = 95{,}000 \text{ mm}^3 = 9.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3 $$

$$ \tau_{\max} = \frac{V_{\max} \cdot Q_{\max}}{I_{xx} \cdot t_w} = \frac{1500 \times 9.5 \times 10^{-5}}{2.098 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.006} $$

$$ \tau_{\max} = \frac{0.1425}{1.259 \times 10^{-7}} $$

$$ \boxed{\tau_{\max} = 1.132 \times 10^{6} \text{ N/m}^2 = 1.132 \text{ MPa}} $$

### Verification Check:

Both stresses (5.72 MPa bending, 1.132 MPa shear) are well below the yield strength of structural steel ($\sigma_y \approx 250 \text{ MPa}$), confirming the section is **vastly overdesigned** for this loading — but valid for a lightweight robotic link application where stiffness (deflection) rather than strength is the governing criterion.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | I-Section | Solid Rectangle (equal area) | Solid Rectangle (equal depth) | Circular Hollow Section |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Mass for same $I_{xx}$ | **Lowest** | Highest | Moderate | Low |
| $I_{xx}$ for same mass | **Highest** | Lowest | Moderate | High |
| Shear capacity | Moderate (web) | Highest | Highest | Moderate |
| Torsional capacity | **Poor** (open section) | Poor | Poor | **Highest** (closed) |
| Fabrication ease | High (standard) | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate |
| Robotic link suitability | **Excellent** for bending | Overweight | Moderate | Good for torsion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Insight:** I-sections are **poor in torsion** because they are open thin-walled sections. For robotic links that experience significant torque (e.g., wrist joints, screw-driven actuators), an I-section should be **closed** (boxed) or supplemented with a hollow circular cap.

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow: I-Section Geometric Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ISection Cross Section] --> B[Component 1 Top Flange]
    A --> C[Component 2 Bottom Flange]
    A --> D[Component 3 Central Web]

    B --> B1[Width bf]
    B --> B2[Thickness tf]
    B --> B3[Centroid at distance h minus tf over 2 from NA]

    C --> C1[Width bf]
    C --> C2[Thickness tf]
    C --> C3[Centroid at distance h minus tf over 2 from NA below]

    D --> D1[Thickness tw]
    D --> D2[Height h minus 2 tf]
    D --> D3[Centroid coincides with Neutral Axis NA]

    E[Key Geometric Axes] --> E1[Strong Axis x-x horizontal through NA]
    E --> E2[Weak Axis y-y vertical through NA]
    E --> E3[Centroid G at intersection of x-x and y-y]

    F[Material Distribution Principle] --> F1[Flanges far from NA 85 to 95 percent of Ixx]
    F --> F2[Web near NA carries shear primarily]
    F --> F3[Material near NA minimized for weight savings]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: I-Section Property Computation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input[Input Geometric Parameters h bf tf tw] --> StepA[Step 1 Compute Area A]
    Input --> StepB[Step 2 Identify Centroid G by Symmetry]

    StepA --> StepC[Step 3 Compute Ixx Parallel Axis Theorem]
    StepA --> StepD[Step 4 Compute Iyy Parallel Axis Theorem]

    StepC --> StepE[Step 5 Compute Radius of Gyration rxx]
    StepD --> StepF[Step 6 Compute Radius of Gyration ryy]

    StepC --> StepG[Step 7 Compute Section Modulus Zxx]
    StepD --> StepH[Step 8 Compute Section Modulus Zyy]

    StepG --> StepI[Step 9 Apply Flexure Formula sigma max]
    StepG --> StepJ[Step 10 Apply Shear Formula tau max]

    StepI --> Output[Final Design Verification vs Yield Strength]
    StepJ --> Output

    Output --> Pass[Safe Design sigma max less than sigma yield by FoS]
    Output --> Fail[Revise Section  Increase h or bf]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: Stress Distribution in an I-Section under Bending

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Load[Applied Bending Moment M] --> Beam[Robot Link as Cantilever]

    Beam --> NA[Neutral Axis at Centroid]

    NA --> Top[Top Fiber y equals plus h over 2]
    NA --> Bot[Bottom Fiber y equals minus h over 2]

    Top --> Comp[Maximum COMPRESSIVE Stress sigma c]
    Bot --> Tens[Maximum TENSILE Stress sigma t]

    Comp --> Mag1[Magnitude equals M times h over 2 divided by Ixx]
    Tens --> Mag2[Magnitude equals M times h over 2 divided by Ixx]

    NA --> ZeroStress[Zero Bending Stress at NA]

    Beam --> WebShear[Web Carries Transverse Shear V]
    WebShear --> TauMax[Maximum Shear Stress in Web at NA]
    TauMax --> TauFormula[Tau equals V times Qmax divided by Ixx times tw]
```

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper Style]**

### Question A1: [3 Marks]

**Define an I-section. List any two advantages of using I-sections in robotic manipulator links.**

**Model Answer:**

An I-section is a structural cross-sectional profile resembling the capital letter "I", consisting of two horizontal flanges connected by a central vertical web. It possesses two axes of symmetry, with the centroid located at the geometric center.

**Two advantages in robotic manipulator links:**

1. **High stiffness-to-weight ratio:** The flanges placed far from the neutral axis provide a high moment of inertia ($I_{xx}$) for minimum mass, enabling faster robot acceleration and reduced motor torque requirements.

2. **Standardized availability:** I-sections are mass-produced as standard rolled-steel sections (e.g., ISMB, ISWB), making them economical, readily available, and easy to weld to joint fixtures in robotic assemblies.

---

### Question A2: [3 Marks]

**State the formula for the moment of inertia of an I-section about its strong axis ($x-x$). Define each term.**

**Model Answer:**

$$ I_{xx} = \frac{1}{12} \left[ b_f \cdot h^3 - (b_f - t_w)(h - 2 t_f)^3 \right] $$

where:
- $I_{xx}$ = moment of inertia about the strong (horizontal) axis, in $\text{mm}^4$
- $b_f$ = flange width, in mm
- $h$ = overall depth of the section, in mm
- $t_w$ = web thickness, in mm
- $t_f$ = flange thickness, in mm

This compact form treats the I-section as a full rectangle of dimensions $b_f \times h$ from which two side rectangles (each of width $(b_f - t_w)/2$ and height $(h - 2 t_f)$) are subtracted.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU End Semester Examination Style — Module 4]**

### ❓ Question A: [14 Marks]

**(a)** Derive an expression for the moment of inertia of a standard symmetric I-section about its strong axis ($x-x$ axis). State all assumptions clearly. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A robot manipulator link of length $L = 1.2 \text{ m}$ is modeled as a cantilever carrying an end payload of $2 \text{ kN}$. The link cross-section is a standard I-section with $h = 250 \text{ mm}$, $b_f = 125 \text{ mm}$, $t_f = 12 \text{ mm}$, and $t_w = 8 \text{ mm}$. Compute:
   (i) Cross-sectional area $A$
   (ii) Moment of inertia $I_{xx}$
   (iii) Maximum bending stress $\sigma_{\max}$
   (iv) Section modulus $Z_{xx}$

**[7 Marks]**

#### ✅ Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) Derivation of $I_{xx}$ for an I-Section:**

**Assumptions:**
- The section is doubly symmetric.
- Fillets and root radii are neglected.
- Material is homogeneous and isotropic.

**Step 1 — Decomposition:** Subdivide the I-section into three rectangles:
- Top flange: $b_f \times t_f$
- Bottom flange: $b_f \times t_f$
- Web: $t_w \times (h - 2 t_f)$

**Step 2 — Reference frame:** Place origin at centroid $G$ (geometric center by symmetry).

**Step 3 — Parallel axis theorem** for each component:

For each flange, distance from its centroid to global $x$-axis:
$$ d = \frac{h - t_f}{2} $$

Contribution of one flange:
$$ I_{\text{flange}} = \frac{b_f t_f^3}{12} + b_f t_f \left(\frac{h - t_f}{2}\right)^2 $$

Web contribution (centered, no shift):
$$ I_{\text{web}} = \frac{t_w (h - 2 t_f)^3}{12} $$

**Step 4 — Total $I_{xx}$:**

$$ I_{xx} = \frac{2 b_f t_f^3}{12} + 2 b_f t_f \left(\frac{h - t_f}{2}\right)^2 + \frac{t_w (h - 2 t_f)^3}{12} $$

Simplifying:

$$ \boxed{I_{xx} = \frac{1}{12}\left[ b_f h^3 - (b_f - t_w)(h - 2 t_f)^3 \right]} $$

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**(i) Cross-sectional area:**

$$ A = 2 \times 125 \times 12 + 8 \times (250 - 24) $$

$$ A = 3000 + 8 \times 226 = 3000 + 1808 $$

$$ \boxed{A = 4808 \text{ mm}^2} $$

**(ii) Moment of inertia $I_{xx}$:**

$$ I_{xx} = \frac{1}{12}\left[ 125 \times (250)^3 - (125 - 8)(250 - 24)^3 \right] $$

$$ = \frac{1}{12}\left[ 125 \times 15{,}625{,}000 - 117 \times (226)^3 \right] $$

$$ = \frac{1}{12}\left[ 1.9531 \times 10^9 - 117 \times 11{,}543{,}176 \right] $$

$$ = \frac{1}{12}\left[ 1.9531 \times 10^9 - 1.35055 \times 10^9 \right] $$

$$ = \frac{1}{12} \times 0.60258 \times 10^9 $$

$$ \boxed{I_{xx} = 50.215 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4 = 5.0215 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^4} $$

**(iii) Section modulus:**

$$ Z_{xx} = \frac{2 I_{xx}}{h} = \frac{2 \times 50.215 \times 10^6}{250} $$

$$ \boxed{Z_{xx} = 401{,}720 \text{ mm}^3 = 4.0172 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3} $$

**(iv) Maximum bending stress:**

$$ M_{\max} = W \cdot L = 2000 \times 1.2 = 2400 \text{ N·m} $$

$$ \sigma_{\max} = \frac{M_{\max}}{Z_{xx}} = \frac{2400}{4.0172 \times 10^{-4}} $$

$$ \boxed{\sigma_{\max} = 5.974 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 5.974 \text{ MPa}} $$

---

### ❓ Question B: [14 Marks] — (Internal Alternative Choice)

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the stress distribution in an I-section under pure bending. Identify the locations of maximum tensile, compressive, and shear stresses. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** An industrial robot arm link uses a standard I-section: $h = 300 \text{ mm}$, $b_f = 150 \text{ mm}$, $t_f = 15 \text{ mm}$, $t_w = 10 \text{ mm}$. If the link is subjected to a maximum bending moment of $M = 50 \text{ kN·m}$ and a transverse shear force of $V = 25 \text{ kN}$, calculate:
   (i) The maximum bending stress $\sigma_{\max}$
   (ii) The maximum shear stress $\tau_{\max}$
   (iii) The radius of gyration $r_{xx}$

**[7 Marks]**

#### ✅ Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) Stress Distribution Sketch & Explanation:**

*[Schematic: An I-section cross-section with bending moment $M$ applied, showing:*
*- linear stress distribution from $-\sigma_{\max}$ at top to $+\sigma_{\max}$ at bottom*
*- zero stress at neutral axis*
*- parabolic shear stress distribution with maximum in the web at the neutral axis]*

**Explanation:**

Under pure bending, the bending stress $\sigma$ varies **linearly** with the distance $y$ from the neutral axis (NA), according to the flexure formula $\sigma = My / I_{xx}$.

- **Maximum compressive stress** occurs at the **topmost fiber** ($y = +h/2$):
  $$ \sigma_c = -\frac{M h}{2 I_{xx}} $$
- **Maximum tensile stress** occurs at the **bottommost fiber** ($y = -h/2$):
  $$ \sigma_t = +\frac{M h}{2 I_{xx}} $$
- **Zero bending stress** occurs at the **neutral axis**.

The **shear stress distribution** is **parabolic** in the web and **constant** in the flanges:

- **Maximum shear stress** occurs in the **web at the neutral axis**:
  $$ \tau_{\max} = \frac{V Q_{\max}}{I_{xx} \cdot t_w} $$
- Shear stress is approximately **zero** in the outer parts of the flanges (at the extreme top and bottom).

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

Given: $h = 300 \text{ mm}$, $b_f = 150 \text{ mm}$, $t_f = 15 \text{ mm}$, $t_w = 10 \text{ mm}$, $M = 50 \text{ kN·m} = 50 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$, $V = 25 \text{ kN} = 25 \times 10^3 \text{ N}$.

**Preliminary — Moment of inertia $I_{xx}$:**

$$ I_{xx} = \frac{1}{12}\left[ b_f h^3 - (b_f - t_w)(h - 2 t_f)^3 \right] $$

$$ = \frac{1}{12}\left[ 150 \times (300)^3 - (150 - 10)(300 - 30)^3 \right] $$

$$ = \frac{1}{12}\left[ 150 \times 27 \times 10^6 - 140 \times (270)^3 \right] $$

$$ = \frac{1}{12}\left[ 4.05 \times 10^9 - 140 \times 19{,}683{,}000 \right] $$

$$ = \frac{1}{12}\left[ 4.05 \times 10^9 - 2.7556 \times 10^9 \right] $$

$$ = \frac{1}{12} \times 1.2944 \times 10^9 $$

$$ I_{xx} = 107.86 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4 $$

**(i) Maximum bending stress:**

$$ Z_{xx} = \frac{2 I_{xx}}{h} = \frac{2 \times 107.86 \times 10^6}{300} = 719.07 \times 10^3 \text{ mm}^3 $$

$$ \sigma_{\max} = \frac{M}{Z_{xx}} = \frac{50 \times 10^6}{719.07 \times 10^3} $$

$$ \boxed{\sigma_{\max} = 69.53 \text{ MPa}} $$

**(ii) Maximum shear stress:**

$$ Q_{\max} = b_f \cdot t_f \cdot \frac{(h - t_f)}{2} = 150 \times 15 \times \frac{(300 - 15)}{2} $$

$$ = 2250 \times 142.5 = 320{,}625 \text{ mm}^3 $$

$$ \tau_{\max} = \frac{V \cdot Q_{\max}}{I_{xx} \cdot t_w} = \frac{25 \times 10^3 \times 320{,}625}{107.86 \times 10^6 \times 10} $$

$$ = \frac{8.0156 \times 10^9}{1.0786 \times 10^9} $$

$$ \boxed{\tau_{\max} = 7.43 \text{ MPa}} $$

**(iii) Radius of gyration $r_{xx}$:**

$$ A = 2 b_f t_f + t_w (h - 2 t_f) = 2 \times 150 \times 15 + 10 \times (300 - 30) $$

$$ A = 4500 + 2700 = 7200 \text{ mm}^2 $$

$$ r_{xx} = \sqrt{\frac{I_{xx}}{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{107.86 \times 10^6}{7200}} $$

$$ r_{xx} = \sqrt{14{,}980.6} $$

$$ \boxed{r_{xx} = 122.4 \text{ mm}} $$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $(h - 2t_f)$ term:** Many students incorrectly use the full web height $h$ instead of $h - 2 t_f$ when computing the web's contribution to $I_{xx}$. This leads to overestimation by ~20–30%. **Always check: web height = total depth − 2 × flange thickness.**
>
> 2. **Mixing up units:** Using $I_{xx}$ in mm⁴ while $M$ is in N·m produces stress in mixed units. **Convert everything consistently to mm and N** before plugging in.
>
> 3. **Omitting the parallel axis term:** A common error is to compute only $\bar{I}$ (the local centroidal MOI) and forget the $Ad^2$ term for the flanges. Since flanges are far from the NA, the $Ad^2$ term is the **dominant contributor** to $I_{xx}$ — omitting it yields a 90% error.
>
> 4. **Confusing $I_{xx}$ with $I_{yy}$:** Many students interchange the strong and weak axes. **Strong axis is along the flanges (horizontal, larger $I$)**; weak axis is perpendicular (vertical, smaller $I$).
>
> 5. **Sign convention errors in stress:** Forgetting to label tensile vs compressive stresses (or omitting negative signs for compression) costs marks in stress distribution sketches.
>
> 6. **Skipping the schematic in Part (a):** The question explicitly asks for a "neat sketch" — examiners **deduct 1–2 marks** for omitting it. Always draw the cross-section with axes, stress profile, and labeled locations of maximum stresses.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — I-Section Module 4**

- ☐ An **I-section** has two flanges and a web, with **double symmetry** placing the centroid at the geometric center.
- ☐ The **strong axis (x-x)** lies along the flanges and gives the **largest** moment of inertia $I_{xx}$.
- ☐ The **weak axis (y-y)** is perpendicular to the flanges and gives the much smaller $I_{yy}$.
- ☐ The **compact formula** for $I_{xx}$ is:
  $$ I_{xx} = \frac{1}{12}\left[ b_f h^3 - (b_f - t_w)(h - 2 t_f)^3 \right] $$
- ☐ The **web height** in all formulas is $(h - 2 t_f)$, never just $h$.
- ☐ The **parallel axis theorem** is essential — the $Ad^2$ term for flanges dominates $I_{xx}$.
- ☐ **Section modulus** $Z_{xx} = 2 I_{xx} / h$ converts MOI into a usable bending stress measure.
- ☐ **Maximum bending stress** is at the **outermost fiber** ($y = \pm h/2$): $\sigma_{\max} = M / Z_{xx}$.
- ☐ **Maximum shear stress** is in the **web at the neutral axis**: $\tau_{\max} = V Q_{\max} / (I_{xx} t_w)$.
- ☐ Bending stress distribution is **linear**; shear stress distribution is **parabolic**.
- ☐ For robotic links, I-sections provide **maximum bending stiffness per unit mass**.
- ☐ I-sections are **poor in torsion** — use closed sections (boxed or circular hollow) when torque is significant.
- ☐ Always check **units consistency** (mm–N–MPa or m–N–Pa) before final numerical substitution.
- ☐ In KTU exams, **always sketch** the cross-section, axes, centroid, and stress profiles for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT525 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS OF ROBOTIC PARTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** T Section

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — T SECTION: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **T-Section** (also called a **Tee-Section** or **T-Beam**) is a structural cross-sectional profile consisting of a horizontal top element called the **flange** and a vertical bottom element called the **web** (or **stem**), joined together to form a shape resembling the capital letter **"T"**. In the context of **Structural Analysis of Robotic Parts**, a T-section represents a load-bearing structural member of a robotic manipulator, end-effector, or chassis link whose cross-section exhibits this T-shaped geometry.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the T-section is classified as a **built-up unsymmetrical section** (when the flange and web have different widths/thicknesses) and is a fundamental shape in the analysis of **flexural members** used in robotic arm links, gantry structures, and lightweight manipulator frames.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PERAT525 Module 4):**
> The T-Section is studied under the umbrella of *Moment of Inertia of Built-Up Sections* and *Bending Stresses in Composite Profiles*. Mastery of centroid calculation, parallel axis theorem application, and bending stress distribution is mandatory for the End Semester Examination (ESE).

### 1.2 Geometric Anatomy of a T-Section

A T-Section is fully defined by **four standard geometric parameters**:

| Parameter | Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Flange Width | $B$ | Total horizontal width of the top flange |
| Flange Thickness | $t_f$ | Vertical thickness of the flange |
| Web Depth | $D$ | Total vertical depth of the web |
| Web Thickness | $t_w$ | Horizontal thickness of the web |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Convention:** In KTU board problems, the **flange is always placed at the top** and the **web hangs below** the flange. The reference axis (X-X) is taken along the bottom of the web (most extreme fiber in tension when used as a cantilever beam in robotic arms).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Table-Top" Intuition

Imagine a simple **dining table** viewed from its end (cross-section). The horizontal tabletop is the **flange** — broad, flat, and good at resisting sideways bending. The vertical leg supporting it is the **web** — narrow but tall, good at resisting vertical loads. Together they form a "T" shape.

**Real-world robotic analogy:** A robotic arm link that must carry a heavy payload at its tip behaves exactly like an inverted table. The flange (at the top) provides the wide area to resist **bending moment from the payload weight**, while the web (hanging down) connects it to the joint/motor. This is why T-sections are heavily used in:
- **Robotic arm links** of SCARA and Cartesian robots
- **End-effector mounting plates**
- **Gantry crane beams** in automated warehouses
- **CNC machine gantries** in pick-and-place systems

> [!TIP]
> **Why a T and not a Rectangle?** A rectangular section of the same area and depth uses material in the center, where stress is LOW. A T-section removes that low-stress center material, putting it instead at the **extreme fibers (top and bottom)** where bending stress is **maximum**. This gives a **higher moment of inertia per unit weight** — critical for robots where every gram matters for payload capacity and motor torque.

## 1.4 Why T-Sections Are Critical in Robotic Structural Design

1. **Weight Optimization**: Robots need to be light to minimize motor torque. T-sections offer high stiffness-to-weight ratios.
2. **Asymmetric Loading**: Robotic arms often have asymmetric bending (payload on one side). The T-shape aligns material where it's needed most.
3. **Manufacturing Simplicity**: T-sections are easily fabricated by welding a flat plate (flange) to a vertical plate (web) — a common method in custom robotic frames.
4. **Standardization**: Aluminum T-extrusions are off-the-shelf standard items in modular robotic construction kits (e.g., 80/20 profiles, V-slot extrusions).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# MODULE 4 — T SECTION: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three-Stage Analysis Protocol (KTU Board Pattern)

Every KTU examination problem on a T-Section follows this exact three-stage protocol. Internalizing this sequence guarantees full marks.

### **Stage 1: Locate the Centroid (Neutral Axis Position)**
The neutral axis (N.A.) of a T-section does **NOT** pass through the geometric center. Because the flange (top) has more area concentrated at the top, the centroid lies **closer to the flange** and **farther from the bottom tip of the web**.

The centroid $\bar{y}$ measured from a chosen reference axis is:

$$\bar{y} = \frac{\sum A_i \, y_i}{\sum A_i}$$

where:
- $A_i$ = area of each rectangular sub-part
- $y_i$ = distance from the reference axis to the centroid of each sub-part

### **Stage 2: Calculate the Moment of Inertia ($I_{NA}$)**
Using the **Parallel Axis Theorem (Steiner's Theorem)**:

$$I_{NA} = \sum \left[ I_{G,i} + A_i \, d_i^2 \right]$$

where:
- $I_{G,i}$ = moment of inertia of sub-part $i$ about its own centroidal axis
- $d_i$ = perpendicular distance from the centroid of sub-part $i$ to the Neutral Axis (N.A.)
- $A_i$ = area of sub-part $i$

For a rectangle of width $b$ and height $h$ about its own horizontal centroidal axis: $I_G = \dfrac{b h^3}{12}$

### **Stage 3: Compute Bending Stress & Shear Stress**
- **Bending stress** at any fiber distance $y$ from N.A.: $\sigma = \dfrac{M \, y}{I_{NA}}$
- **Maximum bending stress** occurs at the **extreme fiber** (farthest point from N.A.):

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M \cdot y_{max}}{I_{NA}}$$

- **Shear stress** at a distance $y$ from N.A.:

$$\tau = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I_{NA} \cdot b}$$

where $Q$ is the first moment of area of the portion beyond the cut, and $b$ is the width at the cut.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table. Memorize it. Every KTU T-section problem uses these exact formulas.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Area | $A = A_f + A_w = B \cdot t_f + t_w \cdot D$ | $mm^2$ | $A_f$ = flange, $A_w$ = web |
| 2 | Centroid from bottom | $\bar{y} = \dfrac{(B t_f)(D + t_f/2) + (t_w D)(D/2)}{A}$ | $mm$ | Reference: bottom of web |
| 3 | Centroid from top | $\bar{y}_{top} = (D + t_f) - \bar{y}$ | $mm$ | Used to find max distance |
| 4 | Moment of Inertia (own CG) - Flange | $I_{G,f} = \dfrac{B \cdot t_f^3}{12}$ | $mm^4$ | About flange's own centroid |
| 5 | Moment of Inertia (own CG) - Web | $I_{G,w} = \dfrac{t_w \cdot D^3}{12}$ | $mm^4$ | About web's own centroid |
| 6 | Parallel Axis Distance - Flange | $d_f = (D + t_f/2) - \bar{y}$ | $mm$ | Distance from flange CG to N.A. |
| 7 | Parallel Axis Distance - Web | $d_w = \bar{y} - D/2$ | $mm$ | Distance from web CG to N.A. |
| 8 | Total $I_{NA}$ | $I_{NA} = I_{G,f} + A_f d_f^2 + I_{G,w} + A_w d_w^2$ | $mm^4$ | Final answer for bending |
| 9 | Section Modulus (top) | $Z_{top} = \dfrac{I_{NA}}{\bar{y}_{top}}$ | $mm^3$ | Resistance modulus |
| 10 | Section Modulus (bottom) | $Z_{bot} = \dfrac{I_{NA}}{\bar{y}}$ | $mm^3$ | Usually larger (taller side) |
| 11 | Max Bending Stress | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M_{max}}{Z_{min}}$ | $N/mm^2$ | Use smaller $Z$ |
| 12 | Shear Stress (in web) | $\tau = \dfrac{V \cdot Q_y}{I_{NA} \cdot t_w}$ | $N/mm^2$ | Critical at N.A. |
| 13 | First Moment at N.A. | $Q_{NA} = A_{web} \cdot \bar{y}_{web-to-NA}$ | $mm^3$ | Used in shear formula |
| 14 | Radius of Gyration | $k = \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{NA}}{A}}$ | $mm$ | For buckling checks |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In the formulas above, $\bar{y}$ uses `bar y` (LaTeX command) — never write `|y|` with vertical pipes inside markdown table cells, as this breaks the table. Always use `\bar{y}` for the centroid.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility in Robotics

The moment of inertia $I_{NA}$ of a robotic link is the single most important geometric property in structural analysis. It directly determines:

1. **Deflection** of the link under load: $\delta = \dfrac{W L^3}{3 E I_{NA}}$ (cantilever)
2. **Natural frequency** of vibration: $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$, where $k$ depends on $EI_{NA}$
3. **Critical buckling load** for compression members: $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I_{NA}}{L_e^2}$
4. **Required motor torque**: A higher $I_{NA}$ means lower deflection, meaning more precise positioning — the holy grail of robotic structural design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real Robotic Example:** The KUKA KR 16 robot's arm links use T-section aluminum extrusions. By moving material from the center of a rectangular section to the flange tips, engineers achieved a 40% weight reduction with only 8% loss in stiffness — directly extending the robot's payload capacity and reducing motor sizing costs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# MODULE 4 — T SECTION: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Centroid Location of a T-Section

### **Given Problem Setup**
Consider a T-section with:
- Flange: width $B = 120$ mm, thickness $t_f = 20$ mm
- Web: depth $D = 100$ mm, thickness $t_w = 20$ mm

**Reference Axis:** Bottom of the web (most common in KTU problems).

### **Step 1: Decompose into Rectangles**

Split the T-section into two non-overlapping rectangles:

**Rectangle 1 — Flange (top horizontal slab):**
- Area: $A_f = B \times t_f = 120 \times 20 = 2400 \text{ mm}^2$
- Centroid location from bottom: The flange sits on top of the web, so its centroid is at height $D + t_f/2$
- $y_f = 100 + 20/2 = 100 + 10 = 110$ mm

**Rectangle 2 — Web (vertical stem):**
- Area: $A_w = t_w \times D = 20 \times 100 = 2000 \text{ mm}^2$
- Centroid location from bottom: The web's centroid is at half its depth
- $y_w = D/2 = 100/2 = 50$ mm

### **Step 2: Apply the Centroid Formula**

$$\bar{y} = \frac{\sum A_i \, y_i}{\sum A_i} = \frac{A_f \cdot y_f + A_w \cdot y_w}{A_f + A_w}$$

Substituting values:

$$\bar{y} = \frac{(2400)(110) + (2000)(50)}{2400 + 2000}$$

$$\bar{y} = \frac{264000 + 100000}{4400}$$

$$\bar{y} = \frac{364000}{4400} = 82.727 \text{ mm}$$

**Distance from top of flange to centroid:**

$$\bar{y}_{top} = (D + t_f) - \bar{y} = (100 + 20) - 82.727 = 120 - 82.727 = 37.273 \text{ mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The neutral axis lies **37.273 mm below the top of the flange** and **82.727 mm above the bottom of the web**. Notice it is **NOT** at the geometric center (which would be 60 mm from each end). It is biased toward the flange because the flange carries more area at the top.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Moment of Inertia $I_{NA}$

### **Step 3: Calculate Distance from Each Sub-Part's Centroid to N.A.**

**For the Flange:**

$$d_f = y_f - \bar{y} = 110 - 82.727 = 27.273 \text{ mm}$$

**For the Web:**

$$d_w = \bar{y} - y_w = 82.727 - 50 = 32.727 \text{ mm}$$

### **Step 4: Calculate $I_G$ for Each Sub-Part About Its Own Centroidal Axis**

**Flange's own moment of inertia (about its own horizontal centroid, parallel to N.A.):**

$$I_{G,f} = \frac{B \cdot t_f^3}{12} = \frac{120 \times 20^3}{12} = \frac{120 \times 8000}{12} = 80000 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Web's own moment of inertia:**

$$I_{G,w} = \frac{t_w \cdot D^3}{12} = \frac{20 \times 100^3}{12} = \frac{20 \times 1000000}{12} = 1666666.67 \text{ mm}^4$$

### **Step 5: Apply the Parallel Axis Theorem**

$$I_{NA} = I_{G,f} + A_f \cdot d_f^2 + I_{G,w} + A_w \cdot d_w^2$$

Computing each term:

**Flange transfer term:**

$$A_f \cdot d_f^2 = 2400 \times (27.273)^2 = 2400 \times 743.80 = 1785131.4 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Web transfer term:**

$$A_w \cdot d_w^2 = 2000 \times (32.727)^2 = 2000 \times 1070.99 = 2141983.5 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Summing all four terms:**

$$I_{NA} = 80000 + 1785131.4 + 1666666.67 + 2141983.5$$

$$I_{NA} = 5673781.57 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$\boxed{I_{NA} \approx 5.674 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4}$$

### **Step 6: Section Modulus Calculation**

**Top section modulus:**

$$Z_{top} = \frac{I_{NA}}{\bar{y}_{top}} = \frac{5.674 \times 10^6}{37.273} = 152229.7 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Bottom section modulus:**

$$Z_{bot} = \frac{I_{NA}}{\bar{y}} = \frac{5.674 \times 10^6}{82.727} = 68586.6 \text{ mm}^3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** $Z_{top} > Z_{bot}$ because the bottom of the web is FARTHER from the N.A. than the top of the flange. This means **maximum bending stress occurs at the bottom of the web** for a given moment, making the **bottom tip of the web the critical (most stressed) fiber**.

## 3.3 Application Example: Bending Stress in a Robotic Arm Link

**Problem:** The T-section above is used as a horizontal link in a robotic arm. The link is 800 mm long, simply supported, and carries a central point load of 5 kN (representing the weight of a gripped object). Find the maximum bending stress.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Maximum bending moment** (simply supported beam, central point load):

$$M_{max} = \frac{P \cdot L}{4} = \frac{5000 \times 800}{4} = 1000000 \text{ N·mm} = 1 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 2: Critical section modulus** (smaller value gives larger stress):

$$Z_{min} = Z_{bot} = 68586.6 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 3: Maximum bending stress:**

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z_{min}} = \frac{1000000}{68586.6} = 14.58 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 14.58 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Check against allowable stress** (aluminum 6061-T6 allowable $\approx 55$ MPa):

$$\sigma_{max} = 14.58 \text{ MPa} \ll 55 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark \text{ SAFE}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Deflection Check (Bonus):** For the same beam, the maximum deflection would be $\delta = \dfrac{P L^3}{48 E I_{NA}}$. With $E = 70000$ N/mm² for aluminum: $\delta = \dfrac{5000 \times 800^3}{48 \times 70000 \times 5.674 \times 10^6} = 1.34$ mm. Most industrial robots require deflection $< L/500 = 1.6$ mm — so this design is acceptable.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table

| Property | Rectangular Section ($120 \times 120$ mm) | T-Section (our example) | % Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| Area | 14400 mm² | 4400 mm² | **−69.4%** (huge weight saving) |
| $I_{NA}$ | $1.728 \times 10^7$ mm⁴ | $5.674 \times 10^6$ mm⁴ | **−67.2%** |
| Stiffness-to-Weight Ratio | $1200$ | $1290$ | **+7.5%** (better!) |
| Max Bending Stress (same M) | 6.94 MPa | 14.58 MPa | +110% (acceptable if safe) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Trade-off Insight:** The T-section uses **69% less material** but retains **33% of the original stiffness**. The stiffness-to-weight ratio is actually slightly *better* than the equivalent rectangle, making the T-section the optimal choice for weight-critical robotic links.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
