# MODULE 4 — T SECTION: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Short-Answer Conceptual** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Define a T-section. Why is the neutral axis of a T-section not located at its geometric center?

**Model Answer:**

A **T-section** is a built-up structural cross-section consisting of a horizontal **flange** at the top and a vertical **web** at the bottom, forming a shape resembling the letter "T". It is widely used in robotic arm links and machine frames.

The neutral axis (N.A.) of a T-section does **not** pass through the geometric center because the **flange has more cross-sectional area concentrated near the top** than the web does near the bottom. Since the centroid is the "area-weighted average" position, it shifts **upward toward the flange**. The N.A. always passes through the centroid, so it is also displaced upward from the geometric center of the bounding rectangle.

---

### **Question 2: Short-Answer Conceptual** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** State the **Parallel Axis Theorem**. Why is it essential in calculating the moment of inertia of a T-section?

**Model Answer:**

**Parallel Axis Theorem (Steiner's Theorem):** The moment of inertia of a plane area about any axis in its plane is equal to the moment of inertia about a parallel axis through the centroid, plus the product of the area and the square of the perpendicular distance between the two axes:

$$I = I_G + A \cdot d^2$$

It is **essential** for the T-section because the individual sub-rectangles (flange and web) have their own centroidal moments of inertia $I_{G,f}$ and $I_{G,w}$, but these axes are **NOT** the same as the overall Neutral Axis of the T-section. The theorem allows us to "shift" each rectangle's $I_G$ to the common N.A. by adding the $A d^2$ transfer term, giving the correct total $I_{NA}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks, split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. You must answer EITHER Option A OR Option B.

### **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A T-section has flange dimensions $150 \text{ mm} \times 25 \text{ mm}$ and web dimensions $25 \text{ mm} \times 150 \text{ mm}$.**

**(a)** Find the position of the centroid (Neutral Axis) measured from the bottom of the web. (7 marks)

**(b)** Calculate the moment of inertia of the section about the Neutral Axis and hence determine the section moduli at the top and bottom. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Given:**
- Flange: $B = 150$ mm, $t_f = 25$ mm
- Web: $t_w = 25$ mm, $D = 150$ mm
- Reference axis: bottom of web

**Part (a) — Centroid Location:**

**Sub-areas:**
- Flange area: $A_f = 150 \times 25 = 3750 \text{ mm}^2$
- Web area: $A_w = 25 \times 150 = 3750 \text{ mm}^2$
- Total area: $A = 7500 \text{ mm}^2$

**Centroidal distances from bottom:**
- $y_f = D + t_f/2 = 150 + 12.5 = 162.5$ mm
- $y_w = D/2 = 150/2 = 75$ mm

**Apply centroid formula:**

$$\bar{y} = \frac{A_f \cdot y_f + A_w \cdot y_w}{A_f + A_w}$$

$$\bar{y} = \frac{(3750)(162.5) + (3750)(75)}{7500}$$

$$\bar{y} = \frac{609375 + 281250}{7500} = \frac{890625}{7500}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{y} = 118.75 \text{ mm from the bottom}}$$

**Distance from top of flange:**

$$\bar{y}_{top} = (D + t_f) - \bar{y} = 175 - 118.75 = 56.25 \text{ mm}$$

---

**Part (b) — Moment of Inertia and Section Moduli:**

**Step 1: Distances from each sub-area's centroid to N.A.:**

- $d_f = y_f - \bar{y} = 162.5 - 118.75 = 43.75$ mm
- $d_w = \bar{y} - y_w = 118.75 - 75 = 43.75$ mm

*(Note: $d_f = d_w$ here because the two sub-areas have equal area — a special case.)*

**Step 2: Self moments of inertia:**

$$I_{G,f} = \frac{B \cdot t_f^3}{12} = \frac{150 \times 25^3}{12} = \frac{150 \times 15625}{12} = 195312.5 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$I_{G,w} = \frac{t_w \cdot D^3}{12} = \frac{25 \times 150^3}{12} = \frac{25 \times 3375000}{12} = 7031250 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 3: Transfer terms:**

$$A_f \cdot d_f^2 = 3750 \times (43.75)^2 = 3750 \times 1914.0625 = 7177734.375 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$A_w \cdot d_w^2 = 3750 \times (43.75)^2 = 7177734.375 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 4: Total $I_{NA}$:**

$$I_{NA} = 195312.5 + 7177734.375 + 7031250 + 7177734.375$$

$$I_{NA} = 21582031.25 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$\boxed{I_{NA} \approx 2.158 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4}$$

**Step 5: Section Moduli:**

$$Z_{top} = \frac{I_{NA}}{\bar{y}_{top}} = \frac{2.158 \times 10^7}{56.25} = 383680.5 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$Z_{bot} = \frac{I_{NA}}{\bar{y}} = \frac{2.158 \times 10^7}{118.75} = 181725.3 \text{ mm}^3$$

---

### **OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A T-section used as a cantilever beam in a robotic arm has the following dimensions: flange $200 \text{ mm} \times 30 \text{ mm}$, web $30 \text{ mm} \times 200 \text{ mm}$. It carries a uniformly distributed load of $6 \text{ kN/m}$ over a span of $2 \text{ m}$.**

**(a)** Find the position of the centroid and the moment of inertia about the Neutral Axis. (7 marks)

**(b)** Determine the maximum bending stress and maximum shear stress in the section. Take $y$ from the bottom of the web. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Given:**
- Flange: $B = 200$ mm, $t_f = 30$ mm
- Web: $t_w = 30$ mm, $D = 200$ mm
- UDL: $w = 6$ kN/m over span $L = 2$ m

**Part (a) — Centroid and $I_{NA}$:**

**Sub-areas:**
- $A_f = 200 \times 30 = 6000 \text{ mm}^2$
- $A_w = 30 \times 200 = 6000 \text{ mm}^2$
- $A = 12000 \text{ mm}^2$

**Centroidal distances from bottom:**
- $y_f = 200 + 15 = 215$ mm
- $y_w = 100$ mm

**Centroid:**

$$\bar{y} = \frac{(6000)(215) + (6000)(100)}{12000} = \frac{1290000 + 600000}{12000} = 157.5 \text{ mm}$$

$$\bar{y}_{top} = 230 - 157.5 = 72.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Distances to N.A.:**
- $d_f = 215 - 157.5 = 57.5$ mm
- $d_w = 157.5 - 100 = 57.5$ mm

**Self moments of inertia:**

$$I_{G,f} = \frac{200 \times 30^3}{12} = \frac{200 \times 27000}{12} = 450000 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$I_{G,w} = \frac{30 \times 200^3}{12} = \frac{30 \times 8000000}{12} = 20000000 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Transfer terms:**

$$A_f d_f^2 = 6000 \times (57.5)^2 = 6000 \times 3306.25 = 19837500 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$A_w d_w^2 = 6000 \times 3306.25 = 19837500 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Total $I_{NA}$:**

$$I_{NA} = 450000 + 19837500 + 20000000 + 19837500 = 60125000 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$\boxed{I_{NA} = 6.0125 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4}$$

---

**Part (b) — Maximum Stresses:**

**Step 1: Maximum bending moment** (UDL on cantilever):

$$M_{max} = \frac{w L^2}{2} = \frac{6 \times 2^2}{2} = 12 \text{ kN·m} = 12 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2: Maximum shear force:**

$$V_{max} = w L = 6 \times 2 = 12 \text{ kN} = 12000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Section moduli:**

$$Z_{top} = \frac{I_{NA}}{\bar{y}_{top}} = \frac{6.0125 \times 10^7}{72.5} = 829310.3 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$Z_{bot} = \frac{I_{NA}}{\bar{y}} = \frac{6.0125 \times 10^7}{157.5} = 381746.0 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 4: Maximum bending stress** (at the bottom of web, since $Z_{bot}$ is smaller):

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z_{bot}} = \frac{12 \times 10^6}{381746.0} = 31.43 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 31.43 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5: Maximum shear stress** (occurs at Neutral Axis in the web):

$$Q_{NA} = A_w \cdot d_w = 6000 \times 57.5 = 345000 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{V_{max} \cdot Q_{NA}}{I_{NA} \cdot t_w} = \frac{12000 \times 345000}{6.0125 \times 10^7 \times 30}$$

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{4.14 \times 10^9}{1.80375 \times 10^9} = 2.295 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 2.30 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **WRONG REFERENCE AXIS** (loses 2–3 marks): Many students take the reference from the TOP of the flange. KTU always uses the BOTTOM of the web unless stated otherwise. Mislabeling $\bar{y}$ direction loses easy marks.
> 2. **FORGETTING PARALLEL AXIS TERM** (loses 3–4 marks): Simply adding $I_{G,f} + I_{G,w}$ WITHOUT the $A d^2$ transfer terms. This gives a value that is **less than half** the correct $I_{NA}$. Always remember — for built-up sections, transfer terms DOMINATE.
> 3. **WRONG SECTION MODULUS CHOICE** (loses 2 marks): Using $Z_{top}$ instead of $Z_{bot}$ for maximum stress. Remember: the **smaller** section modulus gives the **larger** stress.
> 4. **UNIT MISMATCH** (loses 1–2 marks): Mixing mm with m, or N with kN. Convert all dimensions to mm and forces to N before final calculation.
> 5. **SKIPPING THE SUMMATION IN CENTROID** (loses 2 marks): Writing $\bar{y} = (y_f + y_w)/2$ as if it were an arithmetic mean. The correct formula is the **area-weighted** average, not a simple average.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ☐ A **T-section** is a built-up section with a top **flange** ($B \times t_f$) and a bottom **web** ($t_w \times D$).
- ☐ The **Neutral Axis (N.A.)** of a T-section lies **closer to the flange** than to the bottom of the web, because the flange has more area concentrated at the top.
- ☐ Always use the **bottom of the web as the reference axis** (unless stated otherwise) for consistent KTU board answers.
- ☐ The **centroid formula** is the area-weighted average: $\bar{y} = \dfrac{\sum A_i y_i}{\sum A_i}$ — never a simple arithmetic mean.
- ☐ The **Parallel Axis Theorem** is mandatory: $I_{NA} = \sum [I_{G,i} + A_i d_i^2]$ — the $A d^2$ term usually dominates $I_G$.
- ☐ **Maximum bending stress** occurs at the fiber **farthest from the N.A.** — typically the **bottom tip of the web** for a standard T-section.
- ☐ **Section Modulus** $Z = I_{NA} / y_{max}$ — always use the **smaller $Z$** for conservative design.
- ☐ **Maximum shear stress** occurs at the N.A. in the web: $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{V \cdot Q_{NA}}{I_{NA} \cdot t_w}$.
- ☐ In robotic applications, T-sections are used because they offer **high stiffness-to-weight ratios**, which directly improve **payload capacity** and **positioning accuracy**.
- ☐ **Units discipline:** Lengths in **mm**, forces in **N**, stresses in **N/mm² (MPa)**, moments in **N·mm**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip (from KTU Board Pattern Analysis):** T-section problems in KTU 2024 ESE almost always follow the exact pattern: (a) find centroid, (b) find $I_{NA}$ and stress. Practice this three-step protocol 5 times with different numerical values and you will be guaranteed full marks. The biggest single differentiator between a 7-mark and a 14-mark answer is the **correct application of the Parallel Axis Theorem** — do not skip it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Angle Section (L-Section)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> An **Angle Section** is a structural steel profile consisting of two legs of equal or unequal length joined at a **90° angle**, forming an "L" shape. It is widely used in robotic trusses, gantry frames, brackets, and bracing members due to its high strength-to-weight ratio and ease of connection.

### 1.1.1 Classification
| Type | Symbol | Description |
|------|--------|-------------|
| Equal Angle | ISA (Indian Standard Angle) | Both legs have **equal** length, e.g., 50 × 50 × 6 mm |
| Unequal Angle | ISA | Legs have **different** lengths, e.g., 100 × 75 × 8 mm |

### 1.1.2 Geometric Parameters (Equal Angle)
For an equal angle of leg length **L** and uniform thickness **t**:

- **Leg Length (L):** Length of each leg in mm.
- **Thickness (t):** Uniform thickness of legs in mm.
- **Radius of Gyration (rₓₓ, rᵧᵧ):** Distance from centroid to extreme fiber.
- **Centroid (C):** Located at equal distances x̄ = ȳ from the outer corner (back of the section).

### 1.1.3 Intuitive Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Think of an Angle Section as the "Steel Bookend" of Robotics!**
> Imagine the right-angled metal bracket holding a bookshelf to a wall. The two flat legs grip the shelf and the wall at 90°. An angle section works exactly like this — one leg resists vertical (shear) loads and the other resists horizontal (axial) loads. Because the centroid is **NOT** at the geometric corner, the section has **asymmetric bending behavior**, which makes it tricky but powerful for lightweight robotic arm trusses.

### 1.1.4 Why the Centroid is Offset
For a solid right-angled L-shape, the centroid lies on the **45° axis of symmetry** but is **shifted inward** from the outer corner. This offset makes the principal axes **inclined** to the legs — a critical KTU 2024 board exam concept.

---

## 1.2 Channel Section (C-Section)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A **Channel Section** is an open structural profile with a **flat web** and two **flanges** projecting at right angles from the web, forming a "C" shape. It is denoted as **ISLC** (Indian Standard Light Channel) or **ISHC** (Heavy Channel). It is extensively used as purlins, robotic base rails, and column members in light gantry structures.

### 1.2.1 Geometric Parameters (Standard Channel)
For a channel of overall depth **h**, flange width **b**, web thickness **tᵥ**, and flange thickness **tᶠ**:

- **Overall Depth (h):** Outer-to-outer distance between flanges.
- **Flange Width (b):** Width of each flange.
- **Web Thickness (tᵥ):** Thickness of vertical web.
- **Flange Thickness (tᶠ):** Thickness of horizontal flanges.

### 1.2.2 Intuitive Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Channel Section as a "C-Shaped Track" for Robotic Sliders!**
> Visualize the aluminum extrusion used in CNC linear rails or 3D-printer frames — a C-channel slides a carriage along its length while the lips (flanges) keep the carriage from rotating. In structural terms, the web carries **shear** and the flanges carry **bending** like an I-beam's flanges. Because the centroid is offset from the web, the channel has **asymmetric bending** — bending about the strong axis (x-x) is much stiffer than the weak axis (y-y).

### 1.2.3 Standard Notation (ISLC Example)
| Designation | h (mm) | b (mm) | tᵥ (mm) | tᶠ (mm) |
|-------------|--------|--------|---------|---------|
| ISLC 100 | 100 | 50 | 4.7 | 7.5 |
| ISLC 150 | 150 | 75 | 5.4 | 9.0 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Angle Section — Theory & Formulas

### 2.1.1 Setup & Coordinate System
Consider an **equal angle section** with two legs of length **L** and uniform thickness **t**. Take the origin at the **outer corner** (back of the angle), with x-axis along the horizontal leg and y-axis along the vertical leg.

The section is treated as a **composite** of:
- **Rectangle 1 (Horizontal leg):** L × t — area A₁ = Lt, centroid (L/2, t/2)
- **Rectangle 2 (Vertical leg):** L × t — area A₂ = Lt, centroid (t/2, L/2)
- **Overlap (subtract once):** t × t — area A₃ = t², centroid (t/2, t/2)

Total Area:
$$
A = A_1 + A_2 - A_3 = 2Lt - t^2
$$

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown
**Why centroid is offset inward (not at L/2):**
- The horizontal leg's mass is concentrated to the right (average x = L/2).
- The vertical leg's mass is concentrated upward (average y = L/2).
- However, the heavy overlap square at the corner pulls the centroid back toward the corner.
- The **net result:** x̄ = ȳ < L/2.

**Why principal axes are inclined:**
- The cross-section is **symmetric only about the 45° axis** (the diagonal line y = x).
- This means Iₓₓ = Iᵧᵧ (numerically equal about x and y axes through centroid).
- BUT the product of inertia **Iₓᵧ ≠ 0**.
- Whenever Iₓᵧ ≠ 0, the **principal axes rotate** by an angle θₚ — this is the **Hallmark Question** for KTU Module 4!

### 2.1.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Angle Section

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE (EQUAL ANGLE L × L × t)**

| Property | Formula | Units |
|----------|---------|-------|
| Cross-sectional Area | $A = 2Lt - t^2$ | mm² |
| Centroid (from back/corner) | $\bar{x} = \bar{y} = \dfrac{L^2 t + L t^2 - t^3}{2(2Lt - t^2)} = \dfrac{t(L^2 + Lt - t^2)}{2(2Lt - t^2)}$ | mm |
| M.O.I about x-x axis (centroidal, parallel to legs) | $I_{xx} = \dfrac{Lt^3}{12} + Lt\left(\bar{y} - \dfrac{t}{2}\right)^2 + \dfrac{tL^3}{12} + Lt\left(\dfrac{L}{2} - \bar{y}\right)^2 - \dfrac{t^4}{12} - t^2\left(\bar{y} - \dfrac{t}{2}\right)^2$ | mm⁴ |
| M.O.I about y-y axis (centroidal, parallel to legs) | $I_{yy} = I_{xx}$ (by symmetry) | mm⁴ |
| Product of Inertia | $I_{xy} = $ (non-zero, see derivation in Section 3) | mm⁴ |
| Principal Moment (Max) | $I_{max} = \dfrac{I_{xx} + I_{yy}}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{I_{xx} - I_{yy}}{2}\right)^2 + I_{xy}^2}$ | mm⁴ |
| Principal Moment (Min) | $I_{min} = \dfrac{I_{xx} + I_{yy}}{2} - \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{I_{xx} - I_{yy}}{2}\right)^2 + I_{xy}^2}$ | mm⁴ |
| Principal Axis Angle | $\tan 2\theta_p = \dfrac{-2I_{xy}}{I_{xx} - I_{yy}}$ (For equal angle: θₚ = 45°) | degrees |
| Radius of Gyration | $r_{xx} = \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{xx}}{A}}, \quad r_{min} = \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{min}}{A}}$ | mm |
| Section Modulus | $Z_{xx} = \dfrac{I_{xx}}{y_{max}}, \quad Z_{min} = \dfrac{I_{min}}{y_{max}}$ | mm³ |

### 2.1.4 Real-World Utility in Robotics
- **Equal Angles:** Robot gantry trusses, conveyor frame diagonals, base-plate stiffeners.
- **Unequal Angles:** Tapered robotic arm sections, asymmetric load-bearing brackets where one leg is longer to resist the dominant bending direction.

---

## 2.2 Channel Section — Theory & Formulas

### 2.2.1 Setup & Coordinate System
For a channel with depth **h**, flange width **b**, web thickness **tᵥ**, and flange thickness **tᶠ**:
- Origin at the **outer face of the web** (back of channel).
- x-axis is horizontal (along the flange direction).
- y-axis is vertical (along the web).

The section is decomposed into **three rectangles**:
- **Web:** h × tᵥ, centroid at (tᵥ/2, h/2)
- **Top Flange:** b × tᶠ, centroid at (tᵥ + b/2, h - tᶠ/2)
- **Bottom Flange:** b × tᶠ, centroid at (tᵥ + b/2, tᶠ/2)

### 2.2.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown
**Why the centroid lies away from the web:**
- The two flanges project outward, pulling the centroid toward the **open side** of the channel.
- This makes **x̄** (distance from back of web) **less than b/2** but greater than tᵥ/2.

**Why Iₓₓ >> Iᵧᵧ:**
- Iₓₓ is large because most material (the two flanges) is at the **maximum distance** h/2 from the neutral axis.
- Iᵧᵧ is small because most material is **close to the y-axis** (the web and inner parts of the flanges).
- For typical channels, **Iₓₓ / Iᵧᵧ ≈ 4 to 10**.

**Why principal axes are slightly inclined:**
- Although Iₓₓ ≠ Iᵧᵧ for channel, the product of inertia **Iₓᵧ ≠ 0** because the section is NOT symmetric about the x-axis (the web is on one side).
- This causes a small **principal axis tilt**, typically 5° to 25° from the geometric axes.

### 2.2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Channel Section

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE (CHANNEL h × b × tᵥ × tᶠ)**

| Property | Formula | Units |
|----------|---------|-------|
| Cross-sectional Area | $A = h t_w + 2 b t_f$ | mm² |
| Centroid (from back of web) | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{h t_w (t_w/2) + 2 b t_f (t_w + b/2)}{h t_w + 2 b t_f}$ | mm |
| M.O.I about x-x (centroidal, horizontal) | $I_{xx} = \dfrac{t_w h^3}{12} + 2\left[\dfrac{b t_f^3}{12} + b t_f \left(\dfrac{h}{2} - \dfrac{t_f}{2}\right)^2\right]$ | mm⁴ |
| M.O.I about y-y (centroidal, vertical) | $I_{yy} = \dfrac{h t_w^3}{12} + h t_w \left(\bar{x} - \dfrac{t_w}{2}\right)^2 + 2\left[\dfrac{t_f b^3}{12} + b t_f \left(\bar{x} - t_w - \dfrac{b}{2}\right)^2\right]$ | mm⁴ |
| Product of Inertia | $I_{xy} = 0$ (symmetric about x-axis) — but **principal axes still slightly inclined due to Iₓₓ ≠ Iᵧᵧ** & mass distribution | mm⁴ |
| Radius of Gyration | $r_{xx} = \sqrt{I_{xx}/A}, \quad r_{yy} = \sqrt{I_{yy}/A}$ | mm |
| Section Modulus | $Z_{xx} = I_{xx} / (h/2), \quad Z_{yy} = I_{yy} / (\bar{x})$ | mm³ |

> [!NOTE]
> **⚠ KTU Common Mistake:** Students often write "Iₓᵧ = 0 for channel, so θₚ = 0." **WRONG!** Even with Iₓᵧ = 0, if Iₓₓ ≠ Iᵧᵧ, the principal axes coincide with the geometric axes (no rotation), BUT you must verify Iₓᵧ = 0 by computing it from first principles — see Section 3!

### 2.2.4 Real-World Utility in Robotics
- **Purlins in Robotic Shed Structures:** Channels are excellent as roof purlins.
- **Crane Rails:** Light-duty channel rails for ceiling-mounted robot carriages.
- **Column Members:** Used as primary columns in light robotic workcells.
- **Robot Frame Chassis:** Car-body robotic platforms often use channel sections for the perimeter frame.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Centroid of Equal Angle Section (L × L × t)

### Given
- Equal angle section with leg length **L** and thickness **t**.
- Origin at the outer corner of the angle (back of the section).

### Step 1 — Decompose into rectangles
We split the L-section into three rectangles:

| Part | Description | Area | Centroid (xᵢ, yᵢ) |
|------|-------------|------|-------------------|
| A₁ | Horizontal leg | L × t = Lt | (L/2, t/2) |
| A₂ | Vertical leg | L × t = Lt | (t/2, L/2) |
| A₃ | Corner overlap (subtract) | t × t = t² | (t/2, t/2) |

### Step 2 — Total Area
$$
A = A_1 + A_2 - A_3 = Lt + Lt - t^2 = 2Lt - t^2
$$

### Step 3 — Centroid x̄ (using moments about y-axis)
$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= \frac{A_1 x_1 + A_2 x_2 - A_3 x_3}{A_1 + A_2 - A_3} \\
\bar{x} &= \frac{(Lt)\left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right) + (Lt)\left(\dfrac{t}{2}\right) - (t^2)\left(\dfrac{t}{2}\right)}{2Lt - t^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Simplify numerator
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= \frac{L^2 t}{2} + \frac{L t^2}{2} - \frac{t^3}{2} \\
&= \frac{t(L^2 + L t - t^2)}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Final expression for x̄
$$
\boxed{\bar{x} = \frac{t(L^2 + L t - t^2)}{2(2L t - t^2)}}
$$

### Step 6 — By symmetry (the L-angle is symmetric about the 45° diagonal y = x)
$$
\boxed{\bar{y} = \bar{x} = \frac{t(L^2 + L t - t^2)}{2(2L t - t^2)}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Moment of Inertia Iₓₓ of Equal Angle (about centroidal x-axis parallel to legs)

### Step 1 — Use Parallel Axis Theorem
For each rectangle, the moment of inertia about the **centroidal x-axis** equals:
$$
I_{xx,i} = I_{xx,i}^{c} + A_i \cdot d_{y,i}^2
$$
where $d_{y,i}$ is the perpendicular distance from the rectangle's centroid to the global centroidal x-axis.

### Step 2 — Compute for each part
**For A₁ (Horizontal leg):** dᵧ = ȳ - t/2
$$
I_{xx,1} = \frac{L t^3}{12} + L t \left(\bar{y} - \frac{t}{2}\right)^2
$$

**For A₂ (Vertical leg):** dᵧ = L/2 - ȳ
$$
I_{xx,2} = \frac{t L^3}{12} + L t \left(\frac{L}{2} - \bar{y}\right)^2
$$

**For A₃ (Overlap, subtract):** dᵧ = ȳ - t/2
$$
I_{xx,3} = \frac{t \cdot t^3}{12} + t^2 \left(\bar{y} - \frac{t}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{t^4}{12} + t^2 \left(\bar{y} - \frac{t}{2}\right)^2
$$

### Step 3 — Total Iₓₓ
$$
I_{xx} = I_{xx,1} + I_{xx,2} - I_{xx,3}
$$

### Step 4 — By symmetry
$$
\boxed{I_{yy} = I_{xx}}
$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Verification (Example: L = 100 mm, t = 10 mm)
- Area: A = 2(100)(10) - (10)² = 2000 - 100 = **1900 mm²**
- Centroid: ȳ = 10(100² + 100·10 - 10²) / [2(2000 - 100)] = 10(10000 + 1000 - 100) / 3800 = 109000 / 3800 ≈ **28.68 mm**
- Iₓₓ₁ (horizontal): (100)(10)³/12 + 100·10·(28.68 - 5)² = 8333.33 + 1000·(23.68)² = 8333.33 + 560,742.4 = **569,075.7 mm⁴**
- Iₓₓ₂ (vertical): (10)(100)³/12 + 100·10·(50 - 28.68)² = 833,333.33 + 1000·(21.32)² = 833,333.33 + 454,542.4 = **1,287,875.7 mm⁴**
- Iₓₓ₃ (overlap, subtracted): (10)⁴/12 + 100·(28.68 - 5)² = 833.33 + 100·(23.68)² = 833.33 + 56,074.24 = **56,907.6 mm⁴**
- **Iₓₓ = 569,075.7 + 1,287,875.7 - 56,907.6 = 1,800,043.8 mm⁴**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Principal Axes of Equal Angle

### Step 1 — Compute Product of Inertia Iₓᵧ about centroidal axes
For each part, $I_{xy,i} = I_{xy,i}^{c} + A_i \cdot d_{x,i} \cdot d_{y,i}$. For rectangles with sides parallel to axes, $I_{xy,i}^{c} = 0$.

**For A₁ (Horizontal leg):** dₓ = L/2 - x̄, dᵧ = ȳ - t/2
$$
I_{xy,1} = L t \left(\frac{L}{2} - \bar{x}\right)\left(\bar{y} - \frac{t}{2}\right)
$$

**For A₂ (Vertical leg):** dₓ = t/2 - x̄, dᵧ = L/2 - ȳ
$$
I_{xy,2} = L t \left(\frac{t}{2} - \bar{x}\right)\left(\frac{L}{2} - \bar{y}\right)
$$

**For A₃ (Overlap):** dₓ = t/2 - x̄, dᵧ = ȳ - t/2
$$
I_{xy,3} = t^2 \left(\frac{t}{2} - \bar{x}\right)\left(\bar{y} - \frac{t}{2}\right)
$$

### Step 2 — Total Product of Inertia
$$
I_{xy} = I_{xy,1} + I_{xy,2} - I_{xy,3}
$$

### Step 3 — Verify the Symmetry Argument
Because the section is symmetric about the 45° line (y = x), the principal axes **must** align with this line:
$$
\boxed{\theta_p = 45° \text{ for equal angle section}}
$$

This is a beautiful consequence: the 45° line IS the major principal axis. **KTU examiners love this short-answer question!**

### Step 4 — Principal Moments
$$
\boxed{I_{max} = I_{xx} + \vert I_{xy} \vert, \quad I_{min} = I_{xx} - \vert I_{xy} \vert}
$$

(For equal angle, since Iₓₓ = Iᵧᵧ, the formula simplifies.)

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Centroid of Channel Section (h × b × tᵥ × tᶠ)

### Step 1 — Decomposition
| Part | Description | Area | Centroid (xᵢ, yᵢ) |
|------|-------------|------|-------------------|
| Web | h × tᵥ | h tᵥ | (tᵥ/2, h/2) |
| Top flange | b × tᶠ | b tᶠ | (tᵥ + b/2, h - tᶠ/2) |
| Bottom flange | b × tᶠ | b tᶠ | (tᵥ + b/2, tᶠ/2) |

### Step 2 — Total Area
$$
A = h t_w + 2 b t_f
$$

### Step 3 — x̄ (moment about y-axis through outer face of web)
$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= \frac{(h t_w)\left(\dfrac{t_w}{2}\right) + (b t_f)\left(t_w + \dfrac{b}{2}\right) + (b t_f)\left(t_w + \dfrac{b}{2}\right)}{h t_w + 2 b t_f} \\
\bar{x} &= \frac{\dfrac{h t_w^2}{2} + 2 b t_f \left(t_w + \dfrac{b}{2}\right)}{h t_w + 2 b t_f}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — ȳ
By symmetry about the horizontal centerline:
$$
\boxed{\bar{y} = \frac{h}{2}}
$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Iₓₓ of Channel Section (about centroidal x-axis)

### Step 1 — Web contribution
For the web about its own centroidal x-axis (horizontal), shifted to global centroidal x-axis (which coincides due to symmetry):
$$
I_{xx,web} = \frac{t_w h^3}{12}
$$

### Step 2 — Each flange contribution
For each flange (b × tᶠ), distance from flange centroid to global x-axis = h/2 - tᶠ/2:
$$
I_{xx,flange} = \frac{b t_f^3}{12} + b t_f \left(\frac{h}{2} - \frac{t_f}{2}\right)^2
$$

Two flanges:
$$
I_{xx,flanges} = 2 \left[\frac{b t_f^3}{12} + b t_f \left(\frac{h - t_f}{2}\right)^2\right]
$$

### Step 3 — Total
$$
\boxed{I_{xx} = \frac{t_w h^3}{12} + 2 b t_f \left(\frac{h - t_f}{2}\right)^2 + \frac{b t_f^3}{6}}
$$

The $t_f^3$ term is usually small compared to the $h^3$ term and is **often neglected in preliminary calculations** (but KTU expects the full formula!).

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Iᵧᵧ of Channel Section (about centroidal y-axis)

### Step 1 — Web contribution
Web centroid is at x = tᵥ/2; global y-axis is at x = x̄.
$$
I_{yy,web} = \frac{h t_w^3}{12} + h t_w \left(\bar{x} - \frac{t_w}{2}\right)^2
$$

### Step 2 — Each flange contribution
Flange centroid is at x = tᵥ + b/2; distance to global y-axis = (tᵥ + b/2) - x̄.
$$
I_{yy,flange} = \frac{t_f b^3}{12} + b t_f \left[\left(t_w + \frac{b}{2}\right) - \bar{x}\right]^2
$$

Two flanges:
$$
I_{yy,flanges} = 2\left[\frac{t_f b^3}{12} + b t_f \left(t_w + \frac{b}{2} - \bar{x}\right)^2\right]
$$

### Step 3 — Total
$$
\boxed{I_{yy} = \frac{h t_w^3}{12} + h t_w \left(\bar{x} - \frac{t_w}{2}\right)^2 + \frac{t_f b^3}{6} + 2 b t_f \left(t_w + \frac{b}{2} - \bar{x}\right)^2}
$$

---

## 3.7 Numerical Worked Example — Channel Section

> [!NOTE]
> **📘 EXAMPLE (KTU Board Style):**
> A channel section has dimensions: h = 200 mm, b = 75 mm, tᵥ = 6 mm, tᶠ = 10 mm. Determine A, x̄, Iₓₓ, Iᵧᵧ, rₓₓ, rᵧᵧ, Zₓₓ.

**Step 1 — Area**
$$
A = (200)(6) + 2(75)(10) = 1200 + 1500 = 2700 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Centroid x̄**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= \frac{(1200)(3) + 2(750)(6 + 37.5)}{2700} \\
&= \frac{3600 + 2(750)(43.5)}{2700} \\
&= \frac{3600 + 65{,}250}{2700} = \frac{68{,}850}{2700} = 25.5 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Iₓₓ**
$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{xx} &= \frac{(6)(200)^3}{12} + 2(75)(10)\left(\frac{200-10}{2}\right)^2 + \frac{(75)(10)^3}{6} \\
&= \frac{6 \times 8 \times 10^6}{12} + 1500 \times (95)^2 + \frac{75 \times 1000}{6} \\
&= 4 \times 10^6 + 1500 \times 9025 + 12{,}500 \\
&= 4{,}000{,}000 + 13{,}537{,}500 + 12{,}500 \\
&= 17{,}550{,}000 \text{ mm}^4 = 1.755 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Iᵧᵧ**
$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{yy} &= \frac{(200)(6)^3}{12} + (200)(6)(25.5 - 3)^2 + 2\left[\frac{(10)(75)^3}{12} + (75)(10)(6 + 37.5 - 25.5)^2\right] \\
&= \frac{200 \times 216}{12} + 1200 \times (22.5)^2 + 2\left[\frac{10 \times 421{,}875}{12} + 750 \times (18)^2\right] \\
&= 3600 + 1200 \times 506.25 + 2[351{,}562.5 + 750 \times 324] \\
&= 3600 + 607{,}500 + 2[351{,}562.5 + 243{,}000] \\
&= 611{,}100 + 2 \times 594{,}562.5 \\
&= 611{,}100 + 1{,}189{,}125 = 1{,}800{,}225 \text{ mm}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Radii of Gyration**
$$
r_{xx} = \sqrt{\frac{1.755 \times 10^7}{2700}} = \sqrt{6500} = 80.62 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
r_{yy} = \sqrt{\frac{1.800225 \times 10^6}{2700}} = \sqrt{666.75} = 25.82 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Section Modulus Zₓₓ**
$$
Z_{xx} = \frac{I_{xx}}{h/2} = \frac{1.755 \times 10^7}{100} = 1.755 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3 = 175{,}500 \text{ mm}^3
$$

✅ **Final Results:** A = 2700 mm², x̄ = 25.5 mm, Iₓₓ = 1.755 × 10⁷ mm⁴, Iᵧᵧ = 1.800 × 10⁶ mm⁴, rₓₓ = 80.62 mm, rᵧᵧ = 25.82 mm, Zₓₓ = 175,500 mm³

---

## 3.8 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📊 ANGLE vs CHANNEL — Quick Comparison (KTU Exam Favorite)**

| Feature | Angle Section | Channel Section |
|---------|---------------|-----------------|
| Geometry | L-shape (2 legs at 90°) | C-shape (web + 2 flanges) |
| Symmetry axes | 1 (45° diagonal) | 1 (horizontal through centroid) |
| Iₓₓ vs Iᵧᵧ | Iₓₓ = Iᵧᵧ (geometrically) | Iₓₓ >> Iᵧᵧ |
| Product of inertia Iₓᵧ | ≠ 0 (significant) | ≠ 0 (small) |
| Principal axis tilt θₚ | 45° (along the diagonal) | Small angle (5°–25°) |
| Use case in robotics | Brackets, truss diagonals | Rails, columns, purlins |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Angle Section Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given L, t for Equal Angle] --> B[Decompose into 3 Rectangles]
    B --> C1[Rect 1: Horizontal Leg Lt]
    B --> C2[Rect 2: Vertical Leg Lt]
    B --> C3[Rect 3: Overlap t squared SUBTRACT]
    C1 --> D[Compute Total Area A]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Find Centroid xbar and ybar]
    E --> F[Apply Parallel Axis Theorem]
    F --> G[Compute Ixx and Iyy]
    G --> H[Compute Product Ixy]
    H --> I[Check Symmetry 45 degrees]
    I --> J[theta p equals 45 degrees]
    J --> K[Compute I max and I min]
    K --> L[Compute Radii of Gyration]
    L --> M[Output Section Properties]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Channel Section Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Start: Given h, b, tw, tf for Channel] --> Q[Decompose into 3 Rectangles]
    Q --> R1[Web: h by tw]
    Q --> R2[Top Flange: b by tf]
    Q --> R3[Bottom Flange: b by tf]
    R1 --> S[Compute Total Area A]
    R2 --> S
    R3 --> S
    S --> T[Find Centroid xbar from back of web]
    T --> U1[Compute Ixx using parallel axis]
    T --> U2[Compute Iyy using parallel axis]
    U1 --> V[Check Product Ixy]
    U2 --> V
    V --> W[Determine Principal Axis Angle]
    W --> X[Compute Principal Moments]
    X --> Y[Compute Section Modulus Zxx Zyy]
    Y --> Z[Output Final Section Properties]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define an unequal angle section. Why is its centroid not located at the geometric corner?**

**Model Answer:**
An unequal angle section is an L-shaped structural profile whose two legs have **different lengths** (e.g., 100 × 75 × 8 mm). The centroid lies at the intersection of the lines bisecting the legs and is **offset toward the longer leg** because the longer leg contributes more area, shifting the mass center. It is NOT at the geometric corner because the corner is a single point of negligible area, while the legs (each with finite thickness and length) contain all the material, making the centroid shift inward along the 45° symmetry line.

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Why is the product of inertia Iₓᵧ non-zero for an angle section but zero for a doubly-symmetric I-section?**

**Model Answer:**
The product of inertia Iₓᵧ measures the **asymmetric distribution** of area about the x and y axes. For a **doubly-symmetric I-section**, every element at (+x, +y) has a mirror image at (-x, +y), (-x, -y), and (+x, -y). The contributions cancel out, yielding **Iₓᵧ = 0**. For an **angle section**, the area is concentrated in the first quadrant only (between the two legs). There is no mirror image in the other three quadrants, so the products of xᵢyᵢ do NOT cancel, giving **Iₓᵧ ≠ 0**.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

An **equal angle section** has leg length **L = 120 mm** and thickness **t = 12 mm**.

#### (a) Determine the centroid location (x̄, ȳ), the moments of inertia about centroidal axes parallel to the legs (Iₓₓ, Iᵧᵧ), and the product of inertia Iₓᵧ. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Decompose section into three rectangles**
| Part | Area | Centroid (x, y) |
|------|------|-----------------|
| A₁ (horizontal leg) | 120 × 12 = 1440 mm² | (60, 6) |
| A₂ (vertical leg) | 120 × 12 = 1440 mm² | (6, 60) |
| A₃ (overlap, subtract) | 12 × 12 = 144 mm² | (6, 6) |

**Step 2 — Total area**
$$
A = 1440 + 1440 - 144 = 2736 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Centroid x̄**
$$
\bar{x} = \frac{(1440)(60) + (1440)(6) - (144)(6)}{2736} = \frac{86{,}400 + 8640 - 864}{2736} = \frac{94{,}176}{2736} = 34.42 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — By symmetry** ȳ = x̄ = **34.42 mm**

**Step 5 — Iₓₓ using parallel axis theorem**
For A₁: $I_{xx,1}^c = (120)(12)^3/12 = 17{,}280 \text{ mm}^4$, dᵧ = 34.42 - 6 = 28.42 mm
$$
I_{xx,1} = 17{,}280 + 1440 \times (28.42)^2 = 17{,}280 + 1{,}163{,}318 = 1{,}180{,}598 \text{ mm}^4
$$

For A₂: $I_{xx,2}^c = (12)(120)^3/12 = 1{,}728{,}000 \text{ mm}^4$, dᵧ = 60 - 34.42 = 25.58 mm
$$
I_{xx,2} = 1{,}728{,}000 + 1440 \times (25.58)^2 = 1{,}728{,}000 + 942{,}290 = 2{,}670{,}290 \text{ mm}^4
$$

For A₃ (subtract): $I_{xx,3}^c = (12)^4/12 = 1728 \text{ mm}^4$, dᵧ = 28.42 mm
$$
I_{xx,3} = 1728 + 144 \times (28.42)^2 = 1728 + 116{,}332 = 118{,}060 \text{ mm}^4
$$

**Total Iₓₓ**
$$
I_{xx} = 1{,}180{,}598 + 2{,}670{,}290 - 118{,}060 = 3{,}732{,}828 \text{ mm}^4 \approx 3.733 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4
$$

**Iᵧᵧ = Iₓₓ by symmetry** = **3.733 × 10⁶ mm⁴**

**Step 6 — Iₓᵧ**
For each rectangle, $I_{xy,i}^c = 0$. Use parallel axis:
$$
I_{xy} = A_1 d_{x1} d_{y1} + A_2 d_{x2} d_{y2} - A_3 d_{x3} d_{y3}
$$
- A₁: dₓ = 60 - 34.42 = 25.58, dᵧ = 28.42 → 1440 × 25.58 × 28.42 = **1,046,653**
- A₂: dₓ = 6 - 34.42 = -28.42, dᵧ = 25.58 → 1440 × (-28.42) × 25.58 = **-1,046,653**
- A₃: dₓ = -28.42, dᵧ = 28.42 → 144 × (-28.42) × 28.42 = **-116,332**

$$
I_{xy} = 1{,}046{,}653 - 1{,}046{,}653 - (-116{,}332) = 116{,}332 \text{ mm}^4
$$

**Result:** Iₓᵧ ≈ **1.163 × 10⁵ mm⁴**

#### (b) Find the principal moments of inertia and the orientation of principal axes. Comment on the orientation in light of section symmetry. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply principal moment formula**
Since Iₓₓ = Iᵧᵧ:
$$
I_{max} = I_{xx} + |I_{xy}| = 3{,}732{,}828 + 116{,}332 = 3{,}849{,}160 \text{ mm}^4
$$
$$
I_{min} = I_{xx} - |I_{xy}| = 3{,}732{,}828 - 116{,}332 = 3{,}616{,}496 \text{ mm}^4
$$

**Step 2 — Principal axis angle**
$$
\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{-2 I_{xy}}{I_{xx} - I_{yy}} = \frac{-2(116{,}332)}{0} \to \infty
$$
This gives 2θₚ = 90°, so **θₚ = 45°** (or -45°).

**Step 3 — Comment on Symmetry**
The equal angle section has **diagonal symmetry about the 45° line (y = x)**. This symmetry line MUST be one of the principal axes. The other principal axis is perpendicular to it, also at 45°. Therefore, the principal axes are oriented at **±45° to the legs**, confirming our calculation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

A **channel section** has overall depth **h = 250 mm**, flange width **b = 80 mm**, web thickness **tᵥ = 6 mm**, and flange thickness **tᶠ = 12 mm**.

#### (a) Calculate the cross-sectional area, centroid location x̄, and the moment of inertia Iₓₓ about the horizontal centroidal axis. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total Area**
$$
A = h t_w + 2 b t_f = (250)(6) + 2(80)(12) = 1500 + 1920 = 3420 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Centroid x̄ (from back of web)**
$$
\bar{x} = \frac{(1500)(3) + 2(960)(6 + 40)}{3420} = \frac{4500 + 1920 \times 46}{3420} = \frac{4500 + 88{,}320}{3420} = \frac{92{,}820}{3420} = 27.14 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Iₓₓ**
$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{xx} &= \frac{(6)(250)^3}{12} + 2(80)(12)\left(\frac{250 - 12}{2}\right)^2 + \frac{(80)(12)^3}{6} \\
&= \frac{6 \times 15{,}625{,}000}{12} + 1920 \times (119)^2 + \frac{80 \times 1728}{6} \\
&= 7{,}812{,}500 + 1920 \times 14{,}161 + 23{,}040 \\
&= 7{,}812{,}500 + 27{,}189{,}120 + 23{,}040 \\
&= 35{,}024{,}660 \text{ mm}^4 \approx 3.502 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (b) Compute Iᵧᵧ about the vertical centroidal axis, the radii of gyration (rₓₓ, rᵧᵧ), and the section modulus Zₓₓ. Comment on whether this section is more efficient for resisting bending about the x-x or y-y axis. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Iᵧᵧ**
Web: $I_{yy,web}^c = (250)(6)^3/12 = 4500$ mm⁴; distance = 27.14 - 3 = 24.14 mm
$$
I_{yy,web} = 4500 + 1500 \times (24.14)^2 = 4500 + 874{,}308 = 878{,}808 \text{ mm}^4
$$

Each flange: $I_{yy,flange}^c = (12)(80)^3/12 = 512{,}000$ mm⁴; distance = (6 + 40) - 27.14 = 18.86 mm
$$
I_{yy,flange} = 512{,}000 + 960 \times (18.86)^2 = 512{,}000 + 341{,}532 = 853{,}532 \text{ mm}^4
$$

Two flanges:
$$
I_{yy} = 878{,}808 + 2 \times 853{,}532 = 878{,}808 + 1{,}707{,}064 = 2{,}585{,}872 \text{ mm}^4 \approx 2.586 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4
$$

**Step 2 — Radii of Gyration**
$$
r_{xx} = \sqrt{\frac{35{,}024{,}660}{3420}} = \sqrt{10{,}241} = 101.20 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
r_{yy} = \sqrt{\frac{2{,}585{,}872}{3420}} = \sqrt{756.1} = 27.50 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Section Modulus Zₓₓ**
$$
Z_{xx} = \frac{I_{xx}}{h/2} = \frac{35{,}024{,}660}{125} = 280{,}197 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Comment on Efficiency**
Since Iₓₓ / Iᵧᵧ ≈ 35,024,660 / 2,585,872 ≈ **13.5**, the channel is **about 13.5× stiffer about the x-x axis** than about the y-y axis. It is therefore **highly efficient for resisting bending about the horizontal x-x axis** (i.e., when loads are vertical) and is typically used with the web vertical — e.g., as a purlin or column in a robotic workcell.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Where Students Lose Marks):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to SUBTRACT the overlap** when computing area/centroid for angle sections. Marks lost: ~2.
> 2. **Using Iₓₓ = Iᵧᵧ without verifying symmetry** for UNequal angle sections — they are NOT equal!
> 3. **Omitting the small $t_f^3/6$ term** in Iₓₓ of channel — KTU expects the FULL formula.
> 4. **Stating θₚ = 0° for channel** without justification — you must compute Iₓᵧ from first principles even if it turns out to be non-zero.
> 5. **Mixing up which axis is x-x (strong) and y-y (weak)** for the channel. Remember: **x-x is horizontal (strong, parallel to flanges)**, y-y is vertical (weak, parallel to web).
> 6. **Forgetting units** — KTU mandates mm, mm², mm⁴, mm³. Always state them!

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Angle & Channel Sections**

### Angle Section (ISA)
- ⭐ **Geometry:** Two legs joined at 90° (equal or unequal).
- ⭐ **Area formula:** A = 2Lt - t² (equal angle), with overlap subtraction.
- ⭐ **Centroid:** Offset from outer corner along the 45° symmetry axis.
- ⭐ **Critical property:** Iₓₓ = Iᵧᵧ (for EQUAL angle only).
- ⭐ **Principal axes tilt:** Always exactly **θₚ = 45°** for equal angle.
- ⭐ **Applications:** Robotic truss diagonals, brackets, gusset plates.

### Channel Section (ISLC / ISHC)
- ⭐ **Geometry:** Web + two flanges forming a "C" — open section.
- ⭐ **Centroid x̄:** Measured from back of web, lies between tᵥ/2 and tᵥ + b/2.
- ⭐ **Symmetry:** Only ONE axis of symmetry (horizontal x-axis through centroid).
- ⭐ **Critical property:** Iₓₓ >> Iᵧᵧ (typically 4 to 15 times larger).
- ⭐ **Principal axes:** Slightly tilted from geometric axes (5°–25°).
- ⭐ **Applications:** Robotic purlins, columns, base frames, crane rails.

### Master Formulas to Memorize
- ⭐ Area of equal angle: **A = 2Lt - t²**
- ⭐ Area of channel: **A = h·tᵥ + 2b·tᶠ**
- ⭐ Principal moment: **Iₘₐₓ/Iₘᵢₙ = (Iₓₓ+Iᵧᵧ)/2 ± √[((Iₓₓ-Iᵧᵧ)/2)² + Iₓᵧ²]**
- ⭐ Principal axis: **tan 2θₚ = -2Iₓᵧ / (Iₓₓ - Iᵧᵧ)**
- ⭐ Radius of gyration: **r = √(I/A)**
- ⭐ Section modulus: **Z = I / y_max**

### Common Numerical Values to Remember
- ⭐ For a square (b × b) about its centroidal axis: I = b⁴/12
- ⭐ For a rectangle (b × h) about its centroidal axis: I = bh³/12
- ⭐ Parallel axis theorem: **I = I_c + A·d²**

### Quick Decision Tree for KTU Problems
```
Are you given L and t for an angle?
  → Use overlap-subtraction method
  → Centroid offset along 45-degree line
  → Principal axis ALWAYS at 45 degrees

Are you given h, b, tw, tf for a channel?
  → Three-rectangle decomposition (web + 2 flanges)
  → Ixx uses parallel axis from flanges at distance h/2
  → Iyy uses parallel axis from flanges at distance xbar - tw - b/2
```

> **END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — ANGLE SECTION AND CHANNEL SECTION**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elastic Stability of Columns: Short and Long Columns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT525 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS OF ROBOTIC PARTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Elastic Stability of Columns: Short and Long Columns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — ELASTIC STABILITY OF COLUMNS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **column** is a structural member subjected primarily to **axial compressive loads**, whose primary function is to transfer compressive force between two points. In robotic mechanical systems, columns appear as **linkages, arm segments, vertical struts of parallel manipulators, and pedestal supports** of robotic bases.

**Elastic Stability** refers to the ability of a column to maintain its straight equilibrium configuration under axial compression without undergoing a sudden lateral deformation called **buckling**. The **critical (crippling) load** $P_{cr}$ is the smallest compressive axial load at which the column ceases to be in stable equilibrium and suddenly deflects laterally (buckles).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact):**
> "A column is said to be in a state of elastic stability if, when slightly displaced laterally by a small disturbance, it returns to its original straight equilibrium position. The maximum load at which the column ceases to be elastically stable is called the **Crippling Load** or **Critical Load** or **Buckling Load**."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Picture

Imagine pushing down on a **thin plastic ruler held vertically on a table**:

- Apply a **tiny force**: The ruler stays straight. Stable. ✓
- Apply a **larger force**: Suddenly, the ruler snaps sideways with a sharp "POP". This is **buckling**. ✗
- The ruler was straight, but the moment your force exceeded a threshold, the straight shape could no longer resist the load, and it bent into a curved shape.

In a robot, this is the danger that engineers fear most. A robotic arm link can be **perfectly safe for stress** (its internal material stress is low) yet **fail catastrophically by buckling** if it is too long and slender. This is why **stability ≠ strength**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction (Frequently Tested in KTU):**
> - **Short Column** → Fails by **CRUSHING** (material strength failure)
> - **Long Column** → Fails by **BUCKLING** (geometric instability failure)
> - **Medium Column** → Fails by combined crushing + buckling

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- Modulus of Elasticity of steel: $E = 2 \times 10^5 \ \text{N/mm}^2 = 200 \ \text{GPa}$
- Modulus of Elasticity of cast iron: $E = 1.2 \times 10^5 \ \text{N/mm}^2$
- Modulus of Elasticity of aluminum alloys (used in robot links): $E = 0.7 \times 10^5 \ \text{N/mm}^2$
- Factor of Safety (FoS) for robotic columns: typically **2.0 – 4.0** depending on dynamic loading.

### 1.4 Classification of Columns

| Column Type | Slenderness Ratio $\lambda$ | Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Short Column** | $\lambda < 30$ to $40$ | Pure **crushing** (yielding) |
| **Medium Column** | $30 < \lambda < 100$ (approx.) | Combined crushing + buckling |
| **Long Column** | $\lambda > 100$ (approx.) | Pure **buckling** (Euler) |

where the slenderness ratio is defined as:
$$\lambda = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{k}$$

with $L_{eff}$ = effective length and $k$ = least radius of gyration.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Assumptions of Euler's Theory (Long Columns Only)

1. The column is **perfectly straight** initially and the load is truly **axial (concentric)**.
2. The material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and perfectly elastic** (obeys Hooke's Law).
3. The **self-weight** of the column is neglected.
4. The column fails **only by buckling**, never by crushing.
5. The **end conditions** are ideal (perfectly hinged, fixed, or free).
6. The **flexural rigidity $EI$** is constant along the length.
7. **Shortening** due to direct compression is neglected.
8. The **deflections are small** (so that $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta \approx \theta$).

### 2.2 Euler's Crippling Load — Derivation Setup

For a column hinged at both ends with length $L$ under axial compressive load $P$, the differential equation of the deflected shape is:

$$EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = -M(x) = -P \cdot y$$

which simplifies to:
$$\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + k^2 y = 0 \quad \text{where} \quad k^2 = \dfrac{P}{EI}$$

The general solution is $y = A \cos(kx) + B \sin(kx)$.

Applying boundary conditions $y(0) = 0$ and $y(L) = 0$, we get $A = 0$ and the **buckling condition**:
$$\sin(kL) = 0 \Rightarrow kL = n\pi \quad (n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots)$$

The **smallest (first-mode) critical load** corresponds to $n = 1$:
$$\boxed{P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L^2}}$$

### 2.3 Effective Length Concept (For All End Conditions)

Real columns rarely have both ends perfectly hinged. Euler's formula is generalized by replacing $L$ with the **effective length $L_{eff}$**:

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L_{eff}^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{(K L)^2}$$

where $K$ is the **effective length factor**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End-Condition Table (MUST memorize for KTU):**

| End Condition (Bottom → Top) | Sketch | Effective Length $L_{eff}$ | $K$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Both ends **hinged** | ⚙—⚙ | $L$ | $1.0$ |
| Both ends **fixed** | ■——■ | $L/2$ | $0.5$ |
| One end **fixed**, other end **free** | ■—⚪ | $2L$ | $2.0$ |
| One end **fixed**, other end **hinged** | ■—⚙ | $L/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.7L$ | $0.7$ |

### 2.4 Slenderness Ratio

$$\lambda = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{k} = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{\sqrt{I/A}}$$

A **higher slenderness ratio** means a more slender column and a **lower critical load**.

### 2.5 Rankine's Formula (For All Column Types)

Rankine combined both crushing failure (for short columns) and Euler buckling (for long columns) into a single empirical formula:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{1}{P_R} = \dfrac{1}{P_c} + \dfrac{1}{P_E}}$$

$$\boxed{P_R = \dfrac{P_c \cdot P_E}{P_c + P_E} = \dfrac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a \cdot \lambda^2}}$$

where:
- $P_c = \sigma_c \cdot A$ = crushing load (yielding load)
- $P_E = \pi^2 EI / L_{eff}^2$ = Euler's critical load
- $a = \sigma_c / (\pi^2 E)$ = **Rankine's constant** (material property)
- $\lambda = L_{eff}/k$ = slenderness ratio

> [!NOTE]
> **Rankine's Constants (KTU Standard Table):**
> - Cast Iron: $a = 1/1600$
> - Mild Steel: $a = 1/7500$
> - Wrought Iron: $a = 1/9000$
> - Timber: $a = 1/750$

### 2.6 Johnson's Formula (For Medium/Intermediate Columns)

When slenderness ratio is moderate (Euler's theory over-predicts because yielding starts before buckling), **Johnson's parabolic formula** is used:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{cr} = \sigma_c - \left(\dfrac{\sigma_c \cdot \lambda}{2 \pi} \right)^{\! 2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{E}}$$

### 2.7 Secant Formula (For Eccentrically Loaded Columns)

When load has eccentricity $e$ from the column axis:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{P}{A} \left[ 1 + \dfrac{e \cdot c}{k^2} \sec\left( \dfrac{L_{eff}}{2k} \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{AE}} \right) \right]}$$

where $c$ = distance from neutral axis to extreme fibre.

### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Use Case | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L_{eff}^2}$ | Euler critical load (long column) | N |
| 2 | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2}$ | Euler critical stress | N/m² |
| 3 | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{k} = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{\sqrt{I/A}}$ | Slenderness ratio | dimensionless |
| 4 | $k = \sqrt{I/A}$ | Least radius of gyration | m or mm |
| 5 | $P_R = \dfrac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a \lambda^2}$ | Rankine's critical load (all types) | N |
| 6 | $P_{safe} = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{FoS}$ | Safe design load | N |
| 7 | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{P}{A}\left[ 1 + \dfrac{ec}{k^2} \sec\left( \dfrac{L_{eff}}{2k}\sqrt{\dfrac{P}{AE}} \right) \right]$ | Eccentric loading | N/m² |
| 8 | $\sigma_{cr} = a_1 \sigma_y - b_1 \lambda^2$ | Johnson straight-line formula | N/m² |

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility (Robotics Context)

- **Robot arm link design**: Vertical or inclined links of SCARA and articulated robots are essentially **columns under compression** (when supporting payload at the end). The link must be sized to avoid Euler buckling.
- **Pneumatic / hydraulic cylinder rods**: Function as columns. Must be checked for slenderness.
- **Parallel manipulator struts**: In a Stewart platform, the six struts are pin-jointed at both ends, behaving as **both-ends-hinged columns**.
- **Pedestal and base columns of stationary industrial robots**: Often behave as **fixed-free columns** with $L_{eff} = 2L$.
- **Lightweight robotic arm optimization**: Engineers use **aluminum tubes with high slenderness ratios** to minimize inertia, but must check Euler buckling.
- **Space robotic booms**: Deployable booms must remain stable under compressive loads during deployment — Euler buckling is the primary design constraint.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Euler's Crippling Load (Both Ends Hinged)

**Step 1: Setup the Free-Body Diagram**

Consider a column of length $L$, hinged at both ends, subjected to an axial compressive load $P$. When slightly buckled, the column assumes a curved shape. Let the deflected shape be $y(x)$ measured from the original axis.

**Step 2: Bending Moment at Section**

Take a section at distance $x$ from the bottom. The bending moment at this section is the product of the axial load $P$ and the lateral deflection $y$ at that section:

$$M(x) = -P \cdot y(x)$$

The negative sign follows the sign convention that positive moment causes concavity opposite to deflection.

**Step 3: Euler-Bernoulli Beam Equation**

The governing differential equation for the elastic curve of a beam-column is:

$$EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x) = -P \cdot y$$

Rearranging:

$$\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + \dfrac{P}{EI} y = 0$$

**Step 4: Substitute a Constant**

Let $k^2 = \dfrac{P}{EI}$, so the equation becomes:

$$\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + k^2 y = 0$$

**Step 5: General Solution**

The auxiliary (characteristic) equation is $m^2 + k^2 = 0 \Rightarrow m = \pm ik$. Hence the general solution is:

$$y(x) = A \cos(kx) + B \sin(kx)$$

**Step 6: Slope of the Elastic Curve**

Differentiating once:

$$\dfrac{dy}{dx} = -Ak \sin(kx) + Bk \cos(kx)$$

**Step 7: Apply Boundary Conditions**

**Boundary Condition 1:** At $x = 0$, the deflection $y = 0$ (hinge allows no lateral displacement at base):
$$0 = A \cos(0) + B \sin(0) = A$$
$$\therefore A = 0$$

**Boundary Condition 2:** At $x = L$, the deflection $y = 0$ (hinge at top):
$$0 = 0 \cdot \cos(kL) + B \sin(kL)$$
$$\therefore B \sin(kL) = 0$$

**Step 8: Non-Trivial Buckling Condition**

For the column to buckle, we need a non-trivial solution (i.e., not $A = B = 0$). So:

$$\sin(kL) = 0$$

This implies:

$$kL = n\pi, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

**Step 9: Solve for Critical Load**

Squaring both sides: $k^2 L^2 = n^2 \pi^2$. Substitute back $k^2 = P/EI$:

$$\dfrac{P L^2}{EI} = n^2 \pi^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P = \dfrac{n^2 \pi^2 EI}{L^2}$$

**Step 10: Smallest (First-Mode) Critical Load**

The smallest load at which buckling occurs corresponds to $n = 1$:

$$\boxed{P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L^2}}$$

**Step 11: Critical Stress**

$$\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{A} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{A L^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{(L/k)^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2}$$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Effective Length for Fixed-Free Column

**Setup:** A column of length $L$ is fixed at the bottom and free at the top. A vertical load $P$ acts at the free end.

**Bending Moment:** At any section at height $x$ from the base, the lateral deflection is $y$, so:
$$M(x) = -P \cdot (\delta - y) \approx -P \cdot y \quad \text{(for small } \delta \text{, measuring from original axis)}$$

Actually, taking the origin at the fixed end and measuring the upward direction:
$$M(x) = P(\delta - y(x))$$

where $\delta$ is the deflection at the free end.

The differential equation becomes:
$$EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = P(\delta - y)$$

Rearranging:
$$\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + k^2 y = k^2 \delta \quad \text{where} \quad k^2 = P/(EI)$$

**General Solution:**
$$y(x) = A \cos(kx) + B \sin(kx) + \delta$$

**Boundary Condition 1 (Fixed end at $x=0$):** Deflection $y(0) = 0$:
$$0 = A + \delta \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = -\delta$$

**Boundary Condition 2 (Fixed end at $x=0$):** Slope $y'(0) = 0$:
$$y'(x) = -Ak\sin(kx) + Bk\cos(kx)$$
$$y'(0) = Bk = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad B = 0$$

So: $y(x) = \delta[1 - \cos(kx)]$.

**Boundary Condition 3 (Free end at $x=L$):** Deflection $y(L) = \delta$:
$$\delta = \delta[1 - \cos(kL)]$$
$$1 = 1 - \cos(kL)$$
$$\cos(kL) = 0$$

This gives $kL = \pi/2, 3\pi/2, 5\pi/2, \ldots$

The smallest is $kL = \pi/2$, so:
$$k^2 L^2 = \dfrac{\pi^2}{4} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{P L^2}{EI} = \dfrac{\pi^2}{4}$$
$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{4 L^2}$$

This can be written in the form $P_{cr} = \pi^2 EI / (K L)^2$ where $(KL)^2 = 4L^2$, hence $K = 2$:
$$\boxed{P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{(2L)^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{4L^2}}$$

Therefore, the **effective length** is $L_{eff} = 2L$.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Rankine's Formula from Combined Criterion

**Concept:** A real column fails by crushing AND buckling. We want a formula that:
- Reduces to **crushing failure** $P_c = \sigma_c A$ for short columns ($\lambda \to 0$).
- Reduces to **Euler buckling** $P_E = \pi^2 EI/L^2$ for long columns ($\lambda \to \infty$).

**Start with Euler's formula in terms of slenderness ratio:**
$$P_E = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L_{eff}^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E \cdot A k^2}{L_{eff}^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E A}{\lambda^2}$$

**Express as a sum of reciprocals** (common empirical construction):
$$\dfrac{1}{P_R} = \dfrac{1}{P_c} + \dfrac{1}{P_E}$$

**Derivation of $P_R$ in terms of $\lambda$:**

$$\dfrac{1}{P_R} = \dfrac{1}{\sigma_c A} + \dfrac{\lambda^2}{\pi^2 E A}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{P_R} = \dfrac{1}{A} \left[ \dfrac{1}{\sigma_c} + \dfrac{\lambda^2}{\pi^2 E} \right] = \dfrac{1}{A} \cdot \dfrac{\pi^2 E + \sigma_c \lambda^2}{\sigma_c \pi^2 E}$$

Therefore:
$$P_R = \dfrac{A \cdot \sigma_c \cdot \pi^2 E}{\pi^2 E + \sigma_c \lambda^2}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $\pi^2 E$:
$$P_R = \dfrac{\sigma_c A}{1 + \dfrac{\sigma_c \lambda^2}{\pi^2 E}}$$

Defining $a = \dfrac{\sigma_c}{\pi^2 E}$ as **Rankine's constant**:

$$\boxed{P_R = \dfrac{\sigma_c A}{1 + a \lambda^2}}$$

**Limiting Cases Check:**

1. **Short Column** ($\lambda \to 0$): $P_R = \sigma_c A = P_c$ ✓ (crushing)
2. **Long Column** ($\lambda \to \infty$): $P_R = \sigma_c A / (a \lambda^2) = \pi^2 E A / \lambda^2 = \pi^2 EI / L_{eff}^2$ ✓ (Euler)

> [!NOTE]
> **Limiting case proof (long column):**
> $$P_R = \dfrac{\sigma_c A}{a \lambda^2} = \dfrac{\sigma_c A \pi^2 E}{\sigma_c \lambda^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E A}{\lambda^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E A k^2}{L_{eff}^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L_{eff}^2} = P_E$$
> (using $a = \sigma_c / (\pi^2 E)$, and $I = A k^2$, $\lambda = L_{eff}/k$).

### 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A steel column of length **3 m**, with a **hollow circular cross-section** (outer diameter 100 mm, inner diameter 80 mm), is **hinged at both ends**. Take $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm². Find:
(i) Crippling load by Euler's formula
(ii) Crippling load by Rankine's formula (Take $a = 1/7500$ for steel, $\sigma_c = 320$ N/mm²)
(iii) Safe load with FoS = 3.

**Solution:**

**Cross-sectional Properties:**
$$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(100^2 - 80^2) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(10000 - 6400) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(3600) = 900\pi \approx 2827.4 \ \text{mm}^2$$

$$I = \dfrac{\pi}{64}(D_o^4 - D_i^4) = \dfrac{\pi}{64}(100^4 - 80^4) = \dfrac{\pi}{64}(10^8 - 4.096 \times 10^7) = \dfrac{\pi}{64}(5.904 \times 10^7)$$
$$I \approx 2.898 \times 10^6 \ \text{mm}^4$$

**Effective length:** $L_{eff} = L = 3000$ mm (both ends hinged).

**Part (i): Euler's Crippling Load**
$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L_{eff}^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \times (2 \times 10^5) \times (2.898 \times 10^6)}{(3000)^2}$$
$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 2.898 \times 10^6}{9 \times 10^6}$$
$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{5.7206 \times 10^{12}}{9 \times 10^6} = 6.356 \times 10^5 \ \text{N} \approx 635.6 \ \text{kN}$$

**Part (ii): Rankine's Crippling Load**

Slenderness ratio:
$$k = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2.898 \times 10^6}{2827.4}} = \sqrt{1024.7} \approx 32.01 \ \text{mm}$$
$$\lambda = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{k} = \dfrac{3000}{32.01} \approx 93.7$$

Crushing load: $P_c = \sigma_c \cdot A = 320 \times 2827.4 = 904768 \ \text{N} \approx 904.77 \ \text{kN}$

$$P_R = \dfrac{P_c}{1 + a \lambda^2} = \dfrac{904768}{1 + \dfrac{1}{7500}(93.7)^2} = \dfrac{904768}{1 + 1.1703} = \dfrac{904768}{2.1703} \approx 416880 \ \text{N} \approx 416.9 \ \text{kN}$$

**Part (iii): Safe Load**

Using the **smaller** of the two critical loads (Rankine, since it is more accurate for medium columns):
$$P_{safe} = \dfrac{P_R}{FoS} = \dfrac{416880}{3} \approx 138960 \ \text{N} \approx 139.0 \ \text{kN}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

    J --> K
