## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
*Define the following terms:*
*(a) Crippling Load, (b) Effective Length, (c) Slenderness Ratio*

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Crippling Load:** The maximum axial compressive load at which a long, slender column suddenly deflects laterally (buckles) and loses its stability. It is also called the **critical load** or **Euler load**, denoted $P_{cr} = \pi^2 EI / L_{eff}^2$.

(b) **Effective Length ($L_{eff}$):** The length of an equivalent both-ends-hinged column having the same crippling load as the given column with different end conditions. It equals $K \cdot L$ where $K$ is the effective length factor.

(c) **Slenderness Ratio ($\lambda$):** The ratio of effective length to the least radius of gyration of the cross-section, $\lambda = L_{eff}/k$. It is a measure of the slenderness of the column; higher values mean more prone to buckling.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish clearly between short and long columns. How do you classify a column as short or long?*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Short Column | Long Column |
|---|---|---|
| **Slenderness Ratio** | $\lambda < 30$ to $40$ | $\lambda > 100$ |
| **Failure Mode** | Crushing (material yielding) | Buckling (elastic instability) |
| **Critical Load Formula** | $P_c = \sigma_c A$ (crushing) | $P_{cr} = \pi^2 EI / L_{eff}^2$ (Euler) |
| **Stress Distribution** | Uniform compressive stress | Bending stress dominates |
| **Material Property** | Strongly dependent on $\sigma_c$ | Strongly dependent on $E$ |

A column is classified by computing $\lambda = L_{eff}/k$. If $\lambda$ is below the critical value (transition slenderness), it is a short column; if above, it is a long column. For medium columns in between, Rankine's or Johnson's formula is used.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each) — Internal Choice

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**

A cast iron column of **diameter 60 mm** and **length 4 m** has one end **fixed and the other end hinged**. It carries an **axial load of 80 kN**. Find:
**(a)** The **Euler's crippling load** for the column. (7 marks)
**(b)** The **Rankine's crippling load** and hence the **factor of safety**. (7 marks)

Take $E = 1.2 \times 10^5$ N/mm² for cast iron, $\sigma_c = 550$ N/mm², and Rankine's constant $a = 1/1600$.

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a): Euler's Crippling Load**

Step 1: Cross-section area and moment of inertia for solid circular section:
$$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}d^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(60)^2 = 900\pi = 2827.43 \ \text{mm}^2$$
$$I = \dfrac{\pi}{64}d^4 = \dfrac{\pi}{64}(60)^4 = \dfrac{\pi}{64}(12,960,000) = 636172.5 \ \text{mm}^4$$

Step 2: Effective length for **fixed-hinged** end condition:
$$L_{eff} = \dfrac{L}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{4000}{\sqrt{2}} = 2828.43 \ \text{mm}$$

Step 3: Apply Euler's formula:
$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L_{eff}^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \times 1.2 \times 10^5 \times 636172.5}{(2828.43)^2}$$
$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{9.8696 \times 1.2 \times 10^5 \times 636172.5}{8,000,000}$$
$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{7.5328 \times 10^{11}}{8 \times 10^6} = 94160 \ \text{N} \approx 94.16 \ \text{kN}$$

**Part (b): Rankine's Crippling Load and Factor of Safety**

Step 1: Compute radius of gyration:
$$k = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{636172.5}{2827.43}} = \sqrt{225} = 15 \ \text{mm}$$

Step 2: Slenderness ratio:
$$\lambda = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{k} = \dfrac{2828.43}{15} = 188.56$$

Step 3: Compute crushing load:
$$P_c = \sigma_c A = 550 \times 2827.43 = 1,555,087 \ \text{N} \approx 1555 \ \text{kN}$$

Step 4: Apply Rankine's formula:
$$P_R = \dfrac{P_c}{1 + a \lambda^2} = \dfrac{1555087}{1 + \dfrac{1}{1600}(188.56)^2} = \dfrac{1555087}{1 + 22.222}$$
$$P_R = \dfrac{1555087}{23.222} = 66969 \ \text{N} \approx 66.97 \ \text{kN}$$

Step 5: Factor of Safety:
$$FoS = \dfrac{P_R}{P_{applied}} = \dfrac{66.97}{80} = 0.837$$

**Conclusion:** Since $FoS < 1$, the column will **FAIL** under the given load. The design must be revised by either increasing the diameter or reducing the length or the applied load.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**

A **hollow circular cast iron column** has **outer diameter 150 mm** and **inner diameter 100 mm**. It is **4.5 m long** and is **fixed at both ends**. Determine:
**(a)** The **crippling load using Euler's formula**. (7 marks)
**(b)** The **crippling load using Rankine's formula** and the **safe load** with $FoS = 6$. (7 marks)

Take $E = 1.2 \times 10^5$ N/mm², $\sigma_c = 550$ N/mm², and $a = 1/1600$ for cast iron.

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a): Euler's Crippling Load**

Step 1: Cross-section area:
$$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(150^2 - 100^2) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(22500 - 10000) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(12500)$$
$$A = 9817.48 \ \text{mm}^2$$

Step 2: Moment of inertia:
$$I = \dfrac{\pi}{64}(D_o^4 - D_i^4) = \dfrac{\pi}{64}(150^4 - 100^4) = \dfrac{\pi}{64}(506,250,000 - 100,000,000)$$
$$I = \dfrac{\pi}{64}(406,250,000) = 1.9937 \times 10^7 \ \text{mm}^4$$

Step 3: Effective length for **both ends fixed**:
$$L_{eff} = \dfrac{L}{2} = \dfrac{4500}{2} = 2250 \ \text{mm}$$

Step 4: Euler's critical load:
$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L_{eff}^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \times 1.2 \times 10^5 \times 1.9937 \times 10^7}{(2250)^2}$$
$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{9.8696 \times 1.2 \times 10^5 \times 1.9937 \times 10^7}{5,062,500}$$
$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{2.3613 \times 10^{13}}{5.0625 \times 10^6} = 4.665 \times 10^6 \ \text{N} \approx 4665 \ \text{kN}$$

**Part (b): Rankine's Crippling Load and Safe Load**

Step 1: Radius of gyration:
$$k = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.9937 \times 10^7}{9817.48}} = \sqrt{2030.86} = 45.06 \ \text{mm}$$

Step 2: Slenderness ratio:
$$\lambda = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{k} = \dfrac{2250}{45.06} = 49.93$$

Step 3: Crushing load:
$$P_c = \sigma_c A = 550 \times 9817.48 = 5,399,614 \ \text{N} \approx 5399.6 \ \text{kN}$$

Step 4: Rankine's load:
$$P_R = \dfrac{P_c}{1 + a \lambda^2} = \dfrac{5,399,614}{1 + \dfrac{1}{1600}(49.93)^2} = \dfrac{5,399,614}{1 + 1.5581}$$
$$P_R = \dfrac{5,399,614}{2.5581} = 2,110,770 \ \text{N} \approx 2110.77 \ \text{kN}$$

Step 5: Safe load:
$$P_{safe} = \dfrac{P_R}{FoS} = \dfrac{2110.77}{6} \approx 351.8 \ \text{kN}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong end-condition table** — Students often write $L_{eff} = L/2$ for both-ends-hinged. Memorize: $K = 1$ for both-hinged, $K = 0.5$ for both-fixed, $K = 2$ for fixed-free, $K = 0.7$ for fixed-hinged.
> 2. **Units mismatch** — Convert everything to **mm and N** (or m and N) consistently. Mixing mm and m is the most common error.
> 3. **Forgetting the "least" in least moment of inertia** — A column always buckles about the axis with the *smallest* $I$. For asymmetric sections, $I_{min}$ must be used.
> 4. **Applying Euler's formula to a short column** — Euler's formula over-predicts the crippling load for short/medium columns and is invalid there. Use Rankine's formula.
> 5. **Final answer without units** — KTU examiners deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** if units are missing.
> 6. **Forgetting $\pi^2$ in Euler's formula** — The constant is $\pi^2 \approx 9.8696$, not just $\pi$. Use a calculator carefully.
> 7. **In Rankine's formula, using $L$ instead of $L_{eff}$** — Always use $L_{eff}$ for computing slenderness ratio.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaways for KTU Exam:**
>
> ✅ **Euler's formula applies ONLY to long columns** (slenderness ratio above critical value).
>
> ✅ **The four end conditions** and their $K$ values: Both-hinged $K=1$, Both-fixed $K=0.5$, Fixed-free $K=2$, Fixed-hinged $K=0.7$.
>
> ✅ **Short column → Crushing**: $P_c = \sigma_c A$.
>
> ✅ **Long column → Buckling (Euler)**: $P_{cr} = \pi^2 EI / L_{eff}^2$.
>
> ✅ **Medium column → Rankine**: $P_R = \sigma_c A / (1 + a \lambda^2)$ where $a = \sigma_c / (\pi^2 E)$.
>
> ✅ **Slenderness ratio** $\lambda = L_{eff}/k$ governs failure mode.
>
> ✅ **Least radius of gyration** $k = \sqrt{I/A}$ uses minimum $I$.
>
> ✅ **Critical stress** $\sigma_{cr} = \pi^2 E / \lambda^2$ — independent of cross-sectional area, depends only on $E$ and $\lambda$.
>
> ✅ **Robotic link columns** are often pin-ended (both ends hinged) → use $L_{eff} = L$.
>
> ✅ **Pedestal columns** of stationary industrial robots are typically fixed-free → use $L_{eff} = 2L$ (most critical case).
>
> ✅ **Factor of Safety** $FoS = P_{cr} / P_{applied}$ → must be > 1 for safe design; typically 2–4 for robotic columns.
>
> ✅ **Eccentric loading** is handled by the **Secant formula** — relevant when the load is not perfectly axial.
>
> ✅ **For robotic arm optimization**, increasing $I$ (e.g., hollow circular or box section) is more efficient than increasing $A$ for improving buckling resistance.
>
> ✅ **Standard materials and $a$-values** to memorize: Mild steel $a = 1/7500$, Cast iron $a = 1/1600$, Wrought iron $a = 1/9000$, Timber $a = 1/750$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Euler's Theory of Columns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT525 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS OF ROBOTIC PARTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Euler's Theory of Columns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Euler's Theory of Columns** is a classical buckling theory formulated by the Swiss mathematician **Leonhard Euler (1757)** that predicts the **axial compressive load** at which a slender, initially straight column will suddenly deflect laterally (buckle). This load is termed the **Critical Buckling Load ($P_{cr}$)** or **Euler's Crippling Load**.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 \, E \, I}{L_e^2}$$

Where the symbols represent:
- **$E$** → **Young's Modulus of Elasticity** of the column material (typically measured in **GPa**)
- **$I$** → **Area Moment of Inertia** of the cross-section about the neutral axis of buckling (units: **mm⁴** or **m⁴**)
- **$L_e$** → **Effective Length** of the column, which depends on the end-support conditions (units: **mm** or **m**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Euler's theory is strictly applicable to **long, slender, isotropic, homogeneous columns** where failure occurs due to **elastic instability (buckling)** rather than material crushing. The theory is **invalid for short columns** where compressive/yielding failure dominates.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine holding a **thin, flexible plastic ruler** vertically on a table and pressing straight down on its top with your thumb. Initially, the ruler simply compresses slightly. But as you gradually increase the downward force, at a specific moment — seemingly out of nowhere — the ruler suddenly **snaps sideways** with a sharp *crack*, even though the force you applied was nowhere near enough to crush the plastic.

That sudden sideways snap is **buckling**, and the force at which it occurred is the **Euler Critical Load**.

In the context of **Robotic Parts (PERAT525)**, this is critically important. Consider the **manipulator link** of an industrial robot arm, the **vertical actuator shaft** of a pick-and-place machine, or the **support column of a SCARA robot base**. Each of these components may experience enormous axial compression from payload, gravity, and dynamic forces. If the column is slender and the load is even moderately high, it could buckle sideways — leading to **catastrophic failure of the entire robotic system**. Euler's theory is the engineer's primary tool to prevent this.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants to Remember:**
> - Standard gravity: **$g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$**
> - Young's Modulus of structural steel: **$E \approx 200 \, GPa$**
> - Young's Modulus of aluminum alloys: **$E \approx 70 \, GPa$**
> - Young's Modulus of carbon fiber (used in robotic arms): **$E \approx 70 - 200 \, GPa$**

## 1.3 Why Columns Buckle — The Governing Physics

A straight column under axial compression is theoretically in stable equilibrium. However, any infinitesimal lateral disturbance (a small nudge, a manufacturing imperfection, or a vibration) introduces a **bending moment** equal to $M = P \cdot y$ at the deflected point. If the load $P$ is large enough, the internal restoring moment from the column's flexural rigidity ($EI$) cannot overcome this destabilizing bending moment, and the column collapses sideways.

The **threshold load** at which this instability initiates is precisely Euler's $P_{cr}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of Euler's Buckling Concept

The theory is built upon a sequence of fundamental assumptions, each of which must be understood for proper KTU application:

- **Step 1 — Axial Loading Only:** The column is subjected to a purely compressive axial load $P$ applied at its centroid. No transverse or eccentric loads are considered.
- **Step 2 — Initial Perfectly Straight Geometry:** The column is assumed to be perfectly straight, prismatic (uniform cross-section), and free from any initial curvature.
- **Step 3 — Homogeneous & Isotropic Material:** The material properties (especially $E$) are uniform throughout the column volume and identical in all directions.
- **Step 4 — Elastic Behavior:** The material remains within the **proportional elastic limit**; no plastic deformation or yielding occurs.
- **Step 5 — Pin-Ended Idealization:** The standard form of Euler's formula assumes both ends are hinged (pinned). For other end conditions, an **effective length factor $K$** is introduced.
- **Step 6 — Small Deflection Assumption:** The lateral deflection $y$ is small enough that $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta \approx \theta$, allowing linearization.
- **Step 7 — Bending Dominates Axial Deformation:** The shortening due to compression is neglected compared to the lateral bending displacement.

## 2.2 The Concept of Effective Length ($L_e$)

The **effective length** of a column is the length of an *equivalent pin-ended column* that would buckle under the same critical load as the actual column with its specific end conditions. It is expressed as:

$$L_e = K \cdot L$$

Where **$K$** is the **effective length constant** (also called the *end-fixity factor*) and $L$ is the actual column length.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $L_e$ matters in robotics:** A robotic column fixed to a heavy machine base (fixed end) and free at the top (where a sensor or gripper is attached) will have $K = 2.0$, meaning its effective length is **twice** its actual length. This dramatically reduces its safe load-carrying capacity.

## 2.3 End Conditions and Effective Length Constants

The four classical end-support conditions in KTU syllabus are:

| End Condition (Both Ends) | Schematic Description | Effective Length $L_e$ | K-Value | Buckling Mode Shape |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Both Ends Pinned (Hinged) | Pinned at base & top; free to rotate | $L$ | **1.0** | Single half-sine wave |
| Both Ends Fixed | Clamped at base & top; no rotation | $L/2$ | **0.5** | Two half-sine waves |
| One End Fixed, Other Free | Cantilever (free at top) | $2L$ | **2.0** | Quarter sine wave |
| One End Fixed, Other Pinned | Clamped at base, hinged at top | $L/\sqrt{2}$ | **$1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$** | Asymmetric curve |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The case of *one end fixed and the other free* (cantilever) gives the **lowest buckling resistance** ($L_e = 2L$, hence $P_{cr}$ is reduced by a factor of 4 compared to a pinned-pinned column of the same length).

## 2.4 Slenderness Ratio — The Master Parameter

The **slenderness ratio ($\lambda$)** is a dimensionless number that quantifies how "slender" a column is. It is defined as:

$$\lambda = \frac{L_e}{r}$$

Where **$r$** is the **radius of gyration** of the cross-section, given by:

$$r = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}}$$

- $I$ = least moment of inertia of the cross-section
- $A$ = cross-sectional area

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the "least" $I$?** A column always buckles about the axis with the **lowest** moment of inertia. For a rectangular cross-section, this is the axis parallel to the longer side. For a robotic manipulator link with rectangular section $b \times d$ (where $d > b$), buckling occurs about the axis with $I_{min} = \frac{b d^3}{12}$ ... wait — correction: $I_{min} = \frac{d \cdot b^3}{12}$ (about the axis along the longer dimension $d$).

## 2.5 Critical Buckling Stress

The **critical stress** $\sigma_{cr}$ is the Euler load divided by the cross-sectional area:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{P_{cr}}{A} = \frac{\pi^2 \, E}{\lambda^2} = \frac{\pi^2 \, E \, r^2}{L_e^2}$$

This is famously known as **Euler's Stress Formula** and forms the foundation for all column design codes.

## 2.6 Column Classification by Slenderness

A column is classified based on its slenderness ratio relative to a **limiting slenderness ratio ($\lambda_{lim}$)**:

$$\lambda_{lim} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2 \, E}{\sigma_y}}$$

Where $\sigma_y$ is the **yield stress** of the material.

| Classification | Slenderness Range | Governing Failure Mode | Applicable Formula |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Long Column** | $\lambda \geq \lambda_{lim}$ | Elastic Buckling | Euler's Formula |
| **Intermediate Column** | Moderate range | Inelastic Buckling | Johnson's Formula / Empirical |
| **Short Column** | $\lambda$ very small | Direct Compression / Crushing | $P = \sigma_y \cdot A$ |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Euler's theory is indispensable in:
- **Robotics:** Design of manipulator links, vertical prismatic joints, and pedestal supports.
- **Civil Engineering:** Bridge piers, building columns, scaffolding poles.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Press frames, hydraulic cylinder rods, piston pins.
- **Aerospace:** Wing spars in aircraft, rocket body tubes, landing gear struts.
- **Structural Mechatronics:** Cantilever sensor mounts, precision positioning stages.

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables | Typical Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Euler Critical Load | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \, E \, I}{L_e^2}$ | $E, I, L_e$ | N |
| 2 | Effective Length | $L_e = K \cdot L$ | $K, L$ | mm |
| 3 | Radius of Gyration | $r = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}}$ | $I, A$ | mm |
| 4 | Slenderness Ratio | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_e}{r}$ | $L_e, r$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Critical Stress | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \, E}{\lambda^2}$ | $E, \lambda$ | MPa |
| 6 | Limiting Slenderness | $\lambda_{lim} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi^2 \, E}{\sigma_y}}$ | $E, \sigma_y$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Safe Load (with FOS) | $P_{safe} = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{FOS}$ | $P_{cr}, FOS$ | N |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Euler's Critical Load

We begin by considering a **pin-ended column** of length $L$, subjected to an axial compressive load $P$ applied at its centroid. We will derive the formula from first principles using the **Euler-Bernoulli beam equation**.

### Step 1: Set Up the Coordinate System and Initial State

Let the column's undeformed axis coincide with the x-axis. The column is hinged at both ends, with the origin at the bottom.

When the column buckles, it deflects laterally. Let $y(x)$ be the small lateral deflection at any section located at distance $x$ from the bottom.

### Step 2: Determine the Internal Bending Moment

At the deflected section, the internal bending moment $M$ is generated by the axial load $P$ acting through the eccentricity $y$. By taking moments about the deflected point:

$$M = -P \cdot y$$

The **negative sign** arises from the convention that the bending moment causes the column to bend back toward the original axis (i.e., a restoring moment).

> **Conversion Logic:** The product $P \cdot y$ represents the moment arm of the axial force about the deflected cross-section. We assume $P$ remains vertical (small deflection approximation), so the moment is simply force × perpendicular distance.

### Step 3: Apply the Euler-Bernoulli Beam Equation

The classical differential equation for elastic bending of a beam is:

$$EI \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M$$

Substituting the moment from Step 2:

$$EI \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = -P \cdot y$$

Rearranging to standard form:

$$EI \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + P \cdot y = 0$$

### Step 4: Define the Constant $k^2$ to Linearize

Let us define a positive constant:

$$k^2 = \frac{P}{EI}$$

This substitution is valid because for a buckling (instability) condition, $P > 0$ and $EI > 0$, hence $k^2 > 0$.

The differential equation becomes:

$$\frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + k^2 y = 0$$

### Step 5: Solve the Linear Second-Order ODE

This is a standard second-order linear homogeneous ODE with constant coefficients. The **auxiliary (characteristic) equation** is:

$$m^2 + k^2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad m = \pm i k$$

Since the roots are purely imaginary, the general solution is:

$$y(x) = A \cos(kx) + B \sin(kx)$$

Where $A$ and $B$ are constants of integration to be determined from boundary conditions.

### Step 6: Apply Boundary Conditions

For a **pin-ended column**, the boundary conditions are:

- **At $x = 0$ (bottom pin):** Deflection is zero → $y(0) = 0$
- **At $x = L$ (top pin):** Deflection is zero → $y(L) = 0$

**Applying the first condition** $y(0) = 0$:

$$y(0) = A \cos(0) + B \sin(0) = A \cdot 1 + B \cdot 0 = A = 0$$

> **Conversion Logic:** $\cos(0) = 1$ and $\sin(0) = 0$, so only the cosine term survives, forcing $A = 0$.

This simplifies the solution to:

$$y(x) = B \sin(kx)$$

**Applying the second condition** $y(L) = 0$:

$$y(L) = B \sin(kL) = 0$$

For a **non-trivial solution** (i.e., a buckled shape, $B \neq 0$), we must have:

$$\sin(kL) = 0$$

### Step 7: Solve the Trigonometric Condition

The condition $\sin(kL) = 0$ is satisfied when:

$$kL = n\pi \quad \text{for} \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

Therefore:

$$k = \frac{n\pi}{L}$$

### Step 8: Identify the Critical (Lowest) Mode

Squaring both sides:

$$k^2 = \frac{n^2 \pi^2}{L^2}$$

Recall from Step 4 that $k^2 = \dfrac{P}{EI}$. Equating:

$$\frac{P}{EI} = \frac{n^2 \pi^2}{L^2}$$

Solving for the load $P$:

$$P = \frac{n^2 \pi^2 EI}{L^2}$$

The **critical buckling load** corresponds to the **smallest non-zero value of $P$**, which occurs at $n = 1$ (the fundamental mode of buckling):

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{L^2}$$

For $n = 2, 3, \ldots$ we obtain higher buckling modes with progressively higher critical loads — these are theoretically possible but practically suppressed because the column will buckle at the lowest load first.

### Step 9: Generalize for All End Conditions

For a column with effective length $L_e = K \cdot L$, the boundary conditions modify the characteristic equation. The generalized result is:

$$\boxed{P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 \, E \, I}{L_e^2} = \frac{\pi^2 \, E \, I}{(K \cdot L)^2}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Critical Stress Formula

Starting from the critical load expression:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2}$$

Divide both sides by the cross-sectional area $A$:

$$\frac{P_{cr}}{A} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{A \cdot L_e^2}$$

The left-hand side is the **critical stress** $\sigma_{cr}$. The right-hand side can be regrouped using $I = A r^2$:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E (A r^2)}{A \cdot L_e^2} = \frac{\pi^2 E \, r^2}{L_e^2}$$

Recalling that the slenderness ratio is $\lambda = \dfrac{L_e}{r}$, we can write $r^2 / L_e^2 = 1/\lambda^2$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2}}$$

This is **Euler's Critical Stress Formula**, a direct function of the slenderness ratio.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A solid circular steel column used in a robotic manipulator has length $L = 2.5 \, m$ and diameter $D = 60 \, mm$. Both ends are pinned. Take $E = 200 \, GPa$ and yield stress $\sigma_y = 250 \, MPa$. Determine: (a) The critical buckling load, (b) The critical stress, (c) The slenderness ratio, and (d) Classify the column.

**Given Data:**
- $L = 2.5 \, m = 2500 \, mm$
- $D = 60 \, mm$
- $E = 200 \times 10^3 \, N/mm^2 = 2 \times 10^5 \, MPa$
- $\sigma_y = 250 \, MPa$
- Both ends pinned → $L_e = L = 2500 \, mm$

### (a) Critical Buckling Load

First, compute the moment of inertia for a solid circular cross-section:

$$I = \frac{\pi D^4}{64} = \frac{\pi \times (60)^4}{64}$$

$$I = \frac{\pi \times 12{,}960{,}000}{64} = \frac{40{,}715{,}040.6}{64} \approx 636{,}172.5 \, mm^4$$

Now apply Euler's formula:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2} = \frac{\pi^2 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 636{,}172.5}{(2500)^2}$$

Numerator:
$$\pi^2 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 636{,}172.5 = 9.8696 \times 1.2723 \times 10^{11} \approx 1.2557 \times 10^{12}$$

Denominator:
$$(2500)^2 = 6.25 \times 10^6$$

Therefore:
$$P_{cr} = \frac{1.2557 \times 10^{12}}{6.25 \times 10^6} \approx 2.009 \times 10^5 \, N \approx 200.9 \, kN$$

### (b) Critical Stress

Cross-sectional area:
$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (60)^2}{4} = 2827.43 \, mm^2$$

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{P_{cr}}{A} = \frac{2.009 \times 10^5}{2827.43} \approx 71.05 \, MPa$$

### (c) Slenderness Ratio

Radius of gyration:
$$r = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{636{,}172.5}{2827.43}} = \sqrt{225} = 15 \, mm$$

$$\lambda = \frac{L_e}{r} = \frac{2500}{15} \approx 166.67$$

### (d) Column Classification

Compute the limiting slenderness ratio:
$$\lambda_{lim} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2 E}{\sigma_y}} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2 \times 2 \times 10^5}{250}} = \sqrt{7895.68} \approx 88.86$$

Since $\lambda = 166.67 > \lambda_{lim} = 88.86$, the column is a **long column** and Euler's formula is valid.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Euler's Buckling Analysis Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete analytical workflow that a KTU 2024 student should follow when solving any column buckling problem. This is a block-level functional architecture mapping the logical interactions between each analysis step.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Identify Column Geometry and Material] --> B[Step 2: Extract L, A, I, E, sigma_y]
    B --> C[Step 3: Determine End Conditions]
    C --> D[Step 4: Select K Value from KTU Table]
    D --> E[Step 5: Compute Effective Length Le = K times L]
    E --> F[Step 6: Compute Radius of Gyration r = sqrt I over A]
    F --> G[Step 7: Compute Slenderness Ratio lambda = Le over r]
    G --> H{Decision: Long or Short Column?}
    H -->|lambda greater than lambda_lim| I[Apply Euler Formula: Pcr = pi squared E I over Le squared]
    H -->|lambda less than lambda_lim| J[Use Johnson or Empirical Formula]
    I --> K[Step 8: Compute Pcr and sigma_cr]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Step 9: Apply Factor of Safety FOS]
    L --> M[Step 10: Report P_safe = Pcr over FOS]
    M --> N[Final Design Verification for Robotic Component]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Each block represents a discrete analytical step. The decision diamond in the middle (Block H) is the critical branching point that determines which formula regime applies. The architecture ensures that no computational step is skipped, mirroring the systematic approach expected in KTU board examinations.

## 4.2 End Condition Schematic Overview

The following block diagram maps the four classical end conditions and their associated $K$ values in a single comparative view, which is essential for KTU viva and exam preparation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Both Ends Pinned] -->|K = 1.0| Q1[Le = L]
    P2[Both Ends Fixed] -->|K = 0.5| Q2[Le = L over 2]
    P3[Fixed-Free Cantilever] -->|K = 2.0| Q3[Le = 2L]
    P4[Fixed-Pinned] -->|K = 0.707| Q4[Le = L over sqrt 2]
    Q1 --> R[Compute Pcr from Euler Formula]
    Q2 --> R
    Q3 --> R
    Q4 --> R
```

## 4.3 Buckling Mode Shape Visualization (Conceptual Block Map)

The following conceptual block diagram represents the deflected shapes of columns for the first buckling mode ($n=1$) under different end conditions. Each block maps the boundary conditions to the mathematical deflected shape function.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Pinned-Pinned Mode] --> T1[Deflection: y = B sin pi x over L]
    S2[Fixed-Fixed Mode] --> T2[Deflection: y = B sin 2 pi x over L]
    S3[Fixed-Free Mode] --> T3[Deflection: y = B 1 minus cos pi x over 2L]
    S4[Fixed-Pinned Mode] --> T4[Deflection: Asymmetric curve with tan kL = kL]
    T1 --> U[All yield same Pcr formula with different Le]
    T2 --> U
    T3 --> U
    T4 --> U
```

> **Engineering Insight from Diagrams:** Notice that every end condition, despite having a different deflected shape, funnels into the **same final Euler formula** — only the effective length $L_e$ changes. This is the unifying power of the $K$-factor approach.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Define the terms (i) Effective length of a column, (ii) Slenderness ratio, and (iii) Critical buckling load. State their units.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Effective Length ($L_e$):** The equivalent length of a pin-ended column that would buckle under the same critical load as the actual column with its given end conditions. It is given by $L_e = K \cdot L$, where $K$ is the effective length constant. **Unit: mm or m.**

**(ii) Slenderness Ratio ($\lambda$):** The ratio of effective length to the least radius of gyration of the cross-section, $\lambda = L_e / r$. It is a **dimensionless quantity** that indicates how slender a column is. Higher $\lambda$ means greater susceptibility to buckling.

**(iii) Critical Buckling Load ($P_{cr}$):** The axial compressive load at which a slender column, assumed perfectly straight, becomes unstable and buckles laterally. Mathematically: $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2}$. **Unit: N or kN.**

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: State Euler's formula for critical load. Under what conditions is this formula valid?**

**Model Answer:**

**Euler's Formula:**
$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2}$$

**Validity Conditions (Euler's Assumptions):**
- The column is **initially perfectly straight** and the load is truly **axial** (concentric).
- The column material is **homogeneous, isotropic**, and remains within the **elastic limit** (stress does not exceed the proportional limit).
- The **self-weight** of the column is negligible compared to the applied load.
- The **shortening** of the column due to direct compression is neglected.
- The **end conditions** must be ideal (perfectly pinned, fixed, or free as assumed).
- The column is **slender** — i.e., its slenderness ratio exceeds the limiting value $\lambda_{lim}$, so that failure occurs by **elastic buckling** and not by crushing or yielding.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** For full marks in Part A, students must list **at least 5 of the 6 assumptions** above. Merely stating the formula without the assumptions scores only partial credit.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

A hollow circular cast iron column used in a SCARA robot's vertical support has **outer diameter $D_o = 120 \, mm$** and **inner diameter $D_i = 90 \, mm$**, with **length $L = 4 \, m$**. The column is **fixed at the base and free at the top** (cantilever type). Take $E = 200 \, GPa$ and yield stress $\sigma_y = 320 \, MPa$. A factor of safety of 3.0 must be applied.

**Calculate:**
**(a)** The slenderness ratio of the column. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The safe axial load that the column can carry. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Given Data:**
- $D_o = 120 \, mm$, $D_i = 90 \, mm$, $L = 4000 \, mm$
- Fixed-free end condition → $K = 2.0$ → $L_e = 2 \times 4000 = 8000 \, mm$
- $E = 2 \times 10^5 \, MPa$, $\sigma_y = 320 \, MPa$, $FOS = 3.0$

#### Part (a): Slenderness Ratio

**Step 1: Compute Cross-Sectional Area**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} (D_o^2 - D_i^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} (120^2 - 90^2)$$

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} (14400 - 8100) = \frac{\pi}{4} (6300) = 4948.0 \, mm^2$$

**Step 2: Compute Moment of Inertia (Hollow Circular Section)**

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64} (D_o^4 - D_i^4) = \frac{\pi}{64} (120^4 - 90^4)$$

$$120^4 = 207{,}360{,}000 \, mm^4 \quad ; \quad 90^4 = 65{,}610{,}000 \, mm^4$$

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64} (207{,}360{,}000 - 65{,}610{,}000) = \frac{\pi}{64} \times 141{,}750{,}000$$

$$I = \frac{445{,}281{,}055.6}{64} \approx 6{,}957{,}516.5 \, mm^4$$

**Step 3: Compute Radius of Gyration**

$$r = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{6{,}957{,}516.5}{4948.0}} = \sqrt{1406.13} \approx 37.50 \, mm$$

**Step 4: Compute Slenderness Ratio**

$$\lambda = \frac{L_e}{r} = \frac{8000}{37.50} \approx 213.33$$

**Step 5: Verify Column Classification**

$$\lambda_{lim} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2 E}{\sigma_y}} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2 \times 2 \times 10^5}{320}} = \sqrt{6168.5} \approx 78.54$$

Since $\lambda = 213.33 \gg \lambda_{lim} = 78.54$, the column is a **long column** and Euler's formula is fully applicable. ✓

#### Part (b): Safe Axial Load

**Step 1: Apply Euler's Critical Load Formula**

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2} = \frac{\pi^2 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 6{,}957{,}516.5}{(8000)^2}$$

Numerator:
$$\pi^2 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 6{,}957{,}516.5 = 9.8696 \times 1.3915 \times 10^{12} = 1.3732 \times 10^{13}$$

Denominator:
$$(8000)^2 = 6.4 \times 10^7$$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{1.3732 \times 10^{13}}{6.4 \times 10^7} \approx 2.146 \times 10^5 \, N \approx 214.6 \, kN$$

**Step 2: Apply Factor of Safety**

$$P_{safe} = \frac{P_{cr}}{FOS} = \frac{214.6}{3.0} \approx 71.5 \, kN$$

**Final Answer (b):** $P_{safe} \approx 71.5 \, kN$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

A solid rectangular steel column used as a **robotic manipulator link** has dimensions **$b = 50 \, mm$ (breadth)** and **$d = 100 \, mm$ (depth)**, with length $L = 3 \, m$. The column is **fixed at both ends**. Take $E = 200 \, GPa$, $\sigma_y = 250 \, MPa$, and Factor of Safety = 2.5.

**Calculate:**
**(a)** The Euler critical buckling load about the weaker axis. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The critical stress and verify if the column is a long column. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Given Data:**
- $b = 50 \, mm$, $d = 100 \, mm$, $L = 3000 \, mm$
- Fixed-fixed end condition → $K = 0.5$ → $L_e = 0.5 \times 3000 = 1500 \, mm$
- $E = 2 \times 10^5 \, MPa$, $\sigma_y = 250 \, MPa$, $FOS = 2.5$

#### Part (a): Euler Critical Load

**Step 1: Identify the Weaker Axis**

For a rectangular section, the moment of inertia is minimum about the axis parallel to the longer side $d$. Thus:

$$I_{min} = \frac{d \cdot b^3}{12} = \frac{100 \times (50)^3}{12} = \frac{100 \times 125{,}000}{12} = 1{,}041{,}666.7 \, mm^4$$

**Step 2: Apply Euler's Formula with Effective Length**

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I_{min}}{L_e^2} = \frac{\pi^2 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 1{,}041{,}666.7}{(1500)^2}$$

Numerator:
$$9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 1{,}041{,}666.7 = 9.8696 \times 2.0833 \times 10^{11} = 2.0562 \times 10^{12}$$

Denominator:
$$(1500)^2 = 2.25 \times 10^6$$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{2.0562 \times 10^{12}}{2.25 \times 10^6} \approx 9.139 \times 10^5 \, N \approx 913.9 \, kN$$

#### Part (b): Critical Stress and Column Classification

**Step 1: Compute Cross-Sectional Area**

$$A = b \times d = 50 \times 100 = 5000 \, mm^2$$

**Step 2: Compute Critical Stress**

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{P_{cr}}{A} = \frac{9.139 \times 10^5}{5000} = 182.78 \, MPa$$

**Step 3: Compute Radius of Gyration (about weaker axis)**

$$r_{min} = \sqrt{\frac{I_{min}}{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{1{,}041{,}666.7}{5000}} = \sqrt{208.33} \approx 14.43 \, mm$$

**Step 4: Compute Slenderness Ratio**

$$\lambda = \frac{L_e}{r_{min}} = \frac{1500}{14.43} \approx 103.95$$

**Step 5: Compute Limiting Slenderness Ratio**

$$\lambda_{lim} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2 E}{\sigma_y}} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2 \times 2 \times 10^5}{250}} = \sqrt{7895.68} \approx 88.86$$

**Step 6: Verification**

Since $\lambda = 103.95 > \lambda_{lim} = 88.86$, the column **IS a long column**. Therefore, the Euler critical load calculated in Part (a) is valid. ✓

**Step 7: Compute Safe Load**

$$P_{safe} = \frac{P_{cr}}{FOS} = \frac{913.9}{2.5} \approx 365.6 \, kN$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **❌ Forgetting to use $I_{min}$ (least moment of inertia):** A column will ALWAYS buckle about the weaker axis. Using $I_{max}$ gives an **unsafe, falsely high** critical load. **Marks lost: Up to 4 out of 7.**
> - **❌ Confusing end conditions and $K$ values:** Many students swap pinned-fixed and fixed-pinned. Memorize: *free end = longest $L_e$* ($K=2$); *both fixed = shortest $L_e$* ($K=0.5$).
> - **❌ Not verifying $\lambda > \lambda_{lim}$:** If a student applies Euler's formula to a short column, the examiner will deduct 1-2 marks even if the numerical answer happens to be correct.
> - **❌ Unit mismatch:** Mixing $E$ in GPa with $I$ in $mm^4$ and $L$ in $m$ causes errors by factors of $10^9$. **Always convert everything to consistent units (N, mm, MPa) first.**
> - **❌ Forgetting the Factor of Safety:** The final safe load is $P_{cr} / FOS$, not $P_{cr}$ alone.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Euler's Critical Load:** $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L_e^2}$ — applies only to long, slender, elastic columns.
- ✅ **Effective Length:** $L_e = K \cdot L$; memorize the four $K$-values: **1.0, 0.5, 2.0, 1/\sqrt{2}$**.
- ✅ **Slenderness Ratio:** $\lambda = L_e / r$ — always use the **least radius of gyration**.
- ✅ **Critical Stress:** $\sigma_{cr} = \pi^2 E / \lambda^2$ — independent of cross-sectional area.
- ✅ **Limiting Slenderness:** $\lambda_{lim} = \sqrt{\pi^2 E / \sigma_y}$ — separates long-column regime from short-column regime.
- ✅ **Column Classification Rule:**
  - $\lambda \geq \lambda_{lim}$ → **Long Column → Euler's Formula**
  - $\lambda < \lambda_{lim}$ → **Short/Intermediate Column → Johnson's or empirical formulas**
- ✅ **Always verify Euler's assumptions** before applying the formula (straight, axial, elastic, homogeneous, prismatic).
- ✅ **Buckling occurs about the axis of MINIMUM $I$** (weakest axis), not the strongest.
- ✅ **Factor of Safety (FOS):** Typical values are **2.5 to 4.0** for robotic column design. Safe load = $P_{cr} / FOS$.
- ✅ **Robotic Design Implication:** Cantilever-mounted sensors/columns ($K=2.0$) are the **most vulnerable** to buckling — use larger cross-sections or add intermediate supports.
- ✅ **Cross-Sectional Efficiency:** Hollow circular and hollow rectangular sections give **higher $I/A$ ratios** than solid sections of the same area, making them structurally efficient for robotic links.
- ✅ **Common Material Values:** $E_{steel} \approx 200 \, GPa$, $E_{Al} \approx 70 \, GPa$, $E_{cast iron} \approx 100-150 \, GPa$.
- ✅ **Key Insight for Robotics:** A 50% reduction in $L_e$ (e.g., by adding a mid-support) leads to a **4× increase** in $P_{cr}$ (since $P_{cr} \propto 1/L_e^2$).
- ✅ **Final Exam Strategy:** Always show the **classification step** explicitly — KTU examiners award marks specifically for verifying that $\lambda > \lambda_{lim}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
