> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT525 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS OF ROBOTIC PARTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Assumptions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Structural Analysis of Robotic Parts**, the term **"Assumptions"** refers to the set of mathematically rigorous, physically justifiable idealizations imposed on a robotic link, joint, or sub-assembly so that its governing equilibrium, kinematic, and constitutive equations become analytically tractable. These assumptions convert an otherwise continuous, three-dimensional, non-linear, and anisotropic physical system into a solvable boundary-value problem expressed through ordinary or partial differential equations.

The principal categories of assumptions adopted in this module are:

1. **Geometric (Kinematic) Assumptions** — relating to deformation, cross-sectional behaviour, and shape idealization.
2. **Material (Constitutive) Assumptions** — relating to homogeneity, isotropy, linearity, and elastic response.
3. **Static / Dynamic Load Assumptions** — relating to loading type, magnitude, and time-dependence.
4. **Boundary & Continuity Assumptions** — relating to supports, joints, and inter-component interaction.
5. **Idealization Assumptions** — relating to lumped-parameter representation, rigid-body behaviour, and link discretization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Every assumption must be stated *before* a derivation begins. Examiners explicitly award marks for clearly enumerating the assumptions used. A derivation without its assumptions is considered incomplete under the KTU 2024 scheme valuation key.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain-English Explanation

Imagine you are asked to describe the motion of a long, thin robotic arm swinging under gravity. If you tried to model every single atom, every micro-crack, every thermal fluctuation, and every joint imperfection, the problem would be **impossible** to solve — even with the world's fastest supercomputer.

To make the problem solvable, an engineer makes **carefully chosen assumptions**:

- "Assume the arm is made of the *same* material everywhere" (homogeneity).
- "Assume it bends gently, not violently" (small deformation).
- "Assume the cross-section, which was flat and vertical before bending, stays flat and vertical after bending" (Euler–Bernoulli plane-section assumption).

In essence, **assumptions are the engineering "common-sense filters"** that strip away unnecessary detail while keeping the physics accurate enough to be useful. They are not lies — they are *justified simplifications* backed by experimental evidence and error analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants of Interest (Standard SI Values):**
> - Young's Modulus of structural steel: **E = 200 GPa**
> - Young's Modulus of aluminium alloys (common in robot links): **E = 70 GPa**
> - Poisson's ratio for metals: **ν ≈ 0.30**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**

## 1.3 Why Assumptions Matter in Robotic Part Analysis

Robotic parts are typically slender links, hollow tubes, gear-train housings, and end-effector mounts. Their analysis sits at the intersection of **strength of materials**, **machine design**, and **robot kinematics**. Without assumptions:

- The number of degrees of freedom (DOF) explodes.
- Partial differential equations become non-linear and coupled.
- Closed-form solutions disappear.

With assumptions, we recover classical engineering tools: the **Euler–Bernoulli beam equation**, the **Timoshenko beam equation**, the **stiffness matrix method**, and the **finite element formulation** — all of which are central to the PERAT525 syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Systematic Breakdown of the Core Assumptions

### A. Geometric (Kinematic) Assumptions

| Assumption | Statement | Where It Is Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Small Deformation** | Displacements and rotations are infinitesimally small, so higher-order terms in strain–displacement relations are neglected. | Linear static analysis of all robot links. |
| **Plane Sections Remain Plane (PSRP)** | Cross-sections initially flat and normal to the neutral axis remain flat and normal after deformation. | Euler–Bernoulli beam theory. |
| **No Shear Deformation** | Transverse shear strain γ = 0 across the cross-section. | Euler–Bernoulli beam theory for slender links (L/h > 20). |
| **Constant Cross-Section** | Cross-sectional area A and moment of inertia I do not vary along the length (for simple analysis). | Preliminary design; first-cut stiffness estimation. |
| **Straight, Prismatic Neutral Axis** | The undeformed neutral axis is straight. | Classical beam-column theory. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Quick Rule of Thumb:** If the **length-to-depth ratio (L/h) > 20**, the Euler–Bernoulli assumption is valid. If **L/h < 10**, use the **Timoshenko beam theory** (shear deformation must be retained).

### B. Material (Constitutive) Assumptions

- **Homogeneity:** The elastic modulus E is the same at every point of the body.
- **Isotropy:** The material's elastic properties are the same in all directions at any point.
- **Linear Elasticity:** Stress is proportional to strain, σ = E·ε (Hooke's Law holds).
- **No Creep / No Viscoplasticity:** The material does not deform over time under constant load.
- **No Temperature Effects:** Thermal strains are neglected unless explicitly coupled.

### C. Static / Dynamic Assumptions

- **Static Equilibrium:** ΣF = 0 and ΣM = 0 are satisfied at every instant.
- **Quasi-Static Loading:** Loads vary slowly enough that inertial effects (ρ·∂²u/∂t²) can be neglected.
- **Conservative Forces:** Loads are derivable from a potential (no energy dissipation at this stage).
- **For Dynamic Extension:** Inertia terms are retained, leading to the equation
  $$\rho A \frac{\partial^{2} w(x,t)}{\partial t^{2}} + EI \frac{\partial^{4} w(x,t)}{\partial x^{4}} = q(x,t)$$

### D. Boundary & Continuity Assumptions

- **Perfect Fixity:** A clamped end has zero displacement and zero rotation.
- **Ideal Pin / Roller:** A pinned end has zero displacement but free rotation.
- **Continuity at Joints:** Displacement and slope are continuous across rigid joints.

### E. Idealization for Robotic Links

A robotic link is commonly idealized as a **Euler–Bernoulli beam** of length L, with a lumped mass m at its tip representing the payload, and a torsional spring K_t at the joint representing the actuator stiffness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The assumptions above are not arbitrary. They are validated experimentally — typical errors introduced by Euler–Bernoulli assumptions for L/h > 20 are **less than 1%**, which is well within the design tolerance for robotic arms.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** All mathematical operators and absolute-value bars in the table below use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the bare vertical pipe `|` to prevent breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbolic Form | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Normal strain (linearized) | $\varepsilon_x = \dfrac{du}{dx}$ | Strain under small deformation assumption. |
| 2 | Bending strain (Euler–Bernoulli) | $\varepsilon_x = -y \dfrac{d^{2} w}{d x^{2}}$ | PSRP assumption yields linear strain distribution. |
| 3 | Hooke's Law (1-D linear elastic) | $\sigma_x = E \cdot \varepsilon_x$ | Linear-elastic material assumption. |
| 4 | Bending moment – curvature | $M(x) = EI \dfrac{d^{2} w}{d x^{2}}$ | Constitutive + kinematic assumption combined. |
| 5 | Shear force – slope relation | $V(x) = \dfrac{dM}{dx} = EI \dfrac{d^{3} w}{d x^{3}}$ | From equilibrium assumption. |
| 6 | Governing static beam equation | $EI \dfrac{d^{4} w}{d x^{4}} = q(x)$ | Full set of assumptions combined. |
| 7 | Dynamic beam equation (Euler) | $\rho A \dfrac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial t^{2}} + EI \dfrac{\partial^{4} w}{\partial x^{4}} = q(x,t)$ | Inertia retained. |
| 8 | Timoshenko bending strain | $\varepsilon_x = -y \dfrac{d\phi}{dx}$ | $\phi$ ≠ dw/dx ; shear deformation retained. |
| 9 | Critical slenderness check | $\lambda = L / h$ | Use Euler–Bernoulli if $\lambda > 20$. |
| 10 | Natural frequency (cantilever) | $\omega_1 = (1.875)^{2} \sqrt{\dfrac{EI}{\rho A L^{4}}}$ | First mode of a cantilever robot link. |
| 11 | Tip deflection of cantilever | $\delta = \dfrac{P L^{3}}{3EI}$ | Direct consequence of all assumptions. |
| 12 | Stiffness matrix element | $k_{ij} = \int_{0}^{L} EI \cdot N_i''(x) \cdot N_j''(x) \, dx$ | FEM idealization of link. |

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas above assume **SI units**: force in N, length in m, moment in N·m, E in Pa, I in m⁴, ρ in kg/m³, A in m².

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These assumptions underpin:
- **Industrial robot arm design** (KUKA, ABB, FANUC arms).
- **End-effector stiffness modelling** for precision assembly robots.
- **Space-robot link analysis** where mass minimization demands thin-walled tubes.
- **Surgical robotic manipulators** (Da Vinci style), where sub-millimetre deflections matter.
- **Collaborative robot (cobot) safety analysis**, where impact loads require dynamic beam assumptions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: How Assumptions Lead to the Euler–Bernoulli Beam Equation

The derivation below shows, **step-by-step**, how five core assumptions combine to yield the classical 4th-order beam equation that is the workhorse of robotic-link analysis.

### Step 1 — State the Assumptions (Mandatory under KTU 2024)

1. Material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** (Hooke's Law: σ = E·ε).
2. **Small deformations** → linearized strain–displacement relations.
3. **Plane sections remain plane and normal to the neutral axis** (PSRP).
4. **Shear deformation is negligible** (γ_xz = 0).
5. The cross-section is **prismatic** and the neutral axis is **straight and undeformed in the axial direction**.

### Step 2 — Geometric (Kinematic) Strain

Under the small-deformation assumption, the normal strain at a fibre located a distance y above the neutral axis is obtained from the displacement field u(x) of that fibre. If w(x) is the transverse deflection, then by the **PSRP assumption**, u(x) at height y is:

$$
u(x, y) = -y \cdot \theta(x)
$$

where θ(x) is the rotation of the cross-section. For small rotations, θ ≈ dw/dx, giving:

$$
\varepsilon_x = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = -y \frac{d^{2} w}{d x^{2}}
$$

This is the **kinematic consequence of assumption 3 (PSRP) combined with assumption 2 (small deformation)**.

### Step 3 — Constitutive Relation (Hooke's Law)

Using assumption 1 (linear elastic material), the bending stress is:

$$
\sigma_x = E \cdot \varepsilon_x = -E y \frac{d^{2} w}{d x^{2}}
$$

### Step 4 — Moment–Curvature Relation

The internal bending moment M(x) is the first moment of the bending stress distribution about the neutral axis. By definition:

$$
M(x) = -\int_A \sigma_x \cdot y \, dA
$$

Substituting σ_x from Step 3:

$$
M(x) = -\int_A \left( -E y \frac{d^{2} w}{d x^{2}} \right) y \, dA = E \frac{d^{2} w}{d x^{2}} \int_A y^{2} \, dA
$$

The integral $\int_A y^2 \, dA$ is, by definition, the **second moment of area** I about the neutral axis. Therefore:

$$
M(x) = EI \frac{d^{2} w}{d x^{2}}
$$

This compact equation is the **constitutive–kinematic bridge** between the bending moment and the local curvature of the deformed neutral axis.

### Step 5 — Equilibrium of an Elemental Slice

From the static-equilibrium assumption (assumption set C), consider an infinitesimal beam element of length dx. The shear force V(x) and bending moment M(x) at the two faces differ by infinitesimals dV and dM. Equilibrium of forces in the transverse direction gives:

$$
\frac{dV}{dx} = -q(x)
$$

Equilibrium of moments about the left face of the element gives:

$$
V = \frac{dM}{dx}
$$

Substituting the moment–curvature relation from Step 4 into the moment-equilibrium relation:

$$
V(x) = \frac{d}{dx}\left( EI \frac{d^{2} w}{d x^{2}} \right)
$$

Substituting this into the force-equilibrium relation, and assuming constant EI:

$$
\frac{d^{2}}{dx^{2}}\left( EI \frac{d^{2} w}{d x^{2}} \right) = -q(x)
$$

### Step 6 — Final Governing Equation

Dropping the negative sign by defining q(x) as the load in the positive w direction (a sign convention commonly used in KTU textbooks):

$$
\boxed{EI \frac{d^{4} w}{d x^{4}} = q(x)}
$$

This is the **Euler–Bernoulli beam equation** — a direct, traceable consequence of the five stated assumptions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Recap (Why each step is needed):**
> - Step 1 → marks awarded for stating assumptions explicitly.
> - Step 2 → uses kinematic assumptions.
> - Step 3 → uses material assumption.
> - Step 4 → combines kinematic + material assumptions.
> - Step 5 → uses static-equilibrium assumption.
> - Step 6 → assembles the full equation.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Cantilever Robotic Link Under Tip Load

**Problem:** A robot link of length **L = 0.6 m**, rectangular cross-section **b = 30 mm, h = 10 mm**, made of aluminium (**E = 70 GPa**), carries a tip payload **P = 50 N**. Compute the tip deflection using the Euler–Bernoulli assumptions.

### Step 1 — Compute the Second Moment of Area

For a rectangular cross-section about its neutral axis (bending about the strong axis):

$$
I = \frac{b h^{3}}{12} = \frac{(0.030)(0.010)^{3}}{12} = 2.5 \times 10^{-9} \; \text{m}^{4}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Flexural Rigidity EI

$$
EI = (70 \times 10^{9}) \times (2.5 \times 10^{-9}) = 175 \; \text{N·m}^{2}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Cantilever Tip-Deflection Formula

The tip deflection of a cantilever under a tip load P (a direct consequence of the Euler–Bernoulli assumptions derived above) is:

$$
\delta = \frac{P L^{3}}{3 EI}
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
\delta = \frac{(50)(0.6)^{3}}{3 \times 175}
$$

$$
\delta = \frac{50 \times 0.216}{525}
$$

$$
\delta = \frac{10.8}{525} = 0.02057 \; \text{m} \approx 20.6 \; \text{mm}
$$

### Step 4 — Validate the Assumptions

- **L/h = 0.6 / 0.01 = 60** → 60 > 20, so the **Euler–Bernoulli assumption is valid** (shear deformation negligible).
- **Maximum stress check:** $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M c}{I} = \dfrac{(50)(0.6)(0.005)}{2.5 \times 10^{-9}} = 60 \; \text{MPa}$, well below aluminium's yield strength (~240 MPa), so **linear-elastic assumption holds**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Takeaway:** Always end a derivation with a **validity check** of the assumptions you used. Examiners reward this habit with bonus conceptual marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Hierarchy of Assumptions in Robotic Part Analysis

The diagram below maps how the major assumption families interact, depend on each other, and feed into the final governing equation of a robotic link.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Physical Robotic Link] --> B[Geometric Assumptions]
    A --> C[Material Assumptions]
    A --> D[Static Assumptions]
    A --> E[Boundary Assumptions]

    B --> B1[Small Deformation]
    B --> B2[Plane Sections Remain Plane PSRP]
    B --> B3[No Shear Deformation]
    B --> B4[Prismatic Cross Section]

    C --> C1[Homogeneous]
    C --> C2[Isotropic]
    C --> C3[Linear Elastic Hooke Law]
    C --> C4[No Creep No Viscoplasticity]

    D --> D1[Static Equilibrium Sigma F 0 Sigma M 0]
    D --> D2[Quasi Static Load]
    D --> D3[Conservative Forces]

    E --> E1[Clamped End u 0 theta 0]
    E --> E2[Pinned End u 0 M 0]
    E --> E3[Continuity at Joints]

    B1 --> F1[Linearized Strain epsilon equals du dx]
    B2 --> F2[Bending Strain epsilon equals minus y d2w dx2]
    B3 --> F3[Gamma xz equals 0]
    C3 --> F4[Stress sigma equals E epsilon]
    D1 --> F5[dV dx equals minus q and V equals dM dx]

    F1 --> G[Kinematic Relations]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> H[Constitutive Relations]
    F5 --> I[Equilibrium Relations]

    G --> J[Moment Curvature M equals EI d2w dx2]
    H --> J
    I --> K[Governing Beam Equation]

    J --> K
    E1 --> L[Boundary Conditions]
    E2 --> L
    E3 --> L

    K --> M[Final Solution w of x via Integration or FEM]
    L --> M

    M --> N[Engineering Output Deflection Stress Natural Frequency]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Validity Decision Tree for Choosing the Right Beam Theory

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Robotic Link Geometry Known] --> Q1{Length to Depth Ratio L over h greater than 20}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Material Behaviour Linear Elastic}
    Q1 -- No --> T1[Use Timoshenko Beam Theory with Shear Deformation]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Loads Static or Quasi Static}
    Q2 -- No --> T2[Use Non Linear or Plasticity Theory]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Cross Section Constant}
    Q3 -- No --> T3[Add Inertia Term dynamic beam equation]
    Q4 -- Yes --> R[Euler Bernoulli Solution Valid]
    Q4 -- No --> T4[Use Stepped Beam or FEM Discretization]
    R --> END[Proceed with EI d4w dx4 equals q of x]
    T1 --> END2[Proceed with Timoshenko equations]
    T2 --> END3[Proceed with non linear analysis]
    T3 --> END4[Proceed with dynamic beam equation]
    T4 --> END5[Proceed with FEM stiffness assembly]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - The first block diagram shows the **logical assembly** of all assumptions into the final governing equation.
> - The second block diagram is a **decision aid** that helps a robotic-parts analyst select the correct beam theory for a given link geometry.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State the five fundamental assumptions used in the Euler–Bernoulli beam theory as applied to a slender robotic link. Justify each in one sentence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** — required for Hooke's law σ = E·ε to hold at every point.
2. **Deformations are infinitesimally small** — allows linearization of strain–displacement relations.
3. **Plane sections remain plane and normal to the neutral axis (PSRP)** — gives the linear bending-strain distribution ε_x = −y·d²w/dx².
4. **Shear deformation is negligible** — the cross-section rotates as a rigid body, so γ_xz = 0.
5. **The neutral axis is straight and undeformed axially, and the cross-section is prismatic** — allows EI to be treated as a constant.

*(1 mark for the list, 1 mark for the justification of any one assumption, 1 mark for the formula/justification of the remaining four.)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**A robotic link has length-to-depth ratio L/h = 8. Which beam theory should be used — Euler–Bernoulli or Timoshenko? Justify your choice.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Timoshenko beam theory** should be used.
**Justification:** When L/h < 10, the shear deformation contributes more than ~5% of the total deflection, and the Euler–Bernoulli assumption (no shear deformation) is no longer valid. Timoshenko's theory retains the shear strain γ_xz and treats the cross-section rotation φ(x) as independent of the slope dw/dx, capturing both bending and shear effects. (Full marks for stating the rule L/h > 20, naming the theory, and giving the quantitative reason.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4 Choice A]**

A robot arm link is modelled as a cantilever beam of length **L = 0.8 m**, with a hollow circular cross-section of outer diameter **D_o = 40 mm** and inner diameter **D_i = 36 mm**. The material is structural steel with **E = 200 GPa**, **ρ = 7850 kg/m³**, and **ν = 0.30**. A tip payload of **P = 80 N** acts at the free end.

**(a)** [7 marks] — State all assumptions used in the Euler–Bernoulli analysis and derive the governing differential equation for the deflection of the link.

**(b)** [7 marks] — Compute the maximum tip deflection, the maximum bending stress, and the first natural frequency of transverse vibration. Verify whether the Euler–Bernoulli assumptions remain valid.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Assumptions and Derivation [7 marks]**

*Assumptions (2 marks):*
1. Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic.
2. Deformations are small.
3. Plane sections remain plane and normal to the neutral axis (PSRP).
4. Shear deformation is negligible.
5. The link is prismatic and the neutral axis is straight.

*Derivation (5 marks):*

Starting from the displacement assumption under PSRP:

$$
u(x, y) = -y \frac{d w}{d x}
$$

The bending strain:

$$
\varepsilon_x = -y \frac{d^{2} w}{d x^{2}}
$$

Hooke's Law:

$$
\sigma_x = E \varepsilon_x = -E y \frac{d^{2} w}{d x^{2}}
$$

Moment–curvature:

$$
M = -\int_A \sigma_x y \, dA = E I \frac{d^{2} w}{d x^{2}}
$$

Equilibrium of an element:

$$
V = \frac{dM}{dx}, \quad \frac{dV}{dx} = -q(x)
$$

Combining and dropping the sign (positive q convention):

$$
EI \frac{d^{4} w}{d x^{4}} = q(x)
$$

**(b) Numerical Computation [7 marks]**

*Step 1 — Second moment of area for hollow circular section:*

$$
I = \frac{\pi}{64} \left( D_o^{4} - D_i^{4} \right) = \frac{\pi}{64} \left( (0.040)^{4} - (0.036)^{4} \right)
$$

$$
I = \frac{\pi}{64} \left( 2.56 \times 10^{-6} - 1.6796 \times 10^{-6} \right) = \frac{\pi}{64} (0.8804 \times 10^{-6})
$$

$$
I = 4.323 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{m}^{4}
$$

*Step 2 — Flexural rigidity:*

$$
EI = (200 \times 10^{9})(4.323 \times 10^{-8}) = 8646 \; \text{N·m}^{2}
$$

*Step 3 — Tip deflection (cantilever, tip load P):*

$$
\delta = \frac{P L^{3}}{3 EI} = \frac{(80)(0.8)^{3}}{3 \times 8646} = \frac{40.96}{25\,938} = 1.579 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\delta \approx 1.58 \; \text{mm}}
$$

*Step 4 — Maximum bending stress:*

Maximum moment at the fixed end: M_max = P·L = 80 × 0.8 = 64 N·m.
Maximum fibre distance: c = D_o/2 = 0.020 m.

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max} \cdot c}{I} = \frac{(64)(0.020)}{4.323 \times 10^{-8}} = 2.961 \times 10^{7} \; \text{Pa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{max} \approx 29.6 \; \text{MPa}}
$$

*Step 5 — First natural frequency (cantilever):*

Cross-sectional area:

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} (D_o^{2} - D_i^{2}) = \frac{\pi}{4}((0.040)^{2} - (0.036)^{2}) = 2.199 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^{2}
$$

Using β₁ L = 1.875 (cantilever first mode):

$$
\omega_1 = (1.875)^{2} \sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A L^{4}}} = (3.5156) \sqrt{\frac{8646}{(7850)(2.199 \times 10^{-4})(0.8)^{4}}}
$$

$$
\omega_1 = 3.5156 \times \sqrt{\frac{8646}{6.605 \times 10^{3}}} = 3.5156 \times 1.144
$$

$$
\boxed{\omega_1 \approx 4.02 \; \text{rad/s} \quad (f_1 \approx 0.64 \; \text{Hz})}
$$

*Step 6 — Assumption validity check:*

- L/h = 0.8 / 0.04 = 20. On the boundary of validity; the result is borderline acceptable for Euler–Bernoulli.
- σ_max = 29.6 MPa << σ_yield ≈ 250 MPa, so linear-elastic assumption holds.
- δ/L = 1.58/800 = 0.002 < 0.01, so small-deformation assumption is valid.

**Conclusion:** All assumptions are valid; results are engineering-accurate.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 Choice B]**

**(a)** [7 marks] — Compare and contrast the **Euler–Bernoulli** and **Timoshenko** beam theories with reference to their assumptions, governing equations, and the range of robotic links to which each is applicable. Include the key parameters: shear coefficient k and shear modulus G in your discussion.

**(b)** [7 marks] — A short, stubby robot wrist link has L = 0.20 m, square cross-section 25 mm × 25 mm, E = 200 GPa, G = 77 GPa, k = 5/6, ν = 0.30. Compute the **percentage error** introduced by the Euler–Bernoulli theory in predicting the tip deflection under a tip load P = 30 N, relative to the Timoshenko theory.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison [7 marks]**

| Aspect | Euler–Bernoulli Theory | Timoshenko Beam Theory |
|---|---|---|
| **Shear deformation** | Neglected (γ_xz = 0) | Retained (γ_xz ≠ 0) |
| **Cross-section rotation** | φ(x) = dw/dx | φ(x) is independent of dw/dx |
| **Governing equation** | $EI \dfrac{d^{4}w}{dx^{4}} = q(x)$ | Two coupled equations involving EI and kGA |
| **Order of the system** | 4th-order single ODE | Two 2nd-order coupled ODEs |
| **Boundary conditions** | w, dw/dx, M, V (2 at each end) | w, φ, M, V (2 at each end) |
| **Validity range (L/h)** | L/h > 20 (slender) | L/h ≥ 1 (short, stubby) |
| **Material parameters used** | E, I | E, I, G, A, k (Timoshenko shear coefficient) |
| **Typical robotic application** | Long reach arms, forearm links | Wrist joints, gripper mounts, gear housings |
| **Accuracy for L/h > 20** | < 1% error | < 1% error (overkill) |
| **Accuracy for L/h = 5** | ~20% error | < 1% error |

*The Timoshenko shear coefficient k accounts for the non-uniform distribution of shear stress across the cross-section; for a rectangular section, k = 5/6, and for a solid circular section, k = 9/10.*

**(b) Percentage Error Computation [7 marks]**

*Step 1 — Geometric properties:*

$$
I = \frac{b h^{3}}{12} = \frac{(0.025)(0.025)^{3}}{12} = 3.255 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{m}^{4}
$$

$$
A = b h = (0.025)(0.025) = 6.25 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^{2}
$$

*Step 2 — Slenderness check:*

$$
\frac{L}{h} = \frac{0.20}{0.025} = 8
$$

Since L/h = 8 < 10, **Timoshenko theory is preferred**; Euler–Bernoulli is expected to under-predict the deflection.

*Step 3 — Euler–Bernoulli tip deflection:*

$$
\delta_{EB} = \frac{P L^{3}}{3 EI} = \frac{(30)(0.20)^{3}}{3 \times (200 \times 10^{9})(3.255 \times 10^{-8})}
$$

$$
\delta_{EB} = \frac{30 \times 0.008}{19\,530} = \frac{0.24}{19\,530} = 1.229 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m} = 12.29 \; \mu\text{m}
$$

*Step 4 — Timoshenko tip deflection:*

For a cantilever with tip load P, the Timoshenko tip deflection is:

$$
\delta_T = \frac{P L^{3}}{3 EI} + \frac{P L}{k G A}
$$

The first term is identical to δ_EB; the second term is the **additional shear-induced deflection**.

$$
\delta_T = \delta_{EB} + \frac{P L}{k G A}
$$

$$
\delta_T = 1.229 \times 10^{-5} + \frac{(30)(0.20)}{(5/6)(77 \times 10^{9})(6.25 \times 10^{-4})}
$$

$$
\delta_T = 1.229 \times 10^{-5} + \frac{6}{4.005 \times 10^{7}}
$$

$$
\delta_T = 1.229 \times 10^{-5} + 1.498 \times 10^{-7} = 1.244 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m} = 12.44 \; \mu\text{m}
$$

*Step 5 — Percentage error of Euler–Bernoulli:*

$$
\text{Error \%} = \frac{\delta_T - \delta_{EB}}{\delta_T} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Error \%} = \frac{1.244 \times 10^{-5} - 1.229 \times 10^{-5}}{1.244 \times 10^{-5}} \times 100 = \frac{1.50 \times 10^{-7}}{1.244 \times 10^{-5}} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Error \%} \approx 1.21\%}
$$

*Step 6 — Interpretation:*

Although L/h = 8 is below the classical "safe" threshold of 20, the **stiff aluminium-like steel and the dominant bending stiffness** keep the error small (~1.2%). For softer materials or larger cross-sections, the error would be substantially higher.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state assumptions** before a derivation: −2 marks minimum, even if the math is correct.
> 2. **Sign errors** in the strain–displacement relation (mixing u = -y·dw/dx and u = +y·dw/dx): −1 mark per occurrence.
> 3. **Forgetting the shear-correction factor k** in Timoshenko problems: −2 marks.
> 4. **Computing I incorrectly** for hollow sections (using D_o³ instead of D_o⁴): −1 mark.
> 5. **Skipping the validity check** at the end of a numerical problem: −1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Assumptions in Structural Analysis of Robotic Parts**

- **Five Core Assumptions of Euler–Bernoulli Theory:**
  (i) Homogeneous, isotropic, linear-elastic material;
  (ii) Small deformations;
  (iii) Plane sections remain plane and normal to neutral axis (PSRP);
  (iv) Shear deformation negligible;
  (v) Prismatic, straight neutral axis.

- **Selection Rule for Beam Theory:**
  - **L/h > 20** → Euler–Bernoulli (error < 1%).
  - **L/h < 10** → Timoshenko (shear deformation must be included).

- **Governing Equation to Memorize (Euler–Bernoulli, static):**
  $EI \dfrac{d^{4} w}{d x^{4}} = q(x)$

- **Governing Equation to Memorize (Euler–Bernoulli, dynamic):**
  $\rho A \dfrac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial t^{2}} + EI \dfrac{\partial^{4} w}{\partial x^{4}} = q(x,t)$

- **Moment–Curvature Bridge (most-tested identity):**
  $M(x) = EI \dfrac{d^{2} w}{d x^{2}}$

- **Cantilever Tip-Deflection Formula (consequence of all assumptions):**
  $\delta = \dfrac{P L^{3}}{3 EI}$

- **Timoshenko Shear Deflection (additional term):**
  $\delta_{\text{shear}} = \dfrac{P L}{k G A}$

- **Standard Material Properties (memorize):**
  Steel: E = 200 GPa, G = 77 GPa, ν = 0.30, ρ = 7850 kg/m³.
  Aluminium: E = 70 GPa, G = 26 GPa, ν = 0.33, ρ = 2700 kg/m³.

- **KTU 2024 Habit to Adopt:**
  Always begin a derivation by *stating* the assumptions; always end a numerical problem with a *validity check* (slenderness ratio, stress vs. yield, deflection vs. length).

- **Common Mistake to Avoid:**
  Using Euler–Bernoulli for short robotic wrist or gripper links (L/h < 10) — examiners explicitly set traps of this kind in Part B questions.

- **FEM Connection (preview of Module 5):**
  The assumptions above directly determine the **Hermite cubic shape functions** N_i(x) used in the stiffness matrix of a 2-noded beam element:
  $k_{ij} = \int_0^L EI \cdot N_i''(x) \cdot N_j''(x) \, dx$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effective Length
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT525 - STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS OF ROBOTIC PARTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Effective Length

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Structural Analysis of Robotic Parts**, the **Effective Length** (often denoted as $L_e$ or $KL$) of a structural member (such as a slender robotic link, manipulator arm, column, or strut) is defined as the **equivalent length of an idealized pin-ended column that has the same buckling load as the actual member with its real end conditions**.

Mathematically, the **effective length** of a compression member is given by:

$$L_e = K \cdot L$$

where:
- $L$ is the **actual unsupported (lateral) length** of the member
- $K$ is the **Effective Length Factor** (also called the *buckling coefficient* or *rotational restraint coefficient*)

The **Euler critical buckling load** is then expressed in terms of effective length as:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{(K L)^2}$$

where:
- $E$ = Young's Modulus of Elasticity (in $\text{N/m}^2$ or **GPa**)
- $I$ = Minimum Moment of Inertia of the cross-section about the axis of buckling (in $\text{m}^4$)
- $L_e$ = Effective Length (in **m**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Effective Length is the *single most important* parameter governing the **stability** of compression members in robotic manipulators, gantry structures, end-effector support struts, and chassis columns. A reduction in effective length leads to a *quadratic increase* in critical buckling load.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation

Imagine a long, thin stick (like a ruler) standing vertically on a table. If you push down on its top with your finger:
- A **tall, thin, free stick** buckles very easily — it has high *effective length*.
- A **shorter stick**, or a stick whose ends are clamped in a vice, hardly buckles at all — it has *low effective length*.

The "effective length" essentially **measures how much of the actual length "counts" when it comes to buckling**. Real-world end conditions (clamped, pinned, sliding, fixed) are mathematically converted into an *equivalent pin-ended column length*, which is the **effective length**.

### Real-World Robotic Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Robotic Manipulator Arm:**
> Consider a serial-link robotic arm with a long slender forearm holding a payload. The forearm is a compression-flexion member. If the wrist joint is **rigidly locked** (analogous to a fixed support), the effective length is smaller, and the arm can carry heavier loads without buckling sideways. If the wrist is allowed to **rotate freely** (analogous to a pinned support), the effective length equals the full physical length, and buckling occurs at much lower payloads.

In simpler terms:

| **Support Type** | **Analogy** | **Buckling Tendency** |
|---|---|---|
| Both Ends Pinned | Loose rope held at both ends | High (K = 1.0) |
| Both Ends Fixed | Rod clamped in two vises | Very Low (K = 0.5) |
| Cantilever (Free-Free) | Wobbly stick standing alone | Maximum (K = 2.0) |

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and parameters are **mandatory** in any KTU 2024 numerical problem:

- **Young's Modulus $E$**: Material property (e.g., **Steel ≈ 200 GPa**, **Aluminium 6061 ≈ 70 GPa**, **Carbon Fibre Composite ≈ 150 GPa**)
- **Effective Length Factor $K$**: Dimensionless, between **0.5 and 2.0** for ideal supports; can exceed **2.0** for elastic (semi-rigid) supports
- **Slenderness Ratio $\lambda = \dfrac{KL}{r}$**: Dimensionless, where $r = \sqrt{I/A}$ is the **radius of gyration**
- **Limiting Slenderness Ratio $\lambda_1$**: Used to classify columns as *long*, *short*, or *intermediate*

---

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Module Mapping

| **KTU Course Outcome (CO)** | **Topic Coverage** |
|---|---|
| CO4 – *Analyze stability of compression members in robotic structures* | Effective Length Factor derivation & application |
| CO4 – *Apply Euler's buckling theory to robotic link design* | Critical load calculation using $L_e$ |
| CO4 – *Evaluate effective slenderness for various boundary constraints* | Selection of $K$ based on end conditions |

> [!TIP]
> For **Robotic Parts Structural Analysis**, end conditions of robotic links rarely correspond to *ideal* cases. Always draw the **free-body diagram** of the deformed buckled shape to determine $K$ correctly.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Framework — The "Why" Behind Effective Length

Euler's classical theory of buckling (1757) was derived for a **perfect pin-ended column** of length $L$. However, real engineering members — including robotic links — have varied end conditions:
- **Fixed** (no translation, no rotation)
- **Pinned** (no translation, rotation free)
- **Free** (translation and rotation both free)
- **Sliding/Guided** (translation restrained, rotation free in one direction)

Each support type changes the **shape of the buckled deflection curve** and the **effective distance between "nodes"** (zero-deflection points). The factor $K$ converts the *real problem* into the *ideal pin-ended problem* by scaling the length.

---

## 2.2 Effective Length Factors — Standard Boundary Conditions

The factor $K$ is derived from the **second-order differential equation** of the deflected column:

$$E I \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + P \, y = 0$$

with appropriate boundary conditions. The general solution yields $K$ values summarized below.

### 2.2.1 Boundary Condition Table (KTU High-Yield Reference)

| **Sl. No.** | **End Condition (Top – Bottom)** | **Buckled Shape (Theoretical)** | **$K$ Value** | **Effective Length $L_e$** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | Fixed – Fixed | One half sine wave | **0.5** | $0.5L$ |
| 2 | Pinned – Pinned (Classic Euler) | Full sine wave | **1.0** | $L$ |
| 3 | Fixed – Free (Cantilever) | Quarter sine wave | **2.0** | $2L$ |
| 4 | Fixed – Pinned | ~0.7 of full sine | **0.7** | $0.7L$ |
| 5 | Fixed – Guided (Sliding) | Full sine wave | **1.0** | $L$ |
| 6 | Pinned – Guided | Quarter sine wave | **2.0** | $2L$ |
| 7 | Free – Free (Theoretical) | N/A (Unstable) | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU 2024 Exams:** Memorize the **five idealized cases** (Cases 1–5). The value **K = 0.7** for *Fixed-Pinned* is the **exact transcendental value** $\approx 0.6992$, commonly rounded to **0.7**.

---

## 2.3 Effective Slenderness Ratio and Classification of Columns

The **slenderness ratio** governs whether Euler's theory applies:

$$\lambda = \frac{L_e}{r} = \frac{K L}{r}$$

where $r$ is the **radius of gyration**:

$$r = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}}$$

### 2.3.1 Column Classification (KTU Standard)

| **Class** | **Slenderness $\lambda$** | **Governing Theory** | **Failure Mode** |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---|
| Long Column | $\lambda \geq \lambda_1$ | Euler's Elastic Buckling | Instability |
| Intermediate | $\lambda_s \leq \lambda < \lambda_1$ | Rankine's / Johnson's Formula | Combined |
| Short Column | $\lambda < \lambda_s$ | Crushing / Yielding | Material Failure |

Where:

$$\lambda_1 = \pi \sqrt{\frac{E}{\sigma_y}} \quad \text{(Limiting slenderness ratio)}$$

$$\lambda_s \approx 40 \text{ to } 60 \quad \text{(For steel; standard KTU value = 60)}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the ONLY reference table you need during the KTU 2024 ESE exam for this topic.**

| **#** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $L_e = K L$ | Effective length | m |
| 2 | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2}$ | Euler critical buckling load | N |
| 3 | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2}$ | Euler critical stress | Pa |
| 4 | $\lambda = \dfrac{K L}{r} = \dfrac{L_e}{r}$ | Slenderness ratio | dimensionless |
| 5 | $r = \sqrt{I/A}$ | Radius of gyration | m |
| 6 | $\lambda_1 = \pi \sqrt{E/\sigma_y}$ | Limiting slenderness | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\sigma_{cr}^{\text{Rankine}} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{1 + a \lambda^2}$ | Rankine critical stress | Pa |
| 8 | $P_{cr}^{\text{Johnson}} = \sigma_y A \left[1 - \dfrac{\lambda^2}{4 \lambda_1^2}\right]$ | Johnson's parabolic formula | N |
| 9 | $\rho = \dfrac{1}{\lambda^2}$ | Perry-Robertson parameter | dimensionless |
| 10 | $P_{cr} = A \cdot \sigma_{cr}$ | Critical load = stress × area | N |

**Where:**
- $a$ = Rankine's constant (≈ $1/7500$ for mild steel, **≈ $1/1600$ for cast iron**)
- $\sigma_y$ = Yield stress
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area
- $I$ = Second moment of area

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In robotic structural components, effective length is critical for:

1. **Manipulator arm links** — slender aluminium/carbon-fibre links under payload compression must not buckle.
2. **End-effector support struts** — pneumatic/hydraulic cylinder mounts.
3. **Gantry robot columns** — vertical support members.
4. **Walking robot legs** — subjected to combined bending-compression.
5. **Space robotic booms** — extremely slender, near-ideal Euler conditions.

> [!TIP]
> **Design Insight:** Reducing $K$ (by improving end restraints) is **far more effective** than increasing cross-section area, since $P_{cr} \propto 1/(KL)^2$ but $P_{cr} \propto A$ linearly.

---

## 2.6 Conceptual Logic Flow — How to Solve a KTU Problem

```
Step 1 → Identify the member's end conditions from the figure
Step 2 → Look up the correct K value from the Boundary Table
Step 3 → Compute L_e = K × L
Step 4 → Compute radius of gyration r = √(I/A)
Step 5 → Compute slenderness λ = L_e / r
Step 6 → Compare λ with λ_1 and λ_s to classify the column
Step 7 → Apply the appropriate formula (Euler / Rankine / Johnson)
Step 8 → Compute critical load P_cr and report with units
```
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Euler Buckling Load in Terms of Effective Length

### Assumptions

1. The column is **perfectly straight** and the load is **perfectly axial**.
2. The material is **homogeneous, isotropic**, and obeys **Hooke's Law**.
3. The column fails only by **buckling** (not by yielding).
4. **Self-weight is negligible**.
5. The cross-section is **uniform** along the length.
6. The end-shortening due to direct compression is **negligible** compared to lateral deflection.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Consider a pin-ended column of length $L$ subjected to an axial compressive load $P$. Let the deflected shape be $y(x)$ measured from the original axis.

**Step 1 — Bending Moment at a section**

Taking a section at distance $x$ from the bottom support, the bending moment due to lateral deflection $y$ is:

$$M(x) = -P \cdot y(x)$$

**Step 2 — Euler-Bernoulli Differential Equation**

From the bending equation:

$$E I \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x) = -P \, y(x)$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + \frac{P}{EI} y = 0$$

**Step 3 — General Solution**

Let $k^2 = \dfrac{P}{EI}$. The differential equation becomes:

$$\frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + k^2 y = 0$$

The general solution is:

$$y(x) = A \cos(kx) + B \sin(kx)$$

**Step 4 — Apply Boundary Conditions (Pin-Pin)**

For a pin-ended column:
- At $x = 0$: $y = 0 \implies A \cos(0) + B \sin(0) = 0 \implies A = 0$
- At $x = L$: $y = 0 \implies B \sin(kL) = 0$

For a **non-trivial** (buckled) solution, $B \neq 0$, so:

$$\sin(kL) = 0 \implies kL = n\pi, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

**Step 5 — Critical Load (Lowest Mode, n = 1)**

$$k = \frac{\pi}{L} \implies \frac{P}{EI} = \frac{\pi^2}{L^2}$$

$$\boxed{P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L^2} \quad \text{(Pin-Pin Euler Load)}}$$

**Step 6 — Generalization to Effective Length**

For any end condition, replace $L$ by the **effective length** $L_e = K L$:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{(K L)^2} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2}$$

This is the **fundamental Euler buckling equation** in its most general form.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of K for Fixed-Pinned Column (KTU Important Case)

Consider a column fixed at the bottom ($x=0$) and pinned at the top ($x=L$).

**Step 1 — Boundary Conditions**

- At $x = 0$ (Fixed): $y(0) = 0$ and $\dfrac{dy}{dx}\bigg|_{x=0} = 0$ (no slope)
- At $x = L$ (Pinned): $y(L) = 0$

**Step 2 — Apply BC1 at $x = 0$**

From $y(0) = A = 0$:

$$y(x) = B \sin(kx)$$

From $\dfrac{dy}{dx}\bigg|_{x=0} = B k \cos(k \cdot 0) = B k = 0 \implies B = 0$

This gives the trivial solution — so we must consider **lateral deflection from a non-zero reference**. Instead, take the origin at the pin.

**Step 3 — Revised Setup (origin at pin)**

$$y(x) = A \sin(kx) + B \cos(kx)$$

- At $x = 0$: $y(0) = B = 0 \implies y(x) = A \sin(kx)$
- At $x = L$: $y(L) = A \sin(kL) = 0 \implies kL = n\pi$

This gives only trivial solutions. Hence, for fixed-pinned, the column **buckles in a deformed shape with a non-zero tangent at the pin**, requiring a more refined treatment.

**Step 4 — Transcendental Equation**

The exact $K$ for fixed-pinned is the solution of:

$$\tan(kL) = kL$$

Let $kL = \alpha$. The smallest non-trivial root satisfies:

$$\tan(\alpha) = \alpha$$

Solving numerically:

$$\alpha \approx 4.4934$$

Thus:

$$k = \frac{4.4934}{L} = \frac{\pi}{K L}$$

Solving for $K$:

$$K = \frac{\pi}{4.4934} \approx 0.6992 \approx \mathbf{0.7}$$

**Step 5 — Effective Length**

$$L_e = 0.7 \, L$$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{(0.7 L)^2} \approx \frac{2.046 \, \pi^2 E I}{L^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The exact transcendental solution confirms the **KTU standard value $K \approx 0.7$** for the Fixed-Pinned case. This case is heavily tested in KTU ESE.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Critical Stress and Slenderness Ratio

From $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2}$ and $P_{cr} = \sigma_{cr} \cdot A$:

$$\sigma_{cr} \cdot A = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2}$$

Substituting $I = A r^2$:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E r^2}{L_e^2} = \frac{\pi^2 E}{(L_e/r)^2}$$

Letting $\lambda = L_e/r$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2}}$$

This is the **Euler critical stress formula**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — KTU 2024 Style Numerical

**Problem Statement:**

> A robotic manipulator arm link is made of **aluminium alloy 6061-T6** with the following properties:
> - Length $L = 1.2$ m
> - Hollow circular cross-section: outer diameter $D = 50$ mm, inner diameter $d = 40$ mm
> - End conditions: **Fixed-Pinned**
> - Young's modulus $E = 70$ GPa
> - Yield stress $\sigma_y = 240$ MPa
>
> Determine:
> (i) Effective length $L_e$
> (ii) Critical buckling load $P_{cr}$
> (iii) Slenderness ratio and classify the column

### Solution

**Step (i) — Effective Length**

For Fixed-Pinned condition, from KTU table:

$$K = 0.7$$

$$L_e = K \cdot L = 0.7 \times 1.2 = 0.84 \text{ m}$$

**Step (ii) — Moment of Inertia**

For a hollow circular cross-section:

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}(D^4 - d^4)$$

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}((50 \times 10^{-3})^4 - (40 \times 10^{-3})^4)$$

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}(6.25 \times 10^{-6} - 2.56 \times 10^{-6})$$

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}(3.69 \times 10^{-6})$$

$$I = 1.811 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4$$

**Step (iii) — Cross-Sectional Area**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}((0.050)^2 - (0.040)^2)$$

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(2.5 \times 10^{-3} - 1.6 \times 10^{-3}) = \frac{\pi}{4}(9 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$A = 7.069 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step (iv) — Radius of Gyration**

$$r = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.811 \times 10^{-7}}{7.069 \times 10^{-4}}} = \sqrt{2.563 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$r = 0.01601 \text{ m} = 16.01 \text{ mm}$$

**Step (v) — Critical Buckling Load (Euler)**

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2} = \frac{\pi^2 \times 70 \times 10^9 \times 1.811 \times 10^{-7}}{(0.84)^2}$$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{9.8696 \times 70 \times 10^9 \times 1.811 \times 10^{-7}}{0.7056}$$

Numerator: $9.8696 \times 70 \times 10^9 \times 1.811 \times 10^{-7} = 9.8696 \times 70 \times 1.811 \times 10^{2}$

$$= 9.8696 \times 126.77 \times 10^2 = 12511.3 \times 10^2 = 1.2511 \times 10^6$$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{1.2511 \times 10^6}{0.7056} = 1.773 \times 10^6 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{P_{cr} \approx 1.773 \text{ MN} = 1773 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step (vi) — Slenderness Ratio**

$$\lambda = \frac{L_e}{r} = \frac{0.84}{0.01601} = 52.47$$

**Step (vii) — Limiting Slenderness**

$$\lambda_1 = \pi \sqrt{\frac{E}{\sigma_y}} = \pi \sqrt{\frac{70 \times 10^9}{240 \times 10^6}}$$

$$\lambda_1 = \pi \sqrt{291.67} = \pi \times 17.078 = 53.65$$

**Step (viii) — Classification**

Since $\lambda = 52.47 < \lambda_1 = 53.65$:

$$\lambda < \lambda_1 \implies \text{**Intermediate Column**}$$

The Euler formula is **not strictly applicable**. Use **Johnson's parabolic formula**:

$$P_{cr}^{Johnson} = \sigma_y \cdot A \left[1 - \frac{\lambda^2}{4 \lambda_1^2}\right]$$

$$P_{cr}^{Johnson} = 240 \times 10^6 \times 7.069 \times 10^{-4} \times \left[1 - \frac{(52.47)^2}{4 \times (53.65)^2}\right]$$

$$= 169656 \times \left[1 - \frac{2753.1}{11511.9}\right] = 169656 \times [1 - 0.2392]$$

$$= 169656 \times 0.7608 = 129068 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{P_{cr}^{Johnson} \approx 129.07 \text{ kN}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice the **dramatic difference**: Euler's formula overestimates the buckling load for an intermediate column. In KTU 2024 valuation, using Euler's formula for an intermediate column will result in **partial mark deduction**.

---

## 3.5 Summary of Analytical Steps Matrix

| **Analysis Stage** | **Action** | **Equation Used** | **Output** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1. Geometry | Compute $I$, $A$, $r$ | $I = \pi(D^4 - d^4)/64$ | Inertia, area, radius |
| 2. Effective Length | Identify $K$ from BC | $L_e = K L$ | Effective length |
| 3. Slenderness | Compute $\lambda$ | $\lambda = L_e / r$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 4. Classification | Compare with $\lambda_1$ | $\lambda_1 = \pi \sqrt{E/\sigma_y}$ | Long / Intermediate / Short |
| 5. Critical Load | Apply appropriate formula | Euler / Johnson / Rankine | $P_{cr}$ in N |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Effective Length Determination Flow

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the decision logic for selecting the correct $K$ value based on boundary conditions, as required in the KTU 2024 evaluation scheme.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([START: Identify Member End Conditions]) --> nodeB{Slenderness Ratio Known?}
    nodeB -- No --> nodeC[Compute Cross-Section Properties]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[Compute I_min about buckling axis]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC2[Compute Area A]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC3[Compute Radius r = sqrt I over A]
    nodeC3 --> nodeD
    nodeB -- Yes --> nodeD[Determine Boundary Conditions]
    nodeD --> nodeE{Top End Type}
    nodeE -- Fixed --> nodeF{Bottom End Type}
    nodeE -- Pinned --> nodeG{Bottom End Type}
    nodeE -- Free --> nodeH[Case: Cantilever K equals 2.0]
    nodeE -- Guided --> nodeI{Bottom End Type}
    nodeF -- Fixed --> nodeF1[K equals 0.5 Both Fixed]
    nodeF -- Pinned --> nodeF2[K equals 0.7 Fixed Pinned]
    nodeF -- Free --> nodeF3[K equals 2.0 Cantilever]
    nodeG -- Fixed --> nodeG1[K equals 0.7 Pinned Fixed]
    nodeG -- Pinned --> nodeG2[K equals 1.0 Pinned Pinned]
    nodeG -- Free --> nodeG3[Unstable Configuration]
    nodeI -- Fixed --> nodeI1[K equals 1.0 Fixed Guided]
    nodeI -- Pinned --> nodeI2[K equals 2.0 Pinned Guided]
    nodeH --> nodeJ[Compute Effective Length L_e]
    nodeF1 --> nodeJ
    nodeF2 --> nodeJ
    nodeF3 --> nodeJ
    nodeG1 --> nodeJ
    nodeG2 --> nodeJ
    nodeI1 --> nodeJ
    nodeI2 --> nodeJ
    nodeG3 --> nodeK[STOP: Unstable]
    nodeJ --> nodeL[Compute Slenderness lambda]
    nodeL --> nodeM{Compare lambda with lambda_1}
    nodeM -- lambda greater than lambda_1 --> nodeN[Apply EULER formula]
    nodeM -- lambda less than lambda_1 --> nodeO[Apply JOHNSON formula]
    nodeN --> nodeP[OUTPUT: P_cr in Newtons]
    nodeO --> nodeP
```

---

```mermaid
flowchart LR

        sc1A[Top: Fixed Clamp]:::fixed

    end

    subgraph SC2[Case B: Pinned Pinned Classic Euler]

        sc2C[Bottom: Pin Support]:::pin
    end

    subgraph SC3[Case C: Cantilever Fixed Free]

    end

    subgraph SC4[Case D: Fixed Pinned]

    classDef fixed fill:#ffcccb,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

```

## 4.3 Schematic — Effective Length Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Actual Member Length L] --> nodeCalc{Look up K}
    nodeCalc -- Both Fixed --> nodeLe1[Effective Length = 0.5L]
    nodeCalc -- Both Pinned --> nodeLe2[Effective Length = 1.0L]
    nodeCalc -- Fixed Free --> nodeLe3[Effective Length = 2.0L]
    nodeCalc -- Fixed Pinned --> nodeLe4[Effective Length = 0.7L]
    nodeCalc -- Fixed Guided --> nodeLe5[Effective Length = 1.0L]
    nodeLe1 --> nodeFinal[P_cr equals pi squared E I over L_e squared]
    nodeLe2 --> nodeFinal
    nodeLe3 --> nodeFinal
    nodeLe4 --> nodeFinal
    nodeLe5 --> nodeFinal
    nodeFinal --> nodeOut[Output Critical Buckling Load]
```

---

## 4.4 Critical Load vs. Effective Length — Functional Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Effective Length L_e] -->|Increases| B[Buckling Load P_cr Decreases]
    A -->|Decreases| C[Buckling Load P_cr Increases]
    B --> D[Lower Stability Margin]
    C --> E[Higher Stability Margin]
    D --> F[Risk of Lateral Buckling Failure]
    E --> G[Safe Operating Region]
    F --> H[Design Warning: Reduce L_e]
    G --> I[Design OK]
```
